body:
• Ashmore Group plc reported AuM up US$3.3bn (7%) over the quarter to 30 June 2026, comprising positive investment performance of US$2.0bn and net inflows of US$1.3bn.
  • Net inflows spread across local currency, equities, blended and corporate debt; redemptions fell sharply versus the prior quarter, though a net outflow in external debt persisted on a few institutional redemptions.
  • EM fixed income indices rose 2%–5% and the large-cap equity index gained a notable 24%; proportion of AuM outperforming benchmarks broadly consistent with 31 December.
  • CEO Mark Coombs cited EM resilience after the Strait of Hormuz closure and oil-price spike, and a supportive forward macro backdrop for emerging markets.
  • Local currency AuM includes US$9.8bn in overlay/liquidity strategies (31 March 2026: US$8.9bn).
  • Full-year results for the year to 30 June 2026 due on 7 September 2026.
headline: Ashmore's AuM rose US$3.3bn (7%) in Q4 to reflect positive investment performance of US$2.0bn and net inflows of US$1.3bn.
priority_reason: Quarterly AuM trading statement with fund-flow and performance data ahead of full-year results.

figures.note: net outflow in external debt theme

explainer:
This is Ashmore's fourth-quarter assets under management (AuM) statement — a routine flows-and-performance update rather than full results. AuM is the pool of client money Ashmore manages and earns fees on, so growth here signals higher future revenue. The US$3.3bn rise splits into two parts: US$2.0bn of investment performance (markets going up) and US$1.3bn of net inflows (new client money in, minus withdrawals). Net inflows are the more telling figure because they reflect client demand rather than market moves.

The announcement notes flows were positive across most strategies but that the external debt theme still saw a net outflow due to a small number of institutional redemptions — a mixed but improving picture, as overall redemptions fell sharply versus the prior quarter.

Ashmore reports in US dollars because it is an emerging-markets manager, but as a UK-listed firm its accounts translate into sterling; the statement flags the GBP:USD rates used (1.3273 spot at 30 June, 1.3419 average for the year), which affect reported revenue and balance-sheet values. No profit or revenue figures are given here — those come with full-year results on 7 September 2026. The company issued no formal earnings guidance.

body:
• AGM trading update for the three months to 30 June 2026: British Land reiterates guidance for Underlying EPS of at least 30.5p for FY27, underpinned by like-for-like net rental growth at the top end of its 3-5% target range.
  • 567,000 sq ft of leasing completed in Q1, 4.8% ahead of ERV and 8.7% ahead of previous passing rent; a further 1.1m sq ft under offer, 6.8% ahead of ERV and 15.9% ahead of previous passing rent.
  • Retail parks: 303,000 sq ft of leasing (incl. 157,000 sq ft on retail parks) 4.8% ahead of ERV, with 721,000 sq ft under offer; completed and under-offer deals 13.7% ahead of previous passing rent.
  • Development letting progressing: Broadgate Tower now 63% let or under offer; One Triton Square occupancy at 99% post period end; Life Science REIT portfolio integrated with 82,000 sq ft of deals completed.
  • Capital recycling: £83m of assets sold or exchanged at a 3.2% NIY (further £223m under offer); purchased Telford Bridge retail park for £30m at an 8.3% topped-up NIY.
  • CEO Simon Carter to hand over to Joanne McNamara in September; 3-5% ERV growth expected across the portfolio.
  • Note: figures are operational leasing metrics (% ahead of ERV / passing rent); the update does not print P&L comparatives, so no revenue/profit figures are stated.
headline: British Land reiterates FY27 underlying EPS guidance of at least 30.5p as leasing momentum continues across London campuses and retail parks.
priority_reason: Quarterly operational trading update that reiterates full-year FY27 EPS guidance.

guidance.previous: Underlying EPS of at least 30.5p for FY27
guidance.current: Underlying EPS of at least 30.5p for FY27, underpinned by like-for-like net rental growth at the top end of its 3-5% target range; 3-5% ERV growth expected across the portfolio
guidance.commentary: Guidance is reiterated (unchanged), with management citing continued leasing momentum and demand outstripping supply across campuses and retail parks as supporting the outlook.

explainer:
This is an operational update ahead of British Land's AGM, covering the first quarter of its financial year (the three months to 30 June 2026). It is not a full financial results statement, so it does not give revenue, profit or EPS figures for the period — the numbers quoted are leasing metrics. 'Ahead of ERV' means rents agreed on new lettings are above the estimated rental value (the market rent the property owner's valuers expect), while 'ahead of previous passing rent' means the new rent beats what the previous tenant was paying — both signals of a strong rental market and rising income.

On guidance, nothing has changed: British Land has reiterated its target of Underlying EPS of at least 30.5p for FY27 (the year to March 2027). 'Underlying' EPS strips out revaluation movements and one-off items to show recurring earnings from rent. Management says this is underpinned by like-for-like net rental growth running at the top end of its 3-5% range, plus expected 3-5% growth in ERV across the portfolio.

For a trader, the read is steady-as-she-goes: momentum from the prior year has carried into Q1, with high occupancy on retail parks and continued campus demand, and the guidance reaffirmation removes near-term earnings uncertainty. The CEO succession (Simon Carter to Joanne McNamara in September) is flagged but framed as an orderly handover.

body:
• Watches of Switzerland Group PLC FY26: Group revenue £1,828m, +13% constant currency (+11% reported); excluding the 53rd week +11% cc (+8% reported).
  • US revenue £927m, +24% cc (+18% reported), now more than half of Group revenue and profit; UK revenue £901m, +5%.
  • Adjusted EBIT £155m, +6% cc (+3% reported); Adjusted EBIT margin 8.5%, -60bps (FY25: 9.1%), citing brand margin adjustments, US ecommerce investment and a one-off Roberto Coin debtor write-down.
  • Statutory PBT £133m (FY25: £76m), +76%, mainly lower exceptional costs; free cash flow £162m (FY25: £98m), conversion 80%.
  • Net debt cut to £57m (FY25: £96m) after the Deutsch & Deutsch acquisition; £25m buyback completed June 2025 (£13m purchased in FY26).
  • FY27 guidance unchanged from the Q4 update; encouraging first ten weeks' trading, targeting further strong revenue growth and a return to Adjusted EBIT margin expansion.
headline: Watches of Switzerland reports record FY26 revenue of £1.83bn, up 13% in constant currency, with statutory PBT up 76% and FY27 guidance confirmed.
priority_reason: Full-year results with record revenue and profit, ahead of previous guidance, plus FY27 outlook confirmed.

figures.note: now over half of Group revenue and profit

guidance.previous: FY27 guidance as provided in the Q4 trading update
guidance.current: FY27 guidance unchanged from the Q4 trading update; another year of strong revenue growth expected alongside a return to Adjusted EBIT margin expansion (52-week guidance provided on an organic pre-IFRS 16 basis and an IFRS 16 basis)
guidance.commentary: Guidance is confirmed rather than changed. Management points to encouraging trading in the first ten weeks, good US momentum and early signs of UK market improvement, though the specific numeric guidance tables were not reproduced in the scraped text.

explainer:
The headline growth is flattered by two effects worth separating. First, FY26 contained a 53rd week; stripping that out, constant-currency revenue growth falls from 13% to a more comparable 11% (8% reported). Second, 'constant currency' removes the drag from translating US sales back into a stronger pound — reported growth is lower (11% Group, 18% US) because the actual FY26 average rate was $1.34. The US is now the largest market, so sterling/dollar moves matter increasingly for reported numbers.

The 76% jump in statutory profit before tax (£133m from £76m) largely reflects a fall in exceptional (one-off) costs rather than a similar surge in underlying trading; the cleaner measure, Adjusted EBIT, rose a more modest 6% in constant currency. The Adjusted EBIT margin actually slipped 60bps to 8.5%, squeezed by US tariff-driven brand margin changes, investment in US ecommerce and marketing, and a one-off Roberto Coin department-store debtor write-down.

Cash performance was the standout: free cash flow rose to £162m with conversion jumping to 80% from 51%, helping cut net debt to £57m despite paying for the Deutsch & Deutsch acquisition.

On guidance, nothing has changed — FY27 targets are confirmed as set at the Q4 update, so this is a 'maintained' rather than upgraded outlook. Management flags encouraging early-year trading and, importantly, expects the Adjusted EBIT margin to start expanding again in FY27 after this year's decline.

body:
• BP Q2 2026 trading statement ahead of full results on 4 August 2026; contains inside information covering estimates vs 1Q 2026.
  • Reported upstream production 2,170-2,220mboe/d (1Q: 2,339mboe/d), down on seasonal maintenance in the Gulf of America and Middle East disruption; oil 1,420-1,450mboe/d, gas & low carbon 750-770mboe/d.
  • Realisations to lift earnings vs 1Q: oil +$1.8 to 2.1bn, refining margins +$1.2 to 1.4bn, gas +$0.5 to 0.7bn; Brent averaged $103.85/bbl (1Q: $81.13), bp RIM $29.6/bbl (1Q: $16.9).
  • Net debt expected at $22-23bn (1Q: $25.3bn), after $2.9bn to redeem €2.5bn perpetual hybrids and $1.1bn Gulf of America settlement; total of net debt, hybrids and settlement liabilities down ~$6.3-7.3bn.
  • Exploration write-offs ~$(0.5)bn (Bay du Nord sale, Canada) and post-tax impairment adjusting items of ~$1.0bn in gas & low carbon transition businesses; underlying effective tax rate 33-37%.
  • Refining throughput 1,445-1,475mb/d (1Q: 1,527mb/d) on higher turnarounds and lower Whiting volumes after the April third-party event, now resolved.
headline: BP flags lower Q2 upstream production of 2,170-2,220mboe/d but sharply higher oil, gas and refining realisations, with net debt falling to $22-23bn.
priority_reason: Pre-results trading statement flagging quarter-on-quarter earnings drivers, net debt reduction and impairments ahead of Q2 results on 4 August 2026.

figures.metric: Henry Hub gas (first of month index)

figures.note: Q2 2026, primarily Bay du Nord sale
Q2 2026, gas & low carbon transition businesses

guidance.previous: 1Q 2026 group results (28 April 2026) guidance for Q2 and full-year 2026
guidance.current: 1Q26 guidance items continue to apply unless explicitly stated; hybrid reduction of $4.3bn by end 2027, divestment proceeds $9-10bn including ~$6bn from Castrol
guidance.commentary: BP reiterates that its full-year and Q2 guidance from the 1Q 2026 results still applies. It confirms progress on cutting hybrid debt (€2.5bn perpetual hybrids redeemed in June) and on the Castrol divestment, alongside expected lower turnaround and structural cost reductions.

explainer:
This is a pre-results trading statement, not the actual numbers. BP gives directional guidance on how underlying replacement cost (RC) profit — its preferred earnings measure that strips out inventory holding gains and losses — is likely to move from the first quarter to the second, but does not print a profit figure. Full Q2 results follow on 4 August 2026.

The key swing factors are prices and volumes. Production is seasonally lower because of maintenance in the Gulf of America and Middle East disruption, but this is more than offset by much stronger price 'realisations': Brent averaged $103.85 a barrel versus $81.13 in Q1, and refining margins (the bp RIM) nearly doubled. BP quantifies these as positive quarter-on-quarter earnings impacts of roughly $1.8-2.1bn in oil, $1.2-1.4bn in refining and $0.5-0.7bn in gas.

On the balance sheet, net debt is expected to fall to $22-23bn from $25.3bn, even after paying $2.9bn to redeem €2.5bn of perpetual hybrid bonds and $1.1bn towards Gulf of America (Deepwater Horizon) settlement liabilities. BP prefers investors to watch the combined total of net debt, hybrids and settlement liabilities, which it expects to drop by around $6.3-7.3bn.

Two one-off items sit outside underlying profit: ~$0.5bn of exploration write-offs tied to the sale of Bay du Nord in Canada, and ~$1.0bn of post-tax impairment charges on low-carbon 'transition' businesses. Guidance from the April results is reaffirmed rather than changed, including the plan to cut hybrids by $4.3bn by end-2027 and to raise $9-10bn from divestments (about $6bn from Castrol).

body:
• Transact funds under direction (FUD) reached £85.5bn at quarter end, up 23% year-on-year (Q3 FY25: £69.5bn), on strong global equity markets and continued net inflows.
  • Net inflows of £1.0bn in Q3 FY26 (Q3 FY25: £1.2bn), a 6th consecutive quarter above £1bn, equal to 5.2% of opening FUD annualised.
  • Gross inflows of £2.6bn, 5% ahead of prior year (Q3 FY25: £2.5bn); over 2,000 net new clients added in the period.
  • Record average daily FUD of £82.8bn, up 24% year-on-year (Q3 FY25: £66.8bn), directly benefiting revenue.
  • Cost guidance from December 2025 reiterated: total underlying admin expense growth to slow to c.3% in each of FY26 and FY27, savings weighted to H2 FY26.
  • CEO Alex Scott cites distinctive onshore/offshore bond wrappers and new Flexible Reversionary Trust ahead of pensions entering Inheritance Tax scope from April 2027.
headline: IntegraFin's Transact platform funds under direction hit £85.5bn, up 23% year-on-year, with a sixth consecutive quarter of net inflows above £1bn.
priority_reason: Quarterly trading update with platform flow and funds data plus reiterated cost guidance, directly relevant to valuation.

figures.note: Q3 FY26 vs Q3 FY25; 5.2% of opening FUD annualised
Q3 FY26 vs Q3 FY25; record

guidance.previous: Total underlying administrative expense growth expected to slow to c.3% in each of FY26 and FY27 (guidance issued December 2025)
guidance.current: Cost guidance reiterated: total underlying administrative expense growth of c.3% in each of FY26 and FY27, with savings more meaningfully realised in H2 FY26
guidance.commentary: Cost guidance is unchanged from December 2025. Management reaffirms that cost-management initiatives and operational efficiencies will hold admin expense growth to c.3%, with the benefit skewed to the second half of FY26 as initiatives phase in.

explainer:
This is a quarterly trading update covering the three months to 30 June 2026, focused on platform flows rather than profit. Funds under direction (FUD) is the total client money on the Transact platform; the 23% year-on-year rise to £85.5bn reflects both market gains and continued net inflows. Because IntegraFin earns fees on assets held, the record average daily FUD of £82.8bn (up 24%) is the figure most directly tied to revenue over the quarter.

Net inflows of £1.0bn were slightly below the £1.2bn a year earlier. Management attributes this to the pre-tax-year-end period falling in the prior quarter, and stresses it is the sixth straight quarter above £1bn. Gross inflows (new money in, before withdrawals) were actually up 5%, so the softer net figure reflects timing and outflows rather than weakening demand.

On guidance, nothing has changed: the December 2025 cost outlook is reiterated, with underlying admin expense growth held to about 3% in both FY26 and FY27. The key nuance is timing — those savings will show up more clearly in the second half of FY26 as efficiency initiatives take effect, supporting margin expansion later in the year.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document. [rns.json record]
body:
• Debenhams Group (formerly Boohoo Group) reports continued positive trading through June and July, ahead of today's AGM, per CEO Dan Finley.
  • GMV continues to grow year on year, margins are up and returns are down; hot weather especially benefited the Debenhams brand.
  • Cites two upgrades to guidance in nine months; PLT (Young Fashion) returned to growth and profitability.
  • Expects net debt materially lower this year via improved trading and sale of remaining non-core property assets; committed to reducing it below 1x Adjusted EBITDA by year ending Feb-27.
  • Flags much better conversion of Adjusted EBITDA to reported EBITDA and operating profit as transformation costs pass — a feature of H1 results.
  • Medium-term ambition for Debenhams to be a £multi-billion GMV business with £100m+ EBITDA; H1 update due September.
headline: Debenhams Group reports continued positive trading through June and July, with GMV growing year on year, margins up and returns down.
priority_reason: Pre-AGM trading update with continued GMV growth, margin gains and a firm net-debt reduction commitment.

guidance.previous: Two upgrades to guidance over the prior nine months
guidance.current: Net debt expected materially lower this year; committed to below 1x Adjusted EBITDA by the year ending Feb-27, with potential to eliminate debt via brand licensing and disposals
guidance.commentary: No new numeric upgrade in this statement; it reaffirms the positive trajectory after two recent upgrades and sets a clear net-debt reduction commitment. The group signals better earnings conversion as transformation costs recede, to be evident in H1 results.

explainer:
This is a pre-AGM trading statement rather than a full results release, so it contains directional commentary but no hard numbers to compare against last year. GMV (gross merchandise value — the total value of goods sold across the platform) is said to be growing year on year, with higher margins and lower returns, but the company does not print the actual figures, so none can be quoted here.

The group, recently renamed from Boohoo to Debenhams Group, is mid-turnaround. It references two guidance upgrades in the past nine months but does not issue a fresh upgrade today; instead it reaffirms momentum and sets a net-debt target of below 1x Adjusted EBITDA by the financial year ending February 2027, aided by property disposals and possible brand licensing or business sales.

Management also flags that reported EBITDA and operating profit should convert much better from Adjusted EBITDA this year as the one-off transformation costs fall away — a point to watch in the H1 results due in September. Adjusted EBITDA strips out such exceptional items, so the gap between the two measures narrowing indicates the cost of the restructuring is largely behind the business.

body:
• Tullow Oil plc has agreed to terminate its Kenyan royalty payments and back-in right with Gulf Energy Limited in exchange for $9m additional consideration on the sale of Tullow Kenya B.V. to Auron Energy E&P Limited.
  • The deal accelerates near-term cash; proceeds will be used to strengthen the balance sheet. Completion and receipt of funds expected no later than 17 July 2026.
  • Further to the July 2025 SPA to sell 100% of Tullow Kenya B.V.: Tranche A $40m (received 25 September 2025) and Tranche B $40m (received 9 March 2026).
  • Terminated royalty was $0.5/bbl on 80% of production; back-in right was a 30% participation option in future development phases.
  • Tranche C of $40m (due no later than 30 June 2033, with quarterly $2m payments from Q3 2028 subject to Brent averaging $65/bbl) is unaffected and remains payable.
  • CEO Ian Perks framed the transaction as simplifying the portfolio and securing near-term cash proceeds.

body:
• Trekor has submitted a Detailed Project Description (DPD) for the Yellowhead copper project to the BC Environmental Assessment Office, advancing it to the next phase of the provincial EA process.
  • Proposed open-pit mine near Vavenby, British Columbia is designed to produce 178 million pounds of copper annually over a 25-year mine life.
  • Milestone informs the upcoming Readiness Decision and Process Planning phases, alongside the Indigenous-led Simpcw Process; a site investigation and drilling programme is planned for this summer.
  • CEO Stuart McDonald notes future Yellowhead output would position Trekor as one of North America's largest copper producers, adding to the Gibraltar mine (BC) and new Florence Copper (Arizona) production coming on this year.
  • Alongside the DPD, Trekor released an economic impact study prepared by MNP; no financial figures disclosed — early-stage permitting milestone, no near-term impact on trading or guidance.

body:
• Prologis, Inc. published a new investor presentation pressing the case for a possible combination with SEGRO plc, following engagement with SEGRO shareholders and SEGRO's own presentation the prior day — this is not a firm offer under Rule 2.7 and there is no certainty any offer will be made.
  • Prologis argues it brings a superior, better-capitalised data centre platform — a 5.8GW secured/advanced power pipeline across ~30 projects, rising to a 10GW+ longer-term estimate — versus SEGRO's reliance on project-level JVs funded at a ~70% loan-to-cost ratio.
  • Prologis attacks SEGRO's growth outlook: consensus FY2025-2028 EPS CAGR of 4.7% vs 7.1% for European logistics peers, and calls SEGRO's new 50p/share adjusted earnings guidance by 2030 (implied 6.4% CAGR) uncertain and back-end loaded.
  • Prologis says the combination offers SEGRO holders a substantial upfront premium plus participation in the combined platform, and urges SEGRO shareholders to push the SEGRO Board to engage so a binding offer can be put forward.
  • Advisers to Prologis: Rothschild & Co, J.P. Morgan Cazenove, Eastdil Secured and BofA Securities; Linklaters LLP as legal adviser — the offer period is live, triggering Rule 8 disclosure obligations.

Acquisitions of £42.5m (yield 5.9%) across five food store/drive-thru assets, with £48.4m more food store forward fundings in legals pre-let to Lidl, Waitrose and Tesco.

body:
• Property portfolio independently valued at €185.1m as at 30 June 2026 (31 March 2026: €192.6m), a €7.5m or -3.9% fall over the quarter.
  • Decline reflects weaker investment demand, particularly for secondary offices, amid macro uncertainty, inflation and interest-rate concerns, driving further outward yield pressure and more conservative assumptions.
  • Largest markdown at St Cloud, Paris (office): down €3.9m (-11.0%), with exit yield and discount rate up 100bps and net initial yield now 9.5%; further office falls at Hamburg (-4.5%) and Stuttgart (-4.2%).
  • Industrial assets weaker too: Rumilly -4.7% on higher forecast capex and Rennes -4.2%; Apeldoorn data centre -3.7% on shortening lease term ahead of KPN's end-2026 departure.
  • Reiterates the 24 June 2026 plan to put a managed wind-down to shareholders, expected to take two to three years to complete.

body:
• H1 2026 (six months to 30 April 2026): group revenue marginally up 0.3% to £154.3m, EBITDA up 7.1% to £57.0m (margin stable at 36.9%), but PBT down 3.8% to £32.7m; diluted EPS 6.48p, down 3.9%.
  • Laundry the key growth driver — Wash.ME vending revenue up 16.3% to £54.8m, now 38.4% of group vending revenue and 53.9% of group EBITDA; 499 net new laundry machines installed, on track for c.1,300 in FY 2026.
  • Secured largest-ever laundry partnership with ASDA in the UK (ambition of up to 700 machines longer term); renewed 7-year SNCF and 5-year RATP contracts in France worth >£9.0m; first Aldi tie-up (Aldi Austria, 25 laundry + 25 photobooths).
  • April slowdown in vending revenue, driven by a sharp drop in photobooth activity in a few markets — flagged as extraordinary/temporary, linked to weaker consumer confidence in France and geopolitical (Middle East) uncertainty; equipment sales revenue down 14%.
  • Interim dividend cut 6.5% to 3.60p (returns £13.5m); £18.0m share buyback underway, £4.5m (3.6m shares) purchased to date and cancelled.
  • H2 to date shows recovery — May total vending revenue up 11.1% YoY (Wash.ME +25.9%, Photo.ME +1.8%), trend continued in June; guidance reaffirmed at revised FY 2026 PBT of £69m–£74m.

• Old Mutual to migrate its secondary Zimbabwe listing from the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange (ZSE) to the Victoria Falls Stock Exchange (VFEX), resolving the suspension of trading in its shares that has persisted since June 2020.
  • Migration aims to restore trading freedom for Zimbabwean shareholders; VFEX is a USD-denominated exchange, lowering currency risk versus the ZSE.
  • Board cites VFEX scale: average annual turnover per issuer up from USD0.3m in 2021 to USD7.0m in 2025, with 19 counters now listed (up from one in 2020).
  • No pre-listing statement or shareholder approval required; still subject to VFEX approving the listing application and requisite regulatory rulings.
  • No impact on Old Mutual's business or management, including its Zimbabwean operations — purely a market-driven resolution of the trading suspension. Commencement date to be announced once conditions fulfilled.

• LondonMetric Property Plc and Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust Limited (the Consortium) have sweetened their non-binding, indicative all-share possible offer for Picton Property Income Limited, raising the SREIT exchange ratio to 0.894 new SREIT shares (from 0.881) plus an unchanged 0.190 LondonMetric shares per Picton share.
  • Revised terms imply 77.0 pence per Picton share, valuing Picton at approximately £397.0m — a 6.8% premium to the 72.1p close on 9 July 2026, but a 0.7% discount to the 77.5p undisturbed price and an 8.5% discount to EPRA NTA.
  • Picton Board reaffirms support and is minded to unanimously recommend the revised offer if a firm Rule 2.7 offer is made on these terms, subject to confirmatory due diligence and final documentation.
  • Consortium cites enhanced pro-forma earnings accretion of 39.4% (up from 37.7%) and an immediate dividend income uplift of 47.4% for Picton shareholders.
  • Revised terms follow SREIT's FY results (published same day) showing NAV per share of 60.9p at 31 March 2026, down from 61.7p at 31 December 2025. TR Property Investment Trust plc has given a letter of intent over its 11.4% stake.

  • Gleeson Homes completed 1,968 homes, up 9.8% (FY2025: 1,793), including first-ever partnership completions of 320 units and 301 private multi-unit sales; net reservation rate averaged 0.77 per site per week (FY2025: 0.71), though H2 softened to 0.80 vs 0.88 a year earlier.

  • Gleeson Land to report a small operating loss after three transactions slipped into FY2027 (flagged 9 June 2026); portfolio now five sites / 2,553 plots potential (30 June 2025: eight sites / 1,343 plots).
  • Strong balance sheet: net debt of £2.6m (30 June 2025: £0.8m), land creditors low at £15.5m (c.13% of land assets); note results will exclude exceptional restructuring/impairment/legacy items of £11.8m–£15.0m.

body:
• Prologis, Inc. published a new investor presentation pressing the case for a possible combination with SEGRO plc, following the SEGRO board's own presentation the prior day — this is not a firm Rule 2.7 offer and there is no certainty any offer will be made.
  • Prologis argues its data centre platform is superior, citing a dedicated 75+ person data centre team and a power pipeline of 5.8GW across ~30 projects, with a longer-term estimate of 10GW+.
  • Attacks SEGRO's standalone model, saying reliance on project-level JVs (Pure Data Center JV funded at ~70% loan-to-cost) gives away value to partners without earning fees or promotes.
  • Claims SEGRO's growth lags peers: forecast 2025-2028 EPS CAGR of 4.7% vs 7.1% for European logistics peers, and questions SEGRO's new 50p per share adjusted earnings guidance by 2030 (implied 6.4% CAGR).
  • Says a combination offers SEGRO holders a substantial upfront premium plus participation in the combined platform; Prologis urges shareholders to push the SEGRO board to engage and allow a binding offer.
  • Advisers to Prologis: Rothschild & Co, J.P. Morgan Cazenove, Eastdil Secured and BofA Securities; Linklaters as legal adviser. Rule 26.1 copy due on Prologis' website by noon on 10 July 2026.

• Rachel Addison, Senior Independent Director of Hollywood Bowl Group plc, has been appointed a Non-Executive Director and Chair of the Audit Committee of Rosebank Industries plc, effective today.
  • Routine UK Listing Rule 6.4.9 external-directorship disclosure — no impact on Hollywood Bowl's trading or guidance.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• Workspace Board rebuffs activist Saba ahead of the 23 July 2026 AGM, urging shareholders to vote FOR all Board resolutions (1-20) and AGAINST all Saba resolutions (21-26), following Saba's updated presentation and Q&A published 7 July 2026.
  • Board characterises Saba's plan as a high-risk, stealth 'managed wind-down' — originally a 12-month wind-down demanded in January 2026, since amended — and disputes Saba's estimate of 50-75 credible asset buyers, saying none identified have shown interest.
  • Board challenges Saba's cited 13 FY26 disposals at a 7.2% average discount to latest book value; says versus the valuation six months prior the discount was 19.6%, and three FY27 disposals since have gone at a 22.3% average discount, warning acceleration would force wider discounts.
  • Over £200m of assets currently being marketed for sale via leading London agents; Board says Saba's unidentified external-manager outsourcing model is unclear and third-party flex management is not established in the UK.
  • Portfolio: 3.8m sq. ft. across 57 London/South East locations, ~4,000 businesses; FTSE 250 REIT.
  • Diary: Q1 Business Update on 15 July 2026; AGM vote deadline 11am 21 July 2026.

time_period: FY to 31 March 2027 (year to date since 1 April 2026)
period_detail: FY to 31 March 2027 (year to date since 1 April 2026)
type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• AGM trading update: £7.6bn NNN (triple net lease) portfolio (group total £8bn) continues to perform well; occupancy up to 98.3% from 97.7% at the start of the financial year, vacancy cut to 1.7%, average lease length 17 years.
  • Since 1 April 2026: £139m of investment transactions and £6.7m pa of extra rental income from 72 asset management initiatives.
  • Disposals of £96.7m across 26 assets (c.£100m including subsequent sales), with a further £23m under offer; sales in line with 31 March 2026 book values. Since 21 May, 14 assets sold for £47.3m at 6.6% NIY, incl. nine Travelodge hotels for £37.4m.
  • Acquisitions of £42.5m at 5.9% yield (four forward-funded food stores plus a McDonald's drive-thru at Birmingham Fort); a further £48.4m of food store fundings in legals, pre-let to Lidl, Waitrose and two to Tesco.
  • Rental growth: 49 rent reviews added £4.3m at avg 16% uplift (logistics +23%, open-market logistics +48%); 23 lettings/regears added £2.4m (regears avg +26%); a further £1.4m pa in legals.
  • Picton Property Income offer: since the 16 June 2026 proposed offer, due diligence is advancing and documentation being finalised to enable the Consortium to announce a firm intention (Rule 2.7) offer. CEO Andrew Jones cites a growing pipeline of development fundings and pension-fund asset sales.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• Playtech plc reports excellent H1 2026 trading significantly ahead of market expectations, driven by the US (partnership with Hard Rock Digital) plus continued strength in Mexico, Colombia and certain European markets.
  • H1 2026 Adjusted EBITDA expected to be over €155m, with Americas performance accelerating through May and June.
  • FY2026 Adjusted EBITDA now guided to at least €270m, significantly above consensus — prior analyst estimates ranged €205m–€225m with a mean of €219m across 7 analysts.
  • H2 2026 Adjusted EBITDA expected to be lower than H1: Hard Rock Digital's Past Motor Racing (PMR) revenue normalising to a lower but more sustainable level, investment into a Brazil partnership (contributing from 2027), and full impact of higher UK Remote Gaming Duty (effective April 2026).
  • H1 2026 interim results to be published on 10 September 2026, hosted by CEO Mor Weizer and CFO Chris McGinnis.

• Legal & General Group Plc announces indicative results of its cash tender offers (launched 30 June 2026) across five series of notes, which expired at 4.00 p.m. on 7 July 2026.
  • Expects to set the Final Acceptance Amount at ~GBP499,999,175 (against a Maximum Acceptance Amount of GBP500m), split ~GBP200,005,000 of GBP Notes and ~US$401,330,000 of Tier 2 Notes, at a USD/Sterling rate of 0.7475.
  • Valid tenders received: US$519,662,000 of the US$850m 2047 Tier 2 Notes; GBP219,381,000 of the 2031 Notes; GBP143,866,000 of the GBP200m 2033 Notes; GBP36,000,000 of the GBP40m 2033 Notes; and all GBP10,000,000 of the 2041 Notes.
  • The New Financing Condition is satisfied — the new notes settled on 7 July 2026 — so the buyback is funded by fresh issuance.
  • GBP Notes pricing at ~11.00 a.m. on 8 July 2026; scaling factors apply to oversubscribed series (GBP40m 2033 and 2041 Notes exempt); settlement expected 10 July 2026.
  • Routine liability-management exercise with no impact on trading or guidance.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• Apple signs new multiyear commitment with Broadcom to design and produce custom silicon and wireless connectivity components, with the agreement expected to exceed $30 billion.
  • Deal to yield more than 15 billion U.S.-made chips and marks Apple's largest commitment to date under its American Manufacturing Program (AMP).
  • Broadcom to invest $1.5 billion in capex to expand and modernise its Fort Collins, Colorado facility, producing RF components (including FBAR filters) and advanced wireless connectivity tech.
  • Forms part of Apple's stated pledge to invest $600 billion in the U.S. economy over four years; CEO Tim Cook and Broadcom CEO Hock Tan both cited long-standing partnership.
  • No financial guidance or earnings figures provided; announcement is strategic/supply-chain in nature.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• Associated British Foods plc has completed its acquisition of Hovis Group Limited from Endless LLP, combining it with ABF's UK bakery business Allied Bakeries.
  • From today the two operate as Hovis Bakeries under the line 'Nourishing the Nation', targeting growth segments including healthy options.
  • Integration expected to deliver substantial operational synergies across production and distribution, though restructuring will require upfront costs.
  • Acquisition expected to be marginally dilutive to earnings in FY2027, becoming accretive thereafter.
  • Sarah Arrowsmith appointed Chief Executive of Hovis Bakeries; CEO George Weston flags aim of returning the UK bread business to growth.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• SEGRO and Pure Data Centres Group form a second 50:50 joint venture to develop a 48MW fully fitted data centre in Paris, targeting a pre-let with a global hyperscaler — SEGRO's first data centre development in Continental Europe.
  • Gross capital required of approximately £0.8 billion (including the contribution value of SEGRO's powered land), projected to deliver an attractive yield on cost.
  • SEGRO contributes prime land with pre-secured 75MVA of power at fair market value; cash equity contribution expected to be around £60 million over the total construction period, with the remaining equity from Pure DC.
  • Delivered in phases: first phase complete after approximately three years, final phase roughly one year later, with income generated incrementally; construction starts once planning permission and lease commitments are secured.
  • First JV (Premier Park, Park Royal) progressing well — final planning approval received in March 2026 ahead of schedule, now in active dialogue with two hyperscale customers each interested in the entire site capacity.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• FY26 (year ended 31 March 2026) preliminary results: revenue +4% to £7,482.1m (2025: £7,173.5m) on record passenger numbers; results in line with expectations.
  • Operating profit £439.6m (2025: £446.5m) after absorbing £11.0m of London Gatwick startup costs plus £50m of employment tax and SAF premium increases; profit before tax £551.0m (2025: £593.2m).
  • Basic EPS 211.2p (2025: 213.1p); final dividend 12.4p, up 2% (2025: 12.1p); £363.0m returned to shareholders via buybacks and dividends during the year.
  • New £250m on-market share buyback announced (to complete by 31 May 2027, shares to be cancelled); cash and money market deposits £3,292.5m (2025: £3,155.8m), net cash £2,012.9m.
  • Flown passengers +5% to 20.83m; flight-only +15% to 7.64m; package holiday customers +1% to 6.62m. London Gatwick six-aircraft launch ahead of expectations, further expansion planned for Summer 2027.
  • Outlook: Summer 2026 on-sale capacity 7.7% ahead of Summer 2025, booked-to-date passengers +7.1%, load factor 1.2ppts ahead of prior year; CEO Steve Heapy cites confidence in sustainable long-term growth.

  • Named on lot 1 (projects above £10m) and lot 2 (projects above £30m), covering public sector building works across Yorkshire and Humber, the North East, Lincolnshire, Nottinghamshire, Derbyshire and parts of Leicestershire.
  • CEO Bill Hocking called the framework "an important route to market" for the group's businesses in the region; no contract value to Galliford Try or financial guidance impact stated.

body:
• IG Group proposes a new Jersey-incorporated Group holding company (New HoldCo) via a Court-approved scheme of arrangement, with shareholders swapping shares one-for-one; shareholder circular expected Q3 2026 and the scheme effective Q4 2026, subject to shareholder, FCA and regulatory approval. Listing, tax residence and London operations unchanged.
  • H1 2026 total revenue expected at approx £643m, up around 18% (organic approx £624m, up around 16%); first trades up approx 107% reported / 74% organic; active customers up approx 66% reported / 13% organic.
  • Continued to trade well in Q2 2026; expects full-year results in line with market expectations and reiterates upgraded 2026 guidance — organic revenue growth of 10-15% off the ~£1,100m 2025 base, EBITDA margins in the mid-40s% range, net interest income of £110-120m.
  • Operating model streamlined: UK & Ireland, Europe and APAC & Middle East combined into one unit under Michael Healy (CEO, IG Consumer); North America stays with Michael Vaughan and Institutional led by Andy Biggs (CEO, IG Securities). Changes take effect H2 2026.
  • Strategic review also weighing acquisitions, listing venues and potential combinations with industry participants; outcomes to be set out at a Strategy Update in autumn 2026.
  • Interim results scheduled for 31 July 2026.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• Pre-close trading update: positive momentum continued through H1 2026, with like-for-like revenue up 3% year on year, in line with expectations; full-year outlook reaffirmed.
  • Revenue for the six months to 30 June 2026 rose 10% to £133.1m (H1 25: £121.0m); average members up 5% to 1,002,000 and ARPMM up 5% to £22.14 — passing the 1 million member milestone.
  • Closing membership of 991,000 (vs 923,000 at 31 Dec 2025; 949,000 at 30 June 2025); four new gyms opened in H1, on track to open at least 20 in 2026 (264 sites operated at period end).
  • Net debt £58.0m at 30 June 2026, expected to rise by year end on H2-weighted openings, refurbishments and buyback; 2.2m shares bought, £3.8m of the £10m programme spent.
  • £15m increase in bank facilities agreed in June, taking combined facilities to £117m (£60m Term Loan + £57m RCF) maturing June 2028.
  • Interim results due 9 September 2026; CEO Will Orr "confident in the full year outlook".

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• Vistry Group PLC expects an H1 2026 loss before tax of approximately £30m (before any CEO Review impacts), with new CEO Adam Daniels treating 2026 as a transition year to lower financial leverage; underlying H1 PBT was a modest c.£20m before c.£(50)m of cash generation actions (enhanced discounting, accelerated asset sales, one-off impairments on low/nil-margin sites).
  • FY26 adjusted PBT expected in line with market consensus of £200m, excluding any impact from the ongoing CEO Review — findings to be detailed in half-year results on 24 September 2026.
  • Completed c.6,100 homes (H1 25: 6,889); sales rate 1.03 (H1 25: 1.01); average private-sales discount to book price jumped to 7.1% (H1 25: 1.4%).
  • Net debt of £470m at 30 June 2026; average daily net debt £799m in H1; targeting average daily debt below £650m in H2 and reaffirming a net cash position in excess of £100m at year end.
  • Forward book of £3.9bn, 80% forward sold for FY26; Open Market conditions deteriorated in Q2 on lower confidence tied to the Middle East conflict, with no significant improvement expected in H2 or early 2027.
  • CEO Review initial conclusions: c.£25m annual overhead savings from a Voluntary Exit Scheme, exit from Part Exchange (freeing c.£50m of debt), a more focused regional footprint, and reshaping of the land bank and WIP (unsold private WIP cut from c.£600m to under £300m in H1).

• ZIGUP plc FY2026 (year ended 30 April 2026): underlying revenue +5.2% on vehicle hire revenue +9.8%; total revenue +2.6% amid normalising defleets; EBIT before disposal profits up 9.7%.
  • Underlying PBT £160.1m, 4.1% lower as disposal profits normalise and net finance costs rose £5.3m; reported PBT £102.0m after £26.8m exceptionals (Charged EV and NewLaw exit) and £13.9m depreciation unwind.
  • Steady state cash generation jumped to £95.7m (2025: £16.7m) after £355.2m replacement capex and £132.4m growth capex; on track for >£200m steady state cash in FY2028.
  • Group fleet over 139,400 vehicles (2025: 131,600), +5.9%; Spain vehicle hire revenue +16.2%, UK&I +5.2%; rental margins 19.3% Spain, 16.0% UK&I.
  • Contract wins/renewals incl. National Highways 10-year extension, Howden Insurance and Tesco Insurance; UK&I simplification into Northgate Mobility and FMG live from 1 May 2026, targeting £20m run-rate savings by FY2028.
  • Confident in FY2027 with VOH growth in both geographies; analyst expectations for FY2027 adjusted PBT £162.9m–£170.0m.

• Greatland Resources Limited (AIM/ASX: GGP) appoints Nick Strong as Chief Operating Officer, commencing 5 October 2026, with responsibility for the Telfer and Havieron operations.
  • Strong is a mining engineer with 25+ years' experience, currently General Manager – Hemi & KCGM Growth at Northern Star Resources; prior senior roles at Rio Tinto and Newcrest Mining (incl. Cadia).
  • Otto Richter moves from Acting COO to newly created Chief Technical Officer on Strong's start date, focusing on strategic mine planning and technical studies including West Dome Underground.
  • Reinforces leadership for the planned multi-decade integrated Telfer-Havieron gold-copper hub; no impact on current operations or guidance stated.

body:
• Trading update and Q2 fund valuations: reservations for the 2026/27 academic year running ahead — 86% of Unite beds now reserved (2025/26: 85%), with 71% of Empiric/Hello Student beds reserved (2025/26: 61%).
  • FY2026 adjusted EPS guidance reiterated at 41.5-43.0p; first-half trading in line with expectations, aided by short-term letting of vacant rooms and cost control.
  • On track for 0-2% like-for-like income growth for 2026/27, now expected to be driven by 94-96% occupancy and 1-2% rental growth (previously lower end of 93-96% occupancy and 2-3% rental growth — mix shift toward occupancy).
  • Property valuations lower: H1 2026 valuation deficit expected at 6.0-6.5%; in Q2 USAF down 2.2% (to £2,928m, 5.4% yield) and LSAV down 3.7% (to £1,959m, 5.0% yield), driven by yield expansion on higher rates with broadly stable rents.
  • Confident of £300-400m of disposals in 2026 (Unite share) — £130m completed YTD at a 4.8% weighted average yield, plus c.£500m actively marketed; further strategic update at interims.
  • £165m of buybacks completed in H1 (32.7m shares, c.6% of share capital, at an average 505p); Empiric cost synergies on track (£9m in 2026, £17m p.a. from 2027).

• Sold £69m of non-core assets, including multiple central Dublin holdings and a further non-core investment, at a substantial premium to book value.
  • Central Dublin holdings sold to Transport Infrastructure Ireland to unlock land for the city's planned Metrolink rail system; certain strategic Dublin holdings retained.
  • With the final Leeds disposal in January 2026, total 2026 non-core divestment reaches £75m.
  • Proceeds recycled into balance-sheet strength and JV buyouts — the Company recently completed acquisition of the remaining 50% of the Ilac Dublin city-centre scheme, taking full control.
  • CEO Rob Wilkinson framed the deals as recycling capital from non-core disposals into JV buyouts.

  • Deal combines Elavon's small-business payments and software with Wix's website-creation and unified commerce tech, integrating online and in-person sales, bookings, inventory, payments and real-time analytics on a single tiered platform.

• Investec plc and Investec Limited appoint David Duffy and Ingrid Johnson as independent non-executive directors, effective 14 July 2026.
  • David Duffy is a veteran banker; CEO of Virgin Money UK plc (formerly CYBG) from 2015 to 2024, and previously CEO of Allied Irish Banks and Standard Bank International, with earlier roles at ING and Goldman Sachs.
  • Ingrid Johnson, a chartered accountant, was Group Finance Director of Old Mutual plc from 2014 to 2018 after two decades at Nedbank; currently a director of Teleperformance SE.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• Purdue University/CME Group Ag Economy Barometer fell 6 points to 113 in June, with the Current Conditions Index down to an 18-month low of 102 and the Future Expectations Index off 7 points.
  • High input costs remained the top concern, cited by 47% of producers as the biggest challenge, ahead of low crop and livestock prices at 23%; 42% said high input costs are limiting improvements to their financial position this year.
  • Farm Capital Investment Index slid to 40, its lowest since September 2024; just 12% of farmers said they were better off than a year ago and only 22% expect improvement over the next 12 months.
  • Short-term farmland value expectations dropped from 130 in May to 124, while long-term expectations rose to 166, tying the record high.
  • Survey of 400 farmers conducted 15–19 June 2026; note this is a sentiment survey published by exchange operator CME Group, not a corporate trading update — no impact on CME's own earnings or guidance.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• GameStop Corp. reaffirmed at its 7 July 2026 AGM that it remains committed to pursuing an acquisition of eBay, Inc., despite eBay's board publicly rejecting its initial approach on 12 May 2026.
  • Original 3 May 2026 non-binding proposal was to buy all eBay stock at $125 per share (50% cash, 50% GameStop stock), a 46% premium to eBay's unaffected price; chairman/CEO Ryan Cohen said he would not comment beyond what is already public.
  • GameStop directly owns 4,343,725 eBay shares and holds put/call option pairs (expiring 23 February 2028) giving economic exposure to a further 39,046,658 shares; the HSR antitrust condition was satisfied on 3 June 2026, allowing physical settlement.
  • No definitive agreement exists — transaction is subject to negotiation, financing, regulatory and shareholder approvals; eBay board has not engaged.
  • GameStop withdrew its proposed CEO performance award for Cohen ahead of the meeting so shareholders could focus on the eBay bid and operating performance.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• FirstGroup subsidiary Specialist Passenger Solutions (SPS) awarded the Sizewell C worker transport and site shuttle contract, a term of up to five years from July 2026.
  • SPS to run a zero-emission fleet of c.150 buses (hydrogen and electric), provided by Sizewell C Ltd, creating around 400 new jobs; peak construction workforce estimated at nearly 8,000.
  • Builds on SPS's Hinkley Point C operation, running since 2016 and currently moving more than 12,000 people a day.
  • CEO Graham Sutherland flags Business and Coach segment revenues doubled to £230m in FY 2026; group FY 2026 revenue was £1.72bn.
  • Portfolio-diversifying win with no stated financial guidance impact; contract value not disclosed.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.

• Cairn Homes plc H1 2026 trading update: output up 60% to over 1,100 new homes in the six months to 30 June 2026, driving strong cash generation.
  • Closed and forward order book stands at c.4,800 homes worth €1.8 billion; building across 26 developments in the Greater Dublin Area, Cork and Galway.
  • FY26 guidance reaffirmed; expects a 35% rise in housing output across 2026 and 2027 to c.6,000 new homes, including c.3,200 homes in 2027.
  • Reports sector-leading ROE of 16.6% (FY25); to update on capital allocation plans in September.
  • Interim results due 2 September 2026.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• Shell plc issued its second quarter 2026 update note, giving an overview of current expectations ahead of results due on 30 July 2026.
  • Note flags market dislocations: realised refining and chemicals margins are running lower than the calculated Indicative Refining Margin / Indicative Chemicals Margin and have been adjusted accordingly.
  • Full-year price and margin sensitivities may not reflect realised movements in an individual quarter given the ongoing situation in the Middle East.
  • Company-compiled consensus (managed by Vara Research) is expected to be published on 22 July 2026.
  • Note: the scraped text contains only the accompanying definitions and disclaimers — the actual segmental outlook figures (production, margins, opex, cash flow) did not capture, so specific guidance numbers are unavailable here.

type_workings: Type stated explicitly in the source document.
body:
• Q3 FY2026 (three months to 30 June 2026) Group revenues up 18% vs Q3 2025, driven by Group volumes +17%; Aerospace and Electronics the main growth drivers, with some stabilisation in Medical.
  • Full-year guidance maintained: Board expects FY 2026 underlying PBT of £42m-£44m. ASP steady at £68/kg (Q3 2025: £68/kg).
  • YTD Group revenues +7%, volumes +10%.
  • Profit Improvement Plan on track for annualised cost savings of at least £10m (fully realised FY 2027); c.10% headcount reduction, most affected staff left in Q3, with initial benefits in Q4. FY 2026 cash exceptional items ~£10m.
  • Net debt £43.0m at 30 June 2026 (cash £27.5m), after ~£12m interim dividend payment (13.42p/share).
  • Capital Markets Event 'Unlocking Victrex's Potential' set for 24 September 2026 in London; CEO Dr James Routh flags normal seasonality and macro/energy-cost caution into Q4.

  • Board acknowledges its largest shareholder cast the vast majority of the votes against; further engagement with shareholders across for, against and abstaining positions has continued.
  • Full disclosure on the votes and actions taken to be provided in the FY2026 Annual Report — no impact on trading or guidance.

• Keller Group plc upgrades full-year 2026 guidance: Board now expects revenue and underlying operating profit to be materially ahead of current market consensus, citing accelerating momentum since the 20 May AGM update.
  • Consensus (seven analysts) for year to 31 Dec 2026 is revenue of £3,150m and underlying operating profit of £223m.
  • North America (~60% of Group revenue) materially outperformed on record volumes, driven by infrastructure and data centre demand, more than offsetting a softening south Florida residential market.
  • Europe & Middle East robust (Scandinavia, central Europe, Middle East offsetting weak western Europe); Asia Pacific broadly in line, with Austral offsetting Australian foundations pricing pressure.
  • Order book at a record c.£1.9bn, boosted by the multi-year I-40 highway remediation contract; balance sheet and cash generation remain strong.
  • Interim results (six months to 30 June) due 4 August 2026; Capital Markets Day on 14 October 2026.

• Origin Materials, Inc. filed a Post-Effective Amendment No. 1 to deregister all unsold securities under its $100,000,000 Form S-3 shelf (Reg. No. 333-289615), terminating the effectiveness of the registration statement.
  • Move follows the Board's unanimous approval on 1 May 2026 of a plan of complete liquidation and dissolution, subject to stockholder approval — the company is winding down and terminating all offerings.
  • Filing signed in West Sacramento, California on 6 July 2026; the amendment itself is an administrative deregistration, but signals the company's intended dissolution.

type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'recommended cash offer'
body:
• Rule 2.4 announcement: easyJet Board and Castlelake, L.P. have reached agreement in principle on the key financial terms of a recommended cash offer.
  • Under Castlelake's "Fifth Proposal" (submitted 4 July 2026), it would acquire the entire issued and to-be-issued share capital of easyJet not already held by Castlelake for £6.90 per share in cash, including a partial unlisted share alternative.
  • Offer price of 558.20p last close implies the £6.90 (690p) proposal is at a ~23.6% premium to the prior close.
  • Board concluded the terms are at a value it "would be minded to recommend" to shareholders if a firm Rule 2.7 offer is made on those terms, subject to agreement of all other terms including the partial unlisted share alternative.
  • Castlelake would agree to a "best endeavours" commitment to obtain required regulatory clearances and approvals.
  • Castlelake stated it has "tremendous respect for easyJet and its people, along with its intention to support its future growth and transformation to a stronger, more resilient European airline," and is "supportive of easyJet's fleet modernisation programme," which it regards as "central to the Company's long-term competitiveness, efficiency and sustainability objectives."
  • Proposal remains subject to satisfaction/waiver of customary pre-conditions, including completion of satisfactory due diligence and agreement of definitive transaction documentation.
  • PUSU deadline extended (with Panel consent) to 5.00pm on Monday, 3 August 2026, by which Castlelake must announce a firm intention to bid (Rule 2.7) or walk away (Rule 2.8).
  • Castlelake reserves the right to vary the mix of consideration, to bid on less favourable terms (e.g. with Board agreement, a lower rival bid, or a Rule 9 waiver/reverse takeover), and to reduce the price by any further dividend/distribution paid by easyJet.
  • This is the fifth proposal, indicating a series of prior rejected/escalated approaches.
  • No certainty a firm offer will be made; shareholders advised to take no action.

type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'non-executive' with change verb 'appoints' in opening bullets
body:
• Appoints Melody Meyer and Sandra (Sandy) Stash as Non-Executive Directors and members of the Remuneration Committee, effective 13 July 2026.
  • Both are US-based, explicitly to strengthen and balance the Board "particularly as we continue to grow our US business to support the Group's profitable growth strategy" (Chair Lord Charles Allen).
  • Meyer: current NED at AbbVie (Chair of Public Policy & Sustainability Committee) and Airswift Parent LLC; former NED at NOV Inc. and BP plc (chaired Safety and Sustainability Committee); 37-year Chevron executive career, culminating as President of Chevron Asia Pacific Exploration & Production.
  • Stash: current boards include ACWA Power and Trans Mountain Company (chairs Operations and HSE Committee); former NED roles at Diversified Energy (Senior Independent Director), EVRAZ plc, Medallion Midstream, Lucid Energy and Chaarat Gold; 40+ year energy career including EVP Safety, Operations and Engineering at Tullow Oil, plus Talisman Energy and BP America.
  • Meyer said: "I am delighted to be joining the Board of Balfour Beatty at an exciting time for the Group. I look forward to working with my fellow Directors to support the delivery of its strategy and continued focus on operational excellence, sustainability and long-term value creation."
  • Stash said: "I am pleased to be joining Balfour Beatty's Board to contribute to a business with a strong track record in delivering complex infrastructure projects globally. I look forward to supporting the Group's continued focus on safety, performance and sustainable profitable growth."
  • Board skills emphasis on major projects, safety-critical delivery, operational performance and risk/governance; no financial or trading information disclosed ("No further information is required to be disclosed under LR 6.4.6").

type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'recommended cash offer'
body:
• Scheme Document for Ingredion's recommended cash offer for Tate & Lyle published today (3 July 2026) and being sent to shareholders, implementing the acquisition first agreed on 8 June 2026.
  • Deal structured as a court-sanctioned scheme of arrangement under Part 26 of the Companies Act 2006, requiring shareholder approval at a Court Meeting and a special resolution at a General Meeting.
  • Court Meeting scheduled for 11:00 a.m. on 28 July 2026, followed by the General Meeting at 11:15 a.m., both at 5 Marble Arch, London.
  • Scheme expected to become effective during the second half of 2027, gated on the Material Antitrust Conditions — a long completion window signalling significant regulatory scrutiny.
  • Long Stop Date of 8 December 2027; Court Sanction Hearing expected no later than 14 days after satisfaction/waiver of the Material Antitrust Conditions.
  • Tate & Lyle Directors, advised by Goldman Sachs and Greenhill on the financial terms, consider the terms "fair and reasonable" and unanimously recommend shareholders vote in favour, having given irrevocable undertakings over their own holdings.
  • Latest times for lodging Forms of Proxy: 11:00 a.m. (Court Meeting) and 11:15 a.m. (General Meeting) on 24 July 2026; Voting Record Time 6:30 p.m. on 24 July 2026.
  • On effectiveness, Tate & Lyle listing to be cancelled by 8:00 a.m. on D+3 Business Days, with cash consideration cheques/CREST credits despatched within 14 days of the Effective Date.
  • No change to the previously announced offer terms or price disclosed in this administrative/procedural update.

  • Seller is Potomac View Investments, L.P., managed by EIG Management Company — the placing represents the disposal of EIG's entire residual shareholding in Harbour.

type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'acquisition of'
body:
• Recommended all-cash acquisition of Capricorn by Genel Energy (via Bidco, Genel Energy No.9 Ltd), effected by Scottish scheme of arrangement.
  • Total Acquisition Value of US$4.74 per share (≈357p at the announcement FX rate), split into US$3.75 cash + a US$0.99 special "Permitted Dividend."
  • Premium of ~34% to the 266p undisturbed close (10 March 2026) and ~48% to the 241p three-month VWAP.
  • Implies an equity value of ~US$360m / £271m on a fully diluted basis.
  • The Permitted Dividend represents an aggregate ~US$75m payout — described as substantially in excess of any dividend Capricorn could pay standalone while retaining working capital, and a significant portion of its US$129m cash balance (as at 31 May 2026).
  • Dividend paid in Sterling equivalent of US$0.99, within 14 days of the Effective Date; only payable if the Scheme becomes Effective, and its non-payment is flagged as a risk that could cause the Scheme to fall away.
  • Cash consideration expressed in US$ (reflecting Capricorn's US$-denominated business); a Sterling Foreign Exchange Facility election will be offered (indicative ~282p on the Acquisition Price alone, FX-dependent).
  • Irrevocable undertakings secured over ~39.3% of share capital — Palliser Capital 13.8%, Newtyn 12.2%, Kite Lake 8.0%, Madison Avenue 5.3%, plus CEO Randy Neely's 0.006%; shareholder irrevocables lapse only if a competing bid tops the deal by ≥6.5%.
  • Capricorn Directors intend to recommend unanimously; Canaccord Genuity (Rule 3 adviser) considers terms fair and reasonable.
  • Deal conditional on the "Egyptian Condition" (EGPC/Egyptian Government consent to the transfer of the Western Desert concessions) and an Egyptian Competition Authority merger clearance — Genel may seek Panel consent to invoke the Egyptian Condition if unsatisfied by the Long-stop Date (2 January 2027).
  • Financing via Bidco cash plus a US$125m unsecured bridge facility arranged by Mauritius Commercial Bank (3-month initial maturity), guaranteed by Genel; PJT satisfied funds are available for the Acquisition Price (but not the Permitted Dividend).
  • Enlarged Group would hold pro-forma 2P reserves of 117 mmboe and combined December 2025 exit production of 41,003 bopd, split roughly evenly between Kurdistan and Egypt, with further upside in Oman/Somaliland.
  • Scheme Document within 28 days; Scheme expected Effective in H2 2026.
  • Capricorn to be delisted and re-registered as a private company; deal is a reverse takeover for Genel, triggering a listing-category transfer (Jefferies as sponsor); Enlarged Group HQ to be London.
  • Genel expects a material reduction in Capricorn headcount (particularly corporate/support functions) and cost synergies via rationalisation of systems and contracts; non-executive Capricorn directors to resign on completion.
  • Context: Capricorn cited persistently low trading liquidity and non-operator/single-country (Egypt) concentration risk (reliance on timely EGPC collections) as key drivers; UK North Sea value-realisation efforts have not yet yielded an executable deal.
  • Neely (Capricorn CEO): "Capricorn requires greater scale to materially improve trading liquidity. We believe the transaction with Genel crystallises the value created by Capricorn while providing shareholders with a clear and efficient exit."
  • Weir (Genel CEO): "Today we announce a landmark transaction to acquire a leading oil and gas portfolio in Egypt - a move that delivers our strategic intent, reshapes our company's growth trajectory, diversifies our portfolio of oil and gas fields and begins our role as a partner in Egypt's energy future... we will work with the operator to accelerate production optimisation, replace reserves, reduce unit costs, and capture significant near-term cash flow while preserving optionality for future development."

time_period: June 2026 and Q2 2026 (June ADV and quarter-to-date trading volumes)
period_detail: June 2026 and Q2 2026 (June ADV and quarter-to-date trading volumes)
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q2 2026'
body:
• Record June ADV of 30.6 million contracts, up 19% year-over-year.
  • Second-highest Q2 ever at 29.8 million contracts ADV.
  • All-time monthly volume records set in Equity Index and Agricultural products in June.
  • Interest Rate ADV +17% to 13.6 million contracts (June); U.S. Treasury futures & options +19% to 7.2 million.
  • Within rates: 2-Year Treasury futures +37% to 951,000; 10-Year Treasury options +28% to 1.1 million; 30-Day Fed Funds +33% to 533,000 — front-end/policy-sensitive contracts led.
  • SOFR futures & options +14% to 5.8 million contracts (June).
  • Equity Index ADV +54% to a record 10.1 million contracts (June) — the standout mover.
  • Record Micro E-mini Nasdaq-100 futures ADV of 3.2 million contracts (June).
  • Agricultural ADV +8% to a record 2.3 million contracts (June); Corn futures +20% to 619,000.
  • Metals ADV +12% to 967,000 (June); Micro Silver futures +191% to 69,000 contracts.
  • Foreign Exchange ADV +6% to 1.2 million contracts (June).
  • Cryptocurrency ADV +76% to 334,000 contracts ($10.7bn notional) in June; Micro Bitcoin +46% to 77,000.
  • International ADV +17% to 9.3 million contracts (June): EMEA +15% to 6.7 million, APAC +21% to 2.2 million.
  • Micro products penetration: Micro E-mini Equity Index = 50% of Equity Index ADV; Micro Metals = 53% of Metals ADV; Micro Energy = 8% of Energy ADV.
  • BrokerTec overall ADNV +17% to $1.078 trillion in June; U.S. Repo +11% to $398bn, European Repo +19% to €363bn, U.S. Treasury cash +5% to $93bn.
  • EBS Spot FX ADNV +7% to $68bn (June).
  • Customer collateral balances (rolling 3 months to May 2026): $150.7bn cash, $173.4bn non-cash — proxy for float/interest income base.
  • Q2 Equity Index ADV +13% to 8.6 million contracts; record Micro E-mini Nasdaq-100 of 2.4 million.
  • Q2 Cryptocurrency ADV +32% to 250,000 contracts ($13.7bn notional); Ether futures +10% to 18,000.
  • Q2 Energy included Micro WTI Crude Oil futures +209% to 283,000 contracts; Q2 Micro Silver +263% to 74,000.

• Completed a £445 million refinancing, comprising a £375 million syndicate and £70 million bilateral facility, refinancing all existing unsecured loan facilities maturing over the next two years.
  • Facility structure: £225m syndicated 3-year RCF; £45m bilateral 3-year RCF; £150m syndicated 5-year RCF; £25m bilateral 5-year RCF — each with two one-year extension options.
  • Added two new banking relationships with Lloyds Bank and ABN AMRO, while retaining Barclays, HSBC UK, ING and RBS International.
  • Average margin across facilities is 1.18% above SONIA (drawn basis), delivering an annual interest cost saving of c.£0.3 million.
  • Weighted average debt maturity extended from 2.9 years to 3.8 years; no debt maturing until June 2028.
  • Weighted Average Cost of Debt is 4.4%, and 98% fixed or hedged until June 2028.
  • Barclays acted as sole coordinator.
  • CFO Mike Perkins commented: "The strong support from our existing lenders and new partners in Lloyds and ABN AMRO reflects the ongoing appeal of grocery assets within the lending community. We continue to access bank finance at attractive rates, underlining the quality of our portfolio, the confidence in our strategy, and the strength of our relationships. The improvement in our debt maturity profile further enhances our capital structure which remains well diversified by maturity and source."
  • Context: portfolio valued at £2.1 billion as at 31 December 2025; FTSE 250 constituent listed on the LSE and JSE.

body:
• £13.2 million of new annual rent signed (GPE share: £12.5m) across 21 new leases and renewals, with market lettings on average 3.7% ahead of March 2026 ERV.
  • Fully Managed: 15 leases signed for £9.9m rent roll at an average £245 per sq ft, 2.6% ahead of ERV.
  • Fitted: 2 leases for £1.6m rent roll, 10.3% ahead of ERV.
  • Ready to Fit: 2 leases for £0.8m rent roll, 7.8% ahead of ERV.
  • A further £3.5 million of rent under offer, with market lettings 9.0% ahead of March 2026 ERV.
  • 30 Duke Street St James's SW1: pre-letting campaign completed with a new retail lease to Australian menswear brand M.J. Bale (2,636 sq ft, its first London store), fully leasing the building (prior CD&R office pre-let of 62,300 sq ft in May 2025 and L'Eto retail letting Oct 2025).
  • Strong viewing levels for remaining space at two other onsite developments — The Delft SE1 and the newly rebranded The Alfred WC1 (formerly Whittington House).
  • Kent House W1: two Ready to Fit renewals on 5- and 10-year terms at average £142 per sq ft, 7.8% ahead of ERV, a new benchmark for the building.
  • Hanover Square W1 (1 Medici Courtyard, 5th floor): simultaneous surrender and re-let to a global energy logistics business on a 10-year lease (5-year break), increasing passing rent by ~65%.
  • Fully Managed portfolio: 41,500 sq ft of new leases completed; 170 Piccadilly now 73% let, plus 11,200 sq ft at City Tower and 7,300 sq ft at 16 Dufour's Place; new customers include Sky and PXC.
  • CEO Toby Courtauld: "We have started the year well, with strong demand for our premium HQ and Fully Managed spaces translating into £13.2 million of leasing, 3.7% ahead of March 2026 ERVs."
  • Courtauld added: "With further leasing under offer, good progress across our development schemes and robust levels of demand for well-located investment properties, GPE remains well placed to deliver continued rental growth and further value creation."
  • AGM held at 11.30am on 2 July 2026 at The Royal College of Nursing, 20 Cavendish Square, London W1G 0RN.

body:
• Headline: "WIZZ AIR JUNE TRAFFIC UP 27% YEAR-ON-YEAR" — carried 7.48m passengers in June, +27.2% YoY.
  • Capacity up 27.5% YoY to 8.14m seats.
  • Load factor 91.9%, down 0.2ppts YoY (from 92.1%) — capacity growth marginally outpaced demand.
  • Rolling 12-month passengers 74.0m, +13.8% YoY; rolling capacity 81.6m seats, +14.4%; rolling load factor 90.7%, -0.5ppts.
  • CO2 emissions 588,656 MT in June, +15.7% YoY (well below capacity growth); rolling 12m 6.25m MT, +6.3%.
  • CO2 intensity 49.5g per pax/km, -3.3% YoY (rolling 12m 50.2g, -2.9%) — continued improvement in emissions efficiency; figures exclude SAF uplift benefit.
  • Operational milestone: flew 1,200 daily flights for the first time, maintaining strong completion and on-time performance.
  • Starlink connectivity deal announced — first European low-cost carrier to offer in-flight internet, rolling out across the fleet from January 2027.
  • Fleet stands at 270 Airbus A320/A321 aircraft; carried 69.7m passengers in FY2026.

body:
• Board has approved the proposed appointment of Ernst & Young LLP (EY) as external auditor, following a competitive audit tender launched in 2025 and led by the Audit and Risk Committee.
  • EY's appointment takes effect from the financial year ending 2 July 2028, subject to shareholder approval at the 2027 AGM.
  • Change is driven by UK statutory rotation rules: audit must be tendered every 10 years and auditor rotated at least every 20 years.
  • Incumbent Deloitte LLP (first appointed 2007, reappointed after tender in 2017) must be replaced for the FY ending 2 July 2028 audit as it approaches the 20-year rotation limit.
  • Deloitte continues as auditor for the financial years ending 28 June 2026 and 4 July 2027, the latter subject to shareholder approval at the 2026 AGM.
  • Board thanked Deloitte for its "significant contribution" and thanked all firms that participated in the tender.
  • Further detail on the tender process will be disclosed in the 2026 Annual Report and Accounts.

  • Board frames it as a "value-enhancing use of cash" while maintaining financial flexibility to invest in operations.
  • Executed via Panmure Liberum; commences immediately and runs no later than 1 May 2027, subject to market conditions.
  • Maximum shares purchasable is 77,712,214 (2025 AGM authority less shares already repurchased since 5 September 2025).
  • No guarantee the programme is implemented in full or that any shares are repurchased.

body:
• Agreed to sell 100% of its interest in Sports Direct Malaysia to strategic partner MAP Active (PT MAP Aktif Adiperkasa Tbk).
  • Frasers to receive c. US$150 million for the sale, subject to final completion adjustments.
  • Enters a long-term agreement under which MAP Active will further grow and develop Sports Direct in Malaysia, with a continuing income stream payable to Frasers.
  • Builds on existing MAP Active partnership across Indonesia, Philippines, Thailand, Vietnam and Cambodia — streamlining Southeast Asian operations.
  • Shared ambition to open over 350 stores long-term, reaching over 600 million consumers in the region.
  • CEO Michael Murray: "MAP Active is a valued strategic partner, and this transaction further deepens our relationship as we accelerate Sports Direct's growth across Southeast Asia. Together, we are creating a strong platform to deliver our ambitious growth plans. I look forward to continuing to work with the MAP Active team to unlock further value."
  • V.P. Sharma, Group CEO of PT Mitra Adiperkasa Tbk: "We are delighted to be strengthening our relationships with Frasers Group through this transaction. Leveraging on our local expertise and regional retail network, we look forward to offering more of Sports Direct's world-class offerings to our customers across the region - and unlocking new opportunities that create long-term value for both companies."

body:
• SEGRO confirmed heads of terms for a new 50:50 UK big-box logistics joint venture with a major international institutional investor (partner selected via competitive process started H2 2025).
  • JV will develop and operate three major UK logistics parks, expected to deliver ~925,000 sq m of space when fully-let, ~£135m headline rent and fully-developed gross asset value of ~£3 billion.
  • SEGRO intends to seed the JV with three prime parks — Radlett, Coventry and Northampton — currently 225,000 sq m of fully leased income-producing assets (£25m headline rent) plus 380 acres of developable land.
  • Parks to be sold into the JV at ~£1 billion, in line with latest reported book value plus assumed capex to completion.
  • Future development capex to complete the parks is expected to be ~£820 million, with delivery targeted through to 2030, funded via partner equity and non-recourse third-party debt at JV level.
  • SEGRO will earn fee income as manager to the JV (asset, property, development, financial and administrative services).
  • CEO David Sleath: "This latest strategic partnership allows us to bring together some of the UK's most attractive logistics parks within a capital-efficient structure, deepening our investment capacity and showcasing the strength of our asset management platform."
  • Definitive transaction documents expected in H2 2026, subject to final approvals, confirmatory due diligence and long-form documentation; no certainty a transaction will ultimately be agreed.
  • GAV/rent assumptions based on a seven-year projected exit date of 30 September 2033; Morgan Stanley, JLL and Slaughter and May advising SEGRO.

body:
• CFO Harry Stokes is stepping down to pursue other interests; he leaves the Board on 2 July 2026 and remains an employee until 17 July 2026 to support an orderly transition.
  • Patrick Symons, CA, appointed CFO and to the Board effective 3 July 2026 — an internal promotion; he joined CLS in 2017 and has been Group Financial Controller since 2023.
  • No further Listing Rule 6.4.8 disclosures required regarding Symons (i.e. no unusual matters to report).
  • Board reiterated its long-term strategy of owning/managing high-quality, well-located, multi-let office assets in Europe's three largest economies.
  • Near-term strategic priorities restated: reducing vacancy, strengthening the balance sheet via active refinancing and disposals, and investing in properties.
  • Chairman Lennart Sten: "Patrick has worked closely with our last two CFOs and, having been with us since 2017, knows the business extremely well. CLS remains focused on executing its strategic priorities, strengthening the balance sheet and delivering long-term value for shareholders."
  • Harry Stokes: "I have enjoyed working with the incredibly talented team at CLS, but I have regretfully decided that the CFO role is not aligned to my long-term aspirations and as such I have chosen to leave to pursue other interests."

body:
• Successfully refinanced existing debt with a new £800m unsecured club term loan and multi-currency RCF provided by eight banks (NatWest as Sole Coordinator, plus Lloyds, Barclays, HSBC, Santander, Deutsche Bank, ABN Amro, CaxiaBank), including three new counterparties.
  • Structure: £300m 3-yr term loan; £250m 3-yr RCF; £250m 5-yr RCF, each with two 1-yr extension options subject to lender approval.
  • Credit margin varies with LTV but expected to be on average 40bps cheaper than replaced facilities once leverage returns to the 40–50% target range.
  • £500m drawn initially, leaving £300m undrawn headroom (vs £571m at 31 Dec 2025).
  • Proceeds used to partially refinance the £1bn Assura acquisition bridging facility (£335m of the bridge repaid/cancelled) and to cancel £720m of legacy secured/unsecured facilities across Assura RCF, Barclays, RBS, HSBC, Lloyds and Santander lines (total £1,055m of commitments cancelled).
  • CFO Richard Howell: "The successful refinancing is an important step on PHP's journey to becoming a fully unsecured borrower".
  • Howell added: "The new facility enhances our capital structure, reduces our cost of capital and supports our wider funding strategy... We now look ahead to refinancing the balance through completing our deleveraging initiatives."
  • Advised by Rothschild & Co.

body:
• CFO Richard Hutton to retire after 28 years at Greggs and 20 years on the Board.
  • Ben Waldron appointed as successor, joining 27 October 2026 as CFO-designate and Executive Director, assuming CFO role from 1 January 2027.
  • Hutton remains CFO and Executive Director until 31 December 2026 to oversee an orderly handover.
  • Waldron's background: 14+ years at Bakkavor Group plc, where he served as CFO plus senior operational roles including Group Strategy Director, CEO of Bakkavor Asia and CEO of Bakkavor USA.
  • Post-Bakkavor, Waldron has been based in Australia across a portfolio of advisory roles; previously spent 12 years at Ernst & Young as an Audit, Advisory and Transaction Director.
  • Chair Matt Davies said Waldron's "strong financial and commercial expertise, together with his experience in senior operational leadership roles within an international food manufacturing business, will be an invaluable asset to Greggs."
  • CEO Roisin Currie described Hutton's contribution as "outstanding" and highlighted continuity as Greggs continues to "deliver our ambitious strategic plans."
  • Waldron stated he looks forward to supporting "the next stage of the Company's development."
  • Remuneration to be in line with the approved Directors' Remuneration Policy; details to follow in the Directors' Remuneration Report.
  • No additional disclosures required under UKLR 6.4.8R.

body:
• Total £20m shareholder return announced, flagged at 17 June 2026 FY results.
  • £10m share buyback programme approved; shares purchased will be cancelled to reduce share capital.
  • Special dividend of 1.75p per share, aggregating to ~£10m.
  • Buyback max authorised: 48,281,353 shares (2025 AGM authority net of prior programme completed 25 March 2026).
  • Programme split equally between Jefferies and Peel Hunt (£5m each); Jefferies executes the first £5m tranche.
  • Buyback runs until no later than 31 March 2027, including during closed periods.
  • Special dividend timetable: ex-date 9 July 2026, record date 10 July 2026, payment 31 July 2026; cash in GBP, no scrip/DRIP.
  • Board rationale: "strong financial position, cash generation and disciplined approach to capital allocation, while retaining the flexibility to invest in the business and pursue its strategic priorities."
