body:
• Appointed Uyi Akpata as Non-Executive Director with immediate effect, following prior flagging on 9 October 2025.
  • Intended to become Chair of the Audit and Risk Committee after publication of FY 2025 annual accounts and a handover period.
  • Akpata (aged 62) is a Chartered Accountant with a 40+ year career, retiring from PwC on 30 June 2024 where he was Senior Partner for Nigeria, Regional Senior Partner for West Africa, and Head of Oil and Gas for Africa.
  • Founded Rusa Advisory in October 2024 (governance and risk advisory).
  • Other current roles include Chairman of emPLE Life Insurance, Chairman of the Advisory Board of Unified Payments, and President of the Nigerian Cricket Federation.
  • Holds no shareholding in the Company.
  • Chair Joseph Pagop Noupoué: "His leadership roles at PwC, including as Senior Partner for Nigeria and Regional Senior Partner for West Africa, together with his deep knowledge of the African energy industry, make him an excellent addition to the Board."
  • Akpata: "Savannah has built a strong and diversified energy business with a clear strategic focus and significant growth opportunities."

time_period: Q3 FY2026 (16 weeks to 20 June 2026 for Primark; 12 weeks to 23 May 2026 for Food)
body:
• Group Q3 revenue £5,304m, +3% actual / flat constant currency; YTD £14,774m, +1% actual / -1% constant currency.
  • Full-year outlook unchanged except for Sugar; Group adjusted operating profit and adjusted EPS still expected to be below last year.
  • Sugar guidance downgraded: now expects an adjusted operating loss of £25m to £60m in FY2026, driven by higher gas costs from the Middle East conflict, potential onerous contracts on the 2026/27 beet crop, possible Malawian kwacha devaluation, and Tanzania new factory ramp-up risk.
  • FY2027 Sugar result expected to deteriorate further from the £60m upper-end loss; European sugar market likely to remain in surplus despite reduced production due to high 2025 inventory carryover.
  • Management flagged further cost base action to come, particularly in European Sugar.
  • Sugar Q3 revenue £451m, -4% constant currency; lower European ASPs, Tanzania rain-related delays, South Africa hit by import surge from delayed tariff adjustments.
  • Primark Q3 sales +3% (new stores contributed 5%); like-for-like -2.2%.
  • Primark FY2026 adjusted operating margin guidance maintained at ~10%.
  • Primark UK: total sales +1%, LFL broadly flat (-0.1%); gained market share; weak April/May due to Middle East conflict impact on sentiment and unseasonal weather, improved in June.
  • Primark Europe (ex UK/Ireland): total sales -1%, LFL -3.6%; weak consumer confidence; new integrated marketing campaign launched in Spain.
  • Primark US: sales +16%, 3 new stores (41 total), including first Manhattan store opened in May; trading described as "mixed".
  • Grocery Q3 revenue +1% constant currency; Twinings strong in US/UK/Australia on wellness teas; US oils weak due to reduced Hispanic consumer spending; CMA approved Hovis acquisition, working towards completion.
  • Ingredients Q3 revenue +3% constant currency; AB Mauri and ABFI both solid; guidance unchanged.
  • Agriculture Q3 revenue -14% constant currency on lower compound feed sales; disposed of one of nine UK compound feed mills; guidance unchanged.
  • Retail/Food demerger on track to complete before end of 2027 calendar year (as announced 21 April 2026).
  • Lucy Slinger to join as Primark CFO in September (announced 11 June).
  • George Weston: "The Group delivered a resilient trading performance in the third quarter."
  • Weston on Sugar: "the duration and severity of the Middle East conflict have increased gas price expectations for next year, which has impacted our European profit outlook."
period_detail: Q3 FY2026 (16 weeks to 20 June 2026 for Primark; 12 weeks to 23 May 2026 for Food)

body:
• Group revenue (inc. CTD) £75.6m, down 1.8% YoY, hit by weak market, CTD store closures and Topps store closures.
  • Group revenue (ex. CTD) £69.4m, up 0.6% YoY.
  • Topps Tiles Q3 like-for-like revenue flat YoY, with deterioration in H2 of the quarter vs H1.
  • Topps outperformed the wider market, which declined ~1.6% in the quarter, but performance was below internal forecast.
  • Margin pressure emerged since the interim update as macro uncertainty shifted demand toward lower-priced products.
  • Extreme heatwaves caused temporary work stoppages among housebuilders/traders; expected catch-up over six months but unlikely to fully recover within FY ending September.
  • Guidance: Adjusted PBT now expected to be above £6.5m (implying reset lower given ongoing headwinds).
  • Self-help initiatives on track: network optimisation, new lower-cost flexible labour model, head office role consolidation.
  • Online revenue (inc. CTD) rose to 23.3% of sales, +2.3ppts vs H1 and +3.7ppts vs Q3 FY25.
  • Topps Tiles App launched with almost 11,000 downloads in first month; overall trade growth -0.8% YoY.
  • New "hard surface" category extensions (acoustic panels, outdoor tiles, shower panels) grew 10.9% YoY.
  • CTD now trades from 23 stores (down from 31), reflecting property decisions and CMA-mandated disposal of four stores.
  • Board reiterates confidence in medium-term Mission 365 priorities.
  • CEO Alex Jensen: *"Topps continues to outperform the wider market despite weaker consumer sentiment and an increased focus on lower priced products. We're making significant strategic progress across our priorities and the self-help actions we are taking to support profitability are working and will position the business for long-term sustainable growth. In the short term, the macro-economic environment continues to remain challenging."*
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q3 FY26'

body:
• Completed acquisition of Evelyn Partners for £2.7bn enterprise value on 30 June 2026, creating the UK's leading Private Banking & Wealth Management business.
  • Evelyn had £69bn AUMA at end-2025; combined with NatWest's £59bn = £127bn total AUMA and £188bn Customer Assets & Liabilities (~20% of Group CAL).
  • Fee income boosted by c.20% pre-revenue synergies; expected accretive to growth and RoTE in year one of ownership.
  • Estimated annual run-rate cost synergies of c.£100m, with costs to achieve of c.£150m.
  • CET1 impact: c.130bps reduction (based on expected 31 Dec 2026 position, proforma for RWAs at 1 Jan 2027), comprising c.£2.7bn CET1 capital deduction (goodwill/intangibles), c.£1bn operational RWAs at completion, and c.£40m transaction costs in H1 2026 other operating expenses.
  • Evelyn Partners will be consolidated from 30 June 2026; impact on FY 2026 guidance to be detailed at Interim Results on 31 July 2026.
  • Chris Kenny formally took up role as CEO of Evelyn Partners on completion, reporting to Emma Crystal (CEO of PBWM at NatWest).
  • CEO Paul Thwaite: "This is a unique opportunity for the Group, delivering unmatched scale and capabilities in a market with considerable growth potential."
  • Thwaite framed timing around rising retail engagement: acquisition comes "at a time when the benefits of saving and investing are increasingly part of the national conversation."
  • Combined group will serve over 20 million customers across banking, savings and wealth, including Evelyn's Bestinvest platform.
  • No immediate changes to customer/client service; integration to proceed in phases.

body:

  • EBITDA guidance set at £250m.

  • Upgrade driven by "exponential and exceptional growth in our B2B business," with momentum continuing to build and scale since FY2026 results.
  • B2B platform scale is driving higher profit margins as income grows against fixed costs.
  • Management flags "several important milestones expected over the next 12 months" and a continuous pipeline of new B2B opportunities.

body:
• Signed and completed assignment of the Atlanta fulfilment centre lease (to a global consumer brand) and disposal of associated automation assets (to a DHL Group member).
  • Net proceeds c.£48m (c.$63m); marks completion of the non-core asset sale programme.
  • Annual cash cost savings of c.£6m (c.$8m) from rent/occupancy at current FX.
  • One-off profit before tax of c.£78m (c.$103m) after adjustments to associated property liabilities, to be recognised as an adjusting item in FY26 results.
  • Site was already non-operational and fully written down in prior periods.
  • Balance sheet impact: cash position was £209.5m as at 1 March 2026; pro forma net debt (ex-leases) c.£180m vs c.£295m at 1 March 2026, reflecting the £67m Lichfield and £48m Atlanta proceeds.
  • Sits alongside prior deleveraging actions: November 2025 refinancing, April 2026 repayment of 2026 convertible bonds, and May 2026 Lichfield disposal.
  • Net proceeds retained for financial flexibility rather than earmarked to specific uses.
  • CEO Jose Antonio Ramos: "The disposal of Atlanta is another clear demonstration of us delivering on our commitments - strengthening the balance sheet, simplifying the business and maintaining strict discipline in how we allocate capital."
  • On US strategy: "Since closing the Atlanta fulfilment centre, we have successfully transitioned to our new US operating model, giving customers access to a broader product assortment from our global inventory. The strength of customer engagement gives us confidence in this approach and reinforces the progress we are making as we execute our strategy and reposition ASOS for long-term, sustainable growth."

  • Horseferry House: sold to overseas investor for £131.8m (£129.3m net of rental top ups), at a 6.0% net yield to purchaser, delivering an ungeared IRR of 8.4% over 21-year ownership.

  • Management "focused on redeploying receipts accretively and in line with our capital allocation framework."
  • Context: portfolio valued at £5.1bn as at 31 December 2025; YTD disposals of £279m equate to ~70% of the £400m 2026 target.

  • 15% acquired from the Government of Kenya for KES 204 billion (€1.36 billion) cash.
  • 5% acquired from Vodafone itself for KES 68 billion (€0.45 billion) cash — via sale of Vodafone International Holdings B.V.'s remaining 12.5% stake in Vodafone Kenya Ltd.

body:
• Board unanimously and unequivocally rejected Prologis's possible offer, stating it "falls a long way short of SEGRO's own views on value."
  • Proposal currently worth 881p per SEGRO share, reflecting a 5% decline since the offer period began (down from 925p on 23 June, driven by Prologis share price falling from $145.3 to $138.9).
  • SEGRO to publish a detailed growth strategy and value update in the week commencing 6 July, covering development pipeline and data centre strategy.
  • Key rejection arguments:
  • Inadequate: fails to reflect logistics development pipeline, data centre pipeline, portfolio scarcity, operating platform quality, or any change-of-control premium.
  • Opportunistic: timed to share price dislocation from the Middle East conflict; pre-conflict (27 Feb 2026) the proposal implied only a 6% headline premium (890p vs 841p share price).
  • One-sided: SEGRO holders would swap 100% exposure to SEGRO for only a c.10.5% stake in the combined group (based on 0.084 exchange ratio).
  • Board emphasises "Capital is not a constraint" to delivering the standalone growth strategy — balance sheet and platform sufficient without Prologis.
  • Chairman Andy Harrison: "Prologis is trying to acquire SEGRO on the cheap when our share price has been dislocated by the Middle East conflict and at a price that reflects none of the quality, scarcity and growth embedded in the business."
  • Harrison added: "We have unanimously rejected their Proposal because we continue to believe our compelling standalone investment case can deliver superior shareholder value."
  • SEGRO urges shareholders to take no action.
  • PUSU deadline: 5.00pm on 22 July 2026 — Prologis must either announce a firm Rule 2.7 offer or walk away (subject to Panel extension).
  • Rule 2.9: 1,353,927,858 ordinary shares in issue; no treasury shares.
  • Advisers: Evercore, Morgan Stanley (also joint corporate broker), UBS (also joint corporate broker); Slaughter and May as legal adviser; FTI Consulting for PR.
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets reference an offer approach: 'possible offer'

body:
• Launched new Sallie Mae® Parent Loan, a private loan for parents, guardians, or significant others to finance undergraduate/graduate education — positioned as an alternative as federal loan programs evolve (notably Parent PLUS).
  • Explicitly pitched to offer rates that can be lower than federal Parent PLUS loans, with no origination fees (vs. PLUS which charges an origination fee and accrues interest on it).
  • Covers up to 100% of school-certified cost of attendance; fixed and variable rate options; interest-only or P&I repayment choices; 0.25pp rate reduction for autopay.
  • Soft-pull prequalification available (no credit score impact); 100% U.S.-based support.
  • Extends existing suite alongside undergraduate, graduate, and career training loans (nursing, health, medical, aviation, law).
  • Patrick Freeman, SVP: "Families have different priorities when deciding how to pay for college; while many parents choose to cosign a student loan, others prefer to borrow in their own name to support their student's higher education goals."
  • Strategic read-through: product launch targets the Parent PLUS addressable market at a time of federal student lending reform, potentially expanding SLM's private origination TAM beyond its traditional cosigned undergraduate channel.

body:
• Announcement sets the Maximum Acceptance Amount at £500 million aggregate nominal across the multi-tranche tender offer launched earlier on 30 June 2026.
  • Tender covers five series: US$850m Fixed Rate Reset Subordinated Notes due 2047 (Tier 2), GBP350m 5.875% Notes due Dec 2031, GBP200m 5.875% Notes due Apr 2033, GBP40m Fixed Rate Notes due Apr 2033, and GBP10m Fixed Rate Notes due Apr 2041.
  • Funded by pricing (earlier same day) of a new sterling-denominated fixed rate reset perpetual Restricted Tier 1 contingent convertible note issuance — classic RT1-for-Tier 2/senior liability management exercise.
  • Offers remain subject to the New Financing Condition; Offeror retains discretion to accept significantly more, less, or none of the £500m indicative cap.
  • Allocation between series is at the Offeror's sole discretion, with pro rata scaling on oversubscribed series — except GBP40m 2033 Notes and 2041 Notes, which will be accepted in full with no scaling if accepted at all.
  • New RT1 notes to be issued in minimum denominations of £200,000; not registered under US Securities Act, no US distribution.
  • Dealer Managers: Santander, Barclays, BNP Paribas, Citi, HSBC, J.P. Morgan, BofA (Merrill Lynch); Tender Agent: Kroll Issuer Services.
  • Read-through: L&G is refinancing legacy sub debt/senior guaranteed paper with fresh RT1 capital, optimising Solvency II own-funds composition rather than raising net new leverage.

body:
• Launching a new US$1 billion share repurchase programme, as previously flagged on 20 May 2026.
  • Programme starts after completion of the prior buyback announced 30 January 2026 and must complete by no later than 30 June 2027.
  • Maximum shares to be purchased: 63,288,150, under authority granted at the 2025 AGM.
  • Stated purpose: to reduce share capital (i.e. capital return, not treasury reissuance).
  • Executed under existing general authority and in compliance with UK Listing Rules.

body:
• Approved for dual listing on Nasdaq Texas, LLC, a new Dallas-headquartered venue for companies with strong Texas ties.
  • Primary listing remains on Nasdaq Global Select Market; dual listing under the same "LGIH" ticker.
  • No impact on investors' ability to buy or sell the stock.
  • CEO Eric Lipar: "Dual listing on Nasdaq Texas reflects our deep commitment to this state, its communities, and the first-time homebuyers we serve every day."
  • Symbolic/branding move rather than a capital-raising or structural change — reinforces Texas identity (founded, HQ'd in The Woodlands, TX).

body:
• FY26/27 guidance reiterated: Total underlying operating profit £975m–£1,075m and Retail free cash flow >£500m, with management flagging continued uncertainty from the Middle East conflict.
  • Total Retail sales (exc. fuel) +2.7% YoY to £9,153m, a deceleration from Q4 25/26 (+3.8%) and Q1 25/26 (+4.8%).
  • Like-for-like sales (exc. fuel) +2.1%, slowing sequentially from +3.1% in Q4 and +4.6% a year ago — fifth consecutive quarter of LFL deceleration.
  • Like-for-like sales (inc. fuel) +3.9%, boosted by fuel +17.5% (vs -10.1% in Q4) reflecting prior-year base effects.
  • Sainsbury's banner sales +3.1% to £8,041m; Grocery +3.6% to £7,603m (vs +4.5% in Q4).
  • GM (Sainsbury's) & Clothing -3.7% to £438m — reversal from +1.0% in Q4; underlying GM -6.3%, Tu Clothing -2.1%.
  • Argos sales -0.5% to £1,114m, though volumes +2.2% — weakness driven by lower ASP / mix shift to lower-ticket items.
  • Fresh food sales +5%, Taste the Difference +6%, Groceries Online +12.5%.
  • Record customer satisfaction scores on Supermarket availability and Groceries Online order completeness (highest since programme began March 2022).
  • Cost savings programme on track: £1bn over three years to March 2027.
  • Aldi Price Match maintained as largest in market for over a year; ~11,000 products on Nectar Prices; customers saving avg >£16 on an £80+ shop.
  • Nectar360 Pollen retail media platform onboarding underway; early campaigns showing >2.5x incremental sales from multi-channel and up to 10x higher conversion on targeted activity.
  • Facial recognition tech now live in 55+ stores with >90% of identified offenders not returning; rollout to up to 150 further stores before Christmas.
  • Argos transformation: Supplier Direct Fulfilled range expanded with 5,000 new SKUs added in Q1 (26,000 total); Marketplace launch on track for later this FY; app now ~30% of digital sales.
  • GM space being deliberately reduced in favour of food to improve sales/profit densities.
  • Roberts headline: "Customers are looking for value now more than ever... an encouraging start to the year with continued volume growth and market outperformance."
  • Roberts on health push: "we're going further to make healthy everyday essentials more accessible and affordable, starting with fibre, fruit and veg" — Full on Fibre labelling on 500+ products.
  • Outperformed market at Mother's Day, Eid al Fitr, Easter and May heatwave; record sales in berries/burgers (heatwave) and best-ever lamb sales over Easter.
  • Interim results scheduled for 22 October 2026.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Routine annual Report on Payments to Governments disclosure under UK Regulations 2014; mining activities only (excludes Transport Division).
  • Total payments of US$695.4m in 2025, with over 99% paid in Chile (US$695.2m) and just US$200k in the US (Twin Metals fees to Minnesota DNR).
  • Breakdown: Taxes on income US$647.1m, Royalties US$30.3m (ad valorem 1% on copper sales), Fees US$18.0m; no production entitlements, dividends, bonuses or infrastructure payments.
  • Payments by project in Chile: Los Pelambres US$531.2m (the dominant contributor, and the only mine subject to the new royalty regime from 1 Jan 2024), Centinela US$107.0m, Antucoya US$7.8m, Antofagasta Minerals S.A. US$25.2m, Inversiones Los Pelambres US$10.6m, exploration US$12.3m.
  • Centinela and Antucoya remain under the previous royalty system (5%–14% of taxable operating profit) via tax stability agreements, while Los Pelambres is under the new regime (8%–26% margin-based royalty plus 1% ad valorem).
  • Chilean total effective tax rate reiterated at 35% (27% first category + 8% withholding on distribution).
  • CEO Iván Arriagada: "All of our operating mines are located in Chile and this is where over 99% of these taxes and other payments to governments were paid during the year."
  • Mining represented over 95% of Group revenue and profit before tax in 2025.
  • No new operational, guidance or financial performance information; disclosure is compliance-driven.

body:
• Group CFO Emilie McCarthy stepping down, leaving MAB on 30 September 2026 to pursue other opportunities after ~2 years in role.
  • Jo Stent appointed Group CFO Designate, joining 6 July 2026; to become Group CFO from 1 October 2026 subject to transition and regulatory approval.
  • Stent to be appointed to the Board as executive Director upon becoming CFO.
  • Stent background: former CFO of Frontier IP Group PLC (AIM, joined 2024) and previously CFO of Argentex Group PLC; earlier senior finance roles at European Tour, Vodafone, TELUS; trained at EY and Deloitte; Chartered Accountant with 25+ years' experience.
  • CEO Peter Brodnicki on McCarthy: "Emilie has played an incredibly important role in the MAB group's development, including supporting the Company's transition from AIM to the Main Market of the London Stock Exchange."
  • CEO on Stent: "Jo's combination of financial leadership, strategic insight and public company experience makes her an excellent addition to the executive team."
  • No trading or guidance update included in the announcement.

body:
• Tender offer launched at $3.15/share in cash by ANV Group Holdings (jointly owned by AmTrust and Blackstone Credit & Insurance) via Lakers Acquisition Sub, pursuant to Merger Agreement dated 15 June 2026.
  • Open Lending Board unanimously recommends shareholders tender their shares.
  • Offer expires one minute after 11:59pm NYC time on 27 July 2026; Outside Date of 15 October 2026 (auto-extended to 15 December 2026 if HSR is sole outstanding condition).
  • Deal structured as Section 251(h) DGCL merger — no shareholder vote required post-tender.
  • Premium: ~78% to 90-day VWAP as of 15 June 2026; ~132% premium vs 13 February 2026 unaffected price; ~48% vs 11 June 2026 close of $2.13.
  • Supporting Stockholders (CEO Jessica Buss, Bregal Sagemount, Nebula Holdings) holding ~12.8% of shares have signed Tender and Support Agreements.
  • Termination fee: $13.58m (~3.5% of equity value) payable by Open Lending in specified scenarios; reduced from ANV's initial 4.5% proposal.
  • Financing: $100m equity commitment + $250m debt commitment; no financing condition.
  • Background highlights:
  • AmTrust (key insurance partner) made unsolicited approaches in 2025 at $2.00 (April), raised to $3.00 (May), both rejected; AmTrust paused in July 2025 and extended insurance partnership through 2033 in August 2025.
  • ANV (newly formed AmTrust/Blackstone MGA platform) approached December 2025; verbal offer escalated from $3.25 → $3.50 then cut to $3.00 in Feb 2026 citing "concerns regarding the Company's recent certified loan volume trends," before settling at $3.15.
  • Targeted market check of 8 parties (5 sponsors, 3 strategics) produced no competing offers.
  • Process paused late Feb–May 2026 while ANV restructured; ANV reaffirmed $3.15 on 26 May 2026.
  • Updated June 2026 Management Forecasts (provided to ANV) — Revenue: 2026E $118.6m, 2027E $185.0m, growing to $274.4m by 2030E; Adjusted EBITDA: 2026E $43.8m, scaling to $168.0m by 2030E.
  • June 2026 Base Case (used by FT Partners for fairness opinion): Revenue 2026E $103.6m → 2030E $149.1m; Adj EBITDA 2026E $29.7m → 2030E $60.6m; Unlevered FCF 2026E $8m rising to $46m by 2030E.
  • Board noted Q1 2026 results led to decreased certified loan volume guidance but increased Adj EBITDA from improved profit share economics.
  • FT Partners DCF implied $2.68–$3.91/share; selected public companies $1.35–$2.80; precedent transactions $1.74–$2.42.
  • FT Partners fee: ~$8.7m total ($1m on opinion, remainder contingent on close).
  • CEO Jessica Buss golden parachute: $8.24m total (cash $3.58m, equity $4.62m, benefits $37,630).
  • Largest shareholder payouts: Bregal Sagemount ~$23.83m, Nebula Holdings ~$23.77m for share consideration.
  • Regulatory: HSR filings expected by 8 July 2026; Texas Department of Insurance non-disapproval required under Section 4001.253.
  • Merger Agreement requires Surviving Corp to maintain employee comp/benefits for 12-month continuation period post-close; six-year D&O tail insurance.

body:
• Statutory disclosure under UK Report on Payments to Governments Regulations 2014; no trading, earnings or guidance content.
  • Total 2025 payments to governments: $82.77m, split Taxes $67.18m / Royalties $12.54m / Fees $3.05m.
  • Peru: $61.27m (74% of total) — almost entirely income tax of $60.50m to SUNAT via Compañía Minera Ares (Inmaculada); fees of $0.76m including small amounts at legacy Selene and Pallancata projects.
  • Argentina (San Jose mine, Minera Santa Cruz): $18.23m — Taxes $6.68m, Royalties $10.17m (to Santa Cruz province), Fees $1.38m.
  • Brazil (Mara Rosa, Amarillo Mineracao): $2.36m, entirely royalties to ANM; no income tax paid (consistent with ramp-up phase).
  • Chile: $0.91m in fees only (Andina Minerals, Volcan project) — no taxes or royalties, reflecting non-producing status.
  • Materiality threshold: payments below £86,000 per government excluded; figures unaudited; no in-kind payments.

body:
• Appointment of Erminia (Ernie) Johannson as independent non-executive director, effective 1 July 2026.
  • Brings 30+ years of financial services experience across investment management and banking, with strengths in payments, digital and data analytics.
  • Most recently Group Head of North American Personal and Business Banking at Bank of Montreal (BMO) and a member of BMO's Executive Committee, overseeing ~10 million customers across Canada and the US.
  • 12-year tenure at BMO including prior role as Chief Risk Officer for Canadian P&C and North American P&B Banking; previously held senior roles at Fidelity Investments and CIBC.
  • Former Chair of the Canadian Bankers Association; prior board roles at U.S. Consumer Bankers Association, Payments Canada and Moneris Solutions Corporation.
  • Named one of American Banker's Most Powerful Women in Banking for six consecutive years from 2019.
  • Chair Rick Haythornthwaite: "I am delighted to welcome Ernie to the NWG Board. Ernie brings over three decades of experience in financial services across investment management and banking. Her deep strengths in banking including payments, digital and data analytics, together with her leadership experience in financial services will be a valuable asset to the Board."
  • No further matters requiring disclosure under Listing Rule 6.4.8R.

body:
• Operating under highly constrained conditions due to war in Ukraine; still running only one of four pellet lines, exporting to European and Middle East customers.
  • Sold owned transhipping vessel "Iron Destiny" for net proceeds of US$7.7 million.
  • Cash runway extended beyond previously stated end of August 2026 — based on current production, forecast energy prices over next quarter, and optimised sales mix (net accessible cash less funds blocked at Mbaer bank).
  • Runway estimate caveated by iron ore price volatility, energy costs, no restrictive measures from the insolvency manager at Ferrexpo Poltava Mining, and no final adverse legal/administrative outcomes.
  • Continues to advance workstreams for an equity fundraise of at least US$100 million ("Intended Fundraise"); reiterates equity raise is the most viable solution in the timeframe required.
  • Warns: "If the withholding of VAT refunds and funding issues are not resolved in sufficient time, this could give rise to material negative consequences for the Group."
  • No certainty fundraise will conclude successfully; full terms to follow in a further announcement.
  • Q2 2026 production update scheduled for 15 July 2026.

body:
• Guidance restated to exclude International division following today's announced 50:50 JV with Verizon combining their international operations into a new multinational connectivity company.
  • International (incl. five non-core FY26 divestments) will be reported as discontinued operations until JV completion.
  • Adjusted Group revenue FY27: £17.1–£17.6bn (vs prior £19.0–£19.5bn incl. International) — c.£1.9bn reduction reflecting International carve-out.
  • Adjusted UK service revenue FY27: £15.1–£15.4bn (unchanged).
  • Adjusted EBITDA FY27: £8.1–£8.2bn ex-International (vs prior £8.2–£8.3bn incl. International) — growth maintained.
  • Capex ex-spectrum: £4.2–£4.3bn (prior c.£4.3bn); mid-term guide unchanged at >£1.0bn reduction from FY26 level.
  • Normalised free cash flow FY27: c.£2.0bn (unchanged); mid-term target c.£3.0bn by end of decade reaffirmed.
  • Dividend guidance unchanged: low-to-mid single-digit growth until metrics consistent with BBB+ rating reached, after which residual cash flow available for enhanced distributions.
  • Mid-term guidance across all metrics unchanged.
  • Reference consensus for BT International (published 19 June 2026): FY27 Adjusted Revenue £1,821m; FY27 Adjusted EBITDA £108m — implying the carve-out removes a relatively low-margin revenue stream (EBITDA margin c.6%).

body:
• BT and Verizon to combine international enterprise operations into a 50:50 JV, focused on multinational connectivity.
  • JV will serve >3,000 customers across >180 countries, with ~$4bn combined annual revenue.
  • Verizon to pay BT a $625m equalisation payment; equal voting rights.
  • JV incorporated in Jersey, headquartered and tax resident in the UK.
  • Combines BT International with Verizon's international enterprise wireline arm; positioned as a cloud-first, AI-ready, sovereign-capable platform.
  • Martijn Blanken appointed CEO-designate (ex-Telstra, Openwave, EXA Infrastructure, KPN); joins BT from 1 September.
  • Clive Selley continues as CEO of BT International through to completion; Verizon leadership unchanged.
  • Completion expected in 2027, subject to regulatory clearances and employee consultations.
  • Strategic rationale: allows parents to refocus on domestic markets (UK for BT, US for Verizon) while supporting JV as equal shareholders.
  • Allison Kirkby (BT CEO): "Today's announcement marks a major milestone for BT International, and an important step forward for BT as a whole, as we deliver on our UK-focused strategy."
  • Dan Schulman (Verizon CEO): JV is "a cutting-edge, AI-ready and secure platform run by a single global organization"; Verizon retains direct US customer relationships.
  • Advisors: Goldman Sachs (lead financial), Deloitte (transaction services), Freshfields (legal) for BT; Morgan Stanley and Kirkland & Ellis for Verizon.

body:
• Agreed to acquire Kayne Anderson Real Estate (KARE) for upfront enterprise value of ~$1,393m: $759m cash + ~189m newly issued Bridgepoint shares (~$634m equity), plus performance-based earn-out.
  • Vendor is Kayne Anderson Capital Advisors; KARE is a Boca Raton-based real estate platform with $22bn AUM across equity and debt strategies.
  • Combined Enlarged Group AUM ~$117bn across 5 verticals: PE $40bn, credit $21bn, infrastructure $30bn, real estate $22bn, secondaries $4bn.
  • Valuation: high single-digit multiple of 2027E EBITDA, falling to mid-single-digit by 2028; KARE 2027E EBITDA guided $130–190m.
  • EPS accretion: mid-single-digit % in 2027, >20% in 2028; FRE accretive from 2027.
  • Cash funded via existing balance sheet and credit facilities (incl. £400m bridge facility entered 5 June 2026 with Lloyds).
  • Expected to close end of 2026; subject to shareholder approval, regulatory approvals, fund consents. Shareholders representing ~36% have given irrevocable undertakings to vote in favour.
  • Earn-out: up to 102.5m additional Bridgepoint shares in 2030, tied to management fee revenue hurdles ($100m minimum / $150m target Earn-Out Revenue), measured at 31 Dec 2029.
  • KARE management (CEO Al Rabil, CIO David Selznick) to continue running business under new "Kayne Bridgepoint" brand; equity subject to staggered lock-ups to 2029.
  • Acquired perimeter: 100% of KARE FRE, 15% of carry in historic funds (KAREP VI/VII, KAROD II, KARED IV), 100% of KCRED economics, up to 35% of carry in future funds.
  • Pro forma mix shift: US-domiciled management fees rise from 28% → 42%; real assets rise from ~⅓ → ~45% of AUM; FRE/EBITDA rises from ~50% → ~60%; largest-fund concentration drops from ~20% → ~15%.
  • Adds >115 new institutional LP relationships with limited overlap (only 5 of Bridgepoint's top 50 LPs overlap with KARE).
  • KARE track record: ~20% AUM CAGR 2019–May 2026; KAREP VII closed oversubscribed at $5.12bn on 15 May 2026, nearly 2x predecessor.
  • KARE 2025 financials: $141m net management fees, $63.6m gross profit; gross assets $598.2m (predates KAREP VII final close).
  • KARE standalone medium-term guidance: $15bn fundraising expected over next 3 years at >1% blended fee rate; management fee growth 20–30% p.a.; FRE margin 60–70%; EBITDA margin ~65–70% in 2027, >70% medium-term; PRE 5–10% of income in 2027, 20–30% medium-term.
  • Incremental interest expense: ~£30m in 2027, ~£25m p.a. from 2028.
  • Updated Bridgepoint standalone guidance: 2027 EBITDA £390–460m; 2024–2026 fundraising guidance raised to €28bn.
  • Pro forma management fees rise from £435m to £540m for 2025; illustrative Enlarged Group EBITDA CAGR >30%, EBITDA margin trending above 60%.
  • Current trading: H1 2026 FRE broadly in line with consensus (potential upside if ECP VI next close lands in Q2); PRE guidance at top of 20–25% range, phasing now ~⅔ in H1; H1 2026 EBITDA expected above consensus.
  • Constitutes a "significant transaction" under UK Listing Rules; circular and General Meeting to follow.
  • Raoul Hughes (CEO): "This marks another major step forward in our strategy to strengthen our position as a leading global middle-market private markets platform... The Transaction is highly complementary and immediately accretive."
  • Al Rabil (KARE CEO): "We are in the beginning of a super cycle for the alternative real estate sectors on which we focus, and joining together with Bridgepoint provides additional global resources to capitalise on this opportunity."
  • Advisers: Moelis (lead), Goldman Sachs (capital markets), Simpson Thacher (legal); BNP Paribas, JPM, Morgan Stanley joint brokers. KARE advised by Evercore and Kirkland & Ellis.

time_period: Ore Reserve Estimate as at 31 March 2026
body:
• Group Ore Reserve grew 62% to 5.0Moz Au (157Mt at 0.99g/t Au & 0.12% Cu) plus 196kt Cu, post 15 months of mining depletion vs December 2024 ORE.
  • Telfer Reserve +150% to 1.8Moz Au (119Mt at 0.46g/t Au & 0.06% Cu) plus 68kt Cu, after depletion — driven by just the first ~half of the FY26 240,000m drill program.
  • West Dome Open Pit Reserve +375% to 1.4Moz Au (90.6Mt at 0.46g/t Au); higher-grade component 70.8Mt at 0.53g/t for 1.2Moz.
  • Main Dome Underground maiden Reserve of 0.2Moz (3.6Mt at 1.33g/t Au & 0.31% Cu), confirming multi-year underground mine-life extension.
  • Stockpiles contribute 22.5Mt at 0.36g/t Au for 0.3Moz Au + 11kt Cu, providing processing flexibility.
  • Havieron Reserve unchanged at 3.3Moz Au & 128kt Cu (38.5Mt at 2.63g/t Au & 0.33% Cu).
  • West Dome Underground (WDU) and Vertical Stockwork Corridor (VSC) deliberately excluded from this ORE — both remain in Resource pending ongoing studies, flagged as the key future growth lever.
  • Telfer residual resources outside ORE: WDO 3.5Moz (244Mt at 0.45g/t), Underground 2.6Moz (53.9Mt at 1.53g/t Au & 0.37% Cu) across MDU/WDU/VSC.
  • Upside flagged: potential to convert 42% more gold from Inferred treated as waste in open pit shell, and 8% more in underground shapes; Inferred→Indicated conversion running at >85%.
  • Telfer March 2026 MRE update: ~15Mt converted from Inferred to Indicated at WDO; total WDO MRE now 336Mt at 0.45g/t Au for 4.9Moz Au & 148kt Cu.
  • Economic assumptions: Telfer medium-term prices A$4,000/oz Au and A$6.00/lb Cu; Havieron long-term A$2,500/oz Au and A$4.60/lb Cu — both well below spot (~A$5,930/oz Au, ~A$8.70/lb Cu), implying material reserve price leverage.
  • Financial close achieved on Facility B of the $500m corporate debt facility — a $225m undrawn 7-year RCF for working capital, general corporate purposes and Havieron development.
  • MD Shaun Day: "Telfer and Havieron's combined Reserve of 5.0Moz gold and 196kt copper is an outstanding achievement and an important step towards a multi-decade mine life for the world class Telfer-Havieron gold-copper complex."
  • Strategic direction (Day): "Our strategy is to increase the contribution of high-grade underground ore as a proportion of our processing feed over time."
  • Day on WDO upside: "If converted into Reserve this material could meaningfully lower our strip ratios."
  • Drilling cadence to be maintained into FY27; WDU and VSC studies ongoing as the primary path to next reserve uplift.
period_detail: Ore Reserve Estimate as at 31 March 2026

company: Aston Martin Lagonda Global Holdings PLC
body:
• Customary closing conditions satisfied for the previously announced £50m committed facility with the Yew Tree Consortium (Yew Tree Overseas Limited and Saint Alexander S.à r.l).
  • Facility was originally announced alongside Q1 2026 results on 29 April 2026.
  • Classified as a notifiable related party transaction under UKLR 8.2.1R, given Lawrence Stroll (lead investor/substantial shareholder) and Michael de Picciotto are directors and related parties.

body:
• Dean Banks to join as Group CEO and Executive Director with effect from 21 September 2026, following the 4 March 2026 succession announcement.
  • David Thomas will step down from the Board on Dean's appointment after 11 years as Group CEO and 17 years with the Group.
  • Thomas remains with the Group until March 2027 to ensure a smooth and orderly transition.
  • No further disclosures required under UK Listing Rule 6.4.8R in respect of Dean Banks.

body:
• President Americas role split into two: President North America and President South America, to allow greater regional focus.
  • Jessica Farrell appointed President North America effective 1 July 2026 (currently VP Innovation, formerly WA Nickel Asset President); will also act as interim President South America while recruitment is completed.
  • Edgar Basto moves from Chief Operating Officer to newly created Chief Enterprise Performance Officer (effective 1 September 2026), retaining accountability for Health, Safety & Security and the BHP Operating System, with added remit for contractor safety and operating discipline.
  • Geraldine Slattery continues as President Australia and takes on Copper South Australia, consolidating all Australian operating assets under her leadership (effective 1 September 2026).
  • Changes accompany incoming CEO Brandon Craig's start date of 1 July 2026 (previously announced 18 March 2026).
  • Craig quote: "These new appointments ensure that we continue to build organisational capacity, with the right mix of skills, experience and perspectives to deliver BHP's strategy and pursue our growth agenda."
  • Craig on backdrop: "Our operating environment is increasingly complex, but also rich in opportunity for companies that are best able to positively engage stakeholders, deploy capital to the right opportunities in a disciplined way and deliver safe, reliable operational performance."
  • Full ELT from 1 September 2026: Basto (Enterprise Performance), Cox (Legal/Governance/External Affairs), Farrell (North America + interim South America), Pant (CFO), Raw (Development), Slattery (Australia), Udd (Commercial), van Jaarsveld (Technical), Vodopija (People).

body:
• Took delivery of Acadia, a Jones Act-compliant subsea rock installation vessel, from Hanwha Philly Shipyard in Philadelphia.
  • First U.S.-flagged, Jones Act-compliant subsea rock installation vessel of its kind, marking GLDD's strategic expansion into offshore energy.
  • Vessel will mobilize first to Equinor's Empire Wind 1 project offshore New York.
  • Will then proceed directly to Ørsted's Sunrise Wind project, also offshore New York.
  • After the two U.S. projects, Acadia will mobilize to Europe for a major offshore wind developer, keeping the vessel utilized for the majority of 2027.
  • Designed to transport and precisely place rock materials on the seabed to protect subsea cables and turbine foundations.
  • CEO Lasse Petterson: "Taking delivery of Acadia represents a transformative moment for Great Lakes and underscores our dedication to installing and protecting domestic and international offshore energy infrastructure."
  • CEO added the vessel "positions us at the forefront of subsea rock installation in the U.S. and international markets and enables us to play a critical role in major projects such as Empire Wind 1 and Sunrise Wind."

  • Barclays US LLC has published its own assessment of results, available via the Barclays investor relations website (Fed filings section).

body:
• Revenue +6.5% to £373.0m; Moonpig brand +8.6%, Greetz +1.5% constant currency, Experiences -4.5%.
  • Gross margin down 1.2ppts to 58.4% due to delivery proposition investment (tracked next-day card delivery), New Markets mix, and higher UK NIC/wage costs.
  • Adjusted EBITDA +8.1% to £104.6m; margin +0.4ppts to 28.0%, ahead of 25-27% target range.
  • Reported PBT £68.9m (vs £3.0m, prior year hit by £56.7m Experiences goodwill impairment); Adjusted PBT +13.4% to £76.5m.
  • Adjusted EPS +19.5% to 18.0p (materially ahead of 11.5% Adjusted PAT growth), reflecting 6.7% reduction in weighted average shares from buybacks.
  • Free Cash Flow +11.2% to £73.5m; cash conversion 70% (FY25: 68%).
  • Dividend +25% to 3.75p (final 2.5p, payable 19 November 2026); cover 4.8x.
  • £60.2m of buybacks completed in FY26; intention to repurchase up to £65m in FY27 (split £32.5m H1/H2); first £32.5m programme launched 7 May 2026, £8.5m already executed at avg 215.1p.
  • Net debt £108.1m; net leverage 1.03x (target ~1.0x); £180m RCF maturing Feb 2029, SONIA caps at 4.0-4.5% on £75m notional through Oct 2027.
  • Active customers +2.8% to 12.3m; orders +2.1%; AOV +5.7% to £9.32 driven by trading-up (Next, Boots ranges), card upsell, tracked delivery uptake.
  • Tracked next-day delivery now 41% of UK card-only orders (FY25: 17%).
  • Moonpig Plus/Greetz Plus memberships +29.3% to 1.2m; ~25% of Moonpig orders from Plus members.
  • New Markets revenue +33% to £15.7m (Ireland £6.4m, Australia £6.3m, US £3.0m); Australia prioritised for incremental investment.
  • Experiences: H1 revenue -8.9%, H2 -1.9%; goodwill headroom rebuilt to £18.4m (FY25: £1.6m) — no impairment vs £56.7m charge prior year; pre-perpetuity revenue CAGR assumption 1.4% (FY25: 2.7%).
  • Outlook FY27: trading in line with expectations; expectations unchanged.
  • FY27 guidance: Adjusted EBITDA margin to ease towards 25-27% target range (delivery investment + higher SBP from CEO transition); D&A £18-20m; acquisition amortisation ~£6.5m; ETR 25-26%; capex in lower half of 4-5% revenue range; net leverage ~1.0x; net finance costs to rise.
  • Gross margin headwinds to continue in FY27 from delivery investment and Ireland/Australia mix.
  • CEO Catherine Faiers (joined March 2026): "Since joining the business in March, my conviction in the opportunities ahead has only grown."
  • Strategic framework reaffirmed: mid-to-high single digit revenue growth, 25-27% EBITDA margin, double-digit Adjusted EPS growth, continued capital returns.
  • M&A: "significant M&A is not currently under consideration."
  • Post period: internal transfer of Experience More Limited into Moonpig.com Limited completed 1 May 2026 (structural simplification, no consolidated impact).

body:
• Awarded a £325 million, two-year contract by Scottish and Southern Electricity Networks (SSEN) to deliver the Netherton Hub in Aberdeenshire.
  • Secured via SSEN's Accelerated Strategic Transmission Investment Offshore Framework; builds on Balfour's 2025 appointment for enabling works at the same site.
  • Scope: earthworks and civil infrastructure including five platforms supporting two substations and three converter stations, plus an operations base and associated facilities.
  • Strategic role: hub will reinforce the UK national grid and enable transmission of renewable power from northern Scotland to demand centres.
  • Workforce peak of over 800, with >5% apprentices/graduates (group-wide figure currently 8.9%).
  • CEO Philip Hoare: "Securing this contract reinforces Balfour Beatty's position at the forefront of the UK's energy infrastructure market."
  • Hoare added: "The Netherton Hub will play a pivotal role in enabling the delivery of secure, homegrown, low-carbon power at scale. We are proud to be working with SSEN to deliver infrastructure of such national importance - drawing on our proven expertise in complex, large-scale projects."

body:
• Revenue: c.£2.5bn, +c.3% YoY, with organic growth of c.1% and c.2% net M&A contribution.
  • Underlying operating profit: c.£155m, ~+6% YoY, with margin progression above 6%.
  • Full-year guidance reiterated: revenue c.£5bn (organic growth ~3%), underlying operating profit c.£300m (~+10% vs £272m in 2025), free cash flow c.£160m, adjusted net debt c.£165m.
  • FY margin guided to c.6.0%, ~40bps higher than 2025, at the upper end of 5-6% medium-term target; H2 margin to moderate vs H1 on mix.
  • UK & Europe: strong organic growth from Defence and Justice contract mobilisations, improved Justice contract performance, and higher-than-expected immigration-related services revenue.
  • North America: procurement delays from late 2025 have continued into H1; MT&S contribution offsetting, pipeline expanded further.
  • Middle East: revenue partially affected by regional conflict causing activity reductions and contract rescoping; Mubadala partnership progressing with new FM/asset contracts (UAE University, M42, Tawazun).
  • Asia Pacific: revenue declined on immigration contract exit, but >£500m order intake including 5-year extensions for Acacia Prison and Adelaide Remand Centre, plus 1-year ADF health services extension.
  • Pipeline increased to £12.5bn; >£2bn of contract awards and extensions in H1.
  • Adjusted net debt c.£250m at end-June, leverage <1.0x.
  • £75m buyback to conclude by end-July; Board will review capital position at the half year.
  • RCF refinanced: increased from £350m to £400m, maturity extended to June 2031.
  • North America CEO Michael LaRouche departing for a CEO role at a US-listed international business; succession process underway.
  • Sector disclosure simplified from H1 2026: Transport and Health folded into Citizen Services; Defence, Justice & Immigration retained.
  • FY26 net finance costs guided to ~£52m; underlying effective tax rate ~25%; weighted average shares 980m basic / 1,000m diluted.
  • CEO Anthony Kirby: "The Group has delivered a good first half, with revenue and margin increases including strong organic growth in UK & Europe and progress in our Asia Pacific business."
  • Kirby on geopolitics: "our global footprint and sector diversity remains a core strength for Serco, with over half our profits coming from our international business, ensuring financial and operational resilience."

body:
• Exchanged contracts to dispose of two non-core sub-scale multi-use business parks in the Sheffield area for combined consideration of £5.3 million, at a 3% premium to book value.
  • Both Sheffield sites are stable and well occupied but offer limited scope to drive further income or valuation growth; sold to a single repeat purchaser.
  • Simultaneously exchanged contracts to acquire three digitally automated UK self storage development sites in Leicestershire, Bedfordshire, and Merton (Greater London) — all subject to planning.
  • Total site acquisition cost ~£12.6 million, funded by the £5.3m Sheffield recycling plus a further £7.3m from additional non-core UK disposals expected this calendar year.
  • Leicestershire and Bedfordshire sites to open spring 2027; Merton site to complete in 2028.
  • Each self storage project forecast to generate double-digit IRRs in excess of cost of capital.
  • Builds on existing self storage framework across Germany/UK and the recently announced Berlin Gartenfeld development.
  • Portfolio context (as of 31 March 2026): 145 assets, 11,736 tenants, book value ~€3.0bn, annualised rent roll €258.6m, plus 35% stake in Titanium JV with BNP Paribas AM Alts.
  • CEO Andrew Coombs: "The disposals, agreed at a premium to book value, demonstrate Sirius's continued focus on disciplined capital recycling, crystallising value from smaller, mature assets where we see limited scope for further income or valuation growth and redeploying the proceeds into opportunities with stronger returns potential."
  • Coombs added the three acquisitions represent "an exciting further step in our self storage strategy, as we expand on what is already an extensive, high-yielding and resilient part of our platform."

body:
• Received a fourth indicative and conditional proposal from Castlelake, L.P. on 23 June 2026 at £6.50 per share in cash for the shares not already held by Castlelake.
  • Proposal includes a partial alternative for shareholders to elect for unlisted, non-transferrable, non-voting shares in a vehicle within Castlelake's structure.
  • Bidding vehicle ownership: 49% Castlelake and co-investors including Brookfield Asset Management; 51% EU nationals including Peter Bellew and Mark Breen (structure designed to meet EU airline ownership rules).
  • Castlelake indicated it could further improve value if granted access to limited commercial information.
  • Board unanimously rejected the Fourth Proposal, citing substantial undervaluation of the Company and its prospects, and continuing significant questions of deliverability.
  • However, Board willing to grant limited commercial diligence access in the hope of eliciting a more attractive proposal.
  • Board flagged concerns over ownership structure, deliverability, and the time required to satisfy conditions and its impact on present value of any offer; seeking "satisfactory assurances and commitments" from Castlelake.
  • PUSU deadline extended by 9 days (with Panel consent) to 5:00 pm Sunday, 5 July 2026 for Castlelake to either announce a firm offer under Rule 2.7 or walk away.
  • Board reiterated easyJet is in a position of strength, with an investment grade balance sheet and net cash position, and remains focused on its medium-term target of >£1 billion profit before tax.
  • Announcement made without Castlelake's consent.
  • Shareholders advised to take no action at this time.

body:
• Shares +16% on the day to ~209p, reflecting underlying PBT ahead of consensus and FY27 upgrade.
  • Group Revenue (52-wk basis) £1,764.5m, +2.9% YoY (reported 53-wk £1,801.7m).
  • LfL Revenue +4.8% (Retail +4.1% LfL; Autocentres ex-Avayler +5.8% LfL).
  • Retail Motoring LfL +2.9%; Cycling LfL +6.4% (Tredz +9.0% LfL); e-bikes flagged as growth driver for FY27 with expanded Carrera range.
  • Autocentres consumer garages LfL ~+8%, with SMR offsetting weak tyres; tyres showing late-year stabilisation.
  • Gross margin 52.8%, +210bps YoY — highest in a decade, driven by Better Buying, pricing/promo effectiveness, FX tailwind on hedged COGS, and 30bps from supplier contract restructuring (offset in opex).
  • Net operating costs +7.6% YoY on labour inflation (NI/NLW), managed services pricing, plus investments in Fusion, brand and digital.
  • Underlying PBT £45.4m (52-wk), +4.1% YoY; on like-for-like accounting treatment, +8.1% to £41.5m vs prior £38.4m.
  • Reported PBT £43.6m vs £(30.0)m loss prior year (FY25 included £49.1m goodwill impairment).
  • Retail underlying operating profit £37.5m, -3.8% YoY (gross margin +160bps to 50.9% offset by cost inflation/investment).
  • Autocentres ex-Avayler underlying operating profit £22.3m, +21.8% YoY; segment margin +50bps to 3.1%. Avayler operating loss widened to £3.4m (FY25: £2.6m) on revenue £2.1m.
  • ROCE +160bps to 14.2%, above 10.6% cost of capital.
  • Free cash flow £25.3m (52-wk) / £33.3m (53-wk); FY25 was £43.0m — decline partly due to FY25 bonus payments paid in early FY26.
  • Net cash (ex-IFRS 16) £11.2m at 27 March (£19.1m at 3 April) vs £10.1m prior. Total net debt (incl. leases) £228.7m vs £261.3m.
  • Capex £56.1m, within £55–65m guided range; Autocentres capex £36.4m (Fusion £12.6m).
  • Total FY26 dividend 9.0p (+0.2p); final 6.0p (FY25: 5.8p), payable 15 September 2026.
  • £180m RCF undrawn; one-year extension to 16 April 2029 exercised in August 2025.
  • Fusion garage rollout at 103 sites, contribution doubles at maturity; ~35 more sites in FY27, then approach to be applied more broadly at lower cost per garage. Premium Motoring Club at 420,000 members generating ~£22m annual subscription revenue.
  • Accounting policy change: amortisation of acquired intangibles (£3.9m FY26; £5.2m FY25) reclassified as non-underlying, applied retrospectively.
  • FY27 guidance: underlying PBT expected at top end of new consensus range (£45.7m–£52.3m, c.£49.0m midpoint), weighted to H1. Old consensus was £45.3m (£42.0m–£48.6m) before the accounting change.
  • Trading in April, May and June "has continued to be strong"; no observed Middle East impact yet but flagged as H2 2026 risk to consumer sentiment.
  • Board: Chair Keith Williams to step down at 10 September 2026 AGM; Jock Lennox (ex-EY partner; Barratt Redrow, Johnson Service Group) joins 1 September 2026 as Chair-designate.
  • Next update: HY27 trading update in October, interim results in November.
  • CEO Henry Birch: "I am very pleased with the progress we are making in the 'Optimise' phase of our strategy, resulting in the strong results we are announcing today. With good sales growth, higher margins and an increased dividend, we are delivering improved shareholder returns alongside a more compelling customer proposition."

body:
• Jock Lennox appointed as Non-Executive Director and Chair Designate, joining the Board on 1 September 2026 and taking up the Chair role after the AGM on 10 September 2026.
  • Outgoing Chair Keith Williams steps down at the AGM on 10 September 2026, as previously communicated, in line with good governance practice.
  • Lennox currently serves as Non-Executive Chair of Johnson Service Group PLC and Chair of Clarion Housing Group (UK's largest not-for-profit housing association).
  • Previous roles include Senior Independent Director and Audit Committee Chair of Barratt Redrow PLC, Chair of Enquest PLC and Hill & Smith Holdings PLC, plus board positions at Dixons Carphone, Oxford Instruments and A&J Mucklow.
  • Spent 30 years at EY in UK and global leadership positions; brings track record in strategic transformation, capital markets, corporate governance and stakeholder engagement.
  • No further disclosures required under UKLR 6.4.6R and UKLR 6.4.8R.

body:
• Rule 2.4 announcement from Castlelake, L.P. regarding a possible offer for easyJet — explicitly not a Rule 2.7 firm intention to bid.
  • Castlelake notes easyJet's constructive engagement and welcomes a nine-day extension to the PUSU deadline, now 5.00pm London time on Sunday 5 July 2026 (per Rule 2.6(c)).
  • No financial terms, structure or indicative price disclosed; "A further announcement will be made as and when appropriate."
  • Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC (Mark Sorrell, Owain Evans) acting as financial adviser; Slaughter and May and Milbank LLP as legal advisers to Castlelake.
  • Bidder background: Castlelake is a global alternative investment manager founded 2005 with ~$38bn AUM, strategically partnered with Brookfield (>$1tn AUM); positioned as a scaled aviation investor with >$24bn invested in aviation since 2005 and >200 airline relationships — signalling financial firepower and sector credibility behind the approach.
  • Market reaction: EZJ shares quoted +7.67% at 581p intraday, market cap ~£4.35bn, implying the market is pricing in rising probability of a firm bid following the PUSU extension.

body:
• Board unanimously and unequivocally rejected an unsolicited possible offer from Prologis, received on 16 June 2026, for the entire issued and to be issued share capital.
  • Proposal terms: 0.084 new Prologis shares per SEGRO share, implying 925 pence per SEGRO share based on Prologis' $145.3 close and 1.32 GBP:USD on 23 June 2026.
  • Implied offer represents a modest premium to SEGRO's prior close (~871p), which the Board views as falling "a long way short of SEGRO's own views on value."
  • Board characterised the approach as "opportunistically timed", exploiting the dislocation between SEGRO's share price and underlying business value.
  • Cites major geopolitical issues adversely impacting UK/European real estate trading valuations relative to US REITs as accentuating the dislocation — a key strategic rationale for Prologis (US REIT) bidding for a UK peer.
  • Board reiterated confidence in standalone strategy, citing strong balance sheet, building occupational market momentum, large development pipeline, and "exceptional data centre platform."
  • PUSU deadline: 5.00pm London time on 22 July 2026 — Prologis must either announce a firm Rule 2.7 offer or walk away by then (extendable only with Panel consent).
  • Announcement made without Prologis' consent; no certainty of firm offer.
  • Advisers: Evercore, Morgan Stanley, UBS (financial); Slaughter and May (legal); FTI Consulting (PR).
  • Shares in issue: 1,353,927,858 ordinary shares; no treasury shares — implies indicative deal value of ~£12.5bn at 925p.

body:
• Announced a c.$48 million partial repayment of outstanding 10.25% Senior Secured Notes due 2028.
  • Principal amount repaid: $48,211,589.95 at a redemption price of 101% of principal.
  • Remaining outstanding principal post-repayment: $1,162,034,475.
  • Repayment executed just two months after closing the April 2026 refinancing (Closing Date 24 April 2026; Indenture amended/restated 10 April 2026).
  • Interest paid on redemption date includes uncapitalised Mandatory PIK Interest, Mandatory Cash Interest on Redeemed Notes, and uncapitalised PIYC Interest on outstanding principal prior to redemption.
  • Redemption structured as a pro rata pass-through distribution of principal per the Indenture; no Notes of $1,000 or less redeemed in part.
  • Issuer: Tullow Holdco 2 Limited; Paying Agent: GLAS Trust Company LLC.
  • CFO Richard Miller: "This partial repayment of the Notes has been enabled by our strong operational and financial performance in 2026. Being able to take this meaningful step only two months after closing the refinancing transaction is reflective of our disciplined approach to capital allocation and the quality of our assets. We continue to focus on deleveraging and exploring longer term refinancing and strategic options, underpinned by our enhanced value proposition."

body:
• Two new Non-Executive Director appointments announced to the Board.
  • Gretchen Watkins joins from 1 July 2026; appointed to the Nominations, Culture & Governance Committee and the Safety, Energy Transition & Tech Committee.
  • Watkins was President of Shell USA Inc. and EVP of Global Shales (May 2018–July 2025); previously CEO of Maersk Oil; currently NED at The Mosaic Company.
  • Alessandra Genco joins from 1 September 2026; appointed to the Nominations, Culture & Governance Committee and the Audit Committee.
  • Genco was Group CFO of Leonardo SpA (October 2017–November 2025) — directly relevant aerospace & defence finance experience; currently Supervisory Board Member and Audit Committee Chair at BASF SE, and NED at Italmobiliare SpA.
  • Chair Dame Anita Frew on Watkins: "Gretchen's experience of transformation, safety and sustainability in complex organisations across multiple countries will be of great value to our Board discussions."
  • Chair on Genco: "Alessandra's financial expertise and deep understanding of the aerospace and defence industry will be invaluable to the Board."
  • Genco commented: "This is a pivotal time of strategic decision-making for the industry and the Company, and I am eager to contribute to the future success of Rolls-Royce."
  • Succession context: NEDs Beverly Goulet and Nick Luff to retire at the 2027 AGM after completing nine years on the Board (as previously disclosed).

company: Schroder European Real Estate Investment Trust Ltd
body:
• Board proposes managed wind-down and return of capital to shareholders, subject to investor approval; circular and general meeting (expected August 2026) to follow, requiring 50% of voting shareholders in favour.
  • Board concluded that continuation, corporate sale or thematic pivot would not meaningfully close the discount given structural shift to larger, more liquid UK equities.
  • Wind-down expected to take c.2–3 years to allow asset management initiatives to maximise realisation values.
  • Board states portfolio can be realised in the direct property market "at a value in excess of what is currently implied by the prevailing share price".
  • Underlying EPRA earnings (pre-exceptionals) €3.6m vs €3.9m prior year (-7.7%).
  • IFRS profit of €1.1m (vs €(0.1)m loss in H1 FY25).
  • NAV €151.3m / 115.1 cps, down from adjusted €154.2m / 117.3 cps at 30 Sept 2025 (-1.9% per share); driven by unrealised revaluation losses.
  • Prior period error of €2.5m identified (service charge misclassification 2021–2024), restating opening NAV down by 1.9 cps.
  • NAV total return +0.7% (H1 FY25: +0.3%).
  • Dividends declared 2.96 cps for the half, 93% covered by EPRA earnings; yield c.8.6% on 59.6p share price (19 June 2026).
  • Quarterly dividend maintained at 1.48 cps; dividends to continue during wind-down to preserve investment trust status but will decline as capital is returned.
  • Direct portfolio valuation €192.6m, down €2.2m or -1.1% net of capex (vs €194.0m at Sept 2025).
  • Rumilly +19% (+€2.0m) and Stuttgart +6% (+€1.1m) from lease re-gears; offset by Alkmaar -35% (-€3.8m) on tenant insolvency and Cannes -15% (-€1.1m) on Stellantis departure notice; Apeldoorn -8% (-€1.0m) on shortening KPN lease.
  • Four leases/re-gears signed generating €1.9m annual rent at WAULT 8.4 years; combined Rumilly + Stuttgart re-gears delivered 18% uplift on passing rent and extended portfolio WAULT by c.1 year.
  • Occupancy 93%; portfolio WAULT 4.1 years; rent collection 97%.
  • Net LTV 27% (29% gross); cash €5.7m available (total cash €27.3m including €14.1m restricted).
  • Total third-party debt €64.3m across 5 facilities; blended all-in rate 3.8%; average maturity just 1.8 years.
  • Post period-end: Berlin facility (Deutsche Pfandbriefbank) extended 13 months at 1.8% margin; refinancing terms substantially agreed.
  • French tax dispute unresolved: €14.9m notice of adjustment (tax years 2021–2023) appealed; no provision recognised; €12.1m ring-fenced via bank guarantee with Credit Agricole (Nov 2025); appeal implicitly dismissed in March 2026, escalating to court process expected to take up to 2 years.
  • Material uncertainty over going concern flagged by auditor (EY) — tied solely to outcome of wind-down vote, not solvency/liquidity.
  • Key tenant risks flagged: KPN (20% of rent, Apeldoorn) departing Dec 2026 — replacement tenant deemed "remote"; alternative use/sale being progressed. Stellantis (Cannes) leaving Sept 2026.
  • Investment Manager fee structure to be revised under wind-down to better align with timely returns; details in forthcoming circular.
  • GRESB score +4 points to 86; four-star status retained.
  • Chairman Phil Redding: "it does not expect these strategies to significantly close the discount or support long-term growth" — on rejected alternatives to wind-down.
  • Fund Manager Jeff O'Dwyer: orderly realisation over 2–3 years provides flexibility to "implement targeted asset management initiatives that enhance value, reduce execution risk and improve liquidity".

body:
• Ian Brown appointed as new CEO, effective 28 September 2026, joining the Board as an Executive Director.
  • Brown will join Trainline on 7 September 2026 to support an orderly transition with outgoing CEO Jody Ford.
  • Brown was most recently CEO of Flutter Entertainment's UK & Ireland division, where he "delivered sustained double-digit growth and strengthened its market position."
  • Prior roles include CEO of ANS Group and CEO of the Trips Business Unit at Booking.com — bringing online travel marketplace experience directly relevant to Trainline.
  • Chair Brian McBride: "Ian brings a strong track record in scaling digital platforms and marketplace businesses, with significant experience in online travel."
  • Brown commented Trainline is "a business with real purpose, scale and significant opportunities ahead" and pointed to delivering "the next phase of growth."
  • Remuneration to follow the Directors' Remuneration Policy approved at the 2026 AGM, with full details in the FY27 (year ending 28 February 2027) Remuneration Report.
  • No disclosures required under UK Listing Rule 6.4.8R (i.e., no other directorships/issues to flag).

body:
• Rule 2.4 announcement: Prologis disclosed an indicative all-share proposal to acquire SEGRO, sent to SEGRO's Board on 16 June 2026.
  • SEGRO Board unequivocally rejected the proposal on 23 June 2026; Prologis is going public to pressure engagement ("Prologis urges SEGRO shareholders to encourage the SEGRO Board to engage").
  • Exchange ratio: 0.084 new Prologis shares per SEGRO share, implying 925p per SEGRO share and valuing SEGRO at ~£12.6bn.
  • Implied premia vs 23 June close: 24.6% to spot (742p), 26.7% to 1-month VWAP, 31.4% to 3-month VWAP.
  • Offer pitched at SEGRO's 31 Dec 2025 EPRA NTA of 925p (i.e. zero premium to NTA) — likely a key sticking point for the SEGRO Board.
  • SEGRO holders would own ~10.5% of the enlarged Prologis (market cap ~$140.9bn).
  • PUSU deadline: 5:00pm London on 22 July 2026 to either firm up under Rule 2.7 or walk away.
  • Prologis reserves right to lower terms with Board recommendation, on a competing bid below this value, or following a SEGRO Rule 9 waiver/reverse takeover; and to adjust for any SEGRO dividends.
  • Strategic rationale highlighted by Prologis:
  • SEGRO has traded at an average discount to EPRA NTA of 19% (2yr) and 17% (3yr), framed as a structural constraint.
  • Balance sheet gap: Prologis Net Debt/EV 22% vs SEGRO 37%; Net Debt/Adjusted EBITDA 4.8x vs 8.4x.
  • Claims superior capital access can accelerate monetisation of SEGRO's development, power and data centre pipeline.
  • TSR comparison: Prologis 3yr 37% / 5yr 39% vs SEGRO 3yr 19% / 5yr -20%.
  • Anticipates "significant synergies" (unquantified) from integration.
  • Advisers to Prologis: Rothschild & Co, J.P. Morgan, Eastdil Secured; Brunswick PR; Linklaters legal.
  • No financing details, cash alternative, or synergy quantification disclosed — pure stock deal as currently structured.
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'to acquire'

company: Schroder European Real Estate Investment Trust Ltd
body:
• Board intends to present formal proposals to shareholders for a managed wind-down of the Company and return of capital, after reviewing options to address the persistent NAV discount.
  • Drivers cited: equity market disadvantage for sub-£100m market cap vehicles, institutional preference for larger vehicles with better liquidity/diversification/cost efficiencies, and macro uncertainty.
  • Alternatives considered and rejected: share buybacks and transition to thematic/sector-specific investments — not expected to materially close the discount.
  • Board view: portfolio can be realised in the direct property market at a value in excess of what is currently implied by the prevailing share price.
  • Portfolio comprises 14 assets across France, Germany and the Netherlands.
  • Wind-down expected to take approximately two to three years, allowing for targeted asset management and management of the French tax litigation.
  • Realisations via single or multi-asset disposals; proceeds to repay borrowings and return capital to shareholders.
  • Investment Manager (Schroders) in discussions on revised management terms during wind-down to align with maximising shareholder returns; details in forthcoming Circular.
  • Circular to be published in due course; general meeting to seek ordinary resolution modifying investment objective and policy.
  • Dividends to continue to maintain investment trust status, but level will decline as portfolio income reduces and capital is returned.
  • Over £80 million of dividend payments since IPO (December 2015) cited as track record.
  • Fund Manager Jeff O'Dwyer: "the ongoing discount to NAV has hindered our ability to scale and achieve our growth potential, particularly against the deteriorating market backdrop."
  • Chairman Phil Redding: "more recent interest rate and inflationary concerns" have exacerbated the persistent material discount to NAV.
  • Redding added: "a managed wind-down strategy and return of capital is in the best interests of the Company's shareholders."

body:
• H1 revenue growth c.+6.5% (+7.6% ex THG Nutrition Asia), vs H1 2025: -2.5% — a clear inflection back to growth.
  • H1 Adjusted EBITDA of at least £40m, c.+95% YoY adjusting for the Claremont Ingredients disposal (Sept 2025).
  • LTM Adjusted EBITDA c.£94m to May 2026, +36% YoY (LTM May 2025: £68.9m), driven by cost discipline, gross margin maintenance and targeted category expansion.
  • H1 2026 free cash flow expected to be the strongest since 2021.
  • Full year revenue, Adjusted EBITDA and cash guidance reiterated, in line with company consensus (dated 18/06/2026).
  • Term Loan B trading above par in H1 2026 for the first time since issuance in March 2025 — a record high, signalling lender confidence.
  • THG Beauty: skincare +9.2% YTD; Lookfantastic outpacing UK prestige beauty market; new brand launches include Dyson, BEAME, Dr. Loretta.
  • Lookfantastic ranked #1 multi-brand beauty retailer on TikTok Shop, with Q2 YoY revenue growth of c.+48%.
  • THG Nutrition: ex-Asia H1 revenue growth c.+11%; Asia pivot to local licensing/manufacturing on track.
  • Myprotein YTD unit growth +60%; c.18% of D2C customers purchased activewear in May 2026.
  • Licensed product sell-in on track to exceed 60m units in FY 2026 (FY 2025: 43m), led by ready-to-drink in UK retail.
  • Myprotein to enter energy, protein water, and breakfast categories in H2 2026.
  • Myprotein outpacing sports nutrition market by +50% in B2B; 84% share of clear whey sales; bars/snacks beating market by >160bps.
  • VAT update: c.£78m retrospective claims to HMRC for protein powders/supplements (Sun Warrior ruling); Upper Tribunal dismissed HMRC's appeal right; substantive HMRC response expected late Spring 2026 but not yet received.
  • CEO Matthew Moulding: "We are on track with our growth and margin expansion strategy across the Group. By prioritising home markets and trending categories in THG Beauty, we continue to drive high-quality growth across an expanding customer base."
  • CEO on Nutrition: "Myprotein is reaching more consumers than ever. Year-to-date unit growth of +60% has been underpinned by our rapid retail expansion and category diversification, with c.18% of D2C customers purchasing activewear in May 2026."
  • CEO flagged macro headwind: "The Group continues to deliver strong year-on-year Adjusted EBITDA growth, notwithstanding the broader macroeconomic backdrop, including unprecedented whey commodity inflation levels."

body:
• FY27 results now expected to be ahead of current market expectations, vs prior consensus FY27 adjusted PBT of £24.5m (range £23.5m–£25.1m, 3 analysts at 18 June 2026).
  • Like-for-like volume growth across all channels: new retail, Motability, used vehicle, and fleet & commercial.
  • Aftersales contributing to year-on-year Group profit growth; Group margins stable; overall trading ahead of prior year.
  • Operating costs remain tightly controlled.
  • First Omoda and Jaecoo dealership to launch 1 July 2026 at an existing Group site in Burton; a second Group outlet to be re-franchised to Omoda & Jaecoo on 1 October 2026.
  • Will have 15 sales outlets representing Chinese automotive brands after these openings.
  • Sheffield Mazda to be relocated from a stand-alone site into a multi-franchise operation alongside Nissan, delivering cost benefits.
  • Network stands at 191 sales and aftersales outlets.
  • CEO Robert Forrester: "The Group has made an encouraging start to FY27 with full year results now expected to be ahead of market expectations, supported by growth across all key revenue channels and continued momentum in our high margin aftersales operations. While we remain mindful of wider consumer pressures and the ongoing impact of the Zero Emission Mandate, we are confident in the resilience of the business and our ability to capture growth opportunities, including through the launch of our first Omoda and Jaecoo dealership in Burton."

body:
• Completed definitive agreement with Codelco to implement a joint mine plan for the adjacent Los Bronces (50.1%-owned AAS) and Andina copper mines in Chile, following receipt of competition/regulatory approvals and fulfilment of conditions precedent.
  • Follows the landmark agreement originally signed in September 2025.
  • Joint mine plan expected to unlock 2.7 million tonnes of additional copper over 21 years, averaging 120,000 tpa of additional low-cost copper production, shared equally.
  • At least $5 billion pre-tax in shared additional value with minimal capital investment.
  • Implementation remains conditional on environmental permits, with final implementation currently expected by 2030.
  • Both parties retain flexibility to develop standalone projects (including respective underground resources) in a coordinated manner during the joint plan's term.
  • CEO Duncan Wanblad: "The next important milestone for Los Bronces - Andina is the timely receipt of the permits which will allow us to begin delivering the additional volume and value that we are targeting".
  • Wanblad framed the deal as supporting "Chile's ambition to lift national copper production to 6 million tonnes per year by 2030."
  • Codelco Chairman Bernardo Fontaine emphasised "maximizing returns to the State, while ensuring operations remain profitable without increasing debt".
  • Sustainability principles agreed to safeguard social programmes and existing environmental commitments.

body:
• Pre-tax profit £451.4m, down 14.7% vs £528.9m, described as in line with guidance.
  • Revenue £2,383.3m, -4.2% YoY (residential £2,265.2m vs £2,432.2m; commercial sharply up to £110.4m from £14.8m).
  • 4,076 homes completed (+127 in JVs) vs 4,047 (+282) prior year; ASP fell to £546k from £593k on mix.
  • Gross margin 25.1% (vs 26.6%); operating margin 18.7% (vs 20.1%) — within new 17.5–19.5% target range.
  • Operating costs cut to £150.4m (-6.2%); cumulative 25% real-terms reduction since pre-downturn.
  • Basic EPS 331.6p, -10.8%; pre-tax ROE 12.5% (14.9%); ROCE 13.8% (16.5%); Core ROCE 14.9% (16.9%).
  • Net cash rose to £363m (from £337m) despite £233m buybacks, £253m land creditor payments and £69m BTR capex.
  • NAVPS +9.0% to £39.17; buybacks executed at avg £37.10 (below NAV).
  • Cash due on forward sales fell sharply to £1,006m from £1,403m (-£397m / -28%); sales reservations value ~15% below prior two-year run-rate.
  • Land holdings future gross margin £6.44bn (vs £6.72bn); pipeline ~11,000 plots (vs 12,000); land creditors down to £486m from £714m (and from £900m two years ago).
  • Berkeley 2035 rephased (announced 1 April 2026): targeting £1.4bn pre-tax profit cumulatively over FY27–FY30, and 15% Core ROCE "as soon as possible" (11–15% interim).
  • No dividend paid (FY25: £2.40/share including special); full shareholder return shifted to buybacks — £233m completed (6.3m shares).
  • Next shareholder return target £640m by 30 Sept 2030, of which £112m already done; £528m remaining; further £23.2m bought back post year-end through 19 June 2026.
  • Bank facility refinanced post year-end: increased to £1.4bn (from £1.2bn) per headline, with new 5-yr term to June 2031 (+2x 1-yr extensions to 2033); detail in notes shows £1.0bn bank facility (£240m drawn Term Loan + £760m undrawn RCF) plus £400m Green Bonds — total liquidity £1.8bn.
  • Berkeley has stopped acquiring new land (except via JVs) citing inability to make required returns given tax/regulatory burden; focus on optimising existing 50,000+ home holdings.
  • BTR ("Berkeley Living"): six buildings / 1,122 homes in platform; first three sites in lease-up with "leasing velocity and rents at target levels"; ~£400m total cost when complete in FY28; committed to 4,000 BTR homes by FY35 but phasing of second tranche under review.
  • New planning consents at Borough Triangle (890), Hemel Hempstead (485), Brighton Gasworks (480); expanded Green Quarter Ealing masterplan to 8,000 homes; Woodberry Down to 6,500 homes; >£250m value added to land holdings via re-planning.
  • Setback: Planning Inspectorate blocked Peckham scheme (~850 homes) after 10 years of engagement; Beckton and Motspur Park Gasworks blocked locally (called in by GLA).
  • Building safety: 99% of 825 relevant buildings have third-party assessments; 39 buildings still require works.
  • Effective tax rate 29.5% (vs 27.8%) reflecting 25% CT + 4% RPDT.
  • Rob Perrins (Executive Chair) headline framing: "Resilient operating performance in a volatile environment" with focus on cash generation enabling "enhanced share buy-backs" while share price is below NAV.
  • Trading commentary: first 4 months stable, weakness around pre-Budget tax uncertainty, brief New Year optimism, then Middle East conflict reintroduced caution — market "characterised by caution and lacking in urgency"; good transactions from needs-based/liquid buyers, off-plan weak.
  • Cost outlook: build costs stable in FY26 but management flags inflationary pressure expected H2 2026 into 2027 driven by energy costs and supply chain strain.
  • Policy asks: SDLT capped at 3% on new homes (zero for FTBs), removal of surcharges, full implementation of "Homes for London" package, reduction in apartment delivery timeline from 8 years back to 5; claims measures would lift national GDP by 1%.
  • London delivering <10% of MHCLG annual new homes target; new starts ~10% of target.

  • Confirms it is in advanced discussions with an investor regarding contributing the private hospital portfolio to seed a new joint venture.
  • Consistent with previously flagged strategic review exploring options including JV arrangements with "highly credible investors" to enhance long-term value of the private hospital portfolio.
  • Caveats: "Discussions remain ongoing, will be subject to all necessary approvals and there can be no certainty that any transaction will be agreed, nor as to the terms on which any transaction might be concluded."
  • PHP "continues to evaluate all options" — JV is not the only path being considered.
  • Context: private hospital exposure was inherited via the 2025 Assura combination, which created the UK's largest listed healthcare REIT; a JV would allow PHP to recycle capital out of non-core private hospital assets while retaining its core primary care GP surgery focus (£6bn portfolio across UK and Ireland).
