• Completed acquisition of Boteco Srl, an Italian family-owned designer and manufacturer of mechanical components, following satisfaction of pre-close conditions (originally announced 20 May 2026).
  • Acquisition multiple of 6.5x EBITDA for the twelve months to 31 December 2025.
  • Expected to be accretive to Group margins and adjusted EPS in the first full year post-completion.
  • Anticipated ROIC of 15% in the third full year of ownership (calculated on adjusted operating profit over initial + deferred contingent consideration plus capex and transaction fees).
  • Strategic rationale: complements existing product expertise and provides adjacency to machine and automation end-markets.
  • Second bolt-on in the past twelve months, following Device Technologies (announced December 2025).
  • CEO Scott Fawcett: "Boteco is a clear example of Essentra's disciplined bolt-on M&A strategy: acquiring specialist businesses that enhance our product capabilities, broaden our customer offering and generate attractive financial returns."
  • Fawcett reiterated that "disciplined and value-accretive M&A remains an important lever for Essentra to drive sustainable growth and incremental value for shareholders."
  • No deal consideration value or funding details disclosed in the announcement.

body:
• Recommended cash acquisition of Ramsdens by FirstCash at 600p/share, plus an interim cash dividend of up to 9p/share payable 9 October 2026.
  • Total equity value of approximately £206 million / $273 million USD.
  • Structured as a court-sanctioned scheme of arrangement via FirstCash's UK subsidiary Chess Bidco Limited.
  • Unanimously approved by both Boards; subject to Ramsdens shareholder vote and UK regulatory approvals.
  • Expected to close by end of 2026.
  • Ramsdens operates 174 pawn stores across England, Scotland and Wales with 877 employees.
  • Combines with FirstCash's existing UK H&T operations to create a scaled UK platform of ~470 stores with limited location overlap.
  • Post-close, FirstCash expects over 3,500 pawn locations worldwide (vs current 3,300+).
  • Immediately accretive to revenue, EBITDA and EPS upon closing per management.
  • Ramsdens TTM financials to 31 March 2026 (USD, IFRS): Revenue $200m, Net income $26m, Adjusted EBITDA $40m (implies ~20% adj. EBITDA margin; deal at ~6.8x adj. EBITDA).
  • FirstCash flags risk that it "will incur additional indebtedness to finance the Acquisition, which may not be on favorable terms."
  • Rick Wessel (FirstCash CEO): "Ramsdens is a well-respected operator with a proven track record of operating successfully in the U.K. pawn market. This transaction will not only provide immediate revenue and earnings accretion to FirstCash upon closing, but also enhances our long-term growth profile through continued expansion of its industry-leading brands and platform."
  • Peter Kenyon (Ramsdens CEO): "I am exceptionally proud of Ramsdens' transformational growth since our IPO in 2017. FirstCash is an internationally established sector leader, and I share their confidence and conviction in the outlook for Ramsdens, which is underpinned by our diversified model and established reputation for consistently doing the right thing for our customers and our fantastic people."
  • Advisors: Jefferies (FirstCash financial); Gowling WLG and Alston & Bird (FirstCash legal); Cavendish (Ramsdens financial); Addleshaw Goddard (Ramsdens legal).
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'acquisition of'

• Completion of the £390m acquisition of DIY Kitchens (parent of Ultima Furniture Systems) confirmed today, with all conditions now satisfied.

-

12,725,469 new ordinary shares of £0.10 each were admitted to trading on the LSE main market

, issued to seller Ling Chase Holdings (the Ellis family investment vehicle), with Admission effective 8.00 a.m. today.

  • Completion took place automatically on Admission of the New Ordinary Shares.
  • Context vs the 3 June deal announcement:

funded via £292.5m cash and £97.5m in new shares

;

~8.5x LTM EBITDA (to March 2026) and expected to be immediately accretive to revenue, EBIT margin and EPS

.

  • DIY Kitchens financial profile:

2025 revenue of £136m and EBIT of £37m (27% EBIT margin), with revenue growth of over 17% p.a. over the past five years

.

-

Transaction includes freehold property assets valued at about £55 million

.

  • Operating model:

DIY Kitchens will continue to operate separately from Howden's trade-only business; the deal does not affect the previously announced £100m share buyback for 2026, with the £50m first tranche started and to be completed by year-end

.

-

Funded from existing cash and a new bank facility; Howdens retains a net cash position and keeps dividend policy intact

.

  • Strategic rationale: gives Howdens direct access to non-trade/self-managing consumers via an online channel for the first time, complementing the trade-only depot model.

body:
• Board unanimously rejected a preliminary, highly conditional take-private proposal received from Railpen on 16 June 2026; described by Railpen as "final".
  • Proposal terms per IP Group share: 59.0p cash + dividend in specie of the Oxford Nanopore (ONT) stake + a CVR of up to 5p (3p if Istesso disposal completes within 12 months at ≥£98m gross proceeds to IPO; further 2p if proceeds exceed £200m).
  • Implied value (ex-CVR) based on ONT's 22 June close: 69.4p per share, a 6.7% premium to IPO's prior close but a 37.2% discount to last reported NAV of 110.4p (31 Dec 2025).
  • Notes IPO shares traded at 70.6p as recently as 2 June 2026 — i.e. the offer is below the recent undisturbed price.
  • Proposal conditional on Railpen forming a consortium (the "Pensions Growth Alliance Consortium" of UK pension funds) to deliver a firm offer; Railpen does not yet have funding in place.
  • Board concluded the Proposal "materially undervalued the Company, its unique framework of origination capabilities and its future prospects".
  • Fourth approach overall — prior indicative offers rejected at implied 64.4p (23 April), 65.5p (5 May), and 69.5p (12 June); earlier bids lacked the CVR structure.
  • Railpen had told the Board on 4 June it did not intend to proceed, before returning with the third (then fourth) proposal.
  • Board declined to grant due diligence access absent a proposal at recommendable value/certainty, but facilitated management engagement with prospective consortium members.
  • PUSU deadline: Railpen must announce a firm intention or walk away by 5:00 p.m. on 20 July 2026.
  • Announcement made without Railpen's prior consent.
  • Chair Michael Queen: "Since 2024, IP Group has taken decisive action to drive cost efficiencies, realised over £250 million of cash and undertaken a buyback to retire ~15% of its share capital."
  • Queen added the Board "remains open to engagement with any party that recognises the inherent value of IP Group's unique portfolio."
  • Shares in issue: 883,427,642 ordinary shares; BofA Securities acting as sole adviser to IPO.

  • Completion expected July 2026.
  • At 31 March 2026, SFS net assets were £10.1m; intra-group funding balance was £33.2m.
  • Transaction will add ~£40 million to tangible net assets and +0.5% to CET1 ratio, net of allocated goodwill, reflected in full-year accounts.

  • 100 employees in Northampton and UK workshops to transfer to NRG under TUPE on completion.

At the same time it will simplify Paragon's operational platforms and the additional capital created will be used to continue supporting growth in our Commercial Lending division."

body:
• Board unanimously rejected an unsolicited, indicative and conditional proposal from Castlelake, L.P. received 20 June 2026 to acquire all shares not already held by Castlelake at £6.25 per share in cash, with a partial alternative of unlisted, non-transferrable, non-voting shares in a Castlelake-structured vehicle.
  • Bidding vehicle would be 49% owned by Castlelake and 51% by EU nationals and potentially other undisclosed investors.
  • This is the third proposal — prior bids at £5.60 and £6.00 per share were also unanimously rejected.
  • Board called the bid "highly opportunistic", delivered against a "temporarily depressed share price" and said it "fundamentally undervalues easyJet and its prospects."
  • Board criticised Castlelake's analysis as relying on Middle East conflict-affected share prices, short-term earnings and analyst reports, with no adequate control premium.
  • Board flagged concerns over opaque ownership structure, elevated leverage and overall conditionality of the proposal.
  • Reaffirmed investment grade balance sheet with net cash position.
  • Cited 46% increase in pre-tax profit over the two financial years to September 2025, driven by easyJet Holidays growth and operational improvement.
  • Reiterated medium-term target of >£1bn profit before tax, despite current-year Middle East conflict disruption.
  • Fleet renewal: 17 A320neo/A321neo deliveries in FY26, a further 73 across FY27–FY28, with 79 older A319s retired on accelerated basis — driving upgauging and fuel efficiency gains.
  • easyJet Holidays: delivered prior £250m PBT target early; new target of £450m PBT by 2030 with no capital intensity.
  • Additional growth levers cited: route maturity gains from network rebalancing (incl. Milan Linate and Rome Fiumicino slot acquisitions), tech-driven cost efficiencies, and premiumisation (business traffic, loyalty, bag connectivity, partnerships).
  • Board concluded the proposal represents an opportunistic attempt to acquire easyJet "on the cheap".
  • Announcement made without Castlelake's consent; shareholders advised to take no action.

body:
• Railpen (18.4% holder, 162.36m shares, shareholder since 2019) has made a Rule 2.4 possible offer announcement for the whole of IP Group, having explored a bid for several months with board assistance.
  • Possible Offer rejected by the IP Group Board (most recent proposal tabled on 16 June 2026).
  • Total indicative value: 69.7p per share (plus up to 5p CVR), comprising:
  • 59p cash per share ("Cash Offer")
  • ~10.7p per share representing pro rata value of IP Group's stake in Oxford Nanopore (ONT) at 116.5p ONT close
  • CVR of up to 5p linked to disposal of Istesso stake within 12 months: 3p if proceeds ≥£98m, additional 2p if >£200m
  • Cash Offer represents a 48% premium to IP Group's 25 March 2026 price (stripping out ONT stake and net cash) — 25 March being the date Saba Capital disclosed lifting its stake to 12.01%.
  • Railpen intends to roll over its entire existing 18.4% stake into the transaction (not cash out).
  • Strategic rationale: Railpen believes IP Group's "strategic aims would therefore be best served in the private markets," citing persistent NAV discount, difficulty raising equity, and mismatch between public market horizons and patient venture capital.
  • Plans to assemble a consortium of pension funds managing >£235bn collectively to back a scaled third-party venture/scale-up manager aligned with HMG's productive finance agenda.
  • Pre-conditions: (1) consortium formation; (2) satisfactory confirmatory due diligence; (3) unanimous unconditional board recommendation.
  • Istesso context: IP Group holds undiluted 56.5% stake, carried at £98m in 31 Dec 2025 NAV; described as developing oral regenerative medicines.
  • PUSU deadline: 5.00pm on 20 July 2026 for Railpen to announce firm intention or walk away.
  • Rothschild & Co acting as financial adviser to Railpen.
  • Railpen invites institutional shareholders to engage and pressure the board to enter discussions.
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets reference an offer approach: 'possible offer'

body:
• Fully pre-let 30 Duke Street St James's, SW1 ahead of practical completion, following final retail lease exchange with Australian menswear brand M.J. Bale.
  • M.J. Bale takes 2,636 sq ft of prime retail on the Jermyn Street frontage for its first London store.
  • Completes earlier lettings: 62,300 sq ft office pre-let to Clayton, Dubilier & Rice (CD&R) in May 2025, and Piccadilly retail unit let to L'Eto (restaurant group) in October 2025.
  • Total lettings of c. 67,700 sq ft secured across office and retail.
  • Rents achieved 6.7% ahead of ERV on average.
  • Blended ERV of £186 per sq ft, generating £12.6 million of total annual rent.
  • Dan Nicholson, Executive Director: "Fully pre-letting 30 Duke Street St James's ahead of practical completion is a great result and highlights the enduring appeal of the West End. From CD&R to M.J. Bale, the quality of the customer line-up demonstrates the continued pull of St James's and the depth of demand which our development pipeline is well placed to capture."
type_workings: Rule 'commercial': opening bullets say 'pre-let'

body:
• Revenue: £5,177.7m, +7% statutory (+8% organic at constant FX); ex-Type 31 reversal revenue was £5,273.2m (+9%, +10% organic).
  • £140m Type 31 contract charge (covers remaining cost of programme, fully recognised in FY26) — includes £95.5m revenue reversal; cash costs spread over remainder of contract.
  • Underlying operating profit: £293.3m (-19%); excluding Type 31, +19% to £433.3m.
  • Underlying operating margin: 5.7% (FY25: 7.5%); ex-Type 31 8.2%, +70bps, above 8% target.
  • Underlying EPS: 39.6p (-21%); ex-Type 31 60.5p (+20%). Statutory basic EPS 42.1p (FY25: 49.1p).
  • Underlying free cash flow: £261.8m, +71%; underlying operating cash conversion 119% (84% ex-Type 31).
  • Net debt: £329.0m (FY25: £373.3m); net debt ex-leases just £22.7m; covenant gearing 0.2x (FY25: 0.3x); liquidity ~£1.4bn.
  • Contract backlog: £9.8bn (FY25: £10.4bn) — reflects FY25 big awards and execution.
  • Full-year dividend 7.5p, +15% (final 5.0p).
  • £200m buyback completed April 2026; further £200m announced, to complete in FY27.
  • Outlook: FY27 expectations unchanged; medium-term guidance reiterated — mid-single-digit organic revenue growth, underlying margin ≥9%, cash conversion ≥80%; ~70% of FY27 revenue under contract at 1 April 2026.
  • Type 31 update: rework on ship one outfitting higher than expected due to design changes and out-of-sequence build; ships 1 & 2 floated off, ship 3 keel laid, ship 4 steel cut; programme typically <4% of Group revenue.
  • Segmental (constant FX):
  • Marine: revenue +2% to £1,591.5m; underlying op loss £29.8m (Type 31 charge); ex-Type 31 margin 6.5% (+40bps).
  • Nuclear: revenue +14% to £2,070.4m; margin +70bps to 9.5%; Cavendish Nuclear +18%.
  • Land: revenue -3% to £1,084.4m (back to growth in H2); margin +110bps to 8.8%.
  • Aviation: revenue +34% to £431.4m; margin +90bps to 7.1% (Mentor 2 ramp, BC HEMS, UK military scope).
  • Key contract wins/strategic:
  • Indonesia Maritime Partnership Programme framework up to £4bn; LOI for two further Arrowhead 140 licences.
  • HII partnership expanded to US Virginia Class submarine build programme; initial engineering contract live.
  • GB Energy-Nuclear Owner's Engineer role for UK SMRs via Litmus JV — up to £300m over 14 years.
  • Teaming agreement with Patria on 6x6 APC for UK; teaming with Hanwha Ocean for Canadian Patrol Submarine Project; first South Africa defence contract (submarine support).
  • Six-month FMSP bridging agreement signed; LOI to finalise multi-year replacement (Gateway) by October 2026; two-year Royal Navy surface ship support extension.
  • £1bn DSG British Army vehicle support contract mobilised; 270 Light Utility Vehicles for British Army awarded.
  • Australian Border Force eight-year A$230m helicopter contract; new 10-year French H145-D3 support contract.
  • Not selected for Swedish Luleå Class corvette programme (announced late May 2026).
  • Legacy US legal case: unexpected court judgement — c.£17m in operating expenses (~half from this case) plus £8.6m interest expense; timing of cash flow uncertain pending appeal.
  • CEO succession: David Lockwood to retire; Harry Holt (current Nuclear CEO) becomes CEO on 1 August 2026.
  • Defence/civil mix: ~80% defence & nuclear; FY26 74% defence-related, 70% UK.
  • Pension: IAS 19 deficit widened to £34.0m (FY25: £8.4m); technical provision deficit ~£100m (FY25: ~£125m); ongoing deficit repair payments ~£20m p.a.
  • RCF maturity extended by 365 days to 18 July 2031 (June 2026); £300m bond matures October 2026.
  • Civil nuclear UK opportunity quantified at £25–30bn addressable through 2050; global new nuclear spend expected to reach $2.2trn by 2050.
  • CEO Lockwood: "We remain on track to deliver our medium-term guidance... Babcock is a more resilient business today, with clear momentum and strong visibility."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Full year ended 31 March 2026'

body:
• Castlelake has made a Rule 2.4 announcement disclosing three rejected non-binding indicative cash proposals for easyJet, going public after the Board declined to engage.
  • First Proposal: 560p cash (12 June) – rejected 16 June.
  • Second Proposal: 600p cash (17 June) – rejected 20 June.
  • Third Proposal: 625p cash per share (20 June) – rejected 21 June; now disclosed to shareholders ahead of the PUSU deadline of 5.00pm on 26 June 2026.
  • 625p represents a ~59% premium to the 394.20p undisturbed price (28 May 2026) and ~71% premium to the VWAP since the 16 April interim trading update (365.42p).
  • 35% premium to the 464.00p close on 27 February 2026 (pre US-Iran conflict) and ~39% to the 12-month VWAP of 449.50p.
  • Offer is above every closing price since 25 February 2022 and above all post-16 April 2026 analyst price targets (range 280p–570p; Morgan Stanley lowest, Barclays highest).
  • Implies a 16.5x P/E on 2027E EPS (37.9p FactSet).
  • Castlelake argues 625p compares favourably to the NPV of easyJet's share price even assuming the company achieves its >£1bn medium-term PBT aspiration (modelled for 2029) at 5–10yr average EV/EBITDA multiples of 3.2x–4.3x.
  • Partial equity alternative to be offered, allowing shareholders to roll into the private vehicle, subject to a cap and transfer restrictions.
  • Castlelake reserves right to reduce offer by any dividend/distribution declared post-announcement.
  • EU ownership compliance structure: EU national partners Peter Bellew and Mark Breen (ex-LCC executives) will hold a controlling stake via an EU-owned/controlled vehicle ("EU Partner"), mirroring structures used by other EU airlines.
  • Bellew acquired 50,000 easyJet shares (~0.007%) between 2 March and 20 May 2026 at a top price of 437.14p – under Rule 6, any firm offer cannot be below 437.14p.
  • Financing: fully funded via committed equity from Castlelake funds, co-investors and the EU Partner, with Goldman Sachs "highly confident" of arranging required debt.
  • Castlelake is strategically partnered with Brookfield (>$1tn AUM) and has invested >$24bn in aviation since 2005 across 200+ airline relationships; AUM ~$38bn.
  • Intends to implement via scheme of arrangement (with right to switch to contractual offer); pre-conditions include satisfactory due diligence and definitive documentation.
  • Post-acquisition, easyJet stated to remain "well-capitalised, with a robust capital structure and ample liquidity."
  • Castlelake quote on rationale: ambition to support easyJet as "a stronger, more resilient European airline under European control."
  • No certainty a firm Rule 2.7 offer will be made; next required announcement by 5pm 26 June 2026 unless Panel extends.
type_workings: Rule 'dividend': body declares a dividend with payment details

body:
• Brief RNS issued in response to weekend media speculation (Sky News, 21 June) that the board is lining up a successor to co-founder CEO Tim Steiner, with Vonage (Ericsson) CEO Niklas Heuveldop reportedly sounded out for the role.
  • Ocado's statement is a non-denial: confirms "the CEO and the Board continually engage in long-term succession planning and regularly engage with potential candidates."
  • No confirmation, timeline, or shortlist disclosed; no changes announced to Steiner's current role.
  • Context: speculation frames a potential strategic pivot toward positioning Ocado as a pure-play technology/SaaS infrastructure business rather than a UK online grocer, given Heuveldop's telecoms/cloud-platform background.
  • No financial, trading or guidance content in the release.
type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'board' with change verb 'successor' in opening bullets

body:
• Fattal Hotels has confirmed it does not intend to make an offer for PPHE Hotel Group, ending the approach disclosed on 28 May 2026 and 8 June 2026.
  • Following the initial announcements, Fattal and PPHE engaged in discussions on potential offer terms but "it has not been possible to reach agreement".
  • Statement made under Rule 2.8 of the City Code, meaning Fattal is now restricted from making a renewed bid for 6 months.
  • Standard carve-outs preserved: Fattal reserves the right to set aside Rule 2.8 restrictions with PPHE board agreement, on a third-party firm offer, a PPHE Rule 9 waiver/reverse takeover, or a material change of circumstances as determined by the Panel.
  • Context: Fattal (controlled by David Fattal) already holds a significant stake in PPHE alongside founder/chair Eli Papouchado; the failed deal removes a key near-term takeout catalyst for PPHE shares.

body:
• The Takeover Panel has removed Fattal Hotels Ltd as a named potential offeror for PPHE Hotel Group from the Disclosure Table.
  • Fattal had been identified as a potential offeror at 17:10 on 27-May-2026; no Rule 2.6 "put up or shut up" deadline had been set.
  • PPHE remains in an offer period (commenced 07:00 on 14-Nov-2025) but is now listed with "No named offeror", indicating the Fattal approach is no longer live while the company stays formally in an offer period.
  • Relevant securities in issue: 41,858,631 ordinary shares NPV (ISIN GG00B1Z5FH87).
  • Read-across: with Fattal (controlled by David Fattal, who is also PPHE's Chairman/largest shareholder) dropping out, the potential management/insider-led take-private route appears to have lapsed, leaving PPHE "in play" without an identified bidder.

body:
• Non-regulatory statement responding to the UK Government's publication of its home buying and selling reform roadmap; no trading, financial or guidance content.
  • Reforms focus on upfront information, greater transparency, improved use of property data, digitalisation, and higher professional standards.
  • LSL positions itself as both a supporter and beneficiary of reform, citing its presence across conveyancing, property searches and property condition reports via its Estate Agency Franchising, Surveying & Valuation, and Financial Services divisions.
  • CEO Adam Castleton: "We are pleased to see the Government set out the next steps in reforming the home buying and selling process... we support the ambition to make buying and selling homes more efficient, more transparent and easier for consumers to navigate."
  • Castleton flags execution risk: "The key will be practical and proportionate implementation."
  • Scale reminder: 2,600+ advisers (~12% of purchase/remortgage market), 60 franchisees operating 294 branches, and surveying services to 5 of the 6 largest UK lenders.

body:
• Fattal Hotel Group's £22.00/share indicative cash proposal (announced 27 May 2026) is no longer capable of being delivered in its current form, per the Independent Committee.
  • Blocker: Euro Plaza Holdings (~33% of issued share capital) informed the Independent Committee it opposes the Fattal Proposal; Fattal has said it would not proceed against Euro Plaza's opposition.
  • Board had previously (with Rule 3 adviser) unanimously determined the £22.00 Fattal Proposal represents fair value.
  • Independent Committee consulted shareholders representing ~83% of issued share capital to gauge feedback before reaching this conclusion.
  • New indicative proposal received from another interested party on 31 May 2026 — described as "very preliminary stage and is currently being assessed."
  • Founder Shareholders (Eli Papouchado via Euro Plaza Holdings, and Boris Ivesha) collectively hold ~44% of voting rights; offer period began 14 November 2025 following their confirmation of meetings with financial investors.
  • Independent Committee excludes Roni Hirsch (Euro Plaza Holdings' representative Director).
  • Board signals intent to conclude the >7-month Strategic Review "as expeditiously as possible".
  • Rule 2.6(a) 28-day deadline dispensation remains in place; Fattal not subject to deadline while still participating in the Formal Sale Process.
  • Announcement made without Fattal's consent (per Note 3 on Rule 2.5).
  • No certainty any firm offer will be made.

body:
• Appointed Rebecca Napier as CFO and Executive Director, effective 3 August 2026.
  • Napier was most recently CFO of Britvic plc until its acquisition by Carlsberg Group in 2025.
  • Previously spent 17 years at IAG plc, including as CFO of British Airways; began career at Deloitte as a Chartered Accountant.
  • Chair Caroline Silver framed the hire around the near-completion of the Barratt/Redrow integration, citing her "capital markets experience" as critical to building on combination benefits.
  • Napier said: "Barratt Redrow is the industry-leading housebuilder with three trusted brands, a strong balance sheet and an excellent land pipeline supporting a clear focus on delivering high-quality homes and places for customers and communities."
  • No trading, guidance or financial update accompanied the announcement.

body:
• Formal contracts signed with five German Public Transport Authorities in North Rhine-Westphalia and adjacent regions, implementing the structural changes flagged on 29 January 2026.
  • Rhein-Münsterland Express (RME): contract (RE 7, RB 48) converted to a gross contract structure from 1 January 2026, removing revenue risk from National Express; terms revised to current industry norms; term extended by two years to 2032.
  • Rhein-Ruhr-Express (RRX): the loss-making contracts (RE 1, RE 5, RE 6, RE 11, RE 4) will be shortened by 3 years, now ending 2030, to enable coordinated retendering aligned with NRW's regional transport plan.
  • Further detail to come in the audited results for the 15-month period ending 31 March 2026, expected in July 2026.

body:
• Jansen Stage 2 capex estimate raised from US$4.9bn to US$6.9bn (+41%), including contingencies.
  • First production for Stage 2 pushed to late FY2031 (previously FY2029, extended to FY2031 in Aug 2025).
  • Majority of cost increase driven by additional construction hours, materials quantities, and escalation identified in the detailed review.
  • Stage 2 is 16% complete; engineering 83% complete at end-May 2026, de-risking remaining estimate.
  • Stage 2 production target unchanged at ~4.36Mtpa; combined Jansen output of 8.5Mtpa after two-year ramp-up = ~10% of global potash supply.
  • Updated Stage 2 IRR of 11% at consensus prices (FY31-FY41 avg US$330/t FOB Vancouver); payback 8 years.
  • Stage 2 EBITDA margin still >65%; combined Jansen unit cost guided at US$114-130/t (equivalent to prior US$105-120/t in real 2023 terms) — lowest-cost Canadian potash mine.
  • Stage 1 on track for first production mid CY2027, hitting January 2026 critical path milestones.
  • FY2027 Group capex guidance unchanged at ~US$11bn.
  • Expected impairment charge of ~US$2.3bn (pre and post-tax) on the Jansen project at 30 June 2026, reflecting higher forecast capital intensity (including Stages 1, 2 and potential future expansions).
  • CEO-elect Brandon Craig: "With the reset of Jansen Stage 2, we are progressing with our intention of building a Tier 1 asset. The combined Jansen Stage 1 and 2 will be a low cost, long life asset with almost 60 year mine life and is expected to generate benefits for shareholders for decades."
  • Shares closed -4.40% at 3,327p on the day of the announcement.

  • Ex-dividend date: 25 June 2026; record date: 26 June 2026; payment from 29 June 2026.

body:
• Seok-Hee Lee appointed EVP of Intel Foundry, reporting directly to CEO Lip-Bu Tan, leading advanced packaging, system integration, back-end technology development, and back-end manufacturing.
  • Intel is establishing advanced packaging as a focused standalone business with dedicated leadership, reflecting its growing role in AI system performance and heterogeneous integration.
  • Lee joins from SK On (President & CEO), and was previously President & CEO of SK hynix; he has prior engineering leadership experience at Intel.
  • Lip-Bu Tan said Lee is "the right leader to build and scale this critical part of the Intel Foundry business as we prepare to ramp advanced packaging technologies, including EMIB-T and HBI, to high volume."
  • Lee on joining: "I'm excited to return home and to join the Intel team as we help advance the company's technology leadership, manufacturing capabilities, and customer commitments in this critical area."
  • Naga Chandrasekaran (EVP Intel Foundry) retains front-end role, continuing to report to Tan and leading front-end technology development and manufacturing, with focus on ramping Intel 18A, Intel 14A, and future nodes; also retains design enablement and customer-facing functions.
  • Operating model now splits Foundry into front-end (Chandrasekaran) and back-end/packaging (Lee) under the CEO, aimed at delivering "with speed, consistency, and predictability."
  • EVP Navid Shahriari to retire after a 37-year career at Intel.
  • Context: announcement comes as Intel pushes packaging (EMIB-T, Hybrid Bonding Interconnect/HBI) as a differentiator versus TSMC's CoWoS in the AI accelerator supply chain; share price closed up 10.78% at $134.16 on the day.
type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'ceo' with change verb 'appointed' in opening bullets

body:
• Armenian subsidiary Ameriabank CJSC placed USD 50m of 8.0% perpetual subordinated callable AT1 capital notes.
  • Represents the second tranche of a USD 100m issuance programme; first tranche placed February 2026.
  • Placement conducted in the domestic Armenian market; Notes to be listed on the Armenia Securities Exchange.
  • Ameriabank acted as arranger.
  • Note terms: USD 10,000 face value, minimum 5 Notes per purchase, perpetual with issuer call option after year 5, 8.0% coupon paid semi-annually.
  • Second tranche priced at a YTM 50bps lower than the first tranche, reflecting strong investor demand.
  • CFO Hovhannes Toroyan: "the second tranche of AT1 notes was also fully allocated well ahead of the planned timeline, further demonstrating Ameriabank's strong franchise value and the high level of investor trust in the Bank."
  • Group CFO Giorgi Shagidze: "This second AT1 issuance at a lower coupon rate underscores the market's confidence in Ameriabank's credit fundamentals and capital strength."
  • Transaction strengthens capital position, enhancing financial resilience and capacity to support growth in the Armenian market.

body:
• SJP unveiled a simplified financial reporting framework to be implemented from H1 2026 results.
  • No change to financial business model, anticipated profitability, financial ambitions or shareholder returns guidance — purely a presentational change.
  • Primary performance metric 'Underlying cash result' renamed to 'adjusted IFRS profit after tax' — same number, different name.
  • New presentation shows income and expenses separately and before tax, consistent with IFRS requirements.
  • New disclosures aim to give greater clarity on profitability drivers, including contributions from funds under management and new business inflows.
  • Management stated the framework is "intended to provide a clearer and more transparent presentation of financial performance, better reflecting the Group's financial business model, charging structure, and statutory IFRS reporting."
  • On-demand webcast hosted by CFO Caroline Waddington with pro-forma financials and illustrative reconciliations; live Q&A held 18 June 2026 at 16:30 BST.

body:
• Publication of historical comparative financial data in US dollars ahead of previously announced reporting currency change.
  • Switch to USD reporting takes effect from interim results for H1 2026 (to be announced 12 August 2026), as flagged on 11 March 2026.
  • Rationale: 63% of 2025 revenues and 79% of operating profits originated in US dollars; move aims to give investors "greater transparency" and "reduce foreign exchange volatility over time".
  • Dividends to be declared in USD from the interim dividend for FY2026 (payable January 2027); shareholders default to sterling payment unless they elect USD.
  • Re-presented USD primary financial statements provided for FY2025, FY2024 and H1 2025, plus a KPI presentation covering 2022–2025.
  • No change to trading, guidance, or operational performance disclosed — purely a presentational/accounting change.
type_workings: Rule 'results headline': opening bullets say 'interim results'

body:
• Group adjusted revenue +25% to £1,716m; adjusted operating profit marginally lower at £219.4m (FY25: £222.8m), but +18% ex-DfT TOCs.
  • Adjusted EPS 20.3p vs 19.4p (+4.6%), aided by FY26 £50m buyback; basic EPS 21.4p (FY25: 21.3p).
  • Statutory revenue down to £4,751.9m (FY25 restated: £5,233.9m) reflecting SWR transfer to DfTO in May 2025.
  • Final dividend 5.0p; full-year 7.2p (+10.8% vs 6.5p); policy to move from c.3x cover toward 2.5x cover over time.
  • New £100m buyback announced, to complete over next 12 months (follows £50m completed in FY26); £89m total returned in FY26.
  • c.£400m free cash flow guided over next three years, including c.£90m cash inflow from DfT TOCs/Rail Services as GWR and WCP transition.
  • Adjusted net debt £137.7m (FY25: £86.9m); reported net debt £725.3m (FY25: £985.6m) post IFRS 16 lease run-off; £364.1m undrawn committed headroom; leverage policy <2.0x adjusted net debt:EBITDA.
  • Free cash flow £73.8m before acquisitions/returns (FY25: £113.5m); capex stepped up to c.£190m, mainly First Bus electrification.
  • First Bus revenue +33% to £1,443.6m; adjusted op profit +7% to £102.8m despite £26m fare-funding cut, c.£15m NIC headwind, and 3% volume decline (transition to £3 fare cap, weaker consumer confidence).
  • First Bus margin -180bps to 7.1% (lower-margin franchising mix); Regional Bus margin 8.8% (FY25: 9.7%), with NIC a 1.7pp drag.
  • First Bus London contributed £310m revenue, ahead of acquisition assumptions; 99% of FY27 contracted revenues secured, 77% for FY28.
  • Business & Coach revenue +28% to £230.1m; five coach acquisitions (3 in-year, 2 post period); Flixbus launched and extended.
  • Electrification: 26% of UK bus fleet zero emission (43% of London red buses); 4 fully + 17 substantially electrified depots; third-party charging at 15 depots; minority stake in Palmer Energy Technology for battery storage.
  • Fuel hedging: 88% of FY27 diesel hedged at 43p/litre, 54% of FY28 at 41p; electricity 77% FY27 at £74/MWh, 72% FY28 at £73/MWh.
  • First Rail adjusted revenue £272.4m (FY25: £288.8m); adjusted op profit £129.9m (FY25: £148.8m), hit by Stirling mobilisation costs of £6.3m, c.£2m higher Lumo infrastructure charge, SWR transfer, and normalised DfT TOC variable fees.
  • Open access revenue £109.3m (+3%); journeys +4% to 3.1m; H2 yields pressured by LNER East Coast capacity/price competition and weaker leisure demand; pence per seat mile flat at 11.1p.
  • London Overground contract started 3 May 2026: c.£300m annual revenue, 8-year term + 2-year option (c.£3bn total); TfL retains revenue risk.
  • On track to more than double open access capacity in 2-3 years: Lumo Glasgow extension and extra Hull Trains/Lumo paths from Dec 2025; Stirling-London fully operational July 2026; Carmarthen and Lumo 10-car from FY29; applications for c.1bn additional annual seat miles under ORR review.
  • DfT TOC transitions: SWR transferred May 2025; GWR to transfer 13 December 2026 (c.£15m op profit / c.£9m earnings reduction); WCP expected around end of FY27.
  • FY27 outlook: Group adjusted EPS to be maintained from higher-quality earnings base.
  • FY27 segment guidance: First Bus revenue just over £1.5bn with further op profit growth and c.£140m decarbonisation capex; open access revenue c.£130-150m with margin progressing to mid-teens post 2-yr Stirling/Carmarthen ramp; London Overground c.£300m.
  • First Bus capex to normalise to c.£80-100m p.a. from FY28; Stirling and Carmarthen routes each targeted at c.£50m annual revenue at mid-teens margin post 2-year ramp.
  • c.£9m Group cost savings delivered in FY26 from restructuring; central costs down to £13.3m (FY25: £22.0m).
  • Prior-year accounting restatements: £167.6m DfT levy reclassified from revenue deduction to expense (no profit impact); First Bus London PPA finalised.
  • CEO Graham Sutherland: "Our strong performance in FY 2026 against significant headwinds has reinforced our track record for delivery and shareholder returns."
  • Steve Montgomery, MD of First Rail, retiring end of June 2026 after 11 years leading the division.
type_workings: Rule 'capital returns': opening bullets announce a share buyback

body:
• Total Group sales +2% to £727m, with Premier Inn UK and Germany growth partly offset by planned UK F&B decline from the Accelerating Growth Plan (AGP).
  • Like-for-like total Group sales +2% (UK +1%, Germany +7%).
  • Premier Inn UK accommodation sales +3%; RevPAR +2%, outperforming the M&E market by +1.0pp on sales and +1.2pp on RevPAR; RevPAR premium widened to £6.81 (vs £6.04 in Q1 FY26).
  • London notably strong: accommodation sales +7%, RevPAR +4% (occupancy +2.7pp to 79.2%, ARR +0.5%); Regions sales +1%, RevPAR +1% (occupancy flat at 79.1%).
  • UK F&B sales -5% (£147.5m), in line with expectations under AGP transition.
  • Premier Inn Germany accommodation sales +13% in local currency (+16% GBP); total German sales +18% GBP / +15% EUR to €85.2m.
  • Germany occupancy +4.3pp to 71.7%; total estate RevPAR €63, established hotels (17-hotel cohort) RevPAR €73; ARR -6.7% in EUR reflecting new openings dilution.
  • Six new leasehold hotels opened in Germany during the quarter.
  • Germany F&B +32% GBP (+29% EUR); outperformance vs M&E market noted, with peer market weakness attributed to fewer high-impact events YoY.
  • Forward booked position ahead of last year in both UK and Germany, supported by peak leisure bookings; full year outlook reiterated.
  • FY27 business rates impact unchanged vs prior guidance; continuing to lobby UK Government on FY28/FY29.
  • Reaffirms New Five-Year Plan (announced 30 April): exit all remaining UK branded restaurants to become pure-play hotel business; refocused UK growth; accelerated Germany cash flow.
  • Plan targets: reduce capital intensity by £1bn and generate £2bn of free cash flow available for shareholders by FY31.
  • Paul: "We delivered a strong and improved performance in the first quarter."
  • Paul on outlook: "Strong leisure bookings mean that our forward booked position is ahead of last year and we remain confident in the full year outlook."
  • FY27 interim results scheduled for 15 October 2026; AGM held later today.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 FY27'

body:
• Board acknowledges letter and accompanying presentation issued by activist Saba Capital Management L.P. on 17 June 2026 to Workspace shareholders.
  • Board states it "firmly believes in the strength of the Company's strategy, as announced alongside its Full Year Results on 10 June 2026, to maximise long-term sustainable value for all shareholders."
  • Board, with advisers, is carefully reviewing the Letter and Presentation; full considered response to follow in due course.
  • Notice of AGM to be posted to shareholders on 19 June 2026.
  • Shareholders advised to take no action at this time and await further communications.
  • J.P. Morgan Cazenove, Rothschild & Co and Stifel advising the Company — signals preparation for a potentially contested engagement.
  • Context: Saba, known for activist campaigns against UK investment trusts, is now targeting a FTSE 250 REIT (3.8m sq ft, 57 London/South East flexible workspace locations), marking an expansion of its UK activism beyond closed-end funds.

body:
• Group sales £16,826m, LFL +1.0%; UK & ROI LFL +1.8%, Booker LFL -3.2%, Central Europe LFL +0.8%.
  • FY26/27 guidance reiterated: adjusted operating profit £3.0–3.3bn; free cash flow within £1.5–2.0bn medium-term range.
  • £750m buyback: £341m completed between 16 April and 17 June 2026; balance to finish by April 2027.
  • UK LFL +1.8% on an exceptionally strong prior-year comp (record weather, competitor disruption); two-year LFL +6.9%.
  • UK NPS 31, up +6pts YoY, with QoQ progress on value and range.
  • UK Food sales +2.6%, Fresh +3.6%; Finest sales +9% (two-year +29%).
  • UK Online sales +8.9%; Whoosh expanded into 34 further large stores; Aldi Price Match extended to over 2,000 Express stores.
  • Over 520 new/improved products launched, including 220+ Finest lines; Your Clubcard Prices delivered ~100m tailored offers since March launch.
  • Tesco Insurance & Money Services policies in force +15% YoY to 2.7m; Tesco Mobile now serving nearly 6m customers.
  • ROI LFL +3.3%, total sales +5.6% at constant FX; Online +10.9%; Food +3.7% with volume growth.
  • Booker core retail -1.5% (c.200bps drag from August 2025 exit of a lower-margin national account); core catering -3.3%; Tobacco -9.7% (two-year -17.2%); 146 net new retailer partners added.
  • Central Europe LFL +0.8%; Online +17.4%; Non-food +0.9% helped by Mother's Day and Easter.
  • UK fuel sales £1,742m, LFL +19.5%; total UK revenue inc. fuel +3.7%.
  • Ken Murphy: "I am pleased with our progress in the first quarter, with customer satisfaction up strongly and continued sales growth building on the exceptional performance we delivered last year."
  • Management flags "conflict in the Middle East creating ongoing uncertainty for many households" as the key macro overhang shaping the value focus.
  • Interim Results scheduled for 8 October 2026.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Revenue £71.8m, down 0.4% vs £72.1m prior year.
  • Cost of sales up 11.1% to £33.3m, driving Gross Profit down 8.5% to £38.6m.
  • Administrative expenses essentially flat at £6.75m (modest saving).
  • Net finance costs reduced over 20% to £3.97m (£4.97m prior), reflecting lower borrowings and disciplined purchasing.
  • Profit Before Tax £27.1m, down 13.4% vs £31.3m.
  • EPS 522.1p, down 13.3% vs 602.5p.
  • Final dividend maintained at 275p, total dividend held at 525p (yield ~6% at current share price); payable 17 August 2026, ex-div 9 July, record date 10 July.
  • Net assets per share broadly flat at £103.2 (vs £103.3).
  • Major balance sheet deleveraging: long-term borrowings cut from £78.7m to £18.4m; however short-term loans/overdrafts rose from £1.4m to £39.0m (refinancing/reclassification rather than net debt reduction of that scale).
  • Inventories of trading properties reduced by £23.8m to £443.0m, driving strong cash generation from operations of £55.8m (vs £14.6m).
  • Net cash from operating activities £43.6m vs £1.9m prior year.
  • Net borrowings repaid £21.3m during the year (vs £12.2m increase prior year).
  • Investment property fair value decrease of £0.7m (vs negligible prior); no disposal gains this year (vs £0.9m prior).
  • No share buybacks; equity base essentially unchanged at £402.5m.
  • CEO Duncan Sinclair commentary notably political: "operating in the housing market under this government is not easy" and shareholders "should not be sacrificed at the altar of the government's incompetence."
  • Capital allocation discipline reiterated: "We are in a good position to make the right purchases at the right price but we will not make purchases that will struggle to make a proper return."
  • AGM scheduled 12 August 2026.

body:
• Selected by Norfolk County Council to design and build new road infrastructure for the West Winch Housing scheme near King's Lynn.
  • Contract is a significant part of the overall £122m scheme; design starts this year, construction expected to commence in 2027.
  • Scope: new link road connecting the A47 with the A10 serving up to 4,000 new homes, new roundabouts, a cycle path, and dualling of just under one mile of the A47 trunk road.
  • Positioned as aligned with Government's growth mission supporting housing, data centres and industrial development.
  • Reinforces existing east of England footprint: Eastern Highways Alliance Framework (11 local authorities), Anglian Water Strategic Pipeline Alliance, 1,625km of Cadent gas mains upgrades (168,000 homes), and Stansted consultancy work.
  • CEO Alex Vaughan: "This award is built on our long-established highways delivery expertise and our proven track record in delivering best-in-class, value-for-money services for local authorities across the UK."
  • CEO added the project "will significantly improve people's lives and help support the local economy in the east of England."
  • No contract value disclosed for Costain's specific scope, and no financial guidance impact stated.

body:
• Gross Development Value tripled to USD 23bn in FY2025 (FY2024: USD 7.5bn), spanning 5 major master-planned communities and branded residences.
  • Revenue +124% to USD 539m; EBITDA at record USD 126m.
  • ~6,100 units under construction across 16 projects.
  • Balance sheet: USD 702m cash & equivalents at Dec 2025, plus newly secured USD 250m financing facility for growth.
  • Geographic footprint now covers Saudi Arabia, UAE, Oman, Qatar, Spain, Greece and the UK; investor base from 125+ nationalities.
  • Diversification into new verticals: luxury hotels under development in Dubai, Riyadh, Muscat and the Maldives; golf destinations and private members' clubs being built across international markets.
  • Launched Dar Global Capital Partners (asset management platform) and advancing real estate tokenization initiatives.
  • Brand partnerships include Trump Organization, Lamborghini, Aston Martin, Pagani, FENDI, ELIE SAAB, Missoni, Mouawad, Marriott Residences, W Hotels and Nickelodeon Hotels.
  • First GCC-based company upgraded to LSE's ESCC category — flagged as evidence of governance/transparency standards.
  • Forward pipeline: master communities planned in Muscat, Riyadh, Jeddah, Southern Spain, plus an additional large-scale community in Greece.
  • CEO Ziad El Chaar: "What began as a luxury real estate developer has evolved into a diversified platform spanning real estate, hospitality, golf, private clubs, asset management and emerging real estate technologies."
  • CEO outlook: "We believe we are only at the beginning of our growth journey."

body:
• Terry Duffy to step down as CEO on 1 March 2027, transitioning to Executive Chairman after 25+ years at the helm (Chairman since 2002, CEO since 2016).
  • President and CFO Lynne Fitzpatrick will be appointed CEO and join the Board on the same date; she has been at CME since 2006 and CFO since 2023.
  • No interim CFO successor announced in this release.
  • Context cited: ADV of 28.1 million contracts last year and market cap >$95bn, up >8,000% since the 2002 IPO under Duffy.
  • Duffy quote: "I am pleased our company is so well positioned and have never been more optimistic about its future potential."
  • Duffy on Fitzpatrick: "With more than 20 years of strategic and financial expertise and strong leadership abilities, Lynne is the right person at the right time."
  • Fitzpatrick quote: "I look forward to working with our investors, clients and employees around the world as we grow our core business and create value for our shareholders."
  • No changes to financial guidance, capital return, or strategy disclosed — succession is internal and pre-announced ~9 months ahead, signalling continuity.
type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'ceo' with change verb 'step down' in opening bullets

body:
• Launched GenStudio for Commerce Media Networks, a new offering targeting retailers' commerce media ad networks (one of the fastest-growing ad channels), enabling brands to spin up campaigns by auto-building profiles/creative from product listings, website content and category context, activated inside the retailer's ad network.
  • Native integration with Adobe Real-Time CDP Collaboration plus a LiveRamp partnership will link retailer purchase data into campaign targeting and measurement.
  • Positioned as part of Adobe CX Enterprise, a new end-to-end agentic AI system covering the full customer lifecycle — signalling continued push to bundle GenAI across the CX stack.
  • New Simulate Skill in Adobe Brand Intelligence: simulates audience reactions using synthetic audiences modelled on real customer data, allowing creative testing pre-spend.
  • New MCP integration in Adobe Workfront connecting to LLM apps for agentic campaign/task management.
  • Firefly Graph for enterprise launched within Creative Cloud for Enterprise (Edition 5) — node-based visual canvas for automated creative workflows mixing Adobe and third-party AI models.
  • Firefly Custom Models now available in Photoshop for on-brand image generation.
  • GenStudio for Performance Marketing adds a cross-channel insights dashboard with CTV via new MNTN integration, plus Ad Recommendations and forthcoming Next Best Creative auto-assembly.
  • New Unified Campaign Metadata Service to standardise asset records across planning, creation and activation.
  • Varun Parmar (SVP/GM, Adobe GenStudio and Firefly Enterprise): "The volume and quality of content brands need to produce has outpaced what teams can realistically deliver… GenStudio closes that gap by giving teams the tools to produce content that engages their customers wherever they are."
  • Context: announcement lands as ADBE shares closed -4.62% at $197.74, reflecting ongoing market scepticism on Adobe's GenAI monetisation versus competitive pressure from OpenAI/Canva/Figma — today's release is squarely aimed at the enterprise commerce media and retail-media-network monetisation angle rather than a financial update.

  • Following hedging arrangements announced on 23 March 2026, ~90% of the new Bank Hapoalim facility carries an all-in interest rate of 5.853%, with the remaining ~10% floating.
  • Portfolio context: £2.2bn portfolio valued December 2025 by Savills and ZANE, primarily prime freehold and long leasehold assets in Europe.
  • Note: announcement references Rule 29 of the Takeover Code, flagging that asset valuation reports would be required in the event of a firm offer being announced for PPHE — consistent with the group being in an active bid situation.

body:
• Q2 2026 EPS guidance of $3.51–$3.55 diluted, vs Q1 2026 $2.78 (+~26% QoQ) and Q2 2025 $2.01 (+~75% YoY).
  • Guidance includes a $16m hit from asset write-downs tied to relocating the planned second satellite aluminum recycled slab center from Arizona to Columbus, Mississippi, after "differences with Arizona state officials risked the construction and operations of the facility."
  • Steel operations: profitability expected "meaningfully higher" QoQ, driven by metal margin expansion as realized selling prices rose more than scrap costs; order activity strong, low steel inventories supporting pricing; demand led by non-residential construction, energy, automotive, industrial.
  • Metals recycling: earnings similar to Q1, as higher ferrous/non-ferrous shipments offset by expected non-ferrous unrealized hedging losses.
  • Steel fabrication: earnings "incrementally below" Q1 — stronger shipments and steady pricing offset by higher steel raw material input costs.
  • Fabrication order backlog nearly 40% higher YoY, extending through end of 2026 and into 2027; demand from commercial construction, data centers, warehouses, manufacturing, healthcare.
  • Further volume improvement expected into 2027, supported by domestic manufacturing investment, US infrastructure spend, stimulus and onshoring.
  • Aluminum operations: earnings expected to "improve significantly" QoQ on higher shipments and realized pricing.
  • Columbus, MS aluminum flat rolled mill ramp: 2 of 3 cold mills operational, third to begin qualifying material in July; first CASH line (auto products) operating and shipping qualification material; second CASH line qualifications slated for Q4 2026.
  • Buybacks: $170m repurchased QTD in Q2 2026, ~0.5% of shares outstanding.
  • Q2 2026 earnings release scheduled for after market close on July 20, 2026; call July 21 at 11:00 a.m. EDT.

body:
• Launched Adobe Brand Visibility, a unified Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) solution combining Semrush's AI visibility intelligence with Adobe LLM Optimizer, sold standalone or integrated with Adobe Experience Manager.
  • Part of Adobe CX Enterprise, the new end-to-end agentic AI customer lifecycle system.
  • First major product integration following the close of the Semrush acquisition; management says "early customer demand has exceeded expectations."
  • Draws on ~300 million real-world AI search prompts (claimed largest global database), plus Semrush's 28.5bn keywords and 43tn backlinks corpus.
  • Covers brand presence across ChatGPT, Google AI Mode, Microsoft Copilot and Perplexity AI, with mention frequency, audience reach, share-of-voice and content gap analytics.
  • Adobe data cited to justify the opportunity: AI traffic to US retail sites up 1,324% and travel up 2,215% between Oct 2024 and May 2026.
  • Anil Chakravarthy (President, Customer Experience Orchestration): "In a world where customers often interact with an AI tool before ever reaching a business's website, visibility is everything now."
  • Functional scope: visibility insights, prompt strategy, competitive brand comparison, auto-optimization deployable "in minutes," and SEO intelligence, with outputs tied to bookings/pipeline/revenue via Adobe analytics integrations.
  • Strategic read-through: positions Adobe's Digital Experience segment directly in the GEO/AI-search monetization layer, leveraging Semrush to defend against AI-driven disruption of traditional SEO/web traffic funnels.

body:
• Entered a development management agreement (DMA) with Tritax Management LLP to deliver a 125MW data centre scheme at Chelmsford, Essex — the second site in its data centre pipeline.
  • Structure mirrors the existing DMA for the first data centre at Manor Farm, Heathrow; Manager handles planning, construction oversight, pre-letting, technical/electrical and power-related elements.
  • Manager economics: c.£3.3m payment for project assembly services to date; development management fee of up to 5% of development cost (contingent on planning consent); and a 17.5% profit share on total development profits, contingent on full delivery of a practically completed and let asset.
  • 50% of the Manager's profit share will be reinvested into BBOX shares (subject to a 12-month lock-up) — aligns Manager with shareholders.
  • Development fee phasing: 3.5% payable quarterly from grant of planning permission; 1.5% payable on the later of planning consent or exchange of an acceptable pre-letting agreement.
  • Targeted yield on cost of 10–11% for the scheme, inclusive of payments to the Manager.
  • Classified as a related party transaction under UKLR 11.5.4R; Board advised by Jefferies (sponsor) that terms are fair and reasonable for shareholders.
  • Context: BBOX's "power first" strategy has now secured >250MW of data centre development opportunities, with a pipeline of c.1GW of further opportunities.

body:
• Revenue +11.4% to £1,266.6m (FY25: £1,137.5m), including full-year musicMagpie contribution.
  • B2C Retail revenue +9.5% to £911.0m, with market share gains across all key categories.
  • Adjusted PBT +16.1% to a record £50.5m; statutory PBT +145% to £50.5m (no adjusting items this year vs £22.9m prior).
  • Adjusted PBT margin c.4%, progressing towards medium-term 5% target.
  • Gross margin +0.7ppts to 25.0%, helped by Mobile pivot to profit and full year of musicMagpie.
  • Basic EPS 6.36p (vs 1.70p); adjusted basic EPS 6.36p vs 5.70p.
  • Balance sheet swung from £35.9m net debt to £16.4m net funds (£52.3m improvement); total liquidity £201.3m; £120m RCF undrawn to Oct 2028.
  • Free cash flow £66.4m vs £26.3m (+152%); operating cash flow £95.7m vs £58.0m.
  • New £20m shareholder return announced: £10m special dividend + £10m new buyback (following £10.1m buyback completed in March 2026).
  • FY27 outlook: confident in delivering PBT in line with current market expectations (as at 28 May 2026); reiterates 5% PBT margin medium-term target.
  • Segment revenue mix: B2B -11.9% to £103.0m (deliberate profit focus, small single-digit decline expected in FY27); Mobile -18.4% to £77.0m (volume sacrificed for profitability); Recommerce +180.5% to £119.5m (musicMagpie full year); 3PL +9.2%; Recycling +6.8%.
  • MDA market share +1ppt to 17.1%; 720k new customers added; total customer base 13.3m.
  • First retailer globally to exceed 1m Trustpilot reviews at 4.9/5.
  • Mobile business now profitable; improved network commercial terms agreed in principle.
  • musicMagpie now run-rate profitable on annualised basis (from c.£6m loss-making at acquisition 15 months ago), aided by US exit, warehouse consolidation, and Timpson trade-in partnership (1,300+ stores).
  • Switch24 launched H2 FY26 (initial iPhone supply constraints); AO Mobile MVNO launched post year-end in friendly user trials, full launch in Q1 FY27.
  • Offshoring of inbound sales to South Africa delivered c.£2m savings (c.£4m annualised); majority of customer engagement to be offshore by end FY27.
  • SG&A +14.9% to £266.7m (21.1% of revenue vs 20.4%); advertising +20.4% driven by PPC +£8.4m, partly offset by -£4m Mobile acquisition spend; warehousing +21.1% on labour inflation and musicMagpie.
  • National Insurance/NMW changes added c.£8.5m to Group operating costs YoY; 80% of fuel hedged to March 2027, electricity fixed to October 2027.
  • FY27 capex/lease investment guided to c.£29m (vs lower current levels), including 360 new vehicles (c.£22m lease-funded), ERP, and recycling expansion.
  • ERP spend £3.8m in FY26 (vs £2.0m); WMS upgrade targeted for FY28 completion.
  • Covenants: interest cover ≥4x, leverage ≤2.5x net debt/EBITDA; breach risk deemed remote.
  • CEO John Roberts: "These results represent an incredible team effort with revenue up 11% to £1.27 billion, profit up 16% to a record £50.5 million, and the strongest balance sheet in our history. And all delivered against a backdrop of rising costs."
  • Roberts on outlook: "I started AO 26 years ago with a small team and a big idea and I'm as excited about the next 26 as I've ever been."
  • Outlook caveat: "the external environment remains uncertain, with ongoing geopolitical volatility and continued inflationary pressures impacting both consumers and input costs."

body:
• Skilled Person Review undertaken following FCA engagement identified areas for improvement in UK Wealth Management around Consumer Duty implementation, compliance, oversight and assurance.
  • Two-year remediation programme to be conducted addressing review recommendations.
  • Targeted review of a portion of clients to assess whether they received good outcomes.
  • Voluntary pause of up to 12 months on onboarding new Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) clients — relevant gross inflows from EDD clients totalled ~£370m in the last 12 months.
  • Voluntary pause on accepting inflows into general investment accounts from some existing EDD clients, affecting ~4,700 clients (4% of 119,000) with related gross inflows of ~£530m over the last 12 months.
  • Estimated £60m of costs (net of insurance recoveries) to be recognised as non-underlying expenses over the next two years.
  • Dividend policy unchanged; previously announced £20m share buyback approved by PRA and will commence shortly.
  • Pricing review underway: ceasing investment management fees on cash balances within discretionary portfolios from 1 July, expected to reduce 2026 underlying PBT by ~£9m.
  • CEO Jonathan Sorrell: "We are committed to operating to the highest standards on behalf of our clients. The work we are undertaking will support and accelerate our vision to be the best wealth manager in the UK, by far. Our strategy is unchanged and we continue to make strong progress against the plan set out in February. I am grateful for the constructive engagement with the FCA, and the continued trust of our clients as we implement these improvements."
  • Shares closed -17.01% at 1,620p on the day of the announcement.
  • Note: this is a reissue of the 07:00 RNS solely to confirm inside information status under UK MAR; all other content unchanged.

body:
• ANV Group Holdings to acquire Open Lending at $3.15/share in all-cash tender offer, taking the company private and delisting from Nasdaq.
  • Price represents a ~78% premium to the 90-day VWAP as of 15 June 2026.
  • Shares reacted accordingly, trading up +48% to ~$3.11 intraday on the announcement.
  • Deal unanimously approved by Open Lending's Board; expected to close Q3 2026, subject to regulatory approvals and majority share tender condition.
  • Second-step merger will mop up untendered shares at the same $3.15 price.
  • Acquirer ANV is an independent global insurance intermediary platform formed in 2025 via a transaction between AmTrust Financial Services and Blackstone Credit & Insurance, operating across US, UK and Europe.
  • Strategic rationale for ANV: expands US footprint and reinforces credit as a core insurance product; fits its insurance-backed credit strategy and M&A-led growth approach.
  • Rationale for Open Lending: access to capital, distribution, and stability after prolonged share price weakness.
  • CEO Jessica Buss: "This transaction delivers compelling and immediate value for our stockholders while providing Open Lending with the capital, stability, and strategic support to accelerate product innovation, deepen relationships with our financial institution partners, and drive sustainable growth over the long term."
  • ANV CEO Adam Karkowsky: "This transaction directly advances our insurance-backed credit strategy, and we see significant value creation ahead… Open Lending will continue to serve its customers and distribution partners as it does today, while benefiting from ANV's platform and resources."
  • Advisors: Financial Technology Partners (financial) and Jones Day (legal) to Open Lending; Evercore (financial) and Paul, Weiss (legal) to ANV.
  • Tender offer vehicle named Lakers Acquisition Sub, Inc. (ANV subsidiary).

body:
• Update on the 12 May 2026 Rule 2.4 indicative all-share offer for Picton; confirmatory due diligence "advancing well" and documentation being finalised with a view to a Rule 2.7 firm offer announcement.
  • Proposed consideration unchanged: 0.190 LondonMetric shares + 0.881 SREIT shares per Picton share.
  • Based on 15 June 2026 prices (LMP 185.2p, SREIT 47.3p), offer values Picton at £396m / 76.9p per share, a 9.0% premium to Picton's 15 June close and split 46% LondonMetric / 54% SREIT.
  • Implied earnings accretion for Picton holders of 37.7% on a pro-forma basis (using FY26 for Picton/LMP and LTM Dec-25 for SREIT).
  • Dividend income uplift of 46.2% for Picton shareholders (vs Picton Q4 2026 declared dividend of 0.95p).
  • 5.8% discount to Picton GAV (EPRA NIY 4.7%) and 8.6% discount to EPRA NTA.
  • Joint statement from Andrew Jones (LMP CEO) and Nick Montgomery (SREIT FM): "Since announcing our proposal on 12th of May, we have made steady progress and are advancing towards a Rule 2.7 announcement… material and immediate uplift in dividend income for Picton shareholders, while allocating assets to the platforms that we believe are best positioned to unlock their full value".
  • Asset allocation aligned with Picton debt structure:
  • LondonMetric to take Canada Life-secured pool: 22 assets, £320m, 81% industrial / 12% office, NIY 4.7%; plus ~£24m of Picton net cash.
  • SREIT to take Aviva/NatWest/uncharged pool: 22 assets, £380m, 55% industrial / 29% office / 12% retail warehouse, NIY 4.7%.
  • Pro-forma SREIT net LTV ~32% (within 25–35% target range); sub-sector mix largely unchanged.
  • Deal is marginally earnings accretive for LondonMetric and earnings accretive for SREIT in year one.
  • SREIT manager fee concessions: 10bps IMA fee reduction across all tiers, revised tiering 50% NAV / 50% lower of NAV and market cap, plus 1-year IMA fee waiver (spread over 24 months) on Picton NAV share, lowering pro-forma EPRA cost ratio below both standalone levels.
  • Succession planning underway for Nick Montgomery as SREIT fund manager; he remains committed and will lead through transition, retaining oversight as Chair of Schroders' direct real estate Investment Committee; Bradley Biggins continues as Co-Fund Manager.
  • Shareholder support: letter of intent from TR Property Investment Trust (Picton's largest holder, 11.4%, 58.43m shares) to vote in favour; TR reiterated support in its 10 June 2026 final results.
  • Lender support: Canada Life (£129.0m facility) and Aviva (£79.0m facility) have indicated preliminary support and willingness to grant pre-emptive change-of-control waivers, subject to credit approval.
  • Permitted dividends: Picton's 0.95p Q4 dividend (period ending 31 March 2026) plus future ordinary-course dividends covered by cash earnings; any excess triggers consideration reduction or equalisation payment.
  • Pre-conditions to Rule 2.7: completion of due diligence, lender consents/waivers, LMP & SREIT board approvals, and unanimous Picton board recommendation.
  • Consortium not subject to Rule 2.6(a) 28-day PUSU deadline while participating in Picton's formal sale process (Panel dispensation).
  • Deal expected to be implemented via Guernsey scheme of arrangement; no LMP or SREIT shareholder vote required for new share issuance.
  • Track records cited: SREIT EPS CAGR 7.0% and DPS CAGR 5.2% (FY20–FY25); LondonMetric EPS CAGR 7.2% and DPS CAGR 7.6% (FY21–FY26), fully covered.
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Mark Dixon, founder and CEO, transitions to Executive Chair; will continue providing strategic guidance to the Board and act as adviser to the CEO.
  • Christian Schmitz appointed CEO, joining the board on 16 June 2026; he joined IWG last year as Chief Transformation Officer, then became Global Head of all Regions.
  • Douglas Sutherland moves from Non-Executive Chair to Deputy Chair, overseeing Board effectiveness through the transition.
  • Schmitz background: former McKinsey partner (San Francisco), Director at KKR Capstone, and six-year CEO of KKR portfolio company Selecta (European food tech/workplace services).
  • Board frames the move as an "orderly CEO transition process" designed to retain Dixon's long-term strategic perspective.
  • Trading update embedded: "The business continues to perform in line with expectations" — no change to guidance.
  • Network now stands at 6,000 locations open and in the pipeline across more than 120 countries (vs ~120 countries previously); ~83% of Fortune 500 are customers.
  • Dixon on outlook: "demand for flexible workspaces continues to grow worldwide, the business is exceptionally well positioned to capitalise on this trend."
  • Schmitz on ambition: "I am energised by the scale of what we can achieve... drive our most ambitious phase of growth yet."
  • Announcement flagged as containing inside information under MAR.

body:
• Mark Clare appointed Independent Non-executive Chair effective 1 July 2026.
  • Mark Allen transitions from Executive Chair to Group Chief Executive on the same date, confirming the succession flagged on 31 March 2026.
  • Clare's relevant board experience includes Chair of Grainger plc (2017–Feb 2026), Chair of Ricardo plc (2022–2025), and current Non-exec roles at Wickes Group and Drax Group; executive background includes CEO of Barratt Developments (2006–2015) and CFO of Centrica (1997–2002).
  • Allen on the strategic direction: "implement our clear plan arising from our strategic review to focus the business on its core capabilities and deliver sustainable long-term growth."
  • Clare framed the agenda around "three growth levers, disciplined capital allocation and updated medium-term targets" with focus to "maximise the business' core, enhance its mix and expand geographically."
  • CEO remuneration: Allen base salary £850,000, annual bonus opportunity 150% of salary, LTIP 175% of salary; eligible for 2026 bonus and LTIP awards.
  • Chair fee: Clare to receive £250,000 per annum.

• Appointment of Sharmila Nebhrajani OBE as independent Non-Executive Director, effective 29 June 2026.
  • Will sit on Audit and Risk, Remuneration, Nomination and ESG Committees.

  • Current NED roles: Halma plc (Audit Committee Chair), Severn Trent plc (Remuneration Committee Chair) and ITV plc; also Chair of NICE and director of Genomics England.
  • Chair Steve Good: "We are delighted to have secured Sharmila's extensive experience in a number of sectors which will make a major contribution to the Board and Company and support our growth strategy."
  • Nebhrajani: "I look forward to working with Steve and the Essentra Board to help drive growth and to create value for all stakeholders."

body:
• Update on the joint Consortium (LondonMetric + Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust) non-binding indicative all-share offer for Picton Property Income (originally announced 12 May 2026); confirmatory due diligence is "advancing well" with transaction docs being finalised ahead of a Rule 2.7 firm offer announcement.
  • Proposed consideration unchanged: 0.190 LondonMetric shares + 0.881 SREIT shares per Picton share, implying 76.9p per Picton share and valuing Picton equity at £396m (based on 15 June closing prices of LMP 185.2p, SREIT 47.3p).
  • Implied premium of 9.0% to Picton's 15 June close; 5.8% discount to GAV and 8.6% discount to EPRA NTA; EPRA NIY of 4.7%.
  • Split: LondonMetric takes 46% of Picton's assets, SREIT takes 54%, mirroring underlying interests.
  • For Picton holders: earnings accretion of 37.7% pro-forma and dividend uplift of 46.2% (vs Picton Q4 2026 DPS of 0.95p) based on LMP Q1 2027 target DPS 3.15p and SREIT Q4 2026 DPS 0.90p.
  • For acquirers: marginal EPS accretion for LondonMetric, EPS accretive for SREIT in year one.
  • LondonMetric acquires the Canada Life-financed portfolio: 22 properties valued at £320m (31 March 2026), 81% Industrial / 12% Office / 3% Retail Warehouse / 4% Retail & Other; NIY 4.7%; top assets include Parkbury Industrial Estate (Radlett), Datapoint (London), Sundon Business Park (Luton).
  • LondonMetric also acquires Picton's ~£24m net cash on completion.
  • SREIT acquires the Aviva/NatWest-financed and uncharged assets: 22 properties valued at £380m, 55% Industrial / 29% Office / 12% Retail Warehouse / 4% Retail & Other; NIY 4.7%; SREIT pro-forma net LTV ~32% (within 25–35% target range).
  • SREIT manager (Schroders) to cut IMA fees by 10bps across all tiers, revised tiering 50% NAV / 50% lower of NAV and market cap, plus one-year IMA fee waiver (spread over 24 months) on the Picton NAV share.
  • Succession planning underway for SREIT Fund Manager Nick Montgomery; he remains in role through transition, with Bradley Biggins as Co-Fund Manager.
  • Lender support: Canada Life (£129m facility) and Aviva (£79m facility) have indicated preliminary willingness to grant pre-emptive change of control waivers, subject to credit approval.
  • Shareholder support: Letter of intent from TR Property Investment Trust (Picton's largest holder, 11.4% / 58.4m shares) to vote in favour; TR reiterated support at its 10 June 2026 results.
  • Permitted dividends: Picton's 0.95p Q4 dividend retained; future ordinary course dividends permitted only if covered by cash earnings, else Consortium reserves right to cut exchange ratios.
  • No LondonMetric or SREIT shareholder approval required for the new share issuance; transaction expected via Guernsey scheme of arrangement.
  • Pre-conditions to Rule 2.7: completion of DD, lender consents, LMP/SREIT board approvals, and unanimous Picton Board recommendation.
  • Joint statement from Andrew Jones (LMP CEO) and Nick Montgomery (SREIT FM): "we have made steady progress and are advancing towards a Rule 2.7 announcement. The transaction is expected to deliver a material and immediate uplift in dividend income for Picton shareholders, while allocating assets to the platforms that we believe are best positioned to unlock their full value and providing exposure to two enlarged, more liquid and complementary UK-listed REITs."

body:
• Adjusted EBITDA £53.3m, up 34.6% YoY (margin 5.8% vs 3.3%), following two trading upgrades during the year; every brand now profitable at Adjusted EBITDA level.
  • Group GMV (pre returns) fell 21.6% to £1,820.7m as focus shifted to profitable sales; GMV improved sequentially each quarter through FY26.
  • Revenue down 24.7% to £917.0m — fall greater than GMV post-returns (-19.7%) due to marketplace mix shift (commission-only recognition).
  • Gross margin +40bps to 51.1% — first improvement since FY22, driven by marketplace sales recognised at 100% margin.
  • Debenhams brand: GMV +11.6% to £730m (now largest brand in Group); Adjusted EBITDA +38.5% to £34.8m (65.3% of Group EBITDA); marketplace scaled to 25,000+ partner brands (from c.15,000); 5.7m active customers; FY22-FY26 GMV CAGR 53.4%.
  • PrettyLittleThing turnaround: £15m swing to £14.0m Adjusted EBITDA profit (FY25: £1m loss); Board decided in January 2026 to retain PLT within continuing operations; FY25 restated on that basis.
  • Marketplace mix rose to 34.1% of GMV (FY25: 23.3%); marketplace GMV +14.9% to £620.4m.
  • Returns rate improved 160bps to 28.2%, aided by marketplace mix and product fit investment.
  • Operating costs -28.2% to £415.4m; fixed cost exit rate cut to £119m (vs £130m guided, FY25: £175m); on track for £100m exit rate in FY27 — c.£200m cumulative reduction by new management.
  • Adjusted EBIT returned to profit at £7.0m (FY25: £30.5m loss); Loss before tax narrowed 69.2% to £108.6m; statutory loss after tax down £218.1m to £108.3m; exceptionals fell to £68.1m (FY25: £271.7m).
  • Free cash flow improved to -£18.4m (FY25: -£40.2m); capex reduced to £16.0m (FY25: £27.5m).
  • Net debt £93.2m (1.75x Adjusted EBITDA) vs £78.2m; FY27 target <1x.
  • Refinanced in August 2025 to a new £175m facility maturing August 2028 (drawn £143.7m); completed oversubscribed £40m equity raise in February 2026 at 18p/share.
  • Headcount reduced to ~1,500 (from 6,189 in January 2024); warehouse consolidation into automated Sheffield DC delivering c.£33m recurring savings; single AI-powered tech platform saving c.£38m annually; 150+ contracts renegotiated for c.£35m savings.
  • Current trading: Q1 FY27 Group GMV +0.5% YoY, May 2026 ~+8%, June trading "continuing to be strong" — described as "a significant inflection point."
  • FY27 guidance: double-digit improvement in Adjusted EBITDA; net debt <1x EBITDA by year end; depreciation falling from £46.3m to ~£20m; capex ~£8m; lease payments ~£13m in FY27, ~£8m FY28, ~£6m FY29; exceptional costs expected to reduce materially; FY27 expected to deliver sustained free cash flow generation and a return to profit before tax.
  • US distribution centre sublease (Elizabethtown, PA) to ID Logistics agreed post period-end for remaining ~8.5 years: $9.5m (£7.1m) average annual rent income; ~$20m (£15m) of lease costs covered; triggers expected ~£40m non-cash exceptional credit in H1 FY27, strengthening net asset position.
  • Burnley warehouse classified as held for sale at £26.0m after £33.1m impairment; Units 1 & 3 sold post period-end for £5.0m in April 2026.
  • International expansion: Debenhams marketplace launched in US via Macy's, Bloomingdale's, Nordstrom and Amazon Fashion (~600m monthly shoppers); also live in Ireland and Australia.
  • Lost significant influence over Revolution Beauty on 19 Feb 2026; reclassified from associate to FVOCI investment (£8.5m carrying value); £3.5m fair value gain recognised, offset by £8.1m share of losses prior to reclassification.
  • No dividend recommended.
  • Inventory £105.3m (FY25: £72.2m underlying / £93.1m including held-for-sale) — net higher due to lower prior-year provisions; gross stock reduced in line with marketplace shift.
  • CEO Dan Finley: "This has been a year of significant and successful transformation for Debenhams Group...The turnaround is firmly on track...the best is yet to come."
  • Clear medium-term target: £1bn GMV and £50m+ EBITDA at Debenhams brand within three years.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives full-year period 'FY26 (year ended 28 February 2026)'

body:
• Skilled Person Review (following FCA engagement) identified areas for improvement in UK Wealth Management around Consumer Duty implementation/embedding and compliance, oversight and assurance arrangements.
  • Remediation programme expected to run over two years, alongside a targeted review of a portion of clients to assess outcomes.
  • Voluntary pause (up to 12 months) on onboarding new Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) clients; relevant gross inflows from such clients totalled ~£370m in the last 12 months.
  • Voluntary pause on inflows into general investment accounts from some existing EDD clients, affecting ~4,700 clients (4% of 119,000); related gross inflows in the last 12 months were ~£530m.
  • Estimated £60m of costs (net of insurance recoveries) to be recognised as non-underlying expenses over the next two years.
  • Dividend policy unchanged; previously announced £20m share buyback now PRA-approved and will commence shortly.
  • Pricing review underway: investment management fees on cash balances within discretionary portfolios to cease from 1 July, expected to reduce 2026 underlying PBT by ~£9m.
  • CEO Jonathan Sorrell: "We are committed to operating to the highest standards on behalf of our clients. The work we are undertaking will support and accelerate our vision to be the best wealth manager in the UK, by far. Our strategy is unchanged and we continue to make strong progress against the plan set out in February. I am grateful for the constructive engagement with the FCA, and the continued trust of our clients as we implement these improvements."

  • Purves background: most recently CEO of Aztec Group (previously Chair/NED); ex-Group CEO of IFG Group; former Chair of Risk Committee at Nucleus and ICG; former Chair of Redington and Saunderson House; ex-CRO of Partnership Assurance Group; investment roles at Phoenix Equity, Deutsche Bank, Partners and UBS Capital.
