body:
• Portfolio company Istesso has commenced dosing in IST-03, a Phase 2 randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of lead candidate leramistat in patients with sarcopenia caused by rheumatoid arthritis.
  • Trial evaluates effects on muscle quality, repair and function; sarcopenia currently has no specific pharmacological therapies available.
  • Builds on prior RA data showing leramistat improved disability/fatigue and reduced markers of muscle loss, plus preclinical data showing muscle recovery in aged animals to levels above younger baseline.
  • Leramistat is the lead asset from Istesso's novel Mitochondrial Complex I Modulators (MCMs) class, targeting adaptive tissue repair across muscle, bone, gut and lung.
  • Enrolment underway at Newcastle University and Newcastle Hospitals with NIHR support.
  • IP Group holds an undiluted 56.5% stake in Istesso.
  • CEO Greg Smith: "This marks an important inflection point for Istesso as it moves into Phase 2 evaluation of a genuinely novel approach to regenerative medicine. The programme is supported by a consistent body of clinical and preclinical data, and we believe the potential to influence underlying tissue repair represents a differentiated and scalable opportunity. Success here would be an important validation of the approach and open up multiple opportunities across large areas of unmet need."

body:
• Launching a US$10 million share buyback programme over 60 calendar days commencing 15 June 2026.
  • Board cites recent widening of the discount to NAV as rationale; views current share price as an attractive opportunity to increase shareholder exposure to uranium holdings at a discount to underlying value.
  • Buybacks will only occur when the prior day's closing mid-market price is at a discount of 10% or more to proforma NAV.
  • Operates under existing AGM authority (4 September 2025) permitting purchase of up to 21,685,645 Ordinary Shares.
  • Repurchased shares will be held in treasury; purpose stated as reducing issued share capital.
  • Price cap: higher of last independent trade/highest current independent bid, or 105% of 5-day average market value.
  • Canaccord Genuity appointed to execute purchases as riskless principal, making timing decisions independently.
  • Company describes itself as "well capitalised, with a strong balance sheet and significant liquidity".
  • Currently holds 23.11 million pounds of U3O8 stored in Canada and France.
  • CEO Andre Liebenberg: "We remain confident in the long-term outlook for uranium and continue to believe that Yellow Cake provides investors with a compelling means of gaining exposure to the commodity. At the current discount to NAV, we believe share repurchases represent an attractive opportunity to create value for shareholders and to increase their exposure to our uranium holdings at a discount to their underlying value."

body:
• Sold Harrow industrial estate for £38.4 million, with a £2 million retention released subject to certain conditions.
  • Proceeds will fund build-out of 11 new stores plus one replacement store on Group-owned land.
  • Pipeline of 12 stores expected to generate £35 million net operating income, an income return of 16.5% on £212 million total cost to complete.
  • Board states: "currently there is no other use for the capital that comes close to delivering this level of long term returns."
  • Development to be completed within existing funding arrangements — no new financing flagged.
  • Net debt/EBITDA guidance reiterated at 3.5x–4x short-to-medium term, trending to ≤3.5x preferred level.
  • Board reiterates: "the unwarranted risk from elevated levels of debt outweighs the potential rewards" — signals continued conservative leverage stance.
  • Pipeline totals 0.9 million sq ft, taking fully built-out portfolio to ~7.6 million sq ft (vs current 6.7 million sq ft lettable across 113 stores).
  • Several pipeline stores noted as being in key Central London locations; 75% of revenue currently from London and commuter towns.
  • Effectively a capital recycling signal: monetising non-core industrial asset to fund higher-returning core self-storage development rather than buybacks or special distributions.

body:
• Update on the £8.5m Scorpion Portfolio acquisition (completed 2 March 2026 via majority-share acquisition of Scorpion Properties Limited).
  • Portfolio performing in line with expectations with occupancy at 100%.
  • 31 March 2026 valuation 3.6% ahead of purchase price, described by management as demonstrating the accretive nature of the deal.
  • Sold the Coventry asset in June 2026 as non-core due to size; proceeds of £0.4m, in line with valuation and £0.1m ahead of allocated purchase price.
  • Remaining four properties sit within the UK's "Golden Triangle" for logistics, with management citing "significant potential for value creation through capturing latent rental growth at upcoming lease events."
  • Managing Director Richard Shepherd-Cross: "our innovative strategy of achieving growth by utilising our REIT status to acquire privately held portfolios through corporate acquisitions has proved an effective way to achieve scale alongside earnings enhancement."
  • Strategy to continue targeting family property companies facing succession and tax issues in the UK.
  • On completion (2 March 2026), 4,942,650 new ordinary shares issued as initial consideration.
  • Completion accounts now finalised, triggering issue of a further 314,370 new ordinary shares; Admission expected 8am on 18 June 2026, ranking pari passu.
  • Total voting rights post-Admission: 489,067,079.

body:
• Secured a c.£140m extension to its role on South West Water's Network Services Alliance (NSA), as sole contractor.
  • Extension runs to March 2028 (~2 years), continuing a relationship of more than two decades.
  • Scope covers repair and maintenance, leakage activity, network reliability schemes, metering and developer services.
  • Operational scale: over 25,000 repair/maintenance jobs and 32,000+ metering activities delivered in the last year.
  • Reinforces Kier's AMP8 water franchise, adding to existing appointments with Anglian Water, Severn Trent, Southern Water, Thames Water, United Utilities and Yorkshire Water — i.e. presence across 7 of the major UK water utilities.
  • Non-regulatory RNS Reach release; no financial guidance change disclosed.

body:
• Completed sublease of 1.1m sq. ft Elizabethtown, Pennsylvania distribution centre to ID Logistics, a global 3PL operator.
  • The DC opened August 2023, operated for ~15 months as a manual (non-automated) facility, and was closed on 11 November 2024 with US fulfilment returning to the UK.
  • Group has incurred ~$124m of costs at the site to date (rent, opex, capex).
  • Lease has ~8.5 years remaining, representing ~$100m of future lease and holding costs now materially mitigated.
  • ID Logistics to commence occupation 1 August 2026 through to end of Group's lease.
  • Unaudited non-cash exceptional credit of ~£40m to the income statement (recognition of asset for future sublease payments), to be reflected in H1 results subject to audit.
  • Average annual rent income under the Sublease of $9.5m; additional ~$20m of lease-related costs covered under sublease terms.
  • Lease cost trajectory: £13m FY26 → £8m FY28 → £6m FY29; the £6m run-rate covers automated Sheffield warehouse, Manchester HQ and small London footprint.
  • CEO Dan Finley: "This is a significant development. The US DC was a major contributor to the challenges that the company has faced. One of my first actions in role was to close the US DC and the sublease of it mitigates a material future liability. Our turnaround strategy continues at pace."
  • Reinforces transition to an asset-light operating model as part of ongoing turnaround.

• Chair Danuta Gray appointed as a Non-Executive Director of Lloyds Banking Group plc with effect from 1 July 2026.
  • Concurrently becomes a member of Lloyds' Responsible Business Committee and Chair and Non-Executive Director of Scottish Widows Group (Lloyds subsidiary).
  • No further matters to disclose under UK Listing Rule 6.4.9.

body:
• Record revenue of $6.62bn, +13% YoY (+11% cc), beating prior guidance midpoint.
  • Subscription revenue $6.42bn vs $5.64bn YoY; product $89m; services $113m.
  • GAAP diluted EPS $4.25; Non-GAAP EPS $5.96 (+17.8% YoY vs $5.06); GAAP hit by $0.17/share non-cash goodwill impairment tied to Publishing & Advertising unit.
  • AI-first ARR tripled YoY and exceeded $500m — key inflection on AI monetisation.
  • Total ARR $27.10bn exiting quarter, including ~$480m from Semrush.
  • GAAP operating income $2.24bn; Non-GAAP operating income $2.95bn (margin ~44.5%).
  • Operating cash flow $2.17bn (broadly flat YoY).
  • RPO $22.27bn; cRPO 67% of total.
  • Repurchased ~8.5m shares ($2.11bn cash outflow on buybacks in the quarter).
  • Segment growth: Business Professionals & Consumers subs revenue $1.85bn, +16% YoY (+15% cc); Creative & Marketing Professionals subs revenue $4.54bn, +13% YoY (+11% cc).
  • CFO Dan Durn departing 15 June 2026 to pursue a new opportunity; Steve Day (SVP Corporate Finance, 20-year Adobe veteran) named interim CFO reporting to CEO Narayen.
  • Balance sheet: cash & ST investments $5.63bn (down from $6.60bn at FY25 year-end); current debt jumped to $1.84bn (from $0) reflecting near-term maturity reclassification; goodwill rose to $14.04bn post Semrush; acquisitions consumed $1.56bn cash in quarter.
  • FY26 guidance raised: total revenue $26.50–26.60bn; GAAP EPS $17.90–18.00; Non-GAAP EPS $24.35–24.45; total ARR growth ~10.2%; non-GAAP operating margin ~45%.
  • Q3 FY26 guidance: revenue $6.67–6.72bn; Non-GAAP EPS $6.05–6.10; Non-GAAP operating margin ~44%.
  • CEO Shantanu Narayen: "Adobe delivered record revenue of $6.62 billion in Q2 reflecting strong AI-driven demand across our customer groups and we are raising our full-year fiscal 2026 revenue and non-GAAP EPS targets on the strength of that performance."
  • Context: shares closed -11.4% at $206.67 on results day, suggesting market reaction focused on the surprise CFO exit and/or AI competitive concerns rather than the raised guide.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q2 FY2026'

body:
• Paul F. De Cock, current President & COO, appointed CEO and Board director effective 30 September 2026.
  • Jeffrey S. Lorberbaum to retire as CEO after 25 years in the role (since January 2001) but remains Chairman of the Board (held since May 2004).
  • De Cock joined via the 2005 Unilin acquisition; held senior roles across laminate, wood, LVT and carpet; promoted to President & COO in February 2025 as part of formal succession planning.
  • Lead Independent Director John M. Engquist framed the move as enabling "a seamless transition of leadership."
  • De Cock's stated strategic priorities as CEO: "sharpening our focus, accelerating growth, and pursuing new pathways that will define our future."
  • No financial guidance, trading update or capital allocation changes accompanied the announcement.
  • Context: shares closed +8.16% at $110.05 on the day, suggesting market views the internal, telegraphed succession positively amid an ongoing weak flooring demand cycle.

body:
• Secured 12 new Fully Managed leasing deals totalling over 41,000 sq ft, delivering £8.0m of annual rent.
  • Average rent achieved of £195 per sq ft.
  • Lettings completed at an average of 3.7% ahead of March 2026 ERV, signalling continued pricing strength.
  • Builds on recent activity including pre-letting of over 13,000 sq ft at Elsley House, W1 ahead of launch, plus continued momentum at City Tower, EC2.
  • Next phase of Fully Managed pipeline progressing: The Courtyard, WC1 and The Howlett, W1, both under construction and due for completion in 2027.
  • Anthony Osho (Head of Flex Customer Relationships): "Securing over 41,000 sq ft of lettings ahead of ERV since March, demonstrates both the strength of demand for our Fully Managed offer and the quality of our customer relationships. By consistently delivering for our customers, we're driving repeat business, rental growth and sustained momentum across the portfolio."

body:
• Jonathan Murphy appointed Chief Executive and Executive Director, effective 1 September 2026.
  • Murphy was CEO of Assura plc (FTSE 250 healthcare REIT) from Feb 2017 to Sep 2025, having joined as Finance Director in Jan 2013; oversaw portfolio growth and diversification through to Assura's acquisition by Primary Health Properties in 2025.
  • Other relevant roles: British Property Federation director since 2019; EPRA Advisory Board 2018–2025.
  • Succeeds Paul Williams, who flagged retirement in January 2026; Williams steps down from the Board on 1 September 2026 and leaves the business on 22 January 2027 to support transition.
  • Remuneration: base salary £650,000, 15% pension allowance (in line with wider workforce), plus annual bonus and LTIP participation under the policy approved at the 15 May 2026 AGM.
  • Chairman Mark Breuer framed the strategic priority: *"continue to recycle capital through disposals and position the business to deliver earnings growth and long-term returns for shareholders."*
  • Murphy on the opportunity: *"The Group has a distinctive culture and brand, impressive property expertise, and a well-positioned portfolio which presents many opportunities to drive returns."*
  • Context: portfolio valued at £5.1bn as at 31 December 2025; remains the largest London office-focused REIT.

body:
• Return to earnings growth: Adjusted Diluted EPRA EPS 19.4p, +2.1%; underlying PBT £44.6m, +2.3%.
  • Total Revenue £120.6m, +6.9% (CER +5.6%).
  • LFL Revenue £117.3m, +5.0% (CER +3.5%) — positive LFL growth across all geographies.
  • Underlying EBITDAR £67.9m, +3.7% (CER +4.3%); underlying store EBITDAR +5.6% to £78.9m.
  • Statutory operating profit down 52.8% to £53.3m and statutory PBT down 62.6% to £36.3m — reflects £12.1m revaluation loss vs £49.5m gain in H1 2025 (non-cash, property valuation noise).
  • Geographic LFL split: UK +2.4% (revenue £83.9m, +3.3%); Paris +1.8% (€26.7m, +4.6%, occupancy hit by self-cannibalisation); Expansion Markets LFL +16.8% (€15.4m, +25.7%) — Spain LFL +21.2%, Netherlands +14.0%, Belgium +5.2%.
  • LFL REVPAF £29.08, +6.3% (CER +5.1%); LFL closing occupancy 77.0% (+0.4ppt).
  • UK partitioning programme: further 80k sq ft converted (total 280k of planned 500k); UK average LFL rate +6.5%.
  • Interim DPS 10.2p, +1.0% — progressive policy while rebuilding cover.
  • FY 2026 EPS guidance: at the lower end of consensus range (consensus 42.4p, range 41.5–43.7p), reflecting higher H2 interest rates.
  • Underlying net finance cost guidance raised: now expected to increase by £2–3m (vs prior) due to elevated floating rates.
  • LFL cost of sales growth now guided to lower end of 3–6% range.
  • FY 2026 new-store capex £86m; MLA additions 225k sq ft in H2, plus 733k sq ft in FY 2027+.
  • Incremental EBITDA from non-LFL + pipeline reduced to £30–35m (from £35–40m at FY25) — purely due to FY24 openings reclassifying into LFL, not a downgrade.
  • Pipeline: 17 stores / 1.0m sq ft remaining, total capex £192m (£103m remaining); one new site added since FY25.
  • Net debt £1,100.8m (+£42.2m YTD); LTV 29.1% (FY25: 28.1%); ICR 3.9x; investment property valuation £3.5bn.
  • Refinancing secured: new €150m USPP arranged (5.6-yr term, 4.10% coupon) to fund Oct 2026 maturities (€70m + £35m USPPs maturing, weighted coupon 1.75% — implies higher finance costs ahead).
  • Average cost of debt 3.34% (down 12bps from FY25); 60.6% fixed.
  • EPRA NTA 1,120p, -0.8% vs FY25 (FX-driven).
  • Exceptional £1.7m relates to new SaaS finance system implementation (further costs in H2).
  • Four stores opened in H1 (Paris x2, London, Madrid) adding 192k sq ft; on track for 10% yield-on-cost hurdle.
  • CEO Frederic Vecchioli: "the significant investment we have made in our expansion is now clearly translating into both revenue and earnings growth."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives half-year period 'H1 FY2026 (six months ended 30 April 2026)'

body:
• CFO Ben Guyatt to depart after 20 years with Forterra and its predecessor organisations, to pursue new challenges.
  • Guyatt will remain as CFO until 31 October 2026 to assist with an orderly transition.
  • Formal search process for a successor has commenced; further announcement to follow once appointment is confirmed.
  • CEO Neil Ash credited Guyatt with playing "an important role in both establishing the Group and its listing in 2016, as well as in the Group's continued growth and development since then."
  • Guyatt commented: "I am proud of what we have achieved and believe that Forterra is well-placed for future success. I remain fully committed to supporting the Group over the coming months to ensure a seamless transition."
  • No trading or guidance update provided alongside the announcement.

body:
• Net profit collapsed 99.4% to €1.3m (F25: €213.9m), distorted by absence of a €194.2m one-off F25 deferred tax credit; PBT actually rose 37.1% to €27.0m.
  • Revenue +8.0% to €5,691.4m; passenger ticket revenue +8.4% to €3,161.4m; ancillary +7.6% to €2,530.0m.
  • EBITDA +16.2% to €1,318.3m; EBITDA margin +1.6ppt to 23.2%.
  • Operating profit down 16.6% to €139.7m (margin 2.5% vs 3.2%) on higher D&A and maintenance from CEO fleet exit.
  • Passengers +10.0% to record 69.7m; ASKs +8.5%; load factor -0.5ppt to 90.7% (Iran war aftermath).
  • RASK -0.4% to €4.31c; ticket RASK flat at €2.39c; ancillary RASK -0.8% (hit by Abu Dhabi closure and Middle East cuts).
  • Total CASK +0.5% to €4.35c; ex-fuel CASK +5.8% to €3.02c; Q4 ex-fuel CASK +18.1% YoY due to absence of F25 one-off maintenance credit.
  • Fuel CASK -9.6% to €1.34c; avg jet fuel price -3.2% to $890/t; ETS costs +27.2% to €273.3m; €56.5m spent on SAF mandate.
  • D&A +21.9% to €1,178.6m; maintenance +40.1% to €462.8m; crew costs +16.1%; airport/handling +13.0%.
  • Iran conflict in March 2026 risked c.€50m earnings hit, largely offset by pre-existing fuel hedges.
  • Total cash +22.5% to €2,126.4m; net debt broadly flat at €4,941.5m; leverage 3.7x vs 4.4x; liquidity 35.8% (vs 31.5%).
  • €500m 1.00% Eurobond repaid from own cash in Jan 2026; €3bn EMTN programme renewed for 3 years.
  • Fitch rating raised to BB stable; Moody's at Ba2 negative.
  • GTF AOG down to 30 aircraft (vs 42 at F25 end); 24 as of 5 June 2026; guided to 15-20 by end-F27 and zero by end-CY27.
  • Fleet 262 aircraft (+13.4%); avg age 4.5 years (youngest major European fleet); 75% NEO share.
  • 88 A321neo deliveries deferred out of this decade, extended to F33; backlog 254 aircraft.
  • All 11 A321XLRs retained for own longer-range routes after discontinuing transfer discussions.
  • Abu Dhabi base closed in Q2; Vienna base wound down; capacity reallocated to CEE; CEE market share +1.1ppt to 25.3%.
  • New low-cost bases opened (Bucharest Baneasa, Bratislava, Podgorica, Suceava, Targu Mures, Tuzla, Warsaw Modlin, Yerevan); Palermo and Turin announced.
  • Tel Aviv flights resumed from most CEE bases on 28 May 2026; Ukraine resumption planned within weeks of any ceasefire.
  • No F27 guidance issued due to lack of visibility, Iran conflict and Strait of Hormuz closure.
  • H1 F27 indicators: ASKs +15% Q1/+20% Q2; seats +25% Q1/high-twenties% Q2; load factor flat; RASK down mid-to-high single digit in Q1, flattish in Q2; ex-fuel CASK flat to up low single digit.
  • Hedging (as of 29 May 2026): fuel 84% covered H1 F27 at $826/757, 71% H2 at $819/747; FX 81% H1 at $1.17/$1.13, 72% H2.
  • 83% of $4.5bn USD lease liability hedged via cash and cross-currency swaps.
  • Continued OEM compensation from Pratt & Whitney for GTF issues.
  • Disruption costs fell c.€29.9m on improved on-time performance.
  • CEO Váradi: "We will continue to focus on our core markets, restore full fleet utilisation as engine availability improves, maintain discipline in capacity growth and cost control, and further enhance the reliability and quality of our operations."
  • No dividend (continues retention policy to fund fleet).
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with half-year financial figures

body:
• Placed €185.1 million nominal of new notes via taps of two existing corporate bonds, taking each to €500.0 million benchmark size.
  • Tap 1: €150.0m added to existing €350.0m 4.000% notes due Jan 2032 (originally issued Jan 2025).
  • Tap 2: €35.1m added to existing €464.9m 1.750% notes due Nov 2028 (originally issued Nov 2021).
  • New notes priced in line with current trading levels of the respective existing bonds; described as well supported by the market.
  • Settlement expected 17 June 2026.
  • Proceeds earmarked for general corporate purposes and refinancing of existing debt.
  • Reaching €500m benchmark size expected to improve secondary market liquidity across Sirius' public debt platform.
  • Syndicate: HSBC sole structuring bank/global coordinator; Barclays, BNP Paribas, HSBC active bookrunners; ABN AMRO passive bookrunner; Lazard financial adviser.
  • CFO Chris Bowman: "These latest bond taps further demonstrate the continued capital markets support for our strategy and portfolio, as well as the value and income we are able to generate from our operating platform. Taking both of our public bonds to €500 million benchmark size is expected to improve secondary market liquidity, while the proceeds give us further financial flexibility to refinance existing debt and support further accretive growth. We maintain our disciplined approach to leverage."
  • Portfolio context (as of 31 March 2026): 145 assets, 11,736 tenants, ~€3.0bn book value, €258.6m annualised rent roll; plus 35% stake in Titanium JV with BNP Paribas AM Alts.

body:
• Record underlying PBT of £69.0m, up 1.5% YoY (FY25: £68.0m); underlying EBITDA £92.0m, +2.2%.
  • Revenue £337.0m, +8.5% (FY25: £310.7m); prior year included a £14.2m exceptional revenue deduction for the Utilita settlement.
  • Net revenue £190.8m, +1.7% (FY25: £187.7m).
  • Statutory PBT £55.5m, +111.0% (FY25: £26.3m), driven by lower adjusting items (£13.5m vs £41.7m).
  • Diluted underlying EPS 73.6p (+6.5%); diluted EPS 58.4p (+122.1%).
  • Final dividend 20.0p, +2.0% YoY; £60.8m total dividends paid in year (incl. 50p special dividend in October 2025 funded by Collect+ part-disposal).
  • Net corporate debt rose £35.1m to £132.5m (FY25: £97.4m), reflecting buybacks, dividends and capex; target leverage 1.2–1.5x net debt/EBITDA.
  • Over £90m returned to shareholders in FY26 via buybacks and dividends; buyback programme extended at £30m p.a. through FY27 and FY28.
  • Share count down 16.2% since March 2024 (72.7m → 60.8m); a further ~10m reduction targeted, taking cumulative reduction to ~30%; cumulative dividend cash savings projected at ~£17m by March 2028.
  • FY27 outlook: Board "remains confident in delivering further progress, exceeding the underlying profits achieved in FY26 and achieving results in line with market expectations", with profit weighted to H2.
  • New medium-term target: net revenue growth of 5–8% per annum.
  • Business reorganised into four units (announced 30 March 2026): Network Services, Digital Payments & Open Banking, Love2shop, Merchant Services — driving cost savings and investment reallocation.
  • New organisation FY26 net revenue split: Network Services £92.4m (-2.0%), Digital Payments & Open Banking £13.3m (+43.3%), Love2shop £53.5m (+3.5%), Merchant Services £31.6m (-2.5%).
  • Merchant Services strategic reset: moving away from estate growth to focus on net revenue, retention, and mid-market segment; net revenue becomes core sales KPI.
  • Card processed value -6.2% to £6.4bn (Handepay -4.6%, PayPoint/Lloyds Cardnet -9.6%).
  • PayPoint BankLocal: launched Aug 2025 with Lloyds; >£47m of cash deposits processed, weekly run rate ~£3m by FY26 end; Nationwide live April 2026; card deposit sites to expand from 3,000 to 10,000 in FY27; SME deposit solution targeted for FY28.
  • Royal Mail/Collect+ partnership: IDS took 49% of Collect+ for £43.9m (valuing it at £90m); generated £43.4m net proceeds (returned as special dividend) and £34.0m gain through equity; 8,500 sites live with Royal Mail services, 3,000 with over-the-counter; self-serve kiosk pilot planned H1 FY27.
  • Collect+ parcel transactions +1.5% to 135.4m; new 3-year InPost/Yodel deal signed (c.4,000 PUDO sites); Amazon network expanding from 7,000 to 9,000 sites.
  • Love2shop: billings +5.0% to £385.8m; physical gift card billings +313% to £13.5m (InComm partnership); Park Christmas Savings billings £164.4m (+0.9%).
  • Digital net revenue (MultiPay + Open Banking) +25.8% to £19.5m; MultiPay +17.9%; Open Banking +62.5% to £1.3m (ex-obconnect).
  • obconnect: founder buyout completed (now wholly owned); contributed £4.4m net revenue; £4.1m impairment recognised at parent-company level on the investment.
  • Cash payments net revenue -10.3% to £28.8m (in line with expectations).
  • YouLend business finance: >£33m of funding provided, +39% YoY.
  • Capex £21.6m (FY25: £18.8m); cash generation £70.4m (+£1.4m).
  • Borrowing facilities refinanced June 2025: £75m term loan + £90m RCF, both expiring June 2029.
  • Competition Appeal Tribunal ruling (7 May 2026): PayPoint found liable for a historical (pre-2018) competition infringement on energy OTC prepayment contracts; damages of only £169,334 plus interest awarded to Global-365, with CAT confirming PayPoint's contracts were not a significant factor in G365's lack of success.
  • Utilita claim settled May 2025 with a new 5-year OTC prepayment contract agreed.
  • Capital Markets Day scheduled for Q4 2026.
  • CEO Nick Wiles: results delivered "against the background of a generally weak economy, low consumer confidence and some specific business headwinds" with "early indications" of "a positive start to the year with resilient underlying trading and some encouraging new business wins."

body:
• Received conditional admission letter from the ASX to admit Pan African to the official list and grant official quotation, subject to satisfaction of conditions precedent.
  • Will trade under share code "PAF" on the ASX once admitted.
  • Receipt of the letter satisfies part of the ASX admission condition precedent in the Scheme Implementation Deed with Emmerson Resources and Tennant Consolidated Mining Group.
  • Scheme remains subject to outstanding conditions including Emmerson shareholder approval, Australian Court approval, and other conditions in the Scheme Booklet/SID.
  • Scheme Meeting of Emmerson shareholders: Monday, 15 June 2026.
  • Indicative timetable: Second court date 19 June; Effective date 22 June; ASX admission/deferred trading 23 June 2026; Scheme record date 24 June; Implementation date 1 July; normal settlement trading on ASX from 2 July 2026.
  • Pan African will continue as a dual primary issuer on the LSE and JSE following the ASX listing (becoming triple-listed).

body:
• Three corporate acquisitions completed for combined portfolio price of £63.8m (Merlin £19.4m, Grove Court £35.9m, Scorpion £8.5m), adding 40 assets primarily in Midlands/Buckinghamshire; funded partly via new share issuance to vendors.
  • Portfolio value rose to £669.3m (FY25: £594.4m), with like-for-like valuation up 2.7%.
  • EPRA EPS +3.3% to 6.3p (FY25: 6.1p); IFRS EPS 10.4p (FY25: 8.7p); PBT £48.3m (FY25: £38.2m).
  • DPS held at 6.0p, 104.8% covered (FY25: 101.3%); 7.5% dividend yield at year-end; FY27 target DPS reiterated at 6.0p.
  • NAV per share +3.7% to 99.7p; NAV total return 10.0% (FY25: 9.5%); share price total return 12.7% (FY25: 1.2%); post year-end share price rose to 88p.
  • Like-for-like contractual rent +3.4% to £49.2m; ERV like-for-like +3.3%, leaving 13% reversionary potential baked in (ERV £55.6m vs passing rent £49.2m).
  • Sector LFL ERV growth: industrial +4.1%, retail warehouse +2.7%, other +3.7%, office +1.6%, retail +1.3%.
  • Nine rent reviews completed at 6% ahead of previous passing rent and 7% ahead of ERV; 53 new lettings/renewals/re-gears.
  • Occupancy improved to 92.4% (FY25: 91.1%); EPRA vacancy 7.6% (FY25: 8.9%).
  • Disposals of £19.9m achieved at 23% premium to pre-offer valuation; capex £9.5m (Plymouth, Biggleswade, Kettering industrial refurb, Carlisle drive-through).
  • Net gearing reduced to 25.9% (FY25: 27.9%), nearing 25% medium-term target; 65% of drawn debt at fixed rate (FY25: 80%).
  • Weighted average cost of debt rose to 4.1% (FY25: 3.9%) following £20m SWIP loan repayment in Aug 2025 refinanced via 5.5% RCF; RCF limit raised from £50m → £60m → £75m, extended to Nov 2028.
  • Share buyback programme launched July 2025: 6.0m shares bought for £4.8m at 79.1p (avg 17.7% discount to NAV); Board approved further £2.0m of buybacks in May 2026.
  • EPC weighted average improved from C (51) to B (48).
  • Nathan Imlach stepped down 31 Dec 2025; Board now fully independent of Investment Manager.
  • Outlook flags Middle East conflict (commenced 28 Feb 2026) as a valuation headwind; 30-year gilts at 28-year high of 5.8%; Board expects steady valuation recovery driven by rental growth, not yield shift.
  • Richard Shepherd-Cross: "This has been another year of positive operational performance which has provided a strong foundation to continue our long track record of delivering a fully covered dividend for our shareholders."
  • Chairman MacLellan: income and income growth "are likely to form the greater component of total return over the next phase of the property cycle if long-term interest rates continue to remain elevated with persistent inflation."
  • Reverse stress testing: portfolio valuation would need to fall 34% at portfolio level (or 12% at charge pool level) to breach covenants.

body:
• Board has approved voluntary delisting of common stock from Nasdaq and deregistration with the SEC, reaffirming the intention flagged in the 1 May 2026 Company update.
  • Delisting is tied to the Plan of Dissolution being put to shareholders at the 1 July 2026 meeting.
  • Company notes it remains in compliance with Nasdaq listing requirements; the move is framed as eliminating "significant legal, audit, and other costs associated with being a public company."
  • Key timeline:
  • Form 25 to be filed with SEC on or about 22 June 2026.
  • Warrants (ORGNW) expire 24 June 2026; Nasdaq expected to suspend trading pre-market open that day and file a Form 25 to delist them.
  • Common stock delisting expected effective 2 July 2026.
  • Form 15 to be filed on or about 10 July 2026, terminating SEC registration and immediately suspending Exchange Act reporting obligations.
  • No OTC quotation will be pursued — Origin does not intend to apply to have common stock quoted on OTC; if the Plan of Dissolution is approved and a certificate of dissolution filed, there will be no trading market for the shares.
  • Interim CEO Matt Plavan: strategy "to maximize shareholder value, which includes the sale of our technology and associated assets."

body:
• Voluntary public takeover offer for all HUGO BOSS AG shares not already owned by Frasers, made under the German Securities Acquisition and Takeover Act.
  • Cash offer price: €38.00 per HUGO BOSS share; aggregate consideration of ~€1,978.0m (~£1,727.1m) for the 73.94% of share capital / 73.42% of voting rights not held.
  • Frasers already holds 18,347,461 shares (26.06% of capital, 26.58% of voting rights) plus significant sold put options; move pre-empts mandatory offer trigger that would arise above 30%.
  • Net consideration on outstanding put options under Prior Trades could reach ~€1,143.3m (~£998.3m) for an interest in 34.3m shares (expiries up to December 2029).
  • No minimum acceptance threshold; conditional only on merger control clearances.
  • Expected completion H2 2026, subject to BaFin approval of the offer document and regulatory clearances.
  • Financing: new Acquisition Facility Agreement signed 10 June 2026 with BNP Paribas, Deutsche Bank Luxembourg, NatWest and Standard Chartered; unsecured, 12-month term (extendable by up to 12 months), prepayable without penalty. May also use existing term loan and RCF.
  • Classified as a significant transaction under UKLR 7.3.1R/7.3.2R but not subject to shareholder approval.
  • CEO Michael Murray (who sits on HUGO BOSS supervisory board) recused himself from the Board's discussion and decision.
  • Rationale: HUGO BOSS described as "one of the top five brands" across the Group; Frasers remains supportive of Chair Stephan Sturm and CEO Daniel Grieder's sustainable growth strategy.
  • HUGO BOSS FY25 financials cited: revenue €4,269.8m, EBITDA €781.5m, gross assets €3,721.2m, net assets €1,557.7m.
  • Pro forma combined 6-month EBITDA (to 26 Oct 2025): €971.3m / £848.1m (Frasers £448.6m + HUGO BOSS £399.5m equivalent).
  • Pro forma accounting impact: goodwill of €1,117.5m (£975.7m) would be recognised; total liabilities would rise by €1,999.0m (£1,745.4m) including €21.0m transaction costs.
  • Revaluation of existing stake at €38.00 would trigger a €3.7m (£3.2m) charge to the income statement immediately prior to completion.
  • Frasers had no access to HUGO BOSS management or non-public information — assessment based solely on public data, flagged as a key risk.
  • Advisers: BNP Paribas and Deutsche Bank (financial); RPC (English law); Gleiss Lutz (German law).
  • No significant change in Frasers' financial position since 26 October 2025.

body:
• Joe Billante appointed CFO, effective 27 July 2026, succeeding Jay Martin who retires after 23 years.
  • Billante most recently CFO of Barracuda Networks; previously 13 years at eBay (including CFO of core European and Greater China businesses, VP of IR & Communications) and 11 years at GE (including CFO of a GE Healthcare global division).
  • Martin will participate in Q2 earnings alongside management and remain engaged through 31 August 2026 as part of a planned transition.
  • CEO Vinayak Hegde on strategic direction: "As we continue building a more data-driven, customer-focused, and technology-enabled company, Joe's background as a true strategic partner—connecting financial discipline to long-term value creation—makes him exactly the right leader for this next chapter."
  • Billante on joining: "Credit Acceptance has built something truly differentiated—a company with a clear and compelling mission, a strong track record, and a culture that people are proud to be part of."

body:
• Appointment of Tanate Phutrakul (61) as independent non-executive director effective 1 July 2026, joining the Audit and Board Risk Committees from the same date.
  • Phutrakul is the former Group CFO and Executive Board member of ING Group, retiring in April 2026 after 24 years at ING.
  • Brings expertise in financial strategy, capital management, complex regulatory environments and large-scale banking transformation; noted affinity to Asian markets aligned with STAN's footprint.
  • Career highlights: MD of ING Barings Securities Thailand (1998), Head of Wholesale Banking then CFO of TMB Bank Thailand (2003–2008), various ING CFO roles, Group CFO of ING from 2019 for seven years.
  • Fee structure: £123,000 base NED fee, plus £43,000 each for Audit Committee and Board Risk Committee membership (total £209,000 p.a.).
  • Currently holds 3,000 STAN shares; appointment subject to shareholder election at 2027 AGM.
  • Confirmed independent with no relationships to other directors, management or substantial shareholders.
  • Group Chair Maria Ramos: "I am delighted to welcome Tanate to the Board of Standard Chartered. His extensive international banking experience, together with his strong financial expertise, will bring further valuable insight and perspective to the Board discussion."
  • Phutrakul: "I am pleased to be joining the Board of Standard Chartered, which is a highly regarded international bank with a distinctive footprint across key growth markets, and I look forward to contributing my experience to support the Group's continued growth and development."

body:

  • Prior FS/insurance experience: Chair of Direct Line Insurance Group (2020–2025), NED at Aldermore Bank (2014–2021) including SID and Interim Chair roles; former Chair of St Modwen Properties.
  • Executive background in telecoms: former CEO of Telefonica O2 Ireland and Executive Board member of O2 plc.
  • Group Chair Sir Robin Budenberg: "Danuta is a very experienced non-executive director and chair who will bring extensive knowledge and experience of banking and insurance to her roles with the Group. She also has a track record of growing customer-focused and technology businesses. I look forward to welcoming her to the Group."

body:
• Capital raise priced, gross proceeds c.£106m (net c.£102m), comprising Placing, Retail Offer and Director/Management Subscription.
  • Placing price 410p, only a 0.5% discount to the 412p middle market price at agreement — but note shares closed down 16.17% at 412.60p on the day.
  • 25,046,629 Placing Shares placed by Barclays, Goldman Sachs and J.P. Morgan Cazenove with institutions; BofA acting as Financial Adviser.
  • 244,000 Retail Offer Shares subscribed via RetailBook at the Placing Price.
  • 514,631 Subscription Shares taken by directors/management at the Placing Price; CEO Leo Quinn subscribing for 365,853 shares (c.£1.5m), CFO Max Izzard 36,585.
  • Aggregate new issuance represents c.20% of pre-deal share capital; post-Admission shares in issue: 152,258,405.
  • Admission expected 8:00 a.m. on 12 June 2026.
  • 180-day lock-up on further share issuance by the Company (subject to customary exceptions/waivers).
  • Related party transaction: Causeway Capital (>20% holder) subscribing for 6,461,433 shares (c.£26m); independent Board confirms fair and reasonable, advised by J.P. Morgan Cazenove as Sponsor.
  • Use of proceeds: strengthen balance sheet, fund growth/transformation agenda, reduce reliance on debt funding.
  • Leverage target: reduce from current "higher than targeted" levels to around 2x by end of FY26 — key deleveraging signal.
  • Allocations followed soft pre-emption where possible, with excess directed to existing shareholders and wall-crossed investors.

body:
• Announced a conditional retail offer of new Ordinary Shares via RetailBook, running alongside an institutional Placing (accelerated bookbuild) and a Subscription by certain directors/management (collectively, the "Capital Raise").
  • Issue price to be set at close of the bookbuild; same price across Retail Offer, Placing and Subscription.
  • Retail Offer is conditional on completion of the Placing; Admission expected 08:00 on 12 June 2026.
  • Minimum subscription £250 per investor; no commission charged by RetailBook; accessible via ISAs, SIPPs and GIAs through partner platforms.
  • Retail Offer open to UK-resident new and existing shareholders; expected to close same time as the Placing on 10 June 2026 (may close earlier).
  • Board rationale: Capital Raise will "strengthen the balance sheet, enable continued execution of the Group's growth and transformation agenda, provide greater confidence around the Group's leverage position, and reduce the Group's reliance on debt funding".
  • New shares will rank pari passu with existing Ordinary Shares, including dividend rights.
  • Released alongside a separate trading update to be read in conjunction — context for the share price reaction (stock indicated -18.5% at 401p on the day).
  • Company reserves the right to scale back or reject applications; investors warned market price may fall below the issue price during the offer.

body:
• Group WI production Jan–May 2026: 43.1 kboepd (incl. 7.3 kboepd gas), tracking upper end of 34–42 kboepd FY guidance.
  • Ghana drilling on schedule: third of six Jubilee wells (J76-P) onstream this week; logging "supportive of a strong production well".
  • Remaining producers J77-P and J50-P due onstream June and July; final water injection well in September.
  • Facility uptime at Jubilee and TEN FPSOs averaged >99% Jan–May, extending strong Q1 performance.
  • Greater Jubilee Plan of Further Development approved by Ghana's Minister of Energy, supporting up to 20 additional Jubilee wells post current campaign.
  • 2026 FCF guidance unchanged at $70–175m at $70–100/bbl; rises to $110–230m if an additional cargo is delivered in December 2026.
  • Average pre-hedge realisations on five cargos Jan–May: c.$96/bbl (c.$87/bbl post-hedge); May Jubilee cargo achieved $119/bbl.
  • Hedge book: 60% downside protection while retaining 60% upside exposure in 2026.
  • FY2026 capex guidance unchanged at c.$200m; decommissioning spend c.$25m.
  • CEO Ian Perks: "Tullow has performed strongly since the outset of 2026, marked by significant delivery across the business."
  • CEO on outlook: "With group production at the higher end of guidance and our significant leverage to the oil price, including year to date realisations ahead of expectations, we remain well placed to generate significant free cash flow in 2026 and beyond."

body:
• Niamh Staunton appointed Chief Financial Officer, joining from BP where she has spent the last 2.5 years as SVP, Finance and Risk in the Supply, Trading & Shipping business.
  • Prior BP role: Group Treasurer from 2021; before BP, over 15 years at Morgan Stanley in senior capital markets roles; 20+ years' total senior finance experience.
  • Start date not yet confirmed; she will be appointed as an Executive Director later this year.
  • Succeeds Jeff Woyda, retiring as CFO & COO in September 2026 after 19 years (previously announced).
  • COO succession already resolved: Harriet Oliver (Group Head of HR since Oct 2019) confirmed as incoming COO in May 2026.
  • Governance disclosure: Staunton currently serves as personal deputy director for Kate Thomson on the board of Aker BP ASA; role ceases on departure from BP.
  • CEO Andi Case quote: "Niamh's career to date means she joins us with a huge amount of experience and skill that I think perfectly fit what the Company needs to support and execute the next phase of its strategy."
  • Chair Laurence Hollingworth quote: "She brings a wealth of experience and skills in commodities, shipping, finance and capital markets, and has an established record of delivering strong results."
  • Staunton quote: "building on the strong financial position established over the past two decades and its growth momentum in an industry central to the global economy."
  • Context: Clarksons turns 175 next year; 23 consecutive years of dividend growth; FTSE 250 constituent.

body:
• New medium-term ambition: organic trading profit before interest of over £125m per annum (vs FY26 trading profit after interest of £60.5m), under new "Fix, Accelerate, Scale" strategy.
  • FY27 guidance: substantial step-down in trading profit after interest expected, driven by lower opening rent roll, disposals, higher borrowing costs, less capitalised interest, higher expenses and reduced non-recurring items.
  • Net rental income £113.4m, down 7.1% (FY25: £122.1m); underlying (ex-disposals) down 2.4% to £109.9m.
  • Trading profit after interest £60.5m, down 9.4% (FY25: £66.8m).
  • Loss before tax of £120.5m (FY25: £5.4m profit), driven by £159.5m negative property revaluation and £13.8m loss on disposals.
  • Revenue £181.4m (FY25: £185.2m).
  • Property valuation £2,133m, underlying down 7.0% (£160m); Stabilised Portfolio down 5.6% with ERV/sq ft down 3.8% to £48.94 and equivalent yield out 18bps to 6.7%.
  • EPRA NTA/share £6.87, down 11.2% (HY26: £7.21; FY25: £7.74); total accounting return -7.6% (FY25: +0.3%).
  • Stabilised Portfolio occupancy 81.6% at 31 March 26 (Sep 25: 80.5%; Mar 25: 83.0%) — modest H2 recovery (+1.1pp) at expense of pricing.
  • Stabilised Portfolio rent/sq ft £46.31, down 2.1%; rent roll down 4.6% to £108.3m.
  • Lettings 1,310 (FY25: 1,266) with total rental value £50.4m (FY25: £46.4m); enquiry-to-letting conversion improved to 17% (FY25: 15%).
  • Final dividend cut to 16.7p (-12.1%); full-year DPS 26.1p, down 8.1%; new policy targets 1.2x earnings cover from FY26 onwards.
  • Disposals: £125.7m exchanged/completed in year at 7.2% discount to book; net proceeds £111m at 5.9% NIY; further £6.0m exchanged in June (Chiswick Studios, One Crown Square) at 15.5% discount to March book; £60.4m in active discussions towards £200m target; additional £100m+ disposals under consideration.
  • Capex case studies commencing at Salisbury House, Cargo Works, Edinburgh House, Centro Buildings — combined planned capex ~£16-20m targeting incremental yields on cost of 12.5–30% and unlevered IRRs of 10–20%.
  • New "Managed" offer to be launched alongside existing Space-only product.
  • LTV 35% (Mar 25: 34%); £242m undrawn facilities + cash; interest cover 3.6x (FY25: 3.8x).
  • £200m RCF extended by one year to June 2030 (announced June 2026), taking average debt maturity to 2.8 years proforma.
  • Fitch initiated coverage at BBB-/stable; S&P coverage being terminated.
  • Effective interest rate on drawn debt 3.6%; 77% of drawn debt fixed; average cost of debt over year 3.8%.
  • Adjusted underlying EPS 31.3p, down 9.3% (FY25: 34.5p).
  • Refurbishments at Fleet Street and Corinthian House paused pending review.
  • Exceptional costs £7.3m including £3.1m IT systems replacement and £4.2m organisational restructuring.
  • New CEO Charlie Green and CFO Tom Edwards-Moss joined February 2026.
  • CEO Charlie Green: "Our focus is on earnings through disciplined execution, driving higher occupancy with pricing growth while controlling costs...our ambition is to deliver, organically, trading profit before interest of over £125m per annum in the medium term."
  • Chair on outlook: "the external environment remains uncertain and the operating conditions in our market are likely to remain tough for some time."
  • Going concern stress: portfolio could absorb 43% valuation fall / 48% NRI fall before covenant breach.

body:
• FY26 Headline Group PBT guidance set at £75m–£90m, a material downgrade reflecting Middle East conflict disruption, weaker consumer demand and gross margin pressure (notably in North America).
  • Non-pre-emptive equity placing announced to strengthen balance sheet and fund ongoing transformation; shares trading down ~17–18% intraday.
  • Significant non-cash impairment of up to £150m expected for FY26 covering goodwill and store impairments tied to InMotion review, store exits and ROW restructuring.
  • Group total revenue +5% (constant currency), LFL +2%; LFL slowed to +1% in the last 7 weeks.
  • UK total revenue +5%, LFL +2%; Air LFL -1% (Middle East flight disruption + weaker spend per passenger), Hospitals LFL +7%, Rail LFL +2%.
  • One-stop-shop openings at Belfast, East Midlands, Heathrow and Liverpool airports delivering good YoY growth.
  • North America total revenue +10% cc but LFL -1%, deteriorating to -4% in last 7 weeks; Air LFL -2% (last 7 weeks) on lower passenger numbers and airline capacity cuts.
  • InMotion LFL -5% in last 7 weeks with significant footfall decline; portfolio review ongoing.
  • Travel Essentials LFL -1% over last 7 weeks.
  • Resorts LFL -11% in last 7 weeks on lower Las Vegas visitor numbers; 14 uneconomic fashion stores closed or with agreed closure dates, remaining 12 likely to exit this year; Group considering strategic options for the Welcome to Las Vegas business.
  • North America FY26 planning assumptions: revenue growth 4–6%, Headline trading margin c.5%; other divisional assumptions unchanged.
  • ROW total revenue -2% cc, LFL +3%; 5 uneconomic stores closed in Norway, with further landlord talks to exit or move stores to franchise.
  • Gross margin pressure in North America driven by increased promotional activity, reduced brand marketing spend, and ongoing inflation headwinds.
  • Group assumes no near-term improvement in consumer confidence and that jet fuel supplies are maintained; profit remains heavily Q4-weighted.
  • Management framing: "Revised outlook ahead of peak summer trading against a backdrop of ongoing uncertainty. Good progress on restructuring plans."

body:
• Maiden mineral resource estimate for portions of Motapa expected to be published in Q3 2026.
  • Drilling confirmed continuity of mineralisation along a strike length of ~6km within the Bubi Greenstone Belt, with multiple shear zones across Motapa North, Central and South, and both oxide and sulphide mineralisation.
  • Motapa lies directly adjacent to the Bilboes Gold Project (proven & probable reserves of 1.75Moz gold in 24.1Mt at 2.26g/t), enabling potential shared infrastructure and reduced capital intensity.
  • 2025 programme: 22,364m trenching, 1,561.78m DD and 18,547m RC drilling, focused on Motapa North sulphides and Mpudzi (Motapa Central) oxide/sulphide delineation.
  • Selected Motapa North high-grade intercepts: 19.00m at 8.08g/t (JPRC52); 6.38m at 13.95g/t (JDD11); 12.00m at 7.12g/t (JPRC63); 17.00m at 3.25g/t (JPRC51); 13.00m at 3.72g/t (PLV1RC10); 6.00m at 6.89g/t (PLV1RC15); 14.00m at 4.31g/t (PLV5RC4).
  • Motapa Central (Mpudzi) highlights: 7.00m at 2.39g/t (MPZRC79); 3.00m at 4.79g/t (MPZRC64); 2.00m at 5.25g/t (MPZRC117) — oxide mineralisation confirmed to depths of ~35m, supporting near-term development optionality.
  • Motapa North mineralised zones display pinch-and-swell geometry with true widths from ~0.5m up to 17.1m (JPRC52).
  • Historic oxide pits at Motapa North sit only ~250m south of the Bilboes property boundary, close to the planned Bilboes metallurgical facility — supporting integration thesis.
  • 2026 programme will include infill drilling and testing of gap areas between Pluvious 123/Pluvious 4 pits and between Jupiter/Shawl pits, plus increased activity at Motapa South.
  • QAQC: overall CRM compliance rate of 92.2%; underperformance of standard AMIS0786 (43.6% pass) flagged for further investigation; blanks 97.1% pass rate.
  • CEO Mark Learmonth: "While our immediate focus remains the development of Bilboes, targeting first gold in Q4 2028, Motapa represents a compelling opportunity to extend mine life and increase future production across a combined mining complex."
  • Mining lease covers ~2,200 hectares; tenure provides both exploration and mining rights, located 110km north of Bulawayo, Zimbabwe.
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Revenue +8.1% to £1,268.6m (FY25: £1,173.1m), outperforming the wider used car market.
  • Retail revenue +10.0% to £1,130.8m; Wholesale revenue -4.8% to £137.8m.
  • Gross profit +8.9% to £98.9m; gross margin nudged to 7.8% (FY25: 7.7%).
  • PBT +82.9% to £7.5m (FY25: £4.1m); EBITDA +15.1% to £27.5m.
  • Basic EPS 6.6p vs 3.7p (+78.4%).
  • ROCE jumped to 67.2% from 46.6% (+2,060bps).
  • Retail volumes +7.8% to record 64.6k units vs used car market +1.4% (SMMT); total vehicles sold 91.9k (+4.8%); wholesale units -1.8% to 27.3k.
  • Market share (0–10yr) up 22bps to 1.68%.
  • Retail gross profit per unit £1,368 (+£33); wholesale GPU flat at £387.
  • Days in stock rose to 54 (FY25: 43) due to heavy late-year stock build for Easter/FY27 momentum.
  • Customer acquisition cost fell to £163 (-£14); NPS up to 84 (from 80).
  • Net debt of £8.8m vs net cash of £6.6m a year ago — swing driven by £13.1m purchase of two freehold sites in Q4, £5.0m buyback, £1.2m EBT share purchases, and heavy stock buying.
  • Finance expense +9.6% to £10.3m on higher stock holdings; finance commission income still subdued due to elevated rates.
  • Operating costs +4.0% to £81.2m (well below gross profit growth) despite full-year Norwich store costs and wage inflation.
  • Full-year dividend 2.2p (+120% YoY); final dividend 1.2p proposed (FY25: 1.0p), payout ratio 33.3% of basic EPS.
  • Completed £5.0m buyback (3.0m shares cancelled); since March 2024 £11.7m returned to shareholders, share count down >7%.
  • Stocking facilities increased to £210m post year-end (FY25: £165m); new £10m Property RCF secured post year-end to fund store roll-out; Santander RCF extended to June 2028.
  • Total headroom at year-end £75.4m.
  • Sell Your Car direct sourcing scaling fast: 6,603 vehicles (+85% YoY), ~10% of total purchases; FY27 target 12k SYC purchases; running >200/week post year-end.
  • Capex more than doubled to £15.4m (FY25: £7.6m); Derby site sale-and-leaseback completed.
  • Store roll-out: 22nd store (Leeds) opening Summer 2026; three further new-market openings in FY27/FY28; medium-term target ≥30 stores with ≥10% local share; new £2m site bought in May 2026.
  • AI/data: agentic AI sales follow-up delivered 877 incremental sales in FY26; data-led pricing supported record metal margins.
  • Efficiency: retail units sold per FTE +19.1% since FY23; finance headcount cut 10% post ERP roll-out; people cost per retail unit down to £610 (FY22: £656).
  • Historic motor finance commissions: FCA redress scheme (March 2026) confirmed automotive brokers like Motorpoint are not liable — no provision required.
  • Mary McNamara to step down from the Board at the 2026 AGM after 10 years.
  • Current trading (FY27 to date): retail volumes +15.0% in April–May (vs softer comps), stable metal margins, strong supply.
  • CEO Mark Carpenter: "The Group had an excellent year in FY26, and I am delighted to report record sales volumes and an 83% increase in profit before taxation. FY26 has been a step change year for Motorpoint, where the use of data became fundamental within the business and we embraced the tangible benefits of AI."
  • CEO on macro: "Whilst the macroeconomic uncertainty in recent months leads to a degree of caution due to the risks of increased inflation and interest rates, our superior customer service, omnichannel business model and exciting growth plans mean we are well placed to take advantage of opportunities to further increase market share and build long term value for shareholders."
  • Going concern reverse stress test requires -33% volumes and -42% gross profit vs base case to breach covenants — deemed remote.

body:
• ROM deliveries from Molefe Mine to the Sable refinery have recommenced following successful Pit 2 expansion under the updated mine plan combining Pits 2 and 3.
  • Ramp-up profile: ~6,000tpm in June, ~8,500tpm by August, steady-state 10,000tpm by October 2026 — equating to >100% increase in ROM deliveries to Sable.
  • Post pit integration, Molefe targets a step-up from ~12,000t/quarter HG ROM to >30,000t/quarter HG Cu reef ore delivered to Sable.
  • Combined mined copper reef set to reach 60,000t per quarter post Pit 2+3 combination, at an average stripping ratio of 6:1.
  • Accelerated pre-strip programme (started April 2026) remains on track for completion in July 2026.
  • Next development phase: (i) on-site ore sorting and processing for better HG/LG segregation and improved recovery; (ii) resource definition drilling across the greater Molefe district and additional exploration properties.
  • JORC-compliant resource / competent person's report targeted for Q4 CY2026, subject to assay timing (following Phase 2 drilling).
  • Ramp-up will use both newly mined ROM and strategic stockpiled material during the transition to integrated on-site ore sorting/processing.
  • CEO Leon Coetzer: "Molefe Mine is evolving into a cornerstone asset within our integrated mine-to-metals platform, with the operation on track to achieve a meaningful step-up in production over the coming months."
  • CEO on strategy: "Our strategy is to mine while we explore, expanding both production and the resource base simultaneously... we are establishing a blueprint that we believe can be replicated across multiple copper opportunities within the region."
  • Group target reiterated: 25,000 tpa copper production via three-pillar strategy (Roan concentrator, Sable refinery + regional mining, Large Waste Rock Project).
  • Live investor presentation scheduled for 10 June 2026, 14:00 UK time via Investor Meet Company.
period_detail: Operational update as at June 2026; ramp-up profile through October 2026; JORC resource targeted Q4 CY2026

• Clodagh Moriarty, independent Non-Executive Director, to step down from the Board to focus on her role as CEO of Dunelm Group plc.
  • Leaving date to be confirmed.
  • Search process for a replacement is underway to maintain Board balance of skills and expertise.
  • Chair Robert Noel: "On behalf of the Board, I would like to express our sincere thanks to Clodagh for her valued contributions and commitment to the Company during her tenure and we wish her well for the future."

body:
• Solid start to the year; reiterates confidence in achieving FY market expectations for both adjusted revenue and EBITDA.
  • Announces further £30m extension to share buyback programme, on top of £100m completed in FY25 and an ongoing £30m tranche (of which £18.9m returned as of 5 June 2026).
  • US Molson Coors partnership has moved beyond the initial transition period, delivering new account wins, increasing distribution and building momentum.
  • Launched first national US marketing campaign in April spanning TV, digital, e-commerce and nationwide sampling.
  • New UK marketing campaign "Straight Up or Mixed, it's a matter of taste" launched in April, positioning Fever-Tree as both mixer and premium soft drink.
  • Continued strong momentum in Australia, including launch of Lemon, Lime & Bitters with Angostura Bitters.
  • Further market share gains across UK, Europe and US.
  • Hedging significantly extended: 2026 glass fully hedged for elevated energy costs (as previously disclosed); coverage now extended through 2027 and into 2028 for energy, with hedging on all other significant cost components increased into 2027.
  • CEO Tim Warrillow: "this year we are significantly increasing marketing investment and innovating to support our growth ambitions."
  • CEO on macro: "Notwithstanding the current uncertainty in the geopolitical backdrop, we are well hedged from a cost perspective and remain confident in achieving market expectations for both adjusted revenue and EBITDA."
  • Board frames buyback as "highly effective mechanism to drive shareholder value, alongside a progressive dividend policy."
  • Next scheduled announcement: Interim Results on 10 September 2026.

body:
• Awarded a $207m contract variation on the I-40 US highway reconstruction project.
  • Cumulative I-40 work secured now c.$380m, of which c.$70m completed; balance to be delivered over the next 2–3 years.
  • Group order book lifted to a record c.£1.9bn, enhancing multi-year revenue visibility.
  • 2026 full year results remain in line with expectations.
  • CEO James Wroath: "We are delighted to have secured further work on this major project, which reflects both the strength of our technical capabilities and the confidence our client has in Keller's expertise. The reconstruction of the I-40 corridor is a critical infrastructure project, and we are proud to play a key role in helping restore a vital transport link."

body:
• Adjusted Group PBT for FY2026 expected to be ~£7.5m below current market expectations (consensus £17.8m, range £16.4m–£18.5m), implying a ~42% downgrade to roughly £10.3m.
  • Downgrade driven entirely by Gleeson Land transaction slippage into H1 FY2027 — not trading deterioration.
  • Major site sale (~50% of FY2026 forecast plots) will not complete this financial year; most technical approval conditions agreed, completion now expected H1 FY2027.
  • Two further smaller transactions also pushed into H1 FY2027, reflecting national housebuilders reviewing land buying strategies amid current market conditions.
  • One additional site sale still expected to complete in June 2026.
  • Gleeson Homes trading in line with management expectations for the full year.
  • Full year trading update scheduled for 10 July 2026.
  • CEO Graham Prothero: "The Gleeson Land team have cleared almost all of the technical hurdles to facilitate the disposal of the major site and it is frustrating that completion will now fall into the next financial year. The progress to date should not be underestimated and represents a strong performance by the team."
  • CEO on market backdrop: "There is no doubt that housebuilders, in the south of England, are reviewing and reappraising their land buying strategies and we anticipate these conditions will continue through the next financial year. The good news is that there is continuing demand for the highest quality developments, which is where Gleeson Land is now focused with a portfolio of prime sites. Transactions will take longer but we are confident that sales of prime sites will continue."

body:
• Selected as one of three contractors on Transport for London's Infrastructure Improvement Framework, total value ~£700m.
  • Two-year term with option of a two-year extension.
  • Multi-discipline scope: design, engineering, programme delivery and supply chain management.
  • Named projects include South Kensington tube station upgrade and works enabling TfL's step-free access programme.
  • Extends Costain's TfL relationship beyond 25 years; recent works include A40 Westway refurbishment and ongoing Brent Cross Interchange project.
  • CEO Alex Vaughan: "We are a trusted, long-term partner of Transport for London, and our growing relationship with this important customer is testament to our proven track record in delivering best-in-class infrastructure services across the capital."
  • CEO added: "Upgrading London's transport network is critical for a more prosperous, resilient, and decarbonised UK. We look forward to bringing our capabilities in engineering, design and construction to deliver high-impact projects on time and on budget, underpinning London's growth for future generations."

time_period: 1 February to 29 May 2026 (H2 FY26 to date)
body:
• FY26 underlying operating profit guidance reiterated at £320m–£330m, supported by the forward order book, despite demand moderation in recent weeks.
  • FY26 volume guidance reiterated at 9,300–9,500 homes.
  • CEO Jason Honeyman: "Bellway continues to perform robustly in an increasingly challenging market, with customer demand having moderated in recent weeks, after a positive start to the spring selling season."
  • Spring season started strongly vs autumn 2025 but April/May demand softened following the recent rise in mortgage rates; reservation rates still above H1 levels, with incentive usage averaging ~5%.
  • Renewed upward pressure on build costs from higher fuel/energy inputs, with suppliers raising prices and introducing surcharges; FY27 build cost inflation visibility expected later in the year.
  • Private reservation rate down 6.2% to 151/week (2025: 161); per outlet per week 0.65 (vs 0.67); ex-bulk 0.58 (vs 0.61).
  • Overall reservation rate (incl. social) down 5.1% to 186/week; cancellation rate low at 10% (vs 11%).
  • Average outlets 233 (vs 242); full-year average still expected ~240; 40+ new outlet openings planned in H2 plus strong FY27 pipeline.
  • Forward order book: 5,345 homes worth £1,570m (vs 5,759 homes / £1,650m) — volumes down ~7%, value down ~5%.
  • Land: 6,744 plots contracted across just 24 sites for £363m (vs 6,759 plots / 42 sites / £495m prior year) — far fewer sites at lower spend, including ~1,900-plot Dunfermline strategic conversion.
  • 16 new strategic option agreements signed; strategic land bank ~47,000 plots, >50% with positive planning status; medium-term target >20% of output from strategic land.
  • Net debt £236m (vs £73m prior year), in line with expectations.
  • FY26 year-end adjusted gearing expected at 5–10% (vs 8.3% at 31 July 2025).
  • £150m share buyback (launched 14 October 2025) progressing well; intention to continue returning excess capital in future years.
  • Interim dividend lifted to 23.0p (+9.5%), payable 1 July 2026; full-year underlying dividend cover expected ~2.5x.
  • Outlook caveat: "The outlook beyond the current financial year remains uncertain, reflecting ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and a less predictable domestic political environment."
  • Management flags "increasing the risk of a more prolonged period of softer customer demand alongside renewed inflationary pressure on build costs", with response focused on monetising land bank/WIP to improve asset turn and cash generation.
  • Next update: FY26 full-year trading update on 11 August 2026.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives half-year period '1 February to 29 May 2026 (H2 FY26 to date)'

body:
• BASF notified Harbour on 4 June 2026 that its shareholding had fallen below 25%, triggering a reduction in its board nomination rights.
  • Under the 3 September 2024 relationship agreement, BASF's nomination right drops from two directors to one once it holds less than 25% of issued share capital.
  • Hans-Ulrich Engel stepped down as a BASF-nominated Non-Executive Director with effect from 8 June 2026.
  • Chairman R. Blair Thomas commented: "On behalf of the Board, I would like to thank Hans for his contribution during his tenure and wish him every success in the future."

body:
• Rio Tinto is increasing annual community investment in Canada by 30% to C$13 million via the Rio Tinto Canada Fund.
  • Fund has invested close to C$190 million across 600+ organisations since its 2008 inception.
  • Funding directed across three pillars: community resilience, Indigenous reconciliation, and nature.
  • Recent C$1 million contribution to the McGill University Health Centre Foundation's MI4 initiative (antimicrobial resistance research).
  • Supports eight hospital Foundations in Quebec, plus partnerships with Breakfast Club of Canada, Alloprof, Moisson Montréal, Carrier Sekani Family Services, and Indspire.
  • Indigenous partnerships include Haisla Nation, Cheslatta Carrier Nation, Stellat'en First Nation, Nadleh Whut'en, Saik'uz First Nation (BC), and Innu communities of Pekuakamiulnuatsh, Ekuanitshit, Uashat Mak Mani-Utenam (Quebec) — aligned with operating regions for aluminium/lithium assets.
  • Nature pillar includes research tie-ups with University of Northern British Columbia and GREFS (Saguenay fjord ecosystem).
  • Rio Tinto Aluminium & Lithium CEO Jérôme Pécresse: "By increasing our annual contribution to C$13 million and expanding the Fund across Canada, we are reinforcing our commitment to building stronger, healthier and more resilient communities, and we are playing an active role as a key stakeholder in the regions where we operate."
  • Note: announcement is ESG/community-focused with no financial guidance, trading, or operational impact — C$13m is immaterial vs. group scale (US$57.6bn turnover, US$10bn profit) but reinforces social licence in key aluminium/lithium jurisdictions (Quebec, BC).

body:
• Recommended cash acquisition of Tate & Lyle by Ingredion via scheme of arrangement, unanimously recommended by Tate & Lyle Board.
  • Cash consideration of 595p per share, plus permitted dividends of up to 13.2p (FY26 final) and 6.8p (H1 FY27 interim) = total headline value up to 615p per share.
  • Values Tate & Lyle equity at ~£2.7bn ($3.6bn) on cash consideration alone, or ~£2.8bn including permitted dividends; implied enterprise value £3.7bn–£3.8bn ($5.0bn).
  • Headline premium of ~64.0% to undisturbed closing price (13 May 2026), 70.8% to 3-month VWAP, 67.0% to 6-month VWAP.
  • Implied EV/Adjusted EBITDA multiple of ~9.1x (including permitted dividends) / ~8.8x pre-synergy on cash consideration.
  • Initial unsolicited proposal was 530p (80% cash / 20% Ingredion stock) — rejected; deal reached after four further proposals, now all-cash.
  • Irrevocable undertakings secured for 17.1% of share capital, including 16.8% from Huber Equity Corporation (75m shares) and 0.3% from directors.
  • Huber may sell up to 100% of its stake after the earlier of receiving the 2027 Interim Dividend or 1 February 2027 (post-Meetings), with restrictions on transfers to competing bidders.
  • Huber undertaking terminates if a competing offer exceeds the total value by ≥10%.
  • Scheme expected to become Effective in H2 2027, subject to Material Antitrust Conditions in EU, Brazil, Canada, China, Colombia, Mexico, South Korea, Tanzania, UK, US and Zanzibar.
  • Long Stop Date: 8 December 2027 (extendable to 8 June 2028).
  • Financing: $4.225bn JPMorgan bridge facility in place; FX option taken to hedge GBP payment; final funding via cash, new debt and bridge drawdown if needed.
  • Combined Group: ~$9.9bn revenue, $1.8bn adjusted EBITDA, headquartered at Ingredion's Westchester, Illinois office; Jim Zallie to continue as Chairman & CEO.
  • Run-rate net cost synergies of ~$130m by end-2030 (~60% SG&A, ~40% COGS); one-off costs ~$175m.
  • Expected to be >15% accretive to adjusted EPS in first full fiscal year after Effective Date.
  • Pro forma net debt/EBITDA ~3x at close, falling to ~2.5x within 18 months; commitment to preserve investment grade rating.
  • Potential ~3% workforce reduction in the Combined Group post-completion review (corporate, manufacturing, support overlap).
  • £18m cash retention award pool for ~100 Tate & Lyle employees; CEO Nick Hampton and CFO Sarah Kuijlaars receive cash retention of 150% and 125% of base salary respectively (within the £18m pool).
  • Tate & Lyle to be de-listed from LSE and re-registered as a private company post-completion; ADS programme to be terminated.
  • Tate & Lyle FY26 results (already reported 21 May 2026): revenue down 3%, pro forma Adjusted EBITDA down 3%; over $50m productivity savings delivered; 5-year productivity target raised by $50m to $200m by FY28; CP Kelco $50m synergy target met a year early.
  • FY27 outlook reiterated: modest revenue growth (volume-led, H2-weighted) and broadly flat EBITDA before ~$20m hit from rescheduling bio-gums capacity consolidation.
  • David Hearn, Chair of Tate & Lyle: "The Board of Tate & Lyle believes Ingredion's offer represents an attractive opportunity for shareholders to crystallise value in cash, and that it will be an excellent steward of Tate & Lyle."
  • Jim Zallie, Ingredion Chair & CEO: "Combining Ingredion and Tate & Lyle's complementary portfolios creates a global leader in ingredient solutions with the expertise and geographic reach to help shape the future of food."
  • Board candidly acknowledged FY26 performance was "disappointing" and that continued challenging market conditions create risk to timing of delivering its financial algorithm — a key factor in recommending the deal.
  • Tate & Lyle shares closed +14.77% at 564p on 8 June 2026, still ~5% below the 595p cash terms (~8% below the 615p headline), reflecting timing/regulatory risk to H2 2027 close.
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'acquisition of'

body:
• Clarification announcement updating Fattal's 28 May 2026 response to PPHE's 27 May 2026 disclosure of a non-binding £22.00 cash per share proposal for PPHE shares not already owned.
  • Confirms the proposed offeror entity (should an offer proceed) is Fattal Hotels Ltd, which holds 1,660,173 PPHE ordinary shares; right reserved to bid via another group entity or a newly incorporated bidco.
  • Discloses that Guy Vardi (MD M&A, Fattal, and core deal team member) holds 1,350 PPHE shares, including 49 shares bought at £16.64 on 14 November 2025 — within the 3-month pre-offer-period window.
  • As a result, under Rule 6.1 of the Code, any offer must be on terms no less favourable than £16.64 per share (well below the £22.00 indicative price; effectively a floor, not a ceiling).
  • Reiterates the £22.00 indicative price represents a c.47% premium to PPHE's 1,500p close on 13 November 2025 (the Unaffected Date).
  • Transaction envisaged as all-cash, but Fattal reserves the right to alter consideration mix or reduce price with PPHE board consent, to reflect any PPHE dividend/distribution, if a third party makes a lower firm offer, or following a PPHE Rule 9 waiver transaction.
  • No PUSU 28-day deadline applies while Fattal remains in PPHE's formal sale process (Panel dispensation from Rule 2.6(a) previously granted).
  • Standard caveat: no certainty a firm offer will be made.
  • Advisers unchanged: Morgan Stanley (financial adviser), Baker & McKenzie (legal).

body:
• Appointment of Ikdeep Singh as independent Non-Executive Director, effective 1 July 2026.
  • Singh is currently Global President of Mars Pet Nutrition, a division of Mars' $30bn Petcare business operating in 120+ markets.
  • Previously President of L'Oréal's North American business and held senior roles at Procter & Gamble across North America, Greater China and global markets.
  • Holds an MBA from the Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania.
  • Board citing his expertise in business transformation and digital modernisation to support delivery of Greggs' growth strategy.
  • Chair Matt Davies said: "We are delighted to welcome Ikdeep to the Board. He brings significant leadership experience in global consumer businesses, together with strong operational and digital expertise. We look forward to the new perspectives he will bring to the Board."
  • No further disclosures required under UKLR 6.4.8R.

body:
• Announced a new royalty-based licensing partnership with Revolution Beauty to develop beauty and fragrance products across Debenhams Group's brand portfolio.
  • First collections to launch ahead of Christmas 2026, covering fragrance and gifting ranges for PrettyLittleThing, Karen Millen and boohooMAN; further launches planned across other Group brands.
  • Products to be distributed via Debenhams Group channels and selected retail partners (i.e. third-party wholesale reach).
  • Deal structure: Revolution Beauty assumes full responsibility for product development, manufacturing, and worldwide distribution, paying Debenhams an industry-standard royalty on sales; Debenhams retains brand approval rights over products, packaging, marketing and retail channels.
  • Framed by management as a direct expression of the "asset-lite" strategy, with the aim of generating recurring royalty income from the IP portfolio.
  • CEO Dan Finley: "We have been clear that we see significant global licensing opportunities for our brands, and this agreement with Revolution Beauty is a direct expression of our asset-lite strategy in action. Beauty is one of the most compelling category opportunities available to us."
  • Revolution Beauty CEO Tom Allsworth noted fragrance development is already at an advanced stage, with "exceptionally positive" early reaction to tested concepts, and flagged that the relationship has been "completely reset" since his return to the business last year.
  • No financial quantification of expected royalty income provided.

body:
• Launching a share buyback programme of up to £5 million (excluding expenses), commencing immediately and running until the earlier of 30 September 2026 or completion.
  • Peel Hunt appointed to execute the programme as riskless principal via on-market LSE purchases under an irrevocable agreement.
  • Operates under the 2025 Shareholder Authority allowing a maximum of 12,335,519 Ordinary Shares to be repurchased; authority expires at the earlier of 17 October 2026 or the 2026 AGM, after which continuation requires fresh shareholder approval.
  • Stated purpose: "to return surplus capital to shareholders" — repurchased shares to be held in treasury for employee share option obligations or cancelled.
  • Notably, the company will not rely on the MAR safe harbour, citing limited liquidity in the stock; daily purchases may exceed 25% of the 20-day average daily volume.
  • Context: market cap ~£225m, so the £5m programme represents ~2.2% of equity value — a modest but signalling capital return for the small-cap London office REIT.

body:
• Fundraise completed, raising aggregate gross proceeds of US$10.9 million (~£8.1m / C$15.1m) before expenses, comprising the Subscription, Placing, LIFE Offering and WRAP Retail Offer.
  • Breakdown: ~US$1.9m from Purebond subscription, ~US$8.4m from unsecured non-convertible Debt Securities issued to Purebond, and only ~US$0.6m from Placing/LIFE/WRAP investors combined — indicating very limited broader market participation.
  • 26,631,330 New Common Shares placed at Issue Price of 7p / C$0.13, equal to ~8.2% of pre-Fundraise share capital.
  • Purebond subscribing for 20,235,000 shares (First Tranche); Debt Securities not issued at the Issue Price.
  • Debt Securities expected to be repaid and proceeds redirected into Common Shares subject to independent shareholder approval at the 23 July 2026 AGM plus TSX approval; if not approved, Debt Securities remain outstanding on their terms.
  • Purebond's holding rises to ~19.99% on First Admission, increasing to ~36.3% if debt-to-equity conversion is approved — a material change in shareholder concentration.
  • Related party transaction under AIM Rule 13 and Canadian MI 61-101; Special Committee (excluding Kansagra and Baay) deemed terms fair and reasonable; relying on MI 61-101 minority approval exemptions (5.7(a) for shares, 5.7(f) for debt).
  • Material change report to be filed less than 21 days before closing due to accelerated timetable.
  • First Admission to AIM expected ~08:00 BST on 10 June 2026; TSX listing at market open same day.
  • Post-admission issued share capital: 351,364,939 Common Shares (no treasury).
  • FX references used: £1 = US$1.3372 / C$1.8619.

body:
• Launching a share buyback programme of up to £10 million aggregate consideration, commencing today and ending no later than 31 December 2026.
  • Purchased shares will be cancelled, reducing share capital.
  • Builds on £50 million already returned to shareholders via buybacks and dividends across FY25 and H1 FY26.
  • Board determined sufficient surplus cash exists under the capital allocation policy to fund the programme.
  • Brokers appointed: Peel Hunt and Deutsche Numis, acting as riskless/matched principals making independent trading decisions.
  • Maximum shares authorised under the 2026 AGM authority: 14,490,500 Ordinary Shares.
  • Capital allocation priorities reiterated: "investment into the business to deliver organic growth, maintaining a sustainable and progressive dividend policy, investing in additional growth opportunities and returning surplus cash to shareholders."
  • Context: £10m buyback equates to roughly 4.6% of the £218.8m market cap at the 151p closing price on announcement day.
