body:
• Life APE sales +28% to R3,732m; ex-large risk deal in Old Mutual Corporate, underlying growth was +15%.
  • Value of new business margin recovered to 1.6% (from 1.2% at 31 Dec 2025), still below medium-term target range; boost from the large risk deal is expected to normalise over the remainder of the year.
  • Gross written premiums roughly flat at R7,503m (+1%); Old Mutual Insure GWP +4%.
  • Gross flows +14% to R60,008m, led by Liability Driven Investments, Indexation and Equity/Multi-Asset in Old Mutual Investments.
  • Net client cash outflow narrowed 41% to R(3,180)m from R(5,396)m.
  • Gross loans and advances broadly flat at R19,486m; Old Mutual Finance book deliberately managed amid macro pressure; OM Bank to launch lending in H2 2026.
  • Old Mutual Insure net underwriting margin above the 5–8% target range; Limpopo/Mpumalanga floods did not cause material net losses; management flags signs of rate softening in the market.
  • Results from operations R2.5bn, broadly flat YoY despite OM Bank investment; shareholder investment returns significantly lower due to geopolitical-driven market volatility (US/Israel–Iran conflict).
  • OMLACSA solvency ratio rose to 186% (from 167%), within 165–200% target; aided by higher yield curve reducing SCR and lower prescribed equity shock.
  • Group solvency also improved, well within 155–185% target range.
  • R3bn share buyback completed on 15 May 2026: 214,860,122 shares repurchased and cancelled at avg 1,396 cents/share; described as value accretive (below 31 Dec 2025 group equity value per share); issued share capital reduced to 4,498,037,281.
  • Discretionary capital fell to R4.2bn (from R6.1bn at 31 Dec 2025) after R1.5bn buyback allocation in Q1; further R0.9bn allocated in Q2.
  • OM Bank customers jumped to 473,000 from 284,000 at year-end 2025; retail deposits nearly doubled to R541m from R272m.
  • Old Mutual Finance / OM Bank integration on track to conclude by year-end, subject to regulatory approvals.
  • Cost-saving commitments remain on track; Mass and Foundation persistency actions progressing.
  • Management flags ongoing headwind from rotation from guaranteed to market-linked annuities pressuring new business margins, and lower guaranteed annuity sales in Personal Finance.
  • Macro outlook: SA positive but near-term inflation risk from fuel/food could trigger rate hikes; Africa Regions growth positive but US/Israel–Iran conflict raising consumer inflation and pressuring topline/insurance margins.
  • Four strategic priorities reiterated: drive SA competitiveness; deepen Southern Africa leadership; establish OM Bank's right to win; evaluate/pivot on growth markets.

body:
• Launching a WRAP Retail Offer to raise up to US$1.0m (~£0.74m / C$1.4m) via issue of up to 10,640,714 new Common Shares at 7p (~C$0.13) on the Winterflood Retail Access Platform.
  • Issue price represents a 3.4% discount to the 7.25p AIM closing price on 3 June 2026.
  • Sits alongside a broader Fundraise targeting US$10–15m gross (~£7.4–11.2m / C$13.9–20.8m) comprising a Subscription by largest shareholder Purebond Limited, an accelerated bookbuild Placing, and a Canadian LIFE Offering — all at the same 7p issue price.
  • Retail Offer is conditional on completion of the Subscription, Placing and LIFE Offering, but those tranches are not conditional on the Retail Offer.
  • Minimum subscription £100 per investor; offer open only to UK eligible investors.
  • Retail Offer expected to close 4:30pm on 5 June 2026, result announced ~8 June 2026.
  • Admission to AIM and TSX expected 8:00am on 10 June 2026.
  • Canaccord Genuity acting as Nominated Adviser and Lead Bookrunner; Cavendish as Joint Bookrunner.
  • Use of proceeds as set out in the separate Fundraise Announcement (same day) — not re-stated in this retail offer notice.

body:
• Launching integrated fundraise of US$10m–US$15m (£7.4m–£11.2m) at 7p per share, a 3.4% discount to the 3 June close of 7.25p.
  • Largest shareholder Purebond (15.4%) to provide US$10m via subscription (plus unsecured non-convertible debenture mechanism where TSX limits cap equity issuance pre-EGM).
  • Structure: Purebond Subscription + institutional Placing (accelerated bookbuild) + Canadian LIFE Offering + WRAP retail offer (up to £0.74m).
  • Two-tranche issuance: First Admission 10 June 2026; Second Admission ~28 July 2026 subject to shareholder approval at 23 July GM (Purebond stake capped at 19.99% pre-approval).
  • Canaccord acting as NOMAD/Lead Bookrunner; Cavendish as Joint Bookrunner.
  • Use of proceeds: accelerate 2026 drilling, fund workovers, and reduce aged trade payables / resolve historical working capital constraints.
  • Proceeds allocation (US$10m / US$15m case): Working capital & vendor payables US$8.4m / US$10.1m; BR-2 drill US$4.0m; WD-4 two commitment wells nil / US$2.7m; CR-4 drill nil / US$1.8m; workovers US$1.1m; offset by forecast funds flow US$3.5m / US$4.7m.
  • WD-4 and CR-4 wells contingent on raising more than US$10m — lower scenario defers these.
  • Going concern flagged in March 2026 interims; Fundraise expected to mitigate risk of year-end 2026 covenant breach under Trinidad loan agreement.
  • Formal waiver secured from lender eliminating debt service coverage covenant testing for FY2026.
  • Q1 2026 production 4,657 boe/d, +8% YoY; April 2026 averaged 4,700 boe/d.
  • Production potential: ~4,700 boe/d growing to ~6,800 boe/d by March 2027 (+45%) on successful WD-4, Cascadura and Central block execution.
  • LNG pricing inflection: March 2026 Central block realised US$4.22/MMBtu under Train 4; gas above 7.1 Bcf gross threshold (Oct 2025–Sep 2026) eligible for Train 2/3 contract estimated at ~US$11.75/MMBtu (net of fees).
  • Currently redirecting Central field volumes to higher-yielding Train 2/3 plus domestic market through 19 July 2026 due to Train 4 / pipeline maintenance.
  • Train 4 agreement expires May 2027, enabling full transition of Central block to Train 2/3 pricing.
  • FR-1835 and FR-1836 (WD-8 block) drilled, completed and on production mid-May 2026; Cascadura booster compressor commissioning targeted for June 2026.
  • Q3 2026 plan: drill two WD-4 commitment wells, recompletions across Central/Ortoire, spud Central BR-2; Q4 2026: tie in WD-4 and BR-2, spud CR-4.
  • TSX trading halted pending announcement, resuming 5 June 2026.
  • 3-month lock-up on further share issuance post-Admission (standard carve-outs).

body:
• Revenue +13.7% YoY to $106.8m (vs $94.0m), driven by commercial/infrastructure activity, data center momentum, pricing, and more normal weather.
  • Gross profit +14.0% to $41.3m; gross margin +10bps to 38.6% (inflationary pressure on R&M offset by volume).
  • G&A as % of revenue down to 27.3% from 29.7%; absolute G&A up to $29.2m on labour and SBC.
  • Income from operations +46% to $12.1m.
  • Net income $2.5m vs ~breakeven; diluted EPS $0.04 vs $(0.01).
  • Adjusted EBITDA +17.4% to $26.4m; margin +80bps to 24.7%.
  • Segment performance: U.S. Concrete Pumping revenue +15.2% to $71.5m, Adj EBITDA +23.4% to $15.6m (net income swung to $0.7m from $1.6m loss).
  • U.S. Concrete Waste Management (Eco-Pan): revenue +12.7% to $20.3m; Adj EBITDA +15.7% to $7.7m.
  • U.K. Operations: revenue +8.2% to $14.9m (only +3.6% ex-FX, helped by $0.7m Templant contribution); Adj EBITDA -3.4% to $3.1m; swung to $0.1m net loss on labour/fuel/R&M inflation.
  • Residential and light commercial end markets remain weak; high rates cited as headwind.
  • Debt outstanding $425.6m; net debt $386.9m; total liquidity $346.3m (vs $352.5m YoY); leverage ratio 3.8x.
  • Treasury stock purchases of $7.2m in H1 (vs $8.5m prior year); no dividends paid in H1 FY26 (vs $53.1m prior year).
  • FY2026 guidance raised: Revenue $410–425m (prior $390–410m); Adjusted EBITDA $98–105m (prior $90–100m); Free cash flow at least $45m (prior $40m).
  • Outlook continues to assume no meaningful recovery in light commercial/residential markets in FY26.
  • Reiterates plan to pull forward ~$22m of capex from CY2027 into CY2026 ahead of stricter U.S. heavy-duty engine emissions rules taking effect 1 Jan 2027; none of the accelerated capex incurred yet as of 30 April 2026.
  • Q2 capex included ~$11m M&A and ~$3m growth investment (vs ~$2m growth-only in Q2 FY25), reducing Adj EBITDA less capex to $6m from $10m.
  • CEO Bruce Young: "we are raising our full year outlook and remain focused on disciplined operational execution, free cash flow generation, and positioning the business for long-term growth."
  • CEO on demand drivers: "We continue to benefit from healthy activity levels in commercial and infrastructure construction, particularly related to continued momentum in data center and infrastructure projects."

body:
• Net operating income £392.6m, up 15% (FY25: £340.1m) – best performance on record outside FY21 Covid year.
  • PBT £101.3m, up 20% (FY25: £84.5m); PBT margin 25.8% (+1.0ppt).
  • EBITDA £117.8m, up 14%; basic EPS 27.5p, up 22%.
  • Full-year dividend 13.8p, up 21% (final 8.3p); maintains 50% payout policy.
  • FY27 guidance: net operating income £460–480m (at least +17% YoY); opex ex-variable remuneration ~£280m.
  • "The Group has made a positive start to FY2027" with healthy client activity and strong B2B/institutional momentum.
  • Net trading revenue +16% to £289.5m; net investing revenue +30% to £57.8m.
  • Australian stockbroking record: net operating income A$140.3m, up 32% YoY.
  • Net interest income £41.4m, down 3% – resilient vs declining rates.
  • £5.2m Australian remediation charge (margin-netting, industry-wide ASIC matter) recognised in H1; £10.1m of prior provisions utilised in year.
  • Operating expenses +15% to £288.8m: staff +9% (£124.3m), IT £50.9m, legal/professional £21.6m (vs £13.1m), sales & marketing £40.8m (incl. remediation).
  • Westpac white-label partnership: transformational, ~A$39bn AUA across ~500k accounts; combined with ASB Bank, targeted launch in 2027; CMC AUA expected to rise from ~A$90bn to ~A$130bn post-completion.
  • Neobank API partnership delivered "exceptional growth in new account openings and trading activity".
  • Invest UK progressing Tier 1 white-label deal with a major international bank; signed Currys partnership.
  • Multi-asset platform v1 live (24/5 US equities, 24/7 crypto/bullion, CapX); v2 to be foundation of Super App in FY27.
  • CapX private market investments contributed ~£2.4m net trading income (unrealised gains incl. SpaceX, Payward, Blockratize); Strategy Inc preference stock acquired in treasury portfolio.
  • Treasury Management contributed ~£5.5m of treasury-related trading income.
  • CET1 ratio 292% (FY25: 272%); CET1 capital £390.3m; OFR £133.8m – substantial excess capital.
  • Cash £276.5m (+12%); total unencumbered liquid assets £300.1m; £55m undrawn RCF (renewed March 2026, £27.5m to 2027, £27.5m to 2029); £46.8m commercial paper outstanding (weighted avg 3.4%).
  • Post period-end: arrangements commenced for ~£200m European MTN debt issuance – targets more stable funding plus ~£100m regulatory capital benefit; subject to Board approval.
  • Initial margin requirements jumped to £231.4m (FY25: £92.2m) reflecting heightened bullion/commodity hedging needs amid H2 volatility.
  • Australian class action (CFDs/binary products) refined to claim period May 2016–April 2021; trial scheduled October 2027; no reliable estimate of financial effect.
  • FY27 strategic catalysts: Westpac/ASB launches, neobank expansion, Super App rollout, European certificates & warrants entry, tokenisation infrastructure, major sponsorship investments.
  • CEO Lord Cruddas: "FY2026 was another year of exceptional delivery for CMC, against a second half defined by extreme volatility" and notes business now "significantly driven by B2B and wholesale", positioning CMC as no longer "a traditional retail CFD provider".
  • Cruddas reiterated alignment: "I have never sold a share since IPO, and I have no plans to sell any for the foreseeable future."

time_period: Operational update covering May 2026 maintenance and post-shutdown ramp-up
body:
• Roan annual maintenance shutdown completed in May 2026; operations resumed at full capacity targeting 30,000tpm ROM throughput.
  • New fine copper concentrate dewatering circuit commissioning completed; fine-tuning underway for stable continuous operations.
  • Fines concentrate stream represents ~25.5% of contained copper in total ROM feed to Roan; circuit has demonstrated capacity above current fines production levels.
  • Roan holds significant stocks of naturally dried fine concentrate available for transport to Sable to buffer any interruptions.
  • Monthly fines concentrate contains ~140t of copper previously stockpiled at Roan.
  • Copper oxide flotation circuit upgraded during shutdown, targeting ~5% improvement in copper recoveries to partly offset cost inflation.
  • Local fuel prices up ~80%, directly impacting ROM and concentrate transport costs.
  • Acid consumption ~20% and transport ~16% of monthly expenditure — flagged as key cost pressures.
  • Optional front-end DMS circuit to further increase Roan throughput under consideration during June 2026.
  • Updated copper production guidance deferred until steady-state performance across integrated Roan-Sable circuit is demonstrated.
  • May production was impacted by the planned shutdown; June 2026 expected to give clearer read on sustainable run-rate and recoveries.
  • CEO Leon Coetzer: "The successful progression of the Roan dewatering circuit through commissioning and ramp-up marks another important milestone in the expansion of our Zambia copper operations."
  • CEO on flexibility: "The ability to process both newly generated fine concentrate material together with the historical stockpile provides increased operational flexibility and supports the continued scaling of our integrated copper strategy."
  • Group strategic target reiterated: 25,000tpa copper production via Roan concentrator, Sable refinery and Large Waste Rock Project.
period_detail: Operational update covering May 2026 maintenance and post-shutdown ramp-up

body:
• Secured three further lettings at City Tower, EC2, taking leasing of refurbished space to 69%.
  • Deals total 13,600 sq ft, signed at an average 3.1% premium to ERV.
  • Once fully let, the 43,000 sq ft Fully Managed space is expected to deliver £8.5m annual rent at an average £198 per sq ft.
  • GPE has committed to deliver a further 43,600 sq ft of Fully Managed offices, plus new entrance, café, gym and meeting room suite.
  • On completion in early 2027, approximately 90% of the building will have been converted to Fully Managed.
  • Simon Rowley, Leasing & Flex Workspaces Director: "These lettings underline the pace of demand for our Fully Managed offer and our ability to convert that demand into rental income quickly. At City Tower, we are delivering exactly what our strategy is designed to achieve, repositioning well located assets into flexible space that drives income growth and creates long term value."

body:
• Clare Francis to step down as Non-Executive Director and Chair of the Risk Committee at the conclusion of the 2026 AGM, not seeking re-election.
  • Francis has served on the Board for 4 years, also sitting on Audit, Nomination and Remuneration Committees.
  • She will support an orderly handover in the interim.
  • Nomination committee has initiated the search for a new director and Risk Committee Chair.
  • Chairman Paul Wainscott: "The Board would like to express its sincere appreciation to Clare for her valuable contribution as Chair of our Risk Committee and as a non-executive board member. We wish her well for the future as she focuses on the increasing commitments across her other regulated and advisory roles."

body:
• Appointed Dr Claudia Manning and Dr Phumla Mnganga as independent non-executive directors, effective 01 June 2026.
  • Dr Manning joins the Responsible Business Committee (and its Group Customer sub-committee) and the Remuneration Committee.
  • Dr Mnganga joins the Remuneration Committee and the Technology & Platforms Committee.
  • Dr Manning's background: 25+ years in investment, finance and economic development; currently Investment Principal at SA SME Fund; NED at Rothschild & Co South Africa and Chair of Mondi Zimele; prior roles at Adcock Ingram, DBSA and PIC.
  • Dr Mnganga's background: current NED at Altron, Adcorp and Exxaro; founder of Lehumo Women's Investment Holdings; prior chair of Crookes Brothers SA and Novus Holdings; executive roles at Gold Circle, Tongaat-Hulett and Deloitte Consulting.
  • Chairman Trevor Manuel framed the appointments as part of "disciplined growth and value creation" and a "thoughtful and orderly process of Board renewal", reflecting the staggered rotation policy tied to tenure limits since the 2018 incorporation.
  • Board confirmed satisfactory fit and proper assessments; no positive integrity disclosures required under JSE Listings Requirements section 6.74.

body:
• Launching the first £50 million tranche of the previously announced £100 million 2026 share buy-back programme, commencing on or after 3 June 2026 and ending no later than 31 December 2026.
  • Deutsche Bank (London branch) appointed as joint-broker to execute the tranche, acting as principal independently during closed periods.
  • Shareholder authority at 7 May 2026 AGM permits repurchase of up to 53,903,751 ordinary shares.
  • Sole purpose is to reduce share capital — repurchased shares will be cancelled or held in treasury (treasury shares used for employee share option obligations, no voting/dividend rights).
  • Consistent with capital allocation policy: priority remains investment in depots, manufacturing and logistics, alongside a progressive dividend.

• Closed 498 homes in May 2026, up 19.7% YoY vs 416 in May 2025.
  • Figure includes 24 currently or previously leased single-family rental homes (i.e. bulk/rental conversions inflating the headline).
  • 150 active selling communities as of 31 May 2026.
  • Share price reaction: stock closed -4.56% at $47.55 on 4 June 2026, suggesting the market viewed the underlying retail closings pace (~474 ex-rentals) as soft despite the headline growth.

body:
• Fredrik Tønnesen appointed Group CEO, effective 3 August 2026, replacing Alex Baldock.
  • Tønnesen currently CEO of Currys' Nordics business (c.40% of Group revenue) since March 2023; 20-year Currys veteran who started as a shop-floor sales assistant.
  • Under his Nordics leadership, operating profits more than tripled alongside world-class colleague and customer satisfaction scores.
  • Appointment followed a comprehensive search of internal and external candidates.
  • Alex Baldock steps down from the Board on 3 August 2026, supports transition until departure on 31 August 2026.
  • Baldock's leaving arrangements per Directors' Remuneration Policy and service contract; full details to be disclosed on corporate website and in 2025/26 Annual Report.
  • Reiterates prior 19 May 2026 guidance: FY adjusted PBT c.£191m and year-end net cash of more than £170m.
  • Full year results scheduled for 2 July 2026.
  • Chair Ian Dyson: "He understands our customers, our colleagues and our culture from the inside, and brings the right combination of clarity, energy and leadership to take Currys forward, building on the strong foundations that Alex and the team have put in place."
  • Incoming CEO Tønnesen: "My job, with the full support of the leadership team and all my colleagues, is to keep this momentum going and find every way to accelerate it."
  • Outgoing CEO Baldock: "Fredrik is an outstanding leader… His experience and track record speak for themselves, most recently in leading the Nordics' recovery."

body:
• First retailer globally to exceed 1 million Trustpilot reviews while maintaining a 4.9/5 rating — other retailers have hit 1m reviews but none at this score.
  • Approaching 1 million five-star reviews within the next few months.
  • Non-regulatory PR/marketing announcement — no financial, trading, or guidance content.
  • CEO John Roberts: "That rating, at that scale, is only ever earned... highlights AO's structural advantage, which we have been building with world class quality at scale since 2000."
  • Trustpilot CEO Adrian Blair attributes the achievement to AO owning "every step of a customer's journey, from click to installation through to recycling."
  • Retail Director Vicky Monk frames trust as the key competitive differentiator vs peers (implicit read-across vs Currys and other electricals retailers).

body:
• Arno Kitts and Rick Lacaille appointed as Non-Executive Directors, effective 3 June 2026; both to stand for election at 2027 AGM.
  • Kitts brings 30+ years in asset management/insurance, formerly MD of BlackRock UK Institutional and CEO of BlackRock Life; founder of Perspective Investments; ex-NED at Pension Insurance Corporation.
  • Lacaille spent 20+ years at State Street Global Advisors, latterly Global CIO and Global Head of ESG; currently Senior Advisor at Enzo Advisors and Executive Advisor at Arab Invest.
  • NED fees set at £75,000 per annum each; neither holds any ABDN shares at date of announcement.
  • Post-changes, Board comprises 2 executive directors, 7 NEDs plus the Chair (4 women, 6 men); both deemed independent under UK Corporate Governance Code.
  • Chair Jonathan Asquith: "I am delighted to welcome Arno and Rick to our Board. Both have considerable experience in global asset management, combining significant investment expertise with distribution, strategy and governance. Together, they bring highly complementary skills and perspectives and significant industry insight, which will be of considerable value as we execute Aberdeen's strategy to become the UK's leading wealth and investments business."

body:
• Group GMV returned to growth, up 0.5% YoY, with May notably stronger at ~8% GMV growth.
  • Growth led by Debenhams brand and PrettyLittleThing; improvements also at Boohoo, BoohooMan and Karen Millen.
  • Gross margin expanded to 53.5% from 52.1% YoY; returns rate down c.5% in the quarter.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin expanded materially with a substantial YoY increase in Adjusted EBITDA.
  • Exceptional costs cut by 72% in Q1; capex down 54% YoY.
  • Reiterating guidance: double-digit % growth in FY27 Adjusted EBITDA from the £53m guided for FY26 (given in March).
  • Net debt / Adjusted EBITDA on track to fall below 1x this year, aided by trading cashflow and disposals of the Burnley property and the US warehouse (both planned for current year).
  • Fixed costs on track to reduce to £100m through 2027 — a c.£200m cumulative reduction delivered by the new management team since appointment.
  • Asset-light transition: all brands now on marketplace model; c.25k brands/partners in the Group ecosystem.
  • Capex trajectory: £27.5m FY25 → £16m FY26 → £8m FY27 (50% YoY reduction).
  • Lease costs to reduce to £13m this year, falling further to £6m once US vacant property lease is exited (ongoing £6m covers automated Sheffield warehouse, Manchester HQ, small London footprint).
  • Interest costs and exceptional items also expected to fall materially in FY27.
  • Board confident of delivering double-digit Adjusted EBITDA growth and free cash flow in FY27.
  • Full year results for period ended 28 February 2026 to be released within the next two weeks.
  • CEO Dan Finley: "Debenhams Group has returned to growth, and Q1 marks the inflection point we have been working towards."
  • CEO Dan Finley: "With the cost out ahead of plan and strong momentum carried into the year, the Board's confidence has grown and we are reiterating our guidance of double-digit Adjusted EBITDA growth in FY27."

body:
• Completed sale of six logistics assets to EQT Real Estate for £199 million in cash.
  • Assets sold in line with book value; comprise big box and urban logistics properties at Leamington Spa, Peterborough, Didcot and Kettering.
  • Disposed assets generate £12 million of contracted annual rent (implied gross yield ~6.0%).
  • Sale was pre-flagged in the most recent trading update.
  • Proceeds to be recycled into development-led logistics (target yield on cost 6-8%) and data centre projects (target yield on cost 9-11%).
  • CFO Frankie Whitehead: "we have been one of the most proactive recyclers of capital in our sector, delivering sales of nearly £1 billion over the past three years."
  • Proceeds will "support investment in higher returning development-led opportunities and further strengthen our balance sheet."
  • Context: company has secured first data centre development opportunities of over 250MW, with a pipeline of c.1 GW of further opportunities.

body:
• AUM up 31% to £171.8bn (FY25: £130.8bn), driven by £18.3bn Sanlam take-on, £19.9bn positive market/FX, and £2.8bn net inflows.
  • Return to positive net flows of £2.8bn vs net outflows of £(4.9)bn in FY25 — a key inflection after prior-year redemptions.
  • Including Sanlam, total net flows £21.1bn.
  • Average AUM up 18% to £151.8bn.
  • Net revenue £650.2m (+9% from £594.6m); adjusted operating revenue £659.3m (+9%).
  • Adjusted operating profit +12% to £211.3m; adjusted operating margin 32.0% (+80bps from 31.2%).
  • Profit before tax £207.5m (+2%); profit after tax £153.5m (+2%).
  • Basic EPS 17.5p (+2%); Adjusted EPS 17.4p (+12%) — note adjusted EPS uses weighted share count to neutralise Sanlam share issuance.
  • Full-year dividend 13.4p (+10%); final dividend 7.4p, payable 6 August 2026.
  • Sanlam transaction completed, adding £18.3bn AUM (£13.7m shares for Sanlam UK + 112.0m shares for Sanlam SA issued).
  • Net flows by asset class: Equities +£1.24bn (vs -£2.39bn), Fixed Income +£1.11bn (vs -£1.75bn), Multi-asset -£1.08bn, Alternatives +£0.44bn, SA fund platform +£1.11bn.
  • Net flows by region: Asia Pacific +£3.64bn the standout; Europe +£0.97bn, Americas +£0.78bn; UK -£1.47bn and Africa -£1.09bn remained in outflow (though UK outflows narrowed sharply from -£3.90bn).
  • Investment performance mixed: 1-year outperformance dropped to 56% (from 68%), 5-year to 63% (from 72%), 10-year to 75% (from 81%); 3-year improved to 69% (from 59%).
  • Operating expenses £468.5m (+12%); staff expenses £297.5m (+14%) — cost growth running ahead of revenue ex-Sanlam effects.
  • Net interest income fell to £12.0m (FY25: £16.0m); adjusted net interest income £15.4m vs £19.3m.
  • Shares in issue rose to 1,005.1m (FY25: 896.8m) following Sanlam issuance.
  • CEO Hendrik du Toit: "Ninety One is a resilient and robust business with positive momentum. The demand recovery for emerging markets is visible and our offering competitive. We are in a stronger position than a year ago."
  • Du Toit added: "We are investing through the cycle in talent and technology to be future fit... We are committed to cost and operating discipline and our focus remains on investment performance and client service."
  • UK outflows attributed to client rebalancing outweighing large new wins, "with almost all still remaining clients in the UK".

body:
• Acquiring DIY Kitchens (Ultima Furniture Systems) for £390m enterprise value on cash/debt-free basis: £292.5m cash + £97.5m in Howdens shares (12.7m new shares at 766p, 5-day VWAP).
  • Cash portion funded via existing cash plus a new £240m bank facility.
  • Multiple ~8.5x LTM EBITDA to March 2026; deal includes freehold property assets valued at ~£55m.
  • Immediately accretive to revenue, EBIT margin, and EPS; returns above cost of capital; subject to customary regulatory approvals.
  • DIY Kitchens 2025 financials: revenue £136m, EBIT £37m (27% EBIT margin); revenue growth >17% p.a. over past 5 years.
  • Provides Howdens direct access to non-trade (consumer) end-customers via online channel — distinct from its trade-only model; operated as a standalone business post-completion.
  • Vertically integrated, made-to-order online model; self-manufactured product ~40% of COGS; supported by 2 destination showrooms with a 3rd under construction in Scotland.
  • Cost synergies identified over time in raw materials, sourcing and machinery (not quantified).
  • Balance sheet: Group expected to remain in net cash post-transaction.
  • £100m share buyback for 2026 unchanged; progressive dividend policy maintained; capital allocation priorities unchanged.
  • Current trading: "Year to date trading has continued to be in line with our expectations and we remain on track with the outlook for 2026."
  • H1 2026 results scheduled for 23 July 2026.
  • Howdens reiterates depot expansion target: 893 UK locations rising to 1,000 over time.
  • Seller is Ling Chase Holdings, investment vehicle of the founding Ellis family.
  • CEO Andrew Livingston: "The acquisition of DIY Kitchens, which will be operated on a standalone basis, adds a complementary very profitable, business to the Group, providing access to non-trade end customers through its direct online channel with self-service planning, design and ordering tools."

I cannot summarise an article whose body text was not included. The only retrievable facts from the page are:

Paragon Banking Group Plc (PAG)

Publish Date 02 June 2026

body:
• Ag Economy Barometer fell to 119 in May, down from 121 in April.
  • Current Conditions Index dropped 8 points to its lowest level since December 2024; Future Expectations Index rose 1 point.
  • High input costs cited as top concern by a record 51% of respondents; 46% said input costs are limiting financial improvement this year.
  • Only 14% reported their farm is better off financially than a year ago; 22% expect improvement over the next 12 months.
  • Farm Capital Investment Index fell 3 points to 41, lowest since September 2024, signalling reduced appetite for large investments.
  • ~two-thirds expect 2026 net farm income to be reduced.
  • Among 2025 corn planters: ~50% expect break-even prices up to 6% higher; 30% expect break-even prices up 10%+.
  • Labour: 39% hire nonfamily workers; of those, 44% report some/a lot of difficulty hiring in 2026.
  • AI adoption scepticism: 59% say AI tools would not improve their labour/equipment situation; 37% a little; only 4% a lot.
  • Farmland value expectations rose despite weaker current sentiment: Short-Term Index 121→130; Long-Term Index 155→160.
  • "Right direction" sentiment for the U.S. fell to 52% in May, the lowest since the question began (July 2025), vs ~71% average in H2 2025.
  • Sharp divergence in 5-year outlook: only 31% expect good times for crop producers vs 68% for livestock producers.
  • Quote — Michael Langemeier (principal investigator): "The persistence of high input costs is especially challenging in an environment where many producers are already operating with tighter margins."
  • Quote — Langemeier: "When producers see fewer opportunities to improve their financial position despite strong operational management, it tends to weigh heavily on perceptions of current conditions and investment plans."

body:
• Joanne McNamara appointed Chief Executive Officer, joining from Oxford Properties (the real estate arm of Canadian pension fund OMERS), where she is currently Executive Vice President, Europe.
  • McNamara has over 20 years' real estate experience, having joined Oxford in 2010 and built its European team to 70+, overseeing a portfolio of ~£8bn AUM across offices, retail, logistics and residential; previously at Hammerson and DTZ.
  • Sits on Oxford's Global Executive and Investment Committees and OMERS portfolio management committee; was a Non-Executive Director of ESR Group until 2025.
  • Maximum 6-month notice period; expected to join by end of November 2026 at the latest.
  • Base salary of £800,000 per annum, consistent with the Directors' Remuneration Policy approved at the July 2025 AGM.
  • Buyout package to compensate for forfeited Oxford awards: up to £900,000 cash in 2027 (replacing lost 2026 bonus), a £1m restricted share award vesting in 2027 (service-only), and two share awards of £2m each subject to service, performance, malus and clawback conditions mirroring the 2025 CFO LTIP and forthcoming 2026 LTIP.
  • Will also receive a 2026 LTIP award of up to 250% of salary on joining (pro-rated for start date).
  • Chairman William Rucker noted Oxford is a business British Land knows well as joint venture partner on the Leadenhall Building, describing McNamara as "one of Europe's most respected real estate professionals" with "a strong reputation for decisive leadership."
  • McNamara stated: "British Land is a business that I have always admired, with an impressive track record of delivering and managing best in class places across the UK and an expert team at its helm."
  • Context: portfolio valued at £15.8bn (British Land share: £10.1bn) as at 31 March 2026; appointment fills the CEO seat with a candidate bringing private capital/JV expertise relevant to British Land's London campuses and retail parks strategy.

body:
• Passengers up 26.1% YoY to 7.13m, accelerating from 22% growth in April heading into summer.
  • Capacity rose 25.4% YoY to 7.77m seats.
  • Load factor 91.7%, +0.5ppts YoY (rolling 12m load factor 90.7%, -0.5ppts).
  • Rolling 12m passengers 72.4m (+12.3%); capacity 79.9m seats (+12.9%).
  • Tel Aviv services resumed 28 May after suspension during Middle East conflict, reconnecting Israel to Athens, Bratislava, Bucharest, Budapest, Krakow, Larnaca, London, Milan, Rome, Sofia, and Warsaw.
  • Italian expansion: 11th aircraft at Milan Malpensa from 14 Sept; 3rd at Naples and 4th at Catania from 14 Dec — based fleet in Italy growing from 29 (summer 2025) to 41 aircraft across 7 bases this summer.
  • New distribution agreement with Atlas (corporate travel API), following similar deals with Kyte and Travelfusion.
  • Operated 77 incremental flights from London and Paris for UEFA Champions League final in Budapest at 99.9% load factor.
  • CO2 emissions 566,401 MT (+12.8% YoY); CO2 intensity 49.4g per pax/km, -3.2% YoY (rolling 12m intensity 50.3g, -3.0%); figures exclude SAF uplift benefit.
  • Fleet now 264 Airbus A320/A321; FY2026 passengers cited at 69.7m.

body:
• Completed acquisition of Flock, a digital commercial fleet insurance provider with telemetry-based proposition, following regulatory approval.
  • Deal values Flock equity at £80m (originally announced 12 February).
  • Flock becomes Admiral's telemetry fleet insurance proposition; existing platform and team integrated into Admiral's fleet offering.
  • Flock CEO Ed Leon Klinger joins Admiral Pioneer's leadership team; sits within Admiral's venture-building arm.
  • Since announcement, Admiral has already launched a new haulage fleet insurance product citing "strong early demand" — first major segment expansion.
  • Strategic rationale: broaden motor proposition into commercial fleet, leveraging data/tech, pricing, claims and risk management strengths.
  • Flock founder Antton Peña: "As part of Admiral, we will be able to support even more fleets and launch products that push what is possible in motor insurance."
  • Advisors: GP Bullhound (financial) and Clifford Chance (legal) for Admiral; Continuum (financial) and Osborne Clarke (legal) for Flock.
  • No financial impact guidance (earnings, capital, solvency) disclosed — bolt-on scale relative to Admiral's ~£10bn market cap.

time_period: H1 2026 (six months ended 30 April 2026) / FY 2026 outlook
body:
• FY 2026 PBT guidance cut to £69m–£74m as Board takes a more cautious view; prior commentary at 23 March 2026 FY25 results had indicated start of FY26 in line with expectations.
  • H1 2026 Group revenue +2%, with trading softening sharply in April, particularly in French photobooth and laundry businesses.
  • Photo.ME revenue down 17% in April vs -6% across H1 2026, hit by weaker demand for official photo ID amid travel uncertainty.
  • Wash.ME (higher-margin) revenue +3% in April vs +17% in H1 2026 overall — material deceleration in the growth engine.
  • Equipment sales revenue down 14% YoY in H1, reflecting strategic shift toward operating instant-service equipment rather than selling it.
  • Board attributes softness to "a shift in consumer spending patterns driven by lower consumer confidence due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East."
  • Trading improved through May, but Board "does not expect trading patterns to normalise while conflict in the Middle East and the subsequent uncertainty in the macroeconomic landscape continue."
  • Wash.ME rollout of 1,300+ laundry machines in FY 2026 remains on track — capex/expansion plans unchanged.
  • Balance sheet described as strong; Board reiterates confidence in long-term growth plans.
  • Interim results scheduled for latter half of July 2026.

• Rule 2.4 announcement from Castlelake, L.P. confirming it is in the early stages of considering a possible offer for easyJet, following recent market speculation.
  • No approach has yet been made to the easyJet Board.
  • No certainty that any firm offer will be made, nor as to terms.
  • PUSU deadline: 5.00pm on 26 June 2026 — by which Castlelake must either announce a firm intention to bid under Rule 2.7 or walk away (extendable only with Takeover Panel consent).
  • Statement issued by Castlelake (US-based alternative asset manager, known for aviation/aircraft leasing investments) rather than easyJet itself; no indication of cash vs. securities consideration, price, or financing structure.
  • easyJet market cap referenced at ~£2.98bn (share price 398p) at time of announcement.

body:
• FFO up 8.4% to €133.5m (2025: €123.2m); FFO per share +4.5% to 8.82c, progressing towards near-term €150m target with new €175m FFO target launched.
  • Revenue +9.4% to €347.5m (2025: €317.5m).
  • PBT +4.9% to €211.4m; profit after tax +29.0% to €229.8m, boosted by €40.2m one-off deferred tax release from phased German corporate tax rate cut (15% → 10% by 2032).
  • EPRA EPS down 7.8% to 7.43c (2025: 8.06c) on FX translation, finance fees and equity raise dilution; basic EPS +24.3% to 15.16c.
  • Like-for-like annualised rent roll +6.4% to €224.2m; total rent roll +18.4% to €258.6m including acquisitions — 12th consecutive year of LFL rent roll growth above 5%.
  • Germany LFL rent roll +7.3%; UK LFL rent roll +4.6% (£).
  • LFL occupancy improved to 87.5% (from 86.5%); total occupancy down to 85.0% (from 85.9%) due to acquisition mix.
  • Portfolio value +20.5% to €2,969.4m; €111.3m valuation gain (vs €81.0m prior year) — largely income-led, minimal yield compression.
  • Adjusted NAV/share +5.0% to 124.78c, ahead of consensus; EPRA NTA/share +4.3% to 122.71c.
  • 25th consecutive dividend increase: H2 DPS 3.22c (+4.2%); full-year DPS 6.40c (+4.1%).
  • €463.3m of acquisitions completed/notarised (13 assets): €271.1m across 9 German assets (8.2% gross yield, 85.7% occupancy); £166.2m (€192.2m) across 4 UK assets (6.7% gross yield, 84.2% occupancy), including Hartlebury Industrial Estate for £107.0m.
  • €155.8m deployed into defence-related tenant assets — strategic tilt highlighted as a key growth driver.
  • Balance sheet: cash €372.7m (down from €571.3m) plus €300m undrawn RCF (doubled from €150m in March 2026, Barclays added to syndicate), ahead of €400m bond repayment due June 2026.
  • Net LTV rose to 36.1% (2025: 31.4%); Net Debt/EBITDA 6.6x (5.2x); WACD 2.5% (2.6%); WAULT of debt 3.2 years (4.2 years).
  • Capital actions: €105m tap of 2028 bonds (Sept 2025); oversubscribed €88.3m equity raise in Feb 2026 (€85.9m net).
  • Capex: 38% average ROI on €29.2m value-add capex transforming 250,000+ sqm over 3 years; first stand-alone self-storage store under construction in Berlin Gartenfeld (opening end of year).
  • Outlook: "Trading in line with management expectations in the new financial year"; no impact yet seen from Middle East conflict on occupier demand.
  • CEO Andrew Coombs: "The projected rise in UK and German government defence spending is expected to have a material effect on demand for the types of industrial space Sirius provides, with the urgency of need making existing stock the only feasible option at scale."
  • Post-balance-sheet activity: notarised Kiel acquisition (€93.4m, completed 1 April 2026); Fulda acquisition (€49.8m); conditional Luton self-storage acquisition (£5.6m).
  • Vantage Point (Gloucester): largest tenant managed out; ~one-third re-let at >50% of prior rent; remaining space being subdivided/repurposed — excluded from LFL metrics.

body:
• Rule 2.4 response to Castlelake, L.P.'s 29 May 2026 announcement that it is in the early stages of considering a possible offer for easyJet.
  • Board confirms no discussions, approach or proposal has been received from Castlelake.
  • Board will assess any proposal on valuation and deliverability, flagging both as concerns.
  • On valuation: Board calls timing "highly opportunistic" with share price "temporarily depressed due to the current situation in the Middle East and its impact on customer confidence and jet fuel prices."
  • On deliverability: cites "considerable regulatory, financial and other execution challenges" of a takeover.
  • Defends standalone case: investment grade balance sheet with a net cash position, strong customer satisfaction and high employee engagement.
  • Reiterates medium-term target of >£1bn profit before tax.
  • PUSU deadline: Castlelake must announce firm intention or walk away by 5.00pm on 26 June 2026.
  • Shareholders advised to take no action; easyJet now in an offer period under the Code.
  • Shares in issue at 29 May 2026: 758,010,025 ordinary shares; no treasury shares.

time_period: FY26 (year ending 30 June 2026) operational update, with FY27 guidance
body:
• Record annual gold production of ~275,000oz, up ~40% YoY (FY25: 196,527oz), landing at the low end of FY26 guidance range (275,000–292,000oz).
  • H2 FY26 production ~147,000oz, up ~14% vs H1 (128,296oz).
  • Strong contributions from Elikhulu, MTR, Evander and Barberton underground offset slower-than-expected ramp-up at Tennant Mines (Australia).
  • FY26 AISC expected at top end of guidance ~US$1,870/oz (US$/ZAR 17.00), despite inflationary pressures; Group remains fully unhedged.
  • Projected cash position of ~US$220m at FY26 year-end; Group now in net cash position (vs net debt of US$46.2m at 31 Dec 2025); only outstanding debt is DMTNs of US$49.7m.
  • Cash impacted by US$10.3m strategic investment in CuFe Limited and US$7.0m to secure strategic cyanide supplies.
  • FY27 production guidance: 280,000–302,000oz at AISC US$2,075–US$2,175/oz (US$/ZAR 17.00) — note material AISC step-up (~11–16% vs FY26) reflecting above-inflation cost increases for reagents, electricity and key inputs.
  • FY27 capex guidance raised to US$324m from US$267m previously (US$282m expansion + US$42m sustaining), driven by accelerated White Devil development, Nobles plant crusher/filter belt, fast-tracked exploration and finalised renewable energy project costs.
  • FY26 capex forecast US$180m, including US$10m for White Devil, ~US$8m Tennant solar plant, ~US$3m for Soweto Cluster DFS.
  • Tennant Mines (Australia): FY26 production ~34,000oz; FY26H2 ~18,500oz (+19% vs H1); head grade lifted to ~1.5g/t (H1: 1.3g/t); Q4 annualised run-rate ~45,000oz; FY27 guidance 48,000–52,000oz with target of ~100,000oz p.a. within three years.
  • White Devil open pit: Indicated Resource >3Mt at 3.73g/t (378koz); Juno 1.96Mt at 4.16g/t (262koz); Golden Forty 0.5Mt at 7.25g/t (114koz) — to underpin Tennant production over next five years.
  • MTR: FY26H2 ~30,500oz, FY26 ~52,000oz; calcine layer continues to impact grade/recoveries, expected to be mined through in Q1 FY27, after which production forecast >60,000oz/year.
  • Soweto Cluster DFS on track for completion by June 2026 — potential 600ktpm standalone facility producing ~30,000–35,000oz p.a. over 15-year LOM.
  • MTR expansion study for hard rock circuit could add a further 20,000–30,000oz p.a.
  • Elikhulu: FY26 production >56,500oz (FY25: 52,606oz) — highest since FY20.
  • Evander underground: FY26 ~47,000oz, +68% YoY; recovered grade jumped to >11g/t (FY25: 6.8g/t) on 8 Shaft reaching 24 Level B-Raise.
  • Barberton underground: FY26 ~72,000oz (FY25: 68,550oz), +5% YoY.
  • BTRP: FY26 ~13,000oz (FY25: 15,224oz); US$5.9m flotation circuit approved (completion end-2026) to improve recoveries and enable refractory ore treatment.
  • Emmerson Resources acquisition (Tennant Creek consolidation): Scheme Meeting 15 June 2026, implementation 1 July 2026; Pan African will undertake an ASX listing as part of the transaction.
  • Acquired 15% stake in ASX-listed CuFe for ~A$15.35m on 19 May 2026 to unlock synergies with Group's Warrego copper-gold project.
  • Middle East contingency: rolling three-month cyanide supply secured at South African operations; one-month on-site diesel storage at Tennant Mines.
  • Final results scheduled for ~16 September 2026.
  • CEO Cobus Loots: "It has been an exceptional year for Pan African Resources, with the Group set to achieve its record gold production target, increasing gold output by some 40% year-on-year. Financially, the Group has never been in a stronger position."
  • CEO on outlook: "Our very robust financial position will allow us to continue our considered growth trajectory, executing into initiatives to expand annual gold output to 300,000oz and beyond, while also further increasing cash returned to shareholders."
  • CEO on Tennant: "In the next financial year, we expect a much improved performance from Tennant Mines, with a full year of mining from the high-grade White Devil deposit, and a clear pathway to growing Australian gold production to ~100,000oz per annum in the next three years."

body:
• CEO Jim Johnson has given notice of intention to retire as a Director by mid-2027.
  • Nomination Committee has launched a succession process considering both internal and external candidates.
  • Johnson joined Hunting in 1992, became COO in 2011, and was appointed CEO in 2017; he has overseen delivery of the Hunting 2030 Strategy announced in 2023.
  • Chair Stuart M. Brightman: "Having successfully led the repositioning of the Group since 2020, particularly in relation to our subsea and offshore businesses, Jim will leave the Company in a strong position to capitalise on future energy and power demand and I look forward to continuing to work with him over the coming months to ensure an orderly hand over to his successor in due course."
  • Johnson: "It has been a huge privilege to play a part in the Hunting story and to have led the business over the last nine years. I am very proud of how Hunting has navigated a period of almost unprecedented change to emerge as one of the leading businesses in our sector."
  • No interim CEO named; further updates on the search to follow.

body:
• CEO Andrew Knott to acquire 128,550,000 existing Ordinary Shares (~6% of issued share capital) from the Savannah Energy 2022 Trust (EBT) for aggregate consideration of ~£8.74m.
  • Acquisition takes Knott's aggregate interest in the Company to ~20% of issued share capital, made via wholly-owned vehicle Lothian Capital Partners 6 Limited.
  • Purchase price of 6.8p per share, equal to the middle market closing price on Friday 29 May 2026 (i.e. no premium).
  • Non-dilutive secondary acquisition — no new shares issued, no treasury/cancellation activity by the Company.
  • Payment structure: ~£1.75m Initial Consideration in cash on Completion, balance deferred and payable in cash over 6 years, with 6% p.a. interest (~£420k annual interest payments); deferred balance due on 6th anniversary.
  • Purchaser has the option to settle some/all of the final deferred amount by transferring Savannah ordinary shares (at market value) to the Trust.
  • Knott has provided a personal guarantee to the EBT for the deferred consideration plus an asset and liability letter to the Trustee.
  • Purchaser has 50 business days from 30 May 2026 to pay the Initial Consideration; shares transfer on payment.
  • EBT currently holds 205,701,993 Ordinary Shares (was issued 210m new shares on 22 October 2025).
  • Cancellation of all Knott's outstanding options over 38,347,622 Ordinary Shares for nil consideration, conditional on Completion — 21,312,418 over EBT-held shares and 17,035,204 over unissued shares — reducing future dilution risk.
  • Relationship Agreement entered into with Knott and his vehicles, providing minority shareholder/governance protections: arm's length related-party dealings, related party voting restrictions, commitment to maintain at least two independent Directors, AIM Rules compliance.
  • Board nomination rights: at ≥20% but <27.5% holding, right to nominate one Director but only if Knott is not himself a Director; at ≥27.5%, right to nominate one Director if Knott is a Director, or up to two if he is not — capping additional representation while Knott remains CEO.
  • Governance/representation provisions suspend if Knott's aggregate holding falls below 20%, reinstated if threshold re-met.
  • Company framing: transaction "significantly increase[s] Mr Knott's ownership interest in Savannah, further strengthening his alignment with the Company's long-term shareholders."
  • Board rationale on option cancellation: "in the best interests of shareholders as a whole, as it reduces potential future dilution and simplifies Mr Knott's long-term economic alignment with shareholders by replacing option-based economics with a materially increased direct equity interest."
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'to acquire'

body:
• Executed $500m corporate debt facility with Tier 1 syndicate of ANZ, ING, HSBC, NAB and Westpac, formalising the binding commitment letter from December 2025 with no mandatory hedging requirement.
  • Facility structure: Facility A $250m RCF (undrawn, 5-year tenor); Facility B $225m RCF (undrawn, 7-year tenor); CIF $25m (drawn to $17.87m at 31 May 2026) for bank/performance guarantees.
  • Financial close achieved on Facility A and CIF; Facility B close targeted end-June 2026 pending updated Telfer Ore Reserve estimate.
  • Former $75m ANZ/ING/HSBC working capital facility cancelled; prior $25m CIF refinanced into the new facility.
  • Board approved Final Investment Decision (FID) for Havieron gold-copper project, following receipt of State and Federal primary environmental approvals.
  • Substantive development post-FID awaits grant of certain secondary environmental approvals.
  • Net cash of $1,208m at 31 March 2026, taking pro-forma total liquidity to over $1,700m (assuming Facility B close) — described as fully funded to deliver Havieron.
  • Havieron Feasibility Study capex: $1,065m pre-production (to first gold) plus $673m expansion capex (largely self-funded by Havieron cash flows), June 2025 cost base.
  • Management flagged optionality to accelerate elements of expansion capex where it de-risks delivery/schedule, given balance sheet strength.
  • MD Shaun Day: "The execution of the corporate debt facility and Havieron Final Investment Decision provides the opportunity to deliver one of Australia's premier gold-copper projects."
  • MD Shaun Day: "The development of Havieron, alongside the successful delivery of Telfer life extensions, has the potential to underpin a multi-decade, world class gold-copper mining hub in the Paterson Province."

body:
• Castlelake (majority-owned by Brookfield Asset Management) is in the "early stages of considering a possible offer" for easyJet, per FT, but has not yet approached the board with no certainty of a firm offer.
  • Under UK Takeover Code, Castlelake has until 26 June to declare its intention (put up or shut up deadline).
  • Castlelake is Minneapolis-based with ~USD37 billion AUM; previously explored a Spirit Airlines bid this year (did not proceed) and held a stake in SAS later sold to Air France-KLM.
  • Context: easyJet was relegated from FTSE 100 to FTSE 250 in March 2026.
  • Prior H1 guidance: headline pretax loss of GBP540-560 million for six months to end-March, vs GBP394 million loss prior year, hurt by fuel spike from US-Israeli war with Iran.
  • Underlying H1 result described as "broadly" in line ex-GBP25 million additional March fuel costs and GBP30 million net rise in legal provisions.
  • 70% fuel-hedged for H2 at USD706/tonne; every USD100 fuel move = ~GBP40 million H2 cost impact.
  • Q1 (to 31 Dec) headline pretax loss of GBP93 million vs GBP61 million prior year, worse than GBP88 million Bloomberg consensus.
  • Historical precedent: easyJet rejected an unsolicited Wizz Air bid in 2021.
  • Shares closed +0.5% at 396.10p Friday; down 31% over the past year.
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets reference an offer approach: 'possible offer'

body:
• New partnership with Asda to deploy the Ocado Smart Platform across Asda's UK online grocery business, with go-live targeted for early 2027.
  • Shares surged +14% to 236.41p on the announcement.
  • Scope: end-to-end deployment including Ocado's 'webshop' front-end, in-store fulfilment, and software — replacing/upgrading Asda's existing e-commerce infrastructure.
  • Asda context: 2025 sales of >£21bn, 1,100 stores, and >700,000 weekly online orders — a materially larger online base than existing Ocado UK partner Morrisons.
  • No material financial impact expected in FY26 (year ending 29 November 2026).
  • Cash flow guidance reiterated: cash flow positive in H2 FY26, and positive full-year cash flow in FY27.
  • CEO Tim Steiner: "We're delighted that Asda has chosen Ocado to support the next phase of their online growth. The UK remains one of the world's most competitive and fast-evolving online grocery markets, where technology, scale and continuous innovation are increasingly important for retailers looking to maintain leadership positions."
  • Important offset to recent OSP setbacks: Sobeys closing 1 of 3 CFCs (announced Jan 2026); Kroger closing CFCs in Frederick (MD), Pleasant Prairie (WI) and Groveland (FL) — vs the original 2018 plan for ~20 CFCs.
  • Follows February's restructuring: 1,000 job cuts, scaled-back R&D spend, and simplified operating model.
type_workings: Rule 'commercial': opening bullets say 'partnership with'

body:
• Statutory DTR 4.3A disclosure of payments to governments for FY2025; total group payments of $3,757m across 9 countries.
  • Income taxes dominated at $3,454m (~92% of total), reflecting heavy fiscal take from upstream operations.
  • Norway was the largest single contributor at $1,907m, almost entirely income tax to Skatteetaten ($1,901m) — consistent with Harbour's enlarged Norwegian portfolio following the Wintershall Dea acquisition.
  • UK payments totalled $1,518m, with $1,505m of income tax to HMRC (reflecting Energy Profits Levy / windfall tax exposure) and $13m in licence fees to the North Sea Transition Authority; Southern North Sea showed an $11m income tax credit.
  • Germany: $74m, almost all royalties ($73m), with Mittelplate/Dieksand the largest single asset payment at $51m.
  • Argentina: $98m total ($25m income tax, $65m royalties, $8m bonus payments).
  • Other country totals: Indonesia $51m, Egypt $43m, Libya $26m, Vietnam $21m, Mexico $19m.
  • Production entitlements: 2 mmbbls / $115m, concentrated in Indonesia ($44m), Egypt ($43m) and Libya ($26m).
  • No dividends or infrastructure improvement payments made to any government in the period.
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Announced partnership with Asda to develop and run Asda's UK online grocery business, replacing existing e-commerce infrastructure with Ocado's platform; go-live targeted early 2027.
  • Deployment covers Ocado's end-to-end solutions: front-end "webshop", in-store fulfilment, and software (software-only deal, no CFC/automation component).
  • Shares surged 9.5% to 228p, having traded as high as 240p intraday; stock still >90% below 2020 all-time highs.
  • Asda context: >£21bn total sales in 2025, 1,100 stores, >700,000 weekly ecommerce orders; annual online sales ~£3bn (BofA/Kantar).
  • No material financial impact expected in FY26 (ending 29 November).
  • Reiterated guidance: cash flow positive in H2 FY26, positive full-year cash flow in FY27.
  • BofA estimates fees of 0.7%–1.0% of sales, implying ~£21m–£30m annual revenue once fully rolled out by FY28; contract described as highly profitable with most revenue dropping to EBITDA and low capital intensity.
  • JPMorgan estimates £25m–£30m annual revenue from 2027, translating to ~£20m incremental EBITDA; calls deal "incrementally positive" but flags it is not the long-awaited store-based automation (SBA) win needed for a material re-rating.
  • JPM notes deal is "supportive for a heavily shorted name".
  • RBC views deal as a "small negative" for Tesco and Sainsbury's; Tesco -1.5% at 433.5p, Sainsbury's -0.9% at 300.6p on the day.
  • CEO Tim Steiner: "We're delighted that Asda has chosen Ocado to support the next phase of their online growth. The UK remains one of the world's most competitive and fast-evolving online grocery markets, where technology, scale and continuous innovation are increasingly important for retailers looking to maintain leadership positions."
  • BofA: "We view the agreement as a positive development for Ocado, reinforcing its positioning as a software provider. However, its scope remains relatively limited, given the exclusive focus on software."
  • AJ Bell's Russ Mould called it "much-needed good news" and a "ray of light after a difficult period", but noted it is "a world away" from the pandemic-era global platform licensing narrative.
  • Recent setbacks recap: Sobeys (Jan 2026) closed one of three Ocado-powered CFCs; Kroger last year closed three CFCs (Frederick MD, Pleasant Prairie WI, Groveland FL) vs original 2018 plan for ~20 CFCs.
  • Prior FY25 (to Nov 2025) adjusted EBITDA was £178m, up from £112m — reference point for scale of new contract economics.
  • Precedent cited: June 2025 Bon Preu deal (CFC near Barcelona; 138 Bonpreu supermarkets, 60 Esclat hypermarkets, 14 minimarkets).
type_workings: Rule 'commercial': opening bullets say 'partnership with'

  • Ottawa Fleet Services has been listing on GovDeals since 2013, selling fleet vehicles, trucks, utility equipment, shop tools and industrial items on an "as is, where is" basis.

body:
• Commissioning started on the US$1.5 billion AP60 smelter expansion at Complexe Arvida, Quebec, with start-up beginning in March and full ramp of all 96 new pots expected by end-2026.
  • Adds ~160,000 tpa of primary aluminium capacity, bringing total AP60 output to 220,000 tpa.
  • AP60 expansion plus the planned Arvida recycling centre will more than offset closure of the older Arvida potrooms, with shutdown expected to complete in June.
  • Carbon footprint: ~1.6 t CO2e/t aluminium vs 3.2t at old Arvida and 10.9t industry average; combined with hydropower, ~1/6 of industry-average emissions.
  • Expected ~290,000 tonnes/year CO2 reduction vs the old Arvida smelter, plus up to 90% reduction in fine particulate matter.
  • Described as the first major primary aluminium project in the West in more than a decade.
  • Supports ~100 permanent local jobs; peak construction created 1,500+ jobs and generated >C$1 billion in economic benefits for Quebec.
  • Linked to ELYSIS partnership demonstration plant in Quebec (carbon-free smelting, oxygen by-product) — AP60 is the first licence of the breakthrough technology.
  • Jérôme Pécresse (CEO Aluminium & Lithium): "Rio Tinto is now strongly positioned for decades to come with one of the most advanced smelting technologies operating at commercial scale."
  • Premier Christine Fréchette flagged the backdrop of "economic uncertainty related to U.S. tariffs", framing the project as securing Quebec's aluminium future.
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Revenue +17% YoY to US$104.1m (4M 2025: US$89.1m).
  • Cash collections +48% YoY to US$183.5m (4M 2025: US$124.1m), reflecting focus on Nigerian receivables.
  • Trade receivables cut 22% since year-end to US$395.2m (FY25: US$507.2m).
  • Cash balances US$64.7m (FY25: US$42.8m); net debt reduced to US$641.7m (FY25: US$658.6m) after repayment/cancellation of debt at Company level, Accugas Holdings UK and the remaining Accugas US$ facility.
  • New £32m unsecured loan facility from largest shareholder NIPCO, 36-month term, 4.5% simple interest, 5% arrangement fee; Tranche A £20m available now, Tranche B £12m from 1 July 2026 (subject to NIPCO consent).
  • Facility includes a Company-only optional conversion right at 8p/share (vs current 6.8p), exercisable on change of control or pre-maturity; requires Takeover Panel Rule 9 dispensation and independent shareholder waiver resolution.
  • Proceeds earmarked for general corporate purposes including capex, debt repayments, acquisitions and future share buybacks.
  • Group average gross production 15.7 Kboepd in 4M 2026 vs 18.8 Kboepd FY25, constrained by drilling activity and customer gas demand; 80% gas.
  • FY 2026 production guidance: 20–22 Kboepd, supported by higher H2 gas output.
  • Stubb Creek production +8% YoY to 3.1 Kbopd; full expansion programme targeting up to 4.7 Kbopd, EPC contract signing expected H2 2026.
  • Uquo NE development well drilling completed end-May 2026; first gas targeted July 2026.
  • Uquo South exploration well: site ready early June 2026, targeting unrisked gross GIIP of 131 Bscf.
  • Niger R1234 PSC: discussions with Government ongoing; work will only recommence on satisfactory agreement. R3 East redesigned to ~10 Kbopd plateau (vs 5 Kbopd prior); updated internal unrisked PV10 of US$179.6m at US$65/bbl (US$381.0m at US$95/bbl), vs prior CPR US$150m; FY24 carrying value ~US$175m under impairment review for FY25 accounts.
  • Power: Niger Tarka wind project ESIA submission expected H2 2026; Cameroon Bini a Warak (up to 95 MW) JDA negotiations advanced; East African hydropower acquisition still pending completion; exited exclusivity on >100 MW Sub-Saharan renewables portfolio (referenced 4 Feb 2026).
  • Chad/Cameroon arbitration: expected to conclude H2 2026; SCI/SMIL claims >US$775m (+US$215m interest) for Chad nationalisation, plus US$330m (+US$67m interest) re COTCo; Chad counterclaims ~US$699.1m disputed as baseless.
  • Hedging: 475,000 bbls put options at avg US$55/bbl strike and 335,000 bbls collars (US$55/US$84.3) for Q2–Q4 2026; >75% of 2026 forecast oil production retains unlimited upside.
  • February 2026 buyback of 32,063,641 shares completed and cancelled; authority remains for up to 318,098,135 shares.
  • AIM Quotation Review: shareholder consultation in June 2026, outcome expected Q3 2026, following LSE's "Shaping the Future of AIM" feedback statement; reverse takeover rule reforms seen as relevant to Savannah's M&A ambitions.
  • CEO Andrew Knott: "We are also pleased to have secured a new £32 million loan facility from NIPCO plc ("NIPCO"), our largest shareholder, strengthening our financial flexibility and further underpinning our confidence in delivering continued operational, financial and strategic progress through 2026 and 2027."
  • M&A: pipeline active across hydrocarbons and renewables; confident of announcing further transactions over the next 24 months.
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with full-year financial figures

body:
• Extension of the first tranche ("Initial Programme") of the current £750m share buyback programme.
  • Initial Programme (announced 22 April 2026) increased by up to £100m, from up to £250m to up to £350m.
  • Total Programme size unchanged at £750m by April 2027.
  • Citi arrangement amended to reflect the higher £350m cap; purchases continue on a riskless principal basis via LSE and Cboe Europe (BXE/CXE).
  • Authority remains that granted at the 2025 AGM (and any subsequent authority); Citi acts independently, including in closed periods.
  • Pricing capped per UK Listing Rules Ch.9: max of (i) 105% of 5-day average middle market closing price, or (ii) higher of last independent trade and highest current independent bid.
  • Sole purpose: reduce share capital (i.e. cancellation, not treasury).

body:
• New ecommerce partnership with Asda to deploy the Ocado Smart Platform (OSP) across Asda's UK online grocery operations.
  • Asda context: 2025 sales >£21bn, c.1,100 stores, >700,000 ecommerce orders/week from stores and dark stores.

  • Cash flow guidance reiterated: cash flow positive in H2 FY26, with full-year cash flow positivity expected in FY27.

  • Strategic read-through: first major new UK OSP retail partner win, expanding OSP beyond CFC-based deployments into a hybrid store/dark-store model for a top-tier UK grocer.

body:
• AGM scheduled for 29 June 2026; Notice and Form of Proxy published.
  • 2025 Annual Report and Accounts still not published — missed the 30 April 2026 DTR 4.1.3R deadline due to delay in the Intended Fundraise (announced 28 April 2026), with bookbuild completion required to sign off going concern.
  • Company "continues to work towards publication" but is "not yet in a position to do so."
  • As a result, resolutions on Annual Report and Accounts, auditor re-appointment/remuneration, and directors' remuneration report will not be tabled at this AGM; a separate shareholder meeting will be convened post-publication.
  • AGM therefore limited to routine business: director re-elections, renewal of market share buyback authority, and authority to call general meetings on 14 clear days' notice.
  • Vitalii Lisovenko retiring from the Board at conclusion of AGM, having served over nine years; will not stand for re-election.

body:
• Acquiring Cormetech Inc (Durham, NC; SCR catalysts for stationary power/industrial emissions) for enterprise value USD360m, plus up to USD100m earn-out payable in 2028/2029 on hitting targets.
  • Cormetech FY25: USD129m sales, USD16m EBITDA; FY26 EBITDA expected to rise to USD35m, implying 10x multiple (6.5x including USD20m run-rate synergies).
  • Strategic rationale: exposure to US power generation demand driven by AI data centre build-out; bolts onto Clean Air Solutions; completion expected June/July.
  • ~350 employees, two US factories; founded 1989.
  • Reported pretax profit from continuing operations GBP91m, down 77% from GBP403m (prior year benefited from GBP482m disposal gain vs GBP5m this year).
  • Revenue +14% to GBP12.57bn (from GBP11.02bn).
  • Reported operating profit (continuing) -65% to GBP161m; underlying operating profit +14% to GBP340m — in line with previously upgraded guidance.
  • Full-year dividend held flat at 77.0p.
  • Catalyst Technologies sale to Honeywell for GBP1.33bn on track to complete by end-August; GBP1bn net proceeds to be returned to shareholders post-completion (price was cut from GBP1.80bn in February).
  • FY27 guidance: low-to-mid single digit % growth in underlying operating profit at constant PGM prices and FX, excluding Catalyst Technologies and Cormetech.
  • Reiterates path to sustainable FCF of at least GBP250m p.a. by FY28, with at least GBP200m p.a. returned to shareholders from FY27.
  • Board: Julie Southern appointed Senior Independent Director from 16 July AGM, replacing Barbara Jeremiah; Southern is chair of NXP Semiconductors and NED at Ocado.
  • CEO Liam Condon: "Cormetech will materially enhance the scale of Clean Air Solutions and create a global leader in stationary emissions control, including for the rapidly growing data centre market. Together with the progress we are making on strengthening our core businesses, we are on track to achieve our medium-term targets and deliver enhanced shareholder returns."

body:
• Secured US$1.5m unsecured convertible loan note earmarked for accelerated development of the greater Molefe region in Zambia.
  • Investor was part of the team behind Mantos Copper and its merger with Capstone Copper, signalling credible sector backing.
  • Discussions ongoing for a further US$10m staggered investment to fund licence acquisitions, exploration and Molefe area advancement (no certainty of completion).
  • CLN carries an initial interest rate below typical bank rates for similar structures.
  • Conversion price = 20-day VWAP on LSE prior to drawdown date; conversion to occur immediately after shareholder approval.
  • Company has 60 days to obtain shareholder approval at an EGM; if not obtained, interest rate steps up to typical medium-term unsecured rates and loan becomes repayable within 12 months of drawdown.
  • Strategy: combine near-term copper production via the Sable refinery with concurrent resource drilling at Molefe.
  • Reiterates target of 25,000 tpa copper production via three-pillar strategy (Roan concentrator, Sable refinery, regional mining assets + Large Waste Rock Project).
  • Also in discussions with the Investor on potential collaboration leveraging Jubilee's processing expertise across broader initiatives.
  • CEO Leon Coetzer: "The facility provides near-term funding flexibility to aggressively pursue additional exploration and mining opportunities within the greater Molefe region, while we advance discussions around a potential longer-term strategic partnership aimed at supporting resource expansion, processing capacity growth and increased copper production across the broader Zambia platform."
  • CEO Leon Coetzer: "Importantly, our approach combines ongoing copper production from our processing operations with the concurrent development of a scalable resource base at the Molefe Mine, reflecting Jubilee's differentiated model of building resources alongside production."

body:
• Article is a sector piece on AI executives walking back job-loss warnings; Standard Chartered referenced as a recent corporate example.
  • STAN announced last week plans to cut thousands of jobs by 2030, with AI replacing staff in a range of administrative roles.
  • Context: Snap cut ~1,000 jobs last month citing AI-driven efficiency gains.
  • Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang pushed back on CEOs blaming AI for layoffs: "The narrative that connects AI to job loss, for many of the CEOs that are doing it – it is just too lazy."
  • Huang: "How is it possible that AI became productive and useful only six months ago, and they were somehow laying people off two years ago because of AI? It doesn't make any sense."
  • Huang: "It was just a way for them to sound smart, and I really hate that. I think we're scaring people and that's irresponsible."
  • OpenAI's Sam Altman conceded at CBA's Accelerate AI Conference in Sydney that there has been no "jobs apocalypse," saying: "I thought there would have been more impact on entry-level white-collar jobs being eliminated by now than has actually happened… that is an area where my intuitions were just off."
  • Anthropic's Dario Amodei softened tone: even if 90% of jobs are automated, the remaining 10% of human workers would be vastly more productive.
  • Reversals from Altman and Amodei coincide with expected high-profile IPOs at OpenAI and Anthropic requiring broad investor buy-in.
  • Fed Governor Lisa Cook warned full AI employment effects may still lie ahead: "We could be approaching the most significant reorganization of work in generations," noting job losses could precede gains.
  • ECB and most economic institutions say AI has had only minor employment effects so far.
  • Read-across for STAN: its 2030 AI-driven headcount plan stands out against a narrative shift where leading AI CEOs are now downplaying near-term displacement, potentially increasing scrutiny of the bank's stated AI productivity rationale.

  • No capex, capacity, timeline or offtake terms disclosed — exploratory stage only.
  • Shares quoted at AUD61.28; +59% over 12 months.

body:
• Pro forma underlying operating profit up 6% at constant PGM prices and FX (in line with previously upgraded guidance); +14% including PGM price benefit to £340m vs £298m.
  • Reported operating profit down 65% to £161m (vs £454m) due to absence of £482m prior-year disposal gain, partly offset by lower impairment/restructuring charges (£192m vs £327m).
  • Free cash flow £168m, up 163% (vs £64m); cash from operating activities £495m (vs £330m).
  • Sales excl. precious metals down 7% at constant FX to £2,555m; Revenue £12,573m (+14%).
  • Underlying EPS 128.5p (+16%); reported basic EPS (54.1)p vs 176.0p (impacted by £170m deferred tax asset de-recognition tied to CT sale).
  • Net debt £880m (vs £810m); net debt/EBITDA 1.8x unchanged; ~£1.5bn liquidity available.
  • Final dividend 55.0p; total FY dividend held flat at 77.0p.
  • Cormetech acquisition agreed: $360m EV (10.3x 2026 EBITDA pre-synergies), plus up to $100m earn-out in 2028/29; completion expected end-June/July 2026; targets US data centre stationary emissions market.
  • Combined Clean Air Solutions + Cormetech expected to generate >£200m sales with at least mid-teens operating margin in 2026/27.
  • Catalyst Technologies £1,325m sale to Honeywell on track for completion by end-August 2026; £1bn to be returned: £800m special dividend with share consolidation + £200m on-market buyback.
  • FY27 guidance: low-to-mid single digit % growth in group underlying operating profit at constant PGM prices/FX (ex-CT and Cormetech), H2-weighted.
  • Capex raised to c.£230m (from c.£140m) for FY27 due to higher UK PGM refinery fit-out costs (sub-contractor industrial action impact); to be fully offset by working capital efficiencies.
  • Clean Air: underlying op profit +12% to £307m; margin up 270bps to 14.5% (H2: 16.5%); on track for 16-18% margin by 2027/28; sales -7% on weak NA HD diesel and European LD diesel; headcount -11%, R&D/SG&A -20%.
  • Clean Air FY27: good op profit growth expected despite assumed 3% decline in global LDV production.
  • PGM Services: op profit -20% to £119m, hit by £48m operational metal loss at US refinery, lower metal recoveries and refining volumes; partly offset by £24m PGM price benefit.
  • New UK PGM refinery: early-stage commissioning started March 2026; operational in calendar year 2027.
  • PGM Services FY27 op profit expected in line with FY26 (higher process loss provisions, lower recoveries, higher maintenance offset by lower US refinery losses).
  • Hydrogen Technologies: loss narrowed to £19m (vs £39m); run-rate breakeven achieved Q4; FY27 guided to operating profit breakeven and cash flow positive; took £88m impairment reflecting ~55% reduction in demand forecasts vs 2025, citing US "Big Beautiful Bill Act" and slow EU RED III implementation.
  • Discontinued Catalyst Technologies: sales -14%, underlying op profit -51% to £44m on weaker first-fill/refill catalyst and licensing.
  • Corporate costs £67m (down £20m); headcount -15%.
  • PGM price tailwind: at current levels, expects at least £25m operating profit benefit in FY27; FX a £2m headwind.
  • Pro-forma net debt/EBITDA expected ~1.8x at 31 March 2027 post-CT disposal/Cormetech; target 1.0-1.5x by 31 March 2029.
  • Medium-term targets reaffirmed: sustainable FCF ≥£250m p.a. by 2027/28; ≥£200m p.a. shareholder returns from FY27 onwards; at least mid single digit CAGR in pro forma underlying operating profit.
  • Board changes: Barbara Jeremiah and John O'Higgins to retire at AGM (16 July 2026); Julie Southern to become SID; Sinead Lynch to chair RemCo. Societal Value and Investment Committees disbanded.
  • Veranova litigation: High Court dismissed claim 1 May 2026; 90% costs awarded to JM; Veranova granted leave to appeal (heard 2027).
  • CEO Liam Condon: "The significant increase in cash generation shows our strategy is working."
  • Notes ongoing risk from Middle East conflict (no material FY26 impact) on demand, supply chains and inflation.
type_workings: Rule 'capital returns': body announces both a share buyback and a special dividend

body:
• WD-8 block: FR-1835 and FR-1836 development wells brought online mid-May 2026, averaging ~175 bbls/d combined of medium-gravity crude, in line with expectations.
  • Drilling costs for both WD-8 wells were fully funded by the drilling operator under the 2025 Fyzabad strategic asset exchange — the divested Fyzabad asset produced only ~49 bbls/d in 2025.
  • Atlantic LNG Train 4 in 54-day planned shutdown from 26 May to 19 July 2026; main transportation pipeline also offline 15–29 June 2026.
  • Central block gas being redirected to Train 2/3 and domestic market, expected to positively impact net realized gas price — Train 2/3 pricing formula is structurally higher than Train 4; domestic petrochemical/fertilizer demand described as "exceptionally strong".
  • Cascadura booster compressor mechanical/electrical installation on schedule; commissioning expected to begin June 2026 — designed to lower wellhead backpressure and mitigate pipeline capacity constraints.
  • CR-3 well currently flowing ~2.2 MMcf/d gas and 14 bbls/d condensate with no prior interventions; coiled tubing cleanout and acid stimulation planned to address localized inflow restriction from residual drilling/completion fluids.
  • CR-3 intervention delayed by sole local coiled tubing provider's mechanical issues; Touchstone evaluating whether to defer the shut-in until after the Train 4 maintenance window to capture higher gas pricing.
  • CEO Paul Baay: "Operationally, our focus remains on efficiently converting reserves into near-term cash flow."
  • Baay on capital discipline: "We continue to prioritize disciplined capital allocation, focusing on high-return well and facility optimizations."

body:
• Fattal confirms it submitted a non-binding proposal on 27 May 2026 to acquire 100% of PPHE not already owned at £22.00 cash per share, valuing PPHE at approximately £930 million.
  • Offer represents ~37.5% premium to PPHE's 26 May 2026 closing price of ~£16, and ~47% premium to the £15.00 unaffected price on 13 November 2025 (prior to PPHE confirming shareholder meetings with potential investors).
  • Fattal already owns ~4% of PPHE's share capital.
  • This follows an earlier non-binding proposal in March 2026, preceded by financial and commercial due diligence.
  • PPHE Board, with its adviser, has evaluated the Proposal and determined it represents fair value, and intends to engage with major shareholders to assess deliverability.
  • Fattal targets announcing a firm offer within the next 4 weeks.
  • Envisaged as an all-cash offer, though Fattal reserves Rule 2.5 rights to alter consideration or reduce price under specified conditions (board consent, PPHE dividend/distribution, lower competing offer, Rule 9 waiver).
  • Takeover Panel has granted a dispensation from the Rule 2.6(a) 28-day PUSU deadline while Fattal participates in PPHE's formal sale process.
  • PPHE asset portfolio valued at ~£2.2 billion (Dec 2025); ~9,625 rooms mainly in UK, Croatia, Netherlands, Germany.
  • Advisers: Morgan Stanley (financial adviser to Fattal); Baker & McKenzie (legal).
  • No certainty a firm offer will be made, or that final terms will not differ materially.
