body:
• WD-8 oil wells online: FR-1835 and FR-1836 development wells completed and brought on production mid-May 2026, collectively averaging ~175 bbls/d of medium-gravity crude, in line with expectations.
  • Turnkey drilling costs for both WD-8 wells fully funded by the drilling operator under the 2025 strategic asset exchange agreement (Fyzabad swap, which had produced just ~49 bbls/d in 2025).
  • Atlantic LNG Train 4 in 54-day planned maintenance shutdown from 26 May to 19 July 2026; main transportation pipeline also offline 15–29 June 2026.
  • Central block gas being redirected to Train 2/3 and domestic market, expected to positively impact net realized gas price — Train 2/3 pricing formula is structurally higher than Train 4; domestic petrochemical/fertilizer (methanol, ammonia) demand described as "exceptionally strong".
  • Cascadura booster compressor on schedule, with commissioning expected to commence June 2026; designed to lower wellhead backpressure and mitigate regional pipeline capacity constraints.
  • CR-3 well currently producing ~2.2 MMcf/d gas and ~14 bbls/d condensate with stable pressures; planned coiled tubing cleanout and acid stimulation to address localised inflow restriction (likely residual drilling/completion fluids).
  • CR-3 intervention potentially deferred: sole local coiled tubing service provider has mechanical issues; Touchstone evaluating whether to defer the shut-in until after the Train 4 maintenance window to capture the higher gas pricing.
  • CEO Paul Baay: "Operationally, our focus remains on efficiently converting reserves into near-term cash flow."
  • Baay on capital discipline: "We continue to prioritize disciplined capital allocation, focusing on high-return well and facility optimizations."

body:
• Agreed to acquire CORMETECH Inc., US-leading SCR catalyst manufacturer for stationary power generation/industrial emissions control.
  • Enterprise value of $360m cash on completion, representing 10.3x 2026E EBITDA; multiple drops to c.6.5x including run-rate synergies.
  • Additional earn-out of up to $100m payable in cash across 2028/2029 contingent on financial performance targets.
  • Funded from JM's existing debt facilities; expected to close end of June or July 2026, subject to regulatory approvals.
  • Cormetech 2025 sales $129m (+15% CAGR since 2020) and EBITDA $16m (+65% CAGR since 2020); 2026E sales c.$180m and EBITDA c.$35m.
  • Secured c.$300m orderbook (deliveries 2026/2027) plus c.$1bn project pipeline, primarily US data centre-related.
  • Cormetech serves 400+ customers (90% >10-year relationships) with largest global installed SCR base (2,500+ systems, 300GW+); operates 2 US sites, c.350 employees.
  • JM's existing Clean Air Solutions generated FY2025/26 sales of £67m and underlying operating profit of £10m; combined business expected to deliver sales >£200m with at least mid-teens operating margin in 2026/27.
  • Run-rate synergies of at least $20m EBITDA by 2030 (c.60% of Cormetech 2026E EBITDA); ~70% revenue, ~30% cost; $6m one-off costs in 2026/2027 to deliver.
  • EPS accretive from first full year of ownership on pre-synergy basis; ROIC to exceed cost of capital within 3 years.
  • Addressable global SCR + oxidation catalyst market $1.1bn in 2025, forecast c.11% CAGR to 2030, driven by US data centre buildout, tighter NOx regulation and distributed generation.
  • Pro forma leverage c.1.8x net debt/EBITDA at 31 March 2027 post-deal, post-Catalyst Technologies disposal and post-£1bn capital return; de-leveraging to target 1.0–1.5x by 31 March 2029.
  • Guidance reiterated: £1bn return of net proceeds to shareholders following Honeywell sale of Catalyst Technologies (completion expected by end August 2026); annualised sustainable FCF ≥£250m by 2027/28; shareholder returns ≥£200m p.a. from 2026/27 (dividends + buybacks).
  • Cormetech also developing advanced carbon capture technology expected to be commercialised over medium term.
  • Enlarged Clean Air business to be operated independently of rest of Clean Air post-completion to drive synergy realisation.
  • CEO Liam Condon: "The acquisition of Cormetech is the next step in delivering our strategy of focusing on Johnson Matthey's strengths."
  • Condon added: "Together with the expected sale of our Catalyst Technologies business, this transaction represents another important milestone in our strategy to further strengthen JM, driving long-term value creation and cash generation."
  • Evercore acting as Financial Adviser; Eversheds Sutherland as US Legal Adviser.

body:
• Received an indicative proposal from Fattal Hotel Group regarding a possible cash offer at £22 per share, following the Strategic Review and Formal Sale Process commenced on 21 November.
  • Board, with its Rule 3 adviser, has evaluated the Proposal and determined it represents fair value.
  • Proposal subject to customary pre-conditions including completion of confirmatory due diligence.
  • Independent Committee formed, excluding Roni Hirsch (representative Director of Euro Plaza Holdings) — reflecting the major shareholder's separate interest.
  • Board intends to engage with major shareholders to assess deliverability; no certainty a firm offer will be made.
  • Takeover Panel dispensation means Fattal is not subject to the Rule 2.6(a) 28-day PUSU deadline while participating in the formal sale process.
  • Fattal reserves the right to lower the £22 cash price if: Board recommends/consents; PPHE pays a dividend/distribution (price reduced equivalently); a third party announces a lower firm offer; or PPHE announces a Rule 9 waiver transaction.
  • Fattal also reserves right to introduce/vary forms of consideration (e.g., securities).
  • Shareholders advised to take no action at this time.
  • Context: share price reacted +26% to ~2,035p on the news, still trading at a discount to the £22 (2,200p) indicative cash offer, implying market scepticism on deal certainty.

  • Valley Electric (founded 1982, Everett, WA) — one of the largest full-service electrical contractors in the Western U.S.; serves commercial, industrial, transportation/heavy civil and marine markets.
  • Comet Electric (founded 1976, Chatsworth, CA) — commercial & industrial contractor in Southern California; exposure includes mission-critical/data centers, aviation, water/wastewater, healthcare.
  • Strategic rationale: strengthens C&I segment service offerings and geographic reach on the U.S. West Coast.
  • CEO Rick Swartz: "expected to continue to strengthen our Commercial & Industrial segment service offerings and geographic reach, while expanding our market position."
  • Stifel acting as exclusive financial advisor to Valley.
  • No explicit EPS accretion, margin, or synergy guidance disclosed.

body:
• Mandatory Report on Payments to Governments for FY2025 filed at Companies House under UK Reporting Regulations 2014.
  • Headline: Rio Tinto paid US$9.9bn of taxes and royalties in 2025, plus a further US$1.9bn on behalf of employees.
  • Note: the mandatory filing total (extractive activities only) is US$6.28bn, materially lower than the headline US$9.9bn group figure which includes non-extractive payments captured in the voluntary Taxes and Royalties Paid Report.
  • Australia remains overwhelmingly dominant, accounting for ~US$5.66bn of the US$6.28bn extractive total (~90%):
  • Australian Taxation Office: US$3.59bn corporate tax
  • State of Western Australia royalties: US$1.78bn
  • Pilbara project alone: US$5.41bn in total government payments
  • Mongolia (Oyu Tolgoi) payments: US$373m, including US$254m royalties and US$93m fees (incl. US$74m to Umnugovi Province and US$12m to the Oyu Tolgoi Catalyst Fund).
  • Canada: ~US$120m across IOC (US$65m), Diavik, RTFT, Galaxy Lithium and Indigenous government fees.
  • Argentina lithium build-out visible: Rincon US$18m, Sal de Vida US$53m, Fénix US$18m — indicating tax footprint emerging as lithium projects ramp.
  • US payments net negative at the IRS (-US$31.9m refund); Resolution Copper showing -US$30m and Australian exploration/Winu also reporting negative tax entries (reflecting tax group settlements rather than economic refunds, per Notes E & F).
  • South Africa (Richards Bay Minerals): net US$17m, with royalties in negative US$6.6m.
  • Voluntary Taxes and Royalties Paid Report 2025 also published, aligned with GRI 207 tax standard.

body:
• Ousted chair Albert Manifold publicly rejects BP board's characterisation of his conduct, saying he was "removed without warning and without explanation" and will not allow a "false narrative to go unchallenged."
  • Board removed Manifold with immediate effect on Tuesday, citing "serious concerns" over conduct, oversight and governance.
  • Manifold defended his record: "During my time as chairman I worked to drive genuine change at BP – cutting costs, challenging excess, and holding the organisation to higher standards."
  • Ian Tyler appointed interim chair; formal search launched for permanent replacement.
  • Manifold (ex-CRH CEO) had only joined BP in October 2025, tasked with driving a performance turnaround — tenure of roughly 13 months.
  • Senior independent director Amanda Blanc acknowledged Manifold brought "welcome focus and pace" but cited governance/conduct issues "it deems unacceptable."
  • Removal follows ~20% of shareholder votes cast against his election a month earlier, with Glass Lewis having recommended against him on governance concerns.
  • BP shares -1.3% to 522.10p Wednesday morning (subsequently trading around 513–515p intraday, -2.5% to -3%).
  • Leadership vacuum adds uncertainty to BP's ongoing strategic reset under CEO Murray Auchincloss, particularly the pivot back toward hydrocarbons and cost-cutting agenda Manifold was championing.

body:
• Follow-up to 26 May announcement on GKN Aerospace's Garden Grove facility in California.
  • OCFA lifted the remaining mandatory evacuation area at 7.30pm local time on 26 May; only a small temporary restricted zone directly around the facility remains.
  • OCFA publicly confirmed no chemical leak occurred and no risk to the public.
  • Work continues with local authorities and customers on operational recovery and supply plans.
  • Site context: GKN Aerospace Transparency Systems facility designs/manufactures military and commercial transparencies, employs ~500 people, and generated £136m of sales in FY2025.

body:
• Group statutory revenue £1,469.6m, down 0.8%; Group consumer revenue +1.0% to £1,981m.
  • LFL: Group LFL revenue -0.5%; Retail LFL -0.9%; Vet Group LFL +2.7%.
  • Group underlying PBT £92.8m, down 30.2%; statutory PBT £86.5m, down 28.3%.
  • Retail underlying PBT collapsed 57.8% to £30.8m (margin 2.4% vs 5.6%); Vet underlying PBT +10.4% to £83.8m (margin 47.4% vs 43.3%).
  • Retail consumer revenue -1.0% to £1,292.9m; Q4 Retail returned to growth at +2.2% (vs -5.2% in Q4 FY25), H2 +0.4%.
  • Vet Group consumer revenue +5.0% to £688.1m; JV consumer revenue +6.3% to £619.8m.
  • Group gross margin -120bps to 45.7%: Retail -180bps (incl. c.80bps planned price investment); Vet +310bps to 55.7%.
  • Operating costs +1.9% (+1.1% ex-Insurance start-up); £20m Group overhead reduction programme completed, full-year benefit in FY27.
  • Underlying EPS 14.8p (-29.7%); statutory EPS 13.8p (-27.7%).
  • Free cash flow £61.9m (-26.1%): Vet £74.2m (+9.9%), Retail FCF crashed to £2.7m from £30.6m.
  • Adjusted net debt £19.4m vs net cash £6.2m last year; leverage 0.1x EBITDA. RCF £300m (£40m drawn).
  • Dividend rebased to 7.4p (-43.1%), equal to 50% of EPS; final 2.7p. Additional £50m buyback announced for FY27 (£150m completed over 4 years, ~10% of shares).
  • Insurance: FCA approval achieved, on track for 2026 launch; FY26 start-up costs £5.2m, slightly higher in FY27.
  • Subscription % of consumer revenue 15.2% (vs 13.0%); >50% of Vet clients now on a Care Plan.
  • Active Pets Club members fell to 7.4m from 8.2m (-10.5%), partly methodology change; ACV +11.7% to £195.
  • Vet network: 8 new practices opened, 17 extensions; 71% of JV practices debt-free (vs 48% in FY22); JV partner dividends £48.1m, avg £165k per debt-free practice; average practice revenue +3.9% to £1.5m.
  • 460 pet care centres (net +1); 407 JV vet practices (+11); 48 company-managed (-4, due to JV conversions).
  • Food price action: >1,000 products cut by average 12% in November, drove Q4 food volumes +3.7%.
  • CMA: Final Decision Report on vet services welcomed, recognised competitive prices and JV model.
  • Non-underlying costs £6.3m (vs £12.4m), related to completion of Support Office restructuring.
  • FY27 guidance: comfortable with consensus Group underlying PBT of £98m.
  • YTD Retail sales growth has accelerated to mid-single digit with faster volume growth.
  • Retail: market growth 1-2%, expects to outperform; underlying PBT progress; one-off low single digit £m benefit from PetPlan agreement exit.
  • Vet: low single digit sales growth; further profit growth; step-up in costs from new practice management system rollout.
  • Insurance start-up costs slightly higher YoY.
  • Capex c.£50m; effective tax rate 26%; £50m buyback over next 12 months.
  • FX hedging: c.90% USD secured at 1.34 (vs 1.28 in FY26); c.80% of energy secured.
  • CEO James Bailey (new): "Material progress has been made over the past 6 months stabilising the Retail business, delivering improved satisfaction and better availability."
  • Bailey on opportunity: "build momentum through profitable volume led growth in Retail while continuing to execute the proven growth levers of our Vet business and launch our Insurance offering."

body:
• Revenue £141.5m, up 9.5% (H1 FY25: £129.2m); UK revenue +9.4% to £118.4m, Canada +9.9% (constant FX) to CA$42.9m / £23.2m.
  • Group LFL revenue growth +2.3% (vs +1.6% prior year).
  • UK LFL +2.6% (vs +1.3%); Canada LFL +0.5% (vs +3.7%), impacted by heavy snowstorms closing some eastern centres.
  • Group Adjusted EBITDA after rent £42.2m, +8.9%; Group Adjusted PBT £32.1m, +8.1%.
  • Reported PBT £27.2m, -3.9%; reported PAT £19.5m, -5.3%; basic EPS 11.70p, -2.5% — dragged by a £2.8m non-cash impairment of one under-performing UK centre opened within last 3 years facing intense local competition.
  • Adjusted EPS 14.51p, +11.3%, helped by 2.8% reduction in weighted average shares from prior buyback.
  • Interim dividend 4.52p, +10.2% (policy: 34% of prior year final; 55% of Adjusted PAT payout maintained).
  • New £5m share buyback announced for H2 FY26.
  • Net cash £26.0m (vs £22.7m); £25m RCF (Barclays, expiring May 2028) undrawn → total liquidity £51m. Covenant: net debt/EBITDA pre-IFRS16 ≤1.75x — operated within covenants.
  • UK Spend Per Game £12.77, +7.6%; UK amusements SPG £3.69, +12.7%. Canada SPG CA$19.10, +9.7% (LFL SPG +10.1%); Canada amusements SPG CA$3.34, +17.6%.
  • Modest UK game volume decline, improving trend in Q2, more than offset by SPG growth.
  • Gross margin 62.8%, -100bps, driven by centre staff costs +18.5% (9.5%pt from new centres, plus ~£1m each from NI and above-inflation NLW increases suppressing margin by ~1pt). UK centre payroll <20% of revenue; Canada <26%.
  • ~70% of Group revenues not exposed to COGS inflation; 76% of electricity hedged to end-FY29 (incl. 12% on-site solar).
  • Corporate costs £15.1m, +18.6% — inflation plus deliberate investment in Canadian senior leadership, UK ops/LFL growth roles, marketing, and property pipeline functions.
  • Capex £8.5m in H1 (£4.6m maintenance, £3.9m investment) — accelerating in H2 with two UK and one Canadian new centre under construction.
  • Adjusting items £3.3m: £2.8m non-cash impairment + £0.5m Teaquinn earn-out accrual (vs +£0.4m net income prior year that included £1.6m Covid insurance benefit).
  • Canada now 16% of Group revenue (vs 9% in FY22); 16 centres; new Edmonton centre opened in H1 trading well; legacy Splitsville refurbishment programme almost complete; new Canadian CEO in place.
  • Canada target accelerated to 35 centres by 2032 (from prior 2035); 5 openings planned FY27, 40 further potential locations identified.
  • UK: Liverpool Edge Lane (rebuilt) and Reading "exceeded expectations"; Norwich refurbished; pipeline of 2 H2 openings, 4 across FY27/FY28, 20 further sites identified. 95-centre UK target by 2035 reaffirmed.
  • New Cardiff centre (largest UK venue, includes E-karting) opens H2, ahead of planned closure of existing Cardiff centre in FY31.
  • Cash conversion 71% of Group Adjusted EBITDA after rent; free cash flow before investment £30.0m (vs £28.4m).
  • Outlook: "The Group remains confident in delivering on expectations for FY26"; accelerated new-centre pipeline into FY27.
  • CEO Stephen Burns: "Our strong performance in the first half has been driven by continued demand from customers for our high-quality and affordable leisure experiences... we are confident in delivering on expectations for FY26, as customer appeal for our value offer remains robust, and we continue to maintain a tight grip on costs."

body:
• Shares fell 5.2% to 483.00p on Tuesday morning following the chemical incident update.
  • Issue identified Thursday in a tank storing methyl methacrylate at GKN Aerospace's Garden Grove, California site; described as "a thermal issue".
  • Methyl methacrylate is a flammable irritant (used in plastics) that can cause respiratory/neurological reactions, prompting mandatory evacuation orders affecting ~50,000 people (per AFP).
  • No active leak, no injuries, no contamination confirmed by authorities on Monday evening; continuous live atmospheric monitoring in place.
  • Tank pressure declining; temperature fell to ~34°C from 38°C on Monday.
  • Evacuation zone reduced after consultation with technical specialists, emergency services and authorities, though ~16,000 people remain impacted.
  • Management statement: "GKN is working closely with customers on operational recovery and supply plans" — flagging potential aerospace supply chain disruption risk.
  • Garden Grove Police Chief Amir El-Farra: "Please understand that we are doing this for your safety, and we will continue to work diligently."

body:
• Barclays joins the existing £275m RCF syndicate alongside NatWest, Santander and HSBC; facility was originally refinanced in November 2025.
  • Facility retains an uncommitted accordion option taking the RCF up to £325m if exercised.
  • Terms unchanged for Barclays: core margin of 200bps over SONIA, initial four-year term, extendable to a maximum of five years subject to bank consent.
  • CFO Kitty Patmore: "We are pleased to welcome Barclays as to our RCF syndicate, adding further depth and quality to our relationship banking group. Their addition reflects confidence in our strategy and resilience of our business."
  • Patmore added the broader funding base "enhances our flexibility to accelerate new site development to meet demand as opportunities arise, supporting our growth ambitions and unlocking long-term value realisation while maintaining balance sheet discipline."

  • Will succeed Rosie Shapland as Chair of the Audit, Risk and Governance Committee from 1 August 2026; Shapland's retirement on 31 July 2026 was previously announced on 9 February 2026.
  • Buckley brings ~25 years of financial services experience: ex-Head of Investment Solutions at Hermes GPE, Head of UK at Adveq, and prior roles at McQueen; ACA qualified at Deloitte; Oxford Chemistry Master's.
  • Concurrent NED roles: Caledonia Investments plc, Leeds Building Society, and Aurora UK Alpha plc (Audit Committee Chair at Caledonia and Aurora).

body:
• Bank of America downgrades Rio Tinto to 'neutral' from 'buy', price target 9,300p.
  • Rationale: "fairly full valuation", China concerns, and rising oil-related global macro risks tied to Middle East conflict.
  • BofA stresses: "To be clear, this is very much a top-down call rather than a call on stock specifics."
  • Flags China's credit impulse has turned negative year-on-year, with mining equities historically lagging this signal by ~6-8 months.
  • China still accounts for ~50% of demand for most metals, and ~70% of the seaborne/contestable iron ore market.
  • Valuation framework: large-cap diversified miners trade 0.6-1.2x P/NPV through the cycle, peak multiples during high commodity prices/earnings upgrades.
  • Earnings revisions still positive, but BofA believes the "easy" gains in this cycle have already been made.
  • Notes Rio shares have outperformed the UK market by ~60% since 2025 lows.
  • Stock reaction: +1.7% to 7,905p on Tuesday afternoon; 12-month change +76%.

body:
• EU Commission cleared BP's sale of the Gelsenkirchen refinery to Klesch Group, finding no competition concerns given limited combined market position.
  • Gelsenkirchen is one of Germany's largest refineries: two-site complex with ~12 million tons/year crude processing capacity, employing ~1,800 staff, producing road/aviation fuels and petrochemical feedstock.
  • Sale to Malta-based Klesch Group (owned by A. Gary Klesch, already operates two European refineries) was announced March 2026; completion expected H2 2026 subject to remaining regulatory approvals.
  • German Economy Ministry still conducting a separate investment review (per Der Spiegel, April), citing Klesch's opaque Jersey-based corporate structure; Germany can block non-EU acquisitions under certain conditions — a residual deal risk despite EU clearance.
  • Shares fell 5.7% to 520.07p Tuesday afternoon, though the move was driven by a separate governance announcement rather than the refinery clearance.
  • BP separately announced the immediate removal of Albert Manifold as chair and director, citing "serious concerns raised to the board related to important governance standards, oversight and conduct."

body:
• Chair Albert Manifold removed as chair and director, with BP citing "serious concerns" raised to the board "related to important governance standards, oversight and conduct."
  • Shares fell 5.2% to 522.70p on the news; closed -4.03% at 529.00p.
  • Director Ian Tyler appointed interim chair, effective immediately; Tyler also chairs Grafton Group PLC. Permanent succession process to be initiated.
  • Manifold had only been chair since October (joined board September) — tenure of roughly eight months.
  • Second major executive removal for conduct reasons in three years (after CEO Bernard Looney in 2023).
  • Senior Independent Director Amanda Blanc: "Albert has helped bring a welcome focus and pace to bp's transformation. However, the board has been surprised and disappointed to learn of governance oversight and conduct issues it deems unacceptable and has taken decisive action."
  • Manifold credited with reversing BP's renewables push, boosting oil & gas investment, and orchestrating the removal of former CEO Murray Auchincloss, replaced by Meg O'Neill.
  • Reportedly clashed with CEO Meg O'Neill; survived a shareholder rebellion last month.
  • Interim Chair Tyler on O'Neill: "She has already taken bold action to simplify and strengthen the organization such as announcing the move to a clearly defined upstream/downstream model. Under her leadership we are building a simpler, stronger, more valuable bp."
  • Analyst reaction — Citi (Syme): reasons for dismissal "are, in our view, moot"; flags that Manifold "was part of the equity story" and many investors "were re-engaging in the equity story because of his driving of change"; key question is whether the investment pathway is "now largely set."
  • Morningstar (Stewart): BP has "the most volatile boardroom of the oil supermajors"; corporate governance "must a priority of the interim chair and his eventual successor."
  • XTB (Brooks): raises "genuine concerns about HR policies at BP, and the corporate culture"; questions how breaches "weren't unearthed during the hiring process"; risk that the pivot back to oil & gas could be threatened or delayed.
  • RBC Capital Markets: if underperformance continues, "BP shares would become more attractive for a potential acquirer" — explicit takeover speculation angle.
  • Near-term read-through: derails BP's YTD catch-up versus oil & gas peers, though could give O'Neill room to accelerate her strategic agenda.
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Barclays upgrades to 'overweight' from 'equal weight'; price target raised to 465p from 450p.
  • Thesis: BLND returning to earnings growth after three years of "broadly flat" earnings; valuation "screens attractively" on improved visibility.
  • Framing shift: market now rewards recurring earnings, EPS growth and cash flow delivery rather than NAV, ERV growth and valuation yields — favourable for BLND.
  • Five-year EPS CAGR raised to 4.5% from 3.7%.
  • Leasing momentum highlighted, "in particular from AI and technology tenants," in an "increasingly bifurcated London office market."
  • Barclays: "This is not a NAV story; it is an income and income-growth story, and we think British Land's high-quality office portfolio and well-performing retail park portfolio will lead to continued operational strength."
  • Peer call: 'overweight' reiterated on Land Securities; "UK sentiment remains a headwind, but we think both companies deserve more investor attention."
  • Share price reaction: 407.60p, up 2.9% Tuesday afternoon; YTD only +2.5% (underperformer), 12-month +5.2%.

body:
• Thermal issue identified on 21 May in a storage tank containing methyl methacrylate (MMA) at GKN Aerospace's Garden Grove, California facility; MMA is used in high-strength aerospace acrylic materials.
  • Orange County Fire Authority issued mandatory evacuation orders for parts of the surrounding community on 22 May as a precaution.
  • Evacuation area significantly reduced on 25 May following positive progress and reduced risk.
  • As of 6pm local time on 25 May: no injuries, no leaks, and no contamination confirmed by local authorities.
  • GKN working with customers on operational recovery and supply plans.
  • Garden Grove site (GKN Aerospace Transparency Systems) employs ~500, designs/manufactures military and commercial transparencies, and generated £136m of sales in FY2025 (context: ~3.8% of group £3.59bn turnover).
  • Further announcements to follow as appropriate.

body:
• Proposing a name change to "Trekor Metals Limited", subject to shareholder approval at the AGM on 24 June 2026.
  • Rationale: reflect the growing business and expanded asset base in North America; positioning around ambition to grow North America's copper sector.
  • CEO Stuart McDonald: "Our journey will continue as Trekor, a name that embodies our values and reflects our ambition to continue to grow North America's copper sector."
  • AGM to be held in person at the Terminal City Club, Vancouver, on 24 June 2026 at 2:00 p.m. Pacific Time.
  • Other AGM resolutions: setting number of directors at nine, election of directors, reappointment of PwC as auditors, and advisory say-on-pay vote.
  • Board unanimously recommends shareholders vote FOR all resolutions.
  • Proxy voting deadline: 2:00 p.m. Pacific Time on 22 June 2026; record date 5 May 2026.
  • Laurel Hill Advisory Group acting as proxy solicitation agent.
  • No changes to operational guidance, financials, or capital structure disclosed in this release.
type_workings: Rule 'routine': opening bullets reference the AGM

body:
• Grand opening of Allen Townhomes in Arlington, Washington (25 minutes north of Everett), marking expansion north of Seattle.
  • Small community: only 36 lots at full build-out, with pricing starting in the $530s.
  • Two new floor plans offered: two-bedroom Berlin (with first-floor flex room) and three-bedroom Cyrus (with first-floor guest suite and larger laundry room); both feature open-concept layouts and attached one-car garages.
  • Introducing the CompleteHome Plus™ package at this community, including quartz countertops, Whirlpool stainless appliances, wood cabinetry, designer lighting — all included in sales price.
  • Sales incentives: interest rate buy-downs and builder closing cost contributions available; HOA-maintained exteriors/yards.
  • Grand Opening event scheduled for May 30, with limited-time new home savings available for one weekend only.
  • Zachary Penrod, VP of Sales: "We expect strong demand for this community. It's tucked away yet conveniently located to everything Snohomish County has to offer."
  • Context: LGI operates across 36 markets in 21 states and has closed over 80,000 homes since 2003.

body:
• JPMorgan sector review argues Middle East conflict tailwinds for European cyclical chemicals may be fading, with most cyclical names already up 15%–40% YTD despite weak structural outlook.
  • Croda kept as a "preferred long" with 'overweight' rating, alongside Fuchs SE.
  • Croda shares +3.8% to 2,977p in London on the day.
  • Rating actions on peers: Evonik downgraded to 'underweight' from 'neutral'; Lanxess cut to 'neutral' from 'overweight'; BASF placed on 'negative catalyst watch' into Q2 results (July) on H2 2026 consensus downside risk; Wacker Chemie retained at 'underweight' with 'negative catalyst watch'; Brenntag stays 'underweight'.
  • JPM notes chemical prices/spreads have fallen sharply from late-March/early-April peaks as "panic buying eases, some demand is destroyed, and supply shortages prove less severe than initially feared."
  • Broker expects weaker European pricing/margins from June as disrupted supply ramps back up and feedstock constraints ease.
  • A prolonged Strait of Hormuz disruption viewed as net negative for European chemicals — producers/governments sourcing feedstocks via alternative routes limits effective supply cuts.
  • In an oversupplied market with fragile demand, higher feedstock costs unlikely to be fully passed through — raising margin squeeze risk.

body:
• Duncan Tatton-Brown, Non-executive Director and Audit and Risk Committee Chair, will not seek re-election at the 2026 AGM.
  • Will retire from the Board on 18 June 2026 after seven years of service (since IPO).
  • Successor as Audit and Risk Committee Chair to be announced in due course.
  • Chair Brian McBride commented: "Duncan has stewarded the Audit and Risk Committee masterfully since IPO and has provided immense guidance, constructive challenge and advice to me, the Board and Trainline during his tenure. We look forward to announcing his successor as Audit and Risk Committee Chair in due course."
  • Announcement made in accordance with LR 6.4.6.

body:
• IFRS pretax profit slumped to £5.7m from £27.9m, with basic EPS of 4.6p vs 22.8p prior year.
  • Prior year boosted by £32.2m net gain on sale/revaluation of investment properties vs just £2.7m this year.
  • Revenue rose 4.1% to £33.3m (from £32.0m).
  • Net rental income fell 21% to £15.4m (from £19.6m).
  • EPRA net tangible asset value per share up 0.9% to 351p (from 348p).
  • Final dividend cut to 1.00p (vs 3.50p); total payout halved to 2.50p (vs 5.00p).
  • Additional return of 13.9p per share proposed from realised profits on sale of 100 New Bridge Street.
  • Two further office developments on track to complete within the next eight months.
  • Management "very confident" of capitalising on progress, citing "material" near-term upside from letting space at rents "significantly ahead of the original underwrite".
  • Demand commentary: in a market of "increasingly constrained supply", demand is driving rental growth as occupiers act to ensure they don't miss out.

time_period: Branch network update, ahead of June 2026
body:
• Nationwide to overtake Lloyds as UK's largest branch network next month, with 696 branches vs Lloyds' shrinking estate.
  • Lloyds starts a new tranche of 95 closures later this month across Lloyds, Halifax and Bank of Scotland brands, leaving the group with 610 branches by March 2027.
  • Nationwide pledges to keep 605 Nationwide and 91 Virgin Money branches open until at least 2030; no immediate plans for new openings but extending opening hours (Saturdays, longer hours) on rising demand.
  • Current account openings via Nationwide branches +20% year-on-year.
  • CEO Debbie Crosbie: "We see huge success in our branch network and continue to see it, so perhaps the tide is turning and we will certainly continue to invest in our branches."
  • Crosbie on customer behaviour: "more and more people really value face-to-face, human contact… particularly for areas like fraud, bereavement and power of attorney."
  • Nationwide announces fourth "Fairer Share" payment: £100 to ~4.4 million eligible members in June; cumulative returns to members will reach ~£1.5 billion since 2023.
  • Nationwide FY (year to end-March) pre-tax profit £1.49bn, down from £2.3bn (–35%), prior year flattered by one-off gain on Virgin Money acquisition.
  • Virgin Money brand to be phased out, customers migrated to Nationwide, with first branch rebranding in 2028.
  • Lloyds shares –1.7% to 98.18p Thursday morning.
period_detail: Branch network update, ahead of June 2026

body:
• Armenian subsidiary Ameriabank secured a loan facility of up to USD 100m from the Asian Development Bank (ADB) to fund MSME lending and green investments.
  • Described as ADB's largest private sector transaction in Armenia to date.
  • Allocation: up to 75% for on-lending to MSMEs, with ≥10% earmarked for women-owned businesses and ≥25% for green finance (renewables, energy efficiency).
  • Additional up to $125,000 in technical assistance from ADB to strengthen Ameriabank's sustainability framework.
  • Ameriabank CFO Hovhannes Toroyan: "We are delighted to strengthen our partnership with the ADB in supporting MSMEs across Armenia through greater access to affordable financing."
  • Lion Finance CFO Giorgi Shagidze: "This facility will enable Ameriabank to continue empowering entrepreneurs across the country. The dedicated funding for green projects and women-owned enterprises underscores our shared commitment to building a sustainable and inclusive economy."
  • ADB Armenia Country Director Lyaziza Sabyrova framed the facility as longer-term financing to support jobs and a climate-resilient economy.

body:
• Appoints Rafael 'Rafa' Carrasco as CEO, North America, effective 3 August.
  • Carrasco joins from WM (formerly Waste Management Inc), where he was most recently president of WM Healthcare Solutions and SVP of strategy.
  • At WM, he "oversaw multi-site operations with 200+ branches and over 10,000 corporate and field-based employees across the US, Canada, the UK, Ireland and Western Europe."
  • Described as having "a distinguished track record of leadership in large-scale, route-based service industries" and "highly experienced at executing a recurring revenue model, serving commercial and residential customers."
  • Outgoing North America CEO Alain Moffroid to remain in the NA leadership group, supporting the company through end-2027 to ensure smooth transition.
  • CEO Mike Duffy: "[Carrasco] has an impressive track record in field-based, recurring service operations, making him the ideal leader to drive our North American business forward. He is an execution-focused leader, committed to colleagues and culture, and enhancing the customer experience."
  • Context: North America is Rentokil's largest and most scrutinised division following the Terminix integration; appointment of an external, route-based services operator signals continued focus on execution and operational delivery.
  • Shares 467.30p; up 33% over 12 months.

body:
• Pretax profit +29% to £152.5m (from £117.8m).
  • Revenue +25% to £117.9m (from £94.2m).
  • Surplus from investment property +19% to £99.4m (from £83.2m).
  • Basic EPRA EPS +62% to 8.6p (from 5.3p).
  • IFRS NAV and NTA per share +6.1% to 524p.
  • Total dividend +3.8% to 8.2p (final 5.3p).
  • Total accounting return of 7.9% this year, targeting build-on towards >10% annual RoE.
  • Operational highlights: record leasing significantly ahead of rental values, opportunistic acquisitions at a discount, £0.5bn of asset sales at a premium, completions into a "severely undersupplied" London market.
  • CEO Toby Courtauld: "I am pleased to report on a year of numerous operational successes. Despite the multiple macro-economic and geopolitical uncertainties overshadowing London's economy, we delivered many of the core components of our contra-cyclical strategy, beating expectations".
  • Outlook — CEO: "Whilst the external environment remains volatile, we are well positioned to build on this momentum; demand for our premium HQ and Flex spaces is strong and our pipeline is long, concentrated in the most sought-after, core locations. As a result, we remain confident we can deliver a cost of capital beating outcome for the forthcoming financial year and substantial income and value growth over the medium term."
  • Shares up 1.4% to 306.60p on the morning of release.
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with financial performance figures

time_period: Full year to 31 March 2026 (FY26), with FY27 guidance
body:
• Shares fell 2.6% to 224.90p, though still up 22% YTD; reaction reflected dividend outlook "mildly disappointing" vs heightened pre-print expectations.
  • Pretax profit +7.6% to £1.44bn (vs £1.05bn), boosted by lower depreciation and amortisation.
  • Revenue -3.5% to £19.65bn (vs £20.36bn), narrowly below £19.68bn consensus; dragged by lower International revenue, handset trading declines and weaker adjusted UK service revenue.
  • Adjusted UK service revenue -0.8% to £15.45bn, beating £15.38bn consensus; lower voice volumes offset by CPI-linked price rises and improved Openreach FTTP mix.
  • Adjusted EBITDA £8.23bn vs £8.21bn (consensus £8.21bn).
  • Free cash flow slipped to £1.51bn from £1.60bn.
  • Openreach broadband line losses 825,000 for FY26 (Q4: 203,000), better than 850,000 guidance; FY27 losses guided to ~800,000.
  • Record FTTP build of 4.8m premises passed; 2.2m net FTTP adds; now reaches more than two-thirds of UK homes/businesses, on track for 25m milestone by end-December.
  • Final dividend +1.9% to 5.87p; total FY26 DPS 8.32p, +2.0%, in line with consensus.
  • New dividend policy: low-to-mid single digit growth from FY27 onwards until "metrics consistent with a BBB+ credit rating are reached"; thereafter "residual cash flow will be available for enhanced distributions to shareholders".
  • JPMorgan pushed back credit-rating-driven re-rating by one year but maintains DPS doubling to 18p by March 2031; flags upside risk as forecast £1.6bn FY31 dividend is just 50% of residual pre-pension equity FCF.
  • BofA delayed dividend re-rating expectation to FY29, reiterated Buy with a "'when-not-if' cash return re-rating event".
  • Citi: dividend messaging may "mildly disappoint"; Deutsche: change "well trailed".
  • Transformation plan extended by one year to FY30; targeted savings raised to £3.7bn from £3.0bn.
  • FY27 guidance: adjusted EBITDA £8.2–8.3bn; adjusted revenue £19.0–19.5bn; adjusted UK service revenue £15.1–15.4bn.
  • Capex (ex-spectrum) guided to ~£4.3bn in FY27, down from £5.11bn as fibre build peaks roll off.
  • FCF guided to £2.0bn in FY27, rising to £3.0bn by end of decade (from £1.51bn in FY26).
  • CEO Allison Kirkby: "Our record-breaking Openreach full fibre build hit its upgraded target and today reaches more than two thirds of UK homes and businesses, keeping us well on track for our 25 million milestone by the end of December."
  • HL's Britzman: "BT's results were light on fireworks, but they did what they needed to do… today's reiteration of a stronger cash flow outlook gives investors a clearer path to a more cash-generative business"; adds BT "needs to prove that years of heavy network investment can translate into sustainable growth, not just better cash flow as spending falls."
  • Patricia Cobian succeeds Simon Lowth as CFO on 1 September (previously announced).
period_detail: Full year to 31 March 2026, with FY27 guidance

body:
• CMA published a supplementary interim report on ABF's proposed acquisition of Hovis Group via subsidiary ABF Grain Products.
  • Inquiry group now provisionally concludes the merger may NOT substantially lessen competition in Northern Ireland for bread and certain other bakery products — a reversal of its late-March provisional finding that the deal would threaten NI competition.
  • CMA seeking feedback on the supplementary interim report; comment deadline 28 May, with final decision due by 24 June.
  • ABF noted regulator "recognised that, if the transaction is not allowed to proceed, Allied Bakeries will be unable to continue operating under current market conditions."
  • Deal originally announced August 2025 following strategic review of Allied Bakeries.
  • Shares -0.54% to 1,836p; down 14% over 12 months.

time_period: FY to 31 March 2026 (results) / Board changes effective 6 August
body:
• Chair Philip Hourquebie to step down on 6 August, after 5 years as chair (joined board August 2017).
  • Henrietta Baldock (current SID) appointed as new Chair, effective 6 August, providing board leadership continuity.
  • Nkululeko Sowazi appointed non-executive director with immediate effect.
  • Stephen Koseff (ex-CEO, rejoined as NED in Sept 2020) will not stand for re-election at the 6 August AGM.
  • Further NED appointments flagged as part of ongoing board refresh.
  • Management quote: "Henrietta's appointment provides continuity of board leadership as the group continues to implement its strategy of disciplined growth, while maintaining its focus on long-term value creation, prudent risk management and strong governance."
  • FY results described as "resilient", supported by new clients, lending growth and continued net inflows.
  • Pretax profit +4.5% to £901.8m (vs £863.1m).
  • Revenue +4.2% to £2.28bn (vs £2.19bn).
  • Shares +5.6% to 646p in London on the day; Investec Ltd +4.2% in Johannesburg at ZAR139.36; Investec PLC +5.1% at ZAR142.59.

body:
• BP is facing a lawsuit in Kenya over alleged toxic waste dumping in Kargi and Kalacha villages in Marsabit County, with hundreds of petitioners alleging contamination killed thousands of livestock and caused cancer in locals.
  • Liability stems from BP's 1998 acquisition of US oil firm Amoco, which conducted the 1984–1990 oil exploration; BP did not carry out the original activity.
  • Petitioners' lawyer Kelvin Kubai: "British Petroleum has been sued in respect of the legacy activities of Amoco Corporation" and by acquiring Amoco "it assumes both liabilities and assets".
  • Court papers indicate ~200 petitioners, mostly cancer sufferers, have already died and are now represented by family members.
  • Kubai characterised the situation as "environmental genocide," warning numbers could rise.
  • Supporting evidence cited includes a University of Nairobi study finding nitrates 5–10x WHO recommended levels in drinking water, and a January 2020 incident in Kargi where ~7,000 livestock reportedly died in one day.
  • Documents were formally served via the UK foreign affairs ministry on Thursday after BP had disputed service on procedural grounds.
  • Court mention set for 16 June to confirm receipt of documents; BP has yet to comment.
  • No financial quantum disclosed; shares were up 0.4% at 566.10p on Thursday afternoon (note: article close price shown at 551.20p, -2.48%).
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'acquisition of'

body:
• Board has received a revised requisition notice from Saba Capital (via Vidacos Nominees) on 20 May 2026, escalating its earlier 8 May 2026 demand.
  • Revised Requisition now seeks removal of all six Non-Executive Directors (vs five in the Initial Requisition, which had spared Manju Malhotra).
  • Proposes appointment of six new directors: the original four (Nick Shattock, Andrew Sim, Richard Starr, Gregory Attwood) plus Gautam Garg and Simon Hampton.
  • Saba Capital holds interests in ~21.12% of issued share capital.
  • Background: Saba's 8 January 2026 letter proposed a 12-month managed wind-down to address the NAV discount; Board concluded the proposal "is not achievable, nor will it maximise value for shareholders, and so is not in the best interests of the Company and its shareholders as a whole."
  • Board reiterates it "firmly believes in the strength of the Company's existing strategy" with further detail to come at Full Year Results on 10 June 2026.
  • AGM scheduled for 23 July 2026; shareholders advised to take no action at this time.
  • Advisers: J.P. Morgan Cazenove, Rothschild & Co and Stifel.

body:
• WMATA issued a notice of termination on 12 May 2026 to Mobico's US subsidiary Diamond Transportation Services, ceasing paratransit operations effective that date.
  • Diamond is co-operating with WMATA on transition of services.
  • Follows Diamond's civil lawsuit filed 28 January 2026 for breach of contract against WMATA in the US District Court for the District of Columbia (previously disclosed in unaudited 2025 results).
  • Company states: "Due to ongoing and potential further reciprocal litigation, neither Mobico nor Diamond can comment further. There can be no certainty as to the outcome of any ongoing or potential future litigation in relation to the contract."
  • Context: contract awarded in 2024, 5-year term from 1 July 2024 worth ~$370m total, plus five one-year renewal options at WMATA's discretion.
  • Contract turned unprofitable during 2025 due to lower-than-projected volumes and loss of contracted exclusivity, with an onerous contract provision booked in unaudited 2025 results.
  • Further update to be provided as appropriate.

body:
• IFRS profit collapsed to £5.7m (2025: £27.9m); basic EPS 4.6p (2025: 22.8p), reflecting absence of prior-year revaluation/sale gains.
  • EPRA EPS more than doubled to 4.5p (2025: 2.2p) on increased development profits.
  • See-through Total Property Return £23.0m (2025: £52.1m); net rental income -21.4% to £15.4m; development profits £4.9m (2025: £0.3m); revaluation/sale gain £2.7m (2025: £32.2m).
  • EPRA NTA per share 351p (348p); Total Accounting Return 2.3% (2025: 6.0%), well below 10% target.
  • Final dividend slashed to 1.00p (2025: 3.50p); total dividend declared 2.50p (2025: 5.00p) — 50% cut as prior year included special from JJ Mack sale.
  • Additional £17m (13.9p) capital return from 100 New Bridge Street sale: £12m via B Share Scheme + £5m share buyback "expected to commence shortly"; total return 16.4p (2025: 5.0p).
  • 100 New Bridge Street, EC4 sale to State Street completed 20 May 2026 at £333m (Helical share £166.5m), 5.0% cap yield, £1,712 psf; >£95m equity returned to Group.
  • See-through LTV rose to 36.5% (20.9%); pro-forma LTV post-sale and capital return falls to 20.7%.
  • See-through net borrowings £239.2m (£112.8m); 100% of drawn debt hedged; investment debt cost steady at 3.8%; development debt cost 7.7% (8.5%).
  • Liquidity: see-through cash + undrawn facilities £229.8m (£244.5m).
  • Portfolio valuation +0.5% LFL: investment properties -2.1%, development portfolio +5.0%; see-through portfolio £649.5m (£535.4m).
  • Vacancy on completed assets fell to 18.5% (21.3%), pro-forma 11.3% on agreed Bower lettings; Bower campus vacancy to drop to 3.4% from 18.8%.
  • Contracted rents +5% to £21.2m vs ERV £29.1m (significant reversion).
  • WAULT 2.3 years (3.1), rising to 3.3 on agreed Bower lettings.
  • Delta Paddington, W2 acquired Feb 2026 for £55m (Helical £28.1m); £220m PIMCO development facility signed; Mace appointed; PC targeted Q3 2028.
  • Southwark SE1 forward funded with PfL's PBSA platform (429 beds valued >£200m on completion); affordable element forward sold to Southwark Council; targeting >3.0x return on equity, no further equity required.
  • 63 Charterhouse Street, EC1M: resolution to grant planning April 2026 for c.55,000 sq ft office.
  • Brettenham House (128,000 sq ft) completion due August 2026; 10 King William Street (142,000 sq ft) due December 2026 — 270,000 sq ft delivering into market with new build vacancy near 1%.
  • Bower lettings: incident.io took 5th/6th floors (19,592 sq ft); 3rd floor (10,022 sq ft) let post year-end; 12th floor under offer; terms agreed on c.50,000 sq ft regears.
  • Recurring see-through admin costs -20.8% to £6.0m; total admin -19.2% to £8.8m.
  • TSR -8.9% (-3.9%); 5-year annualised -13.4% vs UK equity market +11.1%.
  • Board changes: Robert Fowlds succeeded Richard Cotton as Chair from 1 May 2026.
  • CEO Bonning-Snook: "very confident we will capitalise on the progress made over the last year and realise material near-term upside for Shareholders through letting this space at rents significantly ahead of the original underwrite."
  • Going concern stress: bank covenants withstand 36% fall in contracted rent or 46% fall in property values.
  • Capex commitments £160.5m (Helical share), primarily Delta Paddington (£122.4m) and 10 King William Street (£30.5m).
  • Net asset minimum requirement under PfL Works Agreements currently £323.75m, not expected to exceed £400m — a key constraint cited in sizing the capital return.
type_workings: Rule 'dividend': body declares a dividend with payment details

body:
• Refiling of the Bilboes Gold Project Feasibility Study NI 43-101 Technical Report on SEDAR+, effective date 31 October 2025, prepared by DRA Projects (Pty) Ltd.
  • Original Feasibility Study filed on EDGAR (Form 6-K) on 24 November 2025; Canadian NI 43-101 version filed on SEDAR+ on 6 January 2026.
  • OSC staff review identified that some authors listed by DRA as qualified persons did not meet the NI 43-101 definition of a qualified person.
  • DRA has replaced those authors with individuals meeting the qualified person definition; updated report retitled "Bilboes Gold Project Matabeleland Zimbabwe National Instrument 43-101 Technical Report" filed on SEDAR+ the prior day.
  • Under US mining disclosure rules, DRA can act as sole qualified person, so the US Feasibility Study is unchanged.
  • Company explicitly states: "there is no effect on the Bilboes Gold Project and the aforementioned change is solely to comply with Canadian mining disclosure rules."

body:

time_period: Branch network update; FY to end-March
body:
• Nationwide to overtake Lloyds as UK's largest branch network next month, with 696 branches vs Lloyds' 610 once closures complete by March next year.
  • Lloyds is starting a new tranche of 95 branch closures this month across Lloyds, Halifax and Bank of Scotland brands.
  • Nationwide has pledged to keep its 605 Nationwide branches and 91 Virgin Money sites open until at least 2030; no immediate plans for new openings but extending opening hours (Saturdays, longer hours) amid demand.
  • Current account openings via Nationwide branches up 20% year-on-year.
  • CEO Debbie Crosbie: "We see huge success in our branch network and continue to see it, so perhaps the tide is turning and we will certainly continue to invest in our branches."
  • Crosbie noted face-to-face demand particularly for "fraud, bereavement and power of attorney."
  • Nationwide announced 4th "Fairer Share" payment: £100 to ~4.4 million eligible members in June; cumulative ~£1.5bn returned to members since 2023 scheme launch.
  • FY pre-tax profit £1.49bn vs £2.3bn prior year (down ~35%), prior year boosted by one-off gain from Virgin Money acquisition.
  • Virgin Money brand to be phased out, customers transitioned to Nationwide, with first branch rebranding in 2028.
  • Lloyds shares -1.7% to 98.18p on the morning.

body:
• Appoints Rafael 'Rafa' Carrasco as CEO, North America, effective August 3.
  • Carrasco joins from WM (formerly Waste Management Inc), where he was president of WM Healthcare Solutions and previously SVP of strategy.
  • At WM he "oversaw multi-site operations with 200+ branches and over 10,000 corporate and field-based employees across the US, Canada, the UK, Ireland and Western Europe."
  • Rentokil cites his experience "executing a recurring revenue model, serving commercial and residential customers."
  • Outgoing NA CEO Alain Moffroid remains in the NA leadership group to support the transition through end of 2027.
  • CEO Mike Duffy: "[Carrasco] has an impressive track record in field-based, recurring service operations, making him the ideal leader to drive our North American business forward. He is an execution-focused leader, committed to colleagues and culture, and enhancing the customer experience."
  • Context: appointment follows ongoing scrutiny of North America integration execution post-Terminix; shares up 33% over 12 months.

body:
• Smackover Lithium (55% Standard Lithium / 45% Equinor JV) has signed an EPCM agreement with Wood Group USA for the upstream well field of the South West Arkansas (SWA) Project, ahead of FID.
  • Well field scope: 4 well pads, 12 supply wells, 10 injection wells, brine gathering/return pipelines to the Central Processing Facility, plus a gas gathering system to a disposal well.
  • Wood's remit covers EPCM for surface facilities (pads, pipelines, third-party interfaces) plus support services for subsurface/drilling/wells, which remain led by the Project team leveraging Equinor's in-house expertise.
  • A separate EPCC agreement for the CPF (housing the DLE process and conversion to battery-quality lithium carbonate) is expected to be signed with a different counterparty, also in Q2 2026.
  • SWA Project initial-phase design capacity reiterated at 22,500 tpa battery-quality lithium carbonate.
  • Upstream well field accounts for less than one-third of total Project capex (per the DFS filed 15 October 2025).
  • Agreement includes a Limited Notice to Proceed (LNTP), covering detailed engineering, permitting advancement, and early procurement/long-lead vendor engagement to de-risk schedule.
  • Full Notice to Proceed contingent on a positive Final Investment Decision, expected in 2026.
  • COO Andy Robinson: "Signing this Agreement was made possible by our 18-month detailed front-end engineering design (FEED) process that yielded strong capital definition and is critical as we prepare for a Final Investment Decision before moving into construction shortly thereafter."
  • Wood's Richard Neale (Regional President, Americas): "Battery-quality lithium carbonate is critical to strengthening the United States domestic supply chain and this Project marks a meaningful step towards securing critical minerals."
  • Project continues to receive support from the US DOE Office of Critical Minerals and Energy Innovation (award DE-MS0000099).
  • Forward-looking section flags ongoing dependency on securing debt financing on acceptable terms as a key gating item alongside FID.

body:
• CMA published supplementary interim report on ABF's proposed acquisition of Hovis Group via subsidiary ABF Grain Products.
  • Inquiry group now provisionally concludes the merger may NOT substantially lessen competition in Northern Ireland for bread and certain other bakery products — a reversal of the late-March provisional finding that the deal would threaten NI competition.
  • Deal originally announced August 2025 following strategic review of Allied Bakeries.
  • Comment deadline on the supplementary report: 28 May; CMA final decision due by 24 June.
  • ABF highlighted the regulator's acknowledgement that "if the transaction is not allowed to proceed, Allied Bakeries will be unable to continue operating under current market conditions."
  • Shares ~1,835.5p; down 14% over 12 months.

body:
• Smackover Lithium (55% Standard Lithium / 45% Equinor JV) signed an EPCM agreement with Wood Group USA for the upstream well field portion of the South West Arkansas (SWA) Project, ahead of Final Investment Decision.
  • Well field scope: 4 well pads, 12 supply wells, 10 injection wells, brine gathering/return pipelines to the Central Processing Facility (CPF), and a gas gathering system to a disposal well.
  • Wood's remit covers surface facilities (well pads, pipelines, third-party interfaces) plus support services for subsurface/drilling/wells, with drilling led by the Project team leveraging Equinor's subsurface expertise.
  • A separate EPCC contract for the CPF (housing the DLE process and conversion to battery-quality lithium carbonate) is expected to be signed with a different counterparty in Q2 2026.
  • SWA Project Phase 1 design capacity unchanged at 22,500 tpa battery-quality lithium carbonate; well field accounts for less than one-third of total capex per the 15 October 2025 DFS.
  • Agreement includes a Limited Notice to Proceed (LNTP) covering detailed engineering, permitting and early procurement/long-lead equipment — designed to de-risk schedule pre-FID.
  • Full Notice to Proceed contingent on a positive FID, still expected in 2026.
  • COO Andy Robinson: "Signing this Agreement was made possible by our 18-month detailed front-end engineering design (FEED) process that yielded strong capital definition and is critical as we prepare for a Final Investment Decision before moving into construction shortly thereafter."
  • Wood's Richard Neale (Americas Regional President): "Battery-quality lithium carbonate is critical to strengthening the United States domestic supply chain and this Project marks a meaningful step towards securing critical minerals."
  • Project continues to be supported by US DoE Office of Critical Minerals and Energy Innovation (award DE-MS0000099).
  • Forward-looking section flags ongoing need to secure debt financing on acceptable terms as a key dependency alongside FID and permitting.

body:
• Routine Form 6-K filing of AGM/Special Meeting materials (Management Information Circular dated 20 May 2026); virtual AGM set for 16 July 2026, record date 20 May 2026.
  • Shares issued and outstanding: 243,859,072 as at record date.
  • AGM resolutions: receive FY25 financials, re-appoint PwC as auditor, fix board at nine directors, elect directors, and re-approve rolling Option Plan and Long Term Incentive Plan.
  • Director slate (nine): Robert Cross (Non-Exec Chair), Andrew Robinson, David Park, Jeffrey Barber, Volker Berl, Claudia D'Orazio, Anca Rusu, Paul Collins, Karen Narwold.
  • FY25 share price rebound: total shareholder return index rose from 64.58 (Dec-24) to 197.85 (Dec-25), outperforming S&P/TSX Composite but lagging S&P/TSX Metals & Mining (249.62); attributed to critical minerals sector strength and project progress.
  • Key FY25 operational milestones flagged: Arkansas Oil and Gas Commission unitisation and royalty established for SWA Phase 1, Definitive Feasibility Study completed, maiden inferred resource published for the Franklin Project (East Texas), and successful equity financing — all co-developed with JV partner Equinor.
  • LANXESS South Plant DLE demonstration plant continues operating for proof-of-concept, process refinement and operator training.
  • NEO total comp (FY25): CEO David Park $2.37m (vs $4.17m in stub FY24, which included a large replacement option grant); President/COO Andrew Robinson $1.75m; CFO Salah Gamoudi $1.27m; CDO Michael Barman $0.99m.
  • All FY25 NEO equity grants (26 Mar 2025) struck at $1.35, vs year-end close of $4.47 — substantial in-the-money value; e.g., Park's unexercised options worth ~$8.7m.
  • Change-of-control payouts if triggered at 31 Dec 2025: Park $9.11m, Robinson $7.46m, Gamoudi $4.83m, Barman $3.59m.
  • Equity overhang: 16,163,654 securities outstanding under equity plans (weighted avg exercise price $2.40), with 7,806,903 remaining available — both Option Plan and Incentive Plan capped at 10% of issued shares.
  • Outstanding awards: 12,161,679 options (8,894,300 held by NEOs/Directors) and 4,952,845 share units.
  • Auditor fees FY25: $297,542 total ($189,156 audit + $108,386 audit-related), down from $342,534 in the six-month stub FY24.
  • Board diversity: 3 of 9 directors are women; no visible minority, Indigenous or disabled directors disclosed.
  • Insider exercises during FY25: Robinson exercised 50,000 options at $3.72 (realised $83,500); Cross exercised 200,000 options at $2.45 (realised $463,000).
  • No dividends, pension plans or deferred comp plans; no material litigation, indebtedness of insiders, or related-party transactions disclosed.

body:
• Announced a broad slate of innovations for da Vinci 5 and supporting ecosystem, plus extended-use Force Feedback instruments and product security/reliability enhancements.
  • >100 updates rolling out to all da Vinci 5 users via measured US launch from June 2026, with global rollout later subject to regulatory clearance.
  • Telepresence upgraded with new full-OR camera, live cursor and enhanced audio to leverage Intuitive's network of >101,000 da Vinci-trained surgeons.
  • New mobile/phone-tap login to da Vinci 5 console with multifactor authentication.
  • SimNow 2 simulation platform expanded with six new exercises plus warm-up/skills playlists.
  • Later-2026 US features (pending clearance): Digital ruler (3–15cm intraoperative measurement), surgeon-initiated tool eject, and multi-arm targeting for multi-quadrant surgery.
  • Force Feedback extended use: 5 of 6 instruments increased from 6 to 15 uses (one cleared for 10) — materially improves per-procedure economics and supply availability.
  • Guidance signal: further core instrument use-life extensions expected in 2027, reducing per-procedure costs.
  • dV5 Complete Care plan guarantees 98% uptime; currently running >99% actual uptime.
  • Installed base update: >1,400 da Vinci 5 systems installed globally, with >380,000 procedures by >12,800 surgeons across 10 countries (as of Q1 2026).
  • Significant enhancement to instrument encryption technology flagged amid rising hospital cybersecurity concerns (pipeline, not yet cleared).
  • CEO Dave Rosa: "We recognize that product quality, safety and reliability are of the utmost importance to our customers, as they increasingly rely on our products to serve millions of patients globally."
  • Iman Jeddi (SVP, da Vinci platforms): da Vinci 5 launched with "10,000 times the computing power of Xi" and is positioned as Intuitive's "most integrated and future-ready platform."
  • Jeddi on Force Feedback: "we expect that Force Feedback will play a central role in advancing augmented dexterity and automated instrument control" — signals direction toward surgical automation.

body:
• Pretax profit up 4.5% to £901.8m (vs £863.1m), described as "resilient" amid uncertain macro.
  • Revenue up 4.2% to £2.28bn (vs £2.19bn).
  • Net interest income down 1.6% to £1.34bn, hit by negative endowment effect from declining global rates.
  • Non-interest revenue up 14% to £945.7m, driven by stronger banking fee income and higher annuity fees in SA Wealth & Investment.
  • Customer-flow trading income lifted by increased client hedging activity amid global market volatility.
  • Southern African wealth FUM up 15% to £27.0bn; net inflows of £987m in discretionary/annuity funds plus £333m from Sept 2025 Swiss acquisition.
  • Associate Rathbones (~41% owned) FUMA up 9.1% to £113.6bn.

  • ROE 13.6% (vs 13.9%); ROTE 15.7% (vs 16.2%) — both slipped modestly.
  • Final dividend 21.0p (+5%); full-year DPS 38.5p (+5.5%).
  • CEO Fani Titi: "The group delivered a resilient performance in an uncertain macro-economic environment, reflecting the strength of our diversified business model and balance sheet."
  • FY27 flagged as peak investment year — implies elevated cost base near-term.
  • FY27 guidance: ROE 13.0–14.0%, ROTE 14.8–15.8% (both within but at lower end of target ranges of 13–17% and 14–18%).

body:
• Pretax profit +7.6% to £1.44bn (vs £1.05bn), aided by lower D&A.
  • Revenue -3.5% to £19.65bn (vs £20.36bn), narrowly missing £19.68bn consensus, dragged by International, handset trading and adjusted UK service revenue.
  • Adjusted UK service revenue -0.8% to £15.45bn, beat £15.38bn consensus; CPI-linked price rises and better Openreach FTTP mix offset lower voice volumes.
  • Adjusted EBITDA £8.23bn (vs £8.21bn), marginally ahead of £8.21bn consensus.
  • Free cash flow eased to £1.51bn from £1.60bn.
  • New dividend policy: grow DPS by low-to-mid single digits annually from FY27 until "metrics consistent with a BBB+ credit rating are reached"; thereafter "residual cash flow will be available for enhanced distributions to shareholders".
  • Final dividend +1.9% to 5.87p; total FY payout +2.0% to 8.32p (in line with consensus).
  • Transformation plan extended one year to FY30; cost savings target raised to £3.7bn from £3.0bn.
  • FY27 guidance: adjusted EBITDA £8.2bn–£8.3bn; adjusted revenue £19.0bn–£19.5bn; adjusted UK service revenue £15.1bn–£15.4bn.
  • Capex (ex-spectrum) guided to ~£4.3bn in FY27, down from £5.11bn — material step-down as FTTP build peaks.
  • Free cash flow guided to £2.0bn in FY27, rising to £3.0bn by end of decade (vs £1.51bn just reported).
  • Openreach broadband line losses 203k in Q4; FY losses of 825k beat 850k guidance; FY27 guidance ~800k losses.
  • Record FTTP build of 4.8m premises passed; record 2.2m FTTP net adds.
  • CEO Allison Kirkby: "Our record-breaking Openreach full fibre build hit its upgraded target and today reaches more than two thirds of UK homes and businesses, keeping us well on track for our 25 million milestone by the end of December."
  • CFO transition: Patricia Cobian succeeds Simon Lowth on 1 September (previously announced).
  • Shares -0.7% at 229.18p on the day; +25% YTD, +35% over 12 months.

body:
• Pretax profit fell 16% to £297.1m (from £352.8m), despite higher rental income and revenue.
  • Revenue rose 17% to £464.6m (from £396.7m).
  • Net rental income increased to £455.3m from £390.6m.
  • Revaluation profit on investment properties dropped to £68.2m from £106.0m; £3.6m loss on revaluation of investments (vs £0.9m gain).
  • £16.3m acquisition costs and £9.6m goodwill impairment booked.
  • Total dividend up 3.8% to 12.45p (from 12.00p); final dividend held at 3.3p.
  • EPRA EPS up 2.4% to 13.5p (from 13.1p).
  • Portfolio grew to £7.6bn; logistics exposure rose to 53% from 46%, boosted by Urban Logistics REIT acquisition.
  • £1.55bn of acquisitions and £318m of disposals completed during the year.
  • Warned global outlook remains "highly uncertain", citing geopolitical tensions, higher bond yields and inflationary pressures.
  • Flagged UK property investment market liquidity remains constrained, particularly for transactions above £20m.
  • CEO Andrew Jones said the company had made progress in creating the UK's "largest and most efficient NNN REIT".
  • Pursuing proposed all-share takeover of Picton Property Income (with Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust), announced previous week.
  • Indicative terms: Picton shareholders receive 0.190 LondonMetric shares + 0.881 SREIT shares per Picton share, valuing Picton at ~£403m (78.2p/share).
  • Offer is a 7.0% premium to undisturbed price but a 9% discount to EPRA NTA and 5.6% discount to December portfolio valuation.

body:
• Pretax loss widened to £552m from £401m loss prior year (loss deepened by ~£151m / 38%).
  • Total revenue +12% to £3.95bn (from £3.53bn).
  • Headline EBITDA +16% to £4.09bn from £3.54bn (figure as reported in article).
  • Airline total headline CASK +4.7% to 6.73p (vs 6.43p), including previously flagged one-offs and £25m additional fuel costs in March.
  • H2 is 58% sold, down 2ppt YoY — forward bookings hit by Middle East conflict, later booking curve.
  • In-month bookings "continue to show year-on-year strength"; full summer schedule to be operated with "normal supply visibility" for next four weeks.
  • H2 guidance: headline CASK ex-fuel to rise low single digits; fuel CASK uncertain due to price volatility.
  • 72% hedged at USD726/mt; every USD100/mt move = ~£35m fuel cost impact.
  • easyJet holidays customers expected to grow low double digits in FY2026 from 3.1m base.
  • FY2026 outlook: "remains uncertainty" over financial outturn due to "lower than normal visibility of forward bookings".
  • CEO Kenton Jarvis on fuel: "not seeing any disruption to fuel supply"; urged passengers to "book with confidence".
  • Jarvis on supply diversification: "there's a lot less oil coming from the Gulf region, but fuel suppliers have successfully diversified, with production increased in Norway, in West Africa, in the Americas, and refining capacity for jet fuel has also increased substantially outside of the Gulf region."
  • Strategy quote: "Our strategy is clear - through disciplined growth, accelerated upgauging, and continued expansion of easyJet holidays, we aim to bounce back from this year's Middle East related setbacks, and then further progress towards our medium-term financial targets and deliver attractive shareholder returns as the operating environment normalises."
  • Shares +1.7% to 353.00p on the morning of release.

  • Pretax profit GBP588.2m, +11% from GBP530.5m.
  • Final dividend 59.3p (+4.6%); total dividend 87p, +4.8% from 83p.
  • Six funds closed at or above target in the last 24 months, including final closes for Infrastructure II and Metropolitan II.
  • Established a strategic relationship with Amundi during the year.
  • CEO Durteste: "We are experiencing clear demand from institutional allocators globally for our strategies, and are unaffected by challenges being faced by certain evergreen vehicles in the US."
  • CEO Durteste: "FY26 was a strong year for ICG. We reinforced our scaled competitive position, established a strategic relationship with Amundi, and built on our track record of strategic and financial resilience."
  • Shares down 0.7% at 1,820p on the morning of release (despite the headline beat, suggesting market had priced in the strength).

body:
• Pretax profit +35% to £92.8m (vs £68.8m YoY).
  • Revenue +19% to £183.0m (vs £153.2m).
  • Interim dividend 5.00p, +11% (vs 4.50p).
  • New £15m share buyback announced, on top of existing £50m programme.
  • Record 79,000 net new customers added, taking total to 723,000.
  • Platform AUA +5% to £108.7bn.
  • Record net inflows of £4.2bn, +19% (vs £3.3bn); favourable market movements added £1.2bn (vs £0.6bn).
  • Guidance upgrade: now expects FY revenue margin, pretax profit and pretax profit margin all higher than previously guided.
  • Plans to invest more than originally planned in H2 in brand/marketing, while "still expecting to deliver materially higher profitability."
  • CEO Michael Summersgill: "The platform market presents significant long‑term growth opportunities, and our continued business investment positions us well to capitalise on these. We remain confident in the outlook, with strong momentum continuing into the second half of the year."
  • Shares rose ~14% intraday, leading the FTSE 250.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives half-year period 'H1 to 31 March (six months)'

body:
• Reported revenue +16% to £2.01bn (from £1.74bn), boosted by CP Kelco consolidation.
  • Adjusted revenue -3% (pro-forma as if CP Kelco owned from start of FY25).
  • Reported pretax profit +49% to £131m (from £88m).
  • Adjusted pretax profit -5% to £238m; adjusted EBITDA -3% to £415m.
  • Adjusted EPS -16% to 40.4p.
  • Final dividend cut 1.5% to 13.2p (from 13.4p); full-year dividend held flat at 19.8p.
  • CP Kelco $1.8bn acquisition completed November 2024; integration described as successfully completed.
  • CEO Nick Hampton: financial performance "disappointing"; "acting with urgency to return the business to top-line growth."
  • Hampton cited "softer market demand than anticipated, an increasingly complex geopolitical landscape and the integration of two large global businesses."
  • FY27 guidance (constant currency): "modest" revenue growth, volume-led and H2-weighted.
  • EBITDA "broadly flat", before a $20m headwind from rescheduled bio-gums capacity consolidation.
  • Outlook assumes limited Middle East conflict impact; mitigating cost inflation via procurement, operational discipline and pricing.
  • Takeover situation: Ingredion made non-binding indicative offer at up to 615p/share, valuing TATE at ~£2.74bn; PUSU deadline 11 June.
  • Shares +0.7% at 525.83p in morning trade (offer represents ~17% premium to that level).
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with full-year financial figures

body:
• FY26 adjusted pretax profit expected at top end of consensus range of £154.5m–£159.3m, vs £166.9m in FY25 (implying a ~5–7% YoY decline even at the top end).
  • Year "ended strongly" driven by vehicle-on-hire growth in Spain and UK & Ireland, with "good" progress in Claims & Services.
  • Average vehicle on hire up 4.9% for the year.
  • Multiple contract wins and renewals secured, underpinning momentum.
  • Steady-state cash generation has "stepped up materially", supporting growth ambitions.
  • CEO Martin Ward: "Customers are clearly seeing significant value in our service-led offering, and I am very pleased with the contributions from across our divisions this year. Average vehicle on hire is up 4.9% for the year and we have secured a number of valued contract wins and renewals, underpinning this good momentum; steady state cash generation has stepped up materially and supports our ambition for further growth."
  • Full-year results scheduled for 8 July 2026.
  • Shares +2.5% on the day; +23% over 12 months.
type_workings: Rule 'trading update': 'trading update' named in period line

body:
• Pretax profit £143m, +6.7% YoY (vs £134m).
  • Revenue £1.49bn, +2.5% YoY (vs £1.45bn).
  • Like-for-like sales +3.3%, with strong performance across brand portfolio.
  • Claims continued market share outperformance in H1, with confidence this extends into H2.
  • Cost headwinds for FY26 guided down to ~£120m, from ~£130m at the Q1 update in January.
  • FY27 cost headwinds expected to normalise to ~£95m.
  • Shares down ~8% on the day to ~230.5p despite the print, suggesting market had expected more.
  • CEO Phil Urban: "Maintaining profits despite the significant inflationary cost challenges facing the sector is testament to the dedication of our teams in delivering the benefits of our Ignite and capital programmes."
  • Urban added: "Despite the backdrop of macro uncertainty our priorities remain unchanged, our guest scores are at record highs, we remain committed to the delivery of quality experiences, and we are well placed to further grow market share."

time_period: Third quarter to end of April 2026
body:
• Loan book +1.1% QoQ to £9.3bn (from £9.2bn at end-Jan); +2% on underlying basis, driven by Motor Finance growth and Invoice Finance seasonality reversal.
  • Annualised YTD net interest margin 7.0%, vs 7.1% in H1; continues to guide full-year NIM "slightly lower" than 7% on loan book mix.
  • Now expects to exceed £25m annualised savings target for FY2026 via simplification, outsourcing and offshoring.
  • Adjusted operating expenses expected to come in below prior £450m guidance.
  • Central functions operating loss now guided to lower end of £45m–£50m range.
  • Motor finance provision raised to £320m following FCA's March consumer redress scheme publication, resulting in an additional £30m P&L charge in Q3.
  • Management: "While there are elements of the scheme that we disagree with, we continue to believe that the existing scheme offers a quick, clear and certain route to resolving this matter for all relevant parties."
  • Caveat: "the ultimate cost to the group remains subject to the outcome of the legal challenges to the scheme and any further legal, regulatory or industry developments including court claims and complaints from consumers."
  • FY2026 results remain on track vs guidance, subject to macro.
  • CEO Mike Morgan: "We have delivered a solid performance in the third quarter and continue to execute our strategy through this important transitional year... Our capital position remains strong after absorbing the additional provision for motor finance commissions, enabling investment in future growth to further support the UK economy."
  • Shares -0.8% at 456.20p on the day.
period_detail: Third quarter to end of April 2026
