body:
• Group revenue down ~10% like-for-like in the four months to 30 April.
  • Domestic brick market volumes fell 11% in Q1; concrete product volumes "relatively weak" amid continued challenging UK residential construction conditions.
  • High single-digit YoY increase in brick volumes in April, with further positive trading in May; Q1 brick market share in line with prior year.
  • Concrete business expected to improve "in the coming months" based on infrastructure project spending cycles.
  • Cost inflation from rising energy and fuel prices expected to continue into H2; hedged over 85% of energy requirements for the first nine months.
  • Shares traded 3.0% higher at 99.60p on the update.
  • Full-year outturn expected "broadly in line with current market consensus expectations" despite Middle East conflict uncertainty.
  • CEO Joe Hudson: "Following challenging market conditions in the early months of 2026, including a very wet start to the year, we have seen a promising improvement in sales volumes in our clay business, with the group successfully maintaining market share."
  • On outlook: "This challenging backdrop is likely to continue in the near term, albeit we continue to see the potential for some improvement in H2."
  • Hudson added: "Whilst we are mindful of the potential impact of the Middle East conflict, the board believes recent activity levels support the group's capability to deliver a full year outturn broadly in line with current market consensus expectations."
  • Hudson on medium-term positioning: "with a well invested, lower-cost, more efficient and sustainable manufacturing network, Ibstock is well positioned to deliver growth in the medium term."
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with full-year financial figures

body:
• Adjusted EPS +4.8% to 82.9p (FY25: 79.1p); basic EPS 77.1p (+5.9%); HEPS 73.1p (+0.7%).
  • Revenue +4.2% to £2,281.4m; adjusted operating profit +3.4% to £951.0m.
  • NII -1.6% to £1,335.8m on endowment drag from lower rates and competitive pricing; non-interest revenue +13.6% to £945.7m with fee & commission income +14.7%.
  • Cost-to-income ratio 52.9% (FY25: 52.6%); operating costs +4.7% reflecting tech/people investment.
  • Credit loss ratio 36bps (FY25: 38bps), within 25–45bps TTC range; ECL charges £124.2m (FY25: £119.2m).
  • ROE 13.6% (FY25: 13.9%); ROTE 15.7% (FY25: 16.2%) — both within target ranges but down YoY.
  • Net core loans +9.6% to £35.5bn; customer deposits +8.7% to £44.7bn.
  • SA Wealth FUM +15.4% to £27.0bn; net inflows £987m in discretionary/annuity funds plus £333m from Swiss acquisition (Sept 2025); £365m non-discretionary outflows.
  • Rathbones FUMA £113.6bn (FY25: £104.1bn); Investec's equity-accounted contribution at 43.15% effective interest (vs 41.25% prior); £4.3m catch-up booked in H1.
  • Total DPS 38.5p (+5.5%), final 21.0p (FY25: 20.0p); 46.4% payout ratio.
  • £110m / c.R2.5bn buyback (announced May 2025) completed.
  • CET1: Investec Limited 13.6% (FY25: 14.8%, impacted by new Basel output floor adding 40bps RWA drag); Investec plc 13.0% (FY25: 12.6%); UK still on standardised approach but progressing to IRB migration.
  • TNAV/share +9.2% to 553.1p; NAV/share +8.3% to 636.6p.
  • FY27 guidance: Group ROE 13.0–14.0%, ROTE 14.8–15.8%; SA ROE 18.0–19.0%; UK & Other ROTE 12.5–13.5% (near lower end of target); cost-to-income 52.0–54.0%; CLR within 25–45bps TTC.
  • FY27 expected to be peak investment year; positive inflection in earnings/returns to commence FY28.
  • FY28 guidance: ROE 13.8–14.6%, ROTE 15.8–16.6%.
  • On track for FY30 targets of c.16.0% ROE and c.18.0% ROTE (upper end of target ranges).
  • UK Specialist Banking adjusted operating profit £401.6m (-2.1%), ROTE 13.7% (FY25: 14.5%); CLR 57bps (FY25: 60bps); Stage 3 stable at 3.4%.
  • SA Specialist Banking adjusted operating profit R10,579m (+6.0% in Rands); CLR 14bps; cost-to-income 48.6%.
  • Wealth & Investment adjusted operating profit +13.9% to £127.9m.
  • Motor finance: existing £30m provision deemed appropriate following FCA's 30 March 2026 scheme scoping; outcome subject to ongoing legal challenges.
  • £3.1bn of sustainable/transition finance facilitated in year one toward £18bn FY30 target; new decarbonisation targets including 20–45% emissions intensity reduction in power generation by FY30.
  • Strategic shift: IW&I UK/Rathbones moving from referral model to integrated assets-under-advice model, with Investec leading client relationships.
  • LCR: Investec plc 349%, Investec Bank Ltd 185.7%; NSFR 139% and 117.3% respectively — well above requirements.
  • CEO Fani Titi: "We are on track to achieve returns at the upper end of our target range by FY2030."
  • CEO on macro: "The Group delivered a resilient performance in an uncertain macro-economic environment, reflecting the strength of our diversified business model and balance sheet."
  • Private Client strategy business update being presented today alongside results.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives full-year period 'FY2026 (year ended 31 March 2026)'

body:
• Rafael "Rafa" Carrasco appointed CEO, North America, effective 3 August 2026 — fills the key role following an extended interim period.
  • Joins from WM (formerly Waste Management), most recently President of WM Healthcare Solutions and SVP Strategy; 10-year WM tenure across route-based, recurring-revenue operations (200+ branches, 10,000+ employees across US, Canada, UK, Ireland and Western Europe).
  • Notably led integration of the $7.2bn Stericycle acquisition at WM, delivering "standout first year cost synergies" — directly relevant given Rentokil's ongoing Terminix integration challenges in North America.
  • Background emphasises operational excellence, frontline turnover reduction, and use of data/technology to drive efficiency — areas Rentokil has flagged as priorities in NA turnaround.
  • Interim CEO NA Alain Moffroid stays on the NA leadership group to support transition through end of 2027, ensuring continuity.
  • CEO Mike Duffy: "He has an impressive track record in field-based, recurring service operations, making him the ideal leader to drive our North American business forward."
  • Carrasco: "My focus will be on driving operational excellence, putting our people first, and building a truly customer-obsessed organisation."
  • No change to financial guidance or trading commentary in the release.
type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'ceo' with change verb 'appointed' in opening bullets

body:
• EPRA EPS up 63.5% to 8.5p; ahead of consensus (compiled 7.9p; Visible Alpha 7.9p).
  • EPRA NTA/share 524p, up 6.1% (vs consensus 522-523p); IFRS profit after tax £154.5m (vs £116.0m).
  • Portfolio valuation £3.0bn, up 4.3% LFL; offices +5.4%, developments +22.2%, retail -2.1%; yield expansion of 11bps to true equivalent 5.6%.
  • Rental values +5.8% LFL (offices +6.3%, prime offices +7.2%, retail +1.6%).
  • FY27 rental growth guidance: 4.0%-7.0%; prime offices 4.0%-8.0% (matches FY26 guidance range delivered).
  • Total dividend raised to 8.2p (final 5.3p, payable 10 July 2026); ROE 7.9%, progressing toward >10% FY27 target.
  • Record leasing year: 88 new leases/renewals at £70.9m p.a., 10.3% above March 2025 ERV; rent roll up 46% LFL to £153.6m, with further organic growth potential of 95%.
  • Rent reviews settled at +30.1% over previous rent and 9.0% ahead of ERV (incl. KKR and Glencore at Hanover Square).
  • Vacancy rate 6.0% (2025: 5.9%); customer retention 76%; AI-led businesses now c.11% of portfolio (27% of Fully Managed).
  • Capital recycling: £490m of disposals at 2.3% premium to book (£1,251 psf), incl. 1 Newman Street (£250m, largest West End deal of 2025), wells&more (£172m), Challenger House (£42m), 103/113 Regent St JV share (£26m).
  • Acquisitions: £69m at just £592 psf (The Gable £18m at 6.4% NIY; 10 South Crescent £51m at 6.8% NIY) adding to Fitzrovia cluster.
  • £200m of further sales under consideration; potential £1.0bn+ over medium term.
  • 2 Aldermanbury Square completed on time/budget, 100% pre-let to Clifford Chance.
  • Three HQ schemes on-site c.50% pre-let (CD&R at 30 Duke Street at 6.5% above ERV with projected 37.1% profit on cost; Quantexa 52,300 sq ft at The Delft).
  • The Delft costs increased by £14m due to supply chain insolvencies; profit on cost now 20.7% (vs prior expectations), IRR 12.0%, yield 7.6%.
  • Three Fully Managed deliveries in year (c.77,000 sq ft); 141 Wardour Street fully let within two months at £279 psf; 170 Piccadilly 73% let/under offer at £294 psf (highest in portfolio).
  • £116m committed capex remaining on three on-site HQ schemes; £223m capex to come across five on-site schemes; total programme expected surplus £131m at current rents/yields, rising to £260m on 10% rental growth.
  • Three pipeline HQ schemes incl. planning secured at St Thomas Yard SE1; total combined committed+pipeline capex £484m for 601,300 sq ft.
  • Fully Managed target of 1m sq ft (currently 654,000 sq ft committed, c.31% of offices).
  • New £525m five-year ESG-linked RCF signed October 2025 at 105bps over SONIA (replacing £450m facility + repaying £75m term loan at 175bps); Moody's Baa2 confirmed.
  • EPRA LTV 28.6% (vs 30.8%); cash & undrawn facilities £411.9m; WAULT debt maturity 5.4 years; weighted avg cost of debt 5.0% (down from 5.2%).
  • Property values would need to fall 45% before covenant breach.
  • 65% of drawn debt at fixed/hedged rates (down from 85% after £200m cap expired Oct 2025).
  • Revenue £117.9m (vs £94.2m); Fully Managed revenue up 128% to £42.8m; Fully Managed NOI £18.0m (+125%).
  • Cost of sales rose to £49.3m (from £35.1m), driven by Fully Managed service expense scaling.
  • Admin costs £44.2m (+£4.2m), incl. £1.6m new finance/property system go-live late 2026 and external whistleblower investigation costs.
  • Gross finance costs £48.6m (+£9.0m); capitalised interest £37.7m (+£11.2m).
  • Three customers entered administration (1.1% of rent roll); 99.3% of rents collected within 7 days.
  • CBRE rotated off as valuer; Knight Frank appointed for first valuation at 30 September 2026 (RICS mandatory rotation).
  • Two internal promotions to ExCo focused on Flex and customer experience.
  • CEO Toby Courtauld: "I am pleased to report on a year of numerous operational successes... EPS was up by 63% and net assets grew by 6.1%, with development values up 22%."
  • CEO outlook: "Whilst the external environment remains volatile, we are well positioned to build on this momentum; demand for our premium HQ and Flex spaces is strong and our pipeline is long, concentrated in the most sought-after, core locations."
  • Market context: only 2.9m sq ft of speculative supply expected annually over next four years vs ~4.5m sq ft take-up.

body:
• Reported revenue £19.7bn, down 3%; adjusted revenue £19.6bn, down 4%, hit by International divestments, handset declines and lower adjusted UK service revenue.
  • Adjusted UK service revenue £15.4bn, down 1% (voice volume drag offset by CPI-linked price rises and FTTP mix).
  • Adjusted EBITDA £8.23bn, flat YoY; like-for-like (ex-divestments) up 1%; landed at top of £8.2-8.3bn guidance.
  • Reported PBT £1.44bn, up 8%; adjusted basic EPS 18.3p, down 3%.
  • Normalised free cash flow £1.5bn, down 6% (higher capex/interest, absence of prior-year tax refund); in line with c.£1.5bn guide.
  • Capex £5.13bn, up 6% (FTTP build/provisioning), slightly above c.£5.0bn guide.
  • Net debt £20.0bn vs £19.8bn; IAS 19 pension deficit widened to £4.2bn from £4.1bn.
  • Final dividend 5.87p (+2%); full-year DPS 8.32p (+2%).
  • New dividend policy: low-to-mid single digit annual growth in FY27+ until BBB+ credit metrics reached, after which residual cash flow available for enhanced shareholder distributions (i.e. potential buybacks signalled).
  • FY27 guidance: adjusted revenue £19.0-19.5bn; UK service revenue £15.1-15.4bn (includes c.£150-200m voice/PSTN headwind); adjusted EBITDA growth to £8.2-8.3bn; capex ex-spectrum c.£4.3bn (down ~£0.8bn); NFCF c.£2.0bn.
  • Mid-term: capex ex-spectrum to fall by >£1bn from FY26 level by end of decade; NFCF reiterated at c.£3.0bn by FY30.
  • Transformation target raised to £3.7bn from £3.0bn, extended one year to FY30; cost to achieve rises to £1.4bn from £1.0bn.
  • £580m gross annualised savings in FY26 at £336m cost; £1.5bn total savings over two years at £0.8bn cost; headcount down 7% to 108k; network energy use -6%; Openreach repair volumes -18%.
  • Openreach FTTP build: record 4.8m premises added; footprint 23m (>2/3 of UK); on track for 25m by December 2026.
  • Openreach FTTP net adds 2.2m; total connected 8.8m; take-up rate >38%; broadband ARPU +4% to £16.7.
  • Openreach broadband line losses 825k for FY (Q4: 203k), better than c.850k guide; FY27 losses guided to c.800k.
  • EE 5G+ population coverage 73%, up from 43%; retail FTTP base +31% YoY to 4.5m; 5G base +10% to 14.5m.
  • Consumer returned to customer growth: +26k broadband, +104k postpaid mobile, +72k TV; but broadband ARPU -1% to £41.7 and mobile ARPU -1% to £19.3 in competitive market; convergence up to 26.6% (from 24.6%).
  • Business: new wins with BAE Systems, NIE Networks, easyJet; sovereign AI data centre partnership with Nscale.
  • International: five non-core divestments completed; revenue -15%, EBITDA -29%, FCF -£117m (vs -£34m).
  • Segment EBITDA: Openreach £4.23bn (+5%), Consumer £2.60bn (-2%), Business £1.27bn (-5%), International £145m (-29%).
  • Group NPS at record 33.4, +4.1pts YoY.
  • BT brand reintroduced with new Consumer and Business products in May 2026.
  • CEO Allison Kirkby: "We have delivered on our financial guidance and we are transforming ahead of plan, offsetting headwinds while successfully competing."
  • CEO on cash inflection: "reiterating our guidance of sustained growth, including cash flow inflection to c. £2.0bn in FY27 and to c. £3.0bn by the end of the decade."

body:
• Trading slightly ahead of expectations; FY26 Group operating profit now expected at top end of analyst range (consensus £158.6m; range £157.6m–£160.5m).
  • Group revenue +5% organic constant currency, driven by double-digit organic growth in US businesses.
  • Group operating margin similar to prior year period.
  • US Engineered Solutions: strong growth across larger platform businesses; margin similar to prior year.
  • Galvanizing Services: strong performance from significant increase in US volumes plus solid UK volume growth; margin slightly ahead YoY.
  • UK & India Engineered Solutions: subdued UK demand continues; prior year benefited from non-repeating transport infrastructure projects; operating margin declined.
  • UK restructuring actions now being implemented; further detail at interim results.
  • Disposed of Hill & Smith VRS Limited (permanent steel road barrier business) to ThreeSixty Investco for £5.25m; VRS had 2025 revenue of £25m at breakeven operating profit.
  • Recent acquisitions Freeberg and Hentech performing in line with expectations; onboarding progressing well.
  • M&A pipeline aligned to priority end-markets being actively progressed.
  • Balance sheet strong with significant headroom for organic and inorganic growth.
  • Alan Giddins steps down as Chair at conclusion of today's AGM, succeeded by Nick Anderson (joined Board 11 March 2026).
  • Group to report in US dollars from interim results 2026; historic data to be restated ahead of transition (consensus translates to ~$209.4m at $1.32).
  • Interim results scheduled for 12 August 2026.

body:
• FY2026 financial outturn expected at the top of market expectations, with consensus range for adjusted PBT of £154.5–159.3m.
  • Performance supported by VoH growth in both Spain and UK&I, plus good progress in Claims & Services.
  • Average VoH up 4.9% for the year.
  • Steady State Cash generation has inflected in FY2026, having passed the inflexion point as fleet replacement investment normalises; upward trajectory expected over coming years.
  • Leverage steady at 1.9x (H1 2026: 1.9x), in line with guidance.
  • UK&I simplification programme progressing with early supply chain wins and launch of new Northgate Mobility brand.
  • Added capacity across fleet, infrastructure and technical capabilities.
  • Secured a number of contract wins and renewals.
  • CEO Martin Ward: "Customers are clearly seeing significant value in our service-led offering... Steady State Cash generation has stepped up materially and supports our ambition for further growth".
  • FY2026 results to be released on 8 July 2026.

body:
• Loan book up 1% in the quarter to £9.3bn (Jan 2026: £9.2bn); +2% on underlying basis excluding Premium Finance personal lines run-off and legacy ROI Motor Finance.
  • Growth in Motor Finance and Invoice Finance seasonality reversal offset weaker Property and planned Premium Finance personal lines reduction.
  • Annualised YTD NIM 7.0% (H1 2026: 7.1%); full-year NIM still expected slightly below 7% due to loan book mix.
  • Cost savings to exceed c.£25m annualised target by FY26 end as actions are accelerated.
  • Adjusted operating expenses now expected below prior c.£450m guidance for FY26.
  • Group central functions operating loss now expected at lower end of c.£45–50m guidance range.
  • YTD bad debt ratio 0.8% (unchanged vs H1); FY26 expected to remain below long-term average of 1.2%.
  • Motor finance provision raised to £320m following FCA Policy Statement PS26/3 (30 March 2026), with an additional £30m income statement charge in the quarter (prior provision £294m).
  • Provision assumes delayed scheme implementation due to legal challenges; ultimate cost remains uncertain.
  • CET1 ratio 14.3% (flat vs Jan 2026) after absorbing the additional motor finance provision; total capital ratio 19.5% (vs 18.8%), boosted by 6.125% Subordinated Tier 2 Notes due 2036 issued at start of quarter.
  • On track to deliver FY26 in line with guidance, subject to macro developments.
  • CEO Mike Morgan: "We have delivered a solid performance in the third quarter and continue to execute our strategy through this important transitional year... Our capital position remains strong after absorbing the additional provision for motor finance commissions, enabling investment in future growth to further support the UK economy."
  • On the FCA scheme: "While there are elements of the scheme that we disagree with, we continue to believe that the existing scheme offers a quick, clear and certain route to resolving this matter for all relevant parties."

body:
• Like-for-like sales +3.3% in H1, with strong Q1 (+4.5%) decelerating to Q2 (+1.8%); food LFL +4.1%, drink LFL +2.4%.
  • Most recent 3 weeks LFL slowed sharply to +1.1%, attributed to tough weather comps, macro pressure on discretionary spend and tube strike disruption.
  • LFL +3.0% in the 30 weeks to 25 April (Easter in both years).
  • Festive period exceptionally strong: LFL +7.7% over core 3-week festive window, +10.5% across the five key festive days.
  • Total revenue £1,490m, +2.5% YoY (HY25 £1,454m); food £809m, drink £630m, services £51m.
  • Adjusted operating profit flat at £181m despite cost inflation; statutory operating profit £185m (HY25 £181m).
  • Adjusted operating margin 12.1%, down 0.3ppts YoY.
  • PBT £143m (HY25 £134m, +6.7%); basic EPS 17.9p (HY25 16.8p); adjusted EPS 17.4p, +3.6%.
  • Continued outperformance vs CGA Business Tracker; sector saw ~20 net closures/week in the 6 months to April 2026 (food-led particularly hit).
  • FY26 cost headwinds guided to c.£120m before mitigation (c.5.5% of cost base), slightly below prior guidance; ~60% H1-weighted due to April 2025 NIC rate annualising (£11m impact).
  • Energy fully hedged for FY26; 15% of FY27 energy already secured.
  • FY27 cost headwinds expected to normalise to c.£95m (c.4% of cost base) — material step-down signal.
  • Net debt excluding leases reduced to £747m (HY25 £860m); including £405m IFRS 16 leases, total net debt £1,152m.
  • Leverage 1.6x EBITDA ex-leases / 2.5x including leases (vs 2.8x prior year).
  • Cash inflow before bond amortisation £98m (HY25 £131m), lower due to higher capex and £11m Pesto contingent consideration payment.
  • FY26 total capex guided up to c.£230m (H1: £117m vs £92m) on accelerated remodels and acquisitions; remodel returns running >30%.
  • 122 investment projects completed in H1 (113 remodels, 4 conversions, 5 acquisitions); targeting return to 7-year refurbishment cycle.
  • 5 sites acquired for £12m (1 freehold conversion of existing leasehold, 4 new sites including 1 in Germany, 2 with hotel rooms); 2 further freeholds acquired post period-end.
  • Guest review scores at record 4.7/5; staff turnover at record lows.
  • Estate: 1,712 sites total, 1,638 directly managed; 83% freehold/long leasehold.
  • Sustainability progress: 274 sites with solar panels, 100 kitchens electrified, gas fully removed at 29 sites, recycling >60% YTD.
  • Disposed of 25% stake in Fatboy Pub Company on 4 December 2025 for £314,000.
  • No dividend mentioned; £3m own shares purchased in period.
  • Phil Urban, CEO: "Despite the backdrop of macro uncertainty our priorities remain unchanged, our guest scores are at record highs, we remain committed to the delivery of quality experiences, and we are well placed to further grow market share."
  • CEO on profit resilience: "Maintaining profits despite the significant inflationary cost challenges facing the sector is testament to the dedication of our teams in delivering the benefits of our Ignite and capital programmes."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives half-year period '28 weeks ended 11 April 2026 (H1 FY26)'

body:
• Merged Western Desert PSC ratified by Egyptian House of Representatives end-Q1 2026, effective 19 May 2026, consolidating eight concessions into one.
  • Merged concession expected to increase netback by ~$5/bbl at $80/bbl oil, unlocking additional reserves and improved fiscal terms.
  • YTD production tracking within 2026 guidance of 18,000–22,000 boepd.
  • Planned BED facility shutdown completed safely and on time in February.
  • Q3 2026 drilling: two wells on newly secured acreage to test whether ARG reservoir trends extend, potentially unlocking new development locations.
  • 2026 capex guidance: $85–95m; opex guidance: $5–7/boe.
  • Now debt free following repayment of remaining debt in April 2026.
  • Group cash balance of $132m as of 30 April 2026.
  • M&A focus: evaluating value-accretive opportunities in Egypt and UK North Sea.
  • Half year results scheduled for 24 September 2026.
  • CEO Randy Neely: "Financial discipline remains central to our cash flow-focused strategy. This discipline supports ongoing investment and positions the business to capitalise on the opportunities created by the merged concession."
type_workings: Rule 'trading update': 'trading update' named in period line

body:
• Preliminary results released; full statement published via RNS PDF link and on the Tate & Lyle website.
  • Revenue from continuing operations totalled £2.0 billion for FY26.
  • Final dividend recommended at 13.2p per share (2025: 13.4p), down ~1.5% YoY.
  • Full year dividend held flat at 19.8p (2025: 19.8p), in line with prior year.
  • Final dividend payable 31 July 2026 to shareholders on register as of 19 June 2026.
  • Results presentation hosted by CEO Nick Hampton and CFO Sarah Kuijlaars at 09:30 BST on 21 May 2026.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Year ended 31 March 2026'

body:
• Full year outturn expected broadly in line with current market consensus expectations, despite challenging start to the year.
  • Group revenues down ~10% on a like-for-like basis for the first 4 months of 2026.
  • UK domestic brick market volumes down 11% in Q1; Group maintained brick market share in line with 2025.
  • Sequential improvement noted: high single-digit YoY increase in brick volumes in April, with positive trading continuing in May.
  • Concrete products weak, linked to private residential and RMI markets; slower start due to infrastructure project spending cycles, but improvement expected in coming months.
  • Annual price increases implemented at the beginning of February.
  • Higher energy and fuel prices driving increased cost inflation, likely to extend into H2 2026, partly attributed to Middle East conflict uncertainty.
  • Energy hedging: >85% covered for Q1–Q3 2026; ~80% cover for full year.
  • Board mindful of Middle East conflict's potential impact on consumer confidence and UK economy; sees potential for some improvement in H2.
  • Strategic projects progressing: working with preferred partner on commercial agreement for calcined clay; Nostell Horizon factory commissioning as planned with orderbooks building.
  • CEO Joe Hudson: "Following challenging market conditions in the early months of 2026, including a very wet start to the year, we have seen a promising improvement in sales volumes in our clay business, with the Group successfully maintaining market share."
  • CEO Joe Hudson: "Whilst we are mindful of the potential impact of the Middle East conflict, the Board believes recent activity levels support the Group's capability to deliver a full year outturn broadly in line with current market consensus expectations. Further, with a well invested, lower-cost, more efficient and sustainable manufacturing network, Ibstock is well positioned to deliver growth in the medium term."
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Revenue up 19% to £183.0m (HY25: £153.2m), driven by recurring ad valorem (+21% to £135.6m) and transactional fees (+37% to £34.4m on higher FX from overseas dealing); recurring fixed fell 21% to £13.0m post-Platinum disposal.
  • Underlying PBT up 15% to £79.0m; statutory PBT up 35% to £92.8m including £13.8m net exceptional gain (£21.4m profit on Platinum disposal less £7.6m Touch impairment).
  • Revenue margin up to 33.4bps (HY25: 32.4bps); underlying PBT margin down 1.7ppts to 43.2% reflecting accelerated brand/marketing investment.
  • Underlying diluted EPS up 18% to 14.61p; statutory diluted EPS up 39% to 17.13p.
  • Guidance upgrade: full-year revenue margin, PBT and PBT margin now expected higher than previously guided (December guidance), with management choosing to invest more than originally planned in H2 while still delivering materially higher profitability.
  • Interim dividend 5.00p, +11% (HY25: 4.50p).
  • Additional £15m buyback announced, on top of the ongoing £50m programme launched December 2025; £77.3m returned to shareholders over the past 12 months.
  • Platform customers +79,000 (record) to 723,000 (+12%); D2C added a record 72,000, including 23,000 new funded D2C customers in March alone post-brand campaign launch.
  • Platform AUA +5% to £108.7bn; record net inflows £4.2bn (HY25: £3.3bn) with D2C net inflows +40% to £3.5bn; advised net inflows £0.7bn (vs £0.8bn) hit by pre-Budget outflows and adviser consolidation.
  • AJ Bell Investments AUM +10% to £9.8bn; net inflows £0.6bn; low-cost solutions now >7% of platform AUA.
  • Customer retention 94.6% (FY25: 94.1%); Trustpilot 4.9 stars; 7.5m trades processed; operational/support FTEs down 2% YoY vs 22% customer growth — operating leverage supporting pricing.
  • Admin expenses +21% to £106.2m: distribution +41% (brand campaign), technology +22%, operational/support +12%; 12ppts of growth from business investment.
  • Platinum SIPP/SSAS sale completed November 2025 for up to £25m (£17.5m cash, £1m InvestAcc shares, up to £6.5m deferred); £3.3bn non-platform AUA transferred; £21.4m disposal profit.
  • Remaining third-party SIPP arrangement to be exited in H2, simplifying to a core platform-only model.
  • AJ Bell Touch discontinued as standalone platform; capabilities folded into Investcentre — drove £7.6m full impairment of Touch intangibles.
  • Cash £167.6m (FY25: £188.2m), reduction driven by buybacks; cash from operations £64.9m; net assets £211.9m; return on assets 32.6%.
  • Strong momentum continuing into H2 with strong platform inflows in April.
  • AI strategy: internal GenAI platform deployed; targeting operational gearing, product development and distribution; long-term partnerships with both core back-office software providers extended.
  • Management flags >£1bn of excess pension withdrawals ahead of the 2024 and 2025 fiscal events due to speculation around pension tax changes; continues to lobby via "Pension Tax Lock" campaign.
  • Summersgill on policy backdrop: "in both pension and ISA markets we continue to see complexity and uncertainty" — opposes FCA CP25/39 friction proposals and the Cash ISA allowance cut to £12,000 for under-65s from April 2027.
  • Addressable off-platform market cited at £2.4 trillion (with total addressable market £3.7tn, ~two-thirds still off-platform).
  • Board change: Evelyn Bourke stepped down 4 February 2026; Elizabeth Chambers appointed NED 1 May 2026.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Six months ended 31 March 2026'

body:
• Headline LBT widened to £(552)m vs £(394)m in H1 FY25; reported LBT £(552)m vs £(401)m — in line with 16 April trading update.
  • Result included £25m unexpected additional fuel costs in March (unhedged spot purchases due to Middle East conflict) and £32m net increase in legal provisions across historic cases.
  • Group revenue +12% to £3,954m; passenger revenue +10% to £2,361m; ancillary +10% to £1,075m; holidays revenue +30% to £518m.
  • Passengers +6% YoY; load factor 90% (+2ppt); ASK +8%, seats +4%; sector length +4%.
  • RASK +1% YoY (Q2 +3% aided by route maturity, FX, earlier Easter); revenue per seat +5% to £73.36.
  • Total headline CASK +5% YoY; CASK ex-fuel +8% (inflation, resilience measures annualisation, digital investment, wet-lease for Italian bases, legal provisions); fuel CASK -5% on favourable hedging (avg jet fuel $726/MT, -8% YoY).
  • easyJet holidays headline PBT £61m (+39%); EBIT £48m (+50%); customers +22% to 1.3m; ASP +8% to £623.
  • Balance sheet: net cash £434m (vs £327m); liquidity £4.7bn; owned assets £5.0bn; Baa2/BBB+ stable; no bond maturities until FY28; book value of owned assets targeted to exceed £7.5bn by FY28.
  • H1 FY25 dividend of 13.2p/share (£100m) paid in March 2026.
  • H2 FY26 trading impacted by Middle East conflict — booking curve shortened, forward visibility reduced.
  • H2 sold 58% (-2ppt YoY); Q3 79% sold (-1ppt, improved +1ppt since April update); Q4 40% sold (-3ppt) with ticket yield modestly up; booked RASK to date -4% YoY (also Easter-timing impacted).
  • Intend to operate the full summer schedule on sale following March net seat reduction of just 0.3%; capacity redeployed from Middle East-adjacent markets to domestic/city routes.
  • H2 FY26 CASK ex-fuel guided to low single-digit increase; fuel 72% hedged at $726/MT vs spot ~$1,350/MT; every $100/MT move = c.£35m fuel cost.
  • Short-term additional fuel hedging temporarily suspended given elevated near-term prices; minimum ticket fares raised; discretionary cost under review.
  • FY26 seat capacity growth 3% (H1 4%, H2 2%); ASK +6% (H1 8%, H2 5%); H1 FY27 capacity growth to moderate after 33% H1 ASK growth over three years.
  • All A319s to be retired by FY29 (accelerated), delivering c.£250m incremental annual cost efficiencies across FY27 & FY28.
  • Fleet 356 aircraft; 86% of A320neo family owned; order book 287 aircraft + 100 purchase rights; gross capex guidance: c.£1.7bn FY26, c.£2.3bn FY27, c.£3.3bn FY28.
  • New bases opened in Marrakech (first outside Europe) and Newcastle; Milan Linate/Rome Fiumicino £30m investment cost in line with plan.
  • Holidays initiatives: flight-plus-hotel launch in late FY26 (hotel inventory rising c.8,000 → c.13,000); distribution via ~500 high-street agents in Berlin; loyalty programme launch FY27.
  • Holidays customers expected to grow low double digits in FY26 from 3.1m base; H2 FY26 76% sold.
  • Targeting £2.5m PBT per aircraft hurdle; incremental FY26 aircraft deployed to bases that exceeded this in FY25.
  • Medium-term target reiterated: >£1bn PBT as conditions normalise.
  • On-time performance 78% (+1ppt); Airline CSAT 84% (+2ppt); Holidays CSAT 85% (+1ppt); 71% of bookings from returning customers; app now 38% of direct airline bookings.
  • Contingent liability: Spanish €29m hand-luggage fine appealed (bank guarantee posted); easyJet maintains policy is lawful; EC has opened infringement procedure against Spain.
  • CEO Kenton Jarvis: "easyJet is well placed to manage the current environment, supported by one of the strongest investment‑grade balance sheets in European aviation."
  • CEO on strategy: "through disciplined growth, accelerated upgauging, and continued expansion of easyJet holidays, we aim to bounce back from this year's Middle East related setbacks."
  • CEO on demand: "easyJet is not seeing any disruption to fuel supply, we continue to operate normally and our customers should book with confidence."
type_workings: Rule 'trading update': 'trading update' named in opening bullets

• Routine Rule 19.6(c) Takeover Code compliance notice issued 12 months after the Highcroft Investments offer period ended.
  • Confirms to the Takeover Panel in writing that LondonMetric has complied with all post-offer statements of intent made under Rules 2.7(c)(viii) and 24.2, as set out in the 27 March 2025 announcement and 24 April 2025 scheme document.
  • Relates to the recommended all-share offer for Highcroft Investments PLC, implemented via scheme of arrangement, which became effective on 21 May 2025.
  • No new financial, operational or strategic information disclosed.

body:
• AUM of $126bn; fee-earning AUM of $87bn, up 11% y-o-y (constant currency), five-year annualised growth of 14%.
  • Fundraising of $17bn, exceeding expectations; $40bn raised since 1 April 2024 vs four-year target of $55bn by 31 March 2028 — on track to deliver target potentially a year early.
  • Management fees £685m, +13% y-o-y (+17% ex catch-up fees of £51.4m).
  • FRE of £350m / 120p per share, up 23% y-o-y (34% ex catch-up); five-year annualised growth of 30%.
  • FRE margin 51.0% (FY25: 47.0%); ex catch-up fees 47.1% (FY25: 40.9%).
  • Performance fee income £127m (FY25: £86m), including £72m one-off transition gain from the October 2025 change in estimate (removal of 24-month forward-looking constraint, recognition now starts when successor fund has first close).
  • Group operating cashflow £861m (FY25: £533m), +62%.
  • Net debt collapsed to £113m (FY25: £629m, -82%); net debt/FRE 0.3x.
  • Total Available Liquidity £1,461m (FY25: £1,098m); £911m available cash + £550m undrawn RCF.
  • Balance sheet portfolio £2,568m (FY25: £2,901m, -11%); net realisations were 18% of opening co-investment value vs 5-year average of 10%.
  • Net Balance Sheet Return £149m / +5% (FY25: £231m / +8%); Debt segment dragged at -2% on CLO MTM moves.
  • Total FY26 dividend 87p (FY25: 83p), +5% — 16th consecutive annual increase; final 59.3p proposed.
  • Fitch upgraded ICG during the year; ratings now BBB+ stable from both S&P and Fitch.
  • Guidance refresh: FMC margin guidance replaced with FRE margin accretion (ex catch-up fees); NIR guidance removed; fundraising ($55bn cumulative FY25-FY28) and performance fees (10-20% of total fee income) targets unchanged.
  • FY27 fundraising expected to be below FY26 given fundraising cycle; SDP 6, Asia Corporate V and Strategic Equity VI expected to launch towards end of FY27.
  • Europe IX on track to surpass €10bn target — ICG's largest-ever commingled fund and largest European structured capital fund globally; final close expected during 2026.
  • Six funds closed at or above target in last 24 months (Infrastructure Europe II final close exceeded extended hard cap; Metropolitan II also closed above target — record year for Real Estate fundraising).
  • Scaling strategies now 19% of management fees vs 13% in FY21.
  • Real Assets management fees £122m, +58% y-o-y (catch-ups £43m vs £9m); Structured Capital & Secondaries fees £405m, +11%; Debt fees £159m, -2% (timing of realisations/deployment).
  • Balance sheet effective tax rate rose to 18.5% (FY25: 14.9%).
  • 870+ institutional clients (+11% y-o-y), including 6 of top 10 US pension funds and 7 of top 10 sovereign wealth funds; wealth channel only 4% of AUM.
  • Amundi strategic partnership progressing — 2.78m shares repurchased into treasury pending issuance of non-voting shares; cumulatively cash-neutral.
  • CEO Benoît Durteste headline: "FY26 was a strong year for ICG... ICG is well positioned to continue generating compounding long-term shareholder value."
  • On industry stress points: "we do not have evergreen funds" in private debt; software exposure ~10% of group portfolio, "only in highly cash generative businesses" — unaffected by US evergreen vehicle challenges.
  • On investment discipline: "pockets of equity valuations have more downside than upside risk, and credit terms remain very borrower friendly in most cases."
  • Headcount down 1% y-o-y; FRE operating expenses +5% to £335m.
  • Dry powder of $36.1bn, of which $18.7bn not yet earning fees.

body:
• AUM of $126bn; fee-earning AUM of $87bn, up 11% y-o-y (constant currency); five-year annualised FEAUM growth of 14%.
  • Fundraising of $17bn, exceeding expectations — now $40bn raised vs. four-year $55bn target (Apr-24 to Mar-28), potentially achievable a year early.
  • Six funds closed at/above target in the last 24 months; Europe IX expected to be ICG's first commingled fund to reach €10bn and the largest European structured capital fund ever raised globally.
  • Management fees £685m, +13% y-o-y (+17% ex catch-up fees); catch-up fees £51.4m (FY25: £61.8m).
  • FRE £350m (120p/share), +23% y-o-y (+34% ex catch-up fees); five-year CAGR 30%.
  • FRE margin 51.0% (FY25: 47.0%); ex catch-up fees 47.1% (FY25: 40.9%).
  • Performance fee income £127m (FY25: £86m, +47%), including £72m one-off transition gain from October 2025 change in estimate (recognition now starts earlier in fund life).
  • Net Balance Sheet Return £149m / +5% (FY25: £231m / +8%); Debt sleeve negative at -2% due to CLO mark-to-market.
  • Group operating cash flow £861m (FY25: £533m, +62%) — aided by £533m net cash from balance sheet portfolio (large Europe VI/VII exits) and £96m performance fees received.
  • Net debt collapsed to £113m (FY25: £629m, -82%); net debt/FRE 0.3x; Total Available Liquidity £1,461m (FY25: £1,098m).
  • Balance sheet portfolio £2,568m (FY25: £2,901m, -11%); net realisations 18% of opening value vs. 5-yr avg of 10%.
  • Fitch upgraded ICG during the year; S&P and Fitch both BBB+ stable.
  • Total dividend 87p (FY25: 83p), +5%; 16th consecutive annual increase; final 59.3p.
  • Financial presentation evolved to align with global alt-asset peers: introduces FRE as headline profitability metric; FMC margin guidance replaced with FRE margin guidance; NIR guidance removed.
  • Updated medium-term guidance: FRE margin accretion ex catch-up fees (FY26: +14% over 5 years); performance fees c.10-20% of total fee income (10% 5-yr avg ex transition gain); $55bn aggregate fundraising target reaffirmed.
  • FY27 fundraising expected to be below FY26 given fundraising cycle; SDP 6, Asia Corporate V and Strategic Equity VI launches anticipated towards end of FY27.
  • Scaling strategies now 19% of management fees (FY21: 13%); Infrastructure Europe II final close exceeded extended hard cap; Metropolitan II also closed above target — "best year on record for Real Estate fundraising."
  • Structured Capital & Secondaries: management fees £405m (+11%); fundraising fell to $7.0bn (-47%) as Europe IX raise progresses; deployment $6.2bn (-47%).
  • Real Assets: management fees £122m (+58%) driven by European Infra II catch-up fees of £32m and Metropolitan II £11m.
  • Debt: management fees £159m (-2%) despite FEAUM growth — timing of realisations/deployments; performance fees £23m vs £2m driven by SDP/NACP transition.
  • Wealth channel still only 4% of third-party AUM; Amundi strategic partnership signed (Nov-25) plus relationships with global private banks positioned as long-term upside.
  • Amundi-related share buyback ongoing: 2,785,365 shares repurchased into treasury at FY26 (£44m); 4,060,926 further shares bought 1 Apr-19 May 2026; 2,270,525 non-voting shares issued at 1,589.24p on 20 April 2026; transaction cash-neutral over time.
  • Headcount down 1% y-o-y; FRE operating expenses £335m (+5%).
  • Effective tax rate 18.5% (FY25: 14.9%).
  • Weighted-average cost of drawn debt 2.71% (FY25: 2.84%); £550m RCF undrawn; all drawn debt fixed rate.
  • CEO Benoît Durteste: "FY26 was a strong year for ICG. We reinforced our scaled competitive position, established a strategic relationship with Amundi, and built on our track record of strategic and financial resilience."
  • On industry stress: "two of the challenges facing the industry today - liquidity strains within evergreen structures and exposure to businesses at direct risk of AI disruption - have limited direct impact on ICG. Our software exposure across the Group portfolio is approximately 10%, and even then only in highly cash generative businesses; while in private debt specifically, we do not have evergreen funds."
  • On capital allocation: progressive dividend, target zero net debt, then organic growth, M&A/partnerships, or shareholder returns — focus on growing FRE per share.
  • Industry context: total private markets AUM raised globally down 21% vs 2021 with number of funds halved; ICG closed 6 funds at/above target in last 24 months, gaining share.
  • Co-investment portfolio expected to generate "significant net cashflow" in coming years as older vintages realise and lower co-invest commitments feed deployment — asset-light trajectory.
  • 870+ institutional clients (+11% y-o-y); includes 6 of top-10 US pension funds and 7 of top-10 SWFs.

body:
• Net rental income +16.6% to £455.3m, boosted by 9 months of Urban Logistics REIT (ULR) takeover contribution.
  • EPRA earnings +13.9% to £305.3m; EPRA EPS +2.4% to 13.5p (+24% over two years).
  • IFRS reported profit £295.7m (FY25: £347.9m, -15.0%), impacted by £42.8m of M&A/debt repayment costs.
  • Dividend +3.8% to 12.45p, 11th consecutive year of growth, 108% covered by EPRA earnings; Q4 dividend of 3.3p declared.
  • Guidance: FY27 Q1 dividend +3.3% to 3.15p; expects further £38m pa of rental uplift over next two years, plus £11m potential from letting vacancies.
  • EPRA NTA per share +0.7% to 200.6p; total accounting return 6.9% (7.7% ex-M&A costs).
  • Sector-leading EPRA cost ratio down 10bps to 7.7%.
  • Total property return 7.1%, outperforming MSCI All Property UK Index by 170bps.
  • Like-for-like contracted rental income growth of 4.2%; ERV growth 3.3%; valuation uplift of £68m with yields unchanged.
  • Portfolio value increased to £7.6bn (from £6.2bn); 680 assets, average value £11m.
  • Logistics weighting up to 53% (from 46%); urban logistics now 38% of portfolio (from 29%).
  • £1,549m acquired (80% logistics), primarily via ULR takeover (£1.1bn of warehousing added); £318m disposed across 57 assets (54% former M&A assets).
  • Post year-end: acquired four M&S-anchored convenience pre-let developments for £40m (6.1% yield on cost); sold £49m further assets.
  • Rent reviews +19% on 5-yearly equivalent basis; urban logistics open market reviews +38%.
  • Asset management added £17m pa of net contracted rent across 327 occupier initiatives.
  • WAULT 17 years (down from 18.5 due to ULR's shorter leases); occupancy 98%; gross-to-net income ratio 99%.
  • Top 3 occupiers reduced to 22% of rent (from 27%): Ramsay 9.1%, Merlin 7.8%, Travelodge 4.9%.
  • Contractual uplifts on 69% of rental income (down from 77%, reflecting greater open market exposure from ULR).
  • Major debt restructuring: £2.7bn of new/refinanced debt; £1.1bn repaid; included debut £500m public bond (A- rated by Fitch, 4.69% coupon, 5.5yr maturity) and £150m US private placement.
  • March 2026: refinanced £1.5bn of unsecured RCFs/term loans, reducing average margin by 49bps to 1.05%.
  • Cost of debt maintained at 4.0%; LTV 36.7% (up from 32.7%); 99.8% hedged; debt maturity 4.4 years; £515m undrawn facilities.
  • No material refinancing required until FY30.
  • Post year-end: completed £350m new interest rate swaps at 3.75% fixed.
  • Net debt/EBITDA at 7.5x (up from 6.4x).
  • M&A: completed ULR takeover (£726.8m consideration, June 2025) and Highcroft (£47.6m, May 2025); £16.3m acquisition costs and £9.6m goodwill impairment recognised.
  • Picton bid: jointly with SREIT, progressing non-binding indicative all-share offer for Picton (implied ~£403.4m); LondonMetric would acquire ~46% of GAV. No firm offer yet.
  • 92% of portfolio EPC A-C rated (A-B improved to 60% from 58%); solar capacity up to 11.9MWp (from 8.1MWp).
  • CEO Andrew Jones: "income compounding is one of the true wonders of investing - the essential ingredient and rocket fuel of long term wealth creation" and the group is "a step closer to dividend aristocracy."
  • CEO on outlook: "In today's environment, scale and efficiency are essential, and our relentless expansion has put us in a strong position with every reason to be optimistic. After all market uncertainty will undoubtedly throw up new opportunities."
  • Risk commentary flags increased investment, valuation and tenant risk due to elevated debt costs, constrained transaction liquidity and geopolitical uncertainty; capital/finance risk decreased following refinancings.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Full year ended 31 March 2026'

body:
• Patricia Cobian to join the Board as Group CFO Designate on 20 July 2026, as previously flagged on 24 July 2025.
  • Cobian will succeed Simon Lowth as CFO on 1 September 2026; Lowth remains at BT until 31 January 2027 to support handover.
  • No additional disclosures required under UK Listing Rule 6.4.8R.
  • Governance changes effective 1 June 2026, including Maggie Chan Jones joining the Remuneration Committee.
  • 2026 Annual Report to be published on 11 June 2026.

body:
• Signed three new production sharing contracts (PSCs) in Indonesia with SKK Migas at the Indonesian Petroleum Association Convention & Exhibition 2026, taking participation in Indonesian oil & gas blocks to 11.
  • Two PSCs cover the Bintuni and Drawa exploration blocks in Papua Barat, located near existing Tangguh LNG operations — proximity flagged as enabling shorter-cycle development tie-ins to existing infrastructure.
  • Third PSC is a 49% non-operated stake in the Barong block (East Java), with INPEX holding 51% as operator.
  • William Lin (EVP, gas & low carbon energy): "These agreements demonstrate our ongoing investment in Indonesia's energy security and economic growth. We already have world-class assets in the country, and, subject to success, the proximity of two of these new blocks to our existing infrastructure could support the potential future development and production of these resources."
  • Shares +0.3% at 565.20p at midday on the day of announcement.

body:
• Pretax profit +26% to USD1.95bn (FY25: USD1.55bn).
  • Benchmark EBIT +15% to USD2.41bn (+13% constant currency), marginally ahead of USD2.40bn consensus.
  • Benchmark EPS +15% to 179.8c (vs 156.9c; consensus 179.2c).
  • Revenue +12% to USD8.45bn (+11% cc), in line with consensus.
  • Organic growth 8%, top of guidance range, with Q4 accelerating to 9%.
  • Regional organic growth: North America +10%, Latin America +8%, EMEA & Asia Pacific +5%, UK & Ireland +2% (notable laggard).
  • Benchmark EBIT margin 28.6%, +50bps (+60bps cc), above company expectations.
  • Final dividend +11% to 48.00c; total FY dividend 69.25c, +11% YoY.
  • New USD1bn share buyback announced, following USD725m repurchased in FY26.
  • FY27 guidance: total revenue growth 8–11%, organic 6–8%, margin expansion at higher end of medium-term framework, another year of double-digit benchmark EPS growth.
  • CEO Brian Cassin: "FY26 was a record year for Experian, with performance at the upper end of our expectations and strong strategic momentum."
  • AI delivering 10–15% coding productivity uplift (>30% in certain areas), helping lower labour costs.
  • Management identifies >USD15bn of AI-enabled market opportunities, addressing investor concerns on AI disruption.
  • Shares fell 5.1% to 2,571.25p, worst performer on FTSE 100; stock down 34% over the last 12 months on AI disruption concerns.

body:
• Pretax profit £450m, +32% vs £342m prior year.
  • Revenue £523m, +15% vs £454m.
  • Net rental income £476m, +8.4% vs £439m.
  • Underlying profit £294m vs £279m, in line with revised guidance.
  • Underlying EPS 28.9p vs 28.5p — matched April-upgraded guidance (raised from 28.5p following £150m Life Science REIT acquisition).
  • EPRA NTA per share rose to 590p from 567p.
  • Final dividend 10.80p, +2.3%; total dividend 23.12p, +1.4%.
  • Total accounting return 8.1%, in line with April guidance.
  • LTV 39.2% vs 38.1% — modest gearing increase.
  • Portfolio occupancy 96.9%; retail parks 99% occupied.
  • FY27 EPS guidance: at least 30.5p, underpinned by LFL net rental growth at top end of 3–5% range.
  • Medium-term: 3–6% annual EPS growth; 3–5% annual ERV growth reiterated.
  • Share gain highlighted: ~5% of London office market but 15% of FY leasing activity, rising to 33% in Q4.
  • CEO Simon Carter: "A record year of leasing has driven strong ERV growth, like-for-like net rental growth and an attractive earnings outlook."
  • Carter on market: "Central London office net take-up is at its highest level in 20 years and our retail parks are 99% occupied."
  • Carter flagged: "the geopolitical and interest rate backdrop has become more uncertain", but occupational fundamentals "as strong as I have seen them."
  • Shares -0.2% at 377.36p on the day; -8.1% over the past year.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Full year ended 31 March 2026'

body:
• Full-year results due Thursday ~07:00 BST for year to 31 March 2026.
  • Consensus EPRA EPS 13.4p (FY25: 13.1p; FY24: 10.9p) — +2.3% YoY.
  • Consensus DPS 12.4p (FY25: 12.0p; FY24: 10.2p) — +3.3% YoY.
  • Consensus NAV/share 209p (FY25: 199p, +3.6%; FY24: 192p) — +5.0% YoY.
  • Shares -6.1% over past 12 months; trading 184.80p Wednesday.
  • Broker split: Goldman 'neutral' 190p; Morgan Stanley 'underweight' 205p (cut in March); Berenberg 'buy' 282p; BofA 'buy' 220p; Barclays 'overweight' 220p.
  • Investor focus: capital recycling, balance sheet discipline, rental growth, continued pivot to logistics and convenience-led assets.
  • Key event: all-share takeover of Picton Property Income announced last week alongside Schroder Real Estate (SREIT).
  • Terms: Picton holders receive 0.190 LMP + 0.881 SREIT shares, valuing Picton at ~£403m / 78.2p per share.
  • Premium of 7.0% to undisturbed price, but ~9% discount to EPRA NTA and 5.6% discount to Dec portfolio valuation.
  • Portfolio to be split 46:54 between LondonMetric and SREIT, with LMP taking the heavier logistics weighting.
  • Deutsche Bank's Max Nimmo: "Roll up...Roll up..." and the deal "is broadly in line with the undisturbed share price but still a -9% implied NTA discount, or -6% discount to Picton's December 2025 book values."
  • Shore Capital ('buy'): deal "looks no different to us" vs prior LMP M&A; expects "moderate earnings accretion" to FY28 forecast of 14.4p EPS.
  • Shore flags "some +50% portfolio rent upside available from vacancy, lease incentives and lease reversion" with LMP having "strong credentials in being able to actively deliver this."
  • Consortium says deal earnings accretive for both LMP and SREIT, and offers Picton holders a "very material, immediate increase in dividend income."
  • TR Property Investment Trust (~11.4% of Picton) has given a letter of intent in support.
  • Track record context: prior acquisitions include A&J Mucklow, CT Property Trust, LXI REIT, Highcroft and Urban Logistics — all positioned as earnings-enhancing and dividend-supportive.
type_workings: Rule 'results headline': opening bullets say 'full-year results'

body:
• Non-Executive Director Cheryl Potter stepping down from the Board effective 7 July 2026; will not seek re-election at the forthcoming AGM.
  • Potter joined the M&S Board on 1 March 2023, serving just over three years.
  • Chair Archie Norman: "On behalf of the Board, I would like to thank Cheryl for her significant contribution to Marks & Spencer. During her time as a Non-Executive Director, Cheryl has been a strong advocate for our transformation priorities and brought challenge and insight to Board discussions. We wish her every success in the future."
  • Potter: "It has been a privilege to serve as a Non-Executive Director at Marks & Spencer. I am proud to have supported the Board and management team as the business progressed its transformation, including strengthening digital capability and driving operational improvements. I would like to thank my fellow Directors and colleagues, and I wish everyone at M&S continued success."
  • Announcement made under UK Listing Rule 6.4.6(R); no successor named.

body:
• Reported an "excellent start" to 2026, with strong trading momentum in the Americas and selected European markets.
  • Stronger-than-expected Americas performance (first flagged in Feb 2025 trading update and March annual results) has continued into 2026.
  • Trading driven by continued strength in US, Mexico and selected European markets, plus solid performance in the Live division.
  • CEO Mor Weizer: returns from investments made in recent years are "continuing to accelerate and contribute meaningfully to profitability".
  • Caliente Interactive partnership in Mexico continues to perform strongly ahead of the upcoming football World Cup.
  • Weizer: "The combination of Playtech's strong expansion in regulated markets, diversified footprint, highly scalable technology and deep partner relationships, leaves the group well positioned to capture the significant market opportunity ahead."
  • Shares up 2.8% at 373.40p in London morning trading.

body:
• Trading for the first 4 months of 2026 in line with expectations and strong vs prior year, especially in North American foundations.
  • Management "remains confident" of delivering full-year results in line with board expectations.
  • Order book strengthened to ~£1.7 billion at end-April; tendering activity described as "buoyant" with good visibility across all divisions.
  • Said to be resilient to macro-driven energy/material cost rises via price increases on new contracts and existing contract mechanisms.
  • H1 net debt/EBITDA expected ~0.2x, similar to prior year and well below the 0.5x–1.5x target range — despite higher dividends and buyback outflows.
  • 2025 total dividend raised 42% to 70.4p; new £100m buyback launched 30 March (follows prior ~£50m programme); ~834,000 shares repurchased for ~£18m to date.
  • Continues to target bolt-on M&A opportunities across markets.
  • Interim results scheduled 4 August; Capital Markets Day on 14 October.
  • CEO James Wroath: "I am very pleased with the performance our teams have delivered so far this year... This has been achieved against a backdrop of continued global uncertainty reflecting the resilience of Keller's portfolio, the diversity of our contracts and the group's ability to pivot to growing markets."
  • CEO outlook: "we continue to target potential bolt-on opportunities across our markets and remain confident in our ability to sustain this momentum and deliver strong performance through the remainder of 2026."
  • Shares +1.2% at 2,292p on the day of the update.

body:
• Pretax profit +47% to £43.9m (from £29.8m); underlying pretax profit +16% to £43.9m (from £37.9m).
  • Revenue +11% to £85.8m (from £77.2m), driven by higher funds under direction.
  • Closing FUD £77.8bn, +18% YoY (from £65.9bn).

  • Diluted EPS +59% to 10.0p (from 6.3p); underlying EPS +14% to 10.0p (from 8.8p).
  • Transact client base +5.6% to 254,700 (from 241,200).
  • Interim dividend 3.8p, +15% (from 3.3p).
  • Reiterated guidance: FY2026 and FY2027 cost growth limited to ~3% per annum.
  • Management quote: "The combination of the group's revenue growth fundamentals and realising savings from the group's cost management initiatives puts [IntegraFin] in a strong position to continue to accelerate profit growth and enhance profit margin in the coming years, driven by recurring revenue and scale benefits."
  • Says platform "well positioned to continue capturing a strong share of adviser platform market net inflow."

body:
• Net income attributable to Golar of $84m (+920% YoY), inclusive of $37m non-cash items.
  • Total operating revenues $137.6m, +120% YoY.
  • Adjusted EBITDA $106m, +158% YoY and +$15m vs Q4 2025, driven by FLNG Gimi overproduction and lower opex, partially offset by lower realised oil/gas derivative gains and higher admin costs.
  • Total Golar Cash $1.0bn; Golar's share of contractual debt $2.7bn; net debt $1.7bn.
  • Dividend maintained at $0.25/share, payable 10 June 2026.
  • Goldman Sachs engaged to evaluate strategic alternatives including potential sale, merger, asset divestiture or corporate restructuring; no defined timetable and no assurance of a transaction.
  • Targeting order of 4th FLNG within 2026; long-lead items reserved in May 2026.
  • FLNG Hilli: 100% economic uptime, offloaded 150th cargo in April (152nd this week); Cameroon contract ends Q3 2026; $50m of $350m upgrade/transition budget spent; Argentina 20-year SESA contract Adjusted EBITDA $285m/yr with ~$30m upside per $1/MMBtu FOB above $8 reference; COD H2 2027.
  • FLNG Gimi: Q1 invoiced day rate 19% above contractual, 33 cargoes offloaded; production expected lower over next two quarters due to warmer ambient temps; Golar's 70% share of net earnings backlog ~$3bn over 20 years; $1.18bn of $1.2bn bank facility drawn.
  • MKII FLNG conversion on time and on budget; $1.2bn spent to date (all equity-funded) of $2.2bn total; completion Q4 2027, Argentina start-up H2 2028; expected $400m annual Adjusted EBITDA to Golar (~$8bn backlog over 20 years) plus ~$40m upside per $1/MMBtu above $8.
  • MKII asset-level financing being arranged with commercial bank and ECAs against confirmed 20-year contract; equity release intended for FLNG growth.
  • SESA-SEFE 8-year agreement finalised for up to 2 MTPA of LNG from 2027, making SEFE Argentina's first long-term LNG customer globally.
  • Golar took 10% equity in San Matías Pipeline S.A. (~500km dedicated pipeline from Vaca Muerta to Gulf of San Matías); EPC proposals received in Q1; bank syndicate financing under discussion.
  • Combined Argentina commodity exposure (Hilli + MKII tariffs + 10% SESA stake): up to $100m upside per $1/MMBtu above $8 FOB, ~$28m downside per $1 below SESA cash breakeven.
  • Divested 2.69% stake in OLT Offshore LNG Toscana to SNAM for $3.1m (gain on disposal); exited final FSRU O&M contract (Italis LNG) in April 2026.
  • Q1 non-cash items: $34m unrealised MTM gains on TTF/Brent derivatives ($30m Brent, $4m TTF) + $3m IRS MTM gain; realised oil/gas derivative gains of $10m.
  • 101.8m shares outstanding at 31 March 2026.
  • CEO Karl Fredrik Staubo: "Geopolitical events continuing in Ukraine and Russia combined with significant escalation in the Middle East has again driven commodity prices higher and put pressure on the global energy markets. We see strengthening demand for energy security and diversification."
  • Management flags potential material impact on global LNG supply/demand into 2028 from Ras Laffan train repair times and possible North Field East delays following Middle East events.
  • Hilli refinancing flagged as significant liquidity opportunity — $503m contractual debt against $5.7bn Adjusted EBITDA backlog.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Customer milestone announcement: Dougherty County, Georgia has surpassed $3 million in cumulative surplus auction proceeds on the GovDeals platform.
  • Achieved via 781 successful virtual auctions since the county began online auctioning in 2006.
  • Trailing 12-month proceeds from the county totalled just over $84,000 — implying the $3m is a long-tail cumulative figure rather than recent run-rate.
  • No financial guidance, KPI, or company-level performance data disclosed — this is a customer PR/marketing release, not a trading update.
  • Reiterates that Liquidity Services' marketplace network has completed more than $15 billion of sales to date.
  • Management quote (Nick Carr, GovDeals VP of Revenue): "Milestones like this are the product of disciplined, ongoing effort—listing consistently, presenting assets well, and taking advantage of a platform built to maximize buyer reach."

body:
• NAV broadly flat at ~99.7p/share vs 99.8p at 31 December (-0.1p).
  • Q4 dividend of 1.5p approved; FY27 target payout of at least 6.0p.
  • Q4 EPRA EPS of 1.5p, "in line with" Q3's 1.7p (Q3 included ~0.2p one-off surrender premium on an industrial property).
  • Portfolio ERV +0.7% LFL, led by industrials +1.2% LFL (42% of portfolio income); broader portfolio ERV growth on LFL basis cited at 3.3%.
  • 13% embedded income growth in the portfolio: ERV of £55.6m at 31 March vs £52.0m three months earlier, against passing rent of £49.2m (up from £45.8m).
  • Completed acquisitions of family-owned property portfolios during the quarter, including Scorpion Properties Ltd in March.
  • MD Richard Shepherd-Cross: "Despite the challenging investment market backdrop, we are seeing more positive underlying sentiment towards listed real estate."
  • On strategy: "we remain extremely focused on what we can control by continuing to remain hands-on in our asset management approach and capturing the 13% of income growth that is already embedded in our portfolio."
  • On pipeline: "we will continue to progress discussions with further families seeking...to continue their investment in regional property for the next generation, while allowing us to grow the company through income accretive share-based acquisitions."
  • Shares +7.8% over 12 months; trading at 84.3p at time of article (implies ~15% discount to NAV).

body:
Golar LNG Limited (NASDAQ: GLNG)

Publish Date 20 May 2026

Time Period Q1 2026 (quarter ended 31 March 2026)

*

Revenue of $137.55m, beating consensus of $128.36m

(+7.2% surprise),

up 120% YoY from $62.50m

.

*

Net income attributable to Golar of $83.58m; adjusted EBITDA of $105.58m

.

*

EPS of $0.49 vs $0.42 consensus (+$0.07 beat)

.

*

Beat driven primarily by FLNG Gimi overproduction, 19% above committed contractual volumes, plus lower opex

.

*

~$37m in non-cash items, largely unrealized gains on oil and gas derivative instruments

.

  • CEO Karl Fredrik Staubo:

"Q1 saw solid operational performance with continued 100% economic uptime for the FLNG Hilli and strong operational performance from the FLNG Gimi producing 19% more than the contractual committed volume"

.

*

FLNG Hilli completed its 150th cargo offload during the quarter

.

*

MKII FLNG conversion on schedule and on budget for Q4 2027 completion

;

contract startup expected H2 2028

.

  • Strategic review launched:

Goldman Sachs hired to explore strategic alternatives including potential sale, merger, or asset divestitures

.

  • Growth pipeline:

targeting an order for a fourth FLNG unit during 2026 as commercial activity accelerates amid Middle East geopolitical developments

.

*

Management cited offtaker demand to diversify away from the two largest LNG producers — US facing high delivered costs and Qatar with ~7mtpa offline for 3–5 years due to conflict

.

*

Signed 8-year LNG supply agreement between Southern Energy S.A. and Securing Energy for Europe, starting 2027

.

*

Entered a 10% / ~$77m investment (in line with SESA equity stake) for the dedicated pipeline delivering gas to the FLNG units

.

  • Portfolio simplification:

divested investment in OLT Offshore Toscana and terminated remaining FSRU O&M contract

.

  • Balance sheet:

total cash of $1.0bn, contractual debt of $2.7bn at quarter-end

.

  • Dividend:

quarterly dividend of $0.25/share, payable 10 June 2026 (record date 1 June 2026)

.

  • Outlook:

fully delivered annual run-rate EBITDA expected to exceed $800m once all three FLNG units operational; dividend capacity could potentially reach over $5/share based on contracted EBITDA

.

*

Shares up 3.32% in pre-market trading on the release

.

body:
• Group profitability for Q1 ahead of both last year and budget; turnaround "continues apace" with trading "healthily above budget".
  • Shares up 9.1% to 2,030p on the update.
  • Transaction growth slowed in the period, but applications for finance at both Advantage and Aspen "remain strong".
  • Advantage (motor finance): average lending rates increased to 15.3%; repayment rates averaging 92.4% vs 89.1% last year.
  • Aspen (property bridging): recent uptick in applications as traders return, pointing to a busier Q2 after a slow start driven by interest rate uncertainty.
  • On regulation: UK "machinery of government and regulation" operating in a "calmer and more pragmatic way".
  • On FCA motor finance commission redress: now subject to judicial review and "administratively cumbersome", but "likely to have little or no bearing on Advantage's future profitability".
  • Pointed political comment: "Government is slowly realising that future fiscal golden eggs from the finance industry mean that the goose cannot be taken for granted."
  • Chair Anthony Coombs: "At this stage in the year, it is usually unwise to attempt year end predictions, particularly in such a fluid political and economic climate. Suffice to say that confidence in our aims for double digits growth and return on equity is exemplified by the resources we plan to make available, and the abilities of our people in achieving them."

body:
• CEO Bill Winters walked back comments describing displaced staff as "lower-value human capital", telling employees in a memo the remarks were taken "out of context".
  • Winters quote: "Where roles do fall away, it reflects changes in the work, not the value of our people."
  • Original comment at Hong Kong investor event: "it's not cost cutting – it's replacing in some cases lower-value human capital with the financial capital and the investment capital we're putting in".
  • Backlash came from shareholders, employees, social media, and former Singaporean president Halimah Yacob, who called the language "disturbing".
  • Context: Tuesday's announcement to cut ~7,800 jobs, equating to >15% of back-office roles by 2030, as AI adoption is scaled across the group.
  • Group headcount ~82,000, predominantly back-office.
  • Strategic targets reiterated: RoTE >15% by 2028 (+3pp vs 2025), lower cost/income ratio, and income per employee +~20% by 2028.
  • Shares +1.4% at 1,905p intraday Wednesday (closed +2.58% at 1,927.50p).

body:
• Shore Capital initiates coverage with a "buy" rating and a 196p price target (vs. 138.40p current).
  • Shares up 2.1% to 138.40p in London; tripled over the last 12 months; Johannesburg listing +1.6% to ZAR30.85, also nearly tripled YoY.
  • Forecasts 5-year EBITDA CAGR of 22% at Shore Cap's gold price assumptions (USD4,500/oz for FY2027).
  • At spot prices, 5-year EBITDA CAGR would rise to 30%.
  • Projects 76% production growth over 2025–2031, funded by reinvested operating cash flows.
  • Stress test: even with a 40% drop in gold prices, PAF could still organically fund its production growth plan.
  • On costs: "We believe PAF's volume growth will continue to keep unit cost growth in the low-to-mid single digit range. We note PAF ranks towards the bottom end of the peer cost curve on an all-in sustaining costs basis."
  • Balance sheet: "We forecast the group will move to a net cash balance by the end of fiscal 2026, supporting the case for potentially higher cash distributions to shareholders."
  • Tennant Mines (Australia) acquisition seen as diversifying: forecast to contribute 30% of group revenue by 2030, up from 18% currently, reducing South Africa concentration.
  • Reminder: PAF acquired the remainder of Tennant Consolidated Mining Group for USD54.2 million in November 2024.
  • Diversification argued to justify a higher valuation multiple.

body:
• IAG has set final terms on two series of senior unsecured bonds, both fully subscribed, totalling EUR 1.0bn in aggregate.
  • Series A: EUR 500m at 3.875% fixed annual coupon, issued at 99.752% of principal.
  • Series B: EUR 500m at 4.500% fixed annual coupon, issued at 99.723% of principal.
  • Bonds redeem at 100% of principal at maturity unless previously redeemed/cancelled (maturity dates not specified in this notice).
  • Settlement expected on or about 28 May 2026.
  • Joint Global Co-ordinators / Joint Lead Bookrunners: J.P. Morgan SE, Morgan Stanley Europe SE, MUFG Securities (Europe) and SMBC Bank EU; Passive Bookrunners: Barclays Bank Ireland and Standard Chartered; Co-Managers: CaixaBank, Commerzbank, Lloyds and NatWest Markets.
  • Signed off by CFO Nicholas Cadbury; no use-of-proceeds disclosed in this final terms notice.

body:
• Revenue $81.6bn, +20% Q/Q and +85% Y/Y, a record; vs Q4 FY26 $68.1bn and Q1 FY26 $44.1bn.
  • GAAP gross margin 74.9% (+14.4pts Y/Y, -0.1pt Q/Q); non-GAAP gross margin 75.0%.
  • GAAP diluted EPS $2.39 (+214% Y/Y); non-GAAP diluted EPS $1.87 (+140% Y/Y). Note GAAP boosted by $15.9bn gains on equity securities.
  • GAAP operating income $53.5bn (+147% Y/Y); operating expenses $7.6bn (+52% Y/Y).
  • Q2 FY27 revenue guide: $91.0bn ±2% — explicitly assumes zero Data Center compute revenue from China.
  • Q2 FY27 gross margin guide 74.9% GAAP / 75.0% non-GAAP ±50bps; opex ~$8.5bn GAAP / $8.3bn non-GAAP.
  • FY27 GAAP/non-GAAP tax rate guided to 16.0–18.0%.
  • Capital return: ~$20.0bn returned in Q1 (buybacks + dividends); $38.5bn remained on prior authorisation.
  • Board approved additional $80.0bn buyback authorisation (no expiration) on 18 May 2026.
  • Quarterly dividend raised 25x from $0.01 to $0.25/share, payable 26 June 2026.
  • Data Center revenue (old segmentation) $75.2bn, +92% Y/Y, +21% Q/Q; compute $60.4bn (+77% Y/Y), networking $14.8bn (+199% Y/Y, +35% Q/Q).
  • Edge Computing revenue $6.4bn, +29% Y/Y, +10% Q/Q.
  • New reporting framework: two platforms — Data Center (split into Hyperscale and ACIE: AI Clouds, Industrial, Enterprise) and Edge Computing (PCs, consoles, workstations, AI-RAN, robotics, automotive).
  • Non-GAAP measures no longer exclude stock-based compensation starting Q1 FY27; historicals restated.
  • No H20 charges in Q1 FY27 (vs $4.5bn charge in Q1 FY26), aiding Y/Y margin comparison.
  • Free cash flow $48.6bn vs $26.1bn Y/Y; operating cash flow $50.3bn.
  • Inventories rose to $25.8bn from $21.4bn at Jan 2026 (+21% in quarter) — likely Blackwell/Rubin ramp.
  • Non-marketable securities jumped to $43.4bn from $22.3bn (+$21bn), reflecting major strategic AI investments.
  • Cash + marketable securities ~$80.6bn; long-term debt steady at $7.5bn.
  • Key product announcements: NVIDIA Vera Rubin platform (incl. Vera CPU "first processor purpose-built for agentic AI"), BlueField-4 STX, Dynamo 1.0 (claims up to 7x inference boost on Blackwell), RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition.
  • New partnerships: expanded Google Cloud (Vera Rubin-powered A5X instances, Gemini on Blackwell), Marvell via NVLink Fusion, multi-year deals with Coherent/Corning/Lumentum on optics.
  • Automotive: expanded Hyundai/Kia and Uber partnerships; BYD, Geely, Isuzu, Nissan building L4-ready vehicles on DRIVE Hyperion; introduced Halos OS safety architecture.
  • Telecom: T-Mobile/Nokia AI-RAN collaboration; 6G commitment with global telecom leaders.
  • CEO Jensen Huang: "The buildout of AI factories — the largest infrastructure expansion in human history — is accelerating at extraordinary speed."
  • Huang on positioning: "Agentic AI has arrived, doing productive work, generating real value and scaling rapidly across companies and industries. NVIDIA is uniquely positioned at the center of this transformation as the only platform that runs in every cloud, powers every frontier and open source model, and scales everywhere AI is produced — from hyperscale data centers to the edge."

body:
• Ian Penrose, Senior Independent Director, to step down from the Board after almost 9 years (appointed 2018).
  • Will remain as Non-Executive Director, SID and Chairman of the Audit and Risk Committee until after publication of FY2026 final results (Spring 2027) to ensure orderly transition.
  • AGM Resolution 6 (Penrose re-election) withdrawn; other resolutions, Notice of AGM and submitted proxies unaffected; numbering of other resolutions unchanged.

  • No successor named yet for either the SID or Audit & Risk Committee Chair roles.

body:
• Pretax profit down 29% to £364.6m (prior: £511.8m), hit by cyber attack disruption.
  • Adjusted pretax profit fell 24% to £671.4m (prior: £881.1m), beating Visible Alpha consensus of £640.3m.
  • Revenue: up 25% to £17.37bn (statutory £17.27bn) from £13.91bn.
  • Food LFL growth +6.7%; total Food sales +7.0%; market share +17bps to 4.1%.
  • Basic EPS down 13% to 12.7p; Adjusted EPS down 25% to 23.8p vs 22.6p consensus.
  • Full-year dividend raised 17% to 4.2p (final 3.0p vs 2.6p) — signals confidence despite cyber hit.
  • Shares +2.1% to 333.8p on the day vs FTSE 100 -0.4%.
  • Described as a "year of two halves: significant operational impact from the cyber incident during the first, followed by a return to sales and profit growth in the second."
  • Food was the standout: adj. operating profit £444.5m (down from £491.8m) — H1 hit by higher markdown/waste, H2 saw sustained volume growth.
  • Fashion, Home & Beauty sales -7.7%; adj. operating profit collapsed to £213.4m from £478.0m (more than halved) due to pause in online trading post-cyber.
  • International sales -7.2%, with H2 improvement partly offset by Middle East shipment delays in the final month.
  • CEO Stuart Machin: "A resilient balance sheet supported by the hard work done on our cash position in recent years allowed us to absorb the cost of disruption without compromising our financial health."
  • Machin flagged a "triple whammy of headwinds" — "increased taxation, a greater regulatory burden and ongoing global conflict."
  • FHB recovery "has taken longer, but there is strong growth potential."
  • Outlook: profit growth "expected to resume" vs FY24/25, but no specific guidance figures given.
  • Cost headwinds cited: "higher fuel, freight and input costs and continued government tax levies and regulatory headwinds for the sector."
  • Mitigation via improved buying, value reinvestment to drive volume, and savings from structural cost reduction programme.

body:
• Announced a strategic collaboration with Microsoft to accelerate digital transformation via adoption of advanced AI tools across operations.
  • Positioned as a "Frontier Firm" partnership aligned with modernising ZIGUP's mobility platform, simplifying and scaling the business for growth.
  • Rollout of Microsoft 365 Copilot to ~3,500 employees across the Group, with a structured AI training and enablement programme for all colleagues.
  • Aimed at sharpening decision making via better data insights, improving customer experience, and driving productivity in branch operations, customer service and central functions.
  • No financial quantification of cost, capex, or expected productivity/margin uplift provided.
  • CEO Martin Ward: "This strategic collaboration is a natural next step in our evolution as a group and it will accelerate our ongoing programme of adopting the latest technologies across the business, both in branch operations and central services."
  • CEO added: "We're building a business that is simpler, faster and more responsive to customer needs and AI has the potential to be a key enabler of that. Combining our expertise with Microsoft, we're scaling this across ZIGUP in a way that will deliver real performance improvement."
  • Theo Michalopoulos (GM, Enterprise Commercial, Microsoft UK & Ireland): "As a Frontier Firm, ZIGUP is demonstrating how organisations that pair deep sector expertise with advanced AI can translate innovation into measurable productivity gains and differentiated customer experience at scale."

body:
• Launching two series of senior unsecured bonds, ~€500m each (€1bn total).
  • Series A: ~€500m, maturing 28 January 2031 (~4.7yr).
  • Series B: ~€500m, maturing 28 May 2034 (~8yr).
  • Fixed coupon to be set via bookbuilding; issued at ~100% of principal, redeemed at 100% at maturity.
  • Issuer call option included.
  • Settlement expected on or about 28 May 2026.
  • Proceeds earmarked for general corporate purposes (no specified refinancing target disclosed).
  • Listing sought on Euronext Dublin Official List / Regulated Market.
  • Restricted to eligible counterparties and professional clients; no retail distribution in EEA/UK; not offered in US, Australia, Canada or Japan.
  • Joint Global Coordinators & Joint Lead Bookrunners: J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, MUFG, SMBC; Passive Bookrunners: Barclays, Standard Chartered; Co-Managers: CaixaBank, Commerzbank, Lloyds, NatWest Markets.
  • Signed off by CFO Nicholas Cadbury.

body:
• Expanded its AI-powered Equipment Agent to be accessible in ChatGPT, marking the first equipment rental application available in the ChatGPT store.
  • Tool uses a conversational interface to guide customers through project requirements and recommend relevant rental equipment.
  • Originally launched earlier in 2026 as a first-of-its-kind AI equipment recommendation solution for the rental industry.
  • Early usage data shows customers using the tool primarily for specification and rental-related queries; company cites "strong demand for faster equipment planning."
  • Tony Leopold, SVP - Chief Technology and Strategy Officer: "By bringing the Equipment Agent into ChatGPT, we're meeting customers in the platforms they already use to plan work, solve problems and make decisions. It's part of our broader focus on creating digital experiences built around the speed, complexity and realities of modern jobsites."
  • No financial guidance, KPI, or trading impact disclosed — purely a digital/product distribution announcement.
  • Operational scale reminder: 1,658 North American rental locations, 44 Europe, 46 Australia, 19 New Zealand; ~27,900 employees; fleet original cost $22.59bn.

body:
• Mortgage applications in the first 19 weeks of 2026 15% higher year-on-year, in line with Board expectations.
  • Slight deceleration vs the 19% YoY growth reported for the first 16 weeks (announced 27 April 2026 alongside prospectus publication).
  • During March/April, four weeks of accelerated refinancing activity occurred as customers fixed rates early ahead of product expiry, driven by heightened macro and interest rate uncertainty.
  • Application levels have since normalised; refinancing activity expected to remain robust through the remainder of 2026.
  • Chair Michael Jones: "The Company's customer acquisition and retention strategies are supporting continued growth in lead flow, underpinning MAB's long track record of year-on-year revenue growth across all market conditions."
  • No change to guidance signalled; trading consistent with prior outlook.
