body:
• Underlying PBT +16% to £43.9m; reported PBT +47% to £43.9m (HY25 reported included £7.5m T4A goodwill/intangible impairment).
  • Underlying diluted EPS +14% to 10.0p; reported diluted EPS +59% to 10.0p (HY25: 6.3p).
  • Group revenue +11% to £85.8m, driven by higher average daily FUD.
  • Platform revenue +11% to £83.2m (recurring annual charges +14% to £76.4m; recurring wrapper charges -10% to £6.0m due to April 2025 single wrapper fee on family-linked pensions).
  • Back-office (CURO) revenue +4% to £2.6m.
  • Closing FUD +18% YoY to £77.8bn; average daily FUD +17% to £77.5bn.
  • Net inflows +14% to £2.4bn; gross inflows record +18% to £6.0bn; outflows +20% to £3.6bn (annualised outflow rate 10% vs 9%).
  • c.25% share of adviser platform market net flows in HY26.
  • Transact platform clients +6% to 254,667; CURO licence users +10% to 3,393.
  • PBT margin >51% for the period.
  • Underlying admin expenses +4% to £46.4m; employee costs +8% to £34.2m (headcount +2%); occupancy -77% to £0.3m on rebates/provision releases.
  • Post-period update: average daily FUD £80.6bn for April 2026; closing FUD £81.4bn on 30 April 2026.
  • Cost guidance reiterated: FY26 and FY27 underlying cost growth limited to c.3% p.a., with FY26 cost growth reduction weighted to H2.
  • First interim dividend 3.8p (+15% vs 3.3p), payable 26 June 2026; ex-div 28 May 2026.
  • Group regulatory capital coverage improved: IFAL 131% (vs 128%), ILUK 136% (vs 133%); ILInt 161% (vs 168%).
  • Liquidity buffer £34.7m (Sept-25: £33.7m); shareholder cash £258.9m plus £31.7m gilts.
  • Deferred tax liability +£4.0m from change in future tax rate assumption (20% → 22% effective April 2027) on policyholder assets.
  • AI/automation investment underway across software development, support functions and the Transact platform; new Transact API rolled out.
  • Board change: Company Secretary David Johnson stepping down 1 June 2026, replaced by Stephanie Wallace.
  • CEO Alex Scott: "I am pleased to report that the Group has delivered a strong first half of the year."
  • CEO on AI: "the human relationship element of financial advice will remain critical, with AI augmenting but not replacing the trust and accountability elements of regulated personal financial advice."
  • Principal risks unchanged from 2025 Annual Report; flagged ongoing Iran conflict as contributing to post-period geopolitical and market volatility.

body:
• Underlying Profit £294m, up 5% (FY25: £279m); Underlying EPS 28.9p, up 1%.
  • IFRS profit after tax £454m vs £338m; IFRS basic EPS 45.4p vs 35.1p.
  • DPS 23.12p, up 1%; final dividend 10.80p (9.34p PID / 1.46p non-PID), payable 24 July 2026.
  • EPRA cost ratio rose to 18.9% (FY25: 17.5%) — notable margin headwind.
  • Total property return +7.4%; total accounting return +8.1% (FY25: 5.0%).
  • EPRA NTA per share 590p, up 4%.
  • Portfolio valuation £10.06bn, up 2.3% (Campuses +2.0%, Retail & London Urban Logistics +2.7%).
  • ERV growth +4.9% (Campuses +6.5%, Retail & LUL +3.6%); net equivalent yield -4bps to 6.0%.
  • Like-for-like net rental growth +6% (Campuses +12% ex-Storey, Retail & LUL +2%).
  • Portfolio occupancy 96.9%; retail parks 99% occupied; Campuses 94.7%.
  • Record leasing: 3.8m sq ft signed, 7.2% ahead of ERV; 1.1m sq ft under offer 12.9% ahead of ERV.
  • Campus leasing 1.7m sq ft (record £143m secured), 6.3% ahead of ERV; Q4 accelerated to 0.8m sq ft (~50% of full-year total).
  • Management notes ~5% share of London office market but 15% of reported leasing FY26, rising to 33% in Q4.
  • Retail & LUL leasing 2.1m sq ft, 8.4% ahead of ERV; retail park H2 deals 6.3% ahead of previous passing rent.
  • LTV rose to 39.2% (FY25: 38.1%); Net Debt/EBITDA improved to 7.7x (8.0x).
  • £3.1bn financing activity incl. £2.0bn new finance; £1.6bn liquidity; no refinancing required until early 2029.
  • Fitch 'A' senior unsecured, stable, affirmed Feb 2026.
  • £106m disposals at 4% above book / 2.9% NIY; £176m further disposals exchanged/under offer post year end.
  • £94m retail acquired (mainly three retail parks) at 7.2% Topped Up NIY.
  • Life Science REIT acquisition completed 20 April 2026, funded via 24.5m new shares plus £49m cash.
  • 1.6m sq ft committed development pipeline progressing on de-risked, capital light basis.
  • 75% of portfolio EPC A/B (up from 68%); GRESB 5* for both standing investments and developments.
  • FY27 guidance: EPS of at least 30.5p, underpinned by LfL net rental growth at top end of 3-5% range.
  • Medium-term framework: 3-6% p.a. EPS growth and 8-10% total returns across the cycle; reiterates 3-5% p.a. ERV growth.
  • Board: Lynn Gladden to step down as NED at 2026 AGM; Amanda James to succeed Loraine Woodhouse as Audit Committee Chair.
  • CEO Simon Carter: "A record year of leasing has driven strong ERV growth, like-for-like net rental growth and an attractive earnings outlook."
  • Carter on market backdrop: "While the geopolitical and interest rate backdrop has become more uncertain, the occupational fundamentals underpinning our portfolio are as strong as I have seen them. Central London office net take-up is at its highest level in 20 years and our retail parks are 99% occupied."

body:
• Group profitability ahead of both last year and budget; trading "healthily above budget" despite weaker UK macro backdrop.
  • Transaction growth slowed at both businesses given weaker consumer confidence, but finance applications at Advantage and Aspen remain strong.
  • Net interest margins improved, particularly at Advantage where average lending rates rose ~20% YoY to 15.3%.
  • Advantage repayment rates averaged 92.4% vs 89.1% last year; Q1 collections of £43.1m.
  • Advantage transactions up 63% YoY, supporting target of 20% net receivables growth and a slight increase from current ~10% share of UK used-car finance market.
  • Advantage capital receivables £392m vs £348m last year, across 57,666 accounts.
  • Bad debt write-offs down ~41% YTD; customer repayments +8% YoY; Trustpilot score 4.9/5.
  • New credit scorecard rolled out; AI embedded in customer relations to improve productivity within Consumer Duty compliance.
  • Aspen Bridging PBT on budget and equal to last year, with recent uptick in applications/deal numbers after a slow start.
  • Aspen capital receivables £191m vs £162m last year; only 15 of 246 live accounts beyond term; Bridge-to-let collections and fees hit £1m in Q1.
  • Anticipates "another record year from Aspen."
  • Funding: Group borrowing £252m vs facilities of £330m; projected 20% net receivables growth p.a. over next three years may require ~£300m additional funding.
  • Securitisation project terms agreed, legal process about to commence with selected financial partners — to deliver medium-term funding at lower rates; details to follow.
  • On FCA motor finance commission redress: now subject to judicial review and "administratively cumbersome," but "likely to have little or no bearing on Advantage's future profitability."
  • Chairman Anthony Coombs: "At this stage in the year, it is usually unwise to attempt year end predictions, particularly in such a fluid political and economic climate. Suffice to say that confidence in our aims for double digits growth and return on equity is exemplified by the resources we plan to make available, and the abilities of our people in achieving them."

body:
• Outlook reiterated in line with Board expectations for the full year; strong start to 2026 despite macro/geopolitical uncertainty.
  • Trading particularly strong in North American foundations operations.
  • Group passing through higher energy/material costs via price increases on new contracts and existing contract mechanisms.
  • Order book strengthened to c.£1.7bn at end-April 2026; tendering activity buoyant with good visibility across all divisions.
  • New £100m share buyback launched 30 March 2026, following completion of the 2025 programme which returned c.£50m.
  • Under the new buyback, c.834,000 shares purchased for c.£18m to date.
  • H1 2026 net debt/EBITDA expected similar to 0.2x at H1 2025, well below the 0.5x–1.5x target range, despite higher dividend and buyback.
  • CEO James Wroath: "I am very pleased with the performance our teams have delivered so far this year. This has been achieved against a backdrop of continued global uncertainty reflecting the resilience of Keller's portfolio, the diversity of our contracts and the Group's ability to pivot to growing markets."
  • CEO on strategy: "we continue to target potential bolt-on opportunities across our markets and remain confident in our ability to sustain this momentum and deliver strong performance through the remainder of 2026."
  • Interim Results scheduled for 4 August 2026; Capital Markets Day on 14 October 2026 in London.

body:
• Agreed to acquire Boteco Srl, an Italian family-owned designer/manufacturer of mechanical components (handles, plastic/metal knobs, feet, hinges) based in Vicenza.
  • Initial cash consideration of €7.4m, plus deferred contingent cash consideration of up to €2.5m (cash-free, debt-free basis); contingent element subject to performance conditions over a two-year period.
  • Acquisition multiple of 6.5x EBITDA for the twelve months to 31 December 2025.
  • Boteco FY2025 revenues of approximately €7.0m.
  • Target ROIC of 15% in the third full year of ownership.
  • Funded through Essentra's existing debt facilities.
  • Boteco has >50 years of history, c.900 active customers, modern facility with in-house tooling, and R&D focused on more sustainable materials.
  • Strategic rationale: extends European manufacturing footprint, strengthens position in machine & automation end-markets, and unlocks cross-selling revenue synergies.
  • Expected to complete in H1 2026 subject to a small number of pre-close conditions.
  • CEO Scott Fawcett: "This acquisition builds on the momentum of our Device Technologies acquisition announced in December 2025 and further demonstrates Essentra's growth strategy, combining organic investment with disciplined, value‑accretive M&A. With a strong acquisition pipeline and a robust balance sheet, we are well positioned to continue to create long‑term value for shareholders."
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'to acquire'

body:
• Benchmark EPS up 15% at actual rates (13% constant) to USc 179.8; at upper end of expectations.
  • Total revenue from ongoing activities +13% actual / +11% constant / +8% organic to US$8,425m; Q4 organic accelerated to 9%.
  • Statutory revenue +12% to US$8,445m.
  • Benchmark EBIT margin 28.6%, +50bps actual / +60bps constant, ahead of Medium-Term Framework guidance.
  • Benchmark EBIT from ongoing activities +15% to US$2,407m.
  • Statutory PBT +26% to US$1,951m (FY25: US$1,549m); Basic EPS +29% to USc 164.5 (helped by non-cash FX gains on Brazilian intra-group funding and lower restructuring).
  • ROCE 17.2% (FY25: 16.6%).
  • Benchmark operating cash flow US$2.2bn, 93% cash conversion.
  • Net debt/Benchmark EBITDA 1.7x; net debt US$5,179m (FY25: US$4,684m).
  • New US$1bn share buyback announced (valid to 30 June 2027), on top of US$725m executed in FY26 (including US$529m from prior US$1bn programme launched January 2026).
  • Full-year dividend +11% to USc 69.25; second interim USc 48.00 payable 24 July 2026.
  • M&A: US$792m deployed across 4 acquisitions (ClearSale, AtData, KYC360, plus other); post year-end completed Own Up (US$175m) and Konfir (US$25m) on 1 April 2026; agreed to acquire idwall in Brazil for c.US$86m.
  • FY27 guidance: total revenue growth 8–11%, organic 6–8%, double-digit Benchmark EPS growth, margin expansion c.+50bps constant (high end of MTF).
  • FY27 modelling: c.1% inorganic contribution, 1–2% FX tailwind, net interest US$250–260m (up materially from US$185m FY26), Benchmark tax rate c.26%, WANOS 880–885m, capex c.8% of revenue trending to 7%, OCF conversion >90%.
  • Regional organic growth: North America +10%, Latin America +8% (H1 4% → H2 12%), UK&I +2%, EMEA & AP +5%.
  • B2B organic +8%; Consumer Services organic +9% with margin +220bps; free members >215m globally.
  • North America Financial Services organic +14%; 100% renewal rate on top strategic accounts with contract durations +c.10% and double-digit value uplifts.
  • Latin America Benchmark EBIT margin -120bps to 30.8% (acquisition dilution); EMEA & AP margin doubled to 6.7% from 3.4% on illion integration.
  • Cloud migration in North America and Brazil (ex-Health) largely complete; material cost savings expected from FY27 as dual-run costs decline.
  • AI: c.10–15% coding productivity uplift (>30% in select areas); labour costs at 32% of revenue, >300bps lower than two years ago; US$15bn+ AI-enabled addressable market identified.
  • US$2bn revenue generated from new and scaling products (Ascend modules, consumer marketplaces).
  • Ascend Platform: 2,300+ client solutions, 37 product capabilities; new multi-year ServiceNow partnership announced.
  • VantageScore 4.0 now accepted by Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac following FHFA pilot — first score competition in conforming mortgage market.
  • Significant new multi-year US bank partnership in Partner Solutions to ramp from FY27, material from FY28 (offsets wind-down of two legacy data breach contracts).
  • Undrawn committed facilities US$2.5bn; two new US$250m facilities executed post year-end (one drawn).
  • Cassin: "FY26 was a record year for Experian, with performance at the upper end of our expectations and strong strategic momentum."
  • Cassin on outlook: expect "another year of double-digit Benchmark EPS growth... whilst taking a prudent approach to macroeconomic uncertainties linked to the Middle East."
  • Ongoing US CFPB lawsuit (filed Jan 2025) on consumer dispute process being "vigorously defended"; directors expect no materially adverse outcome.
  • Board changes: Adam Crozier joined as independent NED and Chair Designate on 12 May 2026; Louise Pentland and Luiz Fleury stepped down July 2025.

body:
• Very strong start to 2026, with continued momentum in regulated markets.
  • Stronger-than-expected Americas performance (previously flagged at FY25 update in Feb 2026 and FY results in March 2026) has continued into 2026.
  • Growth drivers: US, Mexico and certain European markets, plus a solid performance in Live.
  • Management frames the upside as returns on prior-year investments now accelerating and contributing meaningfully to profitability.
  • US singled out as "particularly encouraging" — key inflection point for the investment case.
  • Caliente Interactive partnership (Mexico) continues to perform strongly; 2026 World Cup flagged as a significant opportunity to extend Caliente's market leadership.
  • CEO Mor Weizer: "We have made an excellent start to 2026, with strong trading in the first four months of the year reflecting continued momentum in regulated markets, notably the Americas and certain European markets."
  • Acknowledges "ongoing sector headwinds" but says diversified footprint, scalable tech and partner relationships leave the Group "well positioned to capture the significant market opportunity ahead."
  • No specific revenue, EBITDA or guidance figures disclosed in the update.

body:
• Trading in line; FY26 expectations unchanged.
  • Group revenue +7.2% on constant currency, trading-day adjusted basis vs same period 2025.
  • Like-for-like sales +5.2% (mix of pricing and volume).
  • Inorganic growth contribution of +2.0% from Device Technologies (completed Dec 2025).
  • Order intake momentum maintained and running ahead of sales.
  • Regional LFL (day-adjusted): EMEA high-single digit growth (volume + pricing, modest West Europe recovery, continued Turkey strength); Americas low-single digit (mainly pricing); APAC broadly flat.
  • Faster-growth end-markets (digital infrastructure, energy transformation) offsetting modest softness in automotive and other traditional core markets.
  • Further round of pricing actions implemented across all regions in Q2 to mitigate inflation.
  • Limited direct Middle East exposure; no material trading impact in the period.
  • Margin supported by automation investments and cost control.
  • H1 net debt leverage expected at c.1.6x following Boteco acquisition.
  • Announced acquisition of Boteco (mechanical components designer/manufacturer) for initial cash consideration of €7.4m; expected accretive to Group margins and adjusted EPS in first full year post-completion.
  • Outlook: "The Board's FY26 expectations remain unchanged."
  • Management framing: positioned to "deliver further growth and strategic progress towards its medium-term targets during 2026."

body:
• FY26 DPS of 6.0p delivered in line with target, fully covered by EPRA EPS; 7.1% dividend yield at 84.5p share price.
  • Q4 DPS of 1.5p approved (payable 29 May 2026).
  • FY27 DPS target of no less than 6.0p reiterated.
  • Q4 EPRA EPS of 1.5p vs Q3 1.7p (Q3 had benefited c.0.2p from a one-off industrial surrender premium).
  • Q4 like-for-like ERV growth of 0.7%; FY26 LFL ERV growth 3.3%, with industrial (42% of income) +4.1% and retail warehousing (22%) +2.7%.
  • 13% of income growth embedded in the portfolio: ERV £55.6m vs passing rent £49.2m (vs £52.0m / £45.8m at 31 Dec 2025).
  • Portfolio valuation £669.3m across 174 assets (vs £626.7m); Q4 LFL valuation +0.5%, FY26 LFL valuation +3.1%.
  • NAV £486.7m / 99.7p per share (vs 99.8p at 31 Dec 2025); Q4 NAV total return 1.4%, with acquisition costs offsetting valuation gains.
  • Completed corporate acquisitions of Grove Court (£35.9m) and Scorpion (£8.5m) for aggregate £40.3m (rising to c.£41.9m incl. deferred), via 29.1m new shares (£27.7m) at weighted average issuance NAV of 95.9p plus £12.4m cash; added £3.4m day-one net rental income across 12 smaller-lot assets.
  • Q4 leasing: 10 renewals/re-gears at +22% vs passing rent and +6% vs ERV; 4 new leases adding £0.8m annual rent in line with ERV; 3 rent reviews +10% vs passing.
  • Notable letting: Sonas Bathrooms in Redditch at £669k, +117% on prior passing rent post-redevelopment.
  • Disposals: 7 Merlin Portfolio units in Leicestershire for £2.2m; Glasgow office for £6.0m (24% premium to valuation); post-period Scunthorpe industrial £0.1m.
  • Capex £1.9m (Weymouth freehold £0.7m; Carlisle drive-through construction £0.6m).
  • Net LTV 25.9% (vs 26.2%); drawn debt £185.0m (£120m fixed at WAC 3.3%, 5.0yr WAM; £65m on RCF).
  • WAC of borrowings rose to 4.1% (vs 4.0%) due to RCF drawdown funding acquisitions.
  • Lloyds RCF extended by 1 year to Nov 2028 and upsized from £60m to £75m.
  • Management commentary: "Custodian Property Income REIT performed well during the Quarter, marking a successful close to a financial year in which we have consistently driven rental growth across the Company's portfolio, added scale and income through a number of majority-share corporate acquisitions of family owned portfolios".
  • On outlook: "we are seeing more positive underlying sentiment towards listed real estate which we hope will accelerate if and when geopolitical tensions ease" — sees valuation recovery driven by asset management-led rental growth rather than yield shift.
  • Notes 12.3% share price rally between Nov 2025 Budget and Iran conflict was subsequently reversed by 31 March 2026, with partial recovery since.
  • Pipeline: ongoing discussions with further families seeking tax-efficient share-based exits into regional property.

body:
• Cutting more than 15% of corporate functions roles by 2030, implying at least ~7,800 job losses (Bloomberg estimate based on 52,271 back-office staff at end-2025), driven by automation, advanced analytics and AI adoption.
  • 2026 medium-term targets achieved a year early; new targets set for 2028 and 2030.
  • RoTE target: >15% in 2028, rising to ~18% in 2030 (vs 11.9% statutory RoTE in 2025).
  • Targeting high-teens EPS CAGR and 5–7% income CAGR from 2025 to 2028.
  • 2025 baseline: EPS 195.4 US cents; operating income USD 20.94bn.
  • Cost-to-income ratio target ~57% in 2028 (vs 63% in 2025), supported by positive income-to-cost jaws.
  • CET1 operating range guided to 13–14% (vs 14.1% in 2025) — implies scope for capital return.
  • Income per employee to rise ~20% by 2028.
  • Dividend payout ratio of 30%+ with progressive DPS.
  • Wealth & Retail Banking ambitions pulled forward from 2029 to 2028: USD 200bn net new money target; affluent income to reach 75% of total.
  • CEO Bill Winters: "Our strategy is grounded in a simple belief: the world is becoming more connected, more complex and more cross-border. Clients need a bank that can help them navigate that environment with confidence."
  • Management statement: "We achieved our 2026 medium-term financial targets a year earlier than planned. We now have a more focused, streamlined and efficient organisation, positioning us strongly for the next stage of growth and to deliver our strategy at greater scale and pace."
  • Shares -0.2% at 1,917p on the day of the announcement.

body:
• Total revenue +21% to £339.9m (vs £280.0m YoY); organic revenue +19% to £331.2m.
  • Net trading revenue +26% to £313.3m (vs £248.2m).
  • Organic first trades +63% YoY; active customers +12%, attributed to "increased marketing spend and effectiveness" and new product launches.
  • Shares up 7.4% to 1,693p on the morning.
  • FY26 guidance upgraded: organic total revenue growth of 10–15% off the ~£1.10bn FY25 base, vs prior high single-digit growth guidance.
  • Net interest income guidance raised to £110–120m (from ~£110m) on higher rate expectations.
  • EBITDA margin expected to be sustained in the mid-40s%.
  • Medium-term outlook upgraded: at least 10% organic revenue CAGR beyond 2026, with EBITDA margin remaining mid-40s.
  • Positioning described as a "powerful challenger position" in stock trading and crypto, with a "broadened proposition".
  • Q2 trading: continued to trade well in first 7 weeks, with "underlying commercial momentum continuing to build".
  • Strategic review ongoing, "evaluating routes to maximise shareholder value" — includes acquisitions, review of domicile and listing venues to "unlock capital and enhance strategic flexibility", and potential combinations of parts of the business with other industry participants.
  • Outcome of strategic review to be presented at a strategy update in autumn.

body:
• FY26 results due Wednesday ~0700 BST; this is a sell-side/consensus preview, not the actual print.
  • Consensus revenue: USD8.45bn vs USD7.52bn in FY25 (+12.4% reported).
  • Implied organic revenue growth ~8.2%, up from 6% in FY25.
  • Consensus EBIT: USD2.40bn vs USD2.10bn prior (+14%).
  • Benchmark EPS: 179.2c vs 157.1c (+14.1%).
  • DPS: 70.0c vs 62.50c (+12%).
  • FY27 consensus: revenue USD9.16bn, organic growth 8.0%, EBIT USD2.66bn, EPS 203.1c.
  • Consensus 8.2% organic sits BELOW company guidance of 9–11% issued with FY25 results; margin guidance was +30–50bps p.a.
  • UBS forecasts +8% organic for FY26, implying Q4 continues at +8% (same as Q3); sees adj. EBITDA margin +50bps YoY to 28.2%, top of guided range.
  • UBS flags peer read-across is strong: "sequential improvement for Equifax US Information Solutions to +21% from fourth quarter +12%, and TransUnion US Information Solutions solid at +14%" — though partly FICO mortgage royalty-driven, a smaller exposure for Experian.
  • Capital return watch: Experian accelerated its existing USD1bn buyback in January 2026 after share price weakness; UBS estimates leverage still below the 2.0–2.5x net debt/EBITDA target, leaving room for further buyback announcement.
  • Historical pattern: USD150m buybacks announced with both FY23 and FY24 results.
  • Stock down 31% over 12 months, having hit 2,353p low; shares +2.4% to 2,721.07p on Tuesday.
  • Valuation: ~14x 12-month forward EV/EBITDA, ~20% below 15-year average ~18x, per UBS, attributed to "artificial intelligence-related fears and volatility in the US mortgage pricing market."
  • Key investor focus: management commentary defending positioning vs AI disruption risk to credit bureau model; UBS wants "more details defending Experian's positioning and outlining potential earnings accelerants."
  • Operational focus: US mortgage, auto and credit card originations; FY25 saw North America +6% organic, Latin America +8% organic.
  • CEO Brian Cassin (at FY25): "While we are mindful of the outlook for the broader global economy, we have a broad and resilient portfolio with a strong track record of growth, and we are confident of another good year of growth in financial 2026."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives full-year period 'FY2026 (year to 31 March 2026) results preview'

body:
• FY26 adjusted PBT guidance maintained at £60m–£65m, vs £69.0m delivered in FY25 (implied ~6%–13% YoY decline).
  • Core meat and fresh prepared foods trading described as "good".
  • East region: positive momentum in Australia/New Zealand; further growth in fresh prepared foods in Central Europe.
  • West region: "slightly higher" volumes YTD, with "strong" trading into Easter.
  • Seafood, vegetarian and vegan businesses remain challenging (as previously flagged).
  • Cost reduction plans being implemented at Seachill amid continued volume pressure.
  • Foppen brand shifting US exports from air to sea freight while awaiting FDA feedback on lifting export restrictions on its Greek facility.
  • Canada project on track for full launch in 2027, with bacon now added to scope.
  • Saudi Arabia facility still expected to start operations in H2 2026.
  • Plans being finalised to increase Poland capacity.
  • Capex ~£100m in 2026 as previously guided; net debt expected to rise as a result.
  • Management quote: "Although we remain cautious on the broader inflationary environment and mindful of the potential impacts of the situation in the Middle East, our performance so far in this year underpins our confidence in both the short- and longer-term prospects for the group."
  • Shares +4.2% to 562.5p on the morning of the update.

time_period: Full year ended 2 May 2026 (with 16-week H2 period to 2 May)
body:
• FY adjusted pretax profit guidance raised to ~GBP191 million, +18% YoY, ahead of prior GBP180-190 million range (vs GBP162 million in FY to 3 May 2025).
  • Shares jumped 11% to 140.92p on the update.
  • Group LFL sales +4% for the year; UK&I +3%, Nordics +6% (Nordics ~40% of group sales).
  • H2 LFL +5%; 16 weeks to 2 May LFL +4%.
  • Net cash >GBP170 million at year-end, supporting continued capital returns to shareholders.
  • No impact yet seen from the Middle East conflict; energy costs "well hedged for the coming year".
  • CEO Alex Baldock: "We finished a good year well, with strong performance in the UK&I and the Nordics, a region that represents 40% of group sales and that grew especially strongly."
  • CEO Baldock: "Recent trading has been very solid; we've not yet seen an impact from the Middle East conflict, and our energy costs are well hedged for the coming year."
  • Baldock added the performance plus strong balance sheet "means we are well positioned to navigate any market volatility ahead," and "continue returning capital to shareholders".
  • CEO succession process "progressing well"; Baldock announced departure in March after 8 years; Sky News reported last week he is expected to become next CEO of Boots.

body:
• Swung to pretax profit of £6.6m vs loss of £37.3m year prior.
  • Operating profit jumped to £62.6m from £15.1m.
  • Revenue advanced to £1.76bn from £1.66bn (~+6%).
  • Adjusted pretax profit rose to £36m from £27m (+33%); adjusted operating profit £73.6m vs £67.6m (+9%).
  • Adjusted figures on pre-IFRS 16 basis, described as "consistent with the financial information used to inform business decisions and investment appraisals".
  • Interim dividend lifted to 1.6p from 1.4p (+14%); represents ~1/3 of full-year payout.
  • £100m buyback ~60% complete.
  • Diluted loss per share narrowed to 2.0p from 7.7p; FY25 swung to a 9.3p loss from 3.4p EPS the year prior.
  • FY26 EPS guidance reaffirmed within consensus range of 13.6p–14.8p.
  • Targeting free cash flow (pre div/buyback) above £100m in FY26, up from £80m in FY25.
  • Eyeing progress toward medium-term 20% ROCE goal (FY25: 18.7%).
  • UK, North America and Europe (~80% of sales) "remained largely unaffected by the Middle East conflict to date".
  • Weakness flagged in Asia Pacific, India, Eastern Europe, Middle East and South America "due to a drop in connecting flights as well as by lower local traffic" amid US-Israeli war on Iran.
  • Management: "Visibility to the resolution of this conflict continues to be limited and we are monitoring developments closely. However, our focus remains on what we can control... While mindful of the uncertainty surrounding the conflict and its likely duration, we are confident that the diversification of our global footprint and the resilience of our operating model will help alleviate any adverse effects on our trading".
  • Continental European rail review concluded; implementation underway — CEO Coveney expects "a smaller, more profitable and more cash generative business in this region over time".
  • CEO described H1 as "a period of resilience and progress for SSP" and added: "Given overall good trading at the start of the second half, we remain confident in our prospects for the remainder of the year".
  • Shares +2.9% to 158.90p on the day; -5.0% over the past year.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives half-year period 'Six months ended 31 March 2026 (H1 FY26)'

body:
• Acquired Electricity Distribution Engineering Services Ltd (EDES) for up to £9.0 million via wholly owned subsidiary Excalon Holdings, on a cash and debt free basis.
  • EDES (Manchester) provides high voltage engineering services to the energy distribution market for both underground and overhead lines.
  • Initial cash consideration of £6.5 million funded from existing banking facilities, based on "sustainable" EBITDA of £650,000 (implies ~10x initial / ~13.8x full earn-out multiple).
  • Additional payments of up to £2.5 million contingent on vendors remaining and profit targets being met.
  • Deal described as immediately earnings enhancing; Renew retains a strong balance sheet and continues to evaluate a pipeline of further acquisitions.
  • CEO Paul Scott: "This acquisition represents a strong strategic fit for the group, bolstering our existing, well-established position in the regulated energy market."
  • Strategic rationale: enables Renew to provide "increasingly specialist" electricity engineering services in the regulated energy market.
  • Shares up 0.8% at 895.00p on Tuesday morning.

body:
• Pretax profit down 75% to £474,000 (vs £1.9m), hit by a £2.0m property impairment (vs £396k reversal prior year).
  • Revenue £142.6m, -0.2% YoY, but +12% on an adjusted basis.
  • Adjusted pretax profit -31% to £2.2m (vs £3.2m).
  • Distribution and selling costs +4.1% to £50.5m (from £48.5m).
  • Interim dividend raised 2% to 1.00p (from 0.80p) — note prior year base looks like 0.98p rounded; company states +2%.
  • Current trading: LFL revenue +0.6% in first 7 weeks of H2, returning to positive growth.
  • "Strong growth" in online; record revenue weeks at Pro Tiler.
  • Guidance: H2 profit upside vs H1, with modest YoY profit growth in line with market expectations.
  • CEO Alex Jensen: "Topps remains a market outperformer despite a softer backdrop of weaker consumer sentiment, geopolitical uncertainty and the cumulative impact of cost inflation."
  • CEO on strategy: "self-help measures weighted towards the second half, and we are accelerating growth in digital, trade and category extensions... designed to support modest year-on-year profit growth and provide a stronger financial platform for 2027."
  • Shares -0.3% at 33.89p on the morning of release.

body:
• Shares fell 5.9% to 141.00p on the update.
  • Like-for-like revenue down 11% YoY in the four-month period; trading conditions "remained challenging".
  • 2026 had a "particularly wet start," followed by "some improvement".
  • UK domestic brick dispatches (DBT data) down 11% YoY in Q1; Forterra's brick market share in line with late-2025 levels.
  • Implemented low-to-mid single-digit brick price increases in Q1, sufficient to recover cost inflation expected at the time.
  • Subsequently hit by "significant increases in the cost of diesel, transport services and natural gas" linked to "the ongoing crisis in the Middle East".
  • Forward gas purchasing strategy provided insulation from March's higher prices; some April production rescheduled into H2 to manage costs.
  • Concrete product surcharges already implemented; new brick pricing surcharges effective 1 June.
  • Management: "Whilst events in the Middle East and the associated macro-economic and supply chain risks do not appear to have materially impacted demand for our products to date, we remain mindful as to the potential impact of higher borrowing costs on demand for housing and therefore our products".
  • H2 forecasting "has become more challenging" due to "the elevated uncertainty we presently face".
  • Range of potential outcomes for FY2026 now "greater...than previously anticipated."
  • Board "remain confident that following our recent investments in new production capacity, the group remains well placed to capitalise when our markets return to growth".

body:
• Held investor event in Hong Kong setting out new medium-term financial targets, described by analysts as "conservatively struck".
  • RoTE target of >15% in 2028, building to ~18% in 2030, vs 11.9% statutory RoTE achieved in 2025.
  • Noted 2026 medium-term targets were achieved a year early.
  • Targeting high-teens EPS CAGR and 5–7% income CAGR from 2025 to 2028 (2025 EPS: 195.4 US cents; operating income USD20.94bn).
  • Cost-to-income ratio guided to ~57% in 2028, down from 63% in 2025, supported by positive income-to-cost jaws.
  • CET1 ratio range of 13–14% (vs 14.1% in 2025).
  • Productivity drive: income per employee +~20% by 2028; corporate function roles to be cut >15% by 2030 — implies at least ~7,800 back-office job cuts from 52,271 base.
  • Scaling automation, advanced analytics and AI to streamline processes and improve efficiency.
  • Wealth & Retail Banking ambitions pulled forward from 2029 to 2028: USD200bn net new money, affluent income to reach 75% of total.
  • Dividend payout ratio of ≥30% with a progressive DPS.
  • Notable omissions: no updated 2026 NII guidance and no buyback guidance, despite market expectations post strong Q1 (JPMorgan).
  • Management quote: "We now have a more focused, streamlined and efficient organisation, positioning us strongly for the next stage of growth and to deliver our strategy at greater scale and pace."
  • Shares -0.8% at 1,907p on the day; analysts flag potential muted reaction as expectations had built up.
  • Hargreaves Lansdown (Britzman): headline goals "look broadly achievable rather than wildly ambitious"; headcount cuts will grab headlines but the bigger message is stripping out complexity to fund Asian wealth and corporate banking growth.
  • Barclays: "positive message"; targets in line, may be seen as conservative.
  • Jefferies (Dickerson): targets "conservatively struck", offering mid-teens EPS growth; 2030 >18% RoTE implies ambition "clearly higher" than 2028 guide; sees implied upside to consensus given prior plan delivered a year early.
  • JPMorgan (Abouhossein): "broadly in line"; expects "muted" near-term share price reaction.
  • Shore Capital (Greenwood, Sell): targets modestly ahead of his estimates but in line with pre-Q1 consensus; market "appears to be pricing in a significant degree of delivery… leaving limited headroom for execution hiccups"; will review Sell rating.
  • UBS: cost:income target implies 2028 costs ~USD14.2bn vs consensus USD13.7bn (56.2%); each 10% Wealth income growth adds ~50bps to group RoTE; StanChart "could prove to be a premium growth and RoTE firm, an outcome the stock is not valued for"; targets help close gap to HSBC's 17% 2026–28 RoTE target and 16–17% sector average.

body:
• Egypt's Minister of Petroleum & Mineral Resources Karim Badawi has signed the consolidated and amended concession agreement covering eight of Capricorn's existing Egyptian Western Desert concessions (50% interest, operated by Cheiron Oil & Gas).
  • Agreement is now in effect, with operational start date of 1 July.
  • Extends concession life by up to 20 years — 10-year development term plus two optional 5-year extensions.
  • Amends fiscal terms to promote investment and merges existing concessions for operational/financial efficiencies.
  • CEO Randy Neely: "We are delighted to have received the final signed approval for our consolidation agreement from Minister Badawi. We appreciate EGPC and our operating partner, Cheiron, for their collaboration throughout this process and look forward to working together under the improved terms."
  • Stock at 321.5p; up 49% over 12 months.

body:
• Barclays warns of a "second wave of likely high cost inflation and potentially waning demand, in the space of three years" for UK housebuilders.
  • Sector average operating margins already nearly halved: 11% in 2025 vs 20% in 2022.
  • Even pre-Middle East conflict, lack of HPI suggested price/cost slightly negative for 2026.
  • Previously expected margin recovery from 2027 — now forecasts lower margins YoY instead.
  • New estimates ~25% below Bloomberg consensus for 2027.
  • Base case: pricing flat, build costs +6% over next 12 months.
  • Notes stocks already reflect downgrades after ~30% share price decline in March; only potential government support prevents a "more bearish" valuation stance.
  • A new Help to Buy-style scheme "could be capable of offsetting around two-thirds of the margin pressure we expect to see over the next 12 months".
  • Barratt Redrow: downgraded to 'equal weight' from 'overweight'; PT cut to 260p from 482p (-46%).
  • Bellway: downgraded to 'equal weight' from 'overweight'; PT cut to 2,020p from 3,390p (-40%); "material" earnings risk seen at both, with full cost inflation hit expected in 2027.
  • Berkeley Group: sole 'overweight'; PT trimmed to 3,996p from 4,280p; Barclays notes "Berkeley Group is much better at protecting margin than its peers".
  • Vistry: 'underweight' maintained; PT cut to 185p from 360p (-49%); sees "material" risk to very H2-weighted 2026 guidance and ongoing financing position risk.
  • Taylor Wimpey: 'underweight' maintained; PT cut to 68p from 111p (-39%); continued risk to 2026 consensus and lack of positive margin mix.
  • Persimmon: 'equal weight'; PT cut to 1,122p from 1,514p (-26%).
  • Crest Nicholson: 'equal weight'; PT cut to 77p from 140p (-45%).
  • Share reaction muted/mixed: Barratt Redrow -0.1% at 242.80p, Bellway +0.5%, Berkeley +1.0%, Vistry -1.1%, Taylor Wimpey +1.3%, Persimmon -0.3%, Crest Nicholson +1.7%.

time_period: H1 FY2026 — six months ended 31 March 2026
body:
• Revenue £168.8m, +11% vs £152.7m prior year.
  • Pretax profit £8.4m, +56% vs £5.4m, helped by no loss from discontinued operations (vs £1.8m loss prior year).
  • Adjusted pretax profit fell to £9.4m from £11.8m, but broadly in line with Peel Hunt consensus of £9.9m.
  • Diluted EPS 2.0p vs 1.8p.
  • Interim dividend 0.74p, +5.7% vs 0.70p.
  • H2 trading: "mid-single digit revenue growth in the first six weeks of H2 2026, despite the widely reported subdued consumer sentiment driven by the Middle East conflict and its impact on discretionary spending".
  • Costs: "There have been no material cost increases to date, however, the group remains vigilant to potential inflationary pressure, particularly from imports from China and energy prices."
  • Full-year guidance maintained — revenue and adjusted pretax profit in line with market expectations, but flagged "more subdued" H2 trading.
  • Peel Hunt FY forecast unchanged at £16.5m pretax profit (vs £15.1m in FY2025); views VIC as "poised to reap the benefits from any form of consumer recovery" and notes infrastructure investment gives flexibility on whether to pass through supply chain cost increases in 2027.
  • CEO Stephnie Judge: "Despite the persistent macroeconomic uncertainty, we remain on track to deliver record full year revenue and profit expectations, and we are confident that our strategy will continue to deliver long-term value for all stakeholders."
  • Leadership: Judge succeeded founder Mark Radcliffe last month; Radcliffe moved to non-executive role.
  • Shares -2.4% to 76.60p on the day.

body:
• Climate resolution filed by 21 institutional investors led by Dutch group Follow This, calling for Shell to publish a strategy on creating shareholder value under a declining fossil fuel demand scenario, received 12.7% of investor votes (provisional).
  • Support fell from 20.6% in 2025 and a 30.5% peak in 2021, indicating waning momentum for climate shareholder activism at Shell.
  • Board, ISS and Glass Lewis all recommended voting against the resolution; Shell argued the issue is already covered in existing disclosures.
  • Chair Andrew Mackenzie on long-term outlook: "won't give you a single answer"; "where demand rises, our determination is to help meet it... Where the energy system is ready to change, we are ready to move with it."
  • CEO Wael Sawan said the resolution would "create a precedent for recurring single source scenario-based disclosure, which ultimately adds costs without helping our investors" and called it "damaging but duplicative".
  • Sawan reiterated strategy: "Shell's strategy has long been to secure oil and gas production that is advantaged on costs and carbon, offering us resilience in different demand scenarios."
  • Mackenzie confirmed hydrocarbons (oil and gas) remain central to all Shell scenarios, with decarbonisation focus on the *use* of hydrocarbons rather than exiting them.
  • Reference to Q1 record profits driven by oil price spike from the Iran war and ongoing Strait of Hormuz disruption — Sawan framed crisis as "another reminder of the fundamental importance of energy".
  • Shell has recently weakened several short- and medium-term climate targets citing transition uncertainty, but retains 2050 net zero ambition.
  • Follow This founder Mark van Baal flagged precedent risk: Shell cut dividends by 66% in 2020 when demand collapsed, warning of structural decline risk.
  • Mackenzie criticised shareholder resolutions as a debate mechanism, preferring direct engagement — signalling continued board resistance to AGM-led climate pressure.
  • Protest group Fossil Fuel London staged action outside HQ; references to pending London court cases from Philippines and Niger Delta communities over environmental impacts.
  • Shares +0.4% at 3,301.50p during the AGM session.

body:
• Shipped the 8 billionth tonne of iron ore from the Pilbara, 60 years after first shipment to Japan in August 1966.
  • Milestone cargo departed Cape Lambert aboard the *Juno Horizon* on 19 May 2026, bound for Nippon Steel Corporation.
  • Total includes all iron ore from the Robe River Joint Venture.
  • Reaffirmed pathway to mid-term Pilbara capacity of 345–360 Mtpa.
  • Advancing studies at Rhodes Ridge (Rio Tinto 50%), flagged as potentially supporting a hub of ~100 Mtpa of high-quality iron ore long term.
  • Iron Ore CEO Matthew Holcz: "As global steel demand increases, we'll continue to invest in our Pilbara operations and local communities for decades to come."
  • Largely commemorative release — no changes to guidance, volumes, pricing, or capex disclosed.

body:
• Class B Proposals (Items 4–7) and Class B director elections (Item 8) were adjourned at the 14 May 2026 Annual Meeting due to failure to achieve quorum despite significant outreach.
  • Adjourned meeting rescheduled for Tuesday, 9 June 2026 at 11:00 a.m. CT, held virtually; record date remains 16 March 2026; voting closes 10:59 p.m. CT on 8 June 2026.
  • Board reiterates recommendation to vote FOR Items 4, 5, 6 and 7.
  • Rationale: move away from requirement that director industry experience be tied to a specific exchange/trading division and from candidates individually soliciting support, broadening the nominee pool.
  • Class B election rights date back to 2000 when CME was a single futures exchange; Board argues six mandated directors across three Class B share classes is no longer necessary, and a single class of directors would better align governance with market practice.
  • Cash consideration payable if respective proposals approved: $6,200 per Class B-1 share; $4,100 per Class B-2; $2,000 per Class B-3, payable to all holders of that class as of the record date regardless of vote.
  • Previously submitted proxies remain valid unless revoked or re-voted.
  • Results to be filed with the SEC within four business days of the adjourned meeting.

body:
• Capricorn confirmed it will not make an offer for Deltic Energy, ending the possible-offer talks first disclosed in April.
  • Withdrawal follows Neo Next+ Energy Upstream UK's agreed cash bid for Deltic earlier in May at 7.7p/share, valuing Deltic equity at £7.2m, with full board recommendation.
  • Neo Next+ is the merged entity combining TotalEnergies' UK upstream business with Aberdeen-based Neo Next Energy.
  • Deltic's focus is UK southern North Sea gas exploration — not a strategic fit Capricorn chose to outbid for.
  • Capricorn itself remains in play: multiple unsolicited non-binding all-cash proposals received from Alamadiyaf al-Masiyyah for Trading LLC (Cafani Group), first disclosed in March.
  • PUSU deadline of 3 June for Alamadiyaf to announce a firm intention or walk away.
  • Capricorn shares -0.7% at 320.70p; Deltic +1.7% at 6.71p (trading below the 7.7p offer).

body:
• Octavia Morley appointed Non-executive Director effective 1 June 2026, joining the Audit and Risk, Nominations, Remuneration and Responsible Business Committees.
  • Morley will also become Senior Independent Director, replacing Mark Clare; Clare remains a Non-executive Director and continues as Chair of the Remuneration Committee.
  • Morley's current roles include SID and Remuneration Committee Chair at Currys plc and Marston's PLC, and Chair of Banner Group Limited.
  • Prior executive experience spans Asda, Laura Ashley, Woolworths, CEO/Chair of LighterLife UK, MD of Crew Clothing, and CEO of OKA Direct.
  • Chair Chris Rogers: "We are delighted to welcome Octavia to Wickes. Octavia has extensive board and retail business experience and her skillsets and insight will be of great value to our Board".
  • Laura Harricks to step down as Non-executive Director on 31 July 2026, following her appointment as Chief Customer Officer at Dunelm.

body:
• Sandro Rtveladze appointed Deputy CEO, Retail Banking of JSC TBC Bank, subject to National Bank of Georgia approval.
  • Effective 6 July 2026, or later pending regulatory approvals.
  • Will oversee products, services and channels serving the Bank's retail customers.
  • Joins from Ipoteka Bank, where as chairman of the management board he led the turnaround of OTP Group's Uzbek subsidiary.
  • Previously CEO of TBC Uzbekistan (the Group's digital bank); prior senior roles at OTP Bank Russia, Liberty Bank Georgia and Bayport Management Africa.
  • Brings 11 years of international leadership experience; appointment framed as a "homecoming" to support the Bank's growth and transformation agenda.
  • CEO Vakhtang Butskhrikidze: "We are delighted to welcome Sandro back to the Group. He brings a combination of deep retail banking expertise and strong execution capabilities across multiple markets. I am confident that his leadership will further strengthen our retail banking in Georgia."
  • JSC TBC Bank CEO George Tkhelidze: "Sandro is a highly respected banking executive with a strong track record of delivering growth, innovation and operational excellence. His extensive experience across retail makes him well positioned to lead the next stage of development of our retail banking business."

body:
• New RoTE targets: >15% in 2028 (up >3ppt vs 2025) building to ~18% in 2030.
  • 2026 medium-term financial targets achieved a year earlier than planned.
  • High-teens EPS CAGR and 5-7% income CAGR guided over 2025-2028.
  • Cost-to-income ratio (ex-notables) targeted at ~57% in 2028, down from 63% in 2025, aided by positive income-to-cost jaws.
  • Income per employee to rise ~20% by 2028; corporate functions headcount to be cut >15% by 2030.
  • CET1 ratio to operate within 13-14% range; through-the-cycle loan loss ratio of 30-35bps.
  • Dividend payout ratio of 30%+ with a progressive DPS.
  • Wealth & Retail Banking: pulling forward prior ambitions from 2029 to 2028, targeting $200bn of Net New Money and Affluent income at 75% of WRB total; claims position as third largest and fastest growing wealth manager in Asia.
  • Disproportionate investment to be directed to WRB; parts of Hong Kong retail portfolio to be migrated to digital bank Mox to build affluent pipeline.
  • CIB to invest in higher-returning Financial Institutions segment; consolidating leadership in RMB, Sustainable Finance and Islamic Finance.
  • Scaling automation, advanced analytics and AI to drive productivity.
  • CEO Bill Winters: "Our strategy is grounded in a simple belief: the world is becoming more connected, more complex and more cross-border. Clients need a bank that can help them navigate that environment with confidence - that is where Standard Chartered is distinctive. Our trusted ability to combine network and product capabilities to solve challenging cross-border problems is difficult to replicate. We are investing in the capabilities that will compound our competitive advantages and drive sustainable growth and higher quality returns over time, with clear targets in place."

body:
• Group adjusted revenue £142.6m, +11.6% YoY (driven by inclusion of £12.3m CTD revenue); on a proforma basis (CTD in both years), revenue marginally down 0.2%, but ahead of a market down c.2.5%.
  • Topps Tiles like-for-like revenue +0.1% in H1 (H1 2025: +3.0%).
  • Adjusted gross margin 53.0%, down 0.4ppts YoY due to mix from lower-margin CTD; proforma adjusted gross margin +1.6ppts to 53.0%, with Topps Tiles margin up 1.8ppts.
  • Adjusted PBT £2.2m vs £3.2m (-31.3%); flat YoY on proforma basis (CTD £1.0m loss added to H1 2025 base).
  • Statutory PBT £0.5m vs £1.9m (-73.7%), hit by store impairment, closure costs, CTD one-offs and management succession.
  • Adjusted EPS 0.83p (-25.9%); proforma adjusted EPS +13.7%.
  • Interim dividend 1.0p, up 25% YoY (H1 2025: 0.8p), in line with policy of one-third of prior full-year dividend.
  • Adjusted net debt £3.1m (H1 2025: £1.2m); £30m RCF committed to October 2027, £19.0m drawn, £11.0m undrawn; no covenant breaches.
  • Current trading: Topps Tiles LFL +0.6% in first 7 weeks of H2, a step-up from Q2 LFL of c.-2%; CTD store LFL +3.0%; Pro Tiler recording record sales weeks.
  • Outlook reiterated: "modest year-on-year profit growth in line with market expectations" with profit upside in H2 vs H1, assuming no further macro deterioration.
  • Three self-help cost programmes to deliver c.£3m H2 benefit and c.£6m annualised sustainable savings, targeting 8%+ PBT margin (Mission 365).
  • Closure of 23 loss-making stores over 9 months in 2026 communicated in January; 8 Topps Tiles stores closed in H1, leaving 289 trading stores (vs 298).
  • Trade mix rose to 74.6% of group revenue; trade ex-CTD +4.1%; Pro Tiler Tools revenue £18.4m, +c.20% YoY.
  • Online revenue rose to c.21% of group (H1 2025: c.18%); Online Pure Play sales £21.1m, +25.6%.
  • CTD loss more than halved to £0.4m (from £1.0m); on track to be profitable in H2; CTD store LFL +1.0% in H1; two new housebuilder hubs opening in H2.
  • Fired Earth (acquired Nov 2025 for £3.0m cash from administration) already profitable in first 4 months, delivering c.£1m revenue and exceeding business case; expanded to stockists in 4 countries; 120-colour paint range launched in May.
  • Tile Warehouse (£1.7m H1 revenue, loss-making) to be consolidated into Topps Tiles brand in H2; one-off costs to be treated as adjusting items; net financial impact expected accretive.
  • Capex £2.3m (H1 2025: £4.0m), focused on Trade App, CEP personalisation, new tills and ERP (Microsoft Dynamics 365 BC) upgrade.
  • Adjusted operating profit £6.1m (-1.6%); proforma adjusted operating profit +17.3%.
  • H2 cost headwind of c.£0.8m from April NLW increase; gross margins expected broadly consistent with H1.
  • Inventory days improved to 110 (H1 2025: 114); ROCE 15.0% (H1 2025: 12.9%).
  • New CFO Caroline Browne joins 26 May 2026, replacing interim CFO Rob Swales.
  • New principal risk added for heightened competitive threat; Global Supply Chain and Macro risks updated for Middle East conflict exposure.
  • CEO Alex Jensen: "Topps remains a market outperformer despite a softer backdrop of weaker consumer sentiment, geopolitical uncertainty and the cumulative impact of cost inflation."
  • CEO on strategy: "These actions are designed to support modest year on year profit growth and provide a stronger financial platform for 2027, positioning the Group for long-term sustainable profit growth."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives half-year period '26 weeks ended 28 March 2026 (H1 2026)'

body:
• Half year results delayed from previously scheduled date to 16 July 2026.
  • Delay tied to ongoing covenant relaxation discussions with lenders, first flagged in the 21 April 2026 trading update.
  • Discussions described as "progressing constructively" and expected to conclude by mid-July 2026.
  • Postponement allows time for orderly completion of the covenant reset process and for auditors to complete their review.

body:
• Executive management change at Georgian banking subsidiary JSC Bank of Georgia, effective 1 July 2026.
  • Valerian Gabunia (currently Deputy Head of SME Banking Direction) promoted to lead the Premium Banking Direction.
  • Eter Iremadze, Deputy CEO overseeing Premium Banking since 2021, will step down on the same date.
  • Gabunia joined the SME team in 2023 and led a transformation of the Medium Enterprise segment.
  • CEO Archil Gachechiladze on Gabunia: "He is an experienced and results-oriented banker, with a distinguished career in various executive roles across retail and premium banking in Georgia."
  • CEO on Iremadze's legacy: credited with "building the nation's leading mass affluent franchise."
  • No financial guidance, strategic, or capital impact disclosed.

body:
• FY26 adjusted PBT guidance unchanged at £60m–£65m.
  • Core meat and fresh prepared food businesses trading well; East region benefiting from momentum in Australia/New Zealand plus fresh prepared food growth in Central Europe.
  • West region volumes slightly higher YoY, with strong Easter run-up.
  • Seafood, vegetarian and vegan businesses remain challenging as expected; focus on improving performance to increase "strategic optionality."
  • Seachill: cost reduction plans being implemented against continuing volume challenges.
  • Foppen: now exporting to the US by sea rather than air; awaiting FDA feedback on submission to remove export restrictions from Greece facility.
  • Dalco: still seeking opportunities to fill spare capacity.
  • Commercial partnership with Tesco UK extended.
  • Canada project on track for full launch in 2027, with bacon added to scope.
  • Saudi Arabia facility still expected to commence operations in H2 2026.
  • Finalising plans to increase capacity in Poland for fresh prepared foods growth.
  • Capex guidance ~£100m; net debt expected to increase over the year as a result.
  • Management "remain cautious on the broader inflationary environment and mindful of the potential impacts of the situation in the Middle East."
  • Interim results for 26 weeks ending 28 June 2026 to be announced on 3 September 2026.

body:
• Like-for-like revenue down 11% YoY (adjusted for closure of non-core businesses in H2 2025); trading conditions remain challenging though improving after a very wet start to the year.
  • Q1 domestic industry brick despatches (BEIS data) also down 11% YoY; Forterra's brick market share in line with late-2025 levels.
  • Low-to-mid single-digit brick price increases successfully implemented in Q1, sufficient to recover previously anticipated cost inflation.
  • Middle East crisis driving additional cost inflation via higher diesel, transport and natural gas costs.
  • Gas hedging mitigates impact: ~80% of remainder-of-year gas requirements secured at pre-crisis prices.
  • Some April production rescheduled into H2 to manage gas costs, increasing H2 weighting of full-year result (assuming market conditions allow recovery).
  • Surcharges implemented on concrete products; brick pricing surcharges announced effective 1 June 2026.
  • No material demand impact from Middle East events observed to date, but management flags risk from higher borrowing costs on housing demand.
  • Wider range of potential full-year outcomes than previously anticipated due to elevated uncertainty — effectively a guidance caveat.
  • Board: "the Group remains well placed to capitalise when our markets return to growth" following recent investments in new production capacity.
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with half-year financial figures

body:
• Capricorn confirms it will NOT make an offer for Deltic Energy plc, following its 22 April 2026 possible offer announcement and NEO NEXT+ Energy Upstream UK's Rule 2.7 firm offer announcement on 7 May 2026.
  • Statement made under Rule 2.8 of the Takeover Code, binding Capricorn (and concert parties) to associated restrictions.
  • Reservation rights to set aside Rule 2.8 restrictions if: (i) Deltic board consents; (ii) a third party announces a firm offer — specifically naming Petrogas International E&P Coöperatief U.A. (subsidiary of Mohammed Al Barwani LLC) or Blue Concept Hld AS as potential triggers; (iii) Deltic announces a Rule 9 waiver or reverse takeover; or (iv) a material change of circumstances determined by the Panel.
  • Effectively ends Capricorn's pursuit of Deltic, leaving NEO NEXT+ as the standing bidder; flagged third-party names hint at a potentially contested situation around Deltic's southern North Sea gas assets.
  • Canaccord Genuity continues as Capricorn's financial adviser and corporate broker.
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets reference an offer approach: 'possible offer'

body:
• Revenue +10.5% to £168.8m (H1 2025: £152.7m), outperforming the wider RMI market.
  • Retail revenue (ex-MFI) +9.2% to £166.7m; order volume +12% to record 609k; AOV -3% to £274 (mix shift to tiles & flooring-only orders).
  • Tiles and flooring revenue +84% to £14.0m (8% of revenue vs 5%); trade revenue +8% to £39.0m (23% of revenue vs 24%).
  • Gross profit +9.5% to £83.9m; gross margin -50bps to 49.7% (ex-MFI margin held flat at 50.2%) — impacted by Extended Producer Responsibility tax (effective 1 April 2025) and category mix; third-party product gross margin fell to 29% (H1 2025: 33%).
  • Adjusted EBITDA +1.3% to £15.4m; margin -90bps to 9.1% due to planned MFI investment. Ex-MFI adjusted EBITDA +11.8% to £17.0m.
  • Operating profit +44.1% to £9.8m (prior period had £4.7m of exceptionals from warehouse transformation and Victoria Plum closure).
  • Adjusted PBT -20.3% to £9.4m; adjusted PBT margin -2ppt to 5.6% — reflects MFI investment and full six months of new DC lease cost vs three months in H1 2025.
  • Adjusted diluted EPS -17.9% to 2.3p (H1 2025: 2.8p).
  • Free cash flow flat at £12.9m; operating cash conversion 84% (vs 88%).
  • Net cash £21.2m vs £10.9m (+94.5%); £30m HSBC RCF undrawn; balance sheet debt-free ex-leases.
  • Interim dividend +5.7% to 0.74p (£2.4m); policy dividend cover 2.25x–3.00x.
  • Marketing spend reduced to 28.0% of retail revenue (-80bps); brand awareness improved to 73% (vs 72%).
  • MFI: SKUs expanded from 600 at July 2025 launch to 5,500 by period-end; revenue £0.5m; gross margin 30–40% (mostly UK-sourced); adjusted EBITDA loss £1.6m; Trustpilot 4.7.
  • M&A: acquired Sovereign Transport Services (Merseyside haulage) on 31 January 2026 for £2.3m net of cash (£6.1m total consideration incl. £3.8m cash acquired); £3.4m goodwill recognised; contributed £1.6m haulage revenue post-acquisition.
  • New 2-year title/shirt sponsorship of Bristol City FC from 2026/27 season; renewed Bolton Wanderers and World Snooker UK Championship sponsorships.
  • Productivity: revenue per average FTE rose to £449k (rolling 12m) vs £428k; FTE +2% to 722 vs 7% volume growth.
  • AI rollout: launched 'Ask Vic' agentic chat support in March 2026; trade next-day delivery cut-off extended to 17:00.
  • Considering focused international expansion (Republic of Ireland already growing via UK domain) and vertical integration / expansion-category M&A.
  • Current trading & outlook: H2 first six weeks delivered mid-single digit revenue growth despite subdued consumer sentiment from Middle East conflict; no material cost increases to date but vigilant on China imports and energy prices.
  • FY26 revenue and adjusted PBT expected in line with market consensus (compiled consensus: revenue £329.5m; adjusted PBT £21.8m).
  • CEO Stephnie Judge (first interim results): "we remain on track to deliver record full year revenue and profit expectations, and we are confident that our strategy will continue to deliver long-term value for all stakeholders."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives half-year period 'Six months ended 31 March 2026 (H1 2026)'

body:
• Wholly owned subsidiary Excalon Holdings has acquired Electricity Distribution Engineering Services Ltd (EDES) for total consideration of up to £9.0m on a cash and debt free basis.
  • Initial cash consideration of £6.5m funded from existing banking facilities, based on a sustainable EBITDA of £650k (implies ~10x EBITDA on upfront, ~13.8x at max earn-out).
  • Additional consideration of up to £2.5m contingent on vendor retention and specific profit targets.
  • EDES provides high voltage engineering design services for underground and overhead lines in the energy distribution market.
  • Strategic rationale: expands design/delivery capability and targets the c.£50bn–£60bn of committed network investment over RIIO-2 in power transmission and distribution.
  • Joins recently acquired Emerald Power and PWR-X within Excalon Holdings, enabling a full turnkey solution from design through delivery.
  • Immediately earnings enhancing; Group retains a strong balance sheet and continues to evaluate a further M&A pipeline.
  • CEO Paul Scott: "This acquisition represents a strong strategic fit for the Group, bolstering our existing, well-established position in the regulated energy market."
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with financial performance figures

time_period: Full year ending 2 May 2026 (and 16-week post-Peak period)
body:
• Full year adjusted PBT expected to be around £191m, +18% YoY, ahead of prior £180-190m guidance.
  • Group like-for-like sales +4% for both the 16-week post-Peak period and the full year.
  • LFL by region/period: UK&I +3% FY (+4% post-Peak); Nordics +6% FY (+12% at Peak, +8% H2); Group +5% H2.
  • £74m of cash returned to shareholders during the year.
  • Year-end net cash of more than £170m.
  • UK&I adjusted EBIT expected to grow slightly YoY; sales growth and stable gross margin offset cost headwinds; market share gains plus growth in Services, B2B and new categories.
  • iD Mobile subscribers +18% YoY to 2.6m.
  • Nordics adjusted EBIT showing strong YoY growth, driven by market share gains and very strong Kitchens and computing components; gross margin broadly stable, costs tightly controlled.
  • Nordics now represents 40% of Group sales.
  • Process to appoint a new Group CEO progressing well (succession for Alex Baldock).
  • CEO Alex Baldock: "Recent trading has been very solid; we've not yet seen an impact from the Middle East conflict, and our energy costs are well hedged for the coming year."
  • CEO: "This performance, combined with our strong balance sheet, means we are well positioned to navigate any market volatility ahead, tap into exciting growth opportunities and continue returning capital to shareholders."
  • Free cash flow increased again YoY.
  • Full year results scheduled for 2 July 2026.

time_period: Q1 2026 (three months ended 31 March 2026) + first 7 weeks of Q2 2026
body:
• 2026 guidance upgraded: organic total revenue growth now 10-15% on 2025 base of ~£1,100m (vs prior high single-digits), EBITDA margins sustained mid-40s%.
  • Medium-term outlook upgraded: at least 10% organic total revenue CAGR beyond 2026, mid-40s% EBITDA margins.
  • Net interest income guidance raised to £110-120m (from ~£110m) on higher rate expectations.
  • CEO Breon Corcoran: "We have delivered a strong first quarter... With commercial momentum accelerating, we are upgrading our guidance for 2026 and medium-term outlook."
  • Q1 reported total revenue £339.9m, +21% YoY; organic total revenue £331.2m, +19% YoY, +15% QoQ.
  • Net trading revenue £306.5m organic, +25% YoY, +17% QoQ.
  • OTC derivatives net trading revenue +26% YoY (£250.6m), aided by pricing optimisation and elevated commodity volatility.
  • Exchange traded derivatives (tastytrade) +14% YoY in USD / +7% in GBP; flat QoQ in USD.
  • Organic stock trading & investments +38% YoY; spot crypto £2.4m (vs £0.1m).
  • Net interest income £24.7m organic, -22% YoY, reflecting lower rates and greater customer pass-through.
  • First trades +63% YoY organic (+65% QoQ); active customers +12% YoY organic — fifth consecutive quarter of sequential growth.
  • Marketing payback remains <6 months.
  • Total Group AuA surpassed £20bn in April (£20.7bn at 30 April vs £19.3bn at 31 March, +57% reported / +20% organic).
  • Freetrade AuA £3.6bn at Q1 end (+54% YoY), crossed £4.0bn in April; net flows annualising at 58% of opening AuA; Q1 revenue £6.5m down QoQ as customers rotated into UK assets/funds/cash, hitting FX fees.
  • UK organic stock trading AuA £3.7bn (+26% YoY), net inflows annualising >19%.
  • Independent Reserve (acquired 30 Jan 2026) contributed £2.1m revenue over two months — softer crypto market conditions.
  • Spot crypto launched on FCA-licensed UK platform with 50+ new coins; launched in Australia (March), and France & Singapore in Q2.
  • Q2 QTD: organic active customer growth accelerating beyond 12% YoY; OTC customer income retention in mid-80s%.
  • Upgraded IG-branded UK stock trading proposition launching later in May; next-gen unified multi-product proposition launches summer (UK first).
  • Institutional white-label offering launched in May with first partner onboarded.
  • Strategic review ongoing (announced 19 March 2026), evaluating acquisitions, domicile/listing venues, and potential combinations of parts of the Group; outcome at Strategy Update in autumn 2026.
  • £125m share buyback in two £62.5m tranches; first tranche running 1 April – 30 September 2026; 987,160 shares bought for £14.9m as of 15 May 2026.
  • Interim results scheduled for 31 July 2026.
period_detail: Q1 2026 (three months ended 31 March 2026) + first 7 weeks of Q2 2026

body:
• Revenue £1,763m, +6.2% at both actual and constant FX rates.
  • Like-for-like revenue growth: +5.0% (Q1 +4.6%, Q2 +5.4%); net contract gains +2.0%; (0.8)% drag from German MSA exit and AAHL JV deconsolidation.
  • Underlying pre-IFRS 16 operating profit £50m, +17.8% constant currency, +9.3% actual FX; margin 2.8%, +30bps cc / +10bps actual.
  • Statutory operating profit £63m vs £15m LY (+320%), reflecting lower non-underlying charge (£11m vs £52.5m).
  • Pre-IFRS 16 EPS 1.1p (LY: -0.4p loss); statutory loss per share (2.0)p vs (7.7)p LY.
  • Free cash outflow £(176)m pre-dividend/buyback (LY: £(143)m), after £(124)m seasonal/one-off working capital outflow and £93m capex (LY £130m).
  • Net debt (pre-IFRS 16) £820m; leverage 2.2x (vs 1.6x at FY25); expected at lower end of 1.5–2.0x target by FY.
  • Interim dividend 1.6p (+14% vs 1.4p LY); payout ratio target 30–40% of underlying pre-IFRS 16 EPS maintained.
  • £100m buyback c.60% complete (£57m by 15 May); 4% of share capital repurchased; £42.9m bought in H1.
  • FY26 EPS guidance reaffirmed within consensus range of 13.6–14.8p (post-buyback); FCF (pre-div/buyback) still expected >£100m; further ROCE progress towards medium-term 20% target.
  • H2 trading softer: LFL +3% in first 6 weeks (vs +5% in Q1 & Q2), driven by Middle East conflict impact.
  • Regional H2-start LFL: UK&I +11% (M&S cyber comp benefit), N. America +3%, C. Europe +1%, APAC & EEME (4)% with Gulf operations running at ~60% of usual capacity.
  • Asia PAC & EEME (ex-Gulf, c.14% of sales) LFL fell from +14% in March to 0% in first 6 weeks of H2.
  • Continental European Rail review concluded: intention to exit ~one third of c.330 unit estate; expected c.50% reduction in future Continental European Rail capex; benefits largely from FY27, initially offset by one-off implementation costs.
  • Continental Europe on track for >3.0% operating margin in FY26 (from 2.2% in FY25); H1 margin improvement +70bps cc; medium-term target raised confidence on >5% regional margin.
  • £30m annualised overhead savings programme (£5m in FY25, £25m in FY26) progressing.
  • FY26 capex guidance <£200m (FY25: £212m).
  • Contract renegotiation at major Paris rail station triggered £11.3m one-off settlement (non-underlying).
  • Board changes: Andrew Martin appointed Chair from 1 June 2026; Candace McGraw joins as NED from 1 June 2026.
  • TFS (Indian subsidiary) trading at ~£1.1bn equity value; SSP holds 50.01%; must reduce to ≤75% by July 2028 to meet India free-float rules; Board exploring value-realisation options, timing market-dependent.
  • New financing: EUR180m term loan drawn 17 Oct 2025 (replacing EUR176m); additional EUR150m term loan drawn 30 April 2026 used to part-repay RCF.
  • Available liquidity £369.8m (£240.7m cash + £129.1m undrawn facilities).
  • CEO Patrick Coveney: "This has been a period of resilience and progress for SSP… our strategy and priorities are unchanged… we remain confident in our prospects for the remainder of the year."
  • Downside risk flagged: resumption of Middle East conflict, aviation fuel availability decline, or softer consumer travel sentiment would impact FY performance.

body:
• Appointed Candace McGraw as Independent Non-Executive Director, effective 1 June 2026; will join the Audit and Nomination Committees.
  • McGraw brings 30+ years in global aviation, including 15 years as CEO of Cincinnati/Northern Kentucky International Airport (named Best Regional Airport in North America by SkyTrax seven times during her tenure).
  • Previously Chair of the World Governing Board of Airports Council International (representing ~90% of the world's commercial airports), stepping down December 2025.
  • Other roles: NED of Toronto-listed Flagship Communities REIT, Advisory Member of Fifth Third Bank (Cincinnati), and board positions with Kentucky Community and Technical College System and Kentucky Cabinet for Economic Development.
  • Interim Chair Carolyn Bradley: "Candace's international travel perspective, including her insights into the North American market, will be invaluable to the team, as we deliver our 'Focus 26' agenda and pursue opportunities to create shareholder value."
  • Appointment subject to separate resolution at SSP's 2027 AGM.

body:
• Anglo American agreed to sell its Australian steelmaking coal portfolio to Dhilmar Ltd for up to USD 3.88 billion in cash.
  • Structure: USD 2.3 billion upfront plus a price-linked earnout of up to USD 1.58 billion.
  • Deal expected to complete by Q1 2027; proceeds to be used to reduce net debt.
  • CEO Duncan Wanblad: "This agreement represents another major step in the simplification of our portfolio ahead of completing our merger with Teck. Through this transaction, we will complete our exit from steelmaking coal."
  • Portfolio sold includes 88% in Moranbah North & Grosvenor JVs, 70% in Capcoal JV, 86% in Roper Creek JV, 51% in Dawson/Dawson South/Theodore South JVs, and 50% in Moranbah South JV.
  • Dhilmar is a privately-held miner whose assets include the Eleonore gold mine in Canada, acquired from Newmont last year.
  • Arbitration with Peabody Energy continues over the November 2024 agreement to acquire the same portfolio; Anglo remains confident the Moranbah North incident did not constitute a material adverse change.
  • Moranbah North mine in Queensland was closed following an underground fire in March 2025 — the event Peabody relied on to terminate.
  • Separately, a Chile Second Environmental Tribunal ruling appears to overturn the 2021 environmental authorisation at the Collahuasi copper mine (Anglo 44%, Glencore 44%, Mitsui 12%).
  • Both owners say ruling is "limited to two specific aspects relating to analysis on the effects on a local community and on the marine environment" and neither expects "any immediate impact on production".
  • Market reaction: Anglo American -1.9% to 3,760p, Glencore -1.3% to 567.10p, vs FTSE 100 marginally higher.

body:
• Appoints Manus Costello as Group CFO (pending regulatory approval); takes on interim CFO role with immediate effect.
  • Costello brings 25 years of equity research experience, joined Standard Chartered two years ago, and will report directly to CEO Bill Winters.
  • Replaces Peter Burrill, who had been interim CFO since February following the departure of Diego De Giorgi.
  • Tanuj Kapilashrami appointed Chief Operating Officer, effective immediately; with the group since 2017 and on the management team since 2021.
  • CEO Bill Winters: "I am delighted that Manus has agreed to take on the role of group chief financial officer. Since joining Standard Chartered two years ago, he has made a significant contribution to the group's strategic positioning and engagement of stakeholders, while also bringing strong rigour and an entrepreneurial mindset to the role."
  • Shares down 0.4% Monday morning at 1,881.00p; up 63% over 12 months.

body:
• Acquiring 75% of Bharti Life Insurance from Bharti Life Ventures and 360 ONE Asset Management to reposition Indian operations.
  • Initial cash consideration of ~USD 389 million on completion, funded from existing resources, plus conditional payment of up to USD 78 million.
  • No change to the USD 7 billion shareholder return plan for 2024–2027.
  • Holdco cash and short-term investments of USD 4.3bn and leverage ratio of 13% as of 31 December.
  • Free surplus ratio stood at 211% at end-December.
  • Post-completion, Indian footprint will include majority-owned Bharti Life and Prudential HCL Health Insurance, plus 35% in ICICI Prudential AMC and 22% in ICICI Prudential Life.
  • Plans to cut ICICI Prudential Life stake to under 10% to meet regulatory criteria; working with authorities on divestment timeline.
  • Divestment proceeds to part-fund future growth, with residual capital boosting free surplus.
  • Eyeing strategic distribution agreements with Bharti Airtel and 360 ONE alongside the Bharti Life deal.
  • Separate standalone majority-owned India health insurance business expected operational in 2026, pending regulatory clearance.
  • CEO Anil Wadhwani: "By acquiring a controlling stake in Bharti Life, we are bringing together Prudential's nearly 180 years of global insurance expertise and Bharti's strong and growing local presence to serve the savings and protection needs of Indian consumers."
  • On ICICI: "Our joint partnership with the ICICI group of companies, has, for many decades, provided high-quality financial services solutions in India. We deeply appreciate this partnership and value our relationship with them."
  • Rationale cited: India has "large, unmet demand for savings and protection with a low penetration of life insurance."
  • Shares fell 0.8% to 1,125.50p on Monday morning.

body:
• Corvex Management (~7% stake) publicly demanded Whitbread put itself up for sale, calling it the only "credible" path to unlock value.
  • Activist labelled the status quo "untenable" and said the need for "meaningful strategic and structural reform had become unignorable".
  • Corvex slammed the new 5-year plan (announced April), claiming it "doubled down on many of the core value-destructive components we opposed in the original plan."
  • Argued neither market conditions nor underlying performance "justified" the "ambitious" growth targets, particularly funding "highly uncertain" growth by selling the "most valuable freehold assets".
  • Notes shares are at a 13-year low, trading at a fraction of UK freehold asset value alone; stock down 19% over 12 months.
  • Cites "double-digit negative returns across every reasonable investment horizon – one, three, five, and ten years".
  • Demands: board immediately retain an independent investment bank and publicly commit to a comprehensive sale process.
  • Demands immediate suspension of all non-AGP growth capex and all proposed sale-leasebacks during any process, warning otherwise "these actions would permanently impair the optionality and asset base that a transaction would seek to monetize."
  • Proposes free cash flow be redirected to an accelerated share repurchase program as "a far more accretive use of capital at current valuations."
  • Context — April 5-year plan targets by FY2031: £275m incremental adjusted pretax profit, net capex cut by £1bn, growth funded by £1.5bn freehold disposals, +500bps ROCE, and £2bn free cash flow for shareholder returns.
  • Room target: 96,000 by FY2031 (vs ~86,600 now), long-term aspiration 125,000.
  • Shares traded 0.3% higher at 2,309.50p intraday on the letter's release.

body:
• Pipeline of occupier sales and lettings increased, though residential market softness persists.
  • I&L portfolio described as "well positioned" with demand "holding up".
  • Record 4.0 million sq ft of development-ready land for near-term delivery.
  • Progressing 1.8 million sq ft of letting and land sale negotiations, up from 1.6 million sq ft at year-end (+12.5%).
  • Completed on 155 residential plots; ~900 further plots conditionally exchanged or in legals.
  • 63% of 2026 sales pipeline completed, conditionally exchanged or in legals, with completions back-end weighted.
  • CEO Shillaw: "We continue to see an increase in our pipeline of occupier sales and lettings, albeit the softness in the residential market and the protracted transaction timelines in I&L that we saw in 2025 have persisted, owing to both geopolitical and UK fiscal and political pressures."
  • Flagging supply-chain cost inflation from Middle East war: "We are starting to see some supply-chain cost inflation as a result of the war in the Middle East - largely from higher fuel costs affecting civils and material production, and we are focused on managing any impact and progressing our 35 million sq ft pipeline to drive long term value."
  • Shillaw on resilience: "our low leverage and significant optionality over the timing of development and disposals give the board confidence in Harworth's ability to navigate short‑term uncertainty and deliver on our roadmap."
  • Board change: Tony Quinlan appointed independent NED effective 1 June; currently chair of NextEnergy Solar Fund and SID at Costain and Hill & Smith.
  • Shares +0.4% at 128.70p in early trading (though closed -2.65% at 124.80p).
