body:
• Appoints Manus Costello as Group CFO, pending regulatory approval; becomes interim CFO with immediate effect, reporting directly to CEO Bill Winters.
  • Costello brings 25 years of equity research experience and joined Standard Chartered two years ago.
  • Takes over from Peter Burrill, who had been interim CFO since February following the step-down of Diego De Giorgi.
  • Tanuj Kapilashrami appointed Chief Operating Officer, effective immediately; joined the group in 2017 and has been on the management team since 2019 (corrected from prior release).
  • CEO Bill Winters quote: "I am delighted that Manus has agreed to take on the role of group chief financial officer. Since joining Standard Chartered two years ago, he has made a significant contribution to the group's strategic positioning and engagement of stakeholders, while also bringing strong rigour and an entrepreneurial mindset to the role."
  • Shares up 1.1% Monday afternoon; stock up 65% over 12 months.

body:
• Opened Cypress Trails, a new master-planned community in Hockley (northwest Houston corridor near Cypress, Texas), zoned to Waller ISD.
  • Community will deliver more than 500 new homes, with two parks (The Hideaway, 2.1-acre hub, and Songbird Park) coming soon.
  • Pricing: CompleteHome™ series from mid-$200s; CompleteHome Plus™ from mid-$300s; homes start from the $240s.
  • Floor plans range from two-bedroom villas at 1,186 sq ft up to five-bedroom layouts of 2,916 sq ft.
  • Amenities include pickleball courts, dog park, playgrounds, walking trails, fitness stations, pavilion; management highlights low tax rate and affordable HOA dues.
  • Pat Vedra (RVP Land Acquisitions & Development): "This location offers homebuyers the opportunity to enjoy a welcoming neighborhood in an area where demand continues to grow."
  • Zach Walden (VP Operations): "We are excited to return to Hockley, where we've experienced tremendous success and strong demand from homebuyers."
  • Context: LGI operates across 36 markets in 21 states and has closed over 80,000 homes since 2003.

body:
• Acquiring 75% controlling stake in Bharti Life Insurance from Bharti Life Ventures and 360 ONE Asset Management.
  • Initial cash consideration of ~USD389 million on completion, funded from existing resources, plus conditional payment of up to USD78 million.
  • Panmure Liberum's Abid Hussain: deal implies ~1.5x trailing EV multiple on a business growing APE sales >40%.
  • No change to USD7 billion shareholder return plan for 2024–2027.
  • Holdco cash and short-term investments of USD4.3 billion and leverage ratio of 13% as at 31 December.
  • Free surplus ratio stood at 211% at end-December.
  • Post-completion Indian footprint: majority-owned Bharti Life and Prudential HCL Health Insurance; plus 35% in ICICI Prudential AMC and 22% in ICICI Prudential Life.
  • Will reduce ICICI Prudential Life stake to below 10% to meet regulatory criteria; working with authorities on divestment timeline.
  • Current 22% ICICI Prudential Life stake valued at ~USD1.8 billion (Panmure), more than funding the Bharti acquisition.
  • Divestment proceeds earmarked for funding future business growth, with residual capital boosting free surplus.
  • Plans strategic distribution agreements with Bharti Airtel and 360 ONE.
  • Separately, standalone majority-owned health insurance business in India expected operational in 2026, pending regulatory clearance.
  • CEO Anil Wadhwani: "By acquiring a controlling stake in Bharti Life, we are bringing together Prudential's nearly 180 years of global insurance expertise and Bharti's strong and growing local presence to serve the savings and protection needs of Indian consumers."
  • Wadhwani on ICICI: "Our joint partnership with the ICICI group of companies, has, for many decades, provided high-quality financial services solutions in India. We deeply appreciate this partnership and value our relationship with them."
  • Hussain: "We think [the Bharti Life acquisition] is a good deal in a country with a supportive demographic, rising middle classes, GDP growth prospects and fits the wider group's strategy."
  • Hussain: "It is also good to see management continue to allocate capital more effectively across the group."
  • Panmure Liberum reiterates 'buy' rating, target price 1,610p.
  • Shares up 0.9% at 1,145.50p Monday afternoon.

body:
• Appoints Neil Miller as new CFO, effective Tuesday next week; previously group CFO of security solutions group Praesidiad.
  • Current CFO Andrew Mallows steps down from the board on the same date after taking the role in June 2024, moving into the newly created role of group delivery director.
  • Company highlights progress on its One Franchise Brands strategic initiative since 2024, aimed at improving efficiency and expanding revenue streams.
  • Current priority is completing integration, requiring "a clear focus on finance and on commercially focused delivery."
  • Executive Chair Stephen Hemsley: "One of Franchise Brands' key strategic priorities is to capitalise on the two-year long, multi-million-pound investment we have made in IT and integrating our three significant acquisitions."
  • Hemsley adds the new CFO brings "a strong background in value creation and finance transformation", while Mallows will "drive the more commercial benefits of group-wide integration".
  • Hemsley: "Neil and Andrew will play a key role in completing and realising the benefits of our B2B franchise platform which will be the cornerstone of our future organic and acquisitive growth."

body:
• Revenue £209.1m, +2% (FY25: £204.5m); store revenue £207.6m, +2%.
  • Like-for-like store revenue £207.3m, +2%, driven entirely by rent (occupancy a drag).
  • Average achieved net rent per sq ft +4% to £35.98; closing net rent +3% to £36.28.
  • Closing occupancy 74.2%, down 4.5ppts (LfL down 1.7ppts to 77.0%); driven primarily by four new store openings adding 0.3m sq ft.
  • Post period-end, LfL occupancy gap has narrowed to 0.6ppts from 1.7ppts — key inflection management is flagging.
  • Store EBITDA £146.5m, +2%; margin flat at 70.5% despite occupancy headwind.
  • LfL operating cost inflation just 0.3% (vs +7% prior year); guiding to 2-3% LfL cost inflation in FY27 after absorbing a 9% rise in LfL property rates.
  • Adjusted PBT £117.5m, +2%; adjusted EPS 59.0p, +2%.
  • Statutory PBT £126.2m, -38% (FY25: £203.9m), purely due to lower revaluation surplus (£7.6m vs £79.7m).
  • Full-year dividend 47.2p, +2% (final 23.4p); entirely PID.
  • Net debt £476.7m (vs £388.7m), net debt/EBITDA 3.7x (up from 3.1x) — towards top of 3-4x target range.
  • Average cost of drawn debt down to 4.7% from 5.0% (peak 5.7% Sept 2023); 44% of drawn debt fixed.
  • Undrawn committed facilities of £83m; post period-end put in place a $225m Pricoa shelf facility (7-15yr fixed sterling notes, drawable in £10m tranches).
  • Interest cover 6.1x (flat); comfortably within covenants.
  • Capex £100.8m (vs £58.3m) on new store construction; capital commitments £54.3m at year-end.
  • Four new freehold stores opened: Staines (Jul'25), Queensbury (Oct'25), Slough Bath Road (Feb'26), Wembley (Mar'26). Queensbury, Slough Bath Road and Wembley currently loss-making at EBITDA level.
  • Pipeline: 12 development sites (~0.9m sq ft, 13% of MLA); 9 of 12 now have planning consent. Pipeline cost to complete £231m over 4 years, projected 8.3% return on capital, £39m NOI.
  • Property valuation £3,111m; like-for-like open store revaluation +0.5% (£15.7m uplift); IPUC revaluation deficit £8.1m on higher build costs. Exit cap rate unchanged at 5.5%.
  • Adjusted NAV 1,370.4p/share (vs 1,355.6p), +1%.
  • CEO transition: Jim Gibson to retire as CEO on 20 July 2026; COO John Hunter to succeed. Helen Gordon (CEO of Grainger) to join as SID from 1 June 2027.
  • Increased stake in Doncaster Security Operations Centre (DSOC) to 74% (from 34%) on 1 August 2025; now equity-accounted as JV.
  • Business customers now 36% of occupied space / 32% of revenue; first business occupancy growth (+16,000 sq ft H2) since early 2022.
  • Move-in mix: 40% home movers (23% owners, 17% renters); H1 was subdued post-Stamp Duty pull-forward, H2 improved after four BoE rate cuts in 2025.
  • Energy costs only 1.2% of revenue; solar capacity +13% to 9.6MW across 85 stores; energy pricing fixed to September 2026.
  • New customers paid 0.7% less than prior-year move-ins and 6.9% less than move-outs — implying revenue tailwind if occupancy gap closes.
  • Chairman Nicholas Vetch on outlook: "We recognise that the current operating environment presents considerable challenges for businesses across the sector, and we do not underestimate the headwinds that lie ahead."
  • CEO commentary noted activity stalled in March following "the start of the Iran war"; macro/geopolitical risks (Iran conflict, mortgage rates, US tariffs) explicitly flagged as drivers of softer activity in April/May 2025 and March 2026.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Year ended 31 March 2026'

body:
• Activist Corvex Management (~7% stake) publicly urged Whitbread to put itself up for sale, calling the status quo "untenable" and demanding the board retain an independent investment bank for a "rigorous and comprehensive sale process".
  • Whitbread pushed back, defending its new 5-Year Plan: spokesman said it is "focused on driving stronger returns for all our shareholders" and the plan followed "a rigorous review of our options to maximise value creation".
  • Company reiterated plan target of £2 billion of free cash flow for shareholder returns by FY31.
  • Corvex claims the plan "doubled down on many of the core value-destructive components we opposed in the original plan" and that neither market conditions nor underlying performance "justified" the "ambitious" growth targets.
  • Corvex flagged Whitbread is trading at a 13-year low and at a fraction of UK freehold asset value alone, with double-digit negative returns over 1, 3, 5 and 10-year horizons.
  • Corvex demands: immediate suspension of all non-Accelerating Growth Plan growth capex and all proposed sale-leasebacks, with free cash flow instead redirected to an accelerated share repurchase programme.
  • Reminder of April 5-Year Plan metrics: £275m incremental adjusted pretax profit by FY31, net capex cut by £1bn, growth funded by £1.5bn freehold disposals, ROCE +500bps.
  • Room growth target: 96,000 rooms by FY31 (from ~86,600), longer-term aspiration of 125,000.
  • Shares closed +2.3% at 2,354p on Monday but remain down 17% over 12 months.

body:
• Agreed to acquire a 75% controlling stake in Bharti Life Insurance from Bharti Life Ventures and 360 ONE Asset Management, repositioning Prudential's India operations.
  • Initial cash consideration of ₹3,500 crore (c. $389 million) payable on completion, funded from existing resources.
  • Potential additional consideration of up to ₹700 crore (c. $78 million) contingent on certain conditions.
  • Completion subject to regulatory approvals; regulators expected to require Prudential to cut its stake in ICICI Prudential Life Insurance to under 10% (from current 22%).
  • Prudential will engage with regulators to seek "an appropriate timeframe for the divestment that may be required, in the interests of its shareholders."
  • Part of any ICICIPru Life divestment proceeds to fund future growth; residual capital to contribute to free surplus.
  • Post-completion India footprint: majority-owned Bharti Life and Prudential HCL Health Insurance, plus minority stakes of 35% in ICICI Prudential AMC and (reduced) holding in ICICIPru Life.
  • Standalone majority-owned health insurance operations in India expected to commence during 2026 subject to regulatory approval.
  • Bharti Life to pursue strategic distribution agreements with Bharti Airtel and 360 ONE.
  • Bharti Life FY26 (year-ended 31 March 2026) New Business Premium grew 44% YoY to ₹1,069 crore, c.3x industry growth.
  • Bharti Life embedded value of ₹3,102 crore (c. $345 million) as at 30 September 2025 — implying transaction values 75% stake at a modest premium to proportionate EV.
  • No impact on the previously communicated $7 billion shareholder return plan for 2024-2027.
  • Balance sheet position at 31 December 2025: holdco cash and short-term investments of $4.3 billion, Moody's total leverage ratio of 13%, free surplus ratio of 211%.
  • CEO Anil Wadhwani: "India is a strategically important and exciting market for Prudential. By acquiring a controlling stake in Bharti Life, we are bringing together Prudential's nearly 180 years of global insurance expertise and Bharti's strong and growing local presence to serve the savings and protection needs of Indian consumers."
  • On the existing ICICI relationship: "Our joint partnership with the ICICI group of companies, has, for many decades, provided high-quality financial services solutions in India. We deeply appreciate this partnership and value our relationship with them."

body:
• Manus Costello (50) appointed Group CFO and Executive Director, subject to regulatory approval; interim GCFO with immediate effect, based in London, reporting to CEO Bill Winters.
  • Costello joined Standard Chartered in April 2024 as Global Head of Investor Relations; previously 25 years in equity research, including founding partner and Global Head of Research at Autonomous.
  • Pete Burrill thanked for stepping in as interim GCFO during the transition.
  • Tanuj Kapilashrami appointed Group Chief Operating Officer, effective immediately; role oversees Strategy, Group-wide transformation, and resilience of corporate functions; GMT member since 2021, joined StanChart in 2017.
  • Costello currently holds 8,216 shares; subject to annual re-election and election at 2027 AGM.
  • Remuneration: base salary £1,100,000, pension allowance £110,000 (10% of salary), eligible for discretionary variable pay; first LTIP award in 2026.
  • Winters quote: "I am delighted that Manus has agreed to take on the role of Group Chief Financial Officer... This will further benefit Standard Chartered as we move forward into our next phase of growth and deliver on our medium-term financial objectives."
  • Costello quote: "Standard Chartered is a compelling franchise, offering cross-border capabilities that are deeply valued by our clients. As the Group Chief Financial Officer, I look forward to playing my part in building on our momentum, and in delivering on our future ambition at this exciting time for the business."

body:
• Chile's Second Environmental Tribunal ruled on 15 May 2026 to set aside the 2021 Environmental Authorisation (RCA) for the Collahuasi copper mine's "Infrastructure Development and Production Capacity Improvement" project, which includes a near-complete water desalination plant.
  • Glencore does not currently expect any immediate impact on production, citing existing alternative water sources for Collahuasi.
  • Tribunal's ruling is limited to two specific aspects: analysis of effects on a local community and on the marine environment.
  • Collahuasi is seeking clarification from the Tribunal and SEA on the specific effects, matters requiring SEA re-examination, and any operational impact.
  • Collahuasi maintains the permitting process was conducted per local regulations; RCA was obtained December 2021 and confirmed by the Ministers Committee in August 2023.
  • Collahuasi is independently managed (Glencore holds a 44% stake alongside Anglo American 44% and Mitsui 12%).
  • Further updates to be provided as appropriate.

body:
• Launching an additional €500 million share buyback programme, following completion of the initial €500m tranche (final purchase 14 May 2026) under the €1.5bn total return of excess cash announced on 26 February 2026.
  • Purpose: reduce share capital via cancellation, subject to shareholder approval at AGM.
  • Programme runs 18 May 2026 to no later than 30 September 2026.
  • Split: €374m purchased from market participants and €126m from Qatar Airways on pro rata terms.
  • Qatar Airways participating to preserve its 25.1434% stake; will not sell in the market, instead selling to MS/GS at daily VWAP proportionate to market purchases.
  • Morgan Stanley Europe SE and Goldman Sachs Bank Europe SE engaged to execute in sequential portions as riskless principal.
  • Maximum 300m ordinary shares, representing ~6.5% of issued share capital as of 15 May 2026.
  • Purchases on both London Stock Exchange and Spanish Stock Exchanges.
  • Per-session cap: no more than 25% of 20-day ADV.
  • Price cap: lower of (i) higher of last independent trade/highest current bid, and (ii) 105% of 5-day average market value.
  • Shares held in treasury pending cancellation, subject to AGM approval of related capital reduction.

body:
• Ingredion confirmed a non-binding indicative all-cash offer at 595p per share for Tate & Lyle.
  • Maximum headline value of 615p per share / ~GBP2.74 billion based on 445.4m shares in issue, per Tate & Lyle's earlier statement responding to press speculation.
  • Tate & Lyle shareholders would retain the right to a final FY (March year-end) dividend of up to 13p and an interim dividend of up to 7p for the six months to September, subject to board and shareholder approval.
  • Offer "follows a number of earlier approaches from Ingredion to the board"; discussions ongoing but no certainty of a firm offer.
  • Ingredion has a PUSU deadline of 11 June to declare a firm intention.
  • Ingredion reserves the right to "introduce other forms of consideration and/or vary the mix or composition of consideration of any offer" — leaves door open to non-cash elements.
  • Strategic rationale per Ingredion: "Ingredion believes a potential transaction would deliver significant benefits to customers, consumers, employees and Ingredion shareholders."
  • Shares closed up ~45% at 542p (market cap ~GBP2.42bn), still trading at a notable discount to the 595p indicative level — reflecting deal/antitrust uncertainty given Ingredion is a direct US starches & sweeteners peer.

body:
• 2026 profit growth expected, in line with market expectations.
  • Positive start to the year, building on 2025 momentum; UK housing transactions remain stable despite financial market uncertainty.
  • Short-term pull-forward of refinancing activity in March/April (following product repricing) has now moderated.
  • Strong market positions across core businesses with healthy pipelines; London market (limited LSL exposure) has shown recent softness.
  • Regulatory tailwinds cited: Renters' Rights Act and Home Buying and Selling Reform consultation seen as supportive given LSL's scale.
  • Broker operating platform roll-out on plan — targeting improved broker productivity and protection penetration.
  • Surveying: progressing further AVM opportunities after signing first AVM contract in Q4 2025.
  • Estate Agency Franchising YTD: supported purchase of 6 lettings books and opened 14 new branches.
  • Strategic investment made in a leading digital conveyancing business to strengthen franchisee proposition and cross-sell.
  • Pivotal Growth JV completing further acquisitions and scaling profitably.
  • Enlarged share buy-back programme (launched January) is progressing.
  • "We are actively progressing our plans to drive meaningful cost efficiency and productivity gains" under the "One LSL" operating approach.
  • Next update: Interim Results in September.
type_workings: Rule 'trading update': 'trading update' named in period line

body:
• Announced a new global multi-year partnership with ServiceNow to embed Experian's Ascend capabilities directly into ServiceNow's AI Platform workflows.
  • Partnership targets agentic AI scaling, with initial use cases in employee onboarding, third-party risk management (including fraud and identity verification), and model life cycle/risk governance.
  • Positioning rationale: cites industry research that data limitations are the primary barrier for 8 in 10 organisations adopting agentic AI — partnership aims to move clients beyond pilot deployments.
  • Experian Ascend Platform will be natively connected to ServiceNow AI Platform, allowing AI agents to access Experian's decisioning data within existing enterprise workflows.
  • Keith Little, President - Experian Software Solutions: *"This partnership cements Experian's position as a global leader in AI innovation, giving organisations the foundations to deploy agentic services with confidence."*
  • Cathy Mauzaize, President EMEA at ServiceNow: *"Businesses are ready to move beyond experimentation, and this partnership gives them exactly what they need to scale."*
  • No financial terms, revenue contribution, or guidance impact disclosed; strategically extends Experian Software Solutions' reach into new industries and regulated enterprise workflows beyond core financial services.
type_workings: Rule 'commercial': opening bullets say 'partnership with'

body:
• Full-year 2026 EPS guidance reiterated at up to 21 cent; trading in line with expectations.
  • Closed and forward order book at €1.5bn (Homebuilding + Partnerships), up from €1.3bn on 10 March (+15%).
  • Homebuilding units sold, signed or reserved: 1,828, up from 1,252 on 10 March (+46%).
  • Five additional Homebuilding sites launched plus seven further phases selling across existing developments.
  • Partnerships order book ~€800m, underpinning average annual gross profit contribution of at least €60m.
  • On track to deliver ~2,750 units in 2026, including ~1,600 Homebuilding units.
  • ~€45m of land sales planned in 2026 as part of portfolio optimisation toward larger-scale sites.
  • Homebuilding gross margin expected in excess of 21%, with efficiency gains offsetting build cost inflation despite Middle East conflict macro impact.
  • H2-weighted delivery profile reiterated, supporting strong H2 cash generation and further shareholder returns.
  • Refinancing completed: total committed funding raised to €550m from €450m.
  • New 5-year €450m RCF with AIB, Bank of Ireland, Barclays, HBFI, and new lender ING.
  • Issued €100m of 7-year private placement notes to MetLife, broadening lender base with long-dated institutional capital.
  • Share buyback increased by a further €25m to €50m total; ~€25m already returned under the January 2026 programme.
  • Cumulative shareholder returns since 2021 will reach ~€465m on completion of current programme.
  • Innovation: external wall façade system on track at NUA's Carlow facility; integrated solar PV and battery storage driving demand.
  • Board: Conor Murtagh replaces Stephen Garvey as Executive member of the Environmental and Social Responsibility Committee from conclusion of 2026 AGM.
  • 2026 Interim Results scheduled for 10 September 2026.
  • CEO Stephen Garvey: "We have made a strong start to 2026, building on the momentum from the second half of last year… the business is on track to deliver its full year guidance, with the outlook for the years ahead remaining positive."
  • CEO on funding: "The addition of ING to the banking syndicate and the €100m issuance to MetLife is extremely positive and will assist us in further driving the delivery of much needed homes in Ireland."
type_workings: Rule 'trading update': 'trading update' named in period line

time_period: AGM trading update; 2026/27 academic year reservations & FY2026 guidance
body:
• 2026/27 reservations at 79% of beds, vs 80% prior year — broadly in line with expectations.
  • Sales progress consistent with guidance for occupancy at the lower end of 93%–96% and rental growth of 2%–3%.
  • This implies a step-down from 2025/26 occupancy of 95.2% and rental growth of 4.0%.
  • Reiterated FY2026 adjusted EPS guidance of 41.5p–43.0p, representing a 9.5%–13% decline vs 47.5p in 2025.
  • Increasing disposal plans and exploring options to accelerate portfolio transition toward higher-quality, university-aligned assets.
  • Surplus capital from disposals to be redeployed into share buybacks or university partnerships.
  • CEO Joe Lister: "We are increasing our disposal plans and working with advisors to explore options to further accelerate our transition to a more focused, higher-quality portfolio. This will release surplus capital for reinvestment into share buybacks or university partnerships consistent with our capital allocation framework."
  • Shares down ~0.6% to 478.96p on the morning of release.
period_detail: AGM trading update; 2026/27 academic year reservations & FY2026 guidance

time_period: Trading update YTD 2026, with FY2026 guidance
body:
• FY2026 guidance reaffirmed: on track to deliver 2,750 units (including 1,600 Homebuilding), with H2-weighted performance.
  • Forward order book EUR1.5bn, up from EUR1.3bn at 10 March.
  • Homebuilding units sold/signed/reserved at 1,828, up from 1,252 at 10 March, reflecting robust underlying demand.
  • Five new sites launched plus seven further phases on sale across existing developments.
  • Partnerships order book ~EUR800m, underpinning average annual gross profit contribution of EUR60m.
  • Homebuilding gross margin guided >21% (vs 21.4% achieved in 2025), with "efficiency gains" cited as offsetting build cost inflation despite Middle East-related macro pressures.
  • Land sales of EUR45m targeted in 2026, shifting portfolio toward larger-scale sites for capital efficiency.
  • Debt refinancing: total committed funding raised to EUR550m from EUR450m — new 5-year EUR450m RCF (AIB, BoI, Barclays, HBFI), EUR100m 7-year private placement notes to MetLife, and ING joining the syndicate.
  • Buyback expanded by EUR25m to EUR50m total (EUR25m already returned under the January programme).
  • H2 cash generation plus land sales expected to support further shareholder returns, subject to market/board approval.
  • CEO Stephen Garvey: "We have made a strong start to 2026, building on the momentum from the second half of last year... Underpinned by our standardised, vertically integrated model, strong land portfolio and clear planning visibility, the business is on track to deliver its full year guidance, with the outlook for the years ahead remaining positive."
  • Garvey on financing: ING and MetLife participation is "a positive signal that, in addition to our own capability, Ireland now has a policy environment in place to attract the international capital that is required to move the dial on housing supply."
  • Garvey on Partnerships: remains "the partner of choice for the state in delivering the homes Ireland needs".
  • Shares -0.2% to EUR2.27 on the day.

  • Will also join the Audit & Risk Committee and the Remuneration Committee.

  • Ralph is a Fellow of Chartered Accountants Ireland; currently NED at DCC plc and Origin Enterprises plc, chairing the Audit Committee at both.

  • Default payment in US dollars; alternative currency elections (GBP, EUR, CHF) must be lodged by 11 May 2026.
  • FX reference rates (13 May 2026): GBP/USD 1.3515, EUR/USD 1.1709, USD/CHF 0.7826.
  • Per-share amounts in alternative currencies: GBP 0.062895, EUR 0.072597, CHF 0.066517.

body:
• Olaf Koch to join Board as Non-Executive Director from conclusion of the 2026 AGM, subject to shareholder approval.
  • Koch brings deep German market expertise — relevant given Germany is Vodafone's largest market and ongoing turnaround focus.
  • Maria Amparo Moraleda Martinez to retire at the AGM after 9 years' service; steps down as Board member, Chair of Remuneration Committee and Chair of ESG Committee.
  • Christine Ramon appointed Chair of Remuneration Committee post-AGM.
  • Anne-Françoise Nesmes appointed Chair of ESG Committee post-AGM.
  • Koch's background: CEO of Metro AG 2012–2021, leading wholesale-focused transformation; prior senior roles at Daimler/Mercedes-Benz Cars (Finance, Controlling, Strategy); operational role at Permira; partner at Zintinus since 2021.
  • Currently shareholder representative on Mercedes-Benz Group AG Supervisory Board, chairing Audit and Legal Affairs Committees.
  • Chair Jean-François van Boxmeer: "His extensive experience of leading complex transformations and deep knowledge of the German market will be a valuable addition as the Group continues to execute its strategy."
  • Koch on appointment: "I am thrilled to be joining the Board of Vodafone and look forward to working with Jean-François and my fellow directors to support Margherita and the executive team in driving Vodafone's vision forward."
  • No further disclosures required under UK Listing Rule 6.4.8 regarding the appointment.

• Karen Green, Group Senior Independent Non-Executive Director, Chair of the Sustainability Committee and member of the Remuneration and Risk Committees, will retire from the Board on 30 June 2026 after nine years' service.
  • Katie Murray (currently Audit Committee Chair) will become Group Senior Independent Non-Executive Director from 1 July 2026, retaining her Audit Committee chair and Nomination Committee role.
  • Karin Cook, Dual Independent NED, will become Chair of the Group Sustainability Committee from 1 July 2026.

  • Lyons on the new appointees: "Both Katie Murray and Karin Cook provide exceptional experience and support, and we are pleased to appoint them to their additional roles."
  • Announcement made pursuant to UKLR 6.4.6 (routine board composition disclosure; no financial/guidance impact).

body:
• JPMorgan double-upgrades to 'overweight' from 'underweight'; price target raised to 950p from 620p (~53% increase).
  • Broker views Aegon UK acquisition as "very positive" and considers regulatory approval highly likely.
  • Deal eliminates prior balance sheet concerns, is earnings accretive with potential to beat consensus.
  • Earnings mix shifting to ~60% capital-light; #2 in workplace pensions and becoming one of UK's largest retail savings players.
  • JPM quote: "Standard Life is now an attractive capital-light growth story, with upside risk to our forecasts based on synergy guidance."
  • Aegon UK platform could unlock revenue synergies from recapturing £11–12bn of annual net outflows from legacy retail customers.
  • Accelerates management's ambition of a top-5 position in UK retail savings.
  • EPS forecasts now 3% and 4% ahead of consensus for 2028 and 2029 respectively, including guided cost/capital synergies.
  • Additional upside flagged from company's track record of exceeding acquisition synergy guidance.
  • Stock at 773.69p; +26% over 12 months.

• Expects further profit growth in 2026, in line with market expectations.
  • Management quote: "We have made a positive start to the year, building on the momentum from 2025."
  • On macro: "Despite a backdrop of uncertainty in the financial markets, UK housing transactions continue to remain stable."
  • Strong market positions maintained with healthy pipelines across all core businesses (mortgage intermediary services and franchised estate agency).
  • Flags "limited exposure" to London, which "has shown recent softness" — a relative positive given regional housing market divergence.
  • Views regulatory change, including the Renters' Rights Act, as a tailwind "driving higher standards and increasing focus on professionalism and transaction efficiency."
  • Broker operating platform roll-out progressing as expected.
  • Estate Agency Franchising: 14 new branches opened, plus a strategic investment in a leading digital conveyancing business to support cross-sell.
  • "Actively progressing our plans to drive meaningful cost efficiency and productivity gains" — implies further margin focus.
  • Prior context (March FY2025 results): pretax profit flat at £23.1m; revenue +5.6% to £182.9m (from £173.3m) — today's update suggests profit growth resumes in 2026.
  • Shares +4.0% to 213.28p on the day.

body:
• Sky News reports BT has revived talks over a sale or partnership of its international operations (formerly BT Global), nearly a year after pausing earlier negotiations.
  • Potential counterparties named: AT&T, Orange and Verizon.
  • Unit sits within BT Business and covers operations in Argentina, Brazil, Canada and other markets, providing network, cloud and security services to corporate clients overseas.
  • Lazard or Goldman Sachs expected to advise BT on the discussions, per industry sources.
  • Divestment would align with CEO Allison Kirkby's strategy of refocusing on core UK operations, alongside the revival of the BT Mobile brand next to EE.
  • Topic likely to surface at annual results next week.
  • Shares -1.2% at 231.70p on the day; +~40% over the past year.
  • BT spokesperson declined to comment.

body:
• Citigroup upgrades to 'Buy' (from neutral/high risk); price target raised to 265p from 225p.
  • Upgrade comes despite shares already up ~10% over the last month and +44% over 12 months.
  • Citi sees upside to consensus estimates and significant re-rating potential, particularly in Investments and interactive investor.
  • On Investments: stronger earnings power and improved performance/flows could drive a re-rating; "We estimate the market assigns zero (or negative) value to Investments".
  • On interactive investor: constructive on growth outlook in a large/growing market; "While the strong growth outlook is generally well-understood, we see re-rating potential at ii given the mix shift towards recurring revenues, namely treasury and subscription revenues".
  • Adviser business: a strong improvement in flows would likely drive re-rating.
  • Capital: surplus capital could fund earnings-accretive bolt-on M&A.
  • Break-up: not seen as on the cards near-term, but could become more feasible over the medium-term.
  • Stock reaction: 239.18p, +5.8% on Friday.

body:
• Rule 2.4 announcement: Ingredion confirms a non-binding indicative all-cash offer at 595p per Tate & Lyle share, following Tate & Lyle's earlier disclosure of a possible offer.
  • Offer price represents a ~10.6% premium to last close of 538p (current market cap ~£2.41bn).
  • Tate & Lyle permitted to pay two ordinary dividends without price adjustment: final FY26 dividend up to 13p and interim dividend up to 7p for the six months to 30 September 2026.
  • Ingredion is engaged in discussions and due diligence; "there can be no certainty that a binding offer will be made."
  • PUSU deadline: 5.00pm London time on 11 June 2026 for Ingredion to announce a firm intention or walk away (extendable with Panel consent).
  • Ingredion reserves right to lower terms if recommended by the Tate board, if a third-party offer emerges at a lower value, or following a Rule 9 waiver/reverse takeover by Tate.
  • Ingredion reserves right to alter consideration mix (i.e. introduce non-cash components) and to reduce price for any non-permitted distributions.
  • Strategic rationale stated only in generic terms: benefits to "customers, consumers, employees and Ingredion shareholders" — no synergy quantification.
  • Advisers: J.P. Morgan sole financial adviser; Hogan Lovells legal adviser to Ingredion.
  • Context: combination would unite two of the largest global specialty food & industrial ingredients players (corn-derived sweeteners, starches, texturants), with likely antitrust scrutiny in the US and EU given overlap in starches/sweeteners post Tate's CP Kelco acquisition.
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets reference an offer approach: 'possible offer'

body:
• All 20 resolutions passed at the 2026 AGM (Resolutions 1–16 ordinary; 17–20 special).
  • Turnout: ~46% of issued share capital voted (267,798,543 shares in issue).
  • Resolution 14 (political donations authority) passed with only 79.43% in favour — below the 80% UK Corporate Governance Code threshold, triggering shareholder engagement.
  • Board reiterated policy of not making political donations or incurring political expenditure, and committed to a shareholder engagement update within 6 months and a final summary in the 2026 Annual Report.
  • Notable dissent on remuneration: Directors' Remuneration Report 7.36% against; Remuneration Policy 9.43% against.
  • Director re-election dissent concentrated on Kate Rock (13.79% against); Fiona MacAulay also saw 8.34m votes withheld (low total cast at 42.98%).
  • Final dividend approved (99.97% for).
  • Share buyback authority, allotment authority, and both pre-emption disapplications approved with >98% support.
  • Amendments to the 2023 LTIP Plan Rules approved (99.81% for).
  • PwC re-appointed as auditor (99.94% for).

body:
• All 24 resolutions at the AGM held on 14 May 2026 were passed by the requisite majority.
  • Highest dissent on Resolution 23 (dis-apply pre-emption rights on additional 10% of shares): 5.06% against (94.94% in favour).
  • Directors' Remuneration Report received 3.05% against (96.95% for); Remuneration Policy received 2.75% against.
  • Final dividend for FY2025 approved with 100% support (only 698 votes against).
  • Share buyback authority approved with 100% support.
  • Peter Clarke newly appointed to the Board (99.10% in favour).
  • All re-elected directors received >97% support; Jane Guyett had the lowest director backing at 97.38%.
  • PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP re-appointed as auditors with 99.98% support.
type_workings: Rule 'routine': opening bullets reference the AGM

body:
• Preliminary results from 2026 annual meeting: all 14 director nominees elected to one-year terms expiring 2027 (Duffy, Benesh, Bitsberger, Carey, Durkin, Ford Jr., Gepsman, Kaye, Lockett, Lucas, Seifu, Shepard, Siegel, Suskind).
  • Ernst & Young LLP ratified as independent auditor for 2026.
  • Say-on-pay: executive compensation approved on advisory basis.
  • Final voting percentages to be disclosed in forthcoming Form 8-K filing with the SEC.
  • Class B shareholder proposals adjourned — to be presented at a virtual adjourned meeting on Tuesday, 9 June at 11:00 a.m. CT.
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Chair succession announced: Gerry Murphy (joined Board May 2018, nearing end of 9-year tenure) to retire as Chair effective from interim results in November 2026.
  • William Jackson appointed Chair-designate, joining as Non-Executive Director on 1 July 2026, standing for election at AGM on 15 July 2026, then succeeding Murphy after formal handover.
  • Jackson, 62, is Founder and former CEO/Chair of Bridgepoint Group Plc (LSE-listed 2021); led Bridgepoint until 2024, chairing portfolio brands including Pret a Manger and MotoGP.
  • Prior NED roles: British Land (2011–2020, including 3 years as SID) and Berkeley Group (2021–2025); currently NED at IFM Investors, Chair of Lawn Tennis Association, outgoing Chair of Wellington College Education.
  • Board determined Jackson is independent on appointment under UK Corporate Governance Code.
  • Fee: standard NED fee initially; Chair fee on succession will match existing Chair arrangements.
  • Search led by Senior Independent Director Orna NiChionna; no further disclosures required under UK Listing Rule 6.4.8R.
  • NiChionna on Murphy: "His stewardship during a period of significant disruption and change in the luxury sector has been important in creating a more resilient business with a strong platform for the next chapter of Burberry."
  • Jackson: "Burberry has strong foundations, a clear strategic direction in Burberry Forward, and a talented executive team led by Josh Schulman."
  • Continuity signal: appointment framed around supporting CEO Josh Schulman's execution of the Burberry Forward turnaround strategy — no change to executive leadership or strategy implied.
type_workings: Rule 'results headline': opening bullets say 'interim results'

body:
• Trudi Charles appointed Chief Legal Officer, Governance & Corporate Affairs, succeeding Isabelle Deschamps.
  • Joins from BP plc, where she is Deputy General Counsel and SVP Legal, Supply, Trading & Shipping.
  • Start date: 1 August 2026.
  • Background: 20+ years at BP across downstream and supply/trading/shipping legal roles; previously ~8 years at Herbert Smith Freehills Kramer (London & Hong Kong); admitted in England & Wales, Hong Kong and Queensland.
  • CEO Simon Trott: "Trudi brings deep legal experience and strong commercial insight. I look forward to having her join the executive team and lead our legal, governance and corporate affairs teams."
  • Release authorised by Group Company Secretary Andy Hodges; routine executive appointment with no financial or operational guidance impact.
type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'ceo' with change verb 'appointed' in opening bullets

body:
• Shares jumped ~17% on the update, trading around 620p.
  • Revenue rose 11% to a record £1.83 billion (53 weeks), or +13% at constant currency.
  • Ex-53rd week: revenue +11% constant currency, +8% reported.
  • FY adj. EBIT guided to £152m–£155m, ahead of prior guidance and above £148.4m Jefferies consensus.
  • US revenue +24% constant currency to $1.24bn, now over half of group sales; CEO Brian Duffy: "The US continues to be the primary engine of growth".
  • Luxury watch revenue +13% cc; luxury jewellery +18% cc.
  • UK revenue +5%, with sequential improvement in H2.
  • Demand for key luxury brands, especially Registration of Interest list products, continues to outstrip supply in both US and UK.
  • FY27 guidance: revenue growth 5%–10% constant currency, adj. EBIT margin expansion of 40–80bps, capex £60m–£70m.
  • Duffy: "We enter financial 2027 with confidence and strong momentum, supported by the strength of our differentiated model, our leading market position, and the enduring demand across the luxury categories in which we operate".
  • Notes minimal direct exposure to Middle East or tourist consumers amid geopolitical uncertainty.
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with full-year financial figures

body:
• Pretax profit up 13% to £181.9m (vs £161.3m), with revenue 2.3% higher at £1.18bn (vs £1.15bn).
  • Trading profit +6.7% to £200.4m, ahead of expectations and ahead of previously raised guidance.
  • Dividend raised 20% to 3.36p (from 2.8p); plans to introduce an interim dividend in FY26/27, having previously only paid a final.
  • CEO Alex Whitehouse: "This profit delivery is ahead of previously raised guidance and reflects further branded revenue growth and market share gains, in addition to efficiency benefits from our capital investment programme."
  • Innovation cited as key growth driver — new ranges including Mr Kipling cake bites tubs, OXO bone broth and Angel Delight bubble jelly.
  • Sweet Treats branded revenue growth >7%; described as Mr Kipling's biggest ever year.
  • FY26/27 outlook: "our expectations are unchanged and we expect to make further strong progress across all our strategic pillars."
  • Shares +3.8% to 204.80p in early London trading.
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with full-year financial figures

body:
• Pretax profit fell 12% to £346m (FY25: £393m).
  • EPRA NTA per share up 0.9% to 882p (874p).
  • EPRA EPS up 2.2% to 51.4p (50.3p) — top end of guidance.
  • Net rental income up 1.8% to £562m (£552m).
  • Like-for-like net rental income +4.6%, ahead of 3–4% initial guidance.
  • Dividend raised 2.0% to 41.2p (40.4p).
  • Occupancy at a 20-year high; rents growing at fastest pace in nearly two decades.
  • FY27 guidance: like-for-like net rent +3% to +5%, "no signs" of customer demand slowdown.
  • FY27 EPRA EPS guided stable (in line with prior) — underlying growth offset by ~4% EPS drag from QAM finance lease sale.
  • FY28: high single-digit % EPRA EPS growth expected, driven by development leasing momentum and LfL income growth.
  • FY30: reiterates potential for EPRA EPS of ~62p.
  • CEO Mark Allan: "Over the past few years we have actively positioned Landsec for a higher inflation and higher interest rate world... scaled back development, reduced our overhead costs and maintained our strong capital base."
  • Allan on outlook: "our strong top line growth will increasingly flow through to an acceleration in EPS growth in the near and medium term."
  • Shares +1.5% to 579.00p on the day.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives full-year period '12 months to 31 March 2026 (FY2026)'

body:
• Completed 151 new leases and renewals, adding ~£13.7 million to rent.
  • Leasing transactions struck ~5% ahead of December ERV and 18% ahead of previous passing rents.
  • Available-to-let ERV at 2.5% vs 1.7% at May 2, 2025 — vacancy ticking up year-on-year.
  • Additional 1.2% of ERV under offer; 4.6% (£12.3m ERV) under refurbishment.
  • £16 million invested in capex and acquisitions YTD 2026.
  • Chinatown portfolio fully occupied; Carnaby Street to welcome Sephora's first West End store this summer.
  • Undrawn bank facilities of £700 million; LTV flat YoY at 17%.
  • Net debt rose to £800 million (proportionally consolidated) vs £700 million at May 2, 2025.
  • CEO Ian Hawksworth: "Shaftesbury Capital has made a positive start to 2026, demonstrating the strength and resilience of our prime West End portfolio. Despite an uncertain geopolitical backdrop... There continues to be competitive demand for space in our high-footfall destinations. With high occupancy, a strong leasing pipeline and backed by a very strong balance sheet, we remain confident in achieving our medium-term targets."
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Shares rose 6.2% to 264p, the best performer in the FTSE 100, after FT reported potential bidders — including insurers and alternative asset managers — had been "running the rule" over the business.
  • CEO Antonio Simoes denied any sale or break-up plan, telling the FT he was "100% focused on executing my strategy" and "There's no discussions or anything else going on."
  • Contradicting management, an L&G insider told the FT: "it feels like we're being dressed up for a sale."
  • A US private capital executive said: "It's getting pretty real... People are spending real money on this now."
  • Four people familiar with discussions said potential bidders have considered a full takeover, though any deal would be challenging and politically sensitive given L&G's size and large gilt holdings.
  • Analysts are reportedly questioning whether L&G's dividend is sustainable and whether it can survive as a public company.
  • Strategic alternatives floated by private capital executives include: selling blocks of the insurance portfolio, offloading assets to reinsurers, or bringing in a private capital partner for pension risk transfer (PRT) assets.
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Mandatory disclosure under UK Reports on Payments to Governments Regulations 2014; not a trading update.
  • Total payments to governments of $23.84bn across extractive activities in 2025.
  • Breakdown by payment type: Taxes $10.04bn, Production entitlements $8.04bn, Royalties $3.77bn, Fees $1.63bn, Bonuses $361m, Infrastructure improvements $1.3m.
  • Top recipient countries: Brazil $4.25bn (largest, driven by $1.39bn fees, $1.20bn royalties, $866m taxes), Oman $3.99bn ($3.41bn taxes), Norway $3.77bn ($2.12bn production entitlements, $1.65bn taxes), Qatar $2.91bn (Pearl GTL), Malaysia $2.38bn, Nigeria $2.02bn, Australia $1.82bn, US $1.12bn.
  • UK payments $122.2m, comprising $110.4m taxes to HMRC and $11.9m fees (NSTA, Crown Estate Scotland); Brent & Northern North Sea projects recorded a net tax credit of -$52.0m.
  • US taxes were a net -$23.9m refund (IRS -$25.2m), offset by $1.12bn royalties to ONRR (Gulf of America Central $1.05bn).
  • India recorded a net tax refund of -$14.6m (BG Exploration & Production India).
  • Mexico payments were negative -$0.14m; small materiality items.
  • Large in-kind (barrel) settlements notable in Norway ($2.12bn / 30,514 KBOE at Ormen Lange), Nigeria ($1.24bn to NNPC / 22,346 KBOE), Malaysia (multiple, totalling >$1.6bn in-kind), Oman ($518m at gov. selling price), and Brazil ($459.7m / 7,627 KBOE via Pré-Sal Petróleo).
  • Brazil Libra/Mero project bonuses of $285.8m and BM-S-9/11/Atapu fees of $1.38bn point to continued pre-salt licence/lease cost intensity.
  • Qatar Pearl GTL alone generated $2.91bn in payments to QatarEnergy.
  • Oman Block 6 Concession taxes of $3.39bn — single largest project-level tax line in the report.
  • Materiality threshold £86,000 per payment type; joint-control entity payments excluded (so Pearl-style consolidated entities included, but JV-only flows not captured).
type_workings: Rule 'trading update': 'trading update' named in opening bullets

body:
• Trading in line with expectations in 2026 so far, with FY26 revenue and adjusted operating profit second-half weighted.
  • Guiding to growth in revenue and adjusted operating profit in 2026, with an adjusted operating margin of 4.0% (vs 4.3% in 2025 on revenue of £1.05bn and adj. op. profit of £47.1m) — implying margin compression despite top-line growth.
  • Management: "The group is making good progress towards the expected step change in financial performance in FY27 and beyond. Contract mobilisations and increased activity levels, reflecting an acceleration in customers' investment spending plans, are expected to build during the second half of FY26."
  • Targeting net cash of £175m at end-2026, after partial unwind of historic working capital benefits.
  • £20m buyback underway — £4.0m repurchased as of Wednesday.
  • Plans to double cash dividends.
  • Extended undrawn £100m RCF and £295m bonding facilities by one year to September 2030.
  • Forward work position broadly consistent with the £7bn reported at end-2025; bidding pipeline described as strong across all sectors.
  • Customer mix shift: private/regulated now 51% of forward work (vs 30% two years ago); central government down to 31% from 64%; devolved government up to 17% from 6% — materially less reliance on Westminster.
  • New work secured with National Grid and Severn Trent's water division, among others.
  • Half-year results due 13 August.
  • Peel Hunt forecasts FY26 revenue £1.2bn and pretax profit £55m (vs £1.05bn and £48.2m in 2025); Panmure Liberum, Cavendish and Peel Hunt all on 'buy'.
  • UBS cut stake to nil (from 5.10%) on Tuesday.
  • Shares +2.2% to 201p on the morning.
type_workings: Rule 'trading update': 'trading update' named in period line

body:
• FY26 revenue guidance upgraded to 5–6% growth at constant currency (pro forma for Chairish consolidation), from prior 4–5%.
  • Revenue rose 42% to USD126.1m (vs USD89.0m), boosted by Chairish consolidation and Arts & Antiques momentum.
  • Pretax loss of USD0.6m vs USD8.9m profit prior year, driven by exceptional costs and higher share-based payment expense.
  • Adjusted pretax profit +52% to USD30.9m (from USD20.3m).
  • Adjusted EBITDA +11% to USD42.7m (from USD38.5m).
  • Adjusted diluted EPS +4.7% to 19.9c (from 19.0c).
  • Adjusted free cash flow +87% to USD26.5m (from USD14.2m).
  • April trading consistent with full-year expectations.
  • Operational drivers cited: search/ranking optimisation, AI-generated similar item recommendations, cross-marketplace exposure, retargeting, simplified bidder journey/onboarding.
  • New CEO Duncan Painter: "I am delighted to join ATG. The company is well positioned and performing well with strong first half financials. There is still significant progress to be made, and our focus is to accelerate and deliver upon our potential in the second half and over the medium term."
  • CEO on guidance: "For FY26, having delivered a positive start to the year driven by Arts & Antiques momentum, we are pleased to modestly upgrade our guidance. We expect another period of strong free cash flow generation."
  • Shares +13% to 398.60p on the day.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Six months to 31 March 2026'

body:
• Board confirms Ingredion Incorporated has made a conditional proposal for a possible cash offer for the entire issued and to be issued ordinary share capital, following recent press speculation.
  • Proposal values Tate & Lyle shares at up to 615 pence, comprising 595p cash plus Permitted Dividends.
  • Permitted Dividends: final dividend for FY ended 31 March 2026 of up to 13p plus interim dividend for H1 to 30 September 2026 of up to 7p, subject to Board/shareholder approvals and ordinary course timetable.
  • Offer represents a substantial premium — shares jumped ~44% intraday to ~539.5p from a prior close around 374.8p, implying the stock is trading just below the cash component.
  • Proposal follows a number of earlier approaches from Ingredion to the Board.
  • Board and Ingredion are in discussions regarding the Proposal.
  • PUSU deadline: 5:00 pm on 11 June 2026 for Ingredion to either announce a firm intention under Rule 2.7 or walk away (extendable with Panel consent).
  • Announcement made without the consent of Ingredion.
  • Tate & Lyle has 445,446,940 ordinary shares in issue (ex-treasury), implying an equity value of c.£2.65bn at 595p cash (c.£2.74bn including max Permitted Dividends).
  • Advisers retained: Goldman Sachs, Greenhill, Citi and BofA Securities (financial); Linklaters (legal) — notably heavyweight line-up consistent with a contested/strategic situation.
  • Strategic context: Ingredion (NYSE: INGR) is Tate & Lyle's largest direct US-listed peer in specialty food & beverage ingredients; a combination would consolidate the global specialty sweeteners/texturants space following Tate & Lyle's CP Kelco acquisition.
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets reference an offer approach: 'conditional proposal'

body:
• Achieved practical completion of a 195,000 sq ft office development at 100 New Bridge Street, City of London (between Blackfriars and Farringdon), in JV with Orion Capital Managers.
  • Building forward-sold to State Street Corp (announced April 2025); net price GBP333 million, of which Helical's share is GBP166.5 million.
  • Sale completion due by next Wednesday.
  • Proceeds to repay drawings under the GBP155 million development facility, with surplus distributed to JV partners per the May 2024 JV agreement.
  • ESG credentials: 90% of original structure retained; embodied carbon 61% below GLA office benchmark.
  • Shares fell 1.3% to 184.56p on the day.
  • CEO Matthew Bonning-Snook: "This is a significant milestone for our best-in-class 100 New Bridge Street development, where we have reimagined the building as a high-quality, future-focused workplace that demonstrates the potential of existing assets to meet modern occupier expectations while supporting the City's ambitions around sustainability and carbon reduction. Completing the sale to State Street will unlock significant returns to our investors, as we continue to deliver highly sought after real estate into supply constrained sub markets."

time_period: Leasing update as at 14 May 2026 (six months post completion)
body:
• Completed leasing campaign at Nineteen Wells Street, W1: all floors let or under offer only six months after completion.
  • New lettings: lower ground 1,446 sq ft let to a global leader in business messaging solutions; ground 1,335 sq ft let to a central London development corporation; first floor 2,754 sq ft under offer.
  • Secured £3.7m annual rent at average £245/sq ft, 7.7% ahead of ERV, delivering 20% ungeared IRR since acquisition.
  • Asset: >19,000 sq ft premium workspace across five floors; shared amenities include communal terrace and communal boardroom.
  • Strengthens Fitzrovia “Fully Managed” cluster alongside Elsley House, Kent House and The Howlett.
  • Molly Maguire (Senior Flex Leasing Manager): “The pace of leasing, together with the rents achieved, underlines the continued demand for high-quality, well-located workspace and the strength of our premium product.”

time_period: Event update (practical completion announced 14 May 2026; sale scheduled 20 May 2026)
body:
• Achieved practical completion of 195,000 sq ft repositioning of 100 New Bridge Street (City of London), JV with Orion Capital Managers.
  • Scheme adds 30,000 sq ft additional NIA and includes 9,690 sq ft of terracing across four floors; positioned as a “best-in-class, carbon friendly” office refurbishment.
  • Sustainability metrics: 90% of structure retained; embodied carbon tracking 61.4% below the GLA benchmark for offices; all-electric; targeting/achieved BREEAM Outstanding, NABERS 5, WELL Platinum*.
  • Forward sale to State Street for own occupation scheduled to complete 20 May 2026 at net price £333m (Helical share £166.5m); stated capital value £1,712 psf (c. £2,000 psf topped-up) and implied cap yield 5% (after sales costs + notional rent free).
  • Disposal proceeds intended to repay £155m development facility; surplus distributed to JV partners per JV terms (signed May 2024).
  • CEO Matthew Bonning-Snook: “Completing the sale to State Street will unlock significant returns… as we continue to deliver highly sought after real estate into supply constrained sub markets.”
  • Orion (Aref Lahham): “Completing 100 New Bridge Street brings to a close a highly successful partnership… outcome… reflects… demand… around a limited pool of best-in-class space…”
period_detail: Event update (practical completion announced 14 May 2026; sale scheduled 20 May 2026)

body:
• Average production 4,657 boe/d (+8% YoY), driven by Central field (2,131 boe/d) offsetting legacy declines; production down vs Q4 2025 (4,877 boe/d) due to natural declines in mature crude oil and Ortoire gas.
  • Petroleum & natural gas sales $12.5m (+14% QoQ vs $11.0m), driven by +18% realized gas prices and +25% realized crude pricing recovery; March 2026 crude volumes averaged $86.58/bbl.
  • Product pricing/volumes: crude 929 bbl/d at $67.94/bbl (sales $5.68m); NGL 422 bbl/d at $39.38/bbl (sales $1.50m); gas 19.84 MMcf/d at $3.00/Mcf (sales $5.36m).
  • Operating netback $13.73/boe (+46% QoQ vs $9.41/boe), citing higher commodity pricing and stable royalties offsetting higher opex base.
  • Funds flow from operations $1.85m (up from $0.62m QoQ).
  • Net income: net loss $2.38m (vs Q4 2025 net income $13.62m, which included $14.53m one-time non-cash gains).
  • Capex $3.22m focused on FR-1835 oil well, CR-3 tie-in, and Cascadura compression; period-end net debt $76.07m.
  • Post period-end: Cascadura compressor delivered in April; commissioning expected June 2026 to unlock capacity and improve stability.
  • WD-8 drilling: FR-1836 drilled ahead of schedule; logs indicate ~227 ft net hydrocarbon pay; completion underway; FR-1835/FR-1836 expected onstream imminently.
  • April 2026 production estimate 4,677 boe/d (gas 19.3 MMcf/d, liquids 1,456 bbl/d).
  • CR-3: stable ~2.2 MMcf/d gas and 14 bbl/d condensate; suspected inflow restriction; planning coiled tubing cleanout + acid stimulation (awaiting equipment).
  • Liquidity/going concern: working capital deficit $22.2m (excluding 2028 convertible); financial statements include material uncertainty about going concern due to debt structure and projected 2026 covenant breaches.
  • Recapitalisation plan: lender discussions on amendments/waivers; VAT recovery pursuit (~$10.1m outstanding); expected operational cash-flow uplift from 2026 projects + pricing; evaluating equity initiatives to strengthen balance sheet.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• EPRA EPS 51.4p (+2.2%) at the top end of guidance; dividend 41.2p (+2.0%).
  • Like-for-like net rental income +4.6% (vs initial c.3–4% guidance); rental uplifts on relettings/renewals 15% (vs 8% prior year).
  • EPRA occupancy 98.0% (+80bps LFL) — highest in two decades.
  • ERV growth +6.4% for the year (stated as fastest pace in nearly two decades), supporting further future income growth potential.
  • EPRA NTA per share 882p (+0.9% FY; +2.2% in H2); IFRS profit before tax £346m (down from £393m).
  • Balance sheet: LTV 38.7% (down 60bps); net debt £4,234m (down from £4,341m); ND/EBITDA 8.4x; average debt maturity 8.6 years with no refinancing needed until 2028; 89% of debt cost fixed/hedged.
  • FY27 guidance: like-for-like net rent growth c.3–5%; EPRA EPS stable vs FY26 (growth offset by -4% EPS impact from full-year effect of QAM sale).
  • FY28 guidance: EPRA EPS expected to see high single digit % growth (development leasing + LFL income).
  • Medium-term target: potential FY30 EPRA EPS c.62p, implying c.5% CAGR FY27–FY30.
  • Office-led: LFL net rental income +6.0%; occupancy 98.6% (decade-high); lettings £21m signed/ISH 7% above ERV; relettings/renewals 13% above previous rent; ERVs +7.1%; reversionary potential up to 17%.
  • Retail-led: LFL net rental income +5.5%; occupancy 97.7% (20-year high); lettings £49m signed/ISH 11% above ERV; rental uplifts on relettings/renewals 15%; values +4.6%; ERVs +5.8%.
  • Disposals/capital rotation: sold £705m of low-returning assets (stated 1.1% cost to NTA; broadly neutral EPS impact excluding QAM effect); sold £346m of offices ahead of plan.
  • Development exposure/capex: committed development capex £185m; development exposure expected to fall to c.2% of portfolio value within months; no meaningful new commitments planned in next c.18 months.
  • CEO Mark Allan: “Occupancy is now up to a 20-year high and rents are growing at their fastest pace in nearly two decades… confidence… to deliver c. 5% CAGR in EPRA EPS between now and 2030.”
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Year ended 31 March 2026'

body:
• Headline revenue £1,175.2m (+2.5%); headline branded revenue £1,041.7m (+3.4%), with H2 branded +4.7%.
  • Grocery branded revenue £791.0m (+2.3%); Sweet Treats branded revenue £250.7m (+7.3%).
  • Trading profit £200.4m (+6.7%) and “ahead of previously raised expectations”; trading profit margin 17.0% (+0.6ppt).
  • Adjusted PBT £183.6m (+8.5%); adjusted EPS 15.8p (+8.7%). Statutory PBT £181.9m (+12.8%); profit after tax £136.6m (+9.4%); basic EPS 15.7p (+9.8%).
  • Net debt £95.2m (down £48.4m vs £143.6m; ~34% lower), with leverage 0.4x Net debt/Adj. EBITDA.
  • UK branded revenue +3.7% for the year (H2 +5.0%); further market share gains in both Grocery and Sweet Treats.
  • Capital investment £51.9m (+25%); FY26/27 capex guidance ~£55–60m (Worksop sauces capacity, Barnsley flexibility, IT upgrades).
  • New categories revenue +37% (highlight: launch of FUEL10K yogurt & granola in chilled; Ambrosia porridge pots, Cape Herb & Spice also growing).
  • International headline revenue (1.8%) (constant currency) due to Australia retailer cake destocking; US revenue +17%, Europe +9%.
  • Acquired Merchant Gourmet for £46.1m (net of cash acquired); first seven months ahead of expectations with pro forma annual revenue c.£30m in FY25/26; pipeline for FY26/27 (e.g., gourmet baked beans launched in Q4).
  • All acquired brands (The Spice Tailor, FUEL10K, Merchant Gourmet) delivered double-digit revenue growth.
  • Dividend: proposed final dividend 3.36p (+20%), payable 24 July 2026 (record 26 June 2026, ex-div 25 June 2026); Board plans to introduce an interim dividend in FY26/27 (update at HY results 12 Nov 2026).
  • Financing: RCF increased to £367.5m (from £282.5m) and maturity extended to 2031 (option +2 years); £275m bridge facility to Nov 2027 undrawn at 28 Mar 2026.
  • Pension: deficit contributions stopped; dividend match removed; from April 2026 scheme funds admin fees, saving Group c.£5m p.a.; triennial valuation completed and scheme surplus on buy-in basis; accounting surplus £501.8m (down £146.9m YoY).
  • CEO Alex Whitehouse: “…Trading profit increasing to more than £200m… ahead of previously raised guidance… reflects further branded revenue growth and market share gains… and efficiency benefits from our capital investment programme.”
  • CEO Alex Whitehouse: “Our innovation programme has been particularly strong… Mr Kipling cake bites tubs, OXO bone broth and Angel Delight bubble jelly… Sweet Treats… branded revenue growth of over 7%… Mr Kipling's biggest ever year.”
  • CEO Alex Whitehouse: “Revenue from our entries into new categories grew 37%… FUEL10K yogurt & granola… overseas… impacted by… stock levels of cake in Australia… strong progress in Europe and North America.”
  • CEO Alex Whitehouse: “…even after the Merchant Gourmet acquisition, our financial leverage reduced to 0.4x Net debt/Adjusted EBITDA… increasing the final dividend by 20%… plan to introduce an interim dividend in FY26/27… expectations are unchanged…”

body:
• YTD leasing: 151 new leases and renewals completed, totalling £13.7m of new contracted rent, 5% ahead of Dec-2025 ERV and 18% ahead of previous passing rents.
  • Availability remains tight: only 2.5% of ERV currently available to let; a further 1.2% of ERV under offer.
  • Covent Garden momentum: highlighted renewal for Tiffany & Co. (James Street); new openings including Covent Garden Market Bar (Inception Group) and INITIO Parfums Privés; Burro opened in Floral Court.
  • Seven Dials positioning: new brands introduced incl. Code8 Beauty, Percival, MONC, Islander.
  • Carnaby / Soho progression: seven new concepts incl. Edikted (first store outside US), Sephora (new boutique format; first West End store this summer), Kookaï (UK debut location), K-Way, upsized Subdued; Vagabond Wines and Padella cited as incoming/recent openings.
  • Chinatown: described as fully occupied; new additions include POP MART (largest London store on Charing Cross Road) and Darjeeling Express upsizing to larger Rupert Street restaurant.
  • Asset management / capex: £12.3m of ERV across 149,000 sq ft under refurbishment (4.6% of portfolio ERV); £16m invested YTD in capex and targeted acquisitions.
  • Balance sheet: £0.7bn undrawn bank facilities; loan-to-value 17%; net debt £0.8bn (proportionally consolidated); £275m exchangeable bond repaid at maturity in March 2026 using cash.
  • CEO quote (Ian Hawksworth): “Shaftesbury Capital has made a positive start to 2026… 151 new leases and renewals… 5 and 18 per cent ahead of market rents and previous passing rents respectively… With high occupancy, a strong leasing pipeline and backed by a very strong balance sheet, we remain confident in achieving our medium-term targets.”

time_period: FY26 (53 weeks to 3 May 2026) — trading update; FY27 guidance (52 weeks)
body:
• FY26 Group revenue £1,828m, +13% constant currency (+11% reported); excluding the 53rd week, revenue +11% constant currency (+8% reported).
  • Category growth (constant currency): luxury watches +13%; luxury jewellery +18%.
  • US is now >50% of Group sales and profit; FY26 US revenue +24% constant currency (+18% reported) to $1.24bn.
  • US mix: retail revenue +25% constant currency; Roberto Coin wholesale +22% constant currency.
  • UK revenue +5%, with sequential improvement in H2 FY26 (momentum improving in luxury jewellery in H2).
  • Profitability: FY26 Adjusted EBIT expected £152–£155m, ahead of previous guidance (driven by stronger sales).
  • Net debt £57m following the acquisition of Deutsch & Deutsch.
  • M&A: acquired Deutsch & Deutsch (four Rolex-anchored showrooms in Texas); revenue from acquisition date £16m.
  • Capex: £67m expansionary capex in FY26; showroom projects included new Minneapolis showroom plus 12 expansions/relocations across US/UK, new Mappin & Webb luxury jewellery boutique (Manchester), and new Audemars Piguet House (Manchester, JV).
  • Pre-owned: group pre-owned sales +22%; continued rollout of Rolex Certified Pre-Owned in the UK with further expansion planned.
  • Ecommerce: revenue +21% constant currency; launched upgraded Hodinkee app enabling direct online purchase and loyalty.
  • FY27 guidance (organic, pre-IFRS 16): revenue growth 5–10% constant currency; adjusted EBIT margin +40–80bps expansion; capex £60–£70m; free cash flow conversion ~70%.
  • CEO quote (Brian Duffy): “FY26 marks another year of record revenue performance… FY26 Adjusted EBIT is expected to be £152 - £155 million, ahead of previous guidance… The US continues to be the primary engine of growth… now accounts for over half of Group sales.”
  • Next milestone: FY26 results planned 14 July 2026 (with broader strategy update).

body:
• Net rental income £66.1m (HY25: £61.3m, +7.8%).
  • Like-for-like rental growth +3.1% (FY25: +3.6%): BTR +2.9% (new lets +2.0%, renewals +3.3%); regulated tenancies +5.9%.
  • Occupancy 95.9% (FY25: 98.0%).
  • EPRA earnings £31.4m (HY25: £30.2m, +4.0%); EPRA EPS 4.2p (HY25: 4.1p).
  • IFRS loss before tax £(14.6)m (HY25 profit: £74.0m) driven by valuation decline following ~25bps outward yield shift (portfolio valuation down 1.1%).
  • NAV: EPRA NTA 290p (FY25: 298p, -3%).
  • Dividend: interim dividend 2.94p (HY25: 2.85p, +3%), paid 3 July 2026 (record date 22 May 2026), all as Property Income Distribution (PID); DRIP election deadline 14 May 2026.
  • Balance sheet: net debt £1,524m (FY25: £1,463m, +4%); LTV 40.2% (FY25: 38.4%, +186bps); average cost of debt 3.2% (FY25: 3.3%, -12bps).
  • Refinancing: extended £540m core banking facilities to 2033 at lower margins, reducing finance costs by ~£1m p.a.; weighted average facility maturity (incl. extension options) 4.6 years.
  • Deleveraging plan reiterated: reduce net debt by £300–350m by FY29, targeting ~30% LTV and ~8x net debt/EBITDA by FY29; supported by disposals (states ~£850m low-yielding non-core assets remaining).
  • Disposals: sales revenue £58.8m (HY25: £79.0m, -25.6%) and profit £5.2m (HY25: £19.9m, -73.9%); post half-year exchanged a further £23m of sales.
  • Guidance / outlook: on track for EPRA earnings £60m (+12%) in FY26 and £72m (+35%) by FY29; like-for-like rental growth expected 3.0–3.5% for FY26; EBITDA margin expansion targeted from 56% (FY25) to 60% by FY29.
  • Regulatory: Renters’ Rights Act took effect 1 May 2026; management expects it to accelerate structural shift toward professional landlords.
  • CEO quote (Helen Gordon): “We are on track to deliver our target of £60m (8.1pps) EPRA Earnings for this financial year… and £72m (9.7pps) for FY29… Earlier this month the new Renters' Rights Act took effect… contributing to structural changes in the sector with smaller, private landlords exiting, and larger scale, professional landlords gaining market share.”
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Six months ended 31 March 2026 (HY26)'

body:
• HY26 revenue $126.1m (+41.7% reported) with pro forma constant currency revenue +7.9%, driven by A&A +12.5% offset by I&C -1.8% (pro forma).
  • Pro forma adjusted EBITDA $42.7m (+9.9%) and pro forma margin +0.6ppts; reported adjusted EBITDA margin 33.9% (HY25: 43.3%, -9.4ppts) driven by Chairish consolidation + lower-margin mix (notably shipping).
  • Reported operating profit $7.0m (HY25: $15.0m, -53.3%) and loss after tax $(0.4)m (HY25: profit $7.0m), reflecting exceptional costs and higher share-based payment expense, plus higher finance costs.
  • Adjusted operating profit $36.9m (+8.9%) and adjusted diluted EPS 19.9c (+4.7%); basic EPS (0.3)c (HY25: 5.7c).
  • Cash: adjusted operating cash flow $36.2m (cash conversion 85% vs 84%); adjusted free cash flow $26.5m (+86.6%).
  • Leverage: adjusted net debt $152.0m, down from $174.0m at FY25 (down $22.0m / ~12.6%); leverage 1.8x (FY25: 2.2x). Board to consider capital allocation “towards the end of the year” as leverage approaches ~1.5x.
  • Group KPI framework simplified: Items sold (replaces lots sold/listed, web sessions), updated GMV and take rate definitions (now include Chairish/Real Estate/ESN/white label); THV, conversion rate, lots listed, web sessions, bids placed no longer reported.
  • Trading KPIs (pro forma): items sold 3.6m (+2%); GMV $1.8bn (flat) with A&A GMV +5% to $0.5bn and I&C GMV -2% to $1.3bn; take rate 7.1% (HY25: 6.6%, +0.5ppt).
  • A&A: pro forma take rate 17.7% (+1ppt); LiveAuctioneers improvements delivered +8.2% GMV and +9.4% items sold on that platform.
  • atgShip: mandate coverage expanded to >80% of GMV (from 20% at end of H1 FY25); first-time winning bidders using atgShip show 20% higher repeat purchase rate vs non-users; ~1,400 auction houses onboarded (vs ~1,000 at FY25).
  • Chairish: H1 revenue growth described as “good”; profitable in the first half; operational synergies track $8m run-rate in FY27, with $6m to be delivered in FY26 and $3m realised in H1.
  • I&C: softer H1 attributed to agricultural headwinds, white-label adoption, and auction house consolidation; agricultural GMV down 27%; Proxibid re-platforming: first auction house in testing; rollout targeted during FY27.
  • Additional cost programme launched in March: $4m run-rate savings expected in FY27, with modest H2 FY26 benefit; exceptional cost noted (e.g., $2m in H1 for reorganisation/cost review).
  • FY26 guidance upgraded: Group revenue growth 5–6% (constant currency, pro forma incl. Chairish) vs prior 4–5%; adjusted EBITDA margin 34.5–35.5%; “Group leverage well below 2x by end FY26”; strong free cash flow maintained.
  • Management: Duncan Painter appointed CEO 5 May 2026; quote: “For FY26, having delivered a positive start to the year driven by A&A momentum, we are pleased to modestly upgrade our guidance. We expect another period of strong free cash flow generation.”
  • Capital structure / equity: share premium cancellation approved (special resolution 22 Jan 2026), court-confirmed 3 Mar 2026 and registered 13 Mar 2026, reclassifying £236.8m from share premium to retained earnings.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Six months ended 31 March 2026 (HY26)'

body:
• Share buyback paused to prioritise debt reduction.
  • H1 2026 profit guided "significantly lower" YoY (H1 2025 adj PBT was GBP80.6m), primarily due to up-front profit impact of cash-generation actions.
  • FY 2026 adjusted PBT guided to the middle of GBP168m–GBP283m analyst range (vs FY 2025: GBP268.8m) — implying a downgrade vs prior consensus midpoint.
  • H2 2026 profit expected in line with H2 2025, supported by improved margin mix on active sites and step-up in affordable housing partner demand.
  • Shares fell ~11% to 290.13p, worst FTSE 250 performer on the day.
  • Middle East conflict flagged as creating upward pressure on material costs and, to a lesser extent, labour — expected to persist into H2; mitigation via supplier/sub-contractor engagement.
  • Sales rate YTD up to 1.20 vs 0.91 prior year (+32%); Open Market sales rate ~30% higher YoY but moderating recently due to Middle East uncertainty.
  • Increased incentives/discounts concentrated on low-margin and near-completion sites, pulling profit impact forward into H1; discounting effect expected to reduce in H2.
  • H1 average daily net debt to be higher YoY, reflecting higher early-year land payments and slower conversion of reservations to completions on Open Market (housing chain delays).
  • Year-end 2026 net cash position guided to >GBP100m following cash actions.
  • Cash initiatives: inventory reduction via sales pushes, higher hurdles for land purchases, buyback pause.
  • Partner transaction activity subdued during industry transition between social affordable housing programmes.
  • SAHP 2026–2036 bidding recently closed; grant notifications and Partner status clarification expected in Q3, driving step-up in partner demand late 2026 into 2027 and greater H2 weighting of partner revenues.
  • Management: "Whilst market conditions have become more challenging in Q2, we have continued to increase our focus on initiatives to enhance cash generation."
  • Management on costs: "we will continue to monitor overall build cost inflation for 2026 and into 2027 as macro-economic conditions evolve."

body:
• Revenue +9.1% to £5.27bn (FY25: £4.83bn), or +10% at constant currency; ahead of consensus £5.11bn.
  • Underlying operating profit fell 19% to £293m (vs £363m) due to a £140m non-recurring charge on the Type 31 frigate contract.
  • Ex-charge: underlying operating profit +19% to £433m, operating margin 8.2% (vs 7.5%), underlying basic EPS 60.5p (vs 50.3p).
  • Reported underlying basic EPS -21% to 39.6p.
  • Underlying free cash flow £262m (vs £153m); beat consensus £215m.
  • Net debt narrowed to £329m (from £373m).
  • New £200m share buyback announced, following completion of prior £200m buyback in April.
  • Type 31 charge: £100m recognised as revenue reversal in FY26, balance increases contract loss provision; cash costs spread over remainder of programme.
  • Management on Type 31: "higher than expected levels of rework as a result of changes to the design and the long-term impacts of out-of-sequence build activity earlier in the programme."
  • Added: "Whilst the number of such rework events is not entirely unexpected, the work is being performed in the later stages of completion and therefore is more complex and more costly."
  • Divisional revenue (constant currency): Nuclear +14% (Cavendish Nuclear, submarine support offsetting MIP decline); Marine +8% pre-Type 31 reversal; Land -3% (civil weakness in Rail and South Africa); Aviation +34% (Mentor 2 France ramp, BC helicopter EMS Canada, UK military scope).
  • FY27 expectations unchanged, with ~70% of expected revenue already under contract.
  • Shares +3.6% to 1,043.14p on the day.

body:
• YTD trading marginally ahead of board expectations, but 2026 guidance unchanged citing Middle East conflict uncertainty and its impact on interest rates.
  • Shares fell 1.1% to 824p on the morning of the release.
  • Eastdil Secured acquisition (GBP827m, announced March) expected to close around end of July; will materially increase US real estate investment exposure, currently described as "limited".
  • EMEA market investment -4% YoY in Q1 2026 (constant currency), including -3% in the UK.
  • Asia Pacific investment +16% YoY in Q1 (constant currency), with strong Hong Kong start and improvement in Mainland China.
  • Commercial Transactional Advisory: EMEA capital markets and leasing in-line; significant recovery in APAC capital transaction revenues; North America (occupier-focused) in-line, aided by larger deals.
  • UK Residential: "greater caution" since Middle East conflict began — longer completion timeframes, but no rise in fall-through rates.
  • Middle East: residential sales (~half of regional underlying profit) have slowed materially; Consultancy and Property Management resilient. Region was ~5% of group underlying PBT in FY25.
  • Capital advisory transactions expected to see extended timeframes, mirroring 2025's tariff-driven slowdown; expects "similar strengthening in market activity in the second half" if Middle East resolves.
  • Assumes reduced UK residential transaction levels persist; Middle East slowdown to temper International Residential growth; Less Transactional businesses expected to continue performing well.
  • Management statement: "On the basis of a timely resolution in the Middle East, the board continues to expect the group will perform in-line with its expectations for 2026, with the split of underlying profits between the first half and second half likely to be broadly similar to 2025."
