body:
• BP has acquired a 40% participating interest in a production sharing contract in Uzbekistan, buying 20% from each of SOCAR and Uzbekneftegaz, who each retain 30%.
  • PSA covers six blocks in the North Ustyurt region: Boyterak, Terengquduq, Birqori, Kharoy, Qoraqalpoq and Qulboy.
  • Original PSA was signed in July 2025 between operator SOCAR, Uzbekneftegaz and Uzbekistan's Ministry of Energy.
  • Operations are in first phase, with SOCAR conducting seismic activities (early-stage exploration, no near-term production impact).
  • Gio Cristofoli, BP's regional president for Azerbaijan, Georgia and Turkiye: "We believe Uzbekistan has significant resource potential and see this as an opportunity to support the exploration and development of the country's oil and gas resources, delivering long‑term benefits to the region."
  • Uzbekistan Energy Minister Jurabek Mirzamahmudov called it "not merely a commercial agreement, but a direct testament to international investors' confidence in the reforms being carried out in Uzbekistan and in our strategic course toward the development of the energy sector".
  • SOCAR President Rovshan Najaf framed the deal as deepening Azerbaijan-Uzbekistan energy cooperation.
  • Context: Bloomberg reported earlier this month BP is considering exiting UK North Sea operations, potentially fetching ~GBP2 billion in a full divestment.
  • BP is targeting around USD20 billion in divestments by end-2027, signalling continued portfolio rotation toward lower-cost international upstream barrels.
  • Shares rose 0.5% to 550.50p on Wednesday morning; Brent at USD106.12/bbl vs USD108.07 prior late session.

• Revenue +9.1% to £5.27bn (vs £4.83bn), +10% at constant currency; ahead of £5.11bn consensus.

  • RBC (Pfannes-Varrow): "bittersweet" with "beats across the board" ex-Type 31; consensus had viewed existing provisions as cautious.
  • Citi (Armitage): share price reaction hinges on whether company convinces market the charge is "truly one-off".

body:
• Shares slumped 9.5% to 295p, worst FTSE 250 performer (closed -12.34% at 285.60p).
  • Share buyback paused to prioritise debt reduction.
  • H1 2026 profit guided "significantly lower than prior year" (H1 2025: £80.6m), due to up-front profit impact of cash-generation actions (discounting open-market stock to clear inventory).
  • FY 2026 adjusted PBT expected at the midpoint of £168m–£283m analyst range (vs FY 2025: £268.8m); Stifel sees consensus moving to ~£225m from ~£250m.
  • H2 2026 profit expected in line with H2 2025 on improved margin mix and step-up in affordable housing partner demand.

  • Cash initiatives: inventory reduction via sales push, higher hurdles for land purchases, buyback pause.
  • New CEO Adam Daniels (promoted April) leading operational review; findings by interim results in September.
  • Daniels remains "fully committed" to the Partnerships strategy.
  • Company quote: "Whilst market conditions have become more challenging in Q2, we have continued to increase our focus on initiatives to enhance cash generation."
  • Company on cost inflation: "The events in the Middle East have started to create some upward pressure on material and, to a lesser extent, labour prices which we expect to continue into H2."
  • RBC (Codling): update contains "good and bad news: progress is being made, but market conditions are providing little if any help and execution risks remain high. Vistry is not out of the woods yet, but it is one step closer to the edge of the forest."
  • JPMorgan (Beekawa): "a pretty disappointing update given the magnitude of downgrades implied and, particularly, given street numbers have already come down meaningfully."
  • Stifel: maintains buy but concedes upgrade was "too early"; with H2-weighted profits and rising H1 debt, "there is likely to be more pain in the short term."
  • AJ Bell (Mould): "Prioritising cash generation over profit looks a sensible decision for a company carrying a meaningful amount of debt."

body:
• Sky News reports outgoing Currys CEO Alex Baldock is expected to be named next CEO of Boots, with the appointment to be announced later in the spring.
  • Baldock announced his departure in late March after eight years leading Currys, citing a "new external position".
  • Currys shares -0.5% at 123.00p on the news.
  • Boots is owned by Walgreens Boots Alliance, taken private by Sycamore Partners in 2025.
  • Sky notes Baldock could lead Boots through a "potential multibillion-pound stock market listing".
  • Retail analysts cited by Sky suggest a standalone Boots could be valued at GBP5 billion to GBP7 billion, with a London listing "considered to be the likeliest outcome" amid repeated speculation Sycamore could sell or demerge the business.
  • Boots declined to comment; Baldock was unreachable.

body:
• Issuer exercising early redemption of its outstanding £200m 2.00% Subordinated Tier 2 Notes due 2031 (ISIN: XS2351480566).
  • Residual nominal amount outstanding is just £8.62m, following the tender offer announced 26 January 2026 (results announced 3 February 2026) which mopped up the bulk of the £200m issue.
  • Redemption Date: 11 June 2026, at the price set out in Condition 7(b) of the Conditions.
  • Notes will be cancelled post-redemption, with listing on the FCA Official List and LSE Regulated Market trading admission to be cancelled on/shortly after the Redemption Date.
  • Required holder notice under Condition 7(b) delivered today.

body:
• Non-binding all-share offer agreed with Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust (SREIT) to acquire Picton Property Income.
  • Terms: Picton holders receive 0.190 LondonMetric shares + 0.881 SREIT shares per Picton share.
  • Implied value: ~£403.4m, or 78.2p/share based on offerors' Monday close.
  • Premium: 7.0% to Picton's 73.1p undisturbed price, but a 5.6% discount to Picton's December portfolio value (NAV).
  • Split of acquisition: LondonMetric 46% / SREIT 54% of Picton.
  • Post-deal, Picton shareholders to own 4.0% of enlarged LondonMetric and 48.2% of enlarged SREIT.
  • LondonMetric's existing 11.1% stake in SREIT dilutes to 5.8%; agreed 6-month lock-up post-completion on remaining SREIT stake.
  • Schroders agreed to a 10bp reduction in SREIT management fee across all tiers.
  • Picton board "minded to unanimously recommend" if firm offer made.
  • Letter of intent to vote in favour received from TR Property Investment Trust (~11.4% of Picton).
  • Sector weightings of both acquirers' portfolios expected to be broadly unchanged post-allocation of Picton assets.
  • SREIT LTV expected to fall into its 25–35% target range post-deal.
  • Both acquirers expect the deal to be earnings accretive, with "a very material, immediate increase in dividend income for Picton's shareholders".
  • Consortium statement: offer "retains Picton's good quality portfolio in the UK-listed arena and... provides Picton shareholders with the opportunity to crystallise a premium to the share price at the undisturbed date whilst reinvesting into two enlarged, UK-listed REITs that will enjoy the various benefits of enhanced scale".
  • Background: follows Picton's January strategic review considering "a merger with other UK REITs"; LondonMetric/SREIT consortium shortlisted in March.
  • Share reaction on day: Picton -1.1% to 72.30p; SREIT -2.8% to 46.95p; LondonMetric -1.9% to 184.10p.
  • No certainty a firm offer will be made.
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'to acquire'

body:
• New £50 million share buyback to commence Monday following Friday's AGM, supported by balance sheet and recent disposals.
  • "Good progress" on £1bn three-year disposal target: exchanged contracts on £278m of sales at a blended 5.8% initial yield, c.3% below December book value.
  • In talks on a further £120m of disposals.
  • Management comment: "While investment market conditions have been impacted by the Middle East conflict, investors remain attracted to the London office sector by the strength of the rental outlook and its 'safe haven' status."
  • Network W1 reached practical completion on 5 May, expected to drive positive H1 revaluation; pre-let at rents "well ahead of appraisal".
  • Three on-site development projects forecast at ungeared IRR >10%.
  • 50 Baker Street redevelopment committed, ungeared IRR >12%, completion targeted H2 2029.
  • Holden House: Kier Group appointed for pre-construction services; practical completion H2 2028.
  • Greencoat & Gordon House and Middlesex House refurbishments due 2027; Old Street Quarter planning submission around end-2026.
  • Net debt £1.46bn (vs £1.45bn at Dec 31); EPRA LTV unchanged at 29.4%.
  • Cash and undrawn facilities fell to £383m from £627m after redeeming two facilities at maturity (excludes £278m disposals due to complete shortly).
  • Final 2025 DPS 56.0p (40.0p PID + 16.0p conventional), payable 29 May.
  • CEO Paul Williams reaffirmed near- and medium-term earnings targets, citing "strong activity across the business driven by significant leasing, including the pre-letting of Network at rents well ahead of appraisal" and "very strong rental growth" at 50 Baker Street.

body:
• Pretax profit £25.5m, up 8.6% from £23.5m YoY.
  • Revenue £589.0m, up 3.5% from £569.3m, attributed to "increasingly diversified" end-market exposure and sustained demand for "mission-critical services".
  • Interim dividend raised to 7.0p, up 4.9% from 6.7p.
  • Record order book of £945m (vs £908m prior year), underpinning confidence in full-year outturn.
  • Bolt-on M&A: on Monday, via subsidiary Excalon, acquired PWR-X Ltd (specialist cable jointing services to the power industry) for £1.1m total consideration.
  • Shares +0.9% at 924p late Tuesday morning.
  • CEO Paul Scott: "I am pleased to report that we have delivered another record performance during the first half of the year, providing momentum as we continue to execute against our ambitious long-term growth strategy."
  • CEO outlook: "With a record order book afforded to us by highly visible, committed, long-term spending cycles, we remain confident in continuing on this growth trajectory and we look forward to delivering against our full year expectations."

body:
• System-wide revenue +8.8% to USD1.17bn (Q1 2025: USD1.07bn).
  • Group revenue +3.7% to USD958m (Q1 2025: USD924m).
  • FY2026 guidance reiterated: adjusted EBITDA USD585m–USD625m, vs USD531m delivered in 2025 (implies ~10–18% growth).
  • Company-owned revenue growth guided at at least 4%.
  • Recurring managed fee income guided at USD80m.
  • Shares +2.2% to 192.40p on the day.
  • CEO Mark Dixon: "I am delighted with our strong start to 2026 as we continue the rapid growth of our network supported by increasing sales despite the challenging economic backdrop."
  • Dixon on demand drivers: "Potential customers are requiring more flexibility in their Real Estate strategy to address the uncertainty arising from the impact of conflicts and the growing influence of AI. This is resulting in record levels of Enterprise customer enquiries as our coverage and network enable us to provide unique, flexible, global solutions."

body:
• Swung to pretax profit of EUR1.86 billion vs EUR1.48 billion loss prior year.
  • Revenue +8.0% to EUR40.46 billion (from EUR37.45 billion), driven by service revenue growth and Three UK consolidation, partly offset by FX.
  • Service revenue +8.8% to EUR33.48 billion; +5.4% organic, growth in all segments except Germany.
  • Adjusted EbitdaaL EUR11.35 billion vs EUR10.93 billion — missed consensus of EUR11.48 billion.
  • Adjusted basic EPS 10.72 euro cents (vs 7.87c) — beat consensus 9.23c.
  • Operating profit EUR2.84 billion vs EUR411 million loss prior year.
  • Adjusted free cash flow +2.9% to EUR2.62 billion, above top end of guidance.
  • Net debt rose to EUR25.41 billion from EUR22.40 billion, reflecting VodafoneThree deal.
  • First dividend increase since 2018: total dividend +2.5% to 4.6125 euro cents (from 4.5c).
  • Final EUR0.5 billion tranche of second EUR2 billion buyback completed Monday; buybacks remain "an element of our toolkit".
  • FY27 guidance: EbitdaaL EUR11.9–12.2 billion; adjusted FCF EUR2.6–2.9 billion.
  • Medium-term ambition: double-digit adjusted FCF growth in euro terms.
  • Germany organic service revenue -0.2% for year, improving to +1.3% in Q4; mobile market "very competitive".
  • German broadband base lost 202,000 customers in year, with Q4 losses accelerating to 90,000 (vs 63,000 prior quarter) — flagged by Citi as a concern.
  • UK organic service revenue +0.3%; Consumer/Wholesale growth offset by Business decline from planned managed services contract terminations.
  • VodafoneThree full ownership completed faster than expected; Vodafone now UK's largest mobile operator with 28 million mobile customers.
  • Other Europe organic service revenue +0.5%; Turkey service revenue +10.8%.
  • Africa organic service revenue +12.9% — highest growth in almost two decades.
  • Business organic service revenue +3.2%, with double-digit growth in digital services.
  • Mostly hedged on energy prices for FY27.
  • CEO Della Valle: "After the transformation of the last three years, we are now a simpler company with a stronger growth outlook" and "We are entering a new chapter as a simpler and stronger business".
  • Della Valle: "Our growth portfolio gives us the confidence in our medium-term ambition to deliver double-digit adjusted free cash flow growth".
  • Citi (Murdock-Smith): "Vodafone's Q426 results are solid at a group level, with revenue and adjusted free cash flow ahead of consensus, albeit with EbitdaaL below"; German net adds "may raise some questions".
  • Deutsche Bank: mid-term ambitions not "especially onerous" but "pleasing to see"; reiterates buy.
  • Shares -5.4% to 113.85p on the day despite results, after a 65% 12-month rally.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Financial year ended March 2026'

body:
• Portfolio company Enterprise Therapeutics Ltd reported positive phase-2 trial results for inhaled EDT001, its lead candidate for cystic fibrosis.
  • Trial targeted the 10% of CF patients with the highest unmet medical need and met its primary efficacy endpoint.
  • Demonstrated "clinically meaningful improvement in lung function over a 28-day period compared to placebo", with adverse effects consistent with the patient population.
  • Next step: progression to longer-duration phase 2b trials, combining EDT001 with CFTR modulator therapies.
  • CEO Greg Smith: "This is a highly encouraging clinical milestone for Enterprise Therapeutics and a strong validation of its differentiated approach to cystic fibrosis. Demonstrating a clinically meaningful improvement in lung function in a population with significant unmet need highlights the potential of ETD001. For IP Group, this represents another example of our model providing investors with access to differentiated deep science opportunities."
  • Market reaction muted/negative: IP Group shares down 1.2% to 65.22p on Tuesday morning, suggesting limited near-term NAV uplift priced in despite the de-risking event.
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Morgan Stanley downgrade: cut to 'equal weight' from 'overweight', though price target nudged up to 275p from 265p.
  • Rationale: "balanced risk-reward" after a ~60% YTD share price rally, with consensus already close to FY2028 targets.
  • Broker still views Trustpilot as the clear leader in horizontal consumer reviews and an AI beneficiary, with a credible path to 30% adjusted EBITDA margin.
  • Valuation: now trades at a 25% premium to small-mid cap software peers, versus a 50% discount in December; also at a premium to network-effect/classifieds peers on price-to-growth.
  • MS: "while we view the relative re-rating as justified, we believe the risk-reward is now more balanced."
  • Potential upside catalyst: sustained growth in the key North America market could push margin targets higher.
  • Key risk flagged: need for further evidence Trustpilot can maintain review integrity as AI-generated content rises.
  • Share price reaction: 245p, down 6.1% on the day; 12-month change just +0.3%.

body:
• Revenue $541.2m, +14% y/y (Creative Subscriptions $382.4m +13%; Business Solutions $158.8m +17%).
  • Bookings $585.0m, +15% y/y; ex-FX +11%.
  • Total ARR $1.903bn, +15% y/y.
  • New user cohort bookings +~46% y/y, with meaningful Base44 contribution plus healthy core Wix new cohort performance.
  • Transaction revenue $70.0m (+19%); Partners revenue $203.4m (+19%).
  • GAAP net loss $57.5m (-$1.02/share) vs +$33.8m profit prior year, hit by $37.9m acquisition-related expenses and step-up in S&M (+79% to $199.6m) and R&D (+40% to $178.2m).
  • Non-GAAP net income $42.5m ($0.68 diluted) vs $93.9m ($1.55) prior year.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin 66% (vs 69%); Creative Subs non-GAAP GM 80% (vs 84%); Business Solutions non-GAAP GM 32% (vs 31%).
  • Non-GAAP operating margin 5% vs 21% prior year; non-GAAP operating income $27.8m vs $99.8m.
  • FCF $75.0m; FCF ex-acquisition costs $112.3m (21% of revenue) vs $142.4m prior year.
  • Completed $1.6bn modified Dutch Auction tender in early April, repurchasing ~17.5m shares at $92, ~30% of shares outstanding; share count now 41.85m as of 11 May.
  • Funded partly via new $500m credit facility plus $260m private placement proceeds in Q1; cash & equivalents $1.34bn (vs $311m at YE25).
  • Base44 reached ~$150m ARR as of May (vs the broader ramp continuing).
  • Wix Harmony now running on Wix's own proprietary LLM, reducing reliance on third-party LLMs; new Base44 features include Superagents AI assistant and Figma→Base44 integration.
  • FY26 guidance reiterated: bookings and revenue growth of mid-teens % y/y; Q2 revenue also expected mid-teens % y/y.
  • FY26 FCF margin ex-acquisition costs guided to high-teens (vs low-to-mid 20s on a pre-tender capital structure), reflecting foregone interest income, credit facility interest expense, and ~$64m FX headwind from shekel strength net of hedging.
  • Management flags "softer start to the year in our Partners business" and productivity headwinds from the Middle East war pushing out product rollouts for professional audience; expects to offset via core Wix top-of-funnel and existing user base initiatives, plus continued Base44 outperformance.
  • CEO Abrahami: "We recently built our own proprietary LLM that is now powering Wix Harmony... this model creates a flywheel that we believe is unmatched and creates a significant competitive advantage."
  • CFO Shemesh: "Bookings from our Q1'26 new user cohort increased almost 50% year-over-year"; expects Creative Subs gross margin to remain stable as AI costs stay minimal while scaling Harmony.
  • Headcount 5,277 at end-Q1'26.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• CEO-elect Brandon Craig signals openness to new partnerships and bolt-on M&A to drive growth, ahead of taking over from Mike Henry on 1 July 2026.
  • Craig: "One of my priorities will be ensuring we have options to grow well beyond 2035."
  • Growth strategy to include increased exploration, partnerships "to unlock value in adjacent operations", and smaller bolt-ons "when the value case supports them".
  • Emphasised continued capital discipline: "We'll continue disciplined, consistent growth – developing the pipeline already in front of us and staying ready to act when value-accretive opportunities emerge."
  • Flagged balance sheet firepower: "our diversified model and strong balance sheet gives us the ability to move at pace if the right opportunity presents itself".
  • Balance sheet position (as of 31 December): USD13.47bn cash, total current assets USD25.93bn, total assets USD116.01bn.
  • Shares +3.3% to AUD61.72 in Sydney following the comments.
  • Remarks delivered at the BofA Global Metals, Mining and Steel Conference in Miami.

body:
• Fitch upgraded long-term Issuer Default Ratings (IDR) of key NWG subsidiaries to 'AA' from 'AA-', following an update to Fitch's Bank Rating Criteria (action taken 12 May 2026).
  • Entities upgraded: National Westminster Bank Plc, The Royal Bank of Scotland plc, NatWest Bank Europe GmbH, NatWest Markets Plc, NatWest Markets N.V., and The Royal Bank of Scotland International Limited.
  • Long-term senior unsecured debt ratings also upgraded to 'AA' from 'AA-' across the same entities (ex-RBS plc).
  • Short-term rating unchanged at F1+; Outlook remains Stable on all entities.
  • Driver is criteria-based (Fitch methodology update), not an idiosyncratic NWG credit event — supportive for wholesale funding costs.

body:
• £140m non-recurring charge on Type 31 frigate contract fully recognised in FY26; c.£100m as revenue reversal, balance increasing contract loss provision.
  • Revenue £5,273m vs £4,831m (+10% organic at constant FX) before c.£100m Type 31 reversal; beat consensus of £5,112m.
  • Underlying operating profit ex-Type 31: £433m (+19% cFX), vs consensus £411m; including charge £293m (-19%).
  • Underlying operating margin ex-Type 31: 8.2%, exceeding 8.0% FY26 target (FY25: 7.5%); including charge 5.7%.
  • Underlying basic EPS ex-Type 31: 60.5p vs 50.3p; reported 39.6p.
  • Underlying free cash flow £262m vs £153m (+71%); operating cash conversion 85% ex-Type 31; beat consensus £215m.
  • Net debt reduced to £329m (FY25: £373m); net debt/EBITDA 0.2x; net debt ex-leases just £23m (FY25: £101m).
  • Contract backlog £9.6bn vs £10.4bn.
  • New £200m share buyback announced, commencing around FY26 results; prior £200m programme completed April 2026.
  • FY27 expectations unchanged; ~70% revenue under contract at 1 April 2026.
  • Medium-term guidance reiterated: mid-single-digit revenue growth, underlying operating margin of at least 9%, cash conversion ≥80%.
  • Sector – Nuclear: revenue +14% to £2,070m; op profit +23% to £197m; margin 9.5% (+70bps), hits medium-term target.
  • Sector – Marine: revenue +8% to £1,687m (pre-reversal); ex-Type 31 op profit £110m (+15%), margin 6.5%; including charge loss of £30m.
  • Sector – Land: revenue -3% to £1,084m (returned to growth in H2); op profit +10% to £95m; margin +110bps to 8.8%.
  • Sector – Aviation: revenue +34% to £431m; op profit +52% to £31m; margin +90bps to 7.1%.
  • Type 31: higher-than-expected rework in outfitting from design changes and out-of-sequence build; ship 1 structural completion nearing end; ships 3 and 4 less impacted; cash costs spread over remainder of programme.
  • FY26 audited preliminary results delayed to late June (vs end-May) due to Type 31 re-estimate.
  • Indonesia: Letter of Intent for two further Arrowhead 140 frigate licences under £4bn MPP framework, delivery early FY27.
  • US Virginia Class: authorised to build complex submarine assemblies at Rosyth for Block VI via HII partnership; initial engineering contract underway.
  • UK MoD: 6-month FMSP bridging agreement signed; two-year extension on Royal Navy surface ship maintenance.
  • SMR: Litmus Nuclear JV (Cavendish/Amentum) selected by GBE-N as Owner's Engineer for Wylfa SMR project, contract worth up to £300m over 14 years.
  • Light Utility Vehicles: 270 units for British Army 11 Brigade; first tranche order from Albanian MoD.
  • CEO succession: Harry Holt (ex-Nuclear CEO) became Deputy CEO on 1 April 2026, joins Board June 2026, succeeding David Lockwood.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives full-year period 'FY26 (financial year ending 31 March 2026)'

body:
• Revenue £4,833.8m, +1.5% YoY (FY25: £4,763.9m); gross margin flat at 11.2%.
  • Adjusted PBT £24.5m, -16.4% YoY (FY25: £29.3m), but ahead of consensus £24.0m (range £23.4–24.4m).
  • Reported PBT £20.3m (-18.1%); Basic adjusted EPS 5.71p (-13.2% vs 6.58p).
  • Free cash flow £30.7m (FY25: £37.3m); Net debt (ex-leases) £61.3m (FY25: £66.6m); gearing 17%.
  • Net tangible assets per share 75.9p (FY25: 72.9p) — share price 65.5p trades below TNAV.
  • Full-year dividend held flat at 2.05p (final 1.15p); £10.7m of buybacks in year; new £12m buyback announced March 2026; cumulative £46.5m / 21%+ of issued shares repurchased since 2018.
  • JLR cyber-attack: £3.9m gross profit loss, offset by £3.4m insurance recovery booked in FY26 underlying earnings (cash received post year-end).
  • Aftersales now >46% of group gross profit; LFL service revenue +3.7%, aftersales gross profit +£14.1m; ~160,000 service plans, 50,700+ Motability vehicles.
  • New vehicle gross profit weak: Core Group new car GP -£8.7m YoY (-£19.6m over two years); Fleet & Commercial GP -£4.2m. Motability LFL volumes -16.6% (market -17.8%).
  • LFL BEV retail volumes +71.0% vs UK private BEV market +50.2%.
  • Used: LFL volumes +0.3%, GP per unit £1,501 (FY25: £1,508); Core used GP margin 7.0% (7.2%).
  • Core Group operating costs +1.1% despite NI/wage inflation; £10m cost-efficiency programme delivered for FY27 benefit (£7m headcount savings from ~280 redundancies, £1.2m rebrand efficiencies, £1.4m other).
  • Non-underlying costs £5.1m (closures, restructuring, £0.6m Motorrad goodwill impairment, £0.7m roof remediation provision) offset by £0.9m disposal gains.
  • Surplus property disposals: £5.1m cash proceeds at £0.6m premium to book; further £1.5m Barnstaple sold in March 2026 at £0.5m premium; Glasgow disposal delayed past March completion, may be remarketed.
  • Current trading: trading profit in March-April 2026 ahead of prior year; LFL new retail volumes +6.5%, Fleet car volumes +32.0% (market +10.7%), aftersales GP +£2.9m YoY.
  • Value Cars by Vertu launched 1 April targeting 7-14 year old used car segment; initial signs of incremental profit.
  • Chinese brand expansion: BYD outlets to 5, Geely launched (Glasgow Feb 2026); Jaecoo, Omoda, Lepas, Chery, Leapmotor to be added.
  • FCA Motor Finance Redress Scheme: no provision required; Scheme postponed pending Upper Tribunal review after legal challenges; risk of consumer finance market contraction noted.
  • ZEV mandate: group has formally asked Government to bring forward ZEV review from 2027 to 2026; LCV mandate at 24% BEV vs 9.4% actual seen as particularly disruptive (£15k/van fines).
  • FY27 capex guidance: ~£16m sustaining + £3.6m+ expansion (to rise with new Chinese franchises).
  • £93m RCF (£37m undrawn) to be refinanced summer 2026; covenants comfortably met.
  • CEO Forrester: "trading performance in March and April has been strong and ahead of the prior year period"; group "excellently positioned to take advantage of the inevitable opportunities that will arise as the sector continues to consolidate."
  • Board comment on outlook: "encouraged by the Group's strong start to FY27, which supports confidence in delivering market expectations" despite ZEV and macro headwinds.
  • Risk flag: prolonged Middle East conflict could drive inflation; ~£1m FY27 energy/fuel exposure (80% hedged); Deloitte Consumer Tracker at lowest since 2023 but no adverse trends yet visible in group trading.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives full-year period 'Year ended 28 February 2026 (FY26)'

body:
• Overall YTD sales rate up 32% to 1.20 (2025: 0.91), driven by targeted push on completed/near-completed Open Market stock.
  • YTD Open Market sales rate ~30% higher YoY, though moderating recently due to Middle East conflict uncertainty.
  • Increased incentives/discounts skewed to low-margin, near-completion sites — profit impact pulled forward, weighted more heavily to H1 than previously anticipated; discounting expected to ease in H2.
  • Partner transaction activity subdued during transition between Social Affordable Housing Programmes; SAHP 2026-2036 bidding recently closed, grant notifications expected Q3.
  • Step-up in affordable housing demand expected late 2026 into 2027, driving H2-weighted partner revenues.
  • Forward order book £4.5bn (2025: £4.6bn); £2.3bn for current-year delivery (2025: £2.1bn).
  • Middle East events creating upward pressure on material costs and, to a lesser extent, labour — expected to continue into H2.
  • Share buyback paused to prioritise debt reduction.
  • Other cash actions: inventory reduction, stricter partner deal pricing discipline, slowing build on some sites to match Open Market sales rates, higher hurdles for land buying with focus on low-capital schemes.
  • H1 average daily net debt to be higher YoY due to higher early-year land payments and slower reservation-to-completion conversion (housing chain delays).
  • Now expecting net cash position in excess of £100m at 31 December 2026.
  • H1 2026 profit expected to be significantly lower YoY; H2 2026 profit expected in line with H2 2025.
  • FY 2026 adjusted PBT expected towards the middle of analyst forecast range of £168m–£283m.
  • New CEO Adam Daniels leading operational review; findings to be shared no later than interim results on 24 September 2026.
  • Board reaffirms commitment to Partnerships strategy.
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with half-year financial figures

body:
• Full-year Board expectations unchanged; trading in line YTD despite continued end-market uncertainty.
  • Group revenue £205m, down 1% YoY (2025: £207m).
  • Landscaping Products revenue £86m (flat YoY); regained market share without margin erosion; on track for previously announced £11m annualised cost savings by year-end from performance improvement plan.
  • Building Products revenue £56m (flat YoY); Mortars & Screeds benefited from shift to ready-to-use products; Bricks & Masonry hit by weak new housing and competitive conditions; Water Management building pipeline for AMP8 investment cycle.
  • Roofing Products revenue £63m, down 3% YoY (2025: £65m); Marley facing softer demand and additional industry capacity in concrete roof tiles; Viridian Solar delivered YoY revenue growth with maintained margins.
  • Pre-IFRS 16 net debt £167m vs £171m prior year; £107m of RCF undrawn; expects further deleveraging in 2026 (increase since 31 Dec 2025 is seasonal working capital).
  • Flagged cost inflation from the war in the Middle East being managed via targeted commercial actions and customer cost recovery; UK-centric manufacturing limits direct freight exposure.
  • Outlook caveat: potential for ongoing volatility from Middle East war to further impact trading.
  • CEO Simon Bourne: "Trading in the first four months of the year has been in line with our expectations, and our teams are making clear progress in the areas within our control."
  • CEO on strategy: "In Landscaping, the performance improvement plan continues to deliver progress; in Water Management, we are building momentum; and across Roofing, disciplined commercial and operational execution is supporting performance."
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with financial performance figures

body:
• Appointed Mark Vassella as Non-executive Director, effective 1 June 2026.
  • Vassella brings 40+ years in the global steel industry and materials value chain, directly relevant to BHP's iron ore and steelmaking coal customer base.
  • Was CEO and MD of BlueScope Steel from January 2018 to January 2026, overseeing operations across Australia, New Zealand, North America and Asia.
  • Began career as a cadet at BHP Newcastle (NSW) in the early 1980s; prior leadership roles in Australia, UK and US; former WorldSteel Association Board member.
  • Highlighted skills: large-scale industrial operations, government/Indigenous/community relations, safety, decarbonisation and capital allocation discipline.
  • Chair Ross McEwan: "We are delighted to announce that Mark Vassella will join BHP. Mark has extensive experience in the global steel industry. Mark brings a strong focus on global resource development, values-based leadership and relationships with people and community. We look forward to welcoming Mark to the Board on 1 June 2026."

body:
• Director declaration: Jonathan Howell appointed as an independent non-executive director at Centrica plc, effective 12 May 2026.
  • Howell will additionally become Chair of the Audit and Risk Committee and a member of the Nominations Committee at Centrica, effective 31 July 2026.
  • No further disclosures required under UKLR 6.4.9R — routine external appointment notification, no operational or financial implications for Whitbread.

body:
• Portfolio company Enterprise Therapeutics reported positive Phase 2 results for ETD001, its lead inhaled candidate for cystic fibrosis.
  • Trial met its primary efficacy outcome in the 10% of CF patients with the highest unmet medical need.
  • Demonstrated a clinically meaningful improvement in lung function over 28 days vs placebo.
  • Safety profile: overall well-tolerated, adverse events consistent with expectations for the population.
  • Next steps: advance ETD001 into longer-duration Phase 2b studies, in combination with CFTR modulator therapies, and into other muco-obstructive lung diseases.
  • CEO Greg Smith: "This is a highly encouraging clinical milestone for Enterprise Therapeutics and a strong validation of its differentiated approach to cystic fibrosis. Demonstrating a clinically meaningful improvement in lung function in a population with significant unmet need highlights the potential of ETD001. For IP Group, this represents another example of our model providing investors with access to differentiated deep science opportunities."
  • Note: non-regulatory release; no financial impact, valuation uplift or stake size disclosed.

body:
• Net revenues RMB315.7bn, +4.9% YoY; net product revenues +1.0%, net service revenues +20.6%.
  • Segment revenue mix: JD Retail +1.8%, JD Logistics +29.0%, New Businesses +9.1%.
  • Category mix: Electronics & home appliances -8.4%; General merchandise +14.9%; Marketplace & marketing +18.8%; Logistics & other services +21.7%.
  • GAAP net income attributable to shareholders RMB5.1bn vs RMB10.9bn (-53%); non-GAAP net income RMB7.4bn vs RMB12.8bn (-42%).
  • Diluted EPS per ADS RMB3.54 vs RMB7.19; non-GAAP diluted EPS per ADS RMB5.12 vs RMB8.41.
  • Operating income RMB3.8bn vs RMB10.5bn; operating margin 1.2% vs 3.5%; non-GAAP operating margin 1.8% vs 3.9%.
  • JD Retail operating margin hit record 5.6% (vs 4.9%); operating income RMB15.0bn vs RMB12.8bn.
  • New Businesses operating loss widened sharply to RMB10.4bn (margin -164.9% vs -23.1%) on JD Food Delivery investment; management notes sequential narrowing of losses.
  • JD Logistics operating income RMB1.0bn vs RMB0.1bn; margin 1.7% vs 0.3%.
  • Cost pressures: Fulfillment +18.5% (7.4% of revenue vs 6.6%); Marketing +45.8% (4.9% vs 3.5%) on new business promotion; R&D +48.6%; G&A +48.7%, partly due to RMB635m SAMR fine related to third-party cake-shop food safety compliance failures.
  • Non-GAAP EBITDA RMB8.0bn vs RMB13.7bn; margin 2.5% vs 4.6%.
  • Free cash flow -RMB6.5bn (vs -RMB21.6bn Q1 2025); TTM FCF RMB21.6bn vs RMB37.6bn a year ago.
  • Cash, restricted cash and short-term investments RMB215.7bn (US$31.3bn), down from RMB225.4bn at year-end 2025.
  • Buyback: repurchased ~1.6% of shares outstanding for US$631m in Q1; US$1.4bn remaining under US$5.0bn programme through Aug 2027; annual dividend paid in April.
  • Inventory turnover days lengthened to 38.3 (TTM) vs 32.8 a year ago.
  • Founder award: boards of JD Health, JD Logistics, JD Industrials and JD Property each approved granting Richard Liu RSUs equal to ~2% of each subsidiary's share capital, 4-year vesting.
  • Strategic moves: launched Joybuy online retail in Europe (UK, Germany, Netherlands, France, Belgium, Luxembourg) on 16 March; JoyExpress "Double 11" (211) delivery now covers 30+ European cities/40m people.
  • 7Fresh Kitchen expanded to Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, Shenzhen and Tianjin; JD Food Delivery unit economics improving sequentially.
  • Segment reclassification: from Jan 2026, JD Logistics on-demand delivery shifted to serving third-party merchants directly, reducing internal revenues and New Businesses external on-demand revenues.
  • JD Ecosystem headcount >900,000; trailing 12-month personnel expenditure RMB166.4bn.
  • CEO Sandy Xu: "Our user base and shopping frequency continued to expand robustly, with annual active customers hitting a new record... JD Retail demonstrated remarkable resilience throughout the quarter, with profitability reaching record levels... we remain confident in our full-year trajectory and long-term value creation."
  • CFO Ian Su Shan: performance "anchored by the steady progress of JD Retail, as general merchandise categories and high-margin businesses including marketplace and marketing revenues continued to outperform, alongside a substantial narrowing of sequential losses in our New Businesses, led by JD Food Delivery."

body:
• Announced USD50 million share buyback programme, in line with distribution policy.
  • Buybacks commence Tuesday, expected to complete by 11 May 2027.
  • Purpose: reduce share capital and meet employee share option obligations.
  • Barclays appointed to execute repurchases on Man Group's behalf.
  • Management statement: "The share buyback programme is in line with the company's policy to distribute capital to shareholders while maintaining a prudent balance sheet after taking into account required capital and potential strategic opportunities."
  • Shares rose 1.5% to 274.00p on the announcement.
  • Context: Q1 2026 saw net outflows of USD1.6 billion vs JPMorgan consensus of USD1.8 billion net inflows, dragged by a USD6.1 billion single-client redemption.
  • AuM rose 0.5% to USD228.7 billion at 31 March (vs USD227.6 billion at end-2025), below USD231.1 billion consensus.

body:
• Announced pricing terms of cash tender offer for any and all of its $500m outstanding 3.125% Senior Notes due Nov. 2, 2026.
  • Purchase Price set at $995.83 per $1,000 principal, based on 4.125% UST due Oct 31, 2026 reference yield of 3.786% plus fixed spread of 25bps.
  • Holders also receive accrued and unpaid interest up to (but excluding) the settlement date.
  • Tender Offer expires 5 p.m. NYC time, 12 May 2026; expected settlement 15 May 2026.
  • Tender is conditioned on completion of a contemporaneous New Notes Offering of senior debt securities, whose proceeds will fund the repurchase — effectively a refinancing of the 2026 maturity ahead of its November maturity date.
  • No minimum tender condition; offer may be amended, extended or terminated.
  • Any Notes not tendered are expected to be repaid at maturity, with the Company planning to deposit funds with Deutsche Bank National Trust Company (trustee) to satisfy and discharge the indenture shortly after settlement.
  • J.P. Morgan Securities LLC acting as exclusive Dealer Manager; D.F. King & Co. as tender/information agent.

• Final results of cash tender offer for its 3.125% Senior Notes due 2026, which expired at 5 p.m. NYC time on 12 May 2026.
  • Tenders received for $448,412,000 aggregate principal amount, representing 89.68% of outstanding Notes.
  • Excludes $226,000 principal amount subject to guaranteed delivery procedures.
  • Purchase Price: $995.83 per $1,000 principal, plus accrued and unpaid interest to (but excluding) Settlement Date.

body:
• Top end of FY26 guidance achieved: Adjusted EBITDAaL €11.6bn and Adjusted FCF €2.6bn on guidance basis.
  • Total revenue +8.0% to €40.5bn (FY25: €37.4bn), boosted by Three UK consolidation, partly offset by FX.
  • Service revenue +8.8% reported to €33.5bn; organic +5.4%, growth in all segments except Germany.
  • Adjusted EBITDAaL +3.8% reported to €11.4bn; organic +4.5%.
  • Group Adjusted EBITDAaL margin 28.1% (FY25: 29.2%), down 1.1pps reported.
  • Operating profit swung to €2.8bn from a €0.4bn loss (no FY26 impairments vs €4.5bn prior-year charge).
  • Adjusted basic EPS 10.72c vs 7.87c (+36%); basic loss per share narrowed to 1.20c from 15.86c.
  • Adjusted FCF €2.6bn (FY25: €2.5bn, +2.9%); Free cash flow €1.8bn (-3.0%).
  • Net debt rose to €25.4bn (FY25: €22.4bn) due to VodafoneThree consolidation and €2bn buyback; targeting lower half of 2.25-2.75x leverage range.
  • Liquidity: €12.4bn cash + short-term investments (down from €16.3bn); €7.6bn undrawn RCF.
  • Final dividend 2.3625c; total FY26 DPS 4.6125c, +2.5% YoY (progressive policy from Nov 2025).
  • Second €2bn buyback completed 11 May 2026; total capital returned in FY26 €3.1bn; €9bn returned over three years.
  • FY27 guidance: Adjusted EBITDAaL €11.9–12.2bn; Adjusted FCF €2.6–2.9bn; Europe Adj. EBITDAaL €7.6–7.9bn.
  • FY27 restructuring/integration costs to peak at ~€0.7bn (incl. ~€0.4bn VodafoneThree integration).
  • Safaricom consolidation would add pro-forma ~€1.5bn to Adj. EBITDAaL (subject to Kenya High Court ruling 18 May 2026).
  • Germany: organic service revenue -0.2% FY26 but improved to +1.3% in Q4 (vs -0.2% Q3 reported figure; Q4 organic 1.3%); EBITDAaL organic -3.3%, margin 35.0% (-1.0pp). Broadband ARPU on new customers +30% YoY in Q4.
  • Germany broadband base -202k; mobile contract base -103k; competitive intensity and MDU TV transition (-0.8pp) cited.
  • UK: organic service revenue +0.3%; Adj. EBITDAaL organic +4.5%; reported revenue +30% on Three consolidation; margin 20.5% reported (organic +1.4pp).
  • UK Business organic service revenue -4.5% on planned managed services contract terminations.
  • VodafoneThree: agreed buyout of CKHGT's 49% stake for £4.3bn (€4.9bn) cash via share cancellation (announced 5 May 2026); confirms £700m annual cost & capex synergies by FY30; network sharing across 10,000 sites ahead of plan.
  • Other Europe: organic service revenue +0.5%; Adj. EBITDAaL organic +3.7%; margin +1.5pp organic; Portugal weak on 4th-operator entry.
  • Türkiye: organic service revenue +45.2% (ex-hyperinflation +10.8%); Adj. EBITDAaL organic +47.6%; 100MHz 5G spectrum acquired for $627m; 5G launched April 2026.
  • Africa: organic service revenue +12.9%; Adj. EBITDAaL organic +14.0%; margin 33.9% (+0.6pp).
  • M&A: Vodacom to acquire 20% of Safaricom for €1.81bn total (15% from Kenya govt €1.36bn, 5% from Vodafone €0.45bn); pending Kenya High Court ruling 18 May 2026.
  • VodafoneZiggo sale to Liberty Global agreed: €1.0bn cash + 10% stake in new Ziggo Group entity; expected close H2 2026.
  • Skaylink acquired Dec 2025 for €175m (cloud/digital); Telekom Romania Mobile acquired Oct 2025 for €30m.
  • Vantage Towers: Vodafone's share hit by €464m non-cash impairment of INWIT investment.
  • Vodafone Idea CLAM indemnity settled 31 Dec 2025 via €219m cash payments (offset by VIL service charges) and assignment of 3,280m VIL shares; €256m liability recognised.
  • UK class action (Gutmann loyalty penalty): claim values now £557m (Vodafone) and £197m (Three) post strike-out; defences filed Feb 2026; trial-setting hearing 1-2 July 2026.
  • Adjusted ETR 28.1% (FY25: 25.3%); reported ETR 96.8% inflated by €358m UK DTA derecognition and €305m German DTA write-down.
  • Pre-tax ROCE (controlled) 6.6% (-0.4pp); post-tax ROCE stable at 4.4%.
  • Capital intensity 18%; medium-term ambition reiterated for double-digit organic Adj. FCF growth.
  • CEO Margherita Della Valle: "After the transformation of the last three years, we are now a simpler company with a stronger growth outlook."
  • CEO: "We returned to top line growth in Germany, alongside strong performances across Africa and in Türkiye."
  • CEO: "We are now well set for mid-term growth."
  • Board/Exec changes: Pilar López appointed Group CFO 1 Dec 2025 (succeeding Luka Mucic); Simon Segars new SID; Anne-Françoise Nesmes joined as NED; Guillaume Boutin appointed CEO Vodafone Investments & Strategy May 2025.

time_period: Full year ended 31 March 2026 (FY26) with H1 FY27 outlook
body:
• Guidance upgrade: now expects breakeven to slightly positive net profit for FY26, improved versus previous guidance.
  • Upgrade driven by stronger underlying revenue and a well-hedged macroeconomic mix.
  • Total cash position of €2.1bn at year-end, described as a strong liquidity position.
  • Middle East conflict flagged as near-term risk to fuel costs and customer demand.
  • Summer fuel hedging: ~70% hedged at ~$720 per metric tonne.
  • Fleet fuel efficiency advantage: A321neos now 75% of current fleet, consuming 18% less fuel than legacy aircraft.
  • Fleet size: 262 Airbus A320/A321 aircraft.
  • H1 FY27 scheduled capacity ~51 million seats, +28% YoY.
  • H1 FY27 forward bookings 44% sold, +2ppt YoY.
  • Network expansion includes new operating bases within European footprint, with significant bias to leisure demand.
  • Management has strategically used promotional fares to stimulate H1 FY27 demand and protect load factors amid geopolitical uncertainty.
  • CEO Jozsef Varadi: "we have proactively pivoted the affected capacity to our core markets and are seeing strong demand trends through the peak summer period across our network."
  • CEO on positioning: "Wizz Air's financial strength, including cash liquidity, hedge positions and our highly fuel-efficient fleet, continues to underpin our ability to grow our business with structural competitive advantages."
  • CEO reiterated focus on strengthening leadership in the faster growing CEE region.
  • FY25 reference: 63.4 million passengers carried.

body:
• Rule 2.4 announcement: LondonMetric and Schroder Real Estate Investment Trust ("SREIT") have reached agreement in principle on key financial terms of a non-binding, indicative all-share consortium offer for Picton Property Income.
  • Proposed consideration: 0.190 LondonMetric shares + 0.881 SREIT shares per Picton share.
  • Implied value: 78.2p per Picton share, valuing Picton equity at £403.4m (LondonMetric 46% / SREIT 54%, matching split of Picton's GAV).
  • Premium of ~7.0% to Picton's 11 May close (73.1p); only ~0.9% to the 77.5p Undisturbed Date price (12 Jan 2026, pre-formal sale process); ~13.0% to 1-month VWAPs prior to Undisturbed Date.
  • Implied EPRA NTA discount of ~9.2% and ~5.6% discount to Picton's 31 Dec 2025 portfolio valuation (£699.1m).
  • Post-completion: Picton holders would own ~4.0% of enlarged LondonMetric and ~48.2% of enlarged SREIT; LondonMetric's existing 11.1% SREIT stake would dilute to ~5.8%.
  • Permitted dividends: Picton can pay 0.95p quarterly dividend for period to 31 Mar 2026 plus future ordinary dividends only to extent covered by cash earnings; excess would trigger consideration reduction.
  • Strategic rationale: addresses Picton's sub-scale listed challenges (per its 13 Jan 2026 formal sale process), keeps assets in UK-listed REITs, strengthens SREIT's LTV into 25–35% target range and inherits below-market cost of debt.
  • Expected to be earnings accretive for both LondonMetric and SREIT, with a "very material, immediate increase in dividend income" for Picton shareholders (not a profit forecast).
  • SREIT fee changes: Schroders to cut SREIT management fee by 10bps across all tiers (new tiers: 0.8% up to £500m NAV/mcap, 0.7% £500m–£1bn, 0.6% above £1bn) plus a 1-year IMA fee waiver spread over 24 months on Picton NAV inherited by SREIT; in exchange SREIT moves to a 3-year fixed IMA, then 1-year notice.
  • LondonMetric agrees to a 6-month lock-up on its residual SREIT stake post-completion.
  • Enlarged SREIT to continue to be led by Fund Managers Nick Montgomery and Bradley Biggins; talent review of Picton staff to follow.
  • Picton Board would be minded to unanimously recommend a firm offer on these terms, subject to reciprocal due diligence, finalised terms and documentation.
  • Shareholder support: letter of intent from TR Property Investment Trust covering its 11.4% Picton stake (58.43m shares) to vote in favour.
  • Pre-conditions to firm offer: confirmatory DD, Picton lender consents, final LondonMetric/SREIT board approvals, and confirmation of Picton board recommendation.
  • Likely structure: Guernsey scheme of arrangement (contractual offer reserved as alternative); Panel dispensation means Consortium not bound by Rule 2.6(a) 28-day PUSU while in Picton's formal sale process.

body:
• FY26 guidance reinstated: Adjusted PBT expected in range of £18m–£25m (previously withdrawn at March AGM update due to Middle East conflict).
  • Record H1 booking volumes of 324k, +7% YoY, vs total ATOL market +3%; travelled volumes +22% to 201.6k.
  • Booked TTV £626.2m, +2% (+£14.5m).
  • Revenue £52.2m, down £7.2m (-12%); includes £0.7m exceptional cancellations from Middle East conflict.
  • Adjusted revenue £52.9m (-£6.4m); ABV down 4% on mix shift to Cities, shorter lead times and switch from UAE/Eastern Med to Western Med.
  • Adjusted EBITDA £6.4m vs £12.8m (-50%); margin collapsed to 12.1% from 21.6%.
  • Adjusted PBT £2.3m vs £8.4m; statutory PBT loss of £3.2m vs £4.5m profit.
  • Marketing costs down £4.5m YoY; marketing as % of revenue improved to 41% from 44%.
  • Overheads up £3.1m to £21.6m on annualisation of FY25 tech investments; restructuring cut headcount by 108, delivering c.£4m annualised savings.
  • Exceptional items £2.6m (vs £0.9m): £0.7m Middle East cancellations, £1.5m restructuring, balance legal/share repurchase costs.
  • Net debt £27.5m (improved £2.0m YoY); £88m of facility headroom; £209.9m in customer trust account (-£14.3m YoY).
  • RCF drawn £27m (vs £40m prior year) under £120m facility (+£30m accordion) expiring Sept 2029; covenants not breached.
  • Interim dividend maintained at 1.0p; FY payout policy 25% of PAT, so total FY26 dividend expected lower than prior year.
  • Capital returns: c.£33m committed in H1 (£29.1m share buybacks + £4.2m dividends); 10.2m shares repurchased/cancelled for £22m, plus £3m cancelled from Sept 2025 purchases.
  • Current trading: bookings +9% over last 6 weeks since H1 end as Summer approaches; activity "stabilised to a more consistent trading pattern".
  • Strategic KPIs: App MAUs +29%; app booking mix 38%; in-year repeat bookings +24%; 2-year repeat rates +17%; funnel conversion +24%.
  • Strategic expansion areas: City volumes +116% (Krakow, Amsterdam); Republic of Ireland +74%; Cruise test-and-learn launched.
  • AI: app launched on ChatGPT (24 March) as first mover in UK package space; Anthropic integration in progress; LLM share of voice 15% in Q2, ranked 3rd in UK AI-driven holiday discovery.
  • Automation: 98% of bookings automated (vs 60% in FY22); recently eliminated manual fulfilment of c.130,000 bookings/year.
  • Lead time shift: bookings with <90 day lead time +23%; Winter 26 bookings +20%; higher-value Summer/Eastbound bookings deferred ("wait and see").
  • Operational drag from conflict: rolling UAE cancellations, elevated customer contact, increased flight schedule changes.
  • CEO Shaun Morton: "we have today reinstated guidance and the Board is confident in delivering FY26 Adjusted PBT in the range of £18m to £25m" and "I am confident that On the Beach is well placed to satisfy this demand and deliver a solid Summer trading performance."
  • Addressable market now stated at over 50m passengers following Cities, Cruise and Ireland expansion.

body:
• System-wide revenue $1,166m, +9% YoY; Group revenue $958m, +4% YoY.
  • 2026 guidance maintained: adjusted EBITDA $585m–$625m, Company-owned revenue growth of at least 4% (flat costs), recurring managed fee income $80m, investment grade credit rating commitment.
  • Signings accelerated to 382 in Q1 2026 (vs 224 in Q1 2025, +71%); openings 222 (vs 165, +35%).
  • Managed & Franchised fee income $39m, +70% YoY; recurring management fee revenue $16m, +80% YoY; system (partner) revenue +41% YoY to $260m.
  • M&F rooms open 336,000 (+48%), centres open 2,082 (+53%), pipeline 231,000 rooms — implies potential annual system revenue >$1.9bn when mature.
  • M&F RevPAR: Managed -5% to $164 (drag from immature new openings; ex-2025 openings $222); Franchised & JV +1% to $492.
  • Company-owned: revenue $906m, +2% YoY (return to growth); RevPAR +6% to $389; only 5 new locations signed, 9 opened; capex in line with prior guidance.
  • IWG Network blended RevPAR -2% to $348 (ex-2025 openings $378).
  • Net financial debt rose to $858m (from $715m at YE25 and $721m at Q1 25), driven by $53m share buyback (19.5m shares), annual bonus payments, and a temporary working capital drag from automated invoicing rollout shortening payment days.
  • Net debt expected to normalise through 2026 but end 2026 slightly above YE25 levels.
  • $75m returned to shareholders YTD 2026; $100m of buybacks announced for 2026; >$230m returned since December 2023 Investor Day.
  • Most of the 0.5% Convertible Bond put back to the Company in December 2025; full-year interest cost on €300m 5.125% 2032 bond now hitting 2026; first refinancing not until 2030; all bonds fixed-coupon, hedged into USD.
  • Management taking proactive cost reduction actions in Q2 and beyond to address inflationary pressures from conflicts and macro uncertainty.
  • CEO Mark Dixon: "I am delighted with our strong start to 2026 as we continue the rapid growth of our network supported by increasing sales despite the challenging economic backdrop."
  • Dixon on demand drivers: "Potential customers are requiring more flexibility in their Real Estate strategy to address the uncertainty arising from the impact of conflicts and the growing influence of AI. This is resulting in record levels of Enterprise customer enquiries."
  • Dixon on capital model: "Signings and openings continue to grow, allowing the flywheel of our business model to deliver greater cashflow while requiring less capital to grow than historically."
  • Q1 2025 comparatives now restated under US GAAP (from IFRS) following H1 2025 basis change.
  • Next update: Interim results 11 August 2026; AGM 19 May 2026; final 2025 dividend paid 29 May 2026.

body:
• Group revenue +3.5% to £589.0m (HY25: £569.3m); organic growth of 2.2%, expected to accelerate in H2.
  • Adjusted operating profit +4.4% to £33.4m; adjusted operating margin 5.7% (+10bps).
  • Profit before tax +8.6% to £25.5m; adjusted EPS +9.6% to 30.9p.
  • Interim dividend raised +4.9% to 7.0p (HY25: 6.7p), payable 8 July 2026.
  • Record order book of £945m (HY25: £908m), underpinning FY26 confidence.
  • Balance sheet swing to pre-IFRS16 net cash of £10.6m (HY25: net debt £11.8m).
  • Exceptional/amortisation charge £6.6m (HY25: £5.9m); reported operating profit £26.9m (+3.0%).
  • Discontinued operations loss of £0.9m driven by an increased Allenbuild provision following internal reassessment of contractual claim settlement costs.
  • Rail: subdued renewals as Network Rail prioritises reactive maintenance; delivered record emergency response volumes; remains Network Rail's largest infrastructure services supplier; CP7 (£45.4bn, £31.9bn renewals) runs to March 2029.
  • Highways: RIS3 confirms £27bn over five years with renewals nearly doubling to £8.4bn (vs RIS2 £4.8bn); interim one-year £4.8bn cycle included +18% maintenance/renewals spend (~£1.3bn); SDF1 frameworks extended 5 months to February 2028.
  • Water: record activity levels; working 10 of 12 largest combined regions, 8 in line/ahead; AMP8 commits £104bn investment (£45bn new infrastructure vs AMP7's £11bn); Thames Water work unaffected by client's financial position.
  • Renewables: French subsidiary of Full Circle undergoing restructuring review following underperformance flagged in April trading update; 63 new MSAs signed, total 747 turbines under contract.
  • T&D: RIIO-T3 final determination approved £28.1bn; sits within ~£90bn pipeline to 2031; Emerald Power trading in line with pre-acquisition expectations.
  • Nuclear: Sellafield performance impacted by ongoing site-wide industrial action; underpinned by UK's ~£4bn annual decommissioning programme.
  • M&A activity: Emerald Power acquired Oct 2025 for up to £11.3m (initial £6.8m, £4.5m deferred remuneration); EDS acquired 30 April 2026 for up to £13m (initial £10m); PWR-X acquired 7/11 May 2026 for £1.1m total (£0.75m initial). EDS described as "immediately earnings enhancing".
  • Five-year track record: organic revenue +40%, total revenue +80%, adjusted EPS +63%, 7 acquisitions deploying £135.4m, average ROCE 27%, free cash conversion 63.4%.
  • CEO Paul Scott: "With a record order book afforded to us by highly visible, committed, long-term spending cycles, we remain confident in continuing on this growth trajectory and we look forward to delivering against our full year expectations."
  • Outlook: confident in delivering FY26 expectations; active M&A pipeline supported by strong balance sheet.

body:
• £50m share buyback programme announced, commencing 18 May post-AGM — first capital return programme, underpinned by balance sheet strength and disposal proceeds.
  • Near and medium-term earnings guidance unchanged; CEO Paul Williams: "Our near and medium-term earnings guidance is unchanged and we remain focused on driving income growth and returns."
  • £25.3m of leasing YTD (£20.0m new + £5.3m renewals/regears); open-market lettings 5.2% above December 2025 ERV; further £6.7m under offer.
  • Network W1 pre-let entirely to Databricks for £14.1m at avg £103 psf (£125 psf best) — 5% above Dec-25 ERV and 22% above original underwriting.
  • Network W1 reached practical completion 5 May 2026; expected to drive a positive H1 revaluation.
  • Network W1 + 25 Baker Street will add £18.9m of incremental rental income in 2026.
  • EPRA vacancy rose to 5.2% in Q1 (vs 4.1% at Dec-25) but has fallen in Q2 to date.
  • £278m of disposals exchanged YTD at blended 5.8% initial yield, c.3% below Dec-25 book value — tracking the £1bn three-year target; further c.£120m in discussion.
  • Disposals: Horseferry House £131.8m, 90 Whitfield Street £110.5m, 80-85 Tottenham Court Road £32.6m, plus £5.2m residential at 25 Baker Street (cumulative 26 units sold/exchanged for £120.9m).
  • Committed to 50 Baker Street W1 redevelopment (236,000 sq ft), targeting >12% ungeared IRR; completion H2 2029.
  • Three on-site projects (~290,000 sq ft) forecast >10% ungeared IRRs.
  • Holden House W1 (133,500 sq ft) demolition underway, Kier appointed for main works; PC H2 2028.
  • Refurbs: Greencoat & Gordon House (107,800 sq ft) underway, PC H2 2027; Middlesex House (50,000 sq ft) vacant possession secured, PC H1 2027.
  • Net debt broadly flat at £1.46bn (Dec-25: £1.45bn); EPRA LTV unchanged at 29.4%.
  • Weighted average interest rate reduced to 3.9% (from 4.1%) following redemption of £55m 2.68% PP notes and £175m 6.5% LMS bonds at maturity.
  • Cash and undrawn facilities fell to £383m (from £627m) pre-disposal completions of £278m due in coming months.
  • Fitch reaffirmed BBB+ (stable) IDR and A- senior unsecured rating on 8 May 2026.
  • 2025 final dividend 56.0p (40.0p PID + 16.0p ordinary) payable 29 May; ex-div 23 April 2026.
  • CEO search at advanced stage via Nominations Committee.
  • Management flags investment market impacted by Middle East conflict, but London office sector retains 'safe haven' appeal.

body:
• Group revenue £537m, +1.3%; Group LFL -0.1%.
  • Retail revenue £392m, -0.4% (LFL -1.7%), hit by exceptional rainfall depressing outdoor categories vs favourable prior-year comp; on a 2-year basis Retail revenue +8.9%.
  • Indoor project sales running ~8ppts ahead of outdoor categories.
  • Design & Installation revenue £145m, +6.4% (LFL +4.3%); delivered sales now in growth for four consecutive quarters, fulfilling the 2025 order book.
  • D&I ordered sales by value slightly below prior year: strong growth in bathrooms and Lifestyle Kitchens, but Bespoke Kitchens orders slowed as customers become more considered on spend.
  • TradePro sales +4% YoY; active TradePro members +9% to 662,000.
  • Retail pricing remains deflationary in low single digits, with overall volume growth; market share gains in interior paint, tiling & flooring, and timber.
  • Comfortable with consensus 2026 adjusted PBT of £57.1m mean (range £54.0m–£59.4m, 11 analysts as at 8 May 2026).
  • Expects Retail sales to return to growth; flags benefit from lower business rates following latest revaluation, plus productivity plan.
  • Accelerating store rollout toward ambition of 300 stores (currently 230); FY26 plan: 4-5 new stores and 15-20 refits/refreshes (3 refreshed in the period); property pipeline locations identified.
  • CEO David Wood: "We are pleased to have achieved further volume growth in the first 17 weeks of the year. TradePro continues to go from strength to strength, while the performance in Design & Installation is testament to our broadened offer across kitchens and bathrooms. Our proven growth strategy gives us added confidence as we accelerate our new store rollout and refresh programme."

body:
• Total sales up 7.5% to £800m (2025: £744m).
  • LFL sales +2.5% YTD, accelerating to +3.3% in the most recent 10 weeks with menu development supporting progress.
  • Full year Board expectations unchanged; "Improved trading; expectations for the year remain unchanged."
  • Expect good first half profit progress, partly reflecting a weak comparator plus good operational cost control; new Derby site operating costs primarily hit H2 as previously guided.
  • 41 gross new shops opened, 21 closed (incl. 11 relocations), 20 net openings; 2,759 shops trading (2,141 company-managed, 618 franchised).
  • Reiterating target of ~120 net openings for FY26.
  • First international airport shop opening shortly at Tenerife South Airport with Lagardère Travel Retail — test of offer in international travel hub.
  • Cost inflation guidance unchanged at ~3% on LFL basis.
  • Forward cover: ~5 months on food & packaging; 85% of 2026 energy/fuel fixed; ~50% of 2027 energy/fuel fixed.
  • Flagged Middle East risk: prolonged conflict would likely drive higher cost inflation late 2026 into 2027 across food retail.
  • Supply chain investment on track — new Derby frozen manufacturing/logistics site operational in 2026; Kettering NDC fit-out continuing, operational in 2027.
  • Menu innovation highlights: new Chicken Roll launched April a "standout", Tandoori Chicken Pizza Slice, reformulated salad range (Chicken Caesar; Chicken, Grains and Greens), and Matcha launch "extremely popular" in appealing to younger customers.
  • Franchise and grocery retailer partnerships contributing to overall sales growth (note gap between +7.5% total and +2.5% LFL reflects estate expansion and wholesale channels).
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with full-year financial figures

body:
• Announced a new share buyback programme of up to US$50 million, to be executed by Barclays Bank PLC as riskless principal.
  • Programme runs 12 May 2026 to 11 May 2027.
  • Maximum repurchase capped at 115,151,767 shares under the 2026 AGM authority (plus any subsequent shareholder authority, in any event <15% of relevant equity class).
  • Purchases may continue uninfluenced by the Company during closed periods.
  • Stated purpose: reduce share capital and meet obligations from employee share option/allocation schemes.
  • Framed as consistent with policy to distribute capital to shareholders while maintaining a prudent balance sheet after required capital and potential strategic opportunities.

body:
• Appoints Guy Stevens as CFO, effective 13 July; incumbent Giorgi Megrelishvili steps down and leaves TBC on 1 August.
  • Stevens brings ~30 years of financial institutions corporate finance experience across UBS Investment Bank, Deloitte Corporate Finance and Keefe, Bruyette & Woods.
  • Notable prior link: joint global coordinator on TBC's 2014 IPO.
  • Chair Arne Berggren: "We are delighted to welcome the new group CFO to TBC. His experience, perspective and familiarity with the group will further strengthen the leadership team and support the Board in delivering its priorities."
  • Context: appointment follows last week's Q1 results, where TBC posted improved profit and flagged optimism on Georgia and Uzbekistan growth outlook.
  • Shares +0.3% on the day at 4,674p; +3.0% over 12 months.

body:
• First binding commercial offtake signed with Trafigura: 8,000 metric tonnes/year of battery-quality lithium carbonate over 10 years from start of commercial production, covering over 40% of total targeted offtake commitments for the SWA Project.
  • CEO David Park: "We signed our first binding commercial offtake agreement with Trafigura, covering over 40% of total targeted offtake commitments for the SWA Project."
  • Plan for 2026 remains to approve FID and begin construction at the SWA Project, with first commercial production targeted in 2029.
  • Four remaining FID deliverables: EPCC/EPCM contracts, NEPA approval, finalising offtakes, and closing project financing.
  • Guidance on timing: construction vendor contracting and environmental review expected to complete in Q2 2026; remaining advanced offtake negotiations targeted to conclude by Q3 2026 (will help finalise SWA Project debt size/structure).
  • Arkansas Demonstration Plant milestones: 1 million barrels of real brine processed, >15,000 DLE cycles completed, and ~340,000 man hours over six years with zero incidents.
  • Cash of $141.0m and working capital of $139.5m at 31 March 2026.
  • No term or revolving debt as of 31 March 2026.
  • Engaged The Walsh Group (Lt. Gen. Robert S. Walsh, USMC Ret.) and Global Mineral Strategies (Gary Stanley) as strategic advisors to strengthen engagement with US federal stakeholders on domestic lithium supply chains.
  • Continuing to expand leasehold footprint in East Texas, including the Franklin project (cited as highest known lithium brine grade in North America).
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• FY guidance raised: underlying operating profit growth now "above 11%", up from "around 10%" — driven by ~7% organic revenue growth, ~2% from M&A, and ongoing margin progression.
  • Pretax profit +15% to USD1.47bn (vs USD1.28bn).
  • Underlying operating profit +12% to USD1.84bn (vs USD1.65bn); 1% ahead of Visible Alpha consensus of USD1.82bn.
  • Revenue +11% to USD24.98bn (vs USD22.57bn); 0.5% ahead of USD24.86bn consensus.
  • Organic revenue growth 7.2%, ahead of 7.0% consensus.
  • Client retention strong at 96%.
  • New business wins of USD4.1bn, +14% YoY, with half from first-time outsourcing.
  • Net new business growth slowed to 3.8% in H1 (vs 4.2% LTM) — weather-impacted in Q2 as previously warned; H2 acceleration expected.
  • BofA estimates net new business stepping up to 4.2–4.3%, keeping FY26 within typical 4–5% range; sees Q3 reporting as "key catalyst to prove net new growth is intact."
  • Interim dividend +13% to 25.5c (from 22.6c).
  • Longer-term framework reiterated: mid-to-high single-digit organic revenue growth, ongoing margin progression, profit growth ahead of revenue.
  • CEO Dominic Blakemore: "We've delivered a strong first-half performance, with underlying operating profit up 12%, enabling us to increase our full-year profit guidance. We have great momentum across the business, driven by excellent new business wins, high levels of client retention and margin progression in both regions."
  • Broker stance: UBS 'buy' (results a "modest positive"); BofA 'buy' flags "potential buyback flexibility later at FY26 results"; JPM 'overweight' warns softer Q2 net new may "overshadow" the print; RBC 'overweight' views recent de-rating as a "good entry point", calling GLP-1/AI/consumer concerns "overdone."
  • Shares +2.1% to USD30.11 on the day.

body:
• IAG announced the final results of its reverse bookbuilding invitation to repurchase its outstanding EUR 825m 1.125% Senior Unsecured Convertible Bonds due 2028.
  • EUR 819m in aggregate principal (99.3% of outstanding) was validly tendered and accepted for purchase.
  • Initial Repurchase Price set at EUR 138,950 per EUR 100,000 principal, to be adjusted via a reference delta of 99% based on the two-day VWAP of IAG shares from 11 May 2026 versus the 8 May 2026 closing price.
  • Final Repurchase Price expected to be announced 12 May 2026 after market close; accrued interest of EUR 3.06 per EUR 100,000 principal payable.
  • Settlement expected on or around 19 May 2026, subject to the Financing Condition; tendered bonds will then be surrendered for cancellation.
  • Only EUR 6m principal will remain outstanding post-settlement — below the 15% threshold — triggering IAG's intention to exercise the clean-up call to redeem the residual stub at par plus accrued interest.
  • BofA Securities and HSBC Continental Europe acted as Joint Dealer Managers.
  • Announcement made by Nicholas Cadbury, CFO & Chief Sustainability Officer.
  • Context: removes potential equity dilution overhang from the 2028 convertibles (issued 2021 during pandemic-era liquidity raise) and simplifies IAG's capital structure, consistent with the group's ongoing balance sheet deleveraging following its return to investment-grade metrics.
type_workings: Rule 'results headline': opening bullets say 'final results'

body:
• Launched a consent solicitation on its £750,009,000 Fixed Rate Reset AT1 Perpetual Subordinated Contingent Convertible Securities callable 2029 (ISIN: XS1043552261).
  • Purpose: align the subordination provisions of these Securities with the "Recent AT1 Subordination Provisions" already embedded in all of Lloyds' other AT1 issuances expected to be outstanding at 1 July 2026.
  • These are the last remaining Lloyds AT1s without the updated subordination language, following the 30 April 2026 notice to redeem the $500m 6.75% AT1s callable June 2026.
  • If passed, Lloyds intends to reclassify the Existing Preference Shares as Tier 2 Capital from 1 July 2026, increasing Group Tier 2 Capital by c.£400m.
  • Existing Preference Shares affected: 9.25% (GB00B3KS9W93), 6.413% (USG5533WAA56/US539439AC38), 6.657% (US539439AE93/US539439AF68), 9.75% (GB00B3KSB238).
  • Background: the prefs ceased to count as Tier 1 at end of CRR transition (31 Dec 2021); previously left ranking pari passu with AT1 to avoid infection risk to legacy AT1s — now being remediated in line with the PRA's Nov 2020 "Dear CFO" letter and BoE resolution planning objectives.
  • PRA has provided confirmation of no objection to the Proposal.
  • Rating agencies (Fitch, Moody's, S&P) have been provided the draft memorandum; no rating change expected on the Securities.
  • Consent fees: 0.25% Early Consent Fee (deadline 4:00pm London, 22 May 2026); 0.10% Late Consent Fee (by Expiration Deadline 10:00am, 29 May 2026); equivalent fees available to Ineligible Securityholders for valid abstentions.
  • Meeting: 2 June 2026, 10:00am at Clifford Chance LLP, London.
  • Management signal on optionality: "in the event the Extraordinary Resolution does not pass, the classification of the Existing Preference Shares is one of the factors that the Issuer will consider alongside the review of any potential optional redemption of the Securities at a future date" — implying a possible call of the 2029 AT1s if consent fails.
  • Solicitation Agent: Lloyds Bank Corporate Markets; Tabulation Agent: Kroll Issuer Services.
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Additional €400 million investment to build a new Guinness brewery ("Brewery 2") at Littleconnell, Newbridge, Co Kildare, with construction starting this year over a three-year period.
  • Comes on top of a prior €300 million investment in a new lager/ale brewery at the same site, officially opened by Taoiseach Micheál Martin and CEO Dave Lewis.
  • Brewery 2 will double total site capacity at Littleconnell and be dedicated to Guinness and Guinness 0.0.
  • Existing new brewery: built in under 18 months, powered by 100% renewable electricity, 50 permanent skilled roles; produces Rockshore, Harp, Smithwicks and licensed Carlsberg.
  • Combined with works at St James's Gate (Dublin) and the Belfast packaging site, Diageo will invest nearly €1 billion across the island of Ireland between 2020 and 2029.
  • CEO Dave Lewis said demand for Guinness and Guinness 0.0 is "surging", adding: "How fitting that it's in County Kildare, the birthplace of Arthur Guinness."
  • Taoiseach Micheál Martin called the investment "a powerful vote of confidence in Ireland and in our future as a world-leading, sustainable food and drink exporter."
  • Shares +0.3% at 1,549.00p on the announcement.

body:
• Revenue +1% to £147.1m (+2% constant currency); volumes +6% to 2,137 tonnes, offset by sales mix, pricing and currency.
  • Q2 stronger inflection: Q2 volumes +14%, Q2 revenue +7% to £84.7m vs soft Q1 (858t / £62.4m).
  • ASP £69/kg, stable sequentially, down 4% YoY (H1 2025: £72/kg).
  • Underlying PBT down 18% to £19.0m (-14% cc), as previously guided.
  • Reported loss before tax of £44.0m (H1 2025: £17.2m profit) after £63m of exceptional items.
  • £60.6m non-cash impairment of China (Panjin) manufacturing facility; process tech in final stage cannot deliver 1,500t nameplate capacity; value in use assessed at just £10.2m; Group remains committed to plant.
  • Gross margin 41.7%, down 240bps (H1 2025: 44.1%); ex-China GM 43.9% vs 46.6%.
  • Underlying EPS 17.2p, -24%; basic LPS (37.0p) vs 17.4p EPS.
  • Interim dividend held at 13.42p; payable 26 June 2026.
  • Net debt £45.4m (H1 2025: £40.7m); net debt/underlying EBITDA 0.65x (within 0.5x–1.0x target; vs 0.51x).
  • Operating cash conversion 109% (H1 2025: 128%).
  • FY 2026 underlying PBT guidance: £42m–£44m (vs FY25 £46.4m).
  • Profit Improvement Plan: c.10% headcount reduction (mainly central functions); ≥£10m annualised savings in FY 2027; some early benefits in H2 2026; cash cost to deliver up to £10m in FY 2026.
  • Additional up to £10m non-cash exceptional charge in H2 2026 from portfolio simplification; total FY 2026 exceptionals expected £75m–£85m.
  • Underlying effective tax rate 24.5% (H1 2025: 22.2%); expected to exceed top of 15–19% mid-term range in FY 2026.
  • Capex £7.4m (H1 2025: £8.6m); FY 2026 expected below 8–10% of revenue guidance range; medium-term capex guidance unchanged at 8–10%.
  • New £20m UK term loan drawn (matures Oct 2028) to reduce RCF reliance; total facilities £51m undrawn at report date.
  • China facility expected to remain significantly loss-making and cash negative in FY 2026 (~100t output); China bank facility refinancing planned Nov 2026.
  • Divisional: Sustainable Solutions revenue +3% to £119.5m (Aerospace +9%, Electronics +14%, E&I +19%, VARs +5%, Automotive -6%); Medical revenue -9% to £27.6m with some Spine stabilisation; Spine/Non-Spine mix now 27/73.
  • UK DB pension scheme buy-in completed in Jan 2026 (£46.7m), de-risking balance sheet.
  • Sustainable product revenues now 46% of total (H1 2025: 55%).
  • Capital Markets Day September 2026 — "Unlocking Victrex's potential" — to outline strategy and medium-term profitability roadmap.
  • Board changes: James Routh appointed CEO 1 Jan 2026; Jakob Sigurdsson retired 6 Feb 2026; Peter Kiernan appointed 1 Mar 2026.
  • CEO Routh: "After a slow start in Q1, we regained momentum in our second quarter, led by our Sustainable Solutions division."
  • CEO on execution: "we have not adapted quickly enough to changed market conditions and we must now relentlessly focus on improving our execution."
  • Outlook commentary: "The Group has made a solid start to the second half, though we are mindful of the potential implications for global demand and energy costs given the ongoing events in the Middle East."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives half-year period '6 months ended 31 March 2026 (H1 2026)'
