body:
• Revenue +18.3% to US$66.43m (Q1 2025: US$56.18m), driven by realised gold price of US$4,816/oz (+66.3%) offsetting lower volumes.
  • Gold production at Blanket 14,767 oz (-20.9%); consolidated gold sales 13,784 oz (-28.9%); 3,656 oz bullion on hand at quarter-end.
  • FY2026 Blanket production guidance reiterated at 72,000–76,500 oz, weighted to H2.
  • Production miss driven by constrained access to higher-grade areas: head grade fell from 3.1 g/t to 2.5 g/t (-19.4%), recovery down to 91.9% (from 93.6%); tonnes milled flat.
  • Central Shaft contributed only 56% of milled tonnage vs planned 65%; stockpile (2.0 g/t) filled gap but diluted grade.
  • Assay grades improving month-on-month: ~2.55 g/t Dec 2025 → 3.02 g/t Mar 2026 → ~2.86 g/t April 2026.
  • On-mine cost US$1,740/oz (+44.8%); AISC US$2,765/oz (+53.9%) — both inflated by lower volumes/fixed cost absorption, not absolute cost creep (on-mine US$ total +3.0%, AISC US$ total +9.4%).
  • Gross profit +19.2% to US$32.10m; EBITDA +50.2% to US$33.87m; PAT +69.4% to US$18.91m; Basic EPS +77.8% to US$0.80.
  • Net cash from operations +41.5% to US$18.87m; free cash flow US$12.28m vs US$4.86m (+152.6%).
  • Capex US$5.28m (-8.4%), all at Blanket.
  • Quarterly dividend maintained at 14 US cents/share, payable 5 June 2026.
  • Liquidity transformed: total US$191.1m (cash US$170.0m, bullion US$16.4m), reflecting January 2026 issuance of US$150m convertible senior notes due 2033 (5.875% coupon, conversion price ~US$40.51, 25% premium); capped call options purchased for US$14.44m (cap ~US$56.72).
  • Net fair value gain on derivatives of US$4.00m in the quarter (Q1 2025: US$1.59m loss).
  • Mitigation actions underway: contractor appointed to accelerate development; shift system moving from 6 to 7 days/week; ~36kt stockpile buffer being built ahead of Central Shaft winder upgrade later in 2026; additional ball mill commissioning mid-2026 to lift throughput from 97.55 to 101.98 tph.
  • Bilboes financing progressing: peak funding US$484m; FS (Nov 2025) shows 10.8-year LOM, 150koz/yr avg production, AISC US$1,061/oz, post-tax NPV8% US$582m, IRR 32.5% at US$2,548/oz (significantly better at spot); both interim facility and project finance facility discussions advanced.
  • Deep-level drilling (10,312m completed Mar–Dec 2025) confirms continuity of Blanket, Eroica and Lima orebodies at depth, including new BLK7 orebody; updated resource/reserve statement expected during 2026.
  • Motapa: US$3.8m allocated for 2026; maiden sulphide MRE at Motapa North targeted for 2026.
  • Safety: LTIFR improved to 0.0 (Q1 2025: 2.4); TIFR 2.2 (-54.3%).
  • Diesel only ~3% of operating costs and ~2% of power (vs 8% in 2020); solar covers ~20% of power; exports routed via South Africa — no impact from Middle East geopolitics.
  • CEO Mark Learmonth: "We continue to trade in line with market expectations and with a strong gold price environment, improving operational performance at Blanket and continued progress towards developing Bilboes, we remain confident in our strategy and our ability to deliver long‑term value for shareholders."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Completed disposal of St Pancras Way to USAF for £186m (Unite share: £126m), at a 1% discount to December 2025 book value.
  • Proceeds: ~£115m in cash, balance in new USAF units (net of retentions for committed capex).
  • Unite's stake in USAF rises to 32% (from 30% at Dec 2025), enhancing management fee income while retaining exposure to a high-quality London asset.
  • Share buyback extended by an additional £65m, taking aggregate Buyback Programme to £165m.
  • To date, 19.3m shares acquired at an average cost of 504p, totalling £98m of the initial £100m tranche.
  • Extension funded by St Pancras Way proceeds; balance allocated to capex for on-site developments and university partnerships.
  • Accelerated disposal programme on track to deliver £300–400m (Unite share) of proceeds in 2026.
  • Mechanics: JP Morgan to conduct first half of extension, Deutsche Bank the latter half; all repurchased shares to be cancelled.
  • Extension runs from completion of initial £100m tranche until earlier of completion or 31 October 2026 (subject to AGM authority).
  • AGM authority sought for repurchase of up to 80,179,399 shares (14.99% of share capital as at 31 March 2026), versus 29,546,586 remaining under prior authority.
  • Management framing: buyback "reflects confidence in Unite's long-term prospects and high-quality balance sheet and represents a highly attractive investment opportunity for the Company."

body:
• Operating profit jumped 77% to EUR351m (Q1 2025: EUR198m), beating Bloomberg consensus of EUR246m.
  • Revenue up 1.9% to EUR7.18bn from EUR7.04bn.
  • Basic EPS 6.5 euro cents, up 76% from 3.7 cents.
  • Net debt fell to EUR4.18bn from EUR5.95bn.
  • Available seat kilometres flat at 79.3m; passenger revenue per ASK up 3.5% to 7.85 cents from 7.58 cents.
  • Profit warning: higher fuel costs from Middle East crisis will "inevitably lead to lower profit this year than we originally anticipated" (CEO Luis Gallego); no specific new guidance figure given.
  • JPMorgan notes current full-year consensus operating profit of EUR4.7bn, vs EUR5.02bn in 2025.
  • Full-year fuel cost now expected at EUR9bn including hedges, +27% vs 2025's EUR7.08bn and well above February guidance of EUR7.0–7.4bn.
  • Capacity guidance cut: no longer expects 3% FY group capacity growth; now ~1% in Q2 and ~2% in Q3.
  • ~3% of capacity exposed to Middle East, largely being redeployed.
  • Free cash flow now expected below the EUR3bn guided in February.
  • Capex lowered to ~EUR3.5bn from EUR3.6bn previously.
  • CEO: "We are actively managing the uncertainty created by the fuel price increase and its impact," and "currently see no issues with fuel availability."
  • Strong demand cited in Premium cabins and North/South transatlantic (~50% of capacity combined).
  • Business travel delivering good revenue growth; some long-haul yield pressure at Aer Lingus.
  • Short-haul European market described as "competitive".
  • Shares fell 4.1% to 380.05p on the day (FTSE 100 down 0.8%).
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1'

body:
• Activist Saba Capital Management (18% stake) has filed a requisition notice to remove 5 non-executive directors and install 4 of its own nominees at the AGM on 23 July.
  • Follows Saba's January proposal for a managed wind-down, which Workspace rejected as "neither achievable nor likely to maximise value for shareholders".
  • Board says it remains "open to continuing a constructive dialogue with Saba Capital"; shareholders advised to take no action yet.
  • Management reiterates "clear path to accelerate its strategy to reposition and elevate its offering to deliver sustainable earnings growth".
  • Context: last month Workspace warned of a "substantial step down" in trading profit for FY to March 2027, citing lower starting rents, disposals, higher debt costs, lower capitalised interest, and increased opex/investment.
  • New CEO Charlie Green (joined February): becoming "first-choice provider of space" for start-up/SME/scale-up market requires portfolio investment — "It will take time to deliver on our ambitions and, as we deliberately reposition the business, there will be a step down in profitability."
  • Shares -1.9% to 343.79p; -22% over the last 12 months.
type_workings: Rule 'routine': opening bullets reference the AGM

body:
• Q1 operating profit EUR351m, +77% YoY (vs EUR198m), well ahead of analyst expectations.
  • Revenue EUR7.18bn, +1.9% YoY (vs EUR7.04bn).
  • Net debt fell sharply to EUR4.18bn from EUR5.95bn.
  • Guidance cut: FY2026 profit and free cash flow will be lower than previously anticipated due to Middle East-driven fuel cost surge.
  • FY2026 fuel cost now expected at ~EUR9bn (incl. hedging & sustainability), +27% vs EUR7.08bn in 2025, above prior February guidance of EUR7.0–7.4bn.
  • Capacity growth target lowered from 3% — now ~1% in Q2 and ~2% in Q3; winter schedules under review.
  • ~70% hedged for remainder of year; expects to recover ~60% of higher fuel costs via pricing, capacity redeployment, and cost controls.
  • Only ~3% of capacity had Gulf exposure pre-conflict; redeployed to Bangkok, Singapore where Middle Eastern carriers have cut flights.
  • Q2 booked revenue resilient at 80%, in line with historical levels.
  • Premium cabin demand strong; North & South Atlantic routes (~half of group capacity) remain robust; business travel revenue growing.
  • Softer demand flagged in Eastern Mediterranean; short-haul European market competitive.
  • Margin/profit improvements across all airlines except Aer Lingus, which faces long-haul pricing pressure (per Panmure Liberum).
  • EUR1.5bn buyback continues; remaining EUR1bn to run through February 2027.
  • CEO Luis Gallego: "Demand for travel continues to be robust in our main markets and we have seen resilient booked revenue for the second quarter at 80%, which is in line with historical levels."
  • Davy forecasts consensus FY operating profit ~EUR4.6bn vs EUR5.02bn in 2025: "Very strong Q1 but impact from fuel will be Q2 onwards."
  • Hargreaves Lansdown's Aarin Chiekrie: IAG "much better positioned to navigate the current market challenges better than most of its peers, thanks to its tilt towards more premium passengers."
  • AJ Bell's Russ Mould warned: "Months of further disruption to the flow of crude oil and jet fuel from the Middle East could severely damage the airline industry."
  • Shares -1.2% to 391.31p in London; -1.5% to EUR4.51 in Madrid.
type_workings: Rule 'trading update': 'trading update' named in period line

body:
Construction Partners, Inc. (NASDAQ: ROAD)

Publish Date 8 May 2026 (Q2 FY26 release; ADVFN article link returns "Page Not Found")

Time Period Fiscal Q2 2026 (three months ended 31 March 2026)

  • Massive Q2 beat:

Net income was $9.2 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 and diluted EPS were $0.16, compared to net income of $4.2 million and diluted EPS of $0.08 in the same quarter last year

;

adjusted EPS of $0.18 topped the consensus estimate of ($0.05) by $0.23

.

  • Revenue:

$769.2 million, an increase of 34.5% compared to $571.7 million a year earlier

, well above the ~$678–691m consensus.

  • Organic vs M&A mix:

11% organic increase and a 24% contribution from acquisitions

.

*

Gross profit was $98.9 million vs $71.4 million a year earlier

;

G&A was $63.6m (8.3% of revenue) vs $46.7m (8.2%) prior year

.

  • Adjusted EBITDA:

$93.3 million, maintaining an approximately 12.1% margin on significantly higher revenue

(+35% YoY).

  • Record backlog of $3.14 billion at 31 March 2026;

about 80% to 85% of the next 12 months of contract revenue is covered by backlog

.

  • FY2026 guidance raised to:

revenue $3.59–$3.65 billion, net income $159.0–$162.0 million, Adjusted net income $170.4–$174.2 million, Adjusted EBITDA $552.0–$564.0 million with a 15.38–15.45% Adjusted EBITDA margin

;

organic growth still expected at about 7% to 8% for fiscal 2026

.

*

Company expects about $225 million to $235 million of acquisitive revenue over the remaining six months of the fiscal year

.

  • M&A cadence:

Four Star Paving was the 4th acquisition in FY26 and 17th since FY24; expects to fund Four Star from Q3 cash flow without new long-term debt

.

  • Liquidity:

$77 million of cash on hand and $150 million of availability under its credit facility

;

operating cash flow reached $65.2 million for the quarter, up from $55.6 million a year earlier

.

  • Leverage above target:

debt running at 3.23 times trailing 12-month EBITDA versus a target near 2.5 times

.

  • Input cost hedging:

more than half of liquid asphalt needs now supplied internally; over 80% of revenue is covered by a liquid asphalt index, complemented by physical diesel hedging and terminal storage of roughly two to two and a half months of inventory

.

  • Demand highlights:

eight data center projects in Texas totaling ~$100m of contract value, 12 Nashville warehouse projects totalling ~$28m, FIFA World Cup-related work in Houston, North Carolina road widening tied to the U.S. Open at Pinehurst in 2029, and a taxiway reconstruction at Eglin Air Force Base

.

  • Federal funding context:

management is engaged with Congress on surface transportation reauthorization, with both parties working toward a bill in the $500–$600 billion range; that level was not assumed in the Road 2030 plan, which instead assumes normal mid-single-digit annual increases

.

  • Road 2030 reiterated:

targets doubling the size of the company, generating $1 billion of annual EBITDA and expanding EBITDA margins to about 17%

.

  • Management quote (Exec Chairman Ned N. Fleming, III):

"We are pleased with our team's strong execution this quarter as we continue to advance CPI's proven growth strategy. Our differentiated business model, built on cost pass-through, vertical integration, and a decentralized partnership approach, remains a powerful and often underappreciated driver of sustainable results."

  • Guidance rationale:

"With the peak construction season ahead in the second half of our fiscal year, we are raising our FY 2026 outlook, and we are well-positioned to execute against this record backlog and sustain our growth momentum."

  • Market reaction:

shares up roughly 5% in pre-market following the release, adding to a strong run of ~6.2% over the past week and over 11.6% in the last month

.

body:
• Barratt Redrow: Completed the second tranche of its GBP100 million share buyback announced in January.
  • UBS purchased 16.1 million shares for GBP50 million consideration on Barratt's behalf in this tranche.
  • SRT Marine Systems: Signed a GBP5 million one-year support contract with an existing unnamed sovereign customer for SRT-MDA deployment, covering technical support and data services through year-end.
  • CEO Simon Tucker: "This is an important long term sovereign partnership with significant future system growth ambitions."
  • Forgent PLC (formerly Eqtec): Completed maiden field programme at Green Rock project; 111 rock chip samples submitted to ALS Laboratories in Perth for multi-element assay including copper and gold; assay results expected within four weeks.
  • Uru Metals: Commenced ground-based frequency-domain electromagnetic (FDEM) survey at Zebediela nickel project, Limpopo, following completion of gravity survey; aims to delineate conductive zones potentially indicating semi-massive to massive nickel sulphide mineralisation.
  • Imaging Biometrics: Subsidiary Kirkstall selected as industry partner with Nottingham Trent University to develop a living glioblastoma "tumour on a chip" microfluidic model.
  • Earnz PLC: Subsidiary A&D Carbon Solutions (acquired July last year) awarded a contract by Fortem (working for Sanctuary Housing) on an initial two-year basis, for retrofit insulation, ventilation upgrades and renewables in Yorkshire starting this month.
  • Earnz expects initial revenue of GBP2.6 million per year, with potential 12-18 month extension.
  • Physiomics: New board has begun extensive business review focused on operational optimisation, commercial execution and shareholder value.
  • Physiomics: Immediate emphasis on disciplined capital allocation and strict cost control; all new directors agreed to take a portion of remuneration in ordinary shares.
  • Physiomics: Initial balance sheet assessment indicates sufficient cash runway to 2027.
  • Exec Director Mike Whitlow: "The board sees a clear opportunity to introduce stronger commercial focus, tighter operational discipline and a more dynamic growth strategy, while maintaining the high scientific standards for which Physiomics is recognised."
type_workings: Rule 'capital returns': opening bullets announce a share buyback

body:
• Drilling hit high-grade mineralisation at the West Dome Underground (WDU) extension at Pinnacles, Telfer mine, Western Australia.
  • Key intercept: 58.7m at 6.5 g/t gold and 0.1% copper, including 37.0m at 9.98 g/t gold and 0.14% copper; plus a separate 34.1m at 1.1 g/t gold.
  • Company describes the result as among the best intercepts achieved from WDU drilling to date.
  • Extension lies a significant distance south of the current WDU resource but in similar proximity to existing underground crusher and hoist infrastructure, supporting potential development economics.
  • MD Shaun Day: "It is a credit to our exploration team to have first interpreted the potential extension of the West Dome Underground at Pinnacles, a significant distance from our current area of focus, and then to successfully plan and execute this drill hole from surface to its target at depth."
  • Day cautioned: "While the result is a single drill hole, it demonstrates the potential extension of West Dome Underground mineralisation to the south of our current resource."
  • Additional drilling planned to test mineralisation between the two zones (existing WDU resource and new Pinnacles intercept).
  • Shares closed +1.2% at AUD14.90 in Sydney Friday but -4.4% at 759.33p in London (announcement made Sunday post-close).

body:
• Raised FY2026 underlying operating profit growth guidance from ~10% to above 11% (constant currency), driven by organic revenue growth ~7%, ~2% from M&A and ongoing margin progression.
  • Revenue: $25.0bn, +9% constant currency (+10.7% reported); balanced across both regions.
  • Like-for-like / Organic revenue: +7.2%, comprising net new business +3.8%, pricing +2.7%, LFL volume +0.7%.
  • Client retention strong at 96% (North America 97%, International 95%).
  • New business wins of $4.1bn (+14% YoY), with half from first-time outsourcing.
  • Mobilised net new business +4.2% over last 12 months, +3.8% in H1, with expected acceleration in H2.
  • Underlying operating profit $1,839m, +12% constant currency; statutory +9% to $1,605m.
  • Underlying operating margin +20bps to 7.4% (statutory margin -10bps to 6.4% due to higher acquisition-related charges).
  • Underlying EPS 72.8c, +12% constant currency; statutory EPS 62.9c, +16%.
  • Underlying operating cash flow $1,324m (+14%); underlying free cash flow $825m (+11%); cash conversion 72.0% (vs 71.4%).
  • Capex $0.8bn, 3.4% of revenue (vs 3.0%).
  • Interim dividend 25.5c, +13%.
  • M&A spend $2.3bn: Vermaat (Netherlands) $1.7bn (Dec-25, incl. acquired debt repayment), Pro Care Management (Germany) $270m (Feb-26); GPOs now in 5 of top 10 markets.
  • Net debt up $2.2bn to $8.6bn; leverage at 1.7x net debt/EBITDA, above 1.0-1.5x target range as expected due to M&A, peaking at H1; expected to remain above range in FY26.
  • January 2026 €1.5bn ($1.7bn) bond issue (2029/2035 tranches) to refinance Vermaat acquisition commercial paper; avg debt maturity 4.8 years.
  • Liquidity $3.6bn (vs $5.5bn at Sep-25); RCF $3.2bn fully undrawn to Feb-2031.
  • Credit ratings reaffirmed: S&P A/A-1 Stable; Moody's A2/P-1 Stable.
  • USPP covenants comfortably met: leverage 1.6x (vs ≤3.5x); interest cover 17.7x (vs ≥3.0x).
  • North America: revenue +8.1% cc to $16.7bn; organic +7.2%; underlying op profit +9.5% cc; margin +10bps to 8.4%; Business & Industry double-digit organic growth.
  • International: revenue +9.8% cc to $8.3bn; organic +7.1%; underlying op profit +14.5% cc; margin +30bps to 6.1%; Sports & Leisure strongest growth.
  • Underlying finance costs guided c.$350m; underlying effective tax rate guided ~25.5%.
  • Addressable market estimated at ~$360bn, growing ~5% p.a., potentially reaching ~$600bn by 2035; Compass has <15% share with ~75% of market still self-operated/regional.
  • Principal risks unchanged except escalation of geopolitical and economic volatility risks.
  • Brazil tax dispute (PIS/COFINS, 2014 & 2017): total assessed amount $92m (up from $86m); no provision recorded.
  • CEO Dominic Blakemore: "We've delivered a strong first-half performance, with underlying operating profit up 12%, enabling us to increase our full-year profit guidance. We have great momentum across the business, driven by excellent new business wins, high levels of client retention and margin progression in both regions."
  • CEO on long-term: "we remain confident in our ability to sustain mid-to-high single-digit organic revenue growth, ongoing margin progression and profit growth ahead of revenue growth."
  • Q3 trading update scheduled 21 July 2026; FY2026 results 24 November 2026; interim dividend payable 30 July 2026.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line 'Half Year ended 31 March 2026'

body:
• Agreed sale of Lichfield fulfilment centre to Marks & Spencer for cash consideration of £67.5m following a competitive sale process; contract signed 9 May 2026.
  • Net proceeds of at least £66m and annual cash cost savings of c.£6m (rent and occupancy).
  • Classed as a significant transaction under UKLR 7 (exceeds 25% Consideration to Market Cap test).
  • Expected one-off pre-tax profit of c.£85m (reversal of prior impairments + gain on derecognition of assets/lease liabilities); assets had £nil carrying value at 1 March 2026 following prior mothballing impairments.
  • Completion expected in H2 FY26, subject to customary consents.
  • Pro forma net debt (ex-leases) of c.£228m after adding proceeds to cash position of £209.5m at 1 March 2026.
  • Follows November 2025 refinancing and repayment of 2026 convertible bonds last month.
  • Barnsley and Berlin fulfilment centres stated to provide sufficient capacity for future growth; site had already been mothballed due to excess capacity driven by improved stock turn and scaling of Flexible Fulfilment/AFS (launched H2 FY25).
  • Only one remaining non-core asset post-disposal: Atlanta fulfilment centre (already fully written down).
  • No significant change to financial position since 23 April 2026 interim results date.
  • CEO Jose Antonio Ramos: "The disposal of our Lichfield fulfilment centre represents a further step in strengthening ASOS's balance sheet and improving our capital efficiency. This transaction enables us to unlock value from one of our non-core assets while reducing our ongoing cost base, consistent with the actions we have taken over the past three years to simplify the business and enhance financial resilience. ASOS is a well-invested business and we have significant capacity to support future growth. We will continue to maintain a disciplined approach to capital allocation as we execute our strategy."
  • Key risk flagged: completion conditional on third-party consents; non-completion could damage investor confidence and delay balance sheet strengthening.
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'agreed sale of'

body:
• Wholly owned subsidiary Excalon Limited has acquired PWR-X Ltd for total consideration of £1.1m on a cash and debt free basis.
  • PWR-X is a provider of specialist cable jointing services to the power industry, expanding Excalon's capabilities in the high voltage power market.
  • Initial cash consideration of £0.75m funded from existing banking facilities (implying £0.35m deferred/contingent).
  • Group "continues to evaluate, in line with its strategy, a pipeline of further acquisitions."
  • Bolt-on scale only — immaterial relative to Renew's ~£726m market cap and £1.08bn turnover, but strategically aligned with growing UK high-voltage grid investment demand.

body:
• Launched reverse bookbuilding repurchase invitation for its outstanding €825m 1.125% senior unsecured convertible bonds due 2028 (ISIN XS2343113101), with full €825m principal still outstanding as of 8 May 2026.
  • Repurchase window opens 07:00 and is expected to close 16:30 London time today, 11 May 2026, with IAG retaining discretion to extend, re-open, amend or terminate.
  • Initial Repurchase Price of €138,950 per €100,000 principal (i.e. ~38.95% premium to par), to be adjusted via a reference delta of 99% against IAG's share VWAP on 11–12 May 2026 vs the 8 May closing price.
  • Accrued interest from 18 May 2026 to settlement to be paid in cash (~€3.06 per €100,000 principal on expected settlement date).
  • Stated intention to repurchase up to 100% of outstanding principal; bonds bought will be cancelled.
  • Clean-up call flagged: if outstanding bonds fall to ≤15% of original issuance, IAG intends to redeem the remainder at par plus accrued interest.
  • Settlement subject to a Financing Condition, expected on or around 19 May 2026 — implying the buyback is contingent on a refinancing (typical of convert tender + new issue structures).
  • Aggregate principal accepted to be announced post-close on 11 May; Final Repurchase Price on 12 May.
  • IAG reserves right to acquire further bonds via open market/privately negotiated transactions and to exercise the "soft call" option under Condition 7(b)(i).
  • Joint Dealer Managers: BofA Securities Europe and HSBC Continental Europe.
  • Announcement made by Nicholas Cadbury, CFO & Chief Sustainability Officer.

body:
• Revenue $769.2m, +34.5% YoY (vs $571.7m), including 11% organic growth.
  • Gross profit $98.9m (vs $71.4m); gross margin ~12.9% vs 12.5%.
  • G&A $63.6m (8.3% of revenue) vs $46.7m (8.2%) — modest deleverage.
  • Net income $9.2m (diluted EPS $0.16) vs $4.2m ($0.08) — EPS doubled.
  • Adjusted net income $10.4m, +136% YoY (vs $4.4m); Adj. diluted EPS $0.18 vs $0.08.
  • Adjusted EBITDA $93.3m, +34.6% YoY; Adj. EBITDA margin essentially flat at 12.13% vs 12.12% (no margin expansion despite scale).
  • Interest expense rose to $25.6m (vs $21.6m) reflecting higher debt load.
  • Record project backlog of $3.14bn vs $2.84bn YoY and $3.09bn at Dec-25.
  • Completed acquisition of Four Star Paving in April 2026 via Tennessee platform Pavement Restorations, Inc., expanding middle Tennessee/Nashville exposure.
  • H1 FY26 business acquisitions cash outflow $275.9m (vs $828.7m prior year which included a larger transformative deal).
  • Long-term debt rose to $1.71bn (from $1.57bn at Sep-25); cash fell to $76.9m (from $156.1m).
  • H1 operating cash flow $147.8m (vs $96.3m); capex $81.7m (vs $68.2m).
  • Treasury stock purchases YTD $26.0m (vs $20.1m).
  • FY26 guidance RAISED across all metrics:
  • Revenue: $3.59bn–$3.65bn
  • Net income: $159.0m–$162.0m
  • Adj. net income: $170.4m–$174.2m
  • Adj. EBITDA: $552.0m–$564.0m
  • Adj. EBITDA margin: 15.38%–15.45%
  • CEO Jule Smith: "Favorable weather conditions further supported our ability to advance work efficiently and exceed expectations."
  • CEO on energy/inputs: "energy cost volatility had a limited impact on results due to the pass-through nature of our project contracts, as well as the physical hedge inherent to our vertical integration."
  • CEO on positioning: "With the peak construction season ahead in the second half of our fiscal year, we are raising our FY 2026 outlook, and we are well-positioned to execute against this record backlog and sustain our growth momentum."
  • Executive Chairman Ned Fleming on demand: "Growing infrastructure repair and maintenance needs, sustained population migration, economic expansion, and rising roadway capacity demands across the Sunbelt continue to create a durable and growing addressable market for our services."

body:
• Update on proposed all-share acquisition of ASX-listed Emmerson Resources via Australian scheme of arrangement, first announced 9 March 2026.
  • Acquirer nominee changed on 21 April 2026 to Tennant Consolidated Mining Group (TCMG), a wholly-owned Pan African subsidiary; all other material SID/Scheme terms unchanged.
  • Scheme consideration: 0.1493 new Pan African shares (as ASX CDIs) per Emmerson share.
  • Pan African to pursue a foreign exempt listing on the ASX, becoming a triple-listed issuer (LSE primary, JSE primary, ASX CDIs); LSE and JSE remain dual primary.
  • Emmerson Scheme Booklet registered with ASIC today; Supreme Court of WA ordered dispatch and Scheme Meeting convening.
  • Conditions precedent include ≥75% of votes cast and majority by number of Emmerson shareholders voting at Scheme Meeting; no Pan African shareholder approval required (JSE category 2).
  • Indicative timetable: Scheme Meeting 15 June 2026; Second Court Date 19 June; Effective Date 22 June; Implementation Date 1 July 2026.
  • Consideration: issue of 103,240,570 new Pan African shares at implied US$2.27/share (based on 168p LSE close on 17 April 2026) = total deemed consideration ~US$234.2 million.
  • Pro-forma NAV per share impact at 31 Dec 2025: NAV/share rises from 33.90c to 43.51c (+28.35%).
  • Pro-forma tangible NAV per share: declines from 27.39c to 25.48c (-6.97%), reflecting intangible mineral rights/exploration assets acquired.
  • Pan African ordinary shares in issue post-deal: 2,436,912,099 (2,130,557,041 ex-treasury), up from 2,333,671,529.
  • Pro-forma figures exclude any revenue, cost or synergy benefits from the integration and are unaudited.

body:
• Board received a requisition notice under s338 Companies Act 2006 for the 2026 AGM (scheduled 23 July 2026).
  • Requisition proposes removal of 5 current Non-Executive Directors and appointment of 4 new NEDs.
  • Requisitioner is Saba Capital Management L.P. (US hedge fund) via Vidacos Nominees, holding ~18.21% of issued share capital.
  • Saba's prior 8 January 2026 letter proposed addressing the NAV discount via a managed wind-down over 12 months.
  • Board has rejected the wind-down proposal, concluding it is "not achievable, nor will it maximise value for shareholders, and so is not in the best interests of the Company and its shareholders as a whole."
  • Board remains "open to continuing a constructive dialogue with Saba Capital."
  • Defence anchored on new CEO Charlie Green's 17 April 2026 business update, citing high-quality portfolio and long-term structural demand in London flexible workspace.
  • Further strategy detail to come at Full Year Results on 10 June 2026.
  • Shareholders advised to take no action on the Requisition at this time.
  • Advisers: J.P. Morgan Cazenove, Rothschild & Co and Stifel.

body:
• Certified loans: 21,064, down 23.8% YoY (vs 27,638 in Q1 2025); beat high end of guidance.
  • Total revenue: $20.5m, -16.0% YoY (vs $24.4m); included a $0.7m negative profit share revenue adjustment from historic vintages (vs -$0.9m in Q1 2025).
  • Program fee revenue: $11.4m vs $15.2m (-25.2% YoY).
  • Profit share revenue: $7.0m vs $6.7m (+3.3% YoY).
  • Gross profit: $15.6m vs $18.3m (-14.6% YoY).
  • Net loss of $0.5m vs net income of $0.6m in Q1 2025.
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $2.0m vs $3.2m (-37.3% YoY); Adj EBITDA margin 10% vs 13%.
  • Credit unions/banks share of certifications rose to 90.2% (19,000 loans) vs 87.6% — continued mix shift toward higher-quality lenders.
  • Average profit share revenue per certified loan jumped to $363 vs $278 (+30.6% YoY) — key unit economics improvement.
  • Average program fee per certified loan: $538 vs $550 (-2.2%).
  • Q2 2026 guidance: 22,000–25,000 certified loans.
  • FY 2026 guidance: 100,000–110,000 certified loans; Adjusted EBITDA $25–29m.
  • Share Repurchase Program upsized from $25m to $50m and extended one year to 1 May 2027; $20.1m remained available as of 31 March 2026.
  • Cash and equivalents: $173.3m (vs $176.6m at year-end 2025); long-term debt $75.4m.
  • Operating cash outflow narrowed to $0.8m (vs $3.8m outflow in Q1 2025).
  • CEO Jessica Buss: "We exceeded the high end of our certified loan guidance for the first quarter while improving the quality and mix of our certified loans."
  • CEO on strategy: "we continue to make solid progress on our key strategic initiatives, including Project Red Rocks and ApexOne Auto."
  • CEO outlook: "With healthy application volumes combined with what we believe is the highest-quality portfolio we have seen in years, we believe we are well positioned to deliver sustainable, profitable growth for our shareholders in 2026 and beyond."

body:
• Net revenue $34.4m, down 3.7% YoY.
  • Income from operations $12.4m, up 6.9% YoY.
  • Net income $10.9m, up 6.3% YoY.
  • Basic EPS $0.46 (+7.0% YoY); Diluted EPS $0.46 (+12.2% YoY) — earnings growth despite revenue decline reflects margin expansion.
  • Book value per share $22.66 at 31 March 2026, up 2.1% from 31 December 2025.
  • Received a non-refundable ~$7.1m advance deposit from a single customer for a large workforce housing order, with deliveries beginning Q2 2026.
  • Repurchased 30,740 shares for ~$573k under the $10.0m buyback authorised by the Board on 6 February 2026.
  • Liquidity: $14.1m cash and an essentially undrawn revolver at quarter-end.
  • CEO Kenneth E. Shipley: "Legacy delivered a solid first quarter, growing net income and diluted earnings per share year-over-year despite a modest decline in revenue and continued macro headwinds."
  • CEO on channel mix: "Our retail and direct sales channels showed real strength, our loan portfolios continue to perform well".
  • CEO on workforce housing: "The large workforce housing order received during the quarter underscores the opportunity we see in that market, and we look forward to delivering on that order in the coming quarters."
  • CEO outlook: "We remain confident in the long-term demand for affordable manufactured housing and in Legacy's ability to serve that need."
  • Conference call scheduled 12:00 PM CT, Friday 8 May 2026.

body:
• Group net outflows of GBP1.5 billion, improved from GBP2.3 billion outflows in Q1 2025.
  • Asset Management net inflows of GBP700 million vs flat flows a year ago, driven by strong Wholesale performance offsetting subdued Institutional activity.
  • Asset Management AUMA at GBP343.5 billion (incl. GBP183 billion external), broadly flat vs GBP345.2 billion at end-2025 but +10% year-on-year.
  • Life AUMA fell 2.3% to GBP187.9 billion (from GBP192.2 billion at end-2025), reflecting adverse market moves and expected legacy book outflows.
  • PruFund net outflows of GBP100 million, as March market volatility offset positive January/February inflows.
  • CEO Andrea Rossi: "We have made a strong start to 2026," adding "we are confident in our ability to deliver continued growth this year."

body:
• Group Insurance Contract Written Premiums (ICWP) +10% to USD1.72bn (vs USD1.56bn YoY), driven by accelerating Retail and disciplined big-ticket growth.
  • Retail ICWP +15% (+8.0% constant currency), in line with full-year guidance, with growth stepping up across all businesses.
  • Hiscox UK ICWP +8.9% cc — supported by distribution deals, brand investment, sector specialisms.
  • Hiscox Europe ICWP +6.8% cc — sustained strong growth in Germany and France.
  • Hiscox US ICWP +8.5%.
  • Hiscox London Market +4.0% (slowest segment, reflecting softening big-ticket cycle).
  • Change programme on track to deliver USD75m P&L benefit in 2026, progressing toward USD200m annual P&L benefit target in 2028+.
  • Q1 loss experience within expectations: benign nat cat offset estimated Middle East conflict impact.
  • Shares +5.6% to 1,636p, leading FTSE 100 gainers on the day.
  • CEO Aki Hussain: "Hiscox is building on strong momentum delivered in 2025, through capturing diverse, high-quality growth opportunities across each of our businesses."
  • CEO on big-ticket: "proactively" managed the softening cycle.
  • CEO outlook: "With our sharp focus on profitable growth and good progress on the change programme objectives, the outlook for 2026 is positive."

time_period: FY26 (year ended 31 January 2026), with Q1 FY27 update
body:
• Pretax profit fell 12% to £629m (FY25: £715m); basic EPS down 9.2% to 8.63p.
  • Revenue up 11% to £12.66bn (+12% constant currency).
  • Organic sales growth of 2.1% in a "muted" market.
  • Profit decline driven by higher operating costs and increased interest on lease liabilities.
  • Free cash flow up 36% to £462m (FY25: £339m).
  • Final dividend raised to 0.87p (from 0.67p); full-year dividend up 20% to 1.20p.
  • Launching rolling annual £200m share buyback; first £100m tranche to complete in H1 FY27.
  • Q1 FY27 trading: organic sales flat, like-for-like down 2.3%.
  • LFL by region: Europe -4.2%, UK -4.0%, North America -0.6% (+0.4% ex-Finish Line conversion), Asia Pacific +5.2%.
  • FY27 PBT (pre-adjusting items) guidance widened to £750m–£850m vs £852m in FY26 (FY26 itself -7.7% YoY on this measure).
  • FY27 free cash flow guided to £460m–£520m.
  • CEO Régis Schultz: "We delivered a resilient performance, achieving organic sales growth of 2.1% despite tough market conditions," citing "customer‑led focus, alongside disciplined cost and capital management".
  • Schultz on outlook: "Whilst we continue to expect muted market growth in FY27, we remain confident in JD Group's medium‑term trajectory, underpinned by our strong brand partnerships and agile, multi‑brand model."
  • Shares +5.8% to 71.94p in early trade, top FTSE 100 gainer.

body:
• Reaffirmed ambition to grow adjusted earnings by 50% by 2030, citing greater portion of portfolio subject to rent reviews in 2026/2027 and development pipeline.
  • GBP5.9m incremental annual rental income added YTD from lease events covering 12% of portfolio, with a 13% absolute uplift in rent across these events.
  • Lease events on Blackstone-acquired assets delivered stronger uplifts: UKCM logistics +15% and urban assets +24%.
  • Guides to higher like-for-like rental growth in 2026 as asset management opportunities accelerate.
  • New Newark letting secures GBP3.3m annual rent; new Cambridge pre-let secures GBP1.6m; both expected to deliver yield on cost >7%.
  • Around GBP7m of annual rental income in solicitors' hands.
  • Data centres: first site at Manor Farm, Heathrow (107MW) "primed for launch"; planning decision expected from Secretary of State on or before 9 June; would recognise strong development profits in 2026 subject to planning and securing a pre-let.
  • Planning decision expected near-term on second data centre site in Chelmsford.
  • LTV reduction underway from 33.2% (Dec) supported by capital rotation.
  • >GBP270m of disposals/exchanges completed as at 30 April, majority a logistics portfolio sold above valuation; 93% of non-strategic UKCM assets sold, exchanged or under offer.
  • CEO Colin Godfrey: entered 2026 with "strong operational momentum" and continues "to make excellent progress across each of our three growth drivers."
  • CEO on reversion capture: "We are capturing rental reversion and driving income growth across our high-quality portfolio through our active and hands-on approach, particularly within the assets acquired from Blackstone."
  • CEO on macro/geopolitics: "Occupier demand for UK logistics remains healthy, supporting positive engagement and leasing activity across both our investment and development portfolios. To date, we have not seen any impact from the conflict in the Middle East but continue to monitor the situation closely."
  • CEO on outlook: "Looking ahead, with a greater proportion of the portfolio subject to rent reviews in 2026 and 2027 and substantial development opportunities within logistics and data centres, we are well placed to accelerate income growth. Consequently, we remain confident in our ambition to grow adjusted earnings by 50% by 2030."
  • Shares +0.7% at 154.18p on the day.
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with financial performance figures

body:
• GMV $389.9m, +6% YoY (vs $367.4m).
  • Revenue $120.7m, +4% YoY (vs $116.4m); split: Purchase $77.9m (flat), Consignment & fees $42.9m (+11%).
  • GAAP Net Income $7.5m, +7%; GAAP Diluted EPS $0.23, +5%.
  • Non-GAAP Adjusted EBITDA $16.7m, +37% YoY (vs $12.2m); Adjusted Diluted EPS $0.35, +13%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA growth outpaced net income due to higher tax expense (provision $3.15m vs $0.66m) from reduced stock-based comp tax benefits.
  • Cash & investments $204.0m, zero financial debt (cash $195.3m + ST investments $8.7m); up from $185.8m at FY25 year-end.
  • Operating cash flow H1 FY26 $29.0m vs $9.5m prior year (boosted by $17.8m payables-to-sellers swing).
  • Consignment = 81% of consolidated GMV.
  • Segment GMV: GovDeals +5% (held back by winter weather disruption and lower real estate activity); RSCG +10% (consignment-led, D2C channel growth, purchase GMV ~flat); CAG +3% (heavy equipment strength, industrial lumpy).
  • Segment revenue: GovDeals +11% (higher take rates from service expansion + lower real estate mix), RSCG +1% (but direct profit +29%, margin 26% vs 20%), CAG +12%, Machinio & Software +12%.
  • Registered buyers 6.3m, +8% YoY; completed transactions ~280k, +9%; auction participants ~985k (flat).
  • Q3 FY26 Guidance: GMV $425–465m; GAAP Net Income $7.0–10.0m; Adjusted EBITDA $17.0–20.0m; GAAP Diluted EPS $0.21–0.30; Adjusted Diluted EPS $0.30–0.39.
  • Q3 expected to be seasonally strongest quarter; consignment GMV expected at low-to-mid 80% of total; revenue/GMV mid-to-high 20s; segment direct profit/revenue mid-to-high 40s.
  • Q3 RSCG purchase GMV expected sequentially flat with less favourable product mix; CAG: continued heavy equipment growth and strong energy project pipeline.
  • FY26 effective tax rate guided 30–34%, Q3 in mid-30s; cash taxes rising in FY26 as US federal NOL carryforward fully utilised in FY25.
  • Buyback: $15.0m remaining authorisation; $1.5m repurchased H1 FY26.
  • Diluted share count expected ~33.0m.
  • CEO Bill Angrick: "Our broad industry coverage, robust buyer liquidity and operating leverage drove a strong second quarter with expanded profitability."
  • Angrick on strategy: "we are building a more scalable and attractive marketplace business which will create long-term value for both customers and shareholders."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q2 FY2026'

body:
• Announced GBP55 million share buyback, to conclude no later than 1 March 2027.
  • Company cites "significant headroom" under its recently increased GBP175.0 million committed revolving credit facility and views the buyback as "prudent".
  • Revenue +1.4% to GBP123.0 million (vs GBP121.4 million YoY).
  • Organic revenue growth contribution: 0.7% (implying ~0.7% from M&A).
  • Reiterated guidance: on track for adjusted operating margin of at least 14.0% in 2026.
  • Majority of 2026 energy costs fixed; for 2027, ~60% of electricity and ~70% of gas usage already hedged, with further lock-ins as "opportunities allow".
  • On Middle East geopolitical risk: "We have yet to see any material impact of the ongoing geopolitical uncertainty in the Middle East, the intensity or duration of which we are unable to predict. Given what we know currently, we expect any cost consequences in 2026 to be manageable."
  • Shares +4.7% to 137.40p, market cap ~GBP521.9 million.

body:
• FY26 free cash flow guidance more than doubled to USD1.4 billion (from USD600 million), assuming Brent at USD80/bbl.
  • FY26 production guidance narrowed upward to 480-500 kboepd (from 475-500 kboepd), reflecting strong YTD performance including 520 kboepd in April, ahead of planned UK and Norway maintenance.
  • FY26 capex reiterated at USD2.2-2.4 billion.
  • Q1 revenue USD3.0 billion, up from USD2.8 billion YoY (+~7%), driven by higher oil and gas prices.
  • Q1 production 506 kboepd vs 500 kboepd YoY.
  • Q1 unit opex USD12.8/boe, down from USD13.0/boe.
  • Q1 capex flat YoY at USD0.5 billion.
  • CEO Linda Cook: "Our strong first quarter has allowed us to narrow upwards our production guidance for the full year and, supported by the current commodity price environment, increase our free cash flow outlook for 2026. As a result, we see the potential for accelerated debt reduction while maintaining competitive shareholder returns and disciplined investment in our portfolio, in line with our capital allocation framework."
  • Shares +1.1% to 282.60p on the day.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Excellent start to the year, with Board confidence in full-year expectations enhanced by strong momentum.
  • Broking: spot business, revenue and profits materially ahead YoY, supported by geo-political complexity, robust trading flows, extended voyage distances, elevated chartering rates and asset prices.
  • Financial division: investment banking executed multiple mandates with a strong pipeline; benefitted from positive capital-market sentiment early in the year.
  • Research: revenue and profitability both up; clients value data/insights amid shipping disruption.
  • Support division weaker — pressured by UK government policy on new oil & gas development and offshore energy project delays; Egyptian Agency still impacted by lower Suez Canal transits.
  • Middle East conflict cited as driver of greater shipping complexity, lifting rates in certain markets; Board notes the situation "continues to evolve and may impact trading dynamics."
  • Recent bolt-on M&A: acquisitions of Link Group, Zuma Labs and Serpac International — all trading in line with management expectations at acquisition.
  • Management framing: "The trend of increased global trade dislocation and complexity which we have seen over the last decade has continued."
  • Reiterates positioning for further M&A opportunities; trading environment "continues to support growth."
  • Context: Clarksons has delivered 23 consecutive years of dividend growth; FTSE 250, ~2,250 staff across 70+ offices.
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with financial performance figures

body:
• Grand opening of Willow Estates, a new single-family community in Shelby, NC (west of Charlotte).
  • 246 lots, move-in-ready homes targeting first-time buyers and families.
  • Six floor plans: 3-4 bed, 2-car garage, ranging 1,172–2,002 sq ft.
  • Pricing: homes starting in the upper-$270s, positioned at affordable end of the market.
  • All homes include LGI's CompleteHome™ package (Whirlpool appliances, granite counters, LVP flooring, designer finishes) at no extra cost.
  • Townhomes "coming soon" to the community, adding product mix flexibility.
  • Onsite amenities (playground, gazebo, picnic areas) targeted for completion late 2026.
  • VP of Sales Tyler Zulli: "Willow Estates brings some of our most popular floor plans to Shelby at an exceptional value, with homes starting in the upper-$200s."
  • Zulli added: "We're excited to introduce a community that truly combines affordability, location, and quality."
  • Context: LGI now operates across 36 markets in 21 states, with 80,000+ lifetime closings since 2003.

body:
• Chair Jean-Paul Luksic, at the AGM, said the copper outlook "remains compelling" despite a "heightened level of uncertainty" in the macro environment.
  • Shares up 3.5% to 3,952.00p intraday, vs FTSE 100 -0.6%.
  • Hailed 2025 as a "record" financial year and one of "strong delivery".
  • Los Pelambres and Centinela operations continue to demonstrate quality, scale and long-term potential.
  • Centinela second concentrator construction "advancing well", now moving into installation of key equipment and pre-commissioning.
  • Los Pelambres: work continuing on desalination plant expansion and new concentrate pipeline.
  • Combined growth projects expected to increase copper production by ~30% before 2030, while improving margins, extending mine lives and enhancing operational resilience.
  • Progress on longer-term options via exploration projects in Chile, Peru and the US.
  • On Chile policy backdrop: "we have seen positive progress in the policy environment in Chile, with greater clarity and continued support for investment following the recent presidential election."
  • Noted consensus in Chile on supporting growth, encouraging investment and improving permitting efficiency — key for mining sector competitiveness.
  • On macro: "we are seeing a heightened level of uncertainty, with resilience an important theme for companies in today's environment."
type_workings: Rule 'routine': opening bullets reference the AGM

body:
• Shares down 1.7% to 3,156p as rejigged distribution policy and mixed Q2 outlook offset earnings beat.
  • Adjusted earnings rose 24% to USD6.92bn (Q1 2025: USD5.58bn), beating consensus of USD6.36bn.
  • Adjusted EBITDA up 16% to USD17.74bn (vs USD15.25bn), beating USD16.73bn consensus.
  • Drivers: higher oil prices, higher refining margins, lower opex, higher Lubricants margins; partly offset by lower volumes.
  • Citi noted adjusted net income was 10% above consensus, but consensus had already moved 15% higher post-April trading statement.
  • Downstream led the beat (refining margin capture and oil trading); Upstream held back by lag effects in pricing formulas missing March price rally.
  • CEO Wael Sawan: results were "strong" in a quarter marked by "unprecedented" disruption in global energy markets.
  • Cash flow from operations fell to USD6.06bn (from USD9.28bn); free cash flow down to USD2.93bn (from USD5.32bn).
  • Working capital outflows of USD11.2bn mainly from commodity price impact on inventory and receivables.
  • Basic EPS USD1.01 (vs USD0.79); adjusted EPS USD1.22 (vs USD0.92).
  • Net debt up 27% to USD52.61bn (from USD41.52bn YoY).
  • Distribution policy rebalanced: dividend hiked 9.1% YoY to USD0.3906/share (+5.0% QoQ), vs prior ~4% annual growth target.
  • Quarterly buyback cut to USD3bn from USD3.5bn; first reallocation since 2023; to be completed by Q2 results.
  • 40%–50% CFFO payout policy firmly reiterated; total cash yield ~9% if USD3bn/qtr buyback sustained.
  • 2026 capex guided to USD24–26bn (including ARC Resources), raised from prior USD20bn guidance at FY25 results.
  • 2027–2028 capex outlook unchanged at USD20–22bn.
  • Q2 integrated gas production guided 580–640 kboe/d, well below Visible Alpha consensus of 874 kboe/d.
  • Q2 LNG liquefaction volumes guided 6.8–7.4mt vs 7.9mt consensus.
  • Shortfall reflects Middle East conflict (Qatar) and higher planned maintenance.
  • Pearl facility (Qatar) damage repair expected to take around one year.
  • Australian LNG site also hit by temporary cyclone-related stoppages.
  • AJ Bell's Dan Coatsworth: "it wasn't all plain sailing" given Qatar facility damage and Australia LNG disruption.
  • Jefferies' Wilson saw "logic" to the rebalancing, noting shares up >30% YoY but ~0.5% lower 2026 distribution yield.
  • Citi's Syme said the buyback/dividend rebalance "should have been made several quarters ago".
  • Hargreaves Lansdown's Nathan: ARC acquisition helps diversify supply post-Qatar, but markets cautious as buyback pace slowed with net debt up 27%.
  • Brent traded at USD98.59/bbl on the day vs ~USD102 prior close.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• "Excellent start" to 2026, with current momentum enhancing board confidence in full-year expectations.
  • Performance driven by "good market fundamentals" and increasing demand for industry expertise.
  • Broking division materially ahead of prior year on spot business, profits and revenue.
  • Financials division benefiting from positive capital-market sentiment early in the year.
  • Research division saw an increase in both revenue and profitability YTD.
  • Middle East conflict has led to "greater complexity" across global shipping, with mixed effects across business lines.
  • Link Group (USD80m all-cash North American physical crude and derivatives oil brokerage/data acquisition completed March) trading in line with management expectations at acquisition.
  • Management quote: "The trend of increased global trade dislocation and complexity which we have seen over the last decade has continued. The board recognises that the situation in the Middle East continues to evolve and may impact trading dynamics."
  • Management quote: "While it is still early in the year, the group's strong start to date and current momentum has enhanced the board's confidence in its expectations for the year."

body:
• Priced public offering of $500 million aggregate principal of 6.495% Fixed-to-Floating Rate Senior Notes due 2032, issued at par.
  • Notes expected to settle on 15 May 2026, subject to customary closing conditions.
  • J.P. Morgan and Barclays acting as joint book-running managers; BofA, Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley and RBC as co-managers.
  • Net proceeds to fund the purchase of up to $500m of its 3.125% notes due 2026 via a concurrently announced Tender Offer, plus accrued interest and fees.
  • Any residual proceeds will be used to repay remaining 2026 Notes at maturity — effectively a refinancing of the 2026 maturity wall at a materially higher coupon (~340bps step-up from 3.125% to 6.495%), reflecting the higher rate environment.

body:
• Full-year guidance retained: continues to expect high single digit % growth in profit from operations from earnings-based businesses in 2026, unchanged from March FY guidance.
  • Performed "well" year to date with positive momentum in key markets continuing into 2026 and demand remaining strong.
  • Contract mix said to provide strong protection from the "volatile macroeconomic backdrop and related inflationary pressures."
  • Order book at end of Q1 in line with year-end position, supported by a "disciplined and selective bidding approach in pursuit of a high‑quality, low‑risk forward order book."

body:
• Aberdeen Investments to become alternative investment manager of Herald Investment Trust, bringing ~GBP1.6 billion AUM (incl. Herald Worldwide Technology Fund) under its management.
  • Herald's outgoing lead manager Katie Potts plus seven HIML colleagues expected to transfer to Aberdeen.
  • Herald proposes tender offer for up to 66% of issued shares at close to NAV.
  • Saba Capital (~30% holder, Herald's largest shareholder) has given irrevocable undertaking to tender its shares.
  • Three-year standstill agreement signed with Saba: activist commits to not voting against the Herald board's recommendations at any GM.
  • Broader Aberdeen-Saba deal: up to eight further Aberdeen investment trusts with ~GBP12.5 billion combined AUM can opt into similar standstill arrangements at the discretion of their independent boards — a notable defensive perimeter against further activism.
  • CEO Jason Windsor: "Completion of this transaction will further grow our franchise and demonstrates our innovation and commitment to the sector."
  • AIC CEO Richard Stone called it a "creative solution" and "successful outcome for shareholders."
  • Saba framed it as a "major liquidity win" and "landmark outcome," noting Herald is the sixth of seven trusts from its December 2024 campaign to yield a material liquidity/governance result.
  • Boaz Weinstein (Saba): "This is what shareholder engagement looks like when boards act in the interests of the people they serve, and it is what Saba will keep demanding across the UK investment trust sector."
  • Market reaction: Aberdeen shares +1.8% to 218.20p; Herald +2.4% to 3,030.33p.
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Appoints Michael Queen as chair designate and non-executive director, succeeding Douglas Flint at the AGM on 18 June.
  • Flint retires after almost eight years on the board.
  • Queen was CEO of 3i Group until 2012 (25 years at the firm); currently chair of Coller Capital.
  • CEO Greg Smith: "I am delighted to welcome Michael to the board at a time of growing opportunity for IP Group...He brings deep investment and leadership experience, with a strong track record in scaling institutional capital platforms, which aligns closely with our strategy as we continue to grow funds under management and support our portfolio through their next phase of development."
  • Smith on Flint: "I would also like to thank Sir Douglas for the enormous contribution he has made to IP Group over many years and to wish him every success in the future."
  • Shares +2.8% to 65.80p on the day; +54% over 12 months.
  • Signal: appointment reinforces strategic tilt toward growing third-party FUM alongside balance-sheet investing.

body:
• DLC unification completed: Carnival Corporation and Carnival plc now operate under a single entity, Carnival Corporation Ltd., with Carnival plc becoming a UK subsidiary.
  • Redomiciliation completed: jurisdiction of incorporation migrated from Panama to Bermuda; parent renamed Carnival Corporation Ltd.
  • Common Shares continue to trade on NYSE under ticker "CCL"; Carnival Corporation shareholders received the same number of new Common Shares.
  • Carnival plc shareholders entitled to one Common Share per Carnival plc ordinary share held as of 6:00 p.m. BST on 5 May 2026.
  • Carnival plc securities cancelled from the FCA Official List, LSE and NYSE today; intention to deregister with the SEC and re-register Carnival plc as a private limited company.
  • Stated benefits: single global share price, streamlined governance/reporting, reduced admin costs, and expected increased liquidity and weighting in major U.S. stock indexes.
  • Carnival Corporation Ltd. remains in the S&P 500.

body:
• Revenue: $629.5m, +0.8% YoY (vs $624.7m); beat high end of guidance.
  • Revenue ex-FormSwift: +2.0% YoY; +0.4% on constant currency basis.
  • Total ARR: $2.560bn, +0.3%; ex-FormSwift $2.540bn, +1.3%; constant-currency ex-FormSwift ARR down 0.1% and down $0.8m QoQ.
  • Paying users: 18.09m vs 18.16m (slight decline YoY).
  • ARPU: $141.18 vs $139.26 (+1.4% YoY).
  • GAAP gross margin 79.7% (vs 81.3%); Non-GAAP gross margin 81.1% (vs 82.9%) — ~180bps compression.
  • GAAP operating margin 27.5% (vs 29.4%); Non-GAAP operating margin 40.1% (vs 41.7%) — beat high end of guidance.
  • GAAP net income $114.5m vs $150.3m; Non-GAAP net income $180.4m vs $207.1m — decline driven by higher interest expense from additional term loan draws (interest expense $36.7m vs $14.6m).
  • GAAP diluted EPS $0.48 (vs $0.51); Non-GAAP diluted EPS $0.76 vs $0.70 (+8.6%) — boosted by lower share count (236.7m vs 295.7m diluted shares, ~20% reduction from buybacks).
  • Operating cash flow $204.5m (vs $153.8m); Unlevered FCF $236.4m vs $174.4m (+35.6%) — prior-year period included $36.0m SF HQ lease termination outflow and $10.2m workforce-reduction outflow.
  • Free cash flow margin 32.3% vs 24.6%.
  • Cash, equivalents and short-term investments: $1.289bn.
  • Capital structure activity: drew $1,200m on term loan, repaid $695.8m convertible senior notes; non-current term loan rose to $2,584.6m (from $1,433.7m); total liabilities $5.04bn vs $4.64bn; stockholders' deficit widened to $(2,011.7)m.
  • Buybacks: $366.8m of common stock repurchased in Q1 (vs $499.1m in Q1'25).
  • FormSwift: investment significantly reduced from start of 2025; plan to wind down operations by end of 2026.
  • CEO Drew Houston: "We delivered a strong quarter, exceeding the high end of our guidance for revenue and operating margin... This represents another quarter of positive revenue growth excluding FormSwift and reflects progress in strengthening our core business."
  • Houston flagged "encouraging signs" from retention work in Individuals, funnel/product improvements in Teams, and continued thoughtful expansion of Dash in Dropbox across the existing user base.
  • Forward guidance not disclosed in release — to be provided on the earnings call.

body:
• Total revenue +17% to $1,732m (+12% constant currency); ex-DSP True-Up ($11m) and BMG Termination ($6m) headwinds, revenue +18.1% (+13.4% cc).
  • Recorded Music revenue +17% to $1,380m; Music Publishing revenue +14% to $353m.

  • Recorded Music Adjusted OIBDA margin +210bps to 25.1%; Artist services & expanded-rights revenue +40.2% on French concert promotion and merchandise; Physical +22.3% on strong releases.

  • Quarterly dividend maintained at $0.19/share (payable 2 June 2026).
  • FX a c.$13m headwind to Adjusted OIBDA in the quarter.
  • CEO Robert Kyncl: "Our Q2 results demonstrate the powerful combination of creative and operational success, as well as financial discipline, providing clear evidence that our strategic transformation is working."

body:
• Aberdeen Investments proposed to be appointed investment manager of Herald Investment Trust (HRI) in Q3 2026, bringing all HIML funds (incl. Herald Worldwide Technology Fund) under Aberdeen with combined AUM of c.£1.6bn (HRI c.£1.5bn; Herald Worldwide Technology Fund c.£130m as of 30 April 2026).
  • Eight staff including lead manager Katie Potts to join Aberdeen's London office, retaining tech/small-cap/media investment focus.
  • HRI to launch a tender offer for up to 66% of issued share capital at close to NAV; Saba Capital (largest shareholder) has given irrevocable undertaking to tender its full holding, providing exit route.
  • Three-year standstill agreement signed with Saba covering HRI: Saba commits not to vote against HRI board recommendations at general meetings.
  • Wider standstill option extended to up to eight further Aberdeen investment trusts with combined AUM of c.£12.5bn — materially de-risks Saba activism across Aberdeen's UK trust range, subject to individual board decisions.
  • Standstill terminates early if tender fails or Aberdeen is not appointed manager.
  • Aberdeen currently manages c.£19.8bn in closed-end funds, ranking it fifth largest closed-end fund manager globally.
  • CEO Jason Windsor: "The Herald team has a long track record of backing early-stage technology companies, and driving material long term growth from those investments... Completion of this transaction will further grow our franchise and demonstrates our innovation and commitment to the sector."
  • Christian Pittard (Head of Closed End Funds) on Saba: "Saba's collaboration and determination to find a path forward that benefited all shareholders ultimately made it possible to preserve this trust and, together, deliver an outcome that is positive for everyone."
  • Katie Potts: HIML's "share price and NAV per share of HIML's investment trust and fund are at record levels"; disciplined strategy since 1994 to remain unchanged under Aberdeen.
  • Contracting entity: abrdn Fund Managers Limited (trading as Aberdeen Investments).

  • Dividend hiked 9.1% YoY to USD0.3906/share (+5.0% QoQ from USD0.3720), well above prior ~4% annual growth guidance.
  • Buyback trimmed to USD3bn from USD3.5bn quarterly run-rate; to be completed by Q2 results.
  • CEO Wael Sawan described results as "strong" in a quarter marked by "unprecedented" disruption in global energy markets, and said Shell is "rebalancing" shareholder distributions.
  • 2026 capex guided to USD24–26bn (including ARC Resources acquisition), raised from prior USD20bn guidance given at FY2025 results.
  • 2027–2028 capex outlook unchanged at USD20–22bn.

body:
• AGM held today; no new trading information provided beyond the 28 April 2026 Trading Update.
  • Final 2025 dividend of 16.9p per share proposed, payable 22 May 2026 (record date 10 April 2026), subject to shareholder approval.
  • Interim dividend of 5.0p was paid on 21 November 2025, taking total FY2025 dividend to 21.9p per share.
  • 2026 Half Year results scheduled for 23 July 2026.

body:
• Global RevPAR +4.4%, beating consensus (BofA/Jefferies 3.9%, JPMorgan 3.1%).
  • Regional RevPAR: Greater China +5.7%, EMEAA +5.6%, Americas +3.6%.
  • CEO Maalouf flagged "further acceleration" of growth in Greater China after Q4 2025 return to growth.
  • By segment: group travel revenue +7%, business +6%, leisure only +1%.
  • ADR +2.0%; occupancy +1.5ppt.
  • Net system size +5.0% YoY, +0.9% YTD; now 1.0 million rooms across 7,014 hotels.
  • Full-year consensus growth and profit expectations confirmed: "our confidence of achieving full year consensus growth forecasts and profit expectations is underpinned by the strength of our performance year-to-date."
  • Q2 outlook: "comparable on-the-books global revenue for Q2 indicates continued growth, with the impact of the Middle East conflict and some wider disruption to international travel flows expected to be more than offset by increases in demand elsewhere."
  • >USD1.2 billion to be returned to shareholders in 2026 via dividends plus the USD950 million buyback announced with February's annual results.
  • Shares +3.1% to USD150.40 on the day; +30% over 12 months; market cap USD21.86 billion.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Shares closed up 9.3% in Toronto following the results.
  • Profit swung to CAD16.8 million, from a CAD28.6 million loss YoY.
  • Revenue grew 70% to CAD237.1 million (vs CAD139.1 million YoY).
  • Basic EPS swung to CAD0.05, from a loss of CAD0.09 YoY.
  • Adjusted EBITDA grew 58% to USD93.4 million (vs USD59.1 million YoY).
  • Copper production rose 50% to 30.0 million pounds (vs 20.0 million pounds YoY).
  • Strong production levels from H2 2025 carried into Q1, supporting earnings.
  • February commissioning of SX/EW plant in Arizona; produced 1.5 million pounds of copper cathode by quarter-end.
  • Maintained 2026 guidance of 30–35 million pounds of copper cathode production.
  • CEO Stuart McDonald: "both of Taseko's producing assets performed well in the first quarter. Gibraltar operations have achieved a consistent production level in recent quarters as mining activities have been advancing on plan in the Connector pit."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Revenue flat at €66.3m (vs €66.4m), with CEO citing geopolitical uncertainty and FX headwinds.
  • Gross margin expanded to 57.2% (+190bps) from 55.3%.
  • Adjusted EBIT nearly quadrupled to €2.47m (vs €0.65m); Adj EBIT margin 3.7% vs 1.0%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA €8.05m vs €6.15m (+30.9%).
  • Net profit €1.82m / €0.03 EPS, vs net loss of €0.54m / €(0.01) prior year.
  • Segment performance:
  • Medical: revenue +6.7% to €33.2m, but EBITDA margin compressed to 27.8% (vs 29.1%).
  • Software: revenue -1.4% to €9.6m, EBITDA margin jumped to 11.6% from 6.1%.
  • Manufacturing: revenue -8.1% to €23.5m, but EBITDA turned positive at €0.28m (margin 1.2% vs -1.5%).
  • Net cash position rose €2.0m to €72.8m; cash €133.0m; gross debt down to €60.1m.
  • Operating cash flow €6.9m; capex €1.5m; FCF positive.
  • Share buyback: 511,513 shares repurchased for €2.3m in Q1 under existing programme.
  • Portfolio action: agreed to transfer eyewear business to its management team, following the Rapidfit divestment at end of March; Materialise retains a minority stake; all eyewear employees transfer; impairment charges expected in Q2 2026.
  • FY2026 guidance reaffirmed: revenue €273–283m and Adjusted EBIT €10–12m, despite Rapidfit and Eyewear divestments.
  • CEO de Vet-Veithen: "we have now also reached an agreement to transfer our eyewear activities to the business's management team" — framed as concentrating "capital and resources on its core focus areas."
  • CEO on macro: "we expect macro‑economic and geopolitical uncertainty to persist throughout fiscal year 2026."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Board approved a GEL 55.0 million extension to the existing buyback and cancellation programme.
  • Consistent with capital return policy targeting a dividend/buyback payout ratio of 30-50% of annual profits.
  • Programme commences shortly and ends no later than the 2026 AGM on 22 May 2026; renewed shareholder authority will be sought at that AGM.
  • Maximum shares repurchasable under the programme: 3,698,973.
  • Shares to be purchased in open market; treasury shares cancelled on a monthly basis to reduce share capital.
  • Cavendish Capital Markets appointed to manage the programme, with sole discretion during closed periods.
type_workings: Rule 'capital returns': opening bullets announce a share buyback

body:
• Michael Queen appointed Non-executive Director and Chair Designate, concluding the succession process announced last year.
  • Will succeed Sir Douglas Flint as Chair following the AGM on 18 June 2026; Flint retires after almost eight years on the Board.
  • Queen's background: former CEO of 3i Group plc (retired 2012 after 25 years, founded 3i Infrastructure plc); currently Chair of Coller Capital, Chair of Thames Tideway Tunnel, NED of TAQA, and Pro-Chancellor of the University of Surrey.
  • Prior roles include PM's Business Advisory Group (2010-2012), Head of NHS Private Finance Unit at HM Treasury (1994), and Chairman of the BVCA (2002).
  • CEO Greg Smith on the rationale: "He brings deep investment and leadership experience, with a strong track record in scaling institutional capital platforms, which aligns closely with our strategy as we continue to grow funds under management and support our portfolio through their next phase of development." — signalling continued strategic emphasis on scaling third-party/institutional FUM (notably via Parkwalk).
  • Queen: "I'm excited to be given the opportunity to chair the Group as it scales its model and I look forward to working with the team to help deliver value for all stakeholders."
  • Flint flagged Queen's value-add around "the Group's realisation of value from the portfolio and its refresh", and his understanding of the UK university sector — consistent with IP Group's ongoing portfolio realisations and refresh agenda.
  • Announcement made pursuant to Listing Rule 6.4.6R; no further disclosures required under LR 6.4.8R.
type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'non-executive' with change verb 'appointed' in opening bullets

body:
• Let over 6,200 sq ft at 170 Piccadilly, W1, taking the Fully Managed development to over 73% let or under offer.
  • Facilities management company taking 1,644 sq ft (6th Floor East); global automotive technology company taking 4,585 sq ft (4th floor).
  • Space let 5.9% ahead of ERV, at an average rent of £294 per sq ft.
  • Anna Tweed, Flex Leasing Manager: "Reaching over 73% occupancy at 170 Piccadilly is a clear sign of the momentum behind our Fully Managed offer. Demand continues to be driven by the building's exceptional quality, central location and the flexibility we provide our customers. With strong interest ongoing, we're confident in maintaining this positive leasing momentum across the wider Piccadilly Estate."

body:
• AUMA of £371bn at 31 March 2026, down from £376bn at FY 2025 (-1.3%), described as resilient despite market volatility.
  • Net inflows from open business of £0.6bn, a clear swing vs Q1 2025 net outflows of £0.1bn.
  • Total net flows including legacy were £(1.5)bn, vs £(2.3)bn in Q1 2025.
  • Outlook: CEO "confident in our ability to deliver continued growth this year" across Asset Management and Life.
  • Asset Management AUMA £344bn (incl. £183bn external), broadly flat vs start of year but +10% YoY.
  • Asset Management net inflows of £0.7bn (vs flat in Q1 2025), driven by Wholesale +£0.8bn offsetting Institutional £(0.1)bn.
  • Public markets net inflows £0.4bn; Private markets net inflows £0.3bn — strength in European equities, structured credit, impact funds.
  • Private AUMA edged down to £80.2bn from £80.8bn at FY25; Public AUMA £262.6bn vs £263.7bn.
  • Life AUMA £188bn, down 2% on adverse markets and expected legacy (Traditional With-Profits) outflows of £(1.2)bn.
  • PruFund net outflows of £0.1bn as March volatility offset positive Jan/Feb; management says flows already stabilised in April with return to net inflows expected, aided by upcoming launch on third-party adviser platforms.
  • Completed first With-Profits Bulk Purchase Annuity (BPA) transaction of £0.3bn; BPA volumes expected to grow YoY, weighted to H2 2026.
  • Asset Management inflows boosted by demand from Daiichi Life Group and other external clients.
  • CEO Andrea Rossi: "We have made a strong start to 2026… Thanks to a strong new business pipeline, the introduction of our innovative With-Profits BPA, and the upcoming launch of PruFund on third-party adviser platforms, we are confident in our ability to deliver continued growth this year."
