body:
• Profit GEL 585.0m, +14.0% y-o-y; ROAE 27.4% (vs 28.7% in 1Q25, 30.1% in 4Q25 adjusted).
  • Operating income GEL 1,125.8m, +15.0% y-o-y; operating income before cost of risk +15.7% to GEL 735.9m.
  • Net interest income GEL 809.6m, +18.4% y-o-y; NIM 6.1% (vs 5.9%).
  • Net fee & commission income +27.5% y-o-y to GEL 176.0m; net FX gains -10.6% on lower volatility.
  • Operating expenses +13.8% y-o-y to GEL 390.3m; cost:income 34.7%.
  • Cost of risk ratio 0.3% (vs 0.2% 1Q25); NPL ratio stable at 2.1%.
  • Loan book GEL 41.9bn, +23.1% y-o-y in cc (GFS +17.8%, AFS +34.6%); +4.4% q-o-q in cc.
  • Client deposits GEL 39.7bn, +17.5% y-o-y in cc (GFS +13.0%, AFS +29.7%).
  • Book value per share GEL 207.82, +21.5% y-o-y.
  • Basic EPS GEL 13.72, +16.2% y-o-y.
  • Quarterly dividend declared at GEL 2.85/share (1Q26); ex-div 25 June, payment 10 July 2026.
  • New GEL 55.0m share buyback and cancellation programme approved; previous GEL 53.5m programme completed; shares in issue 43,223,929 at 31 March.
  • Medium-term targets reiterated: c.15% annual loan book growth, 20%+ ROAE, 30-50% payout ratio.
  • GDP forecasts revised upward: Georgia 2026 to 7.0% (from 6.0%); Armenia to 6.0% (upper end of prior range). Georgia 1Q26 real GDP +9.1% y-o-y; Armenia activity indicator +7.1%.
  • NBG raised refinancing rate by 25bps to 8.25% in May 2026; Georgia inflation 5.9% in April (vs 4.0% Dec-25); CBA held rate at 6.5%.
  • Group included in FTSE 100 during the quarter — described by CEO as "a historic event for the Group… validating two decades of consistent execution."
  • GFS (Bank of Georgia): profit GEL 452.1m, +11.6% y-o-y; ROAE 31.5%; NIM expanded 30bps q-o-q to 6.2%; opex +16.6% on staff costs (incl. GEL 3.5m accelerated share-based award on executive departure); NPL improved to 2.0%.
  • AFS (Ameriabank): profit GEL 129.4m, +35.5% y-o-y; ROAE 21.8%; NIM declined 40bps q-o-q to 5.9% on lower loan yield and higher funding costs (AT1 issuance); lending market share #1 at 22.0% (+1.7pp y-o-y).
  • Ameriabank placed inaugural USD 50m AT1 notes at 8.5% in February; launched second USD 50m AT1 tranche at 8% coupon (offering 7 April-31 July 2026).
  • Other Businesses profit GEL 3.5m, -71.9% y-o-y, on lower BNB dealing income from FX competition/volatility.
  • Capital: BoG CET1 17.9%, Tier 1 20.8%, Total 22.2% (all above min); Ameriabank CET1 14.1%, Tier 1 14.9%, Total 17.9%.
  • Digital MAU: Bank of Georgia retail +13.6% y-o-y to 1.9m; Ameriabank retail +47.8% y-o-y to 362k.
  • CEO on outlook: expects NBG to "maintain a moderately tight monetary policy stance throughout the year"; sees rate environment supportive of NII but "lower currency volatility translating into reduced foreign exchange gains."
  • CEO flag on Middle East: "conflict escalation in the Middle East may weigh on the near-term outlook primarily through energy market volatility and higher inflation," but possible upside via Middle Corridor and diverted capital/tourism flows.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Global RevPAR +4.4% (better than expected), with ADR +2.0% and occupancy +1.5%pts.
  • Regional RevPAR: Americas +3.6% (US +3.4%), EMEAA +5.6%, Greater China +5.7%.
  • Q1 rooms revenue by segment: Groups +7%, Business +6%, Leisure +1%.
  • "Confident of achieving consensus growth forecasts and profit expectations" for FY26.
  • Q2-to-date Americas RevPAR has accelerated vs Q1 on an 8-week rolling basis to 2 May.
  • EMEAA April RevPAR ~-7% due to Middle East deterioration; Middle East RevPAR estimated to have declined ~50% in Q2 to date (vs -26% in March, -2% in Q1); Middle East = 5% of global system, 19% of EMEAA.
  • EMEAA other markets Q1 RevPAR: UK +3.0%, Continental Europe +5.4%, East Asia & Pacific +11.0%; May/June on-the-books point to improvement.
  • Greater China: Tier 1 RevPAR +6.4%, Tier 2-4 +2.9%; supported by Chinese New Year leisure and improving business travel.
  • Gross system growth +6.6% YoY; net system growth +5.0% YoY; 14.9k rooms (82 hotels) opened in Q1, +2% YoY; global system now 1,036k rooms / 7,014 hotels (passed 7,000 milestone).
  • Greater China openings +73% YoY at 7.5k rooms (37 hotels); region surpassed 900 open hotels.
  • Signings: 21.4k rooms (163 hotels), +6% YoY ex-Ruby acquisition comparable; pipeline 343k rooms / 2,347 hotels (+3% YoY).
  • Conversions = 35% of rooms opened and 53% of signings in Q1.
  • Brand milestones: first Noted Collection signing in EMEAA; Garner launched in Greater China (4 signings, 1 opening); Garner now >100 hotels open/pipeline in Americas, ~200 globally; debut Ruby signing in US; Ruby global estate now 40 hotels.
  • Currency tailwind: AER RevPAR +7.1% vs CER +4.4% (Global), with EMEAA AER +11.0% and Greater China AER +10.5%.
  • $240m of $950m 2026 buyback completed (25%), 1.7m shares repurchased, reducing voting rights by 1.1% YTD to 150.0m shares.
  • Combined buyback + ordinary dividend (grown 10% p.a. for last four years) to return >$1.2bn to shareholders in 2026, ~5.6% of current $21.9bn market cap.
  • CEO Elie Maalouf: "Our business model is strategically diversified and resilient in capturing demand across geographies, chain scales and the different stay occasions of business, leisure and groups travel, as well as being heavily weighted to domestic and intra-regional travel."
  • CEO on outlook: "the impact of the Middle East conflict and some wider disruption to international travel flows expected to be more than offset by increases in demand elsewhere."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Group revenue +1.4% to £123.0m (Q1 2025: £121.4m); organic growth contributed 0.7%.
  • Workwear organic revenue +3.9%, driven by new/existing customer momentum and price increases.
  • HORECA organic revenue -0.6%, with continued churn in independent hotels/restaurants and challenging price/contract renewals.
  • £55.0m share buyback programme launched, on top of £90.3m returned via buybacks since 2022; details to follow separately today.
  • Medium/long-term target leverage 1.0x–1.5x; capital allocation priorities unchanged (balance sheet, estate investment, accretive M&A, progressive dividend, surplus returns).
  • RCF refinanced and upsized from £135.0m to £175.0m plus £50.0m accordion option; matures April 2030 with one-year extension option.
  • Margin on RCF reduced to range of 1.30%–2.30% over SONIA/EURIBOR; current margin 1.30%. Covenants unchanged (leverage <3x, interest cover ≥4x).
  • Net debt (incl. IFRS 16) £161.9m at end-March 2026 (Dec 2025: £159.2m); expected to rise to ~£195.0m by June 2026 reflecting dividend timing, buyback, working capital and capex.
  • Year-end leverage expected similar to June 2026, with H2 cash generation offset by buyback impact.
  • Energy hedging: 2026 — 85% electricity, 90% gas, 70% diesel fixed; 2027 — 60% electricity, 70% gas fixed.
  • Margin guidance reiterated: on track for adjusted operating margin of at least 14.0% in 2026.
  • Outlook: "The Board continues to expect to deliver another year of progress and margin improvement."
  • No material impact yet from Middle East geopolitical uncertainty; 2026 cost consequences expected to be manageable.
  • HORECA expected to benefit from normal summer seasonality despite slow start.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

time_period: Year-to-date update (to 30 April 2026), in conjunction with AGM
body:
• £10.8 million of annual income added YTD from asset management and development activity.
  • Reiterated ambition to grow adjusted earnings by 50% by 2030 (FY24 baseline: £182.4m).
  • CEO Colin Godfrey: "We entered the year with strong operational momentum, and we continue to make excellent progress across each of our three growth drivers."
  • No impact seen to date from Middle East conflict; situation being monitored.
  • Record 28% rental reversion being captured across the portfolio (as at 31 Dec 2025).
  • Portfolio mix shifted post-Blackstone deal: big box 81% / urban 19% of rental income (vs 89%/11% in 2025).
  • Growth driver 1 – Reversion: £5.9m incremental annual rent added YTD from lease events on 12% of portfolio; 13.3% absolute rent uplift, with open market reviews delivering 39.7% average uplift.
  • UKCM logistics lease events +14.5%; Blackstone urban assets lease events +24.3%, validating reversion thesis on acquired portfolio.
  • Vacancy fell to 5.0% (31 Dec 2025: 5.6%), aided by Newark development letting.
  • Further 21% of contracted rent up for review in remainder of 2026 — flagged as accelerator for like-for-like rental growth.
  • Growth driver 2 – Development: 0.4m sq ft Newark letting securing £3.3m rent + 10-year extension on existing 0.8m sq ft; 0.1m sq ft Cambridge pre-let securing £1.6m rent; both >7% yield on cost.
  • ~£7m of annual rental income in solicitors' hands.
  • 6–8% yield on cost guidance maintained for 2026 development starts (fixed-price construction contracts).
  • Growth driver 3 – Data centres: Manor Farm Heathrow (107MW) primed for launch; powered-shell pre-let progressing; planning decision by Secretary of State expected on or before 9 June 2026 — would book strong development profits in 2026 if approved with pre-let.
  • Chelmsford second site planning decision expected near-term.
  • Total data centre pipeline of c.1GW under first right of refusal.
  • Balance sheet: LTV reduction underway from 33.2% (Dec 2025) via capital rotation.
  • Over £270m of disposals completed/exchanged as at 30 April 2026, majority a logistics portfolio sold above valuation.
  • 93% of non-strategic UKCM assets sold, exchanged or under offer, all above implied acquisition cost.
  • Market backdrop: Q1 2026 national take-up 5.3m sq ft (Q1 2025: 6.0m sq ft); national vacancy down 32bps to 6.8%; speculative space under construction down to 6.2m sq ft (Q4 2025: 6.8m); MSCI distribution warehouse rents +0.7% in Q1 (Q1 2025: +1.1%); prime yields stable at 5.25%.
  • Share issuance: 12,375,336 new ordinary shares to be admitted 8 May 2026 as final post-completion adjustment for the £1.04bn Blackstone portfolio acquisition (announced 13 Oct 2025); total voting rights post-admission: 2,714,497,501.
period_detail: Year-to-date update (to 30 April 2026), in conjunction with AGM
type_workings: Rule 'routine': matched 'total voting rights'

body:
• Sales +10.5% reported / +11.7% constant FX to £12,662m, boosted by annualisation of Hibbett and Courir acquisitions.
  • Organic sales +2.1% at constant FX; LFL -2.1% (in line with expectations).
  • Gross margin flat YoY at 47.0%; underlying -30bps from controlled price investments (especially online) offset by higher marketing contributions.
  • Operating profit before adjusting items (after lease interest) £886m, -4.0% constant FX (FY25: £937m); operating margin -120bps to 7.0%.
  • PBTAI £852m, -7.7% (FY25: £923m); statutory PBT £629m, -12.0%.
  • Adjusted basic EPS 11.71p (-5.5%); statutory basic EPS 8.63p (-9.2%).
  • Free cash flow £462m, +36.3% (FY25: £339m), driven by cost/capital discipline and lower capex.
  • Year-end net cash before lease liabilities £311m (FY25: £52m) after £253m of dividends and buybacks; net leverage 1.4x (FY25: 1.7x).
  • Total liquidity ~£1.8bn; refinanced into £1bn RCF (to Jul 2030) and new $700m Term Loan (to Jul 2028).
  • Dividend +20% to 1.20p; final 0.87p (FY25 final: 0.67p).
  • New rolling £200m annual share buyback; fresh £200m programme launched 23 February 2026 (first £100m tranche to complete H127); prior £200m programme completed Dec 2025.
  • New medium-term commitment: 3-year cumulative FCF >£1.4bn over FY26-FY28.
  • Capital allocation framework formalised: capex normalising to ~3-3.5% of sales medium term; progressive dividend toward higher yield; rolling £200m buyback; leverage headroom maintained for Genesis put/call settlement (deferred to FY30/FY31 under March 2025 amendment).

FY27 Guidance:

  • PBTAI £750m-£850m (wider-than-internal range due to uncertainty; FY26: £852m).
  • FCF £460m-£520m (FY26: £462m).
  • Gross capex ~£400m (FY26: £401m); net new space contribution +2-3% (FY26: +4.2%).
  • Continued controlled price investments weighted to H127; aim to "significantly offset" underlying LFL opex inflation via efficiency.
  • FX assumptions: GBP/USD 1.34, GBP/EUR 1.15.

Q1 FY27 trading (12 weeks to 25 April 2026):

  • Organic sales flat YoY; LFL -2.3%.
  • By region LFL: North America -0.6% (ex-Finish Line +0.4%); Europe -4.2%; UK -4.0%; Asia Pacific +5.2%.
  • Weather drag early in quarter; solid Eid/Easter; April footfall volatile in Europe/UK; apparel outperforming footwear.

Regional FY26 (Organic / LFL):

  • North America (38% of sales): +3.2% / -1.8% (ex-Finish Line LFL +1.2%); Q4 returned to LFL growth; online +12.2%; apparel +c.22%.
  • Europe (33%): +4.2% / -1.2%; online +3.8%; apparel +c.10%.
  • UK (25%): -2.5% / -3.9% (weakest region; tough consumer, Euro 2024 comps).
  • Asia Pacific (4%): +8.5% / +0.4%.

Category mix: footwear 60% (organic flat, end-of-cycle headwinds offset by running/retro basketball); apparel 30% (organic +c.5%); accessories +c.11%.

Strategic / operational:

  • Heerlen DC automation launched (JD Europe replenishment); >£20m cost benefits expected FY27-FY28 as double-running unwinds.
  • E-commerce re-platforming live in North America, SE Asia, Italy; UK and rest of Europe to follow in FY27.
  • Hibbett integration on track for >$25m annualised cost synergies across FY26-FY27; integration spend now expected to exceed $35m.
  • Five FY27 priorities: product range; store productivity (Finish Line→JD conversions, UK 'fewer, bigger, better', addressing underperformers in CEE/NA); e-commerce re-platforming; AI adoption (incl. agentic AI discoverability); data-driven personalisation.
  • Store estate: 4,811 stores (FY25: 4,850); 289 openings, 325 closures; restructuring underway in Germany; Eastern Europe (MIG) optimisation; Sizeer fascia impaired £15m.
  • £93m store impairments (£63m Europe, £28m Hibbett) + £16m Germany restructuring + £14m US historical employment practices litigation provision.
  • Disposed JD Israel JV (Feb 2026, nil consideration); sold residual 9.1% Applied Nutrition stake for £49m (April 2026); sold 40% of JD Thailand to local partner (£11m, retains control).
  • Genesis put liability £787m; net leverage including Genesis option 1.9x (within investment-grade parameters).

CEO Régis Schultz quote: "We delivered a resilient performance, achieving organic sales growth of 2.1% despite tough market conditions… This customer-led focus, alongside disciplined cost and capital management, supported a 36% increase in free cash flow."

  • On outlook: "Whilst we continue to expect muted market growth in FY27, we remain confident in JD Group's medium-term trajectory… For the year ahead we are focused on… 'controlling the controllables'."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives full-year period '52 weeks to 31 January 2026 (FY26)'

body:
• Group ICWP +10.2% to $1,717.1m (Q1 2025: $1,558.0m); +6.9% in constant currency.
  • Hiscox Retail ICWP +15.1% reported / +8.0% CCY to $847.2m, accelerating from 6.3% FY25 — growth broad-based and volume-driven with rate +2%; in line with FY guidance.
  • Hiscox London Market ICWP +4.0% to $342.8m; average rates -4% (double-digit reductions in major property, commercial property, household; +5% in general liability); 75% of book rated adequate or better at 1 Jan.
  • Hiscox Re ICWP +7.1% to $527.1m gross, but net ICWP -5.6% to $209.7m as Hiscox declines to add net nat-cat exposure; rates -13% in Q1 with further slight decline at April renewals.
  • ILS AUM jumped to $2.4bn at 1 April 2026 from $1.5bn at 1 January, with ~$1bn raised from new investors (mostly into cat bond fund — fee income only, not premiums).
  • Hiscox UK +8.9% CCY to $244.3m; Europe +6.8% CCY to $328.2m; USA +8.5% to $274.7m (DPD +9.6%, broker accelerated to +6.7% from 0.7% in 2025).
  • Investment result only $34.1m / 0.4% YTD (Q1 2025: $114.1m / 1.4%), including $69.6m unrealised FV losses on fixed income (excluded from adjusted operating profit) — driven by higher yields on Middle East-driven inflation expectations.
  • Bond portfolio reinvestment yield 4.4%, duration 2.0 years; invested assets $9.3bn; 100bps rate rise sensitivity = $164m investment loss offset by $75m IFIE.
  • Loss experience within expectations — benign nat cat offset Middle East conflict impact; small claims to date in London K&R, WTPV and marine war; new sidecar launched to manage net Middle East exposure.
  • Launched new Hiscox Portfolio Solutions (HPS) division in London Market combining alternative risk with three new lines (beta-follow, global MGAs, structured solutions).
  • Change programme on track for $75m P&L benefit in 2026, building to $200m annual benefit in 2028+; 175 initiatives in flight; first finance processes outsourced and IT consolidated to single provider.
  • $300m buyback (announced 25 Feb 2026): 2.6m shares repurchased for $54.5m as at 6 May 2026.
  • Italy branch licence received following 2025 bolt-on; new MD appointed and premium production started.
  • Anish Jadav (ex-AXA, Zurich) joined as UK Chief Underwriting Officer.
  • CEO Aki Hussain: "With our sharp focus on profitable growth and good progress on the change programme objectives, the outlook for 2026 is positive."
  • CEO on big-ticket: "we are proactively managing the softening cycle, while achieving growth through new business initiatives and in selected existing lines where conditions are more favourable."

body:
• Total operating income £240.7m, up 9.4% vs £220.1m in Q1 2025, driven by improved market levels.
  • FUMA closed at £113.6bn (vs £115.6bn at 31 Dec 2025) — Wealth Management £105.2bn, Asset Management gross £15.7bn (£8.4bn consolidated).
  • Total net outflows £0.8bn (flat vs Q1 2025), with annualised net growth of -2.9%.
  • Wealth Management net outflows £0.4bn (Q1 2025: £0.5bn), of which £0.2bn from execution-only services.
  • Asset Management net outflows £0.4bn (Q1 2025: £0.3bn); Single Strategy funds particularly weak at -17.0% annualised net growth.
  • Wealth flows ex-execution-only were broadly flat after absorbing an estimated £0.2bn of tax-driven withdrawals linked to the October 2024 Budget, concentrated in January.
  • Wealth Management income breakdown: Fees £151.9m (+10.8%), Commissions £26.9m (+10.7%), Net interest income £23.2m (+44.1%), Advisory fees £15.9m (+8.2%), Other income £1.3m (-81.2%) — note reclassification of IW&I client money deposit income from "Other income" to "Net interest income" post-migration.
  • Asset Management income £21.5m, up just 2.4%.
  • MPS & Select services a bright spot with +2.4% annualised net growth; Multi Asset funds also +2.4%.
  • CEO Jonathan Sorrell: "Underlying flows in our wealth proposition continued to stabilise during the first quarter against a volatile market backdrop."
  • CEO on strategy: "We remain focused on executing our strategy with discipline and consistency of purpose as we pursue our ambition to be the best wealth manager in the UK, by far."
  • AGM held 7 May 2026; interim results scheduled for 29 July 2026.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• CEO Linda Z Cook headline: "Our strong first quarter has allowed us to narrow upwards our production guidance for the full year and, supported by the current commodity price environment, increase our free cash flow outlook for 2026."
  • Notes Middle East conflict has caused "unprecedented disruption to energy markets, restricting oil and gas flows and driving significant price volatility."
  • 2026 production guidance narrowed upwards to 480-500 kboepd (prior 475-500 kboepd); April run-rate already at 520 kboepd.
  • 2026 FCF outlook raised to $1.4bn (from $0.6bn) — but on revised price deck of $80/bbl Brent & $13/mscf gas (vs prior $65/bbl & $11/mscf).
  • At lower end of 45-75% payout policy, this implies ~$0.6bn shareholder distributions and ~$0.8bn allocated to debt reduction for 2026.
  • FCF sensitivity: $5/bbl Brent move = ~$170m; $1/mscf EU gas move = ~$150m.
  • 2026 opex guidance unchanged at ~$14.5/boe; capex reiterated at $2.2-2.4bn.
  • Q1 production 506 kboepd (+1.2% YoY vs 500 kboepd); mix 40% liquids / 40% European gas / 20% other gas.
  • LLOG (US Gulf of America) acquisition completed ahead of schedule on 11 February for $3.2bn ($2.7bn cash); contributed two months in Q1, US production 231 kboepd vs nil prior year.
  • UK production declined to 153 kboepd (-7.3% YoY) reflecting natural decline; Norway 181 kboepd flat with strong reliability at Njord and Gjøa.
  • Q1 revenue $3.0bn (+7.1% YoY vs $2.8bn); realised post-hedge oil $76/bbl (+2.7%) and EU gas $14.8/mscf (+6.5%).
  • Pre-hedge realisations: oil $79/bbl, EU gas $14.7/mscf.
  • Flagged widening Dated Brent vs ICE Brent front-month spread to $18/bbl in April vs $3/bbl in Q1 — relevant as most crude priced against Dated Brent.
  • Unit opex $12.8/boe (vs $13/boe Q1 25).
  • Q1 capex $0.5bn flat YoY; Q1 FCF $0.7bn flat YoY (held back by $0.2bn negative working capital from March price step-up).
  • Net debt rose to $6.3bn at 31 March (vs $4.4bn at 31 Dec 2025) driven by LLOG cash consideration.
  • Post period end: repaid c.$240m October 2026 bond from cash; repaid remaining drawn RCF balance in May.
  • Investment grade ratings reaffirmed: Moody's Baa2 (negative), Fitch BBB- (stable), S&P BBB- (stable).
  • TRIR worsened to 1.3 (vs 1.1); GHG intensity stable at 13 kgCO2/boe.
  • M&A pipeline: Indonesia divestments ($215m) to complete this quarter; UK Waldorf acquisition ($170m) on track for mid-year close.
  • Post-period agreed sale of 5% Zama (Mexico) to Grupo Carso subsidiary for $75.25m (structured as carry); Harbour retains 27.3%.
  • Project milestones: Norway Dvalin North start-up Q3, Irpa by year end, Alve Nord/Idun Nord first gas accelerated into 2026; US Who Dat East FID targeted Q3 with second rig arriving by year end; UK Fotla farm-in (45%) and GBA hub FID by year end; Egypt Fayoum-Messinian first gas end-2026.
  • Argentina Southern Energy LNG project on track for end-2027 start-up (country's first LNG export); 8-year 2 mtpa offtake signed with SEFE; EPC awarded for Vaca Muerta pipeline.
  • Exploration: Omega Sør discovery (Norway, near Snorre) being fast-tracked; EZZ appraisal success at Disouq (Egypt); awarded 9 Norway APA licences and 10 GoA leases.
  • CCS: Greensand Future commercial operations targeted around year end; Greenstore 3D seismic completed.
  • Added Q2-Q4 2026 hedges via zero-cost collars: ~19 kboepd EU gas at $16.5-26.5/mscf; ~12 kboepd oil at $68-105/bbl.
  • Confirmed 2025 final dividend of 8.05 cents/share ($150m) payable 20 May 2026.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Adjusted Earnings of $6.9bn, up 124% QoQ from $3.3bn and up 24% YoY from $5.6bn; Adjusted EPS $1.22 vs $0.57 in Q4 and $0.92 in Q1 2025.
  • Income attributable to shareholders $5.69bn (vs $4.13bn Q4); basic EPS $1.01.
  • Adjusted EBITDA $17.7bn, +39% QoQ, +16% YoY.
  • Sequential earnings driver: higher trading & optimisation contributions (notably in Downstream, Renewables & Energy Solutions), higher realised prices, higher refining margins, lower opex, higher Lubricants margins; partly offset by lower volumes.
  • Identified items: net loss of $2.4bn (vs $1.2bn net gain in Q4), driven by unfavourable fair-value movements on commodity derivatives.
  • New $3.0bn buyback programme announced, to be completed by Q2 2026 results; prior $3.5bn Q4 programme completed.
  • Buyback to be suspended during ARC shareholder circular period until ARC EGM concludes; missed buybacks rolled into remaining 2026 programmes.
  • Q1 shareholder distributions $5.3bn ($3.2bn buybacks + $2.1bn dividends).
  • Dividend per share $0.3906, +5% QoQ and +9% YoY.
  • Net debt rose to $52.6bn from $45.7bn (+$6.9bn QoQ); gearing 23.2% vs 20.7%.
  • Net debt build reflects $2.9bn FCF more than offset by $3.9bn lease liability increase (incl. $3.2bn non-cash uplift in variable shipping lease component due to Middle East conflict index increases), $3.2bn buybacks, $2.1bn dividends, $1.0bn interest.
  • Cash flow from operations $6.1bn vs $9.4bn Q4, hurt by $11.2bn working capital outflow (commodity price impact on inventory and receivables) and $2.3bn tax.
  • Free cash flow $2.9bn (vs $4.2bn Q4, $5.3bn Q1 25).
  • Cash capex $4.2bn (vs $6.0bn Q4); ROACE 9.9% vs 9.4%.
  • Total oil & gas production 2,752 kboe/d, -4% QoQ, mainly due to Middle East conflict impact on Qatari volumes and Adura JV deconsolidation in Upstream.
  • LNG liquefaction 7.86Mt (+1% QoQ) helped by LNG Canada ramp-up; LNG sales 19.16Mt.
  • Pearl GTL Qatar damaged 18 March 2026 in attack on Ras Laffan; one of two trains hit, full repair expected to take ~1 year; limited write-off in depreciation.
  • Force majeure declared on certain LNG supply contracts due to Strait of Hormuz blockage; no impairment recognised as long-term price assumptions unchanged.
  • Segment Adjusted Earnings: Integrated Gas $1.82bn (+10% QoQ), Upstream $2.38bn (+51%), Marketing $1.33bn (+131%), Chemicals & Products $1.93bn (vs -$66m in Q4), Renewables & Energy Solutions $348m (vs $131m), Corporate -$908m.
  • Chemicals had negative Adjusted Earnings of -$117m; Products positive at $2,042m; refinery utilisation 99% (vs 95%); chemicals plant utilisation 85% (vs 76%).
  • M&A — ARC Resources acquisition (announced April 2026): CAD 8.20 cash + 0.40247 Shell shares per ARC share, ~$13.6bn equity value; Montney shale focus; expected close H2 2026.
  • Jiffy Lube International sale to Monomoy Capital Partners for $1.3bn announced March 2026, with long-term lubricants supply agreement; expected close H2 2026.
  • FY2026 cash capex guidance $24–26bn (vs $21bn FY2025 actual), including ~$4bn related to ARC acquisition — a notable step-up.
  • Q2 2026 guidance: Integrated Gas production 580–640 kboe/d; LNG liquefaction 6.8–7.4Mt (reflecting Middle East/Qatar impact and higher planned maintenance); Upstream 1,620–1,820 kboe/d (higher maintenance); Marketing volumes 2,500–2,700 kb/d; refinery util 91–99%; chemicals util 76–84%; Corporate net expense $600–800m.
  • Long-term commodity price assumptions and impairment discount rates unchanged despite market volatility.

body:
• Routine regulatory disclosure of 2025 payments to governments under Part 2A, Part 26 and DTR 4.3A; no trading or guidance content.
  • Total payments to Government of Mozambique: $17.135m (sole recipient government).
  • Breakdown: Taxes $7.051m, Royalties $8.930m, Fees $1.154m; nil for production entitlements, dividends, bonuses, and infrastructure improvements.
  • Entity split: KMML $13.575m (taxes $7.051m, royalties $5.603m, fees $0.921m); KMPL $3.560m (royalties $3.327m, fees $0.233m) — KMPL remains exempt from corporation tax under its Implementation Agreement.
  • KMML paid Mozambique corporation tax at 35% on taxable profits of its branch (HMC cash costs plus defined margin).
  • Margin applied under the 2002 Mineral Licensing Contract for FY2025: 45.7% (derived from YoY change in finished product prices, inflation-adjusted).
  • Royalty regime unchanged: KMML 3% mining royalty on HMC sold to KMPL; KMPL 1% revenue royalty.
  • Moma Mine treated as a single project; payments routed via Autoridade Tributária Unidade dos Grandes Contribuintes – Nampula.
  • Report approved by directors on 6 May 2026, signed by Tom Hickey and Andrew Webb.

body:
• Operating revenues of $58.9m, up 36.4% YoY (Q1 2025: $43.2m).
  • Net income of $29.1m, up 65.1% YoY (Q1 2025: $17.6m).
  • Net income per common unit of $0.59 vs $0.36 prior year (+63.9%).
  • Q1 cash distribution of $0.475036 per common unit, payable 14 May 2026 to unitholders of record 4 May 2026 (previously declared).
  • Partnership notes cash distributions are not comparable to net earnings due to timing and other differences including depletion.
  • Earnings uplift comes despite a softer oil tape (Brent ~$100 in this release period vs prior-year comparative), suggesting volume/acquisition-driven growth across its 28-state mineral and royalty interest portfolio.

body:
• Production averaged ~46,000 BOE/d, ~7.5% above midpoint of guidance, with daily oil production up 10% QoQ; sales volumes 45.6 MBoe/d (68% crude oil, 84% liquids), down from 53.1 MBoe/d YoY.
  • LOE 17% below guided range and ~22% below Q4 levels; LOE per Boe of $6.53 vs $6.61 YoY.
  • Q1 capex of $78.4m, ~one-third of planned 2026 program (in line with <30% of full-year capital expectation); 18 gross (17.8 net) wells in progress at quarter-end.
  • Drilled 9 gross (8.9 net) horizontal wells, turned-in-line 12 gross; released one drilling rig in late January, now running 1 rig and 1 frac crew for majority of 2026.
  • Total revenue $215.9m vs $272.3m YoY (-20.7%), reflecting lower volumes and weaker NGL/gas pricing.
  • Net loss of $127.4m (-$1.02/diluted share) vs net income of $36.3m ($0.26) YoY, driven by a $157.0m loss on derivative instruments.
  • Adjusted net loss of $2.7m (-$0.02/share); EBITDAX $133.5m vs $197.3m YoY (-32.3%).
  • Realized prices ex-derivatives: crude $71.79/Bbl (flat YoY), NGL $17.22/Bbl (-28.9%), nat gas $1.32/Mcf (-43.6%); blended $52.57/Boe (-7.7%).
  • Unhedged EBITDAX margin $36.76/Boe vs $41.90 YoY; cash costs $15.81/Boe.
  • Free cash flow ex-working capital of $21.2m (vs -$14.5m YoY); reported FCF -$14.1m after working capital.
  • Cash $95.8m (down from $162.1m at YE25); long-term debt $1,097.6m + current portion $90m; no dividend declared (vs $0.04/share prior year Q1).
  • Hedge book extended: 2026 collars at ~$59.80–$66.84 floors/ceilings on ~9.8–12.4k Bpd, swaps at $63.45–$64.91; 2027 hedges added including swaps up to $72.25 in Q4 2027.
  • CEO Michael Hollis: "first quarter operating results outperforming guidance across the board", results reflect "less than one month of elevated oil prices tied to the current situation in the Middle East."
  • Hollis flagged "one of the most significant crude oil supply shocks the world has ever seen" and intent to use incremental FCF to strengthen the balance sheet rather than expand activity.
  • Forward-looking disclosure references an ongoing Board strategic review that may or may not result in a sale or transaction.

body:
• Revenue $237.1m, up 70% YoY (Q1 2025: $139.1m), from sales of 27.0m lbs copper and 708k lbs molybdenum.
  • Adjusted EBITDA $93.5m, up 172% YoY; Earnings from mining operations before D&A and non-recurring items $114.6m, up 195% YoY.
  • Net income $16.8m ($0.05 EPS) vs. $(28.6)m loss prior year; Adjusted net income $27.5m ($0.08 Adj EPS) vs. $(6.9)m.
  • Cash flow from operations $93.9m (vs $55.9m).
  • Realized copper price US$5.74/lb; offset by $17.4m realized derivative loss on collars with US$5.40/lb ceiling on 27m lbs.
  • Gibraltar copper production 30.0m lbs, +50% YoY; C1 cost US$2.63/lb vs $2.47 in Q4'25 and $2.26 in Q1'25.
  • Head grade 0.25% (in line with LOM avg); recoveries improved to 83%; mill throughput 7.0m tons (lower QoQ on maintenance & ore hardness).
  • Site costs rose on higher diesel (+$5.3m YoY) and explosives (+$6.1m YoY), plus unscheduled maintenance on loaders/dozers.
  • Cost warning: Site diesel currently $0.50/litre above February levels; if sustained, would add ~US$0.15/lb to Gibraltar operating costs in future quarters.
  • Site operating cost per ton milled jumped to $18.15 vs $8.73 in Q1'25.
  • Gibraltar 2026 guidance reaffirmed: 110–115m lbs Cu, expected to continue ±5% through completion of Connector pit (2026–2028); reflects supergene impact on recoveries and more conservative head grade.
  • Molybdenum by-product credit US$0.62/lb (Mo price US$25.73/lb); 2026 Mo production expected similar to 2025.
  • TCRCs expected to remain nominal in 2026 under existing offtake; spot rates attractive, may tender 2027 tons.
  • Florence Copper: SX/EW plant started mid-Feb, first cathodes harvested end-Feb; 1.5m lbs Cu cathode produced in Q1 (sales 619k lbs).
  • Five drill rigs operating; production ramp expected later in Q2 from newly acidified wells.
  • Florence 2026 production guidance maintained at 30–35m lbs Cu cathode.
  • Florence Q1 site costs US$35.7m (commissioning $15.2m, wellfield capex $13.1m, operating $7.4m); fixed-price sulphuric acid contract for 2026 insulates from Middle East supply disruption.
  • No hedges on Florence production; Gibraltar Q2'26 collars: floor US$4.00/ceiling US$5.40 on 27m lbs; Q3'26: floor US$4.75/ceiling US$7.50–8.50 on 24m lbs.
  • Liquidity: cash $169m; total available liquidity $322m including undrawn revolver.
  • Yellowhead added to BC government priority major projects list (29 April 2026); detailed project description targeted for filing summer 2026.
  • New Prosperity: Teẑtan Biny Agreement constraints unchanged; Taseko retains 77.5% but cannot be operator.
  • Harmony Gold: reversion completed late 2025; negotiating new option agreement with JDS.
  • CEO Stuart McDonald: "Taseko is uniquely positioned as a North American copper growth story. Florence Copper is adding low-cost production and cash flow growth this year, to Gibraltar's existing production base."
  • On Florence ramp: "Copper production from the initial wells has achieved a steady rate, in line with our expectations, and the focus is now on expanding the wellfield to ramp-up production over the remainder of the year."

body:
• FY pretax profit guidance raised to £1.22bn (from £1.21bn), vs £1.19bn delivered in the 52 weeks to 31 January 2026.
  • Q1 full price sales +6.2%, well ahead of the 4.0% internal forecast; first 5 weeks were up 11.8% before momentum slowed.
  • Slowdown attributed to onset of the Middle East conflict and tougher comps (last year boosted by unusually warm UK weather).
  • UK sales +4.4% (vs 1.3% guided): Online +10.1%, Retail -3.4%.
  • International sales +12.8%, with notable disruption in the Middle East; service recovered late in the period.
  • Middle East crisis cost now estimated at £47m, up from £15m prior — driven mainly by higher transport and fuel costs.
  • Mitigation: overseas price increases of "no more" than 8% to be implemented in May; UK prices unchanged beyond the previously guided 0.6%, with extra UK costs offset by cost savings and margin gains.
  • Extra £28m sales contribute £8m to profit upgrade.
  • Q2 guidance: full price sales +4.0% (UK +1.0%, International +17.0%).
  • FY full price sales growth raised to 5.0% (from 4.5%): UK +2.8%, International +14.4%.
  • Guidance assumes fuel costs stay around current levels and no further worsening/improvement in factory or global transport disruption.
  • Shares +0.6% to 12,705p in early trade on the day of release.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 FY26'

body:
• Net sales rose 2.3% to USD4.48bn (vs USD4.38bn YoY), beating consensus of USD4.27bn.
  • Organic net sales +0.3%, vs expectations of -2.3% — material beat.
  • Shares +3.9% to 1,532.49p, among top FTSE 100 performers.
  • Regional split: strong organic growth in Europe, LAC and Africa, helped by Easter timing and advance sales ahead of the FIFA World Cup.
  • North America organic net sales declined high-single-digit, reflecting continued US Spirits weakness.
  • Asia Pacific net sales declined slightly: weakness in Chinese white spirits offset low-single-digit growth in international premium spirits (aided by later Chinese New Year timing).
  • CEO Dave Lewis: "We are pleased with the strong growth across Europe, [Latin America & Caribbean] and Africa. North America remains our biggest challenge, where market conditions are soft and our offer needs to be more competitive. Actions are already underway to address this."
  • FY26 guidance reiterated: organic net sales decline of 2%–3%; organic operating profit flat to low-single-digit growth.
  • Lewis: "While we are mindful of continued geopolitical uncertainty, including the impact of the ongoing conflict in the Middle East on energy, supply and distribution; we are reiterating our fiscal 26 guidance."
  • 'Accelerate' programme on track for USD300m of savings by end of FY26; targeting ~USD3bn annual free cash flow from FY26.
  • Strategy redesign and "more competitive operating framework" underway; full strategy update at FY results on 6 August.
  • Context: FY25 net sales USD20.25bn, operating profit USD4.36bn; organic operating profit -0.7%, organic net sales +1.7%.
  • Lewis (ex-Tesco CEO 2014–2020, prior Unilever) became CEO in January following Debra Crew's July departure.

body:
• Shares fell 7.1% to 222.40p, worst FTSE 250 performer on the day.
  • Pretax profit +41% to £114.3m (PY: £80.9m).
  • Revenue +2.4% to £452.7m (PY: £442.1m).
  • Adjusted EBITDA +11% to £177m (PY: £159m).
  • Net ticket sales +7.0% to £6.32bn (PY: £5.91bn).
  • FY27 guidance — Net ticket sales £6.2bn–£6.45bn (range: -2% to +2%).
  • FY27 revenue guidance £440m–£455m (range: -3% to +0.5%) — implies potential top-line decline vs FY26.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin on net ticket sales guided to ~2.9% (up from 2.8%).
  • International Consumer expected to breakeven for the first time this year.
  • Cautioned: "we expect previously-flagged headwinds to weigh on near-term growth".
  • UK headwinds: TfL contactless rail network expansion, and government regulated fare freeze until March 2027.
  • European headwinds: Spanish rail accidents; Middle East geopolitical tensions hitting inbound air traffic and foreign travel sales.
  • CEO Jody Ford: "This has been a year of strong delivery for Trainline; with record net ticket sales and revenue, and continued double-digit growth in profitability. As carrier competition expands across Europe, we are positioning ourselves as the market aggregator."
  • AI initiatives: AI-powered rail disruption features, app integration into ChatGPT, and use of agents in operations.
  • No dividend; £94m bought back under current programme; cumulative £294m / 23% of share capital repurchased since 2023.

body:
• Thierry Garnier has resigned as a Non-executive Director with immediate effect, following his nomination to become President and CEO of Ahold Delhaize (joining its management board).
  • Garnier had served on the Tesco Board since April 2021 (5 years), sitting on the Remuneration, Nominations & Governance, and Sustainability Committees.
  • Chair Gerry Murphy commented: "Thierry has made a valuable contribution to Tesco during his 5 years as a Non-executive Director, and I would like to thank him for his service and wish him every success for the future."
  • Note: departure to a major European grocery peer (Ahold Delhaize) is a notable competitive context, though no replacement or committee reshuffle has been announced.

body:
• Kingfisher CEO Thierry Garnier has resigned to become CEO of Ahold Delhaize, replacing Frans Muller in a planned 2027 succession.
  • Kingfisher stated Garnier is leaving "to take up a senior leadership role at another company outside of Kingfisher's markets."
  • Garnier will serve a 12-month notice period; Kingfisher board has begun the search for a successor.
  • Garnier had been Kingfisher CEO for seven years, previously 20+ years at Carrefour.
  • Ahold Delhaize Supervisory Board Chair Wiebe Draijer described Garnier as "a seasoned leader with a strong track record in retail, digital transformation, deep international experience."
  • Separately, Claude Sarrailh, CEO Ahold Delhaize Europe & Indonesia, has resigned to become CEO of Italian grocer Esselunga (6-month notice).
  • Ahold Delhaize Q1 2026 (13 weeks): net sales EUR22.28bn, -4.3% YoY (EUR23.28bn), but +2.0% at constant currency.
  • Europe sales +2.7% cc; US sales only +1.5% cc (relative weak spot).
  • Operating income EUR895m, +1.7% reported / +9.3% cc.
  • Diluted EPS EUR0.62, +2.8% reported / +11% cc; diluted underlying EPS EUR0.62, +1.3% / +8.9% cc.
  • 2026 guidance reaffirmed: underlying operating margin ~4% (Q1 already at 4.0%); diluted underlying EPS growth mid-to-high single digit % at constant FX.
  • Muller on macro: "Disruptions from geopolitical volatility and tensions, including the recent armed conflict in the Middle East, are a reality our business has managed through before."
  • Market reaction: Kingfisher +0.5% at 282.30p (Frankfurt-listed Ahold Delhaize unchanged at EUR39.46) at time of article; Kingfisher subsequently closed +3.06% at 289.60p.
type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'ceo' with change verb 'resigned' in opening bullets

body:
• Saleable copper production +29% YoY to 2,177 tonnes (vs 1,691 tonnes).
  • Now a pure-play Zambia copper business following disposal of South African chrome and PGM operations in late February.
  • FY2026 copper production guidance (4,500–5,100 tonnes) is under review — prior guide implied a step-up from 2,211 tonnes in FY2025.
  • Roan expanded copper concentrator plant commissioning near completion; ramp-up of dewatered fine copper concentrate underway this month after minor technical and logistical delays.
  • Molefe copper mine: 250,162 tonnes of ore mined over the nine months; 20,064 tonnes transported to Sable Refinery.
  • Heavy rains disrupted Molefe operations, but momentum described as strong with mine plan advancing toward higher production.
  • CEO Leon Coetzer: "We continue to make strong progress in delivering our Zambia copper strategy, with Roan sustaining targeted production rates and the expanded concentrate circuit now entering ramp-up after minor technical and logistical delays, marking a key step-change in our processing capability."
  • Shares +4.4% to 2.87p in London; +4.7% to 67 rand cents in Johannesburg on the day.

body:
• Richard Meddings (68) appointed independent non-executive Director, joining Group Audit, Group Risk and Nomination & Corporate Governance Committees.
  • Meddings to succeed Brendan Nelson as Chair of the Group Audit Committee following Interim Results on 4 August 2026, after a handover period.
  • Meddings' background: ex-Group Finance Director at Standard Chartered; prior senior roles at Barclays/Woolwich; former Audit/Risk committee chair at 3i, Legal & General, Deutsche Bank, JLT, Credit Suisse; chaired NHS England 2022–2025; HM Treasury board 2014–2021.
  • Meddings' fees: £271,150 p.a. (£136,500 NED + £50k GAC + £50k GRC + £34,650 NomCo); subject to election at 2027 AGM, initial three-year term to 2030 AGM.
  • Eileen Murray appointed Senior Independent Non-Executive Director of HSBC Holdings and independent non-executive Chair of HSBC Bank plc; steps down from Group Risk Committee.
  • Dame Carolyn Fairbairn assumed role of independent non-executive Chair of HSBC UK Bank plc on 30 April 2026 (per prior 2 March 2026 announcement).
  • Ann Godbehere to retire as NED at conclusion of 2026 AGM and steps down as Chair of HSBC Bank plc; receives only pro rata May 2026 fees, no loss-of-office payment.
  • Brendan Nelson stood down from HSBC UK board on 29 April 2026; will leave Group Technology & Operations Committee post-AGM and Group Audit/Risk Committees post-Interim Results.
  • Most changes effective from conclusion of 2026 AGM on Friday, 8 May 2026.
  • Chairman Brendan Nelson on Meddings: "He is an experienced financial leader, with excellent knowledge of global banking and the international regulatory environment in which the Group operates."
  • Nelson on Murray: "Eileen's experience in banking, technology and governance make her exceptionally well placed to lead the board of HSBC Bank plc."
  • Group assets disclosed at US$3,306bn at 31 March 2026.

body:
• Net profit before tax GEL408.9m (~USD152.1m), +11% YoY from GEL367.8m.
  • Net interest income +17% to GEL625.0m; interest income +14% to GEL1.22bn; interest expense +11% to GEL595.3m.
  • Net fee & commission income -8.5% to GEL135.4m, dragged by fee & commission expense surging +70% to GEL142.0m; fee income itself +20% to GEL277.4m.
  • Operating expenses +20% to GEL345.5m — cost growth outpacing revenue lines ex-NII.
  • Q1 dividend GEL1.75/share, +17% YoY (vs GEL1.50).
  • ROE 23.4% (vs 23.2% YoY) — broadly stable at high level.
  • Uzbekistan weakness flagged: results held back by a "recalibration" of the loan book in response to regulatory changes; Uzbek net profit GEL20.7m, -4% YoY and -35% QoQ; Uzbek ROE fell to 10.9% from 13.7% YoY and 16.4% in Q4 2025.
  • Macro outlook: management expects GDP growth of 7.4% in Georgia and 7.9% in Uzbekistan in 2026, well above IMF (5.3%/6.8%) and World Bank (5.0%/6.4%) forecasts.
  • Geopolitical context: CEO Vakhtang Butskhrikidze noted "increased global volatility and uncertainty following the start of the US-Iran war in late February" but said "the economic impact has been relatively muted and, for now, we still expect to see strong economic growth in both countries in 2026."
  • Shares +2.8% to 4,831.87p late Wednesday morning on the print.

body:
• Launched Cider Hill, a new townhome community in Winston-Salem, NC, with prices starting in the $350s.
  • Small-scale development: only 36 homesites available.
  • Product positioning: low-maintenance townhomes with private outdoor space, garages, full driveways and lawn maintenance included.
  • Standard spec includes Whirlpool stainless appliances, Moen faucets, Wi-Fi-enabled garage door opener, double-pane Low-E windows and LED ENERGY STAR lighting — consistent with LGI's move-in-ready value proposition.
  • VP of Sales Tyler Zulli: *"Winston-Salem continues to attract homebuyers seeking strong employment opportunities, convenient access throughout the Triad, and a high quality of life... we're excited to introduce Cider Hill and offer thoughtfully designed townhomes with private outdoor space, garages, full driveways and lawn maintenance included."*
  • Context: LGI now operates across 36 markets in 21 states and has closed over 80,000 homes since 2003; this is an incremental community launch rather than a financial/guidance event.

body:
• CEO Thierry Garnier has resigned to become CEO of Ahold Delhaize NV, succeeding Frans Muller in 2027.
  • Garnier will serve a 12-month notice period; the board has begun searching for a successor.
  • Garnier had been in post for seven years, joining from Carrefour where he spent 20+ years.
  • Garnier has resigned immediately as a non-executive director of Tesco, where he had served since 2021.
  • Kingfisher shares +3.0% to 289.33p on the news.
  • Russ Mould (AJ Bell) suggested the new CEO may pursue acquisitions or a break-up, including a potential sale of the underperforming French operations or a Screwfix spin-off which could be worth more standalone.
  • Shore Capital's David Hughes called the departure a "disappointment" after a "distinguished period in the role".
  • Ahold Delhaize Supervisory Board Chair Wiebe Draijer described Garnier as "a seasoned leader with a strong track record in retail, digital transformation, deep international experience, and a clear focus on customers, associates and the future of grocery retail."
type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'ceo' with change verb 'resigned' in opening bullets

body:
• Launched a cash tender offer for any and all $500m of its outstanding 3.125% Senior Notes due 2 November 2026.
  • Purchase price set via fixed spread of +25bps over the 4.125% U.S. Treasury due 31 October 2026 (Bloomberg FIT 3), priced at 2pm NY time on 12 May 2026.
  • Tender expires 5pm NY time, 12 May 2026; settlement expected 15 May 2026.
  • Holders also receive accrued and unpaid interest up to (but excluding) the settlement date.
  • Tender is conditioned on completion of a contemporaneous New Notes Offering of senior debt securities, the proceeds of which will fund the repurchase — effectively a refinancing of the 2026 maturity.
  • No minimum tender condition; offer may be amended, extended or terminated.
  • Any untendered Notes expected to be repaid at maturity; Company plans to deposit sufficient funds with trustee Deutsche Bank National Trust Company shortly after settlement to satisfy and discharge the indenture.
  • J.P. Morgan Securities LLC acting as exclusive Dealer Manager; D.F. King & Co. as tender/information agent.

body:
• Full price sales +6.2% in the 13 weeks, ahead of company forecast of +4.0% but below buy-side expectations of 6–8%+.
  • Strong start with first 5 weeks +11.8%; growth slowed in H2 of the quarter on Middle East conflict disruption and tougher comps (last year boosted by unusually warm UK weather).
  • FY pretax profit guidance raised to £1.22bn from £1.21bn (+£8m), vs £1.19bn delivered in the 52 weeks to 31 Jan 2026; £28m additional sales drop-through.
  • FY full price sales growth guidance lifted to +5.0% from +4.5% (UK +2.8%, International +14.4%).
  • Q2 guidance: full price sales +4.0% (UK +1.0%, International +17.0%).
  • UK sales +4.4% (vs 1.3% guided): Online +10.1%, Retail -3.4%.
  • International sales +12.8%, well short of ~20% buy-side hopes due to Middle East service disruption; exit rate accelerated to +18.3%.
  • Middle East crisis cost now estimated at £47m vs prior £15m, mainly higher transport/fuel costs.
  • Mitigation: overseas price increases of "no more" than 8% to be implemented in May; UK extra costs offset by cost savings and margin gains, no price rises beyond the 0.6% already planned.
  • Guidance assumes fuel costs and supply chain disruption remain at current levels.
  • Jefferies (Frederick Wild): "This upgrade, we believe unexpected by the market, is complemented by a complete offset to Middle East cost increases (more pricing/cost saves offsetting the pressure emerging locally) and no signs of change to underlying consumer behaviour."
  • JPMorgan (Georgina Johanan): "This is a solid statement from Next, although we think it could be seen as slightly disappointing in the context of elevated expectations heading into the print, where some data points had suggested an ongoing acceleration"; views update as "somewhat reassuring" for broader UK retail.
  • UBS raised FY27 PBT forecast by 1% to £1.23bn; views 5.0% sales guidance as "conservative", sticks with 6.0%.
  • AJ Bell (Russ Mould): called Next a "well-oiled machine"; "Such is Next's skill at managing expectations that even when confronted with an event as disruptive as the conflict in the Middle East it still feels able to lift guidance."
  • Peel Hunt described update as "better-than-feared".
  • Hargreaves Lansdown (Aarin Chiekrie) flagged tough Q2 UK comps from last year's warm weather and M&S cyber disruption: "While some caution is wise, Next has developed a track record of under-promising and over-delivering in recent years. And as a leader in the UK market, it's better placed than many UK retail peers to navigate a tougher backdrop".
  • Shares +3.3% to 13,045p on the day.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 FY27'

body:
• Further profit warning: Chair Tim Martin cautioned that "substantial increases in costs" may result in profits "slightly below market expectations" — second downgrade after January's interim cut.
  • Q3 like-for-like sales +3.4% YoY; YTD LFL +4.3% (Q3 slightly below YTD pace).
  • Total sales +4.1% in Q3; +4.9% YTD.
  • LFL sales ran ahead of the NIQ RSM Hospitality Business Tracker for the 43rd consecutive month in March.
  • Year-end net debt guidance unchanged at £740m–£760m; interest costs ~£47m, in line with prior year.
  • FactSet consensus pretax profit £73m (vs FY25 actual £81.4m) — Jefferies notes this was the bar before the latest warning.
  • Estate: 794 managed pubs (8 opened, 8 sold YTD); 21 franchised pubs (13 franchise openings YTD).
  • New pub pipeline: Manchester and Heathrow airports, Paddington and Charing Cross stations, Shaftesbury Avenue.
  • Prior January commentary recap: energy, wages, repairs and business rates rose £45m in the first 25 weeks.
  • Broker reactions:
  • Shore Capital (Hold): FY26F operating profit £135m likely to be nudged down by ~£5m; "recent profit momentum has disappointed, with other stocks offering better earnings momentum, higher quality balance sheets, and lower valuations."
  • Jefferies (Buy, 840p PT): "Wetherspoon continues to gain market share...but cost pressures continue"; warns "yet more cost pressure may mean limited scope for valuation multiple expansion."
  • Shore on Middle East/weather: "Q3 performance would suggest limited impact to date from the fallout of conflict in the Middle East."
  • AJ Bell's Russ Mould: FY26 profit set to come in below pre-pandemic FY19, despite sales being ~£400m higher; flagged NICs, utilities, wages and business rates leaving company "running fast to stand still."
  • Shares +2.0% at 594.50p on the day, having earlier dipped to 574.00p.

body:
• Response to Ryanair CEO Michael O'Leary's proposed two-drink limit at airport hospitality venues; Wetherspoon disagrees with the proposal.
  • Describes the suggestion as difficult to manage and a "big brother" approach; argues airport venues are already highly supervised with CCTV, experienced managers, and police/airport authority oversight.
  • Participates in the industry "One Too Many" campaign to discourage anti-social behaviour on flights.
  • Airport sales mix over last six months: 65% from food, soft drinks, tea and coffee; only 35% alcoholic, with a significant proportion served alongside meals.
  • Drinks breakdown: tea/coffee 17% of overall drink sales; soft drinks 23%.
  • Operating history cited: Heathrow since 1992, Gatwick since 1994, Stansted since 2003 — states it has never been told its customers caused issues on flights.
  • Management view: a two-drink limit would push consumption to unsupervised "off-trade" purchases (off-licences/supermarkets) before passengers reach the airport.
  • Company (and O'Leary) believe the problem is worse on incoming flights from certain destinations where controls are weaker.
  • Chairman Tim Martin: "A two-drink limit would be extraordinarily difficult to implement, short of breathalysing passengers, and would, in our opinion, be an overreaction- especially since many of the problems stem from incoming flights."
  • No financial guidance, trading update, or KPI changes disclosed — statement is reputational/policy-related only.

body:
• Net ticket sales +7% YoY to £6.319bn (within 6-9% guidance range); Revenue +2% to £453m (towards upper end of 0-3% guidance).
  • Adjusted EBITDA +11% to £177m (within previously upgraded 10-13% guidance); operating profit +43% to £122m.
  • Basic EPS +48% to 19.4p; adjusted basic EPS +23% to 23.6p.
  • Adjusted free cash flow £66m, down 9% due to working-capital timing (year-end on weekend inflating receivables).
  • Gross profit +6% to £374m (cost of sales down on MOU-driven industry cost reductions, fulfilment cost cuts, payment/CS efficiencies); marketing -6% to £67m; other admin +7% to £130m on higher legal/public-affairs costs around GBR.
  • FY27 guidance: net ticket sales £6.2-6.45bn; Revenue £440-455m; adj. EBITDA margin c.2.9% of NTS (vs 2.80% FY26), with International Consumer reaching breakeven for first time.
  • Headwinds flagged into FY27: Project Oval (TFL contactless expansion, c.£150m UK NTS at risk), regulated fare freeze to March 2027, TOC self-preferencing, tightening of UK refund rules from April 2026, Spain rail accidents, Middle East geopolitics on inbound air traffic.
  • UK Consumer: NTS +6% to £4.14bn; Revenue -2% to £204m due to commission rate cut from 5.0% to 4.5% (Apr 2025); ex-cut, revenue would have grown 7%. Adj EBITDA -2% to £87m.
  • International Consumer: NTS +3% CCY to £1.10bn; Revenue +10% CCY to £60m; adj EBITDA loss narrowed from -£20m to -£11m. South-East France NTS +26% post Trenitalia expansion (Paris-Marseille avg fares -27%); Spain rebalanced toward profitability; Germany/RoE -6%.
  • Trainline Solutions: NTS +14% to £1.08bn (fastest unit); B2B Distribution +36%, International B2B +58%; revenue +4% to £189m; adj EBITDA +11% to £101m. Lost Cross-Country white-label; ScotRail white-label loss expected in FY27.
  • Buyback: £94m repurchased of current £150m programme as at 30 April 2026; £294m total since Sept 2023 (23% of issued share capital); 60.1m shares cancelled in FY26 at avg £2.52.
  • Net debt £170m (1.0x adj EBITDA), up from £76m (0.5x) - reflecting £147m of buybacks plus £82.7m convertible bond redeemed Jan 2026.
  • New £450m RCF signed July 2025, extended to £600m in Feb 2026, maturity July 2028 with two 1-yr extension options to 2030; covenants unchanged (net debt/EBITDA ≤3.0x; EBITDA/finance charges ≥4.0x); £221.8m undrawn.
  • Capex: £53m (intangibles £37m, PP&E £17m incl. new 10-yr London office lease).
  • Regulatory win: UK Government confirmed (March 2026) that once GBR is established, Delay Repay will be claimable from third-party retailers including Trainline - described as customers' "number one ask".
  • Railways Bill progressing: separation of GBR commercial retail from third-party retail governance, code of practice via ORR with binding enforcement; GBR Online Retail procurement contract award targeted Jan 2027.
  • France: April 2026 French Senate amendment would oblige SNCF Connect to distribute competitor tickets on FRAND terms (early stage).
  • AI: launched Travel Forecast (3m+ users updated), AI Travel Assistant (2m+ conversations), Delay Repay notifications (1m redirected); Trainline app integrated into ChatGPT (March 2026); #1 cited rail app in Google AI Search across all core markets and ChatGPT (ex-France); GenAI delivers c.3% of new International foreign-travel customers.
  • KPIs: 27m active customers globally; 18m UK; digital railcard users +16% to 2.7m; 45% share of 16-30 railcard users; double-digit % growth in hotel and insurance ancillaries.
  • Effective tax rate 30% (FY25: 28%), elevated by deferred tax asset reduction on share-based payments (lower share price).
  • No dividend; capital return policy remains buyback-led.
  • CEO Jody Ford: "This has been a year of strong delivery for Trainline; with record net ticket sales and revenue, and continued double-digit growth in profitability."
  • CEO on Europe: "As carrier competition expands across Europe, we are positioning ourselves as the market aggregator."
  • CEO on regulation: "We strongly welcome the recent decision to open Delay Repay to independent retailers, our customers' number one ask."

body:
• EPRA NTA per share rose 1.0% QoQ to 120.6p (Dec-25: 119.4p), driven by a 0.8% like-for-like valuation uplift from inflation-linked rent reviews.
  • Total accounting return of 2.3% for the quarter (incl. dividend).
  • EPRA "topped-up" NIY unchanged at 6.23%; annualised contractual rent £60.1m (Dec-25: £59.5m).
  • Adjusted EPRA EPS of 1.60p vs 1.58p in Dec-25 quarter (excluding non-recurring historical rent arrears recovery).
  • Third interim dividend of 1.508p per share (fully covered); ex-div 14 May, payment 29 May 2026.
  • Net LTV 15.2%, well below ~25% target, with c.£100m capital available (excl. uncommitted accordion) to redeploy from prior-quarter disposals.
  • Pipeline in excess of capital available, with indicative blended NIY >6%; further acquisitions expected to be committed before June 2026 year-end.
  • Rent collection 99% for the quarter; the home responsible for the arrears was sold at carrying value post quarter-end, with full collection expected in the Q4 (June 2026) quarter.
  • Portfolio: 86 care homes, 31 tenants, valued at £903.2m (Dec-25: £894.6m); WAULT 26.1 years (Dec-25: 26.3 years).
  • Contracted rent +1.0% QoQ: +0.8% LfL from 21 inflation-linked rent reviews averaging 3.7% uplift, +0.1% from re-tenanting, +0.1% from capex rentalisation.
  • Re-tenanted one asset (0.9% of rent roll) to existing tenant: 12-month rent-free in exchange for >6% rent uplift, lease extension by ~14 years to 35 years, and new green lease clauses; £1.6m capex facility granted.
  • Rentalised c.£1.2m of capex across seven properties (PV panels etc.) at blended yield above 6.2% portfolio NIY.
  • Mature home rent cover 1.9x (Dec-25 quarter data) vs 2.0x prior quarter.
  • Debt: £280m committed facilities, £203.5m drawn; weighted avg term 5.4 years; £200m fixed until at least Sept 2030 at 3.89% weighted avg cost.
  • 100% of portfolio rated EPC A or B (compliant with anticipated 2030 MEES).
  • CEO Kenneth MacKenzie: "We have agreed terms and are in legals for purchases at a value in excess of those disposal proceeds and expect these disposal proceeds to have been materially committed by the end of the Company's financial year."
  • CEO on operations: "I am also pleased to note that, subsequent to the quarter end, we disposed of one home which was responsible for the rent arrears in the current quarter and would expect the portfolio to return to full rent collection in the quarter to 30 June 2026."

• Net profit GEL 365m, +15% YoY (-5.8% QoQ); ROE 23.4% (vs 23.2% 1Q25, 24.9% 4Q25).
  • Net interest income GEL 625m, +17.2% YoY; NIM 7.0% (+30bps YoY, flat QoQ).
  • Net fee & commission income GEL 135m, -8.5% YoY and -16.3% QoQ (fee expense +70% YoY a key drag).
  • Total operating income GEL 859m, +11.0% YoY.

  • Credit loss allowance GEL 105m, -11.5% YoY; Cost of risk 1.3% (vs 1.4% YoY, 1.1% QoQ).
  • Income tax just GEL 44m (effective ~10.8%) helped by Uzbekistan tax credits and deferred tax assets on ECL charges.

  • Customer deposits GEL 26.2bn, +16.5% YoY / +2.3% QoQ; MOF deposits surged to GEL 816m (from GEL 216m).

  • Uzbekistan business loan portfolio now >USD 150m, 18% of loan book; >1.0m Salom Cards in issue; payments value +40% YoY.

  • Uzbekistan 1Q26 GDP +8.7% YoY; CPI 7.1%; CBU rate held at 14.0%.
  • Product pipeline: auto loans and collateralised MSME lending to launch around mid-year; in-app AI banking assistant "Lola" rolling out.
  • CEO: "our strong growth outlook for the full year remains in place".

body:
• Full price sales +6.2% YoY, beating +4.0% forecast by £28m, driven by exceptionally strong first 5 weeks (+11.8%).
  • Quarter split: Weeks 1-5 +11.8%, Weeks 6-8 -0.2% (Middle East conflict disruption), Weeks 9-13 +5.3% (recovery).
  • FY profit guidance raised to £1,218m (+5.2% YoY) from £1,210m; £8m uplift from Q1 beat, with rest-of-year sales guidance unchanged.
  • Q1 divisional: UK Online Brand +5.8%, UK Online LABEL +15.7%, UK Online Total +10.1%, Retail Stores -3.4%, Total UK +4.4% (vs +1.3% planned), International +12.8%.
  • Middle East conflict cost estimate raised to £47m (from £15m in March) covering full year, fully offset by £47m of mitigations — net zero profit impact.
  • Mitigations: price increases in Middle East (+£13m), margin gains from better factory gate prices (+£12m), other cost savings (+£16m), European currency gains (+£5m), price increases in other overseas countries (+£1m).
  • International price increases implemented in May, capped at +8% in any non-European territory; no European price increases needed (FX offset).
  • UK prices held to the +0.6% originally forecast — no incremental UK price rises despite cost pressure.
  • Assumptions: fuel costs stay near current levels; factory/transport disruption neither worsens nor improves.
  • FY26/27 guidance updated: Full price sales £5.9bn (+5.0% vs prior +4.5%); Total Group sales £7.3bn (+4.6% vs +4.2%); EPS 792.9p (+6.5% vs +5.8%).
  • FY sales guidance by division: UK +2.8%, International +14.4%, Total +5.0%.
  • Q2 guidance: UK +1.0% (tough comp from warm weather and competitor disruption last year), International +17.0%; H2 International moderates to +14.0% as ZEOS aggregator step-change anniversaries from August.
  • Buybacks: £196m completed YTD at avg £126.52, reducing share count 1.3%; £510m FY target; ERR-based price ceiling raised to £132.
  • Any unspent buyback budget to be returned via special dividend or capital return.
  • Next update: H1 trading statement on 5 August 2026.

body:
• CEO Thierry Garnier has resigned after nearly seven years in role, to take up a senior leadership position at another company outside Kingfisher's markets.
  • Board has a succession plan in place; Nomination Committee leading search across internal and external candidates.
  • Garnier serving a 12-month notice period and remains as CEO/Board member during transition; exact departure date to be announced.
  • Chair Claudia Arney credited Garnier with launching the "Powered by Kingfisher" strategy, navigating COVID-19, and driving innovations including marketplaces and the trade business.
  • Garnier said: "I remain focused on continuing to execute on our strategic plans with all my energies, so as to leave Kingfisher in the best possible shape for the future."
  • Remuneration to be handled per Directors' Remuneration Policy and relevant incentive plan rules; details to follow on company website and in next Annual Report.
  • No further disclosures required under Listing Rule 6.4.

body:
• Total saleable Cu production +28.7% YoY to 2,177t (9m FY2025: 1,691t), excluding stockpiled fine concentrate at Roan and stockpiled Molefe ore.
  • Roan production +112.7% to 1,999t Cu units, including 779t in oxide concentrate fines (new stream commenced August 2025).
  • Sable Refinery cathode output 957t (+27.4% vs 751t).
  • Q3 production softer at 634t vs Q1 939t, reflecting commissioning delays and seasonal rains.
  • FY2026 copper production guidance under review due to: (1) updated Molefe mine plan with accelerated stripping to merge Pits 2 & 3, completion expected July 2026; (2) delay in commissioning/ramp-up of expanded Roan dewatering circuit, which deferred the additional ~110t equivalent Cu units step-up.
  • Updated guidance to be issued "within the coming weeks" once consultant confirms Molefe progress and dewatering circuit reaches steady state.
  • Expanded Roan Cu concentrate dewatering facility currently at ~75% of targeted 110–120tpm contained Cu; ramp-up underway during May 2026.
  • Dewatering circuit design capacity ~230tpm contained Cu, enabling simultaneous processing of the 14,000t historical fine concentrate stockpile (~2.7% Cu) plus ongoing 110–120tpm fine concentrate.
  • Roan concentrator running at ~30,000tpm; front-end upgrade offers potential >40,000tpm post seasonal rains and dewatering ramp-up.
  • Roan annual maintenance shutdown 8 May 2026 for 6 days; Sable to keep operating by drawing down in-process stocks.
  • Molefe Mine: 250,162t of Cu reef ore mined in the period; 20,064t dispatched to Sable at average 1.84% Cu grade; on-site stockpile now exceeds 2.3Mt.
  • Molefe pre-stripping: >250,000t removed in April 2026 alone; post-integration mine expected to ramp from ~12,000t/quarter to >30,000t/quarter of Cu reef ore at expected ~1.4% total Cu grade pre-sorting.
  • JORC-compliant resource competent person's report expected Q4 CY2026, subject to assay turnaround; further drill campaigns to be considered thereafter.
  • Molefe Mine investor presentation planned for June 2026.
  • LTIFR 0.00 for the period (FY2025: 1.40).
  • SA Head Office relocating to Irene, Centurion in early June 2026 — right-sizing after disposal of SA chrome/PGM operations.
  • Group target reiterated: 25,000tpa copper production via the three-pillar strategy (Roan concentrator, Sable refinery, regional mining + Large Waste Rock Project).
  • CEO Leon Coetzer: "We continue to make strong progress in delivering our Zambia copper strategy, with Roan sustaining targeted production rates and the expanded concentrate circuit now entering ramp-up after minor technical and logistical delays, marking a key step-change in our processing capability."
  • CEO on Molefe: "While heavy rains impacted the quarter, momentum remains strong as we advance the mine plan and plan increased production."
  • CEO on outlook: "This integrated approach gives us strong confidence in delivering sustained and scalable growth in copper production."

body:
• Profit warning: Tim Martin flagged that substantial cost increases "may result in profits slightly below market expectations."
  • Like-for-like sales +3.4% in Q3; YTD LFL +4.3% (Q3 decelerated vs YTD).
  • Total sales +4.1% in Q3; +4.9% YTD.
  • Sales growth ahead of NIQ RSM Hospitality Business Tracker for the 43rd consecutive month in March 2026.
  • 8 pubs opened and 8 sold YTD; estate now 794 managed pubs plus 21 franchised (13 franchise openings YTD).
  • Strong new-site pipeline: Manchester airport, Heathrow airport, Paddington station, Charing Cross station and Shaftesbury Ave.
  • Buybacks: 3,845,754 shares repurchased for cancellation YTD at avg £6.80 (~£26.1m).
  • Acquired freehold reversions on 4 pubs for £12.2m; cumulative freehold reversion spend since 2011 now £489m.
  • Year-end net debt guidance maintained at £740–760m.
  • FY26 interest costs ~£47m, in line with FY25.
  • Financial year comprises 52 trading weeks to 26 July 2026.

body:
• Fiscal 26 guidance reiterated — organic net sales down 2-3%, organic operating profit flat to up low-single-digit.
  • Q3 reported net sales +2.3% to $4,477m (helped by hyperinflation adjustment, partially offset by disposals).
  • Q3 organic net sales growth +0.3% (volume +0.4%, price/mix -0.1%).
  • 9M reported net sales -2.2% to $14,937m; organic -1.9% (volume -0.5%, price/mix -1.4%).
  • North America organic net sales -9.4% in Q3; US Spirits -15.4%, weaker than depletions by c.5%, lapping pre-tariff pull-forward and tequila restocking; tequila down double-digit.
  • Diageo Beer Company USA +9.1% led by Smirnoff RTD and Guinness.
  • Europe organic +8.8% led by Guinness GB/Ireland (net sales up double-digit); price/mix +2.6%.
  • Asia Pacific organic -0.8%; Chinese white spirits down double-digit (c.3% drag on region, c.0.6% on group); India hit by Maharashtra excise increase (ex-Maharashtra +HSD).
  • LAC organic +16.2% with FIFA World Cup buy-in and Easter timing benefit; Brazil double-digit; Mexico -HSD.
  • Africa organic +17.1%, double-digit growth in both East Africa and SWC.
  • Accelerate programme on track for c.$300m savings by end FY26.
  • Capex guided to lower end of $1.2-1.3bn range (FY25: $1.5bn).
  • Free cash flow guidance $3bn (FY25: $2.7bn), excluding c.$100m one-off inventory build for SAP S/4 HANA implementation in early FY27.
  • Tax rate before exceptionals c.25%; effective interest rate c.4.0%.
  • USL announced sale of RCB business on 24 March 2026; EABL stake disposal expected to complete in calendar H2 2026 — both to reduce leverage and increase financial flexibility.
  • Strategy Update to be delivered alongside FY26 full-year results on 6 August 2026.
  • FX (using spot at 30 April 2026): expected +$70m to net sales and +$50m to operating profit for FY26.
  • CEO Sir Dave Lewis: "North America remains our biggest challenge, where market conditions are soft and our offer needs to be more competitive. Actions are already underway to address this."
  • CEO on macro: "While we are mindful of continued geopolitical uncertainty, including the impact of the ongoing conflict in the Middle East on energy, supply and distribution; we are reiterating our fiscal 26 guidance."
  • Volume reporting change: Guinness flavour extract/concentrate sales now reported as equivalent finished goods volume (asset-light beer model); priors restated.

body:
• PUSU deadline for Alamadiyaf al-Masiyyah (Cafani Group) extended for a second time, now to 5.00pm (London time) on 3 June 2026 (from 6 May 2026).
  • Extension granted by the Takeover Panel under Rule 2.6(c) at the Company's request, to allow the bidder to continue progressing funding arrangements.
  • Background: original announcement on 11 March 2026 disclosed multiple unsolicited non-binding proposals for an all-cash offer for the entire issued and to be issued share capital.
  • Bidder must by the new deadline either announce a firm Rule 2.7 intention to bid or confirm it will not make an offer; deadline may be extended again with Panel consent.
  • Company notes "There can be no certainty that any firm offer will be made, nor as to the terms of any such firm offer."
  • Shareholders advised to take no action; Company remains in an Offer Period with dealing disclosure requirements continuing to apply.

body:
• Ryanair April pax: 19.3m, +5.5% YoY (vs 18.3m); 108,000 flights operated; load factor steady at 93%.
  • Ryanair rolling 12-month pax: 209.3m, +4.0% YoY (vs 201.3m); load factor steady at 94%.
  • Ryanair made no comment on Middle East conflict in the traffic report; CEO Michael O'Leary previously flagged jet fuel supply disruption risk: "if the war continues, we do run the risk of supply disruptions in Europe in May and June."
  • O'Leary noted Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed by Iran since the start of March, but disruption to jet fuel not expected until May.
  • Wizz Air April pax: 6.6m, +22% YoY (vs 5.4m); capacity +23%.
  • Wizz load factor slipped to 88.9% from 89.8% as capacity outpaced demand growth.
  • Wizz rolling 12-month pax: 70.9m, +11% YoY (vs 63.9m); load factor down to 90.6% from 91.1%.
  • Wizz has secured 70% of summer jet fuel needs; CEO Jozsef Varadi cites strong liquidity and hedging position.
  • Wizz April carbon emissions +9.8% YoY on higher capacity, but -1.9% on per-passenger-km basis.
  • Share reaction: Ryanair -3.0% to EUR22.80 in Frankfurt; Wizz Air -0.9% in London.
  • Context: Wizz closed its Abu Dhabi base in August 2025, removing Middle East exposure that peers retain.

• Bloomberg reports BP is considering exiting part or all of its UK North Sea upstream operations, per people familiar with the matter.
  • Internal review underway; a full divestment could fetch ~£2 billion.
  • Forms part of broader strategy to strip assets and pay down debt, with BP targeting ~$20 billion of divestments by end-2027.

body:
Barclays PLC

Publish Date 1 May 2026

-

Barclays Bank Delaware (operating as "Barclays US Consumer Bank") has completed the acquisition of Best Egg, Inc., a US direct-to-consumer personal loan origination platform

, originally announced on 28 October 2025.

  • Deal value: $800 million (per Cleary Gottlieb and Delaware Business Now), based on

tangible net assets of $275 million

, with

consideration representing a high-single digit Price/Earnings multiple

on 2026 earnings.

-

Expected to consume approximately 16bps of Group CET1 capital upon Completion. The Transaction will not affect Barclays' stated total distribution target of at least £10 billion between 2024 and 2026

— no impact on shareholder distribution target.

  • Best Egg scale:

facilitated over $40 billion in personal loans to more than two million customers since 2013; in 2025, expected to facilitate more than $7 billion in personal loan originations via its platform

;

currently services approximately $11 billion in personal loans

.

  • Capital-light model retained:

Barclays expects to continue this model and retain a small portion of Best Egg's new lending flow on its balance sheet

; loans funded via securitisation programmes and forward flow arrangements with alternative asset managers.

-

The acquisition, including the realisation of synergies, is expected to generate an attractive return on investment for Barclays, comparable to our three highest-returning UK businesses, over time

.

-

The acquired business will continue to operate under the "Best Egg" brand and be managed by Barclays US Consumer Bank

;

Best Egg is headquartered in Wilmington, Delaware and employs approximately 500 employees

.

  • Strategic rationale:

Best Egg adds a scaled, proven, and capital-light personal lending platform that complements the US Consumer Bank's partnership-driven credit card and deposit businesses

.

  • CEO C.S. Venkatakrishnan:

"The U.S. is a strategically important growth market for Barclays. The Best Egg acquisition strengthens our US Consumer Bank with a proven, capital-light personal lending platform. It supports greater diversification, capital efficiency, and attractive returns over time, while enhancing our ability to serve customers across the U.S. consumer finance market."

  • USCB CEO Denny Nealon:

"We're excited to welcome Best Egg's customers and colleagues to Barclays. This acquisition aligns with our strategy by expanding our personal lending capabilities and supporting the development of a more diversified, digitally-focused consumer bank. Best Egg brings a proven platform and a talented team, and we look forward to building on those strengths together."
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Pretax profit down 1.1% to USD9.38bn (Q1 2025: USD9.48bn), driven by higher ECL charges.
  • ECL and other credit impairment charges surged 49% to USD1.30bn (vs USD876m), the key drag on profit.
  • Diluted EPS up 2.6% to USD0.40 (vs USD0.39).
  • Q1 dividend maintained at USD0.10/share.
  • On buybacks: "A decision to recommence buy-backs will be subject to our normal buy-back considerations and process on a quarterly basis" — i.e. no new buyback announced.
  • Net interest income +7.7% to USD8.95bn; net fee income +12% to USD3.72bn.
  • Net operating income +3.3% to USD17.32bn.
  • NIM 1.60%, up 1bp YoY from 1.59%.
  • Operating expenses (ex-amortisation/intangibles impairment) +6.8% to USD8.00bn.
  • CET1 ratio fell to 14.0% from 14.7%, at the bottom of the 14.0–14.5% medium-term target range.
  • Total shareholders' equity USD196.82bn (vs USD190.81bn).
  • Guidance upgrade: banking NII now expected at ~USD46bn for FY2026, up from prior "at least USD45bn", reflecting improved rate outlook.
  • USD1.5bn annualised cost savings on track for delivery by end-June, six months ahead of schedule.
  • All four businesses delivered annualised RoTE >17% ex-notable items.
  • CEO Georges Elhedery: "We remain confident in achieving the targets we set out in February 2026."
  • Outlook: "The macroeconomic outlook is facing heightened uncertainty, creating volatility in both economic forecasts and financial markets resulting in both tailwinds and headwinds."
  • Shares -1.4% at HKD141.40 in Hong Kong midday trade post-release.
