body:
• Reported PBT $9.4bn, down $0.1bn vs 1Q25; PAT $7.4bn, -$0.2bn YoY. Constant currency PBT ex-notables $10.1bn, broadly stable.
  • Revenue $18.6bn, +6% YoY, driven by Wealth fees and banking NII; constant currency revenue ex-notables $19.1bn (+8%).
  • Annualised RoTE 17.3% (1Q25: 17.9%); ex-notables RoTE 18.7%, +0.3ppt YoY.
  • NII $8.9bn, +8% YoY; Banking NII $11.3bn, +$0.7bn YoY.
  • NIM 1.60%, +1bp YoY but -4bps vs 4Q25 (one-off item drag).
  • ECL charge $1.3bn, +$0.4bn YoY — driven by $0.4bn UK fraud-related secondary securitisation exposure to a financial sponsor in CIB, plus $0.3bn overlay for Middle East conflict (onset 28 February 2026).
  • Operating expenses $8.7bn, +8% YoY (target basis +3%); reflects performance pay phasing, inflation, tech spend, FX.
  • Customer lending +$13.6bn vs 4Q25 reported (+$20.1bn constant currency, growth across all segments).
  • Customer accounts -$5.1bn reported (+$9.2bn constant currency, led by CIB Hong Kong).
  • CET1 ratio 14.0%, down 0.9ppt vs 4Q25 — Hang Seng privatisation, dividends and RWA growth, partly offset by profit.
  • First interim 2026 dividend $0.10/share (~$1.72bn distribution).
  • No buyback announced; quarterly review process retained, intent to manage CET1 within 14%–14.5% target range.

Guidance changes (key):

  • Banking NII 2026 raised to ~$46bn (from prior "at least $45bn") on improved rate outlook.
  • 2026 ECL guidance raised to ~45bps of average gross loans (from ~40bps); medium-term planning range 30-40bps retained.
  • All Group financial targets from Feb 2026 FY results retained: RoTE ≥17% ex-notables for 2026, 2027, 2028.
  • Target basis opex growth ~1% vs 2025 retained.
  • Stress scenario warning: integrated Middle East downside scenarios could deliver mid-to-high single digit % adverse PBT impact, potentially bringing 2026 RoTE ex-notables below 17% target if unmitigated.

CEO Georges Elhedery quote: "We continued to make positive progress in creating a simple, more agile, growing HSBC. Each of our four businesses contributed to firm-wide revenue growth and each delivered an annualised RoTE in excess of 17%, excluding notable items... We remain confident in achieving the targets we set out in February 2026."

Notable items / strategic actions:

  • $0.3bn disposal loss on held-for-sale classification of Malta business; $0.2bn FX recycling loss on UK life insurance sale completion (Chesnara, 30 Jan 2026).
  • Hang Seng Bank privatisation completed 26 Jan 2026; targeting $0.5bn pre-tax synergies by end-2028, $0.6bn restructuring costs, plus ambition for further $0.4bn revenue/cost opportunities.
  • Simplification programme: $1.4bn of $1.5bn annualised cost saves identified, on track six months early (by end-June 2026); $0.3bn opex reduction in 1Q26.
  • 4 May 2026: agreed sale of Indonesia retail banking to OCBC NISP, expected H1 2027 close, up to $0.4bn pre-tax gain.
  • 30 April 2026: completed Sri Lanka retail sale to Nations Trust Bank.
  • Strategic reviews ongoing for retail in Australia, Egypt, and HSBC Life Singapore.

Segment highlights (constant currency PBT vs 1Q25):

  • Hong Kong: $2.6bn, +5%; RoTE 44.7%; Wealth fee income +17%.
  • UK: $1.6bn, +4%; banking NII +5% on hedge reinvestment.
  • CIB: $3.3bn, -12%, hit by $0.4bn UK fraud ECL; Debt & Equity Markets fees -26%.
  • IWPB: $1.2bn, flat (strategic transactions $0.2bn drag); Wealth NNM $39bn (vs $23bn in 1Q25), $34bn in Asia.

Balance sheet / risk:

  • Wealth balances $1.6tn (new definition excluding AM third-party); NNM $39bn (1Q26) vs $23bn (1Q25).
  • Hold-to-collect-and-sell unrealised loss reserve widened to $2.4bn (from $1.1bn at 4Q25).
  • Hong Kong CRE: ECL allowance up modestly; 'sub-standard' + 'credit-impaired' exposures $8.6bn (94% secured).
  • LCR 135% (down from 137%); leverage ratio 5.0% (down from 5.3%).
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Operating revenues $367.6m, -3.9% YoY (vs $382.4m).
  • Swung to net loss of $(3.5)m / $(0.13) EPS, vs net income $6.0m / $0.23 EPS in Q1 2025.
  • Operating income $4.8m vs $15.7m (-69%); operating margin 1.3% vs 4.1%.
  • EBITDA $40.7m vs $51.7m (-21%); EBITDA margin 11.1% vs 13.5%.
  • Shares fell -8.31% to $22.08 in after-hours.
  • Intermodal segment the key drag: revenues -32.3% to $47.9m; operating loss widened to $(13.1)m vs $(10.7)m; margin (27.4)% vs (15.1)%.
  • Intermodal load volumes -23.3%; revenue per load ex-fuel -10.4%; tractor count cut to 1,140 from 1,401.
  • Trucking revenues -9.7% to $50.2m; operating income $0.6m vs $2.2m; loads -8.9%, revenue per load ex-fuel -6.0%; tractors reduced to 545 from 633.
  • Contract Logistics revenues +5.3% to $269.5m, but operating income fell to $17.5m from $23.9m; margin 6.5% vs 9.3%; active value-added programs down to 79 from 87.
  • Quarterly dividend maintained at $0.105/share, payable 1 July 2026 (record date 1 June).
  • Cash $17.9m (down from $26.8m at YE25); marketable securities run down to zero (from $10.4m).
  • Outstanding debt $754.7m; debt-net reduced to $750.3m from $797.6m at YE25.
  • Capex $9.6m in the quarter.
  • Interest expense rose to $9.7m from $8.2m YoY.
  • CEO Tim Phillips: "Our first-quarter performance reflects a slow start to the year driven primarily by continued weakness in our intermodal segment, including lower volumes and pricing pressure."
  • On recovery timing: "While the recovery in our intermodal franchise is taking longer than anticipated, we continue to implement operational improvements and remain committed to restoring this segment to profitability."
  • Noted "positive momentum as the quarter progressed" after soft January–February.

body:
• NAV per share fell 60% to 3.2p (from 8.0p a year earlier) as liquidation progresses.
  • 1.7p of the 3.2p NAV relates solely to 'Far Ralia', the company's final remaining holding (land in the Cairngorms).
  • Share price total return of 5.5% for the year, materially lagging the FTSE All-Share REIT index (11.9%) and FTSE All-Share (24.0%).
  • 59.9p per share returned to shareholders to date via the formal liquidation process.
  • Company no longer holds income-producing assets (ex-cash interest); running costs eroding shareholder funds by ~£600,000 p.a. net.
  • Chair Mike Balfour: "The board remain cognisant that the company no longer has any income-producing assets (excluding interest on cash holdings) and the costs of running the company are now eroding shareholder funds by approximately GBP600,000 per annum (net of interest on cash holdings) based on current projections."
  • Chair adds: "The board and investment manager are, therefore, focused on minimising expenditure."
  • Shares down 16% on the day to 2.34p; down 57% over 12 months.

body:
• UBS upgrades to 'Buy' from 'Neutral'; price target raised to 115p (from 110p).
  • Rationale: weak sentiment towards Lloyds offers an attractive entry point into a "strong growth, diversified, low risk franchise at a good price."
  • Q1 pretax profit came in 6% ahead of consensus despite provisions for weaker macro outlook and lower vehicle residuals, delivering a 17% RoTE.
  • UBS acknowledges tactical caution: "We understand the tactical challenges of buying a UK domestic bank into the 7 May local elections and know that Barclays, NatWest and Lloyds are comparatively well held."
  • EPS estimates raised by 3%–4%; UBS models 2025–2028 EPS CAGR of 17%.
  • Expects 30 July strategic update to "roadmap to double digit growth, improved efficiency, ROTE and increased diversification to 2030 as investment in AI, wealth and insurance pays."
  • Current share price 99.26p; stock up 35% over the past 12 months.

body:
• Carnival to exit the FTSE 250, replaced by Singapore-based power control systems maker XP Power Ltd.
  • Exit linked to Carnival's December announcement to collapse the Carnival Corp & PLC dual-listing structure into a single entity, Carnival Corp Ltd, trading solely on the NYSE.
  • Company rationale (verbatim): "Unification will eliminate the different pricing between the two share listings, simplify governance, reporting and administrative complexity, and is expected to increase our company's weighting in key US stock indices and reduce costs – together strengthening our ability to deliver long-term shareholder value while preserving our core business strategy and key shareholder voting and economic rights."
  • Carnival share price 1,956.48p, +51% over 12 months.
  • XP Power share price 1,750.00p, more than doubled from 697.00p over 12 months.

body:
• FY2026 Adjusted Group PBT expected in line with market consensus of £18.2m (range £12.9m–£20.5m), subject to Gleeson Land completing its major land sale.
  • Net reservation rate 0.88 per site per week (vs 0.86 LY); 0.59 ex-bulk (vs 0.64 LY) — slight deterioration in underlying rate offset by bulk deals.
  • Modest build cost inflation since start of calendar year; open-market and partnership selling prices broadly stable; protecting margin remains a priority.
  • Yorkshire East to be merged into Yorkshire South & West from 1 July, with site reallocations to Midlands; annualised overhead savings of £0.9m.
  • Restructuring costs estimated at up to £3.1m (£0.3m cash + £1.5m–£2.8m non-cash land impairment), to be booked as exceptional.
  • New legacy site cost provisions of £5.2m–£7.1m for remedial works (mainly Yorkshire) on previously completed developments to enable road/statutory adoption; spend over next 3–4 years; excluded from Adjusted results.
  • Total adjustments to Adjusted Operating Profit/PBT (exceptionals + legacy provisions) expected at £7.0m–£10.2m, on top of £0.3m H1 exceptionals and the previously announced £4.5m Greater Manchester/Merseyside restructuring cost.
  • Gleeson Land: progressing sale of 5 sites; key site (~50% of FY plot sales forecast) has had technical solution accepted by highways authority, awaiting formal approval before completion.
  • CEO Graham Prothero: "It is too early to forecast the degree to which the crisis in the Middle East will impact customer confidence, mortgage affordability and build cost inflation over the coming months. We have recently seen some softening in footfall and reservations, and limited increases in the cost of some materials."
  • Prothero added caution extends to land investment decisions into FY27, citing planning and site viability challenges.
  • On legacy issues: "frustrating that further work is required on a number of legacy sites" but allows Gleeson Homes to "look forward with confidence."

body:
• Income guidance upgraded: now expects FY26 income excluding notable items at the top end of the previously guided £17.2–17.6bn range; all other FY25 guidance reaffirmed (excludes forthcoming Evelyn Partners acquisition).
  • Total income £4,358m, +9.5% YoY, +0.8% QoQ; income excluding notable items £4,223m, +6.9% YoY, -1.1% QoQ (impacted by two fewer days, tax-related deposit outflows, lower mortgage margins).
  • Operating profit £2,033m, +12.2% YoY; attributable profit £1,432m; EPS 17.9p, +15.5% YoY.
  • RoTE 18.2% (Q1'25: 18.5%; Q4'25: 18.3%).
  • NIM 2.47%, +20bps YoY, +2bps QoQ — driven by deposit margin expansion from strong hedge income.
  • Cost:income ratio (ex-litigation/conduct) 46.5%, improved 2.1ppt YoY; >£100m of additional cost savings generated in Q1.
  • Other operating expenses £2,027m, +4.8% YoY (transformation, pay award, investment), -8.3% QoQ (seasonal Q4 drop-off).
  • Impairment charge £283m (26bps loan impairment rate), vs £189m Q1'25 and £136m Q4'25; includes c.£140m multiple economic scenario (MES) update reflecting weaker outlook from Middle East conflict.
  • ECL provision rose to £3.7bn; coverage 0.84%; PMAs of £0.3bn retained; unsecured Stage 3 flows up due to seasoning of post-2022 credit card growth.
  • CET1 ratio 14.3%, +30bps QoQ (Q1'25: 13.8%); 65bps capital generation pre-distributions in the quarter; £2.2bn of RWA management benefits.
  • RWAs £196.0bn, +£2.7bn QoQ (franchise growth, market/counterparty risk; partly offset by RWA actions).
  • TNAV/share 400p, +16p QoQ, +53p YoY.
  • Net loans (ex-central items) +£7.2bn QoQ to £396.4bn: Retail mortgages +£3.3bn; C&I +£3.8bn (Corporate & Institutions led).
  • Customer deposits (ex-central items) +£3.1bn to £444.8bn; C&I +£5.1bn offset by seasonal tax outflows in Retail and PB&WM.
  • AUMA -£1.8bn QoQ to £56.7bn (negative markets -£1.7bn, gilt redemptions for tax); AUM net inflows £0.9bn, c.23,000 new-to-invest clients (>50% uplift).
  • LCR 144% (-3ppt QoQ); loan:deposit ratio (ex-repos) 89%, +1ppt QoQ.
  • Segment RoEs: Retail Banking 24.6%, PB&WM 21.1%, Commercial & Institutional 18.3%.
  • Retail Banking impairment £184m (vs £114m Q4) — non-repeat of mortgage securitisation benefit, MES update, Stage 3 credit card flows.
  • C&I impairment £94m (vs £19m Q4) on MES update.
  • New partnerships announced: Rightmove (digital mortgage distribution) and Sainsbury's Group (cards, loans, savings); NatWest Boxed reclassified into Retail Banking from Q1'26.
  • Onboarded 24,000 new startups in C&I, +25% YoY; >£1.1bn social housing committed (on track for £10bn by 2028).
  • Capital actions: $0.8bn 5.908% Tier 2 issued March 2026; €0.8bn senior unsecured (2037) issued Feb 2026; redeemed $1.0bn and £0.5bn senior notes.
  • Buyback: 51m shares repurchased/cancelled YTD for £313.7m; further 10.7m shares cancelled post-period for £63.5m.
  • Foreseeable ordinary dividend accrual £2,553m; foreseeable charges £608m (relates to buybacks).
  • Macro assumptions revised: base case inflation peak >3.5%, GDP growth slowing to 0.4%, unemployment peak 5.7%, BoE rate paused; extreme downside weight raised to 14.2% (from 13.1%) reflecting stagflation risk.
  • New litigation disclosed: NWMSI named in Oracle bond offering class action (Sept 2025 issue); plaintiffs allege offering documents misleading regarding AI-related debt plans; Oracle has indemnified underwriters.
  • Swaps antitrust litigation: defendants filed summary judgment motion in March 2026.
  • US fixed-income monitorship paused May 2025 and deemed no longer necessary; probation extended to December 2026.
  • CEO Paul Thwaite: "NatWest Group's strong performance in the first quarter of 2026 reflects our consistent delivery for customers and shareholders."
  • CEO on macro: customers need support to "navigate increasing uncertainty and to achieve their goals."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• Completed the second instalment of the FY26 Share Buyback Programme, originally announced on 7 November 2025 (up to £30m).
  • RBC Europe repurchased 14,256,031 shares for c.£30 million (ex stamp duty/expenses) for cancellation.
  • Total returned via the FY26 Buyback Programme: c.£60 million to shareholders.

body:
• Net sales $2.73bn, +8.0% reported but -2.6% on constant days/FX basis vs prior year.
  • Net earnings $117m; EPS $1.90 vs $1.15 prior year (+65%); adjusted EPS $1.90 vs $1.52 (+25%).
  • Adjusted operating margin 5.5% vs 4.8%; adjusted gross margin 24.8% vs 24.1%.
  • Prior-year comp benefited from system conversion impact and had four fewer days.
  • Q2 2026 adjusted EPS guidance: $2.50–$2.60, excluding restructuring/one-time charges (one less shipping day in Q2).
  • Repurchased 607,000 shares for $64m during the quarter.
  • Net debt $1.24bn; net debt/adjusted EBITDA 0.9x; cash $872m.
  • Free cash flow $7.8m vs -$85.4m prior year; capex $102m vs $89m.
  • Segment performance (reported / adj. constant days & FX):
  • Global Ceramic: sales +10.4% / -0.2%; adj. operating margin 5.0% vs 4.8%.
  • Flooring NA: sales +2.0% / -4.1%; adj. operating margin 4.0% vs 3.0%.
  • Flooring ROW: sales +12.2% / -4.4%; adj. operating margin 9.8% vs 9.1%.
  • Middle East conflict (intensified end-February) flagged as key new headwind: rising energy/oil/gas derivative costs hitting raw materials; price increases being implemented across many categories and geographies, with further increases possible.
  • Cost impact skewed to H2: "The impact of higher cost raw materials will be greater in the second half of the year due to our flow through of inventory."
  • Pricing/cost lag: "We will not see the full impact of our pricing actions and rising input costs until the third quarter."
  • Demand commentary: commercial channel solid across regions; residential remodeling and new home construction soft as consumers defer purchases on economic uncertainty; higher-end products outperforming.
  • Order backlog continues to grow one month into Q2.
  • Tax: Q1 included a one-time $30.7m benefit from US legal entity restructuring and Brazilian tax credits (prior-year items).
  • Management actions: containing costs, reengineering products, limiting capital expenditures, executing prior restructuring projects.
  • CEO Lorberbaum: "Our performance was in line with our expectations despite a challenging environment."
  • Long-term view: "deferred remodeling of the aging housing stock across our regions will significantly increase flooring demand" as cyclical rebound approaches.

body:
• Revenue $111.2bn, +17% YoY — March quarter record (vs $95.4bn prior year).
  • Diluted EPS $2.01, +22% YoY (vs $1.65) — March quarter record.
  • Net income $29.6bn vs $24.8bn (+19%).
  • iPhone revenue $57.0bn, +21.7% YoY — March quarter record, driven by iPhone 17 demand.
  • Services revenue $31.0bn, +16.3% YoY — new all-time high.
  • Mac $8.4bn (+5.7%); iPad $6.9bn (+8.0%); Wearables/Home/Accessories $7.9bn (+5.0%).
  • Products revenue $80.2bn (+16.7%); Services gross margin implied ~76.7%.
  • Greater China $20.5bn, +28.1% YoY — standout reacceleration.
  • Americas $45.1bn (+11.9%); Europe $28.1bn (+14.7%); Japan $8.4bn (+15.1%); Rest of Asia Pac $9.1bn (+25.4%) — double-digit growth across every geographic segment.
  • Gross margin $54.8bn (49.3% vs 47.0% prior year) — ~230bps expansion.
  • R&D $11.4bn, +33.6% YoY — notable step-up (likely AI/silicon investment).
  • Operating income $35.9bn (+21.3%); operating margin 32.3% vs 31.0%.
  • Operating cash flow >$28bn in quarter; $82.6bn for the half (+53% YoY).
  • New $100bn share buyback authorisation.
  • Dividend raised 4% to $0.27/share; payable 14 May 2026, record 11 May 2026.
  • H1 buybacks $37.0bn (vs $49.5bn prior year) — pace moderated.
  • Cash & marketable securities $146.6bn; term debt reduced to $82.7bn (from $90.7bn at FY25 year-end).
  • Intangible assets nearly doubled to $21.3bn (from $11.1bn at Sept 2025) — suggests M&A or licensing activity.
  • New products in quarter: iPhone 17e, M4 iPad Air, MacBook Neo launch.
  • Tim Cook: "Today Apple is proud to report our best March quarter ever, with revenue of $111.2 billion and double-digit growth across every geographic segment."
  • CFO Kevan Parekh: installed base of active devices reached new all-time high across all major product categories and geographies.

body:
• Revenue $111.2bn, +17% YoY — March quarter record for total company revenue.
  • Diluted EPS $2.01, +22% YoY — also a March quarter record.
  • Products revenue $80.2bn (vs $68.7bn) and Services revenue $30.98bn (+16% YoY) — new all-time high.
  • iPhone revenue $57.0bn, +21.7% YoY — March quarter record, driven by iPhone 17 lineup demand.
  • Mac $8.4bn (+5.7%); iPad $6.9bn (+8.0%); Wearables/Home/Accessories $7.9bn (+5.0%).
  • Double-digit growth in every geographic segment: Americas $45.1bn (+11.9%), Europe $28.1bn (+14.7%), Greater China $20.5bn (+28.1%), Japan $8.4bn (+15.1%), Rest of Asia Pacific $9.1bn (+25.4%).
  • Gross margin $54.78bn (49.3% vs 47.0% prior year) — ~230bps expansion.
  • R&D up sharply to $11.4bn (+33.6% YoY) — notable step-up, likely AI/silicon-related.
  • Operating income $35.9bn (+21.3% YoY); net income $29.6bn (+19.4%).
  • Operating cash flow >$28bn in the quarter — March quarter record.
  • New $100bn share buyback authorization; dividend raised 4% to $0.27/share (payable 14 May 2026).
  • Installed base of active devices reached new all-time high across all major product categories and geographies.
  • New product launches highlighted: iPhone 17e, M4-powered iPad Air, and MacBook Neo.
  • Cash & marketable securities ~$146.6bn; total term debt reduced to $82.7bn (from $90.7bn at FY25 year-end).
  • CEO Tim Cook: "Today Apple is proud to report our best March quarter ever, with revenue of $111.2 billion and double-digit growth across every geographic segment."
  • CFO Kevan Parekh: "Our strong business performance during the March quarter generated over $28 billion in operating cash flow and drove new March quarter records for both operating cash flow and EPS."

body:
• Pretax profit fell 19% to £298m (vs £368m prior year) on flat revenue of £2.92bn.
  • Adjusted pretax profit flat at £483m; adjusted basic EPS up 7.1% to 208.5p (vs 194.6p).
  • Total dividend held at 97.0p; final dividend unchanged at 60.6p.
  • Premier Inn UK accommodation sales and RevPAR both +1% YoY, with return to market growth from Q2.
  • UK Food & Beverage sales down 8%; Premier Inn Germany sales up 13%.
  • Profitability hit by high cost inflation and interest costs.
  • Shares fell 6.7% to 2,225p on the day.
  • New five-year plan targeting "material step up in margins and returns" by FY2031.
  • Plan extends the Accelerating Growth Plan: replaces all remaining branded restaurants (Beefeater, Bar + Block, Brewers Fayre), reduced UK/Germany capex, higher cost savings, freehold disposals.
  • 3,800 UK & Ireland job cuts out of ~30,000 workforce (~13%).
  • FY2027 impact: AGP extension to reduce F&B sales by £140–160m and profits by £40m; net £10m profit reduction after offsetting original AGP gains.
  • By FY2031 targets: +£275m incremental adjusted pretax profit, net capex cut by £1bn, £1.5bn freehold property sales to fund growth capex, ROCE +500bps, and £2bn free cash flow available for shareholder returns.
  • Hotel room target: 96,000 rooms by FY2031 (vs ~86,600 currently); long-term aspiration of 125,000 rooms.
  • CEO Dominic Paul: "In light of significant cost increases in the form of business rates and national insurance, as well as the implied market discount to our inherent value, we've looked hard at the options open to us to maximise value creation over the medium and long-term."
  • CEO on model: the "right one" but "we can improve our approach."
  • CEO on capital strategy: "We will refocus our capital spend and recycle more of our freehold real estate, driving increased margins and returns, reducing our capital intensity and increasing cash returns for shareholders."
  • Forward booked position ahead of last year with "positive trading momentum."
  • Flagged "limited visibility of short-term market demand and inflation," citing Middle East crisis.
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with financial performance figures

body:
• 2026 production guidance reaffirmed across all major commodities.
  • Marketing division full-year adjusted EBIT seen "comfortably exceeding" the top end of the USD2.3bn–USD3.5bn long-term guidance range, supported by Q1 performance and energy marketing activities supplying fuel to mining assets.
  • CEO Gary Nagle on Middle East conflict impact: "Although the impact of the conflict on our industrial business was limited in the first quarter, recent and emerging impacts are now manifesting, primarily as an increase in input costs, most notably diesel and acid consumption, and the generally weaker USD."
  • Cost pressures expected to be "more than offset" by stronger commodity prices widening margins.
  • Q1 own-sourced production deltas YoY:
  • Copper +19% to 199,600t
  • Silver +15% to 4.9m oz
  • Chrome ore +3% to 830,000t
  • Cobalt −39% to 5,800t — due to DRC export quota system introduced late 2025, applying until at least end-2027; KCC and Mutanda have sufficient inventory to fill quotas near-term; normalisation expected over next year.
  • Nickel −9% to 17,200t — Sudbury furnace disruption
  • Zinc −17% to 176,900t; Lead −17% to 41,200t — end of mine life at Loretta (late 2025) and lower Kazzinc contribution
  • Gold −53% to 68,000 oz
  • Steelmaking coal −22% to 6.5mt
  • Energy coal −2% to 22.9mt
  • 2026 production guidance vs 2025 actuals:
  • Copper: 810–870kt (vs 851.6kt) — implies flat-to-slightly-down midpoint
  • Zinc: 700–740kt (vs 969.4kt) — sharp step-down
  • Nickel: 70–80kt (vs 71.9kt)
  • Steelmaking coal: 30–34mt (vs 32.5mt)
  • Energy coal: 95–100mt (vs 98.0mt)
  • Shares +2.0% to 564.10p in London; +1.8% to ZAR127.91 in Johannesburg on the day.
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• FY2026 guidance reiterated despite Middle East disruption: underlying operating profit £4.0bn–£4.2bn (vs £3.46bn in 2025, +16–21%) and free cash flow £3.6bn–£3.8bn (vs £3.27bn, +10–16%).
  • CEO Tufan Erginbilgic expects to "fully mitigate the current financial impact of the disruption to our business" from the Middle East.
  • "Strong start to the year across all three divisions" — Civil Aerospace, Defence, and Power Systems.
  • Civil Aerospace: large engine flying hours +5% YoY, reaching 115% of 2019 levels in Q1; FY guidance maintained at 115–120% of 2019.
  • Business aviation flying hours ahead of budget; widebody demand "remains firm."
  • Defence: aftermarket improvement plus OE deliveries up >20% YoY.
  • Power Systems: power generation order intake across gas and diesel engines ~50% higher YoY in Q1, led by data centres and governmental demand; March was a record month for orders.
  • CEO quote: "We have had a strong start to the year driven by our transformation and self-help, as we continue to further expand the earnings, cash, and growth potential of the business. Operational performance has also been strong across the group."
  • Shares rose +5.9% to 1,163.60p on the day (closed +7.59% at 1,182.00p).
  • H1 results scheduled for 30 July 2026.

body:
• Total revenue EUR12.56bn, down 3.3% YoY from EUR12.99bn.
  • Underlying sales growth 3.8%, beating 3.6% consensus (JPMorgan), composed of volume +2.9% and price +0.9%.
  • Divisional USG: Beauty & Wellbeing +3.6%, Personal Care +3.7%, Home Care +6.1%, Foods +2.2% (laggard).
  • Power Brands led with 5.0% USG and 4.0% volume growth.
  • Emerging Markets USG +5.7%, driven by strong India and Latin America recovery.
  • FY2026 guidance unchanged: USG at bottom end of 4–6% range, with at least 2% underlying volume growth.
  • Expects modest improvement in underlying operating margin vs 20.0% in 2025.
  • McCormick Foods combination: stranded costs of EUR400–500m, offset by savings 2027–2029; one-off restructuring costs of EUR500m over the period; deal to close by mid-2027 "at the latest".
  • Follows December 2025 demerger of Magnum Ice Cream Co.
  • EUR1.5bn share buyback starts today, run by Morgan Stanley, expected complete by 6 July.
  • CEO Fernando Fernandez: "We have started the year well with volume-led growth driven by our Power Brands and a positive performance across all business groups."
  • Fernandez added: "We continue to move at speed to build a simpler, sharper Unilever with a structurally higher growth profile and a brand portfolio fit for the future."
  • Shares +1.9% to 4,294.50p on the day.

body:
• Pretax profit USD2.45bn, up 17% YoY from USD2.10bn.
  • Operating income +10% reported (+9% CER) to a record USD5.90bn vs USD5.38bn.
  • EPS rose 31% to 74.2c from 56.6c.
  • RoTE improved to 17.4% from 14.8%.
  • CET1 ratio slipped to 13.4% from 13.8%.
  • Net interest income -3.2% to USD1.53bn; adjusted NII +2.6% reported (+1% CER) to USD2.87bn.
  • Adjusted non-interest income +16% CER (+17% reported) to USD3.03bn.
  • Wealth Solutions record quarter, income +32%; Global Banking income +19%.
  • FY guidance reiterated: CER operating income growth "around the bottom end" of 5%-7% range (i.e. lower end of prior forecast).
  • NII at constant currency expected "to be broadly flat"; statutory RoTE to exceed 12%.
  • CEO Bill Winters: "We delivered a record first quarter performance in 2026, with double-digit growth in Wealth Solutions and Global Banking. Despite ongoing geopolitical tensions and global economic uncertainty, our advantaged market presence and disciplined risk management give us confidence in our ability to perform."
  • Shares +2.8% at 1,839.40p on the day.

body:
• Q1 trading broadly in line with 2026 guidance.
  • Net loan book up 0.9% QoQ to £26.15bn (Dec: £25.92bn).
  • Retail deposits up 1.8% QoQ to £24.70bn (Dec: £24.25bn).
  • CET1 ratio declined to 15.1% from 15.8% at end-December, described as "remained robust".
  • 2026 guidance reiterated: loan book growth "broadly similar" to 2025; NIM of 225bps (vs 228bps in 2025) — implying modest margin compression.
  • Transformation programme in penultimate year; Residential mortgages launch on new lending platform scheduled for Q3 2026, with final-stage testing underway.
  • CEO Andy Golding: "The group delivered a resilient financial performance in the first quarter of 2026 and we continue to operate broadly in line with our 2026 guidance."
  • On outlook, Golding flagged caution: "Looking ahead, we are mindful of the ongoing uncertain geopolitical situation and its impact on the UK economy, the wider mortgage market and borrowers' affordability. In response, we are carefully managing the composition and growth of our loan book."
  • Shares +1.5% to 513.43p on the morning of release (closed 528.00p, +4.35%).

body:
• CFO Himanshu Raja to retire on 12 August 2026, stepping down after five years, following publication of the interim report.
  • Raja will remain an employee for a further 12 months to support an orderly transition.
  • Deputy CFO Richard Shaw appointed interim CFO upon Raja's departure.
  • Recruitment process for a permanent successor to begin now.
  • Shares up 0.1% in London at 326.00p (+29% over 12 months); Johannesburg listing up 1.2% at ZAR74.12 (+18% over 12 months).

body:
• Net premiums earned down 4.9% YoY to $221.4m.
  • Net premiums written down 3.2% to $239.3m: commercial lines +2.2% to $164.1m, personal lines -13.1% to $75.2m.
  • Within commercial: auto +7.5%, workers' comp -6.5%, multi-peril -0.3%, other +8.6%.
  • Within personal: auto -16.8%, homeowners -6.4%.
  • Combined ratio deteriorated to 99.8% vs 91.6% prior year.
  • Net income down 54.3% to $11.5m; diluted Class A EPS $0.31 vs $0.71 (-56.3%).
  • Non-GAAP operating income $11.9m (-53.5%); Class A operating EPS $0.32 vs $0.72.
  • Annualised ROE 7.1% vs 17.8% (-10.7pts).
  • Book value per share $17.54 vs $17.33 at year-end 2025 (+8.0% YoY); reduced by $0.12/share from $4.1m after-tax AFS unrealised losses.
  • Loss ratio 64.1% vs 56.7%; core loss ratio improved to 53.4% vs 54.2%.
  • Weather losses spiked to $17.2m (7.8pts) vs $8.6m (3.7pts) — well above 5-year Q1 average of 4.5pts.
  • Large fire losses $12.2m (5.5pts) vs $7.7m (3.3pts), driven by higher frequency and severity (+$2.3m commercial property, +$2.2m homeowner).
  • Favourable prior-year reserve development $5.7m (2.6pts) vs $10.5m (4.5pts).
  • Expense ratio 35.4% vs 34.6%; systems modernisation allocation contributed ~1.6pts, expected to be 1.4pts for full-year 2026 and gradually subside.
  • Net investment income +19.2% to $14.3m on higher yields (avg yield 3.8% vs 3.6%) and higher invested assets.
  • Total investments $1.50bn; 95.3% in fixed maturities; duration 5.7 years.
  • Statutory commercial multi-peril combined ratio jumped to 113.9% vs 90.3%; workers' comp improved to 112.9% vs 117.6%; personal homeowners deteriorated to 94.6% vs 83.8%.
  • Quarterly dividends declared: $0.1925 Class A / $0.175 Class B, payable 15 May 2026.
  • CEO Kevin Burke on market backdrop: "a softening market has emerged as the availability of capital has led industry participants to reduce rates to win and retain accounts."
  • On personal lines trajectory: decline "will gradually taper over the course of 2026 as actions we have taken to slow the decline take effect."
  • On Q1 underwriting miss: "we primarily attribute the lower profitability to higher-than-average weather-related losses and the impact of several large current-year and prior-year losses."
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

body:
• On track to deliver FY2026 results in line with guidance.
  • Gross written premiums fell 6.1% to USD 668.4 million (Q1 2025: USD 712.1 million).
  • Excluding California wildfire reinstatement premiums, underlying GWP decline was just 1.2%.
  • Insurance revenue up 2.1% to USD 468.6 million (Q1 2025: USD 458.9 million).
  • Middle East conflict exposure described as "limited" and "well within our risk appetite."
  • CEO Alex Maloney: "Clearly, the period has seen very significant geopolitical volatility leading to wider economic uncertainty. Lancashire's exposure to the current events in the Middle East is limited and well within our risk appetite."
  • CEO Alex Maloney: "Lancashire has had a positive start to 2026, holding fast to our core principle of active cycle management. With the strong first quarter, we are on track to deliver results in line with our guidance for the year."
  • Shares fell 0.6% to 569.50p on the morning of release.

body:
• FY2026 guidance maintained: pretax profit expected in line with consensus of GBP462 million, up 3.7% vs GBP445.6m in 2025, "assuming market conditions don't materially deteriorate".
  • Completions guidance 12,000–12,500 homes in 2026 vs consensus 12,205 and 2025 actual of 11,905.
  • Net private sales rate per outlet per week 0.76 vs 0.74 YoY; ex-bulk 0.67 vs 0.65.
  • Forward sales GBP2.46bn, +5.1% vs GBP2.34bn; private forward sales GBP1.80bn, +7.1% vs GBP1.68bn.
  • Average selling prices increased (figure not quantified).
  • Cost inflation warning: early signs of supply chain cost inflation driven by higher energy costs, being mitigated via supplier relationships and vertical integration.
  • Middle East/Iran conflict has not yet materially impacted the business.
  • CEO Dean Finch: "Persimmon has started the year well, building on our strong performance in 2025, with an improved private sales rate and an increase in average selling prices".
  • Shares +2.5% to 1,053p midday vs FTSE 100 +1.0%.

time_period: CFO transition effective 12 August 2026 (post HY 2026 results for period ended 30 June 2026)
body:
• CFO Himanshu Raja will retire and step down as CFO and director on 12 August 2026, following publication of HY 2026 results (for the half-year ended 30 June 2026).
  • Raja will remain employed for the next 12 months and be available to support an orderly transition.
  • Deputy CFO Richard Shaw will become Interim CFO when Raja steps down.
  • Company commencing a formal search for a permanent CFO successor (internal and external candidates).
  • CEO Rob Wilkinson (quote):"Hammerson's FY25 results are a testament to the hard work and exceptional execution of the Hammerson team over the last five years. Himanshu has been an outstanding CFO over that period, leaving the Group well placed for the next phase of growth. I would like to personally thank him for his support and continued commitment to Hammerson's success."
  • CFO Himanshu Raja (quote):"When I look back at my time at Hammerson, what stands out for me is the amazing people and the phenomenal progress we have made together. I'm sincerely grateful to my colleagues, the Executive team and the Board for their support throughout my time here. As I retire, I leave with genuine confidence in the future of the business and its continued success."
  • Chair Robert Noel (quote):"Over the past five years Himanshu has been a valued member of the Hammerson Executive team and Board, supporting the Company's turnaround and transformation and helping to strengthen Hammerson's financial position. On behalf of the Board, I want to thank him for his contribution to Hammerson's success and wish him the very best for the future."
period_detail: CFO transition effective 12 August 2026 (post HY 2026 results for period ended 30 June 2026)

body:
• Gross premiums written (GPW) $668.4m, down $43.7m (-6.1%) YoY; excluding prior-year reinstatement premiums, underlying top-line decline was ~1.2%.
  • Group Renewal Price Index (RPI) 93% (reinsurance 92%, insurance 94%) — indicating continued rate pressure at renewals.
  • Segment GPW: Reinsurance $411.0m (down $71.3m / -14.8%); Insurance $257.4m (up $27.6m / +12.0%).
  • Insurance revenue $468.6m, up $9.7m / +2.1% YoY (reinsurance revenue -0.9%; insurance revenue +4.9%).
  • Loss environment “relatively benign”; exposure to Middle East conflict stated as limited and within risk appetite.
  • Total investment return 0.3% (incl unrealised gains/losses); portfolio duration 2.2 years (2.0 years prior), credit quality A+, book yield 4.6%; managed investments $3.22bn (vs $3.10bn).
  • Management actions: executed a planned reduction in inwards property retrocession in reinsurance; offset by insurance-line growth (notably energy and marine) and continued build-out of Lancashire US franchise.
  • Lloyd’s platform: after acquiring 100% underwriting capacity for Syndicate 2010 from 2026 YoA, intends (subject to Lloyd’s approval) to combine Syndicates 3010 and 2010 under Syndicate 3010 for 2027+ YoA.
  • Senior management transitions: Rachel Sabbarton and Jennifer Wilson succeeded John Spence and Hayley Johnston as CEOs of Lloyd’s and Bermuda platforms respectively.
  • CEO Alex Maloney (quote):“Lancashire has had a positive start to 2026, holding fast to our core principle of active cycle management.

With the strong first quarter, we are on track to deliver results in line with our guidance for the year.

As part of our proactive approach to managing the cycle, we enacted a planned reduction in inwards property retrocession in our reinsurance segment. This was offset by a 12% increase in gross premiums written across our insurance lines to $257.4 million, where we saw attractive opportunities for growth. Insurance revenue increased by just over 2% to $468.6 million, reflecting earnings from the growth delivered over the past few years.

As we move through 2026, we will continue to maximise opportunities where it makes sense, and we have the teams and talent across our Group to do this. The work we have done over recent years to grow our product portfolio and increase our geographic reach puts us in a strong position to continue to generate attractive risk-adjusted underwriting returns through the cycle.

Clearly, the period has seen very significant geopolitical volatility leading to wider economic uncertainty. Lancashire’s exposure to the current events in the Middle East is limited and well within our risk appetite.

The Group’s investment portfolio, including unrealised gains and losses, returned 0.3% over the period.

Since acquiring 100% of the underwriting capacity for Syndicate 2010, with effect from the 2026 year of account, we have been assessing the increased optionality that full alignment offers us. Subject to approval by Lloyd’s, we will combine Lancashire Syndicates 3010 and 2010, under Syndicate 3010, for the 2027 and subsequent years of account.

During the quarter, we also saw planned senior management transitions in our Lloyd’s and Bermuda platforms, with Rachel Sabbarton and Jennifer Wilson, respectively succeeding John Spence and Hayley Johnston as CEOs. Our thanks go to John and Hayley for their valued contributions to Lancashire’s success over many years.

For more than two decades, Lancashire has flexibly managed the (re)insurance cycle with a focus on disciplined risk selection, strong capital management, and adding value in our relationships with clients and brokers. We remain fully focused on keeping our momentum as we drive the business further forward, and we maintain our expectation of a stable top-line, and a high teens RoE for 2026.”
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Reported operating income $5.9bn, up 9% (record quarter).
  • Net interest income (adjusted) $2.9bn, up 1%; non-interest income (adjusted) $3.0bn, up 16%, driven by Wealth Solutions and Global Banking.
  • Wealth Solutions income up 32% (record quarter); Global Banking income up 19% (driven by origination volumes and capital markets activity).
  • Operating expenses $3.1bn, up 1% (growth investments partly offset by efficiency saves).
  • Credit impairment charge $296m, up $79m YoY (+36%), including $190m precautionary management overlays related to the Middle East conflict (partly offset by releases/recoveries).
  • Profit before tax $2.5bn, up 17% (record); profit attributable to ordinary shareholders $1.66bn, up 22%.
  • RoTE 17.4%, up 260bps; cost:income ratio 53.2% (from 56.6%, -340bps).
  • EPS 74.2c, up 31% YoY; tangible NAV per share $17.20, up 10% YoY.
  • Balance sheet: underlying loans and advances to customers +3% QoQ; underlying customer deposits +3% QoQ; LCR 151% (vs 147% YoY).
  • RWAs $266bn, up $8.2bn vs 31 Dec 2025 (credit risk +$5.3bn; market risk +$3.0bn).
  • CET1 ratio 13.4%: down 74bps vs 31 Dec 2025, driven by RWAs and the full 58bps impact of the $1.5bn share buyback (deducted from CET1 even though still ongoing).
  • Share buyback progress: by 31 March 2026, spent $471m to repurchase 22m shares (share count reduced by ~0.96%), but full $1.5bn deducted from CET1 in the quarter.
  • CEO Bill Winters (quote):"We delivered a record first quarter performance in 2026, with double digit growth in Wealth Solutions and Global Banking. Despite ongoing geopolitical tensions and global economic uncertainty, our advantaged market presence and disciplined risk management give us confidence in our ability to perform. We continue to support our clients as they manage their businesses and wealth across borders, and we look forward to setting out our next phase of growth at our Investor Event next month."
  • 2026 guidance unchanged: operating income growth expected around the bottom end of 5–7% range at constant currency; within that, NII expected to be broadly flat YoY at constant currency; costs to be broadly flat at constant currency (including final year of Fit for Growth charges); statutory RoTE >12%.

time_period: Q1 2026 trading statement; share buyback commencing 30 April 2026 (to complete by 6 July 2026)
body:
• Q1 underlying sales growth (USG) +3.8% (volume +2.9%, price +0.9%).
  • Turnover €12.6bn, down 3.3% YoY, with -7.7% FX headwind offsetting USG and net M&A (+1.4%) / disposals (-0.5%).
  • “Power Brands” drove outperformance: USG +5.0%, volume +4.0%.
  • By Business Group (USG): Beauty & Wellbeing +3.6%; Personal Care +3.7%; Home Care +6.1%; Foods +2.2%.
  • Regional mix: Developed Markets USG +1.0%; Emerging Markets USG +5.7% (volume +4.2%).
  • Full-year 2026 outlook reconfirmed: expects USG at the bottom end of 4–6%, with ≥2% underlying volume growth; expects a modest improvement in underlying operating margin vs 20.0% in 2025.
  • CEO Fernando Fernandez (quote):"We have started the year well with volume-led growth driven by our Power Brands and a positive performance across all Business Groups. There is broad-based momentum across our emerging markets business, with a strong performance in India, and a good recovery in Latin America following the decisive actions we have taken in that region.

We continue to move at speed to build a simpler, sharper Unilever with a structurally higher growth profile and a brand portfolio fit for the future. In March, we announced an agreement to combine Unilever's Food business with McCormick, unlocking value by shaping Unilever into a leading pureplay HPC company and creating a global flavour powerhouse in Foods.

Despite heightened macroeconomic uncertainty, the progress we are making to elevate our brands through Desire at Scale and improve operational execution means we remain confident of delivering on our guidance for the year ahead."

  • Foods transaction: agreement to combine Unilever’s Foods business with McCormick; targeted synergies include ~$600m annual run-rate cost synergies (net of reinvestment) plus $100m incremental cost/revenue synergies (to be reinvested); expects €400–500m stranded costs post-separation (to be offset with savings over 2027–2029) and €500m one-off restructuring costs over that period; completion targeted by mid-2027 at the latest (subject to approvals).
  • Quarterly dividend for Q1 2026 declared at €0.4664 per PLC share (or £0.4046), up 3.0% vs Q1 2025.
  • €1.5bn share buyback commences today (30 April 2026); expected to complete on or before 6 July 2026; Morgan Stanley acting under non-discretionary instructions and will trade independently within authority limits.

time_period: Board succession effective from end of AGM on 5 May 2026
body:
• Chairman John Kelly will step down as Chair with effect from the end of the AGM (5 May 2026), as part of the Board’s succession plan.
  • July Ndlovu (currently an independent NED) has offered to become the new Chairman; appointment is subject to Board election immediately following the AGM under the annual Chair election process (to be announced alongside AGM results).
  • Subject to being re-elected as a director at the AGM, Kelly will remain on the Board as a non-executive director to provide continuity.
  • John Kelly (quote):"It has been a privilege to serve as Chairman over the past three years, and to have supported the Company through a period of significant transition and change. Caledonia is well positioned for the future, underpinned by the strong performance of Blanket Mine and the progress being made towards Caledonia's next phase of growth. I have no doubt that July as Chairman will enhance the prospects for our continued success. I look forward to continuing to support the Board and management as a non-executive director and to working closely with the new Chairman."
  • July Ndlovu (quote):"I am honoured to be considered for the role of Chairman at an important point in the Company's development. The business has strong operating foundations and a compelling growth opportunity in the Bilboes project, which has the potential to take Caledonia into its next stage of growth.

"I would also like to thank John, on behalf of the Board, for his leadership and guidance as Chairman. I am very pleased that he will continue to serve as a non-executive director, and I look forward to working closely with him, the Board and management team to help guide the Company through this next phase and to deliver long-term value for shareholders."

  • CEO Mark Learmonth (quote):"On behalf of the Board and management team, I would like to thank John for his contribution and counsel over many years, and particularly for his stewardship as Chairman over the past three years during a period of change for the Company. His guidance and focus on governance and strategic progress have been invaluable, and I am glad that he intends to remain on the Board. I look forward to working closely with July once he is formally elected by the Board as Chairman as we continue to advance Caledonia's strategy, including the development of the Bilboes project."
period_detail: Board succession effective from end of AGM on 5 May 2026
type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'chairman' with change verb 'step down' in opening bullets

body:
• “Persimmon has started the year well… with an improved private sales rate and an increase in average selling prices. As a result, our private forward sales are up 7% on the prior year,” said CEO Dean Finch.
  • Net private sales per outlet per week 0.76 (+3% vs 0.74); excluding bulk sales 0.67 (+3% vs 0.65).
  • Current forward sales £2.46bn (+5% vs £2.34bn); private forward sales £1.80bn (+7% vs £1.68bn).
  • Private average selling price ~£306,900 (+5% YoY); incentives running at ~4–5% on average.
  • Average outlets 273 (+2%); land holdings ~84,900 plots (+1%).
  • Planning: 3,080 plots achieved detailed/reserved matters approval in Q1 (vs 2,781; +~11%), supporting ambition to operate from 300+ outlets.
  • Macro: says Iran conflict/geopolitical uncertainty has had no material impact on trading to date, but flags early signs of increased inflationary pressure and is monitoring potential impacts on confidence/affordability.
  • Since early March, some increases in mortgage rates; enquiries softened slightly in recent weeks, but sales “resilient”; institutional demand (affordable + BTR) “positive”, with 19 new partners added over last 12 months.
  • Supply chain: early signs of H2 2026 / 2027 inflation pressure driven by higher energy costs; mitigation via supplier relationships and vertical integration; additional internal cost reviews underway.
  • Outlook: assuming conditions don’t materially deteriorate, expects PBT in line with consensus and 12,000–12,500 completions in 2026; notes >50% of private and almost all housing association 2026 completions already secured.
type_workings: Rule 'trading update': 'trading statement' named in period line

time_period: Q1 2026 (three months to 31 March 2026; trading update)
body:
• Net loan book £26.154bn at 31 Mar 2026 (+0.9% / +£233m vs 31 Dec 2025).
  • Originations £1.2bn (+11% YoY vs Q1 2025 £1.1bn), with growth in Buy-to-Let and Residential and continued growth in higher-yielding sub-segments.
  • Retail deposits £24.698bn (+1.8% / +£447m vs 31 Dec 2025); repaid £350m of ILTR drawings (outstanding £1.15bn vs £1.5bn at 31 Dec 2025).
  • Total assets £30.975bn (-0.5% vs 31 Dec 2025) as liquidity portfolio optimised.
  • 3+ months arrears 1.7% (flat QoQ); IFRS 9 ECL modelled provisions increased marginally (updated macro scenarios).
  • CET1 ratio 15.1% (down 70bps vs 15.8% at 31 Dec 2025), stated as unaudited incl. Q1 profits net of foreseeable dividend/other charges.
  • RWAs +0.6% (vs net loan book +0.9%), reflecting mix shift toward Buy-to-Let.
  • Share buyback: £100m programme announced March; £30.2m repurchased as at 28 April 2026; due to complete no later than 6 March 2027.
  • CEO Andy Golding: “The Group delivered a resilient financial performance in the first quarter of 2026 and we continue to operate broadly in line with our 2026 guidance… We were agile in repricing products, protecting margins and returns… our 2026 NIM guidance is partially dependent on the cost of retail funding normalising throughout the rest of the year… we are mindful of the ongoing uncertain geopolitical situation…”
  • Reiterated 2026 guidance: loan book growth broadly similar to 2025; NIM ~225bps; admin expenses c.£280m; RoTE low teens; DPS +5% vs 2025.

body:
• Management expectations for FY2026 unchanged.
  • Revenue delivered “good growth”, supported by breadth of products/brands.
  • Insure performed well, driven by continued easing of headwinds in car insurance switching.
  • Money: “solid performance” with borrowing growth fuelled by loans; banking supported by increased availability of attractive current account promotions.
  • Home Services: “encouraging growth” driven by increased broadband conversion and promotional energy deals ahead of the April price cap.
  • Cashback remains impacted by UK corporate marketing budget pressure and consumer/economic uncertainty.
  • SuperSaveClub membership now almost 2.4m; described as loyal/engaged with higher customer lifetime value.
  • Launched MoneySuperMarket ChatGPT app in February (first price comparison site to do so); launched v2 yesterday with expanded functionality in the ChatGPT environment.
  • AI “Price Optimiser” has helped 100,000+ customers save an average £25 since late-February launch.
  • Share buyback: up to £25m launched 23 Feb 2026; £8m+ repurchased to date.
  • Board confidence: expects Adjusted EBITDA in line with published consensus range, with results weighted to H2 as previously indicated.

body:
• Announced strategic reorganisation into a simplified three-business operating model: Checkout Solutions & PayPal, Consumer Financial Services & Venmo, and Payment Services & Crypto.
  • Leadership appointments: Frank Keller (President, Checkout Solutions & PayPal); Alexis Sowa (interim lead, Consumer Financial Services & Venmo); Jeff Pomeroy (interim lead, Payment Services & Crypto); Antonio Lucio (Chief Marketing & Corporate Affairs Officer); Anshu Bhardwaj (Chief AI Transformation & Simplification Officer).
  • “To accelerate growth and unlock our full potential, we need to recommit to our fundamentals—getting much closer to the consumer, aligning the company around three strong businesses, simplifying how we work, sharpening accountability, and prioritizing operational excellence,” said Enrique Lores, President and CEO, PayPal. “By aligning our structure with our strategy in this simplified approach, we will be better equipped to drive sustainable growth and value creation for PayPal, our customers, and our shareholders.”
  • Business scope detail: Checkout unit combines consumer + merchant ecosystems; Consumer Financial Services aims to expand Venmo into a broader consumer financial services platform; Payment Services & Crypto unifies processing/platform capabilities incl. Braintree and PYUSD.
  • Exec departures: Diego Scotti (EVP/GM Consumer Group) and Michelle Gill (EVP/GM Small Business & Financial Services Group).
  • Company to provide additional details on the new operating model during its earnings call on 5 May.

body:
• “eBay’s first quarter results marked a strong start to the year,” said Jamie Iannone, CEO. “We accelerated GMV growth and delivered performance ahead of expectations. Our Focus Categories, C2C and recommerce strategic priorities are driving broad-based momentum, and strengthening our position as the marketplace of choice for enthusiasts.”
  • “Our strong first quarter results, characterized by robust GMV and revenue growth, along with healthy earnings, reflect the powerful operating leverage inherent in our business,” said Peggy Alford, CFO. “We remain committed to disciplined execution of our strategic priorities while continuing to allocate capital thoughtfully to drive long-term value and significant returns for our shareholders.”
  • Definitive agreement to acquire Depop (from Etsy) to deepen C2C fashion presence with younger consumers; closing now expected by end of Q3 2026 (subject to approvals); regulatory clearances received in US and Germany, with reviews ongoing incl. UK and Australia.
  • Revenue $3.089bn (+19% YoY; +17% FX-neutral); GMV $22.2bn (+18% YoY; +14% FX-neutral).
  • GAAP net income (continuing ops) $512m (+2% YoY); GAAP EPS $1.12 (+7% YoY).
  • Non-GAAP net income (continuing ops) $760m (+16% YoY); non-GAAP EPS $1.66 (+21% YoY).
  • GAAP operating margin 19.8% (down from 23.6%); non-GAAP operating margin 29.4% (down from 29.6%).
  • Advertising revenue $581m (2.6% of GMV); first-party ads $555m (+33% YoY as reported; +28% FX-neutral).
  • Operating cash flow $970m; free cash flow $898m.
  • Capital returns $639m (buybacks $500m; dividends $139m); remaining repurchase authorisation ~$2.3bn (as of 31 March 2026).
  • Q2 2026 guide: revenue $2.97–$3.03bn (FX-neutral +8% to +10% YoY); GMV $21.3–$21.7bn (FX-neutral +8% to +10% YoY); GAAP EPS $1.09–$1.14; non-GAAP EPS $1.46–$1.51.
  • Declared dividend $0.31/share, payable 12 June 2026 (record date 29 May 2026).

body:
• Net sales $181.5bn (+17% YoY; +15% YoY ex FX).
  • Segment sales: North America $104.1bn (+12% YoY); International $39.8bn (+19% YoY, +11% ex FX); AWS $37.6bn (+28% YoY).
  • Operating income $23.9bn (vs $18.4bn): North America $8.3bn; International $1.4bn; AWS $14.2bn.
  • Net income $30.3bn (vs $17.1bn); diluted EPS $2.78 (vs $1.59).
  • Q1 2026 net income includes $16.8bn pre-tax gains in non-operating income from investments in Anthropic.
  • TTM operating cash flow $148.5bn (+30%); TTM free cash flow $1.2bn (down from $25.9bn) driven primarily by +$59.3bn YoY increase in purchases of property & equipment (net), “primarily” reflecting AI investments.
  • Q2 2026 guidance: net sales $194.0–$199.0bn (+16% to +19% YoY); operating income $20.0–$24.0bn (vs $19.2bn). Assumes Prime Day in Q2 2026.
  • “We’re making customers’ lives easier and better every day across all our businesses, and their response is driving significant growth,” said Andy Jassy, President and CEO, Amazon. “AWS is growing 28% (our fastest growth in 15 quarters) on a very large base, our chips business topped a $20 billion revenue run rate (growing triple digits year-over-year), Advertising grew to over $70 billion in TTM revenue, and unit growth in our Stores reached 15% (the highest since the tail end of covid lockdowns). We also hit exciting milestones with delivery speed (more than 1 billion items same-day or overnight in 2026 and counting), Project Hail Mary (nearly $615 million at the box office to date and the second most successful non-sequel, non-franchise opening of recent memory), and Amazon Leo continues to resonate with prospective customers, with Delta Airlines the latest to sign on. We’re in the middle of some of the biggest inflections of our lifetime, we’re well positioned to lead, and I’m very optimistic about what’s ahead for our customers and Amazon.”

body:
• “We had a milestone quarter with strong momentum across our apps and the release of our first model from Meta Superintelligence Labs,” said Mark Zuckerberg, Meta founder and CEO. “We’re on track to deliver personal superintelligence to billions of people.”
  • Revenue $56.31bn (+33% YoY); +29% YoY on a constant-currency basis.
  • Costs & expenses $33.44bn (+35% YoY); operating income $22.87bn (+30% YoY); operating margin 41% (flat YoY).
  • Net income $26.77bn (+61% YoY); diluted EPS $10.44 (+62% YoY).
  • Reported tax line reflects an $8.03bn income tax benefit in Q1 2026 (effective tax rate -23%); excluding the benefit, Meta says ETR would have been 37pp higher and EPS $3.13 lower.
  • Family DAP averaged 3.56bn in March (+4% YoY); slight QoQ decline attributed to internet disruptions in Iran and WhatsApp restriction in Russia.
  • Ad impressions +19% YoY; average price per ad +12% YoY.
  • Capex (incl. principal payments on finance leases) $19.84bn; free cash flow $12.39bn; cash/cash equivalents/marketable securities $81.18bn (as of 31 March 2026).
  • Q2 2026 revenue guide $58–61bn (assumes FX is a ~2% tailwind to YoY growth).
  • FY2026 total expenses guide $162–169bn (unchanged); FY2026 capex guide raised to $125–145bn (from $115–135bn) citing higher component pricing and incremental data-center costs.
  • Tax rate expectation for remaining quarters of 2026: 13–16% (absent tax-law changes).
  • CFO flags ongoing legal/regulatory matters (EU/US), including youth-related scrutiny and additional US trials in 2026 that “may ultimately result in a material loss.”

time_period: AGM Statement and trading update to 31 March 2026 (plus noted April 2026 developments)
body:
• CEO Tufan Erginbilgic: "We have had a strong start to the year... Good progress on our transformation... gives us further confidence in our guidance of £4.0bn-£4.2bn underlying operating profit and £3.6bn-£3.8bn free cash flow for 2026... We expect to fully mitigate the current financial impact of the disruption to our business..."
  • 2026 guidance reiterated unchanged: £4.0bn–£4.2bn underlying operating profit; £3.6bn–£3.8bn free cash flow; expects to mitigate current Middle East conflict disruption impacts.
  • Civil Aerospace: large engine EFH +5% to 115% of 2019 in Q1; full-year 2026 still expected 115%–120% of 2019.
  • Middle East effects: Trent XWB EFH for Middle Eastern airlines “fully recovered to pre-conflict levels”; expects no change to large engine shop visit profile in 2026/27 due to conflict.
  • Operational KPIs: large engine OE deliveries +18% in Q1; large engine shop visits +12% in Q1.
  • AOG reduced to single-digit level end-April; targeting zero AOG by H2 2026.
  • Orders disclosed: 40 Trent XWB-97 engines for Atlas Worldwide (20 A350F); Delta Air Lines orders for 30 Trent XWB-84 EP and 32 Trent 7000 engines; April orders for Trent 1000 XE on 8 Boeing 787.
  • Time on wing: certified phase-2 HPT blade improvements for Trent 1000/7000 end-2025; installation underway in OE + shop visits; >1/3 of Trent 1000 TEN fleet upgraded to enhanced Trent 1000 XE durability standard.
  • MRO capacity: Turkish Technic broke ground (Jan) on independent maintenance centre in Istanbul, targeted operational end-2027, supporting up to 200 shop visits annually.
  • Defence: strong start driven by improved aftermarket and >20% YoY increase in OE deliveries; MQ-25 first flight in April powered by AE 3007; March EUROJET order for EJ200 engines for 20 Eurofighter Typhoons for Türkiye; April MT30 selected for up to 11 Australian Navy general-purpose frigates.
  • Power Systems: Q1 order intake (gas + diesel) ~50% higher YoY; March record month; backlog £7.3bn at 31 March; demand led by data centres and governmental.
  • SMR: April—Rolls-Royce SMR signed contract with GBE-N to supply UK with three SMRs at Anglesey, Wales; also signed commercial terms with ČEZ Group to progress Temelin site for first of up to six SMRs in Czech Republic; both entered execution phase and expected to generate revenues and profits this year.
  • Balance sheet / capital returns: Moody’s and Fitch upgraded ratings to A3 and A- (stable outlook); repaid €750m bond in February from free cash flow; progressing £2.5bn 2026 buyback tranche with >£750m completed to date.
  • Next results date: H1 2026 results on 30 July 2026.

time_period: Q1 2026 (AGM Statement and Q1 Trading Update)
body:
• Group delivered record System sales in Q1 2026, up 4% (FY 2025 comparative: +2%); all B2B divisions returned to growth.
  • Water & Waste Services: System sales +8% (FY 2025: 0%), driven by strong Metro Rod trading after pivot to higher quality/value work plus catch-up on work delayed from Q4 2025; Willow Pumps benefited from continued growth in Special Projects.
  • European Pirtek (8 markets): System sales +2% (FY 2025: +1%); improvement in March (Germany + Benelux) offset a weather-hit weak start.
  • Filta International North America: System sales +12% in local currency (FY 2025: +13%) and +5% in Sterling (FY 2025: +9%).
  • Filta launched FiltaClean Pro (kitchen ceiling cleaning) with “strong take-up” by franchise partners; positioned as opening new sector opportunities.
  • Used cooking oil (UCO) sales: +35% local currency (FY 2025: +24%) and +27% in Sterling (FY 2025: +20%), driven by +13% volume (FY 2025: +11%) and +20% price (FY 2025: +12%).
  • Trading + outlook: “well positioned” to deliver full-year performance within analysts’ forecast range; market expectations for FY ending 31 Dec 2026 Adjusted EBITDA £35.9m–£38.0m.
  • Executive Chair Stephen Hemsley: "I am pleased by the reacceleration of System sales... Filta International remains the standout performer... We are confident of significant future momentum... Pirtek's performance reflected resilient underlying trading despite the impact of the severe weather..."

body:
• Envolve (wholly owned subsidiary) acquired Edwards Diving Services Limited (EDS) for total consideration of up to £13m (cash and debt free).
  • Initial cash consideration £10m, funded from existing banking facilities; based on “sustainable EBITDA” of £1.3m (implied multiple ~7.7x).
  • Earn-out: additional up to £3m cash conditional on vendors remaining and meeting specified profit targets.
  • Strategic rationale: expands Envolve/Renew into specialist water services (marine/civil engineering for the water industry) and positions the Group for growth as momentum builds in AMP8.
  • Immediately earnings enhancing; Renew reiterates it retains a strong balance sheet and continues to assess further M&A pipeline.
  • CEO Paul Scott: "This acquisition expands our services into the exciting specialist water and environmental sectors... As we move into the second year of the AMP8 control period, we are increasingly well placed to capitalise on the significant level of investment..."

time_period: FY26 AGM Trading Update (order book position as at late April 2026; 8 weeks since FY25 results on 4 March 2026)
body:
• Closed + forward order book now c.3,850 homes with €1.5bn net sales value.
  • Order book increased by ~400 homes (+11.5%) in eight weeks vs FY25 results (4 March 2026: 3,452 homes).
  • Net sales value increased by €0.18bn (+13.6%) vs 4 March 2026 (€1.32bn).
  • Expects continued order book growth with further spring launches (primarily First Time Buyer) plus pipeline of apartments across mixed-tenure schemes for state partners/private buyers supported by Croí Cónaithe (Cities) Scheme.
  • Momentum + prior-year operational scaling expected to deliver a strong H1 outturn, underpinning full-year growth.
  • Build Cost Inflation YTD running at c.2.5%; geopolitical volatility noted but no expected material impact on full-year outlook “as things stand today”.
  • Passive House delivery milestone: first 600 apartments delivered to high standard to LDA and AHB partners; certification attended by founder of Passive House Institute for three large Dublin developments.
  • Reaffirmed FY26 guidance: Revenue c.€1.05–€1.08bn; Operating profit c.€180–€185m; ROE c.16.5%.
  • Medium-term delivery: positioned to deliver c.6,000 homes across this year and next, including c.3,200 homes in 2027.

time_period: FY ended 28 February 2026 (insurance settlement re: Sept 2025 JLR cyber-attack disruption; resolved early 2026)
body:
• Business interruption insurance claim settled relating to JLR cyber-attack (Sept 2025) that disrupted vehicle supply/parts/connected systems for JLR franchised retailers; business impact resolved by early 2026.
  • Total claim recovery £3.9m, less £0.5m deductible ⇒ net insurance recovery £3.4m.
  • £1.0m interim payment was flagged in the 5 March 2026 trading update; remaining £2.4m uplift now confirmed.
  • Full recovery will be recognised as underlying income in results for the year ended 28 February 2026.
  • FY26 adjusted PBT now expected to be ahead of market consensus £21.6m (consensus range £21.0m–£22.0m) due to the additional insurance recovery.
  • Preliminary results for the year ended 28 February 2026 scheduled for 13 May 2026.

time_period: New Five-Year Plan to FY31 (vs FY26 baseline; includes FY27 transition impacts)
body:
• CEO Dominic Paul: "For over 280 years, Whitbread's success has been driven by continually evolving to meet the needs of our guests. Today our business is built around a world-class brand in Premier Inn, which is synonymous with great quality and value, and has an unrivalled market position in the UK as well as a hugely exciting opportunity in Germany.

"We always challenge ourselves to improve and, in light of significant cost increases in the form of business rates and National Insurance, as well as the implied market discount to our inherent value, we've looked hard at the options open to us to maximise value creation over the medium and long-term. This has been a rigorous process and we've approached all options with an open mind.

"Our conclusion is that our model is the right one... we will refocus our capital spend and recycle more of our freehold real estate, driving increased margins and returns, reducing our capital intensity and increasing cash returns for shareholders...

"In the UK... convert all our remaining branded restaurants to an integrated food and beverage offer... unlock the addition of more highly profitable extension rooms...

"In Germany... now that we have reached profitability... focus on formats and locations which we know deliver the best return...

"This plan will transform Whitbread into a higher-margin, higher-returning pure-play hotel business..."

  • Plan headline outcomes by FY31 (vs FY26): £275m incremental adjusted PBT contribution, £1bn reduction in gross capex and >£1bn reduction in net capex, +500bps Group ROCE, and £2bn free cash flow available for shareholder returns.
  • Funding shift: growth capex to be funded through recycling £1.5bn of freehold real estate; move increasingly to leasehold growth; reduce freehold proportion from ~50% to 30–40% over time.
  • Net capex targeted at ~£200m–£250m per year, representing >£1bn reduction versus the previous five-year plan.
  • UK & Ireland growth plan: reallocate capital to highest-return opportunities while still growing open rooms to 96,000 by FY31 (retaining longer-term potential to reach up to 125,000 rooms thereafter).
  • Accelerating Growth Plan extended: replace all remaining 197 branded restaurants with integrated F&B; expected to enable more higher-return extension rooms and deliver 15%–20% returns on capital by FY31.
  • Restaurant disposals: agreed sale of 51 branded restaurants for £50m; agreed terms (subject to conditions) for sale of a further 60 sites.
  • FY27 transition impact: £40m reduction in adjusted PBT as remaining branded restaurants transition; after offsetting original AGP benefits, net £10m reduction to adjusted PBT in FY27 (subject to required employee consultation).
  • Cost & commercial programme: commercial initiatives to sustain UK outperformance; cost efficiencies target increased to £250m by FY31.
  • Germany: having reached profitability in FY26, growth focus shifts to accelerate FCF/ROIC; still targeting >50% room growth to 18,000 rooms by FY31 (with portfolio/pipeline optimisation proposed).
  • Shareholder returns: plan implies substantial FCF for dividends and buybacks, but share buy-backs paused in FY27 due to incremental AGP investment.
period_detail: New Five-Year Plan to FY31 (vs FY26 baseline; includes FY27 transition impacts)

body:
• CEO Gary Nagle: "Building on our operational delivery over the past two years, first quarter production was largely in line with our expectations, accounting for operational conditions and the Lady Loretta (zinc) and Mount Isa copper mines in Australia reaching their planned economic end of lives during 2025. Accordingly, full year 2026 production guidance remains unchanged from that presented at our Capital Markets Day in December 2025.

"While the Middle East conflict has created numerous dislocations, particularly around the supply of crude, refined products and sulphuric acid, our energy marketing business has supported the supply of fuels to our assets. In addition, our significant metallurgical asset footprint, across copper, zinc and nickel, puts Glencore in a net-long global sulphuric acid position. Although the impact of the conflict on our industrial business was limited in the first quarter, recent and emerging impacts are now manifesting, primarily as an increase in input costs, most notably diesel and acid consumption, and the generally weaker USD.

"Critically, basis the current stronger commodity prices (e.g. year-to-date copper +c.5%, zinc +c.7% and energy coal +c.22%), we expect these cost impacts to be more than offset, which would result in margin expansion. In addition, extrapolating our Q1 Marketing performance, would see this segment's full-year EBIT performance comfortably exceeding the top end of our long-term Adjusted EBIT guidance range of $2.3-3.5bn p.a."

  • FY26 production guidance unchanged vs Capital Markets Day (Dec 2025): Copper 810–870kt, Zinc 700–740kt, Nickel 70–80kt, Steelmaking coal 30–34mt, Energy coal 95–100mt.
  • Own-sourced production (Q1 2026 vs Q1 2025): Copper 199.6kt (+19%); Cobalt 5.8kt (-39%); Zinc 176.9kt (-17%); Lead 41.2kt (-17%); Nickel 17.2kt (-9%); Gold 68koz (-53%); Silver 4,869koz (+15%); Chrome ore 830kt (+3%); Steelmaking coal 6.5mt (-22%); Energy coal 22.9mt (-2%).
  • Copper +31.7kt YoY driven by improved grades at African copper (+27.4kt) and higher throughput/grades at Antamina (+13.5kt); partly offset by Mount Isa copper mine closure impact (-8.9kt).
  • Cobalt -3.7kt YoY due to DRC export quota system introduced late 2025: DRC assets prioritising copper as existing cobalt inventories sufficient for near-term quotas.
  • Zinc -36.7kt YoY mainly from Lady Loretta end of mine life (-22.3kt) and lower Kazzinc contribution (-13.8kt) due to own-sourced feedstock sequencing.
  • Nickel -1.6kt YoY due to 2025 Sudbury furnace disruption impacting matte shipment lead times to Norway.
  • Steelmaking coal -1.8mt YoY due to pit sequencing at EVR, wet weather (Queensland) and planned longwall move at Oaky Creek.
  • H2-weighted guidance drivers: Collahuasi (ore + desalinated water improving), steelmaking coal (Canada pit sequencing + Oaky Creek longwall move), energy coal (Ulan longwall move in H1; lower strip ratios at Bulga/Cerrejón in H2).
  • DRC cobalt update: quota system applies until at least end-2027; excess production stored in-country and sold “as circumstances allow”; may hold cobalt in solution/not reported as production to minimise processing costs.
  • DRC govt extended validity of 2025 quotas to April 2026; Glencore exported most of its 2025 quota in Q1, remainder exported in April 2026; unused Q1 2026 quotas valid until 30 June 2026.
  • Expected cobalt export quota allocation (kt): 2026 (incl. 2025 carryover) 22.8kt (KCC 16.1; Mutanda 6.7) vs 2027 18.8kt (KCC 13.3; Mutanda 5.5).
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

time_period: AGM update (Wednesday) covering 2025 actions and 2026–2027 merger/disposals timeline
body:
• At AGM, Chair Stuart Chambers described 2025 as “highly consequential” in the context of merger/disposal plans.
  • Merger plan: combine with Teck Resources to form “Anglo Teck”, positioned as “one of the world’s largest copper producers”; completion targeted later in 2026 or early 2027.
  • Governance: new board to be announced; Sheila Murray named as chair of Anglo Teck; Anglo and Teck each to nominate 50% of the non-executive board.
  • Chambers: “Despite the volatility in the world around us, we made great strides in 2025 to ensure that Anglo American is a more agile and resilient business...”
  • Strategic focus reiterated: copper, premium iron ore, and crop nutrients, aligned to “structural trends of decarbonisation, improving living standards, and food security”.
  • Portfolio progress cited: sale of Anglo American Platinum (now Valterra Platinum).
  • Disposals still in progress: steelmaking coal, nickel, and De Beers diamond operations.
  • CEO Duncan Wanblad on failed Peabody coal deal: “While we were very disappointed that Peabody decided not to complete... we expect that we will successfully reach an alternative sales agreement for value in 2026.”
  • Safety: Wanblad called the deaths of two employees in Brazil and Zimbabwe last year “simply unacceptable” and reiterated safety priority.
  • Chambers: “In a year characterised by volatile markets and slow economic recovery in China, and with weaker iron ore prices and cyclically low diamond prices, Anglo American delivered a stable operating and financial performance.”

time_period: Three months to March 2026 (Q1 trading update)
body:
• Confirmed full-year guidance: expects adjusted Ebita €435m at constant currency, or €400m after FX headwinds.
  • Q1 adjusted EBITDA +15% YoY (+46% at constant currency).
  • Q1 reported EBIT +25% YoY (+84% at constant currency).
  • Attributed improvement primarily to “sustained cost discipline and the continued benefits of management-led self-help measures,” implemented across 2025–2026.
  • End-market demand: refractory demand weakened in steel markets in Europe, Middle East and Latin America; other regions at expected levels; steel sales volumes broadly in line YoY.
  • Industrial demand: refractory demand decreased slightly in cement and industrial projects.
  • Middle East conflict: no material impact on the quarter overall, but depressed March sales volumes in the region.

body:
• New business profit +10% YoY to $686m (from $625m) at constant exchange rates; new business profit margin 38% vs 36% (+2pp).
  • APE sales +5.2% YoY to $1.82bn (from $1.73bn) at CER.
  • CEO Anil Wadhwani: performance was “broad-based” with higher APE and improved margins, reflecting “our disciplined execution and continued focus on driving high-quality growth.”
  • Bancassurance: reported strong YoY growth in both volumes and margins, with continued traction in key markets.
  • Outlook: “We remain confident in delivering double-digit growth across our key financial metrics in 2026 and achieving our 2027 financial objectives,” (Wadhwani).
  • Regional notes: Hong Kong saw new business profit growth and margin expansion; mainland China JV (CITIC Prudential Life) sustained strong APE momentum from H2 2025; Malaysia saw new business profit growth driven by agency with higher bancassurance margins despite lower volumes; Indonesia modest new business profit growth; Singapore good APE growth.
  • Risk note: some smaller Asian businesses are more exposed to risk of higher inflation from higher energy prices, which “could impact consumer sentiment and buying behaviours in the future.”

• Q1 organic rental growth from rent reviews added £3m of income, representing +3.4% annualised growth (vs +3.2% comparative annualised rate).
  • Annualised contracted rent roll £345m at end-March, up from £342m (+0.9% YoY).
  • Said it continues to see an improving rental growth outlook, especially from rent reviews.
  • Reported “considerable progress” on creating a new vehicle for its private hospital portfolio, targeting a transaction to reduce gearing and provide an alternative capital/growth route; expects to announce a transaction during summer 2026.
  • Transferring a further £103m of assets into the primary care JV is in due diligence and expected to complete before end-July.
  • Cost synergies: achieved £7.8m of £9m target so far; started a rationalisation programme to rightsize the enlarged group.
  • CEO Mark Davies: “We have continued to make excellent progress on the delivery of the post Assura combination objectives… reducing leverage back to our target range of 40% to 50%, net debt/EBITDA below 9.5x, delivering £9 million of annualised cost synergies and integrating the two businesses, all of which we expect to complete ahead of schedule.”
