time_period: Chair succession timeline (12 May 2026 to 22 July 2026 AGM)
body:
• Adam Crozier to join the Board as independent Non-Executive Director and Chair Designate from 12 May 2026.
  • Crozier to succeed Mike Rogers as Chair after the AGM on 22 July 2026; Rogers retiring as Director and Chair at the conclusion of that AGM (served 9 years on Board; 7 years as Chair).
  • Crozier committee roles: will serve on Remuneration and Nomination & Corporate Governance committees; to chair Nomination & Corporate Governance committee following the AGM.
  • Crozier profile: current Chair of BT Group plc (also chairs Nomination Committee; responsibility for security oversight) and Chair of Kantar Group; former FTSE 100 CEO; previously Chair of Whitbread and ASOS; former NED of Sony Group Corporation.
  • Alison Brittain (Senior Independent Director) (verbatim): “Following a comprehensive and rigorous search process, the Board is delighted to appoint Adam as Chair… Adam's strategic judgement, extensive Chair experience and measured approach position him well…”
  • Brian Cassin (CEO) (verbatim): “We are very pleased to welcome Adam to the Board… his insight will be valuable as we continue to execute our strategy and deliver long-term growth.”
  • Adam Crozier (verbatim): “I am delighted to be joining Experian at an important stage in its development… well positioned to capitalise on the opportunities ahead.”
  • Company confirmation: aside from the announcement, no further disclosures required under UK Listing Rule 6.4.8R (1)–(6) regarding Crozier’s appointment.

body:
• Portfolio company First Light Fusion Ltd announced a £25m successful first close of its latest funding round.
  • Round led by East X Ventures via its fusion fund Starmaker One, with a significant strategic investment from the UK Atomic Energy Authority (UKAEA).
  • Also supported by existing shareholders including IP Group and its Hostplus managed fund.
  • Post-completion holdings: IP Group has an undiluted 28.3% beneficial holding in First Light, plus 17.3% held via its managed funds.
  • Proceeds to accelerate commercial development of FLARE Fusion Energy concept — described as a “novel, reactor-compatible approach to Inertial Fusion Energy” aimed at a simpler, lower-cost pathway to fusion.
  • Greg Smith (IP Group CEO) (verbatim): “First Light Fusion continues to make strong progress as it advances a highly differentiated and commercially focused approach to inertial fusion energy. The participation of specialist fusion investors alongside the UK Atomic Energy Authority is a strong endorsement of both the company's technology and its strategic relevance to the UK's long-term energy ambitions… We continue to support the company as it accelerates development of its FLARE concept towards scalable, reactor-compatible fusion.”

body:
• Brazilian Federal Regional Court of the 6th Region monitoring report indicates continued progress under the Brazilian Agreement for reparations following the 2015 Samarco tailings dam collapse.
  • Report tracks actions by Samarco Mineracao SA and public authorities; Samarco is a JV between BHP Brasil and Vale SA.
  • 323,000+ approvals issued during May–Oct 2025, directing BRL 14.1bn (~US$2.85bn) in compensation payments and legal fees.
  • Progress across programmes: individual compensation schemes, income-support initiatives for affected workers, and payments to agricultural producers and traditional communities.
  • Resettlement: collective works in Bento Rodrigues and Paracatu de Baixo completed; additional support for families whose individual projects had not begun when the agreement was approved.
  • Governance actions: further contributions to the Rio Doce fund; launch of a unified public portal to centralise remediation information.
  • Long-term investments: agreement provides for health and basic sanitation investments; new funding directed to hospital and reference-centre projects.
  • BHP statement (verbatim): “The company actively participates in the process and supports Samarco in fulfilling the obligations established by the Brazilian Agreement, prioritizing transparency and dialogue with communities and authorities. This stance aligns with the company's vision of contributing to responsible mining and solutions that promote sustainable development,” BHP said.

  • Alumina output 2.0Mt (+6.1% YoY); aluminium production 840kt (+0.7% YoY).
  • Consolidated copper production 228.6kt (+8.9% YoY).
  • CEO Simon Trott: “Operating excellence drove 9% YoY copper equivalent production growth… as the Oyu Tolgoi copper mine continues to ramp up as planned…”
  • Trott: “The unmatchable mix and scale of our portfolio has ensured growth and supply chain resilience… as we continue to closely monitor the evolving situation in the Middle East.”
  • Exploration & evaluation spend $180m (vs $141m a year earlier; ~+28%), driven by Resolution Copper (Arizona) and higher copper product group activity; copper “just over half” of total spend.
  • Productivity: initiatives delivering $650m in annualised productivity gains; further throughput/cost-efficiency measures planned.
  • Guidance reaffirmed: Pilbara iron sales 323–338Mt; copper 800–870kt; bauxite 58–61Mt; alumina 7.6–7.8Mt; aluminium 3.25–3.45Mt.

body:
• Record steel shipments of 3.6m tons.
  • Net sales $5.2bn; operating income $538m; net income $403m ($2.78/diluted share).
  • Net income up ~52% QoQ (vs $266m in Q4 2025) and ~86% YoY (vs $217m in Q1 2025).
  • Adjusted EBITDA $700m; operating cash flow $148m after $120m annual retirement profit-sharing distribution.
  • Cash dividend increased 6% (Q1 2026).
  • CEO Mark D. Millett: “The teams executed well… with operating income increasing $228 million, or 73 percent… driven by record steel shipments combined with higher steel prices.”
  • CEO: “Underlying steel demand strengthened… customer orders rebounded and backlogs increased… value-added flat rolled steel margins expanded… We are seeing an improved steel market environment…”
  • Steel ops: operating income $557m, +73% vs Q4; average external selling price $1,193/ton (+$86 sequentially); ferrous scrap cost $396/ton melted (+$22).
  • Metals recycling: operating income $47m, +155% vs Q4; shipments “marginally lower” due to winter weather impacting scrap flows.
  • Steel fabrication: operating income $90m (steady QoQ); backlog >38% higher YoY, extending “through the third quarter and into October 2026.”
  • Aluminum ops: operating loss $65m ($17m worse QoQ); finished shipments 22,500 mt vs 14,600 mt in Q4 (~+54%). Startup issues led to a temporary pause and inventory write-offs; company expects shipments and earnings to increase sharply in Q2 2026.
  • Capital allocation/liquidity: capex $138m; dividends paid $72m; share repurchases $115m; liquidity $2.0bn at 31 March 2026.
  • Outlook quote: “We remain constructive that market conditions are in place for domestic steel and aluminum consumption to be strong through 2026…”
  • Aluminum ramp milestones: third cold mill expected to commission Q3 2026; second CASH line expected to commission Q3 2026; first CASH line now operational and producing qualification material for automotive customers.

body:
• Will Wilkins appointed Group Chief Financial Officer and Executive Director, expected to join on 1 August 2026.
  • Will joins from Mpac Group plc, where he has been CFO since 2018; previously held senior finance roles at BSH Home Appliances and began his career at Grant Thornton.
  • No other external appointments disclosed for Will Wilkins.
  • Appointment followed a formal CFO search led by the Nomination Committee, supported by external advisers.
  • Simon Bedford to remain Interim CFO until Wilkins starts (Bedford has been interim since October 2025).
  • CEO Joe Hudson quote: "I am delighted to welcome Will to Ibstock to support the continued delivery of our strategy… [He] will be a great complement to our Board and Executive team." Also: "I would also like to thank Simon for his important contribution as Interim CFO since his appointment in October 2025."
  • Will Wilkins quote: "My focus will be on maintaining strong financial discipline and ensuring the Group is well positioned for long-term growth and value creation."

time_period: Strategic update (demerger process targeted before end of calendar 2027)
body:
• Board decision: proceed with demerger of Primark (Retail) from Food business (“FoodCo”); ABF shareholders to receive shares in both listed entities.
  • Largest shareholder: review conducted with Wittington Investments; Wittington supports demerger and intends to maintain majority ownership of both Primark and FoodCo.
  • Separation economics: expected ongoing dis-synergies <£45m; one-off separation/transaction costs ~£75m.
  • Listing intention: both Primark and FoodCo to list on LSE Equity Shares (Commercial Companies) category; expected both to be FTSE 100 constituents (scale-dependent).
  • Post-demerger naming: FoodCo will retain the Associated British Foods plc name.
  • Target timeline/process: intention for demerger to be effective before end-2027; subject to approvals and tax clearances; expected to be effected via dividend demerger; investor sessions planned for both entities ahead of completion.
  • Governance/management intentions: Chair Michael McLintock asked to remain Chair until completion; intended CEOs: George Weston (FoodCo) and Eoin Tonge (Primark).
  • Regulatory: expected to be a Significant Transaction under UK Listing Rules; additional disclosures to follow once details finalised.
  • Chair Michael McLintock: “...concluded that a demerger of Primark is the best way to maximise long-term returns for shareholders…”
  • CEO George Weston: “...For our Food business… the only FTSE100 pure play food producer. For Primark… appropriate governance to maximise… Primark’s powerful brand…”

body:
• Transact net inflows £1.3bn (+8% YoY) (Q2 FY25: £1.2bn), equal to 6.9% of opening FUD annualised.
  • Gross inflows £3.1bn (+15% YoY) (Q2 FY25: £2.7bn); outflows £1.818bn (Q2 FY25: £1.483bn) → net flows £1.327bn.
  • Platform scale: average daily FUD £78.9bn (+18% YoY); quarter-end FUD £77.8bn (+18% YoY) despite volatility; Q2 market/other movements -£0.78bn.
  • Clients: platform clients 254,667 vs 241,197 (+13,470; +5.6% YoY).
  • H1 FY26: net flows £2.362bn (H1 FY25: £2.129bn; +10.9%); revenue guidance: H1 total Group revenue expected up 11% to ~£85.8m (H1 FY25: £77.2m).
  • April-to-date: average FUD increased with equity market recovery and continued net inflows; monitoring market impact from Middle East conflict; notes diversified client assets dampen index-volatility impact on FUD/revenue.
  • Cost guidance reiterated (from Dec 2025): underlying admin expense growth expected to slow to ~3% in each of FY26 and FY27; FY26 slowing weighted to H2.
  • CEO Alex Scott: “...net inflows… over £1.3bn, and a new record for quarterly gross inflows of over £3.1bn… AI can augment adviser capabilities… we expect to deliver strong, sustainable earnings growth.”

time_period: Trading update since AGM statement (25 March 2026); FY ending October 2026
body:
• Macro backdrop: conflict-driven uncertainty cited as increasing risk of “more prolonged higher interest rate environment”, renewed cost pressures and weaker consumer confidence.
  • Sales: open market reservations “in line” with improved levels since mid-January; strength in Midlands/South-West/Eastern; continued softness in South; no material increase in discounting/incentives/cancellations, but new enquiries and visitor levels down.
  • Volume guidance cut to 1,400–1,500 units (from 1,550–1,700), implying c. 11% cut at the midpoint (1,625 → 1,450); FY order book 1,106 units.
  • Land sales: sentiment “markedly” softened; land sale revenue now ~£40m (from £75m–£100m); expects no material profit on disposals for remainder of FY under revised assumptions.
  • Cash/working capital actions: prioritising cash and balance sheet; faster reduction of finished plots (notably completed apartments); tighter WIP controls.
  • Build costs: assumption of higher build costs in remainder of FY due to higher energy costs.
  • Earnings / leverage: now expects FY EBIT ~£5m–£15m; interest costs ~£15m; year-end net debt £100m–£120m.
  • Banking: seeking temporary covenant relaxation; lender discussions commenced; further update to follow.
  • Fire remediation: no material change to provision; on track to start 80% of affected sites by end-July; FY cash spend now £75m–£80m (slightly lower than planned) and pursuing recovery prospects.
  • CEO Martyn Clark: “...the right and prudent course of action is to adapt quickly… and focus on prioritising cash generation and optimising our balance sheet position.”

body:
• FY26 like-for-like net rental growth 6% (ahead of guidance), including 12% in campuses.
  • Expect FY26 Underlying EPS 28.9p (FY25: 28.5p) and Underlying Profit £294m (+5.4% YoY) (FY25: £279m) — EPS “ahead of guidance.”
  • Upgrading FY27 Underlying EPS guidance to at least 30.5p (from 30.2p) following Life Science REIT acquisition (completed 20 April 2026) and cited ~1% FY27 EPS accretion.
  • CEO Simon Carter: “This is expected to deliver Underlying EPS of 28.9p… and… drive an 8.1% total accounting return… we are confident in our earnings growth outlook for FY27 and beyond.”
  • FY26 portfolio: values +2.3% (campuses +2.0%, retail parks +3.3%); ERV growth +4.9% (campuses +6.5%, retail parks +4.4%); NEY down 4bps to 6.0%; EPRA NTA 590p (FY25: 567p).
  • Balance sheet: LTV 39.2% (FY25: 38.1%); net debt / EBITDA ~7.7x (FY25: 8.0x).
  • Leasing: 3.8m sq ft signed, 7.2% ahead of ERV and 7.6% ahead of previous passing rent; 1.1m sq ft under offer, 12.9% ahead of ERV.
  • Campuses: 1.7m sq ft leasing, 20.0% ahead of previous passing rent; occupancy 95% (ex. last-12-month completions); Q4 deals 834k sq ft (~50% of FY by area). Record campus rents referenced at Broadgate (1 Appold Street).
  • AI/innovation demand highlighted: signed Anthropic for 158k sq ft at One Triton Square; One Triton Square now 78% let and 16% under offer (including all lab space).
  • Retail parks: portfolio 99% occupancy; leasing 3.4% ahead of passing rents for FY (H2: 6.3%); like-for-like net rental growth in retail & London urban logistics 2%.
  • Outlook reiterated: 3–6% p.a. EPS growth medium term; ERV growth guidance 3–5% p.a. across portfolio.

body:
• CEO Simon Trott: “The tragic loss of two colleagues this year, at Simandou and Kennecott…”
  • Copper equivalent production +9% YoY across portfolio; copper production (consolidated) 229kt (+9% YoY); 2026 copper guidance unchanged (800–870kt).
  • Oyu Tolgoi ramp-up continues “as planned”; Oyu Tolgoi metal in concentrates +56% YoY to 102kt; ramp-up remains on track for ~500ktpa (100% basis, recoverable metal) from 2028 to 2036.
  • Kennecott refined copper 34kt (-20% YoY) (unplanned smelter maintenance → low anode inventory; geotechnical constraints); staged restart of Kennecott underground project commenced 16 April after March fatality.
  • Pilbara: production 78.8Mt (+13% YoY) (second-highest Q1 since 2018); Pilbara sales 72.4Mt (+2% YoY); cyclones impacted shipments by ~8Mt, with ~50% expected to be recovered (effects into early Q2).
  • Simandou: first full SimFer shipment delivered to China; first sales realised in April; Q1 crushed ore stockpile at mine gate 2.1Mt (100%); permanent crushing facilities still expected H2 2026; 30-month ramp to full production rates expected during H2 2028.
  • IOC (Canada): production -13% YoY (weather + mine/asset health challenges); sales -3% YoY.
  • Aluminium value chain resilient: bauxite 13.3Mt (-11% YoY) (Weipa/Gove weather + Cyclone Narelle), alumina +6% YoY (QAL now included at 100%), aluminium 0.84Mt (+1% YoY).
  • US tariffs: total RTA shipments to US destination 273kt (Q4: 303kt); total RTA tariff cost $362m (Q4: $416m); average realised tariff cost $1,327/t (Q4: $1,371/t).
  • Lithium: LCE production 12.7kt (YoY not directly comparable due to Arcadium acquisition timing; Mt Cattlin now in care & maintenance); Fenix 1B and Sal de Vida mechanically complete with first production on track for H2 2026.
  • 2026 production/sales and unit cost guidance unchanged, including Pilbara FOB unit cash costs $23.5–$25.0/wmt and copper C1 net unit costs 65–75c/lb.
  • Exploration & evaluation expense $180m (Q1 2025: $141m), including spend linked to Resolution land exchange.
  • Productivity: $650m of annualised benefits fully implemented by March; “substantially more underway”.
  • Resolution Copper: “historic land exchange” completed; drilling now underway; planning to invest ~$500m over two years (Rio share $275m) for enabling works/community funding/infrastructure/initial underground development and ~100 jobs.
  • Portfolio actions: actively “testing the market” for Borates business and evaluating options for Rio Tinto Iron & Titanium.
  • Capital projects: AP60 low-carbon smelter achieved first hot metal in March 2026; gross project costs up ~$122m to ~$1.5bn (construction productivity issues). Nemaska (Bécancour) construction pace slowed during 2026 (optimisation), while stating “no major changes” to overall timeline anticipated.
  • Middle East conflict: “direct impacts… limited” so far; higher input prices (incl. diesel) noted; “limited visibility” on supply-chain impacts in H2; Pilbara unit cost sensitivity from May: each $10/bbl oil move → ~$0.15/t impact (subject to FX/inflation).

body:
• Barclays downgraded Hunting to ‘underweight’ (from equal weight).
  • Barclays raised its price target to 600p (from 550p), a +9.1% increase.
  • Rationale: Hunting viewed as one of the “highest-rated stocks in our coverage universe” after Barclays cut its earnings outlook.
  • Forecast changes: Barclays cut 2026–2028 EBITDA forecasts by 3%–5%.
  • Valuation point: Barclays notes Hunting is around 16x 2026E P/E, making it among the highest-rated names in its coverage.
  • Performance noted: stock up 30%+ YTD, broadly in line with the European, Middle East & Africa Oil & Gas Index (per Barclays).
  • Barclays’ positives despite the downgrade: “Its Middle East exposure is relatively limited and it could be a beneficiary of a US rebound if that occurs. In addition, its Subsea business appears to be going well and the early signs of adoption for its Organic Oil Recovery product are encouraging.”
  • Valuation method detail: Barclays bases valuation on 15x 2027E P/E and notes this requires lifting the terminal margin in its reverse DCF to 2025–2026 levels versus 2023–2024 levels previously used.

body:
• CEO succession: CEO John-Paul Savant to stand down after a “short orderly handover period”; successor search at an advanced stage; further announcement “in due course”.
  • Chair Scott Forbes said the company is looking for a new CEO “with a strong commercial and operational track record as well as considerable marketplace, e-commerce and public company leadership experience”.
  • Chair said company confident of maintaining “good strategic and trading momentum” during transition.
  • Outlook reaffirmed for FY 2026: pro forma constant-currency revenue growth 4%–5%.
  • FY 2026 margin guidance reaffirmed: adjusted EBITDA margin 34.5%–35.5%.
  • Phasing: revenue growth expected to be more weighted to the first half.
  • H1 revenue expectation: expects to report USD125m revenue in H1; Q2 trading remains positive after “good momentum” in Q1.
  • H1 pro forma constant-currency revenue growth expected to be close to 8%.
  • H1 adjusted EBITDA said to be in line with expectations.

  • Reiterated FY 2026 expectations: adjusted EBITDA USD83.1m–USD85.6m on revenue USD239.8m–USD244.9m.
  • Implied growth vs FY 2025: FY 2026 revenue guidance implies +26.1% to +28.8% vs FY 2025 revenue of USD190.2m; EBITDA guidance implies +8.2% to +11.5% vs FY 2025 adjusted EBITDA of USD76.8m.
  • CEO quote: “We continue to focus on our strategy for extending our leading position in the curated second-hand goods market. ATG has delivered good trading and pro forma growth in H1 26, and I'm particularly pleased to report our progress on driving [arts & antiques gross merchandise value] which has been a focus area,”.
  • Share price move: shares down 4.7% to 373.4p; stock down 34% over the last 12 months.
  • Takeover context: in February, FitzWalter Capital decided against a firm offer after its 400p per share proposal was rejected by the board.

body:
• Blanket Mine gold production: 14,767 oz vs 18,671 oz in Q1 2025 (-20.9% YoY).
  • Lower quarter was anticipated due to mining sequence and expected access constraints to higher grade / higher volume areas.
  • Operational impacts in the quarter: equipment availability issues and challenging ground conditions (constraining access to planned ore sources).
  • New mine shift system being implemented to move production from 6 days/week to 7 days/week.
  • Contractor appointed to accelerate access to higher-grade ore sources.
  • Milling capacity expected to increase following commissioning of an additional ball mill in Q2 2026.
  • Management remains comfortable with full-year production guidance of 72,000–76,500 oz; production expected to be weighted to H2 2026.
  • Processing performance: 202,217 tonnes milled with “good operational availability across the processing circuit”.
  • CEO Mark Learmonth comment (full quote): “Gold production in Q1 2026 was below our expectations, reflecting the lower grades mined during the Quarter; however, this is consistent with our guidance of production being weighted towards the second half of the year. Pleasingly, plant performance remained strong, with 202,217 tonnes milled and good operational availability across the processing circuit. This is an important reflection of our continued investment into the future of the processing facility. "The Quarter also included several typical operating challenges associated with deep, mature mining assets, including sequencing considerations, equipment downtime and difficult ground conditions in certain areas. These factors temporarily constrained access to some planned ore sources. "We are actively addressing these challenges through several practical measures, including the acceleration of mine development, supported by the appointment of a contractor, to improve access to planned ore sources. "Importantly, we will be introducing a revised shift system later in the year, moving the operation to a seven-day working week (up from six), which we expect will both reduce worker fatigue and support increased ore production. "Alongside these mining initiatives, we are progressing further plant improvements, including an increase in milling capacity following the commissioning of an additional ball mill. Taken together, these actions are expected to support a stronger production profile in the second half of the year and into 2027. "The challenges experienced in the first quarter do not reflect the underlying quality of the orebody or the long-term fundamentals of the operation. Blanket remains a resilient, cash-generative asset, supported by a capable and committed workforce and a processing plant that continues to perform reliably and where we are growing capacity. Management's focus is on stabilising mining areas, improving equipment availability and restoring access to planned ore sources as we progress through the year.”
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

time_period: Announced 20 April 2026 (vessel sale expected later April 2026; funding runway guidance through end-Aug 2026)
body:
• Agreed sale of owned transhipping vessel “Iron Destiny” for US$7.7m net cash consideration (subject to delivery and customary closing conditions; expected to complete later this month).
  • Rationale for disposal: constrained ability to use vessel since start of the war; continued monthly maintenance and sustaining capex to keep seaworthy; uncertainty on timing of end of war; vessel approaching end of useful life; sale provides immediate cash injection and removes ongoing maintenance / future capex burden.
  • Net accessible cash runway: including expected proceeds from Iron Destiny sale, Ferrexpo estimates runway extended from at least end-June 2026 to approximately end-August 2026 (subject to iron ore price volatility, opex including energy costs, operating conditions, potential actions by the insolvency manager at FPM, and outcomes of ongoing proceedings).
  • Funding options: Board says an equity capital raise is currently the most viable solution within the required timeframe (after discussions with existing and certain potential new institutional investors).
  • Possible Fundraise: likely structured as a conditional placing of new shares to institutional investors to raise a minimum of US$100m to support working capital and short-term operational requirements while operating at a reduced level for the next 18 months.
  • Fundraise conditionality: would require shareholder resolutions at a general meeting to be convened “as early as possible” following launch.
  • Prospectus: company does not intend to publish a prospectus in connection with any Possible Fundraise.
  • Largest shareholder engagement: Fevamotinico S.a.r.l. (holding 49.32% of ordinary shares in issue, excluding treasury shares) is in discussions regarding supporting resolutions and potential participation, given dilution considerations; discussions ongoing.
  • Uncertainty statement: “there can be no certainty” the group will progress a fundraise or an alternative funding solution within a suitable timeframe, or at all.

time_period: Scheme effective 20 April 2026 (record time 17 April 2026; admission/cancellation 21 April 2026; settlement by 4 May 2026)
body:
• The Scheme of Arrangement to acquire Life Science REIT by British Land became Effective on 20 April 2026 following registration of the Court Order; British Land now owns the entire issued and to be issued share capital of Life Science REIT.
  • Life Science REIT intended to be re-registered as a private limited company shortly after the announcement date.
  • Scheme Record Time: 6:00 p.m. on 17 April 2026.
  • Consideration per Scheme Share (for holders on the register at the Scheme Record Time): 0.07 New British Land Shares + 14.1 pence cash.
  • New British Land Shares issuance: application for admission of 24,500,000 New British Land Shares; expected admission to trading 8:00 a.m. on 21 April 2026.
  • CREST settlement timing: New British Land Shares expected to be credited on or as soon as possible after 8:00 a.m. on 21 April 2026, but no later than 4 May 2026.
  • Share certificates (certificated holders): despatch by no later than 4 May 2026.
  • Cash/share consideration settlement deadline: no later than 4 May 2026.
  • Life Science REIT trading suspension: effective 7:30 a.m. on 20 April 2026.
  • Expected delisting/cancellation timing: cancellation of listing and admission to trading expected by 8:00 a.m. on 21 April 2026.
  • Scheme Shares: upon effectiveness, share certificates ceased to be valid documents of title; CREST entitlements are being cancelled.
  • End of offer period: Life Science REIT is no longer in an “offer period” under the Takeover Code; related dealing disclosure requirements no longer apply.
  • Life Science REIT board resignations (effective on Scheme becoming Effective): Claire Boyle, Sally Ann Forsyth, Richard Howell, Robert Naylor, Michael Taylor.
  • Life Science REIT board appointments (effective on Scheme becoming Effective): Gavin Bergin, Michael Wiseman, Paul Case, Rhiannon Owen.
period_detail: Scheme effective 20 April 2026 (record time 17 April 2026; admission/cancellation 21 April 2026; settlement by 4 May 2026)

body:
• FY 2026 revenue and EBITDA expected to be ahead of current market expectations (per Board statement).
  • CEO David Zruia comment (full quote): “The Group delivered an excellent performance in the quarter, with strong growth across key financial and operational metrics, underpinned by our proprietary technology and highly efficient, scalable customer acquisition capabilities. This was supported by robust activity across our global customer base of c.34 million registered customers, providing a strong foundation for continued growth, customer activation and monetisation. Customer Income reached a five-year record high in Q1 2026, driven by the continued execution of our strategic shift toward higher-value customers, as well as heightened market volatility. This was achieved alongside continued improvements in customer acquisition and lifetime value, driving higher quality revenue and earnings. We also made significant strategic progress in the US, including the launch of our B2C prediction markets products and the continued expansion of our B2B partnerships with leading market participants such as the CME Group and FanDuel, further reinforcing Plus500's status as a leading provider of global market infrastructure. Reflecting the strong momentum across the business and our growing, increasingly diversified global footprint, the Board expects FY 2026 revenue and EBITDA to be ahead of current market expectations.”
  • Customer Income: $270.6m (+53% YoY, +33% QoQ) to a five-year record high.
  • Revenue: $242.1m (+18% YoY, +24% QoQ).
  • Revenue mix: trading income $231.7m; interest income $10.4m.
  • New Customers: 39,867 (+48% YoY, +53% QoQ).
  • Active Customers: 157,703 (+21% YoY, +28% QoQ).
  • EBITDA: $95.7m (+2% YoY, +19% QoQ).
  • EBITDA margin: 40% (Q1 2026).
  • Customer Trading Performance: ($38.9m) in Q1 2026 (vs $15.4m Q1 2025; vs ($20.2m) Q4 2025); expected to remain broadly neutral over time.
  • Non-OTC business: ~15% of Group revenue and ~18% of New Customers (continued scaling / diversification).
  • US business: revenue of approximately $35m in Q1 2026 (up ~21% QoQ and ~45% YoY).
  • US B2C prediction markets: launched February 2026; next-generation offering expected during Q2 2026 (enhanced capabilities and expanded products).
  • US B2B expansion: continued growth via partnerships, explicitly citing CME Group and FanDuel; expects additional strategic partnerships in short-to-medium term.
  • India: acquisition of Mehta completed February 2026 (positioned as access to India retail futures market; supports global futures strategy).
  • AUAC: $1,196 (-1% YoY, -5% QoQ).
  • ARPU: $1,535 (-3% YoY, -3% QoQ), with ARPU impacted by higher weighting of new customers.
  • Customer acquisition spend: incremental investment of approximately $16m during the quarter (above prior-year quarterly run-rate).
  • Total customer deposits: $1.8bn (record quarterly level; vs $1.6bn Q1 2025; vs $1.7bn Q4 2025).
  • High deposit volumes drove approximately $4m additional payment processing costs in the quarter.
  • Balance sheet: debt-free; cash balances > $780m at 31 March 2026 (vs $801.6m at 31 December 2025).
type_workings: Rule 'period line': period line gives quarter 'Q1 2026'

time_period: Directorate change announced 20 April 2026 (handover period to follow)
body:
• CEO John-Paul Savant is standing down after more than ten years as CEO and will leave after a “short orderly handover period”.
  • The Board is at an advanced stage of appointing a successor; “A further announcement will be made in due course.”
  • Chair Scott Forbes comment (full quote): “On behalf of the Board, I would like to extend our appreciation to John-Paul for his tenure during which he architected the combination of brands serving curated secondary goods markets in the arts & antiques and industrial & commercial goods segments. He has built a capable team and ATG has a solid foundation of buyers and sellers and an increasingly robust platform for the development of uniquely efficient online marketplaces serving auction and list price buyers and sellers. The Board's CEO search process focused on identifying and securing a candidate with a strong commercial and operational track record as well as considerable marketplace, ecommerce and public company leadership experience. We are confident in being able to secure a strong replacement. In the meantime, we are confident that the Group has good strategic and trading momentum, which will be maintained in the transition period.”
  • John-Paul Savant comment (full quote): “It has been a pleasure to lead ATG for over the last ten years, taking the Group from revenue of c.$20 million to around $250 million in FY26 through building leading marketplace brands. The Group has a strong market position, a team with excellent foundational capabilities and a platform for further profitable growth. As a significant shareholder with a long term belief in ATG I will be an interested observer in its future success.”
period_detail: Directorate change announced 20 April 2026 (handover period to follow)
type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'ceo' with change verb 'handover' in opening bullets

body: • Pursuant to UK Listing Rule 6.4.9(2), Standard Chartered announced that Diane Jurgens (independent non-executive director) will be appointed to the board of Infosys Limited (listed on NSE India, BSE, and NYSE) as an independent non-executive director, effective 22 April 2026.

time_period: Community launch announcement (grand opening weekend 18 April 2026; incentives described as one-weekend-only).
body:
• LGI Homes announced the grand opening of Desert Edge in Grantsville (Tooele County), expanding further into the greater Salt Lake City market.
  • Desert Edge homes are described as upgraded, move-in-ready, with pricing starting from the low-$400s.
  • The community is planned to include 700 new homes.
  • Planned home configurations include 3-, 4-, and 5-bedroom floor plans.
  • Planned floor plan sizes range from approximately 1,328 to 2,662 sq. ft.
  • Quote (Nick Smith, VP of Sales – Salt Lake City): “We are proud to bring thoughtfully designed, high-quality homes to Grantsville that make homeownership more attainable for first-time buyers,” said Nick Smith, Vice President of Sales for Salt Lake City. “As the area continues to grow, we remain committed to delivering lasting value and creating a place that families are proud to call home. This community reflects both the opportunity and bright future of Grantsville.”
  • Each home includes LGI Homes’ CompleteHome™ interior package, positioned as including upgrades “at no additional cost.”
  • Kitchen specs listed: white wood cabinetry, quartz countertops, stainless steel undermount sink, and a full suite of Whirlpool® appliances.
  • Additional interior features listed: luxury vinyl plank flooring (main living areas), Moen® faucets, undermount bathroom sinks, programmable thermostats, and Wi-Fi-enabled garage door openers.
  • Floor plan: Cascade — 1,328 sq. ft., 3 bed / 2 bath; one-story; open-concept; includes a mudroom.
  • Floor plan: Millard — 1,676 sq. ft., 3 bed / 2 bath; single-story; includes kitchen island and a private master suite.
  • Floor plan: Delano — 1,850 sq. ft., 3 bed / 2.5 bath; two-story; “efficient kitchen layout,” “expansive upstairs living space,” and an open back deck.
  • Floor plan: Granite — 1,976 sq. ft., 4 bed / 2.5 bath; two-story; extended kitchen pantry; upstairs laundry room.
  • Floor plan: Provo — 2,445 sq. ft., 4 bed / 2.5 bath; two-story; downstairs master suite; mudroom; walk-in closets in every bedroom.
  • Floor plan: Redrock — 2,662 sq. ft., 5 bed / 2.5 bath; two-story; includes a flex room and an open back deck.
  • Community amenity plan centers on a community park intended to “bring residents together.”
  • Planned park amenities: children’s playground, basketball court, horseshoe pits, covered picnic area, play field, restrooms, walking trails, and shaded seating areas.
  • The release highlights “convenient access” to local parks, dining, and shopping throughout Tooele County.
  • Grand opening event timing: Saturday, 18 April 2026.
  • Promotion: homebuyers can access exclusive pricing and incentives described as “one-weekend-only.”
period_detail: Community launch announcement (grand opening weekend 18 April 2026; incentives described as one-weekend-only).
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

time_period: Analyst reaction to outlook for year to March 2027; referenced recent trading metrics and disposal programme progress
body:
• Workspace warned of a “substantial step down” in profit as it invests to become the “first-choice provider of space” for start-ups and small businesses.
  • Share price reaction described: shares slumped 9.5% to 337.99p; intraday low cited as 313.10p (down as much as 16%); shares also cited as down 22% over the past year.
  • JPMorgan: outlook "not in current market expectations"; focus is on factors driving a "substantial step down" in trading profit in FY2027 vs FY2026.
  • JPMorgan estimated profit headwinds of ~GBP10m–GBP15m from a combination of: lower starting rents, disposals, higher debt costs, reduced capitalised interest, and increased operating expenses.
  • JPMorgan implied FY2027 trading profit of ~GBP45m–GBP50m based on those headwinds.
  • JPMorgan said this implies an 18%–26% downgrade to consensus forecasts (per the article).
  • Dividend pressure flagged: move to 1.2x dividend cover suggests high single-digit % cuts to FY2026 dividend expectations (per JPMorgan).
  • JPMorgan: applying the 1.2x cover approach into FY2027 implies potential dividend reductions up to 32% (per the article).
  • JPMorgan quote: "This is a challenging update with earnings, dividends and NAV expectations now needing to move lower,".
  • Strategy support (JPMorgan): portfolio is "made up of quality buildings in good locations with long-term structural demand"; opportunity is repositioning so Workspace "owns the value category"; but "this repositioning will take some time" and the market will want progress before re-engaging.
  • Berenberg view: company is "laying the groundwork for a sustainable recovery", but expects near-term earnings to fall; expects trading profit towards the "high GBP40 millions" (around 17% below their previous estimates and ~22% below consensus, per the article).
  • Trading metrics cited by Berenberg: occupancy 81.6%; rent per square foot down 0.9%.
  • Peel Hunt: two consecutive quarters of occupancy improvement is "encouraging that this is not a structural problem", but expects a trading profit downgrade; models FY2027 trading profit in the mid- to high-GBP40m range (implying ~25% downgrade, per the article).
  • Peel Hunt dividend impact (per the article): forecasts ~10% reduction in FY2026 dividend and 25%–30% cuts for FY2027.
  • Panmure Liberum: rebasing is "the required medicine to put the business in a position to grow", but warned shares are vulnerable to near-term weakness.
  • Disposal programme: GBP125.7m completed/agreed so far out of GBP200m target (i.e., ~62.9% of the programme achieved); some disposals completed at “significant discounts” to prior valuations, highlighting softer pricing conditions.
period_detail: Analyst reaction to outlook for year to March 2027; referenced recent trading metrics and disposal programme progress
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

time_period: War-risk insurance capacity for Hormuz transits during the current Middle East conflict
body:
• Beazley will lead a “marine war consortium” to provide USD1 billion in additional war insurance coverage for vessels sailing through the Strait of Hormuz.
  • The extra capacity will be provided via Lloyd’s of London.
  • Beazley statement: "The consortium is designed to support the maritime sector with additional war insurance capacity as it deals with a complex and evolving situation in and around the Strait of Hormuz,".
  • Beazley statement: "The facility will be offered to vessels and their cargoes whilst transiting the Strait of Hormuz; coverage will be in line with Beazley's risk appetite and in compliance with all global sanctions".
  • CEO Adrian Cox: the arrangement will help "in keeping global trade moving".
  • Article context: war has driven up shipping insurance payments; about 30 vessels have reported being hit or targeted in the area (per the UK Maritime Trade Operations Centre cited).
  • Article context: Iranian forces have restricted passage through the strait to “all but a trickle” of ships since the war erupted on 28 February (as stated in the article).
  • Lloyd’s Market Association (trade body) position: "Safety concerns, not insurance availability, (are) driving reduced vessel traffic,".
  • Additional corporate context in the article: Beazley earlier this year accepted a GBP8.2 billion takeover approach from Zurich Insurance Group AG.
period_detail: War-risk insurance capacity for Hormuz transits during the current Middle East conflict
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

body:
• Company confirmed the start date for incoming CFO Caroline Browne.
  • Caroline Browne’s appointment will take effect from 26 May 2026.
  • This follows the prior announcement on 1 December 2025 confirming her appointment as CFO.
type_workings: Rule 'board change': senior role 'cfo' with change verb 'appointment' in opening bullets

time_period: Short-term jet fuel supply disruption at Italian airports; resolved within a day
body:
• CEO said Wizz Air experienced jet fuel shortages at three Italian airports: Venice, Brindisi and Catania.
  • CEO Jozsef Varadi: "We had problems because of a shortage of jet fuel in three Italian airports – Venice, Brindisi and Catania".
  • Varadi: "It was all resolved within a day and in some cases within hours, without having to cancel flights."
  • The issue followed warnings about jet fuel supplies linked to the blockage/effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz due to the war in Iran.
  • Article context: Europe normally imports about half of its jet fuel from Gulf countries.
  • Varadi: "In no other European country, so far, have we experienced a fuel shortage,".
  • Varadi: "I think we are currently watching each other (between airlines) to understand how serious the situation might become."
  • Varadi: "Jet fuel has been supplied adequately across Europe, with a minor hiccup in Italy,".
  • Varadi: "For the time being, I think the situation in Europe is not that alarming; the problems are more serious in Asia than in Europe,".
  • Varadi: "I believe we will overcome this crisis as well and that it will not be too damaging."
  • Mitigation approach if shortages recur: first ensure planes carry enough fuel for another journey; only then consider reducing services at the affected airport.
  • Demand commentary: Varadi said "Demand is hesitant" and "People are waiting to see what will happen" for the coming weeks.
period_detail: Short-term jet fuel supply disruption at Italian airports; resolved within a day
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

time_period: Takeover scheme sanctioned; share suspension scheduled for Monday (date not stated)
body:
• Court has sanctioned the scheme for the takeover of Life Science REIT by British Land Co PLC.
  • Life Science REIT shares are to be suspended on Monday (date not stated).
  • British Land agreed the acquisition in January for GBP150 million.
  • Consideration to Life Science REIT shareholders: 14.1p cash + 0.07 of a new British Land share for every Life Science REIT share held.
  • At announcement, the consideration implied 42.8p per Life Science REIT share, based on British Land shares closing at 410p the day before the deal was announced.
  • Using British Land’s “current” share price referenced in the article, the implied value is ~41.8p per Life Science REIT share, which is ~2.3% lower than the 42.8p reference value (42.8p → 41.8p).
period_detail: Takeover scheme sanctioned; share suspension scheduled for Monday (date not stated)
type_workings: Rule 'M&A': opening bullets lead with 'takeover of'

summary: UBS upgrades BP to buy, citing new CEO and potential for cost savings and deleveraging
body:
Time Period Analyst note (focus areas ahead of a likely strategy update later in 2026; deleveraging scenarios out to 2028)

  • UBS upgraded BP to ‘buy’ (from ‘neutral’); price target raised to 700p (from 650p).
  • UBS framing: new CEO Meg O’Neill “takes over at a critical turning point,” with “work to regain investor confidence” after “52% underperformance versus peers since 2018”.
  • Cost base: UBS estimates potential unproductive cost savings of USD3 billion–USD6 billion.
  • Growth: UBS wants clearer definition of key growth opportunities, noting importance of growing the business following buyback suspension.
  • Balance sheet: UBS highlights BP’s sector-high leverage ratio (~47%); expects deleveraging helped by higher oil prices, with leverage potentially falling to ~27% by 2028 in base case (earlier in upside case).
  • Distributions: UBS argues the equity case would be supported by clarifying distribution policy and conditions for reintroducing buybacks.
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

summary: Londonmetric reports record net rental income growth of 16% and raises dividend for 11th consecutive year
body:
Time Period Year ended 31 March 2026 (trading update ahead of full-year results due 21 May 2026)

  • Portfolio: GBP7.6 billion triple-net lease portfolio; continued strong occupier demand.
  • Net rental income +~16% to over GBP450 million (FY2025: GBP390.6 million).
  • Occupancy 98%.
  • Like-for-like income growth 4.2%.
  • Average lease length 17 years (vs 19 years in FY2025).
  • Dividend: expects annual dividend +3.8% to 12.45p (from 12.00p), 11th consecutive year of dividend growth.
  • Disposals: 57 assets sold for GBP318 million at blended net initial yield 5.7% (non-core disposals).
  • Direct investments: 35 investments totalling GBP333 million.
  • CEO Andrew Jones: “Despite macro uncertainty, volatile bond yields and reduced liquidity, we have continued to improve the quality of the portfolio with some excellent disposals and acquisitions… it is wonderfully comforting to see our rental income flowing and growing to record levels. This continued income compounding is enabling us to deliver our eleventh consecutive year of dividend progression.”
type_workings: Rule 'trading update': 'trading update' named in opening bullets

summary: Ashmore's AUM fell 3.4% to USD50.7bn amid Middle East tensions and outflows
body:
Time Period Quarter ended 31 March 2026 (Q3)

  • Assets under management: USD50.7 billion, down 3.4% from USD52.5 billion (end-December).
  • Net outflows: USD900 million in the quarter.
  • Negative investment performance: USD900 million in the quarter.
  • Backdrop: “Positive momentum from 2025… continued into the period but was interrupted by the broadening of the conflict in the Middle East at the end of February… [leading to] heightened global market volatility and a ‘wait and see’ approach…”
  • Flows by theme: net inflow in local currency and equities; net outflow from blended debt (institutional redemption) and small net outflow from external debt.
  • CEO Mark Coombs (markets): reaction “across most asset classes has so far been manageable and with limited price dislocations”.
  • CEO Mark Coombs (investor stance): “investors globally are taking a more measured stance until there is greater clarity… and its implications for… commodity prices, inflation, interest rates and currency trends,”
  • Japan Post Insurance partnership: intends to invest USD1 billion initially into Ashmore-managed EM funds (in addition to existing JPI assets with Ashmore).
  • JPI potential equity stake: envisaged up to 2.9% via open-market purchases.
  • CEO Mark Coombs (partnership): “This new strategic partnership builds on a strong existing relationship in order to capitalise on the growth opportunity across a broad range of attractive emerging market asset classes.”
type_workings: Rule 'fallback': no specific pattern matched; generic corporate announcement

summary: Dunelm Q3 sales growth slowed to 2.1% amid March consumer softening and value-seeking
body:
Time Period 13 weeks ended 28 March 2026 (Q3 FY26); year-to-date FY26

  • Q3 sales +2.1% to GBP472 million.
  • Year-to-date sales +3.1% to GBP1.40 billion.
  • Growth cadence: Q3 improved vs Q2 (+1.6%), but slowed vs Q1 (+6.2%) and vs prior-year Q3 (+6.3%).
  • Trading: “the third quarter started well… however more recently, and particularly in March, we experienced a period of broad-based softening,”
  • Gross margin +30bps YoY in Q3.
  • Mix/discounting: “customers seeking value, buying into more discounted products compared to full price lines.”
  • Middle East: “navigating the impact of instability in the Middle East”; expects only a “small direct cost impact” in FY26.
  • Outlook: “we are not assuming any immediate improvement to consumer confidence,” given global uncertainty.
  • FY26 pretax profit expected towards the lower end of consensus GBP210 million–GBP217 million (FY2025: GBP211.0 million, +2.7% YoY).
  • Digital/app: full launch of mobile app in Q3; downloads now above 300k; “encouraged by early performance on conversion and spend per transaction.”
  • Stores: “significantly stronger pipeline of new openings” expected next FY, including Kingston-upon-Thames store planned to open in summer.
type_workings: Rule 'results headline': opening bullets reference Q3 figures

summary: JD Sports sells entire Applied Nutrition stake for £48.9 million via secondary placing
body:
Time Period Transaction announced 16 April 2026 (stake held since Applied Nutrition IPO in October 2024)

  • Sold entire stake in Applied Nutrition via secondary placing (placing represented ~9.1% of Applied Nutrition share capital).
  • Sold ~22.8 million shares at 215p per share.
  • Total proceeds ~GBP48.9 million.
  • Bookrunner: Peel Hunt (sole bookrunner).
  • Note: this followed a prior indication the stake would be reduced, but the final outcome was a full exit.
type_workings: Rule 'placing': opening bullets say 'secondary placing' — existing shares sold, not new capital

summary: Rentokil Initial reports Q1 2026 revenue growth of 7.8% with solid North America momentum
body:
Time Period Q1 2026

  • Group revenue +7.8% to USD1.68 billion (from USD1.56 billion).
  • Group revenue +4.3% at constant currency.
  • Group organic growth 3.4% (slightly below one consensus read of 3.6%).
  • North America division revenue +4.6% to USD995 million (from USD951 million).
  • North America division organic growth 3.9% (above one consensus read of 3.6%).
  • International revenue +13% to USD682 million (from USD605 million).
  • International organic growth 2.8%.
  • Group Pest Control organic growth 3.7% (North America 4.1%; International 2.8%).
  • Key North America Pest Control Services organic growth 2.8% (vs 2.6% prior quarter), but below one consensus read of 3.4%.
  • Hygiene & Wellbeing organic growth 2.1% (International +2.7%; North America -2.7% due to later phasing of construction timelines).
  • CEO Mike Duffy: “We have made a good start to the year in a seasonally quieter first quarter, with continued momentum in North America and solid progress across our International businesses.”
  • CEO Mike Duffy (outlook): quarter progress “gives us confidence in delivering a full year performance in line with market expectations,” while noting the Middle East crisis.
type_workings: Rule 'results headline': opening bullets reference Q1 figures

summary: Tesco reports strong profit growth and raises free cash flow guidance above expectations
body:
Time Period Year ended 28 February 2026 (statutory); APM sales also shown on a 52-week comparable basis

  • Pretax profit +8.5% to GBP2.40 billion (from GBP2.22 billion).
  • Adjusted operating profit +0.8% to GBP3.15 billion (from GBP3.13 billion), above: (i) consensus GBP3.10 billion and (ii) Tesco’s prior guidance range GBP2.9 billion–GBP3.1 billion.
  • Revenue (ex-VAT, incl. fuel) +5.4% to GBP73.71 billion (from GBP69.92 billion), beating consensus GBP72.55 billion.
  • Sales (ex-VAT, ex-fuel; 52-week comparable basis) +4.6% to GBP66.59 billion.
  • Like-for-like sales +3.5% (52-week comparable basis, ex-VAT, ex-fuel).
  • UK & Republic of Ireland sales +5.1% (52-week comparable basis).
  • UK & Republic of Ireland like-for-like sales +4.2%.
  • Free cash flow +12% to GBP1.96 billion (from GBP1.75 billion).
  • Medium-term free cash flow guidance range upgraded to GBP1.5 billion–GBP2.0 billion (from GBP1.4 billion–GBP1.8 billion).
  • New cost-savings target: “targeting a further GBP500 million saving this year through our Save to Invest programme”.
  • CEO Ken Murphy: “We are committed to doing whatever we can to help keep down the cost of the weekly shop, and with the conflict in the Middle East creating further uncertainty for consumers and the economy more broadly, that commitment matters more than ever. Over the last year, despite cost pressures from new regulation, we have increased our investments in keeping prices low, further improving quality and offering even better service,”
  • Company on upgraded cash guidance: “We are confident that disciplined capital management and progress against our strategic ambitions will allow us to continue to deliver against the sales and profit ambitions of the multi-year performance framework we set out in 2021. Reflecting our confidence in future cash generation, we are upgrading our medium-term free cash flow guidance range,”
  • Company on funding investment: “We will continue to do whatever we can to deliver the very best prices, quality and service for our customers, and are targeting a further GBP500 million saving this year through our Save to Invest programme, to help fund investments in our customer offer.”
  • FY26/27 adjusted operating profit guidance: GBP3.0 billion–GBP3.3 billion.
  • Guidance range widened due to geopolitics: “Reflecting the increased uncertainty caused by the conflict in the Middle East, we are providing a wider range of guidance than we were previously planning. Much will depend upon the duration of the conflict and in particular, the potential implications for UK households and the economy more broadly,”
  • New share buyback: GBP750 million (to be completed by April 2027).
  • Final dividend 9.7p (vs 9.45p); full-year dividend 14.5p (+5.8% vs 13.7p).
  • Special colleague award: GBP65 million.
  • Value investments: tripled “everyday low prices” lines to 3,000; “over 10,000 Clubcard prices” and “more than 600 Aldi price match lines”.
  • Range / premium: “over 2,000 new and improved products”; Finest sales +15% to GBP3 billion.
  • Online: overall online sales +11%; Tesco Whoosh +51%.
  • Analyst takeaways included: “authoritative, disciplined, and statesmanlike year” (Shore Capital) and “testament to the extraordinary delivery over the last year” (Jefferies).
  • Call themes flagged by one analyst note: no discernible change in consumer behaviour so far; “inflation is stable and has been moderating slightly”; reiterated opportunity in non-food; >90% of customers increased spend YoY; upgraded FCF guidance reflects “confidence in future cash flow increasing” (and potentially supports future incremental buybacks).
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with full-year financial figures

summary: Tesco reports 4.6% sales growth with like-for-like sales up 3.5% despite margin pressures
body:
Time Period FY 25/26 (statutory: 53 weeks to 28 Feb 2026; comparable/APMs: 52 weeks to 22 Feb 2026). Includes FY 26/27 guidance.

  • Sales (ex VAT, ex fuel; 52-week): £66,588m, +4.6% YoY (constant rates +4.3%).
  • Group like-for-like sales (52-week): +3.5% (UK +4.2%, ROI +4.6%, Booker +0.2%, Central Europe +2.2%).
  • Adjusted operating profit (52-week): £3,152m, +0.8% YoY (constant rates +0.6%).
  • Adjusted operating margin (52-week): 4.3%, down 12bps YoY.
  • Free cash flow (52-week): £1,957m, +11.8% YoY (+£207m).
  • Net debt: £(10,563)m vs £(9,454)m (increase £(1,109)m, (11.7)% YoY); net debt/EBITDA 2.1x (vs 2.0x).
  • Adjusted diluted EPS: 29.0p, +6.0% YoY.
  • Dividend per share: 14.5p, +5.8% YoY.
  • Statutory revenue (ex VAT, inc fuel; 53-week): £73,712m, +5.4% YoY.
  • Statutory operating profit (53-week): £2,985m, +10.1% YoY.
  • Statutory PBT (53-week): £2,403m, +8.5% YoY.
  • Statutory diluted EPS (continuing ops; 53-week): 27.1p, +16.9% YoY.
  • Statutory profit for the year (after tax; incl discontinued ops): £1,787m, +9.6% YoY.
  • CEO quote (Ken Murphy): “We are committed to doing whatever we can to help keep down the cost of the weekly shop, and with the conflict in the Middle East creating further uncertainty for consumers and the economy more broadly, that commitment matters more than ever. Over the last year, despite cost pressures from new regulation, we have increased our investments in keeping prices low, further improving quality and offering even better service. Customers are choosing to shop more with us as a result, leading to our highest market share for over a decade. Our investments have been made possible by our Save to Invest programme, which has delivered over £2.2bn of savings over the last four years, funding lower prices for customers and higher pay for colleagues, including our recent 5.1% increase in UK hourly pay. Recognising their exceptional service over the last year, I am also pleased to announce a £65m special performance award for colleagues in our stores, distribution centres and customer engagement centres. Our further investments in value included tripling the number of products on Everyday Low Prices to 3,000, running alongside over 10,000 Clubcard Prices and more than 600 Aldi Price Match lines. We have also continued to invest in quality and innovation, with over 2,000 new and improved products across the year, and Finest growing 15% to reach sales of £3bn. We continued to meet customer needs wherever, whenever and however they chose to shop with us, with overall online sales growing 11%, including Tesco Whoosh growth of 51%. Since setting out our multi-year performance framework in 2021, we have delivered meaningful progress for all our stakeholders. As new opportunities and challenges have emerged, we have evolved our strategic ambitions, positioning us well to deliver sustained long-term growth by providing even better value for customers.”
  • “Save to Invest”: >£2.2bn savings over 4 years; FY25/26 delivery c.£535m; new FY26/27 target £500m.
  • New buyback: £750m to be completed by April 2027; completed prior £1.45bn programme (announced April 2025).
  • Upgraded medium-term free cash flow guidance: £1.5bn–£2.0bn (from £1.4bn–£1.8bn).
  • FY26/27 guidance: adjusted operating profit £3.0bn–£3.3bn; free cash flow £1.5bn–£2.0bn.

These new ships will build upon the award-winning Sphere Class platform and continue to leverage the latest guest-facing and marine technologies,”

In addition to these three ships...Carnival Corporation has seven additional new ships under contract that are scheduled for delivery between 2027 and 2033.”

summary: Dunelm Q3 sales grew 2.1% but profit guidance lowered amid consumer softening
body:
Time Period 13 weeks ended 28 March 2026 (Q3 FY26)

  • Q3 total sales £472m, +2.1% YoY.
  • FY26 year-to-date sales £1,398m, +3.1% YoY.
  • Digital participation 43% of Q3 sales, +2ppts YoY.
  • Digital participation 42% year-to-date, +2ppts YoY.
  • Q3 demand pattern: quarter “started well” (good Winter Sale + positive Spring ranges), but broad-based softening more recently, particularly in March.
  • Q3 gross margin +30bps YoY.
  • Margin drivers: continued benefit from earlier FX tailwind, but mix impact from customers seeking value (more discounted products vs full price lines).
  • Profit guide: FY26 PBT expected towards the lower end of consensus.
  • Consensus referenced by company: £213m average, range £210m–£217m.
  • Cost actions: H2 cost plans (set out at Interim Results) remain on track; ongoing productivities + disciplined investment approach.
  • Middle East instability: expected to have only a small direct cost impact in FY26.
  • Q4 trading posture: strong events calendar (incl. Summer Sale) but company not assuming an immediate improvement in consumer confidence.
  • Digital/product: full launch of mobile app in Q3; downloads >300k with early encouragement on conversion and spend per transaction.
  • Space growth: significantly stronger pipeline of new store openings expected next financial year; Kingston-upon-Thames planned to open in summer.
  • Next scheduled event: Q4 trading update on 16 July 2026.
  • CEO quote (Clo Moriarty):

"We saw further sales growth in Q3, against an uncertain backdrop for both customers and businesses. "Although the external environment is not helpful in the short term, we continue to focus on the areas within our control - strengthening our proposition while operating efficiently and effectively. Alongside this, we are making good progress building our long-term growth plans with some exciting developments beginning to emerge, including a much stronger store opening pipeline and some encouraging early results from our recently launched app. "Our final quarter provides multiple opportunities for Dunelm to stay front of mind for customers, including our popular Summer Sale. We remain confident that our comprehensive offer will continue to resonate with homelovers."

summary: Londonmetric reports strong NNN portfolio growth with 16% net rental income increase and 4% dividend progression
body:
Time Period Year ended 31 March 2026 (FY26), trading update ahead of full-year results (21 May 2026)

  • Portfolio size: £7.6bn NNN portfolio; continued performance following Urban Logistics REIT integration.
  • Net rental income up c.16% YoY to >£450m.
  • Occupancy 98%.
  • Average lease length 17 years.
  • Like-for-like income growth +4.2%.
  • Rent review outcomes: average uplift 19%.
  • Logistics open market rent reviews: average uplift 38%.
  • EPRA cost ratio 7.7%, continuing to fall; management expects further reduction.
  • Dividend: expects FY dividend per share +4% to 12.45p (11th consecutive year of progression).
  • Disposals (FY): 57 assets sold for £318m, blended NIY 5.7%, WAULT 12.5 years; sales in line with book values.
  • Disposals included 41 former LXi REIT + ULR assets, netting £171m.
  • Cumulative disposal progress:

– 72 LXi REIT assets sold for £298m (11% of original portfolio). – 17 ULR assets sold for £55m (5% of original portfolio).

  • Since last update: 20 more assets sold for £57m, comprising:

– 11 urban logistics assets (WAULT 4 years) sold for £31.0m (9 were former ULR). – 5 Travelodge hotels sold for £17.2m. – 4 convenience/DIY assets sold for £9.0m, including a drive-thru at 4.2% NIY.

  • Direct investments (FY): 35 acquisitions for £333m, WAULT 33 years, blended NIY 5.5% rising to c.6.1% over next five years.
  • M&A additions: £1.2bn of assets acquired via M&A (in addition to the £333m direct investments).
  • Recent acquisitions: 10 assets for £79m, including:

– 4 Premier Inn hotels (570 bedrooms), WAULT 22 years, £47.8m, 5.6% NIY, CPI-linked reviews, guaranteed by Whitbread PLC. – 106,000 sq ft logistics unit (Irlam, Greater Manchester) for £12.1m. – £19.1m of additional assets incl. ~300-bed Crown Plaza hotel (Manchester Airport).

  • Asset management: added c.£16.5m p.a. contracted rent from 327 initiatives.
  • Rent reviews: 258 reviews added c.£11m p.a., average uplifts 19%; logistics reviews average +21% (open market +38%).
  • Lettings/regears: 69 deals added c.£6m p.a.; 16 former ULR assets added £2m p.a.; deals had WAULT 10 years; regears average uplift 23%.
  • Financing actions (FY): refinanced £1.5bn, raised £1.2bn new debt, repaid £1.1bn.
  • Debt markets: £500m debut senior unsecured bond (Dec 2025), A- (Fitch), coupon 4.69%.
  • Bank facilities: £1.5bn unsecured facilities refinanced (Mar 2026), with longer-dated term loans/RCFs, reduced margins and commitment fees.
  • Year-end debt profile: average drawn debt maturity 4.4 years; only £0.2bn expiring in next two years, covered by sales and/or c.£0.5bn undrawn facilities.
  • CEO quote (Andrew Jones):

"Despite macro uncertainty, volatile bond yields and reduced liquidity, we have continued to improve the quality of the portfolio with some excellent disposals and acquisitions. "Our all-weather portfolio continues to deliver excellent income growth as we benefit from our alignment to the winning sectors. We are reaping the rewards of this strategy, and it is wonderfully comforting to see our rental income flowing and growing to record levels. This continued income compounding is enabling us to deliver our eleventh consecutive year of dividend progression."
type_workings: Rule 'trading update': 'trading update' named in opening bullets

summary: Schroders Q1 AUM fell 1.1% to £814.4bn amid market weakness and equity outflows
body:
Time Period Q1 2026 ended 31 March 2026 (plus shareholder meetings on 16 April 2026)

  • Group AUM £814.4bn at 31 Mar 2026 vs £823.7bn at 31 Dec 2025 (down £9.3bn / -1.1%).
  • NNB excl. JVs/associates: £(2.2)bn in Q1.
  • NNB incl. JVs/associates: £(1.1)bn in Q1.
  • Q1 AUM bridge (Group):

– Net flows: £(1.1)bn. – Investment returns/FX/other: £(5.9)bn (but currency movements added £6.5bn). – Acquisitions/closures: £(2.3)bn.

  • Total AUM excl. JVs/associates: £720.1bn (from £729.6bn), with £(2.2)bn NNB and £(5.0)bn investment returns/FX/other.
  • Asset Management AUM £599.4bn (from £605.7bn), driven by NNB £(2.5)bn + negative market impact.
  • Public Markets AUM £526.0bn (from £533.1bn): net flows £(2.8)bn, investment returns/FX/other £(2.4)bn, acquisitions/closures £(1.9)bn.
  • Demand pattern: positive net flows in Jan–Feb, then March reversal as Middle East escalation drove risk-off sentiment.
  • Asset class flow detail (Asset Management):

– Fixed income NNB +£1.8bn. – Multi-asset NNB +£1.2bn. – Equities NNB £(4.9)bn (March redemptions elevated). – Core solutions NNB £(0.9)bn (run-off of a lower margin mandate + short-term operational cash flow movements).

  • Schroders Capital AUM £73.4bn (from £72.6bn): NNB +£0.3bn; inflows in PE, private debt, credit alternatives, infrastructure offset by real estate outflows.
  • JVs/associates: NNB +£1.1bn in Q1 vs £(8.5)bn in Q1 2025; largest contribution from Bank of Communications China fund management venture.
  • Channel flows (Asset Management):

– Intermediary NNB +£1.9bn. – Institutional NNB £(4.4)bn.

  • Business simplification actions: transitioned out of Brazil and Indonesia operations; expanded outsourcing with strategic partner UST.
  • ETF progress: European Active ETF range (6 months post launch) ranked highest for net new business into European Active ETFs in Q1, with continued work towards further international expansion.
  • CEO quote (Richard Oldfield):

"Our performance in the first quarter of 2026 reflected the shift in market conditions as the period progressed. In January and February, demand trends from late 2025 continued, with strong intermediary net flows into our Public Markets strategies, while Group AUM was buoyed by strong markets. In March we saw a reversal of these trends. As tensions escalated in the Middle East, client sentiment shifted to a more risk-off stance amid heightened geopolitical uncertainty. We continue to focus on supporting clients as they navigate these conditions. "We remain focused on our controllable cost base, whilst continuing to invest in areas of strength. Since the start of the year, we have successfully transitioned out of our operations in Brazil and Indonesia and expanded outsourcing with our strategic partner, UST. Six months after launch, our European Active ETF range has built strong momentum, ranking highest for net new business into European Active ETFs in the first quarter, alongside continued progress towards further international ETF expansion."

  • M&A situation (as referenced): boards announced a recommended all-cash acquisition of Schroders by Nuveen (via Bidco), with shareholder meetings held 16 April 2026.
  • Offer terms referenced publicly: up to 612p/share (590p cash + up to 22p permitted dividends), valuing Schroders at ~£9.9bn.
  • Timetable detail: Scheme shareholder meetings held 16 April 2026 (Court Meeting + General Meeting).

summary: Rentokil Q1 2026 revenue grew 4.3% organically, driven by pricing and North America pest control strength
body:
Time Period Q1 2026 (1 January 2026 to 31 March 2026)

  • Group revenue $1,677m, +4.3% (constant currency), organic +3.4%.
  • Reported revenue growth shown as +7.8% (vs $1,556m in Q1 2025).
  • North America revenue $995m, +4.5% (constant currency), organic +3.9%.
  • North America Pest Control revenue $965m, +4.7% (constant currency), organic +4.1%.
  • Pest Control Services organic revenue growth +2.8% (vs +2.6% in Q4 2025), supported by price increases + recovery from late-Jan weather disruption in the US.
  • Pest Control Services performance benefited from growth initiatives: enhanced marketing investments and improved sales execution.
  • Business Services organic revenue growth +12.7% (vs +7.8% in Q4 2025), driven by pre-spring demand in product distribution + contribution from 2025 new business wins (brand standards) + high-value lake management work.
  • Hygiene & Wellbeing (North America) organic revenue growth -2.7%, impacted by later phasing of construction timelines delaying Ambius jobbing revenue.
  • Retention metrics:

– Colleague retention 82.6% (FY25: 82.2%, +0.4ppts). – Customer retention 80.4% (FY25: 80.5%, -0.1ppts).

  • International revenue $682m, +4.1% (constant currency), organic +2.8%.
  • International: growth in Europe, LatAm, UK & Sub-Saharan Africa driven by pricing + volumes.
  • MENAT + Pacific were a 60bps headwind (Pacific lapped strong Rural/Trackspray comps; MENAT disrupted by Middle East conflict).
  • Category organic growth:

– Pest Control +3.7% (North America +4.1%; International +2.8%; +3.0% ex-MENAT). – Hygiene & Wellbeing +2.1% (International +2.7%; North America -2.7%).

  • M&A: 9 bolt-on deals completed; acquired businesses delivered $19m annualised revenue in the year pre-acquisition.
  • CEO quote (Mike Duffy):

"We have made a good start to the year in a seasonally quieter first quarter, with continued momentum in North America and solid progress across our International businesses. "In my first four weeks as CEO, I have been impressed with the depth of passion and pride that our people have in delivering exceptional customer service, and I am excited by the opportunity to unlock the potential of the Group. "While geopolitical events, as previously noted, continue to create some uncertainty, the progress we report today gives us confidence in delivering a full year performance in line with market expectations."

summary: Ashmore's AuM fell 3.4% to $50.7bn due to geopolitical disruption and institutional redemptions
body:
Time Period Quarter ended 31 March 2026 (Q3 FY26 AuM statement)

  • AuM down $1.8bn to $50.7bn from $52.5bn (≈ -3.4%).
  • AuM movement split: -$0.9bn investment performance and -$0.9bn net outflows.
  • By investment theme (31 Dec 2025 → 31 Mar 2026):

– External debt: $8.4bn → $8.0bn (≈ -4.8%). – Local currency: $15.7bn → $16.4bn (≈ +4.5%). – Corporate debt: $5.3bn → $5.1bn (≈ -3.8%). – Blended debt: $12.5bn → $10.5bn (-16.0%).

  • Asset class totals:

– Fixed income: $41.9bn → $40.0bn (≈ -4.5%). – Equities: $8.8bn → $8.8bn (flat). – Alternatives: $1.8bn → $1.9bn (≈ +5.6%).

  • Narrative driver: positive EM momentum from 2025 continued early in quarter but was interrupted by broadened Middle East conflict (end-Feb) → higher volatility and “wait and see” investor stance.
  • Flow colour: subscriptions “healthy” with net inflows in local currency and equities, but institutional redemption in blended debt drove net outflow there; small net outflow from external debt.
  • Market backdrop: EM indices flat to down 3% over the quarter; strong Jan/Feb then weaker March.
  • Performance messaging: Ashmore claims continued outperformance across equity and fixed income; proportion of AuM outperforming benchmarks consistent near-to-mid term, and improved over longer term.
  • Strategic development: announced strategic partnership with Japan Post Insurance Co., Ltd at period end.
  • CEO quote (Mark Coombs):

"Geopolitical events interrupted some of the macro tailwinds supporting emerging markets, but the reaction across most asset classes has so far been manageable and with limited price dislocations. In the near term, investors globally are taking a more measured stance until there is greater clarity in the outlook for the duration of the conflict and its implications for factors such as commodity prices, inflation, interest rates and currency trends. "As ever, it is paramount to understand the rich diversity of emerging markets and therefore to manage portfolios actively to deliver investment performance. Ashmore's specialist approach and long history of investing in emerging markets, across different cycles and complex geopolitical and macro events, means it is well positioned to navigate the current environment. "At the end of the period, Ashmore was delighted to announce the establishment of a strategic partnership with Japan Post Insurance Co., Ltd. This new strategic partnership builds on a strong existing relationship in order to capitalise on the growth opportunity across a broad range of attractive emerging market asset classes."
type_workings: Rule 'results figures': opening bullets lead with full-year financial figures

summary: easyJet expects H1 FY26 headline loss of £540m-£560m, up 37-42% year-on-year
body:
Time Period Six months ended 31 March 2026 (H1 FY26)

  • easyJet expects headline loss before tax of £540m–£560m for H1 FY26.
  • Prior-year comparative (six months ended 31 March 2025) headline LBT was £394m (implying ~+37% to +42% larger loss YoY vs the expected range).
  • Load factor 90%, +2ppts YoY.
  • easyJet holidays customers +22% in H1.
  • Underlying H1 result described as broadly in line with expectations, with revenue/costs in line excluding:

– ~£25m incremental fuel costs in March due to Middle East conflict. – ~£30m net increase in legal provisions across historic cases.

  • Strategic investments: Milan Linate + Rome Fiumicino performed in line with expectations in first winter year (£30m investment cost).
  • YoY performance also impacted by capacity investments to drive winter utilisation and competitive overcapacity in certain markets.
  • Q2 RASK +~3% YoY, driven by modest route maturity + earlier Easter timing; strength in domestics/cities/Western Med offset by war-related softness in Egypt, Turkey, Cyprus.
  • Operational metrics:

– Airline on-time performance 78% (+1ppt YoY). – Airline customer satisfaction 84% (+2ppts YoY). – easyJet holidays customer satisfaction 85%.

  • FY26 cost outlook:

– Airline headline CASK ex fuel: full-year expectation broadly unchanged, but fuel volatility creates uncertainty for total CASK. – H1 Airline headline CASK ex fuel +~8% YoY (inflation weighted to H1; resilience measures annualisation; airport rate phasing; higher winter load factors; digitalisation; plus ~£30m legal provisions). – H2 Airline headline CASK ex fuel expected to rise low single digits YoY.

  • Fuel dynamics:

– March: Middle East escalation drove ~£25m incremental cost (unhedged March requirements 18% bought at spot). – H1 fuel CASK down ~5% YoY. – H1 Airline headline CASK up ~5% YoY overall.

  • Booking curve: conflict introduced uncertainty; booking curve shortened, reducing forward visibility.
  • Balance sheet/liquidity: net cash £434m, liquidity £4.7bn, and owns 86% of neo aircraft (flexibility).
  • Hedging + sensitivity:

– H2 FY26: 70% hedged at $706/metric tonne (spot at 15/04/26: $1,500). – Every $100 move in fuel prices ≈ £40m cost in H2 FY26.

  • Bookings:

– Q3: 63% sold, -2ppts YoY, ticket yield marginally down; 1ppt Q3 RASK ≈ £26m revenue. – Q4: 30% sold, -2ppts YoY, ticket yield modestly up; 1ppt Q4 RASK ≈ £33m revenue.

  • easyJet holidays:

– H2 currently 67% sold. – FY26 customer growth expected low double-digit YoY (competitive market).

  • Next milestone: Half-year results on Thursday, 21 May 2026.
  • CEO quote (Kenton Jarvis):

“easyJet saw continued positive demand in the first half, driven by our great value flights and holidays, alongside a continued focus on our operations and customer experience. “Despite these positives, our H1 financial performance worsened year on year, impacted by the conflict in the Middle East and the competitive environment in some markets. Following our busiest Easter holiday period ever, the operational ramp up into peak summer continues as planned. “easyJet’s financial strength from our investment grade balance sheet and £4.7 billion of liquidity mean we are well placed to navigate current geopolitical challenges while remaining focused on our medium term targets.”
